Entry-copy of enum-field struct from fresh-temp ctor arg leaks (#22): inline struct-literal arg with an enum leaf, callee consumes it via match, no caller drop — track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg gated on enum-blind aggregate_has_heap_field; added source-level copy-supported gate
Auto-par reduction: for _ wildcard loop var failed to lower (fell back to sequential)

Self-host lexer per-iteration leak — inline enum-ctor temp call arg missing caller-side drop

String collection method (split/contains) on a non-identifier receiver fell through identifier-keyed dispatch — materialize synth local + route to compile_vec_method (Vec-element-typed residual still open)
auto-par parallelized console-output stmts (no resource effect); workers raced on stdout, reordered output

Map field drop hardcoded karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(handle,1,1) — for an occupied scalar Map[i64,i64] the runtime read offset-16 of an 8-byte key as a bogus cap and freed the key VALUE as a pointer (corruption); now compute (drop_key,drop_val) from K/V types via map_drop_flags. Hit both emit_tuple_elem_drops AND the regular struct-field MapOrSet drop (plain struct S{m:Map[i64,i64]} crashed on main)

Map leaf in a tuple inside a struct field double-freed — Maps are caller-retains (origin FreeMapHandle frees) but #21's NestedTuple struct drop added a second freer; fix transfers the handle to the tuple owner at construction (Part B) + TypeExpr tuple-var drop (Part A) + null-on-move-into-struct (Part C1). Original tracker hypothesis (by-value-consume double-free) DISPROVEN: by-value Map param is caller-retains

synthesize_tuple_drop_fn_te memoization key (type_expr_sig) used only the base path segment, so Map[i64,i64] (flags (0,0)) and Map[String,i64] (flags (1,0)) shared one drop fn keyed Map_i64; whichever map shape was synthesized first dropped the other's heap (scalar-first leaked a later Map[String,_]'s keys; String-first ran drop_key=1 over a scalar map, the B-2026-06-13-19 garbage-free class). Fix: fold generic args into the sig. Pre-existing #23-era bug, surfaced by #24 making the call-RHS tuple path reachable

phase-12 #24: a let-bound tuple VAR sourced from a CALL (let p = ret_tuple(i), RHS a Call not a tuple literal, no annotation) whose only heap is an enum/Map/Set leaf leaked 1/iter - track_tuple_var's LLVM walk is enum/Map-blind and tuple_binding_elem_tes missed the call-result source. Fix recovers element TypeExprs from the callee's return type (fn_return_type_exprs). Method-call RHS deferred (leaks, never double-frees)

whole-program 'karac query effects'/'query concurrency' looked each fn up by the call-graph node key, but call_graph::render_target_type keyed impl methods by the rendered receiver (Box[T].m via render_type_expr) while effectchecker+concurrency key by the bare base name (Box.m via segments.last()). Keys agreed for plain receivers but diverged for GENERIC ones, so an impl[T] Box[T] method reported empty effects under 'query effects' and was dropped entirely from 'query concurrency' (silent data loss). Fix: render_target_type extracts the bare base name, matching NodeInfo's documented join contract; also fixes Type.assoc() edges to generic types. Surfaced by the Cartographer dogfood

phase-12 #25: reading a struct field through a <struct>.tuplefield.0.<field> chain (h.ps.0.n / match h.ps.0.tok) mis-compiled - type_name_of_expr's TupleIndex arm resolved only Identifier-rooted tuples, so field_index_for found no element struct type and compile_field_access returned the i64 0 placeholder (scalar read 0; enum-field match scrutinee never resolved -> no String dispatch for arm binding s -> no-handler build-fail). Fix: resolve the element struct type via place_chain_tuple_tes for non-Identifier-rooted tuples. Original 'arm-binding dispatch registrar' hypothesis disproven - typechecker typed s fine, the codegen READ was the bug

phase-12 #26: a method on a Map/Set tuple element (h.m.0.len()) read a GARBAGE handle. Map/Set lower to an opaque ptr handle whose runtime methods resolve via a NAMED slot (compile_map_method -> get_data_ptr), so the dispatch sites gate on object.kind==Identifier; a tuple-index Map receiver fell through to a generic path. Fix: try_compile_tuple_index_receiver_method GEPs the element handle slot (field_chain_place_ptr) and re-dispatches via a synth identifier (shared compile_method_via_synth_elem_ptr, factored out of the FieldAccess peer). Gated to Map/Set (Vec/scalar work via value extraction). Synth aliases h's handle slot - reads borrow, in-place insert mutates, h is sole freer. Original framing (element-0 GEP load) disproven

phase-12 #27: binding a heap-bearing value OUT of a tuple element double-freed at scope exit. let inr = h.ps.0 (heap struct moved out) and let tk = h.ps.0.tok (enum field moved out through the tuple element) each registered the binding's drop (via the lowering type annotation) but did NOT suppress the SOURCE, so both the binding's drop and the owning h's NestedTuple tuple drop freed the same buffer. Fix: (1) call suppress_tuple_index_move_source in the struct-binding path (zero_tuple_elem_cap_at routes a struct element through zero_struct_move_caps); (2) new suppress_place_field_enum_move_source for <tupleindex>.field via place-chain GEP + zero_enum_payload_caps. Part (a) type-registration was already done by the annotation - only source-suppress (b) was missing

phase-12 #28: a Map/Set bound to a LOCAL from a place source (let mm = s.m / let mm = h.m.0) build-failed 'no handler for method len on variable mm' - the let-stmt's unannotated-RHS fallback had Vec/VecDeque/String arms but none for Map/Set, so mm got var_type_names but never the K/V dispatch side-tables. Fix: a Map/Set arm registers them via register_var_from_type_expr keyed off the typechecker's pattern_binding_inner_types. Dispatch-only (no FreeMapHandle) - track_map_var is gated on a fresh-handle RHS, so mm stays a caller-retains alias and the owner is the sole freer (no double-free). Annotated form already worked. Surfaced fixing #26

phase-12 #13: no Unicode char classifier - Kara shipped only u8.is_ascii_* byte predicates. Added char.is_alphabetic/is_numeric/is_alphanumeric/is_whitespace end-to-end: typecheck (char receiver -> Bool, rejected on non-char), interp (Value::Char arm via Rust char methods), codegen (karac_runtime_char_is_* externs - Unicode tables can't be inlined; char lowers to i32, extern returns i8), runtime (char::from_u32(cp).is_some_and). Verified interp==codegen incl Unicode (Greek alpha U+03B1 is_alphabetic, Devanagari digit U+096B is_numeric). Unblocks the lexer multi-codepoint non-ASCII recovery (#29)

`let w = v[i]` for a heap-owned Vec[String]/Vec[Vec] (cap>0) element double-freed at scope exit: compile_index returns a SHALLOW element struct sharing the buffer, so both w's drop and v's element-drop free it. Codegen-only (karac check/run fine, only build SIGABRTs); masked for literal cap-0 elements (#171). Vec-index sibling of the move-out family (B-2026-06-14-8). Fix: deep-clone at the let bind (clone_owned_vec_index_element, matching interp's clone — v[i] stays valid), via the per-type clone fn. Scoped to String/Vec; enum/struct Vec elements -> B-2026-06-14-12

Reading a heap-bearing ENUM or STRUCT Vec element (Vec[E] where E.Tag(String), or Vec[struct{name:String}]) shallow-aliases the container buffer — same root as B-2026-06-14-11 but enum/struct elements fell through clone_owned_vec_index_element's vec-struct gate AND emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr shallow-cloned user enum/struct (no #[derive(Clone)] analog). Codegen-only (interp clones, build SIGABRTs/ASAN double-free). Fix has 3 parts: (1) emit_struct_clone_fn + emit_enum_clone_fn synthesize deep clones (struct mirrors emit_tuple_clone_fn; enum mirrors emit_enum_drop_switch's tag-switch + per-variant word-region field clone), wired into emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr's Path branch; (2) broadened clone_owned_vec_index_element's gate (dropped the llvm_ty_is_vec_struct restriction) so enum/struct/tuple `let w = v[i]` route to the new clones; (3) clone the scrut at the `match v[i] {V(s)=>}` compile site (so the arm extractvalue reads the clone) + materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee now drop-tracks a heap Vec-index scrutinee (expr_is_heap_vec_index) so a no-bind arm frees the clone. ASAN regression: asan_let_bound_vec_enum_struct_element_no_double_free

A `for x in xs` loop binding sharing a name with an earlier same-function `let x` was conflated by the ownership RC analysis: the formal RC predicate paired the prior binding's Consume (e.g. `push(handle)`) with the loop body's use of the loop var (`handle.join()`) as dominance-incomparable across the loop boundary and inserted a spurious RC fallback on the shared name; codegen (which keys RC by demangled name) then RC-boxed the binding and mis-lowered the plain `{i64}` loop element as an Rc pointer -> SEGV (native, exit 139) / TaskHandle.join deadlock (wasm-threads). Root cause: cfg.rs's ExprKind::For arm ignored the loop pattern (no scoping, no Define), so the loop var shared the outer binding's identity. Fix: scope the for-loop binding to a per-loop @forN rename frame (like match arms' @armN) + record a Define for it (src/cfg.rs push_for_rename_frame). demangle_binding strips @forN for rc_values/diagnostics. Surfaced + fixed building Fathom; regression tests/codegen.rs::e2e_for_loop_binding_name_collision_no_false_rc

TaskHandle[T].join() returned a NON-scalar T as garbage + trapped. recover_task_handle_join_return_ty hardcoded i64 (documented slice-4 limitation), so a spawn returning Vec/String/struct had join read i64-shaped bytes off the runtime result buffer instead of the {ptr,len,cap} header - .len() was garbage and the program SIGABRT'd (native exit 133) / 'unreachable' (wasm-threads). Scalar joins were fine; the spawn write side was already correctly sized. Fix: typechecker records each join's T in a new span-keyed task_join_return_types table (intercept in infer_method_call, mirrors the spawn/channel_elem_types pattern), forwarded ast->lowering->codegen; recover_task_handle_join_return_ty now looks it up + lowers via llvm_type_for_type_expr (i64 fallback only for unrecorded sites e.g. discarded-handle accept loops). Verified native + wasm-threads return Vec[u8] row-bands intact. Surfaced building Fathom (parallel Mandelbrot, examples/fathom)

Numeric (int/float) f-string interpolation traps on ALL wasm targets: println(f"{x}") for x:i64/f64 (any to_string-via-snprintf path) aborts with 'unreachable' because wasm-ld emits a trapping `signature_mismatch:snprintf` stub. Root cause: codegen declares int snprintf(char*, size_t n, const char*, ...) with i64 for `size_t n` (src/codegen.rs ~2582, hardcoded i64_type) - correct on 64-bit native but WRONG on wasm32 where size_t is i32 (pointer width), so the declared signature can't reconcile with wasi-libc's snprintf and wasm-ld replaces the call with a trap. Confirmed sequential wasm_browser AND --features wasm-threads; affects wasm_wasi too. String-only f-strings (f"{name}") + plain println work (no snprintf). PRE-EXISTING, not introduced by the Fathom slice - surfaced debugging a Fathom test program with f-string println. FIXED: declare + call snprintf with a pointer-width size_t (i32 on wasm32 / i64 on 64-bit, via the target usize/pointer-int type, not hardcoded i64_type) and pass the 64-byte buf size with matching width at the call sites (src/codegen/runtime.rs ~4958 fst path + control_flow.rs compile_print). Regression: a wasm E2E asserting println(f"{42}") prints 42.

#[derive(Display)] on a baked-stdlib enum (IoError, VarError) renders correctly in the interpreter but degraded in AOT: a compiled main() -> Result[(), IoError] returning Err printed 'Error: 0', f"{io_err}"/println(io_err) printed a placeholder, and a payload-variant match couldn't bind its payload ('Undefined variable m'). Root cause was deeper than the tracker's 'imprecise seeded tags' hypothesis: IoError was NEVER seeded - no codegen layout at all (not in seed_builtin_enum_layouts, not in the user program_snapshot which excludes baked stdlib). That single gap caused all three symptoms - construction fell to an i64 0 placeholder, the payload match couldn't bind, and emit_enum_display_fn had an empty variant set. Companion: the bare variant 'Other'/'PermissionDenied' collide across IoError/Utf8Error/TcpError/TlsError, so qualified construction+match picked a wrong tag by HashMap order. FIXED: (1) seed the IoError layout in src/codegen/declarations.rs::seed_builtin_enum_layouts (mirrors the Utf8Error Other(String) template - 4-word struct, tags NotFound=0..Other=6, Other drops VecOrString); (2) emit_enum_display_fn falls back to crate::prelude::STDLIB_PROGRAMS for variant names+kinds when the enum isn't in program_snapshot; (3) new baked_display_enum_names set (populated once from STDLIB_PROGRAMS via extract_derived_traits) re-admits Display-deriving baked enums in expr_user_enum_name_any despite their seeded status so the f-string/println dispatch reaches the generic Display path; (4) honor the qualified Enum.Variant path in construction (try_compile_enum_variant gains enum_name_override, passed by the qualified compile_assoc_call constructor; refinement Ok/Err pin Result) and in match (compile_pattern_condition Binding/TupleVariant/Struct arms prefer variant_pattern_enum_and_tag). Latent until phase-8 entry-point Slice C's C2 made baked error enums Display-eligible. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_baked_stdlib_enum_display_and_qualified_match. Surfaced by the entry-point Slice C recon (phase-8-stdlib-floor.md line 675).

wasm_browser --features wasm-threads: ANY parallel program (TaskGroup.spawn/par{}) DEADLOCKS in a real browser - the canvas/output never updates. Node (worker_threads) works, so it passed E2E and looked shipped. Root cause is in the emitted threaded glue (src/wasm_glue.rs GLUE_STATIC_BODY): wasi.thread-spawn created the pthread Web Worker FROM INSIDE the calling worker (`new Worker(new URL('#kara-thread-worker', import.meta.url))` in karaThreadWorkerMain), and the caller then immediately blocks in join()/recv() on memory.atomic.wait32. In browsers a nested dedicated worker's module script is fetched on its CREATING agent's event loop; a blocked creator never turns its loop, so the sibling's script never loads (observed in headless Chrome via CDP: 91 worker targets created with url='', none ever ran their module top-level). Every spawned task sits unclaimed, join() blocks forever, and the scheduler keeps spawning workers chasing an idle one -> hard deadlock. The glue's own comment claimed 'the classic PROXY_TO_PTHREAD model' but only proxied the PROGRAM to a primary worker, NOT thread CREATION. FIXED by completing the model (Emscripten-style): route every worker's thread-spawn to the always-live MAIN thread via a shared spawn-request ring (SPAWN_CTL SAB + requestMainThreadSpawn producer + startSpawnService main-thread drain that calls spawnKaraWorkerSync); thread-spawn still allocates+returns the tid synchronously (wasi-threads ABI) and only defers Worker construction. node keeps spawning siblings directly (nodeWorkerThreads branch) - no such constraint there. Verified end-to-end in headless Chrome: examples/fathom now renders the Mandelbrot at ~120fps across 18 cores (132859/153600 non-black px, 127 colors). Surfaced building the Fathom dogfood demo (it just showed a black screen). NOT browser-CI-testable without a headless browser harness (node path can't reproduce it). Regression: src/wasm_glue.rs unit test threaded_glue_renders_pick_constants_and_machinery asserts the spawn-proxy machinery is emitted.
StringSlice v1: slice/find + source-pinned by-value -> StringSlice (first_word); split-views stay v2
ref/mut-ref String stdlib methods degraded to Type::Error (find/slice unrouted); unwrap_or fell through
write_console panic-free (try_with + realloc buffers) restores lean binary floor; @fwrite test->write_console

A body-local owned heap `let` (Vec/String/Map/Set/Slice/array element) declared inside a for-over-COLLECTION loop leaks every iteration but the last. Only compile_for_range[_with_step] pushed a per-iteration scope-cleanup frame; the eight collection for-variants (compile_for_vec_var/slice_var/map_var/set_var/array_var/array_values/string_chars_inner/string_bytes_inner in src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs) called compile_block(body) with NO frame, so each body-local binding registered its FreeVecBuffer/drop in the enclosing FUNCTION frame and only the FINAL iteration's value was freed at the function tail - N-1 iterations' worth leaked (confirmed via `leaks --atExit`: 49999/50000). Surfaced as a hard browser OOM in the Fathom dogfood demo: render_frame's `for handle in handles { let chunk = handle.join(); for byte in chunk {...} }` leaked the joined Vec[u8] (~76KB) every frame, exhausting the wasm32 1GB linear-memory ceiling after ~550 frames (__karac_alloc_or_panic64 -> std::process::abort -> unreachable). NOT join-specific and NOT wasm-specific (native RSS climbed 175->461MB on the repro; wasm just hits a hard ceiling); for-over-range was already correct, which is why only collection loops leaked. FIXED: a shared compile_loop_body_with_cleanup(body, continue_bb) helper that pushes a per-iteration frame, compiles the body, and on normal fall-through drain_top_frame_with_emit()s before the back-edge branch; a body terminator (break/continue/return) pops without emitting (the loop_stack cleanup_depth walk already drained it). Wired into all eight collection variants (array_values inlines the drain since it is straight-line-unrolled, string_chars_inner inlines to preserve its byte_offset store). Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_for_over_collection_body_local_no_leak (Linux-CI LSan is the leak gate; mac checks no double-free/UAF from the added cleanup). Verified: leaks 0 (was 49999); Fathom soaks 1750 frames @120fps no OOM (was ~550); memory_sanitizer 246/0.

wasm-threads browser builds: the WASI-preview1 polyfill's fd_write and random_get (emitted in src/wasm_glue.rs GLUE_STATIC_BODY) pass a SharedArrayBuffer-backed Uint8Array view straight to TextDecoder.decode / crypto.getRandomValues, which BOTH reject shared-backed views ('The provided ArrayBufferView value must not be shared.'). So ANY stdout/stderr write from a threaded browser program - a print, a panic, or the alloc-error abort path - throws a TypeError inside the polyfill, turning a benign diagnostic into a fatal worker error (it masked the real OOM message of B-2026-06-14-21 in the Fathom dogfood until fixed). node (non-shared buffer + fs.writeSync path) is unaffected, so the node E2E never caught it. FIXED: fd_write decodes a .slice() copy (fresh non-shared ArrayBuffer); random_get fills a non-shared scratch Uint8Array via crypto.getRandomValues then .set()s it into linear memory. Verified end-to-end in headless Chrome (Fathom prints/aborts with no 'must not be shared' TypeError). Regression: emit-level needles in wasm_glue::threaded_glue_renders_pick_constants_and_machinery. FOLLOW-UP: 69c49ec0 MISSED the exported readString() helper - the single string-decode funnel used by the threaded main-thread host-service ctx.readString (a host fn taking a string arg, line ~1395) and the rich string-export lift (karaLift case 4) - which has the identical shared-view bug (latent: Plume/Fathom take no string host-fn args so it never fired there; node's lenient TextDecoder hid it). Fixed by .slice()-copying inside readString too, covering all 3 call sites at once; regression needle added to the same wasm_glue test.

Vec/String.with_capacity miscompiled on wasm32 with an i32 count (.len()-derived) — i32*i64 byte-size multiply + i32-into-i64 cap field/alloc param emit invalid IR; latent until loop-bound pre-sizing injected with_capacity(<i32 bound>)

karac-runtime clippy-red under `cargo clippy --all --all-targets -- -D warnings`: `clashing_extern_declarations` on `realloc`. runtime/src/lib.rs's OutputCapture allocator hooks (added by aabc01c8, B-2026-06-14-20 panic-free write_console) declared `extern "C" fn realloc(ptr: *mut c_void, size) -> *mut c_void` while runtime/src/alloc.rs already declares `fn realloc(ptr: *mut u8, size) -> *mut u8` for the SAME libc symbol — two mismatched decls in one crate trip the lint. NOT cfg(test)-gated, so it also breaks CI's `cargo clippy --all -- -D warnings`. Exactly the '--all-targets not --tests/--all' coverage class CLAUDE.md flags. FIXED: align lib.rs's decl to `*mut u8` (matching alloc.rs — same byte allocator) and drop the now-redundant `as *mut c_void`/`as *mut u8` casts at the two call sites; `free` keeps `*mut c_void` (alloc.rs doesn't declare it, so no clash). Surfaced running the events.wheel slice's clippy gate.

Map/Set returned BY VALUE from a call and bound (`let m2 = make_map()`) leaks the handle on Linux LSan (silent on macOS — no LeakSanitizer there). The binding registered its dispatch side-tables (`m2.len()` compiles) but the let-path fresh-handle gate that queues `FreeMapHandle` only matched `clone`/`union`/`intersection`/`difference` MethodCalls, not a plain `Call`. The callee suppresses its own free on the move-out `return m;`, so with no caller-side track the handle leaks. FIXED: extend the gate to an owned-returning `Call`, excluding borrow-returning callees (`fn_ref_return_inner`, e.g. `ref Map` accessors). Latent since the call-sourced-Map-bind+method feature landed this window; authored macOS-only so LSan never saw it. Reddened CI memory-sanitizer (asan_returned_map_explicit_return_no_double_free).

Bare tuple over a `Map.new()`-created var (`let t = (d, i)`) leaks the Map leaf on Linux LSan. The #23 Part A tuple drop never registered because `compile_map_new_stmt`/`compile_set_new_stmt` never recorded the binding's surface type name, so `type_name_of(d)` returned None, `infer_arg_elem_te` produced an empty `Path`, and `type_expr_has_drop_heap` was false — the tuple drop was skipped. The place-source path (`let mm = s.m`) already records "Map" via `record_var_type_name`. FIXED: mirror that recording in the two `*_new_stmt` builders. The tuple-var-moved-into-struct case (`pair`→`Hi{m:pair}`) is balanced by the existing Part C1 move-out suppression (verified no double-free under macOS ASAN). Reddened CI memory-sanitizer (asan_struct_tuple_map_leaf_no_double_free).

A Copy binding destructured from a tuple whose sibling is a move type was misclassified as a move, so `karac check` rejected valid code with a spurious "value 'n' moved here, used again here" (n: i64). The use-classifier's let-RHS type recorder (use_classifier.rs) assigned the WHOLE tuple type to EVERY binding of a destructure; a tuple containing a `String` is non-Copy, so the Copy field `n` in `let (n, s) = f()` (`f -> (i64, String)`) inherited the non-Copy tuple type, its reads in consuming position were tagged `Consume`, and the UAM predicate fired. `karac run`/`build` produced correct output (neither gates on ownership) so only `check` surfaced it. FIXED: decompose a tuple destructure field-by-field (`assign_binding_types`) so each binding is keyed to its own field type; non-tuple shapes fall back to the prior whole-type behavior. Genuine UAM of a move-typed sibling is unaffected (regression-guarded both directions). Surfaced by the bespoke utf8-codepoints kata's recursive 5-tuple-returning style. Latent in selfhost/src/main.kara (no tuple destructure yet) but would go live in the parser port's `(node, pos)` recursive-descent returns.

Parser pre-port recursive-heap gate (struct-wrapped AST shape: shared enum Expr { Add(BinOp) } + struct BinOp { left: Expr, right: Expr }). FOUR bugs flushed by a focused kata burst before writing the parser. (1) OWNERSHIP false-positive: the plain-struct operand wrapper made `breakable` (which required ALL cycle participants shared) reject `Expr -> BinOp -> Expr` non-deterministically (HashMap DFS order decided dedup survival) though Expr being shared gives fixed-size RC indirection + Num gives a base escape -> finite leak-free trees; relaxed to base-escape AND any-shared (ownership.rs). (2) CODEGEN miscompile: chained access through an inline shared field `h.a.v` read const-0 in AOT (interp correct) -- shared_type_for_expr only matched Identifier/self objects, a FieldAccess object fell to the generic struct-value path where the extracted RC pointer missed the StructValue guard; resolve a FieldAccess object via type_name_of_expr+shared_types (types_lowering.rs). (3) CODEGEN leak: a shared-enum struct payload `Add(BinOp)` leaked its inline RC children at box drop -- the RC drop walker classified the struct payload non-walkable (value-path __karac_drop_struct has no shared-field arm); walk them via emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs + record shared NAMES early in shared_type_decl_names (synth_drop/declarations/param_own/codegen). (4) CODEGEN leak: Vec[shared enum] elements leaked -- vec_elem_agg_drop routed a shared enum to the VALUE drop (no rc-dec); synthesize __karac_vec_elem_rc_dec_<T> loading+dec'ing the slot pointer (runtime.rs). All build/drop/move/clone-via-RC-share/by-value-transform/recursive-match-destructure of the struct-wrapped tree now agree interp==AOT and leak-free under macOS leaks (Linux-CI LSan is the gate). Regressions: ownership.rs (3), codegen.rs (2: struct_wrapped_recursive_shared_enum_tree, chained_access_through_inline_shared_field), memory_sanitizer.rs (5 struct_wrapped_* + enum_payload_rc_children + vec_of_shared_enum). Recursive-heap family now QUIET for the struct-wrapper convention; parser port can proceed.

PRE-EXISTING (latent on main, NOT introduced by B-2026-06-14-28; reproduces on the DIRECT-payload shape `shared enum Expr { Num(i64), Add(Expr,Expr) }` too): a `match` on a BY-VALUE shared enum whose taken arm BINDS the payload but does NOT consume every shared child leaks the scrutinee box + its shared children. Minimal: `fn fold(e: Expr) -> Expr { match e { Num(n) => Num(n), Add(b) => Add(BinOp{left:Num(99),right:Num(99)}) } }`. RESOLUTION: DUPLICATE of B-2026-06-14-28 bug #3 (struct-payload box-drop walker not recursing into shared children) -- already closed by 0890627c. The original 'bind-without-consume' hypothesis and the control_flow_match.rs locus did NOT hold: a malloc/free-balance bisect across 0890627c (interposer at O0, runtime-derived non-foldable seed) showed the struct-wrapped `Add(BinOp)` shape leaked UNCONDITIONALLY pre-fix -- the bind-ignore variant and the fully-consuming variant leaked IDENTICALLY (delta driven by allocation volume, not by consumption), disproving the consumption-gated theory; the DIRECT-payload `Add(Expr,Expr)` shape never leaked at any commit. At HEAD (with 0890627c) all four shapes -- bind-ignore, fully-consume, reconstruct-from-fresh, direct payload -- are balanced (no per-iteration growth). The match path needed NO change; closed by regression tests (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_match_byvalue_* x4) that pin no-leak across both axes (struct-wrapped + direct) and both directions (leak==0, no double-free in consuming/reconstruct/Num arms) for the authoritative Linux-CI LSan gate. macOS asan green (no UAF/double-free), AOT==interp.

A zero-length fixed-array binding `let a: Array[T, 0] = []` passed to a `Slice[T]` parameter hard-failed AOT codegen (`karac build`) while `karac run` was correct. `compile_array_literal([])` cannot infer the element type from the empty element list, so it returns a scalar `i64 0` sentinel and the binding's slot type is registered as `i64`, not `[0 x T]`. At the call site, `coerce_to_slice`'s Array->Slice branch keys on `slot.ty` being an `ArrayType`, so it SKIPS the empty array; the raw `i64` then reaches the call where a `{ptr, i64}` slice header is expected, tripping LLVM module verification ("Call parameter type does not match function signature!  %a1 = load i64, ptr %a ... call ... (i64 %a1, i64 0)"). The interpreter accepts the same `[] -> Slice` coercion (it does not lower to a fat pointer), so only the codegen backend surfaced it. FIXED: a new `try_emit_empty_array_let` (codegen/collections.rs), wired into the let-binding path (codegen/stmts.rs) just after the zero-init repeat-let fast path and ahead of the scalar fallback, recovers the element type from the `Array[T, N]` annotation and allocates a real `[0 x T]` slot; the empty array then coerces to a zero-length slice header `{ptr, 0}` like any other array (len 0, so the pointer is never dereferenced). Non-empty array bindings and the no-annotation empty literal are unaffected (the helper returns None and falls through). Regression-guarded: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_empty_array_binding_to_slice_param (empty `[]` and a non-empty `[1,2,3]` to the same `Slice[i64]` param, asserts `0\n6`). Surfaced by the #33 search-in-rotated-sorted-array kata's empty-array edge case (`let a7: Array[i64, 0] = []` passed to the `Slice[i64]` search/fmt params).

B-2026-06-14-28 residual: the shared-enum box-drop walker leaked a Vec[shared] struct-field and a move-out edge; macOS false-green hid it (the Linux-CI memory-sanitizer/LeakSanitizer job was RED on main, two tests/memory_sanitizer.rs cases). Two distinct gaps in the B-28 shared-enum drop area. (1) Vec[shared] FIELD of a struct payload (Call(CallExpr { args: Vec[Expr] }), 560 B / 11 objs): emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) had no Vec arm, so when the shared-enum box freed, the inline Vec's {data,len,cap} buffer (the 80-byte direct alloc) AND every element box (10x48) leaked. FIXED: a Vec arm that drains the buffer (loop 0..len calling the element per-slot drop vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr -> __karac_vec_elem_rc_dec_<T> for Vec[shared], which rc-decs each box and recurses) then free(data) when cap>0; plus a Vec[shared] arm in field_owns_shared (src/codegen/param_own.rs) so a struct whose ONLY shared content is a Vec[shared] field is classified walkable. The Vec arm surfaced the no-double-free crux: a struct payload bound in a match arm (Wrapped(w)) is a by-value VIEW of the box's inline payload, and its __karac_drop_struct_<S> value-drop frees the same buffer the still-live RC box owns -> the box-drop walker then double-frees it (SEGV under ASAN). FIXED by a new pattern_binding_scrutinee_is_shared_enum flag (set/restored in compile_match, gates the user-struct track_struct_var in src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs exactly like the existing Option/Result flag) so the rc-drop walker is the sole owner of buffer + elements. (2) move-out edge (let t2 = t1 then eval(t2), 96 B / 3 objs): the shared-enum let path called the value-enum move suppressor on an Identifier RHS, which emitted a spurious aliasing-acquire emit_refcount_inc on the source on top of the destination inc, pinning the box at rc=1 after both scope-exit RcDecs. FIXED by skipping that suppressor for a shared-enum destination (src/codegen/stmts.rs) — its RC accounting is already complete via the destination inc + dual RcDecs (shared STRUCT moves are excluded the same way via the existing named_aggregate gate). Regression-guarded by tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_struct_wrapped_vec_field_in_struct_payload_no_leak and asan_struct_wrapped_move_out_then_consume_no_leak; verified under Linux ASAN+LSan via the new scripts/lsan-local.sh colima harness (full memory_sanitizer suite 257 passed on both arm64 and x86_64).

tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_inline_index_fn_returned_vec_string_no_leak leaks 6 bytes (1 obj from karac_string_clone) under Linux LSan on BOTH arm64 and x86_64. Inline-indexing a fn-returned Vec[String] temporary (`println(names()[0])` where `names() -> Vec[String]`) does not fully free the returned Vec temp's String element buffers/clone. Pre-existing on the base (reproduced with the B-2026-06-14-31 fix stashed; unrelated to the shared-enum drop class). Surfaced while running the full memory_sanitizer suite under the new local Linux LSan harness (scripts/lsan-local.sh). Not fixed in the B-31 slice (out of scope: String/Vec-temp cleanup, not shared-enum drop). FIXED b08d984f: compile_inline_temp_vec_index deep-clones the indexed non-Copy element into a fresh owned buffer (the temp Vec is dropped right after the read) then de-registers its synth Vec local, so the clone has NO consuming binding and NO scope-exit cleanup of its own. The consumer-side free paths only recognized Call/MethodCall fresh temps (expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp), so an Index-over-call-temp was invisible and the clone leaked once per consume (LSan's '1 obj' is an O2 dedup/DCE artifact of the two identical println sites; both leaked). Added expr_is_inline_temp_vec_heap_index (Index, non-range index, inline_temp_vec_te(object) Some, non-trivially-Copy element) and wired it into BOTH consumer gates: free_fresh_owned_str_arg (println/print String arg) and call_dispatch's is_fresh_heap_call_arg (by-value user-fn arg -> materialize_owned_temp). The cap>0 free guard + the de-registered synth local rule out any double-free. Regression cover: the reported asan_inline_index_fn_returned_vec_string_no_leak (println) now green + new asan_inline_index_fn_returned_vec_string_fn_arg_no_leak (looped sink(names()[i]) by-value-arg sibling). Gates (local Linux LSan harness): memory_sanitizer 261 passed/0 failed; macOS codegen E2E 1604 passed/0 failed; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm + fmt clean.

tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_vec_extend_from_slice_self_alias_rejects FAILS under Linux LSan on BOTH arm64 and x86_64: the test expects emit_panic exit 1 (the source-slice-aliases-destination guard), but a panic-PATH leak (the partially-built Vec is not freed before the abort) makes LeakSanitizer report a leak and flip the process exit code 1 -> 23, so assert_asan_panics_with's `status.code() == Some(1)` check fails. Either the panic path should free the in-flight Vec, or the harness's assert_asan_panics_with should run that case with detect_leaks=0 (panic-path leaks are abort-time, not steady-state). Pre-existing on the base (reproduced with the B-2026-06-14-31 fix stashed; unrelated to the shared-enum drop class). Surfaced via the new local Linux LSan harness. Not fixed in the B-31 slice (out of scope). FIXED b08d984f: took the harness option -- a panic that emit_panics mid-operation aborts the process, so the in-flight allocation (here the grown-but-not-yet-reclaimed Vec the extend_from_slice overlap guard abandons) is an abort-time artifact reclaimed by the OS at process death, NOT a steady-state leak; flagging it just flips the exit code and masks the panic the assertion exists to verify. Added run_under_asan_no_leak_check (ASAN_OPTIONS detect_leaks=0) factored out of a shared run_under_asan_opts(detect_leaks) helper; assert_asan_panics_with now uses it. Steady-state leak coverage is unchanged -- every assert_clean_asan_run still runs detect_leaks=1, and on macOS (no LSan) the flag is moot. Gate (local Linux LSan harness): asan_vec_extend_from_slice_self_alias_rejects green; full memory_sanitizer 261 passed/0 failed.

B-2026-06-14-31 shared-enum drop fix regressed self-host lexer compilation: emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs (the shared-enum-payload RC-dec walker, where B-31 added a Vec[shared] drain arm) appended its nstr.* basic blocks to self.current_fn. Correct from the RC-drop synthesizer (emit_shared_enum_rc_drop_fn sets current_fn to the fn it emits into) but WRONG from the VALUE-drop synthesizer emit_enum_drop_switch, which never sets current_fn -> it stays the OUTER fn (use_stmt/main) -> walker blocks landed in the outer fn while the switch's `br exit` referenced the synthesized __karac_drop_<E> fn -> cross-function basic-block reference -> module verification failed (`Referring to a basic block in another function!`). The self-host lexer's drop-fn memoization order triggered it; --test codegen dodged it because compile_to_object skips verification and the optimizer DCE'd the orphan blocks. SECOND latent defect surfaced by the fix: the B-31 Vec arm unconditionally free'd the Vec buffer that __karac_drop_struct_<S> ALSO frees in the value-drop path (double-free), and the drain read the buffer after the struct drop freed it (UAF/SEGV) -- both masked while the blocks were DCE'd. Fix (8ffe58c4): thread the real drop_fn through the walker explicitly + scope self.current_fn to it for the walker body only (leaf sub-emitters target the drop fn); gate the buffer free on a new owns_buffer_free flag (false value-path / true rc-path) so the struct drop owns the single free while the per-element rc-dec drain always runs; reorder the value-path NestedStruct arm so the walker drains BEFORE __karac_drop_struct_<S> frees the buffer. Two prior dead ends (save/restore builder block; key off builder parent fn) ruled out -- both double-freed because the whole-body current_fn perturbed alloca/cleanup placement. Regression cover: codegen test b34_value_enum_drop_with_nested_struct_vec_shared_field_compiles (cross-fn compile shape without the lexer) + ASAN test asan_value_enum_nested_struct_vec_shared_inplace_drop_no_leak_no_double_free. Gates (Linux LSan/ASAN harness): selfhost_lexer green; memory_sanitizer 258 passed/2 failed (the 2 are open B-32/B-33, reproduced on the B-31 base); codegen 1604 passed/0 failed.

REGRESSION (surfaced by the full-corpus quiet-pass sweep; introduced by 0890627c / B-2026-06-14-28's CORRECT Vec[shared]-element dec, which UNMASKED a latent under-inc): a bare `shared` struct read out of a Vec element into an enum-ctor payload -- `Some(nodes[i])` -- shallow-aliases without an rc-inc. rhs_yields_fresh_ref classifies the ctor as fresh, so the return / let-bind / field consumers skip their receive-inc and rely on the ctor self-inc'ing (the alias discipline `let a = nodes[i]` follows); the bare-Vec-element ctor payload was the one place that never inc'd. The escaping ref (`return Some(nodes[0])` / `cur.left = Some(nodes[i])`) was under-counted and freed when the source Vec dropped -> use-after-free: #226 invert-binary-tree emitted non-deterministic garbage / crashed (recursive 0/888988/3.., iterative 4/886942.. vs expected 2666665501; rust/c/go all correct). Masked pre-0890627c by the Vec[shared]-element leak. FIXED 0f78fc4f: share_bare_shared_ctor_payload (call_dispatch.rs) rc-incs a non-fresh `v[i]` Vec-element ctor payload, SCOPED to the index shape by bare_shared_heap_type_for_expr -- a fresh-local Identifier payload (Some(node)/Some(head) moved into a list) is already owned and a broad first cut double-counted those (caught by asan_option_shared_chain_through_helper_fn + codegen test_ir_fresh_return_someroot_builder_is_count_free). Verified: full #226 kata deterministic; memory_sanitizer 261 + codegen 1604 green; new regression test asan_vec_shared_elem_into_some_returned_no_leak_no_uaf.

REGRESSION (surfaced by the full-corpus quiet-pass sweep; introduced by 1a401c7b "auto-par ordered output"): routing EVERY console write through the runtime karac_runtime_write_console chokepoint linked the OutputCapture machinery (segs realloc + replay + Drop + OUTPUT_REDIRECT thread-local) into every output-bearing binary -- INCLUDING KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 seq-twins and no-par compute binaries that can never install a capture -- regressing the lean binary-size floor ~17 KiB on every compute kata (sweep: brute_force seq 33->50 KiB corpus-wide; aabc01c8's panic-free write_console recovered the bulk of the +268 KiB but left this +17 KiB residual). FIXED ebce9d99: console writes route through an internal __karac_write_console wrapper whose body is defined at module finalization (finalize_write_console_wrapper) -- it calls the capture-capable runtime chokepoint ONLY when the module emits a karac_par_run / karac_par_reduce site (karac_par_run is the sole OutputCapture installer), else a lean direct fwrite(data,1,len,stream). A non-parallel binary then references neither the chokepoint nor the capture machinery, so AOT -dead_strip removes them. Verified: print(42) seq-twin 50432->33632 B (write_console stripped), brute_force seq-twin at the 33 KiB floor; auto-par ordered-output e2e still green (par programs keep the capture path); codegen 1604 + memory_sanitizer 262 + par_codegen 120 + parallax 6 green; new IR test test_ir_nonpar_output_uses_lean_fwrite_not_capture_chokepoint.

REGRESSION (surfaced by the fixed-compiler re-bench's compile-elapsed lane): the auto-par reduction env captured a fixed-size [N x T] array BY VALUE -- the worker alloca'd [N x T], loaded the whole env struct, and store'd the N*8-byte array (a by-value copy); the caller insertvalue'd the array into the env. LLVM's O2 scalarized that ~40 KB load/store into N element ops, then DAGCombiner store-merging (getStoreMergeCandidates/mergeConsecutiveStores) + BasicAA alias analysis went super-linear -- the ENTIRE compile delta was LLVM (zero karac symbols in the `sample` profile). Auto-par compile of brute_force (kata #1) was 0.07 -> 2.15 s (30x); kata #3629 bfs_sieve hit 60 s / 639 MiB. -O0 was ~0.08 s, isolating the O2 pipeline as the victim of the IR shape (not karac analysis). FIXED c3050fc8: a captured ArrayType travels the env BY POINTER (the parent array's address) -- read-only reduction input, parent frame outlives the par_reduce join, matching how a Vec {ptr,len,cap} header already flows. Applied to both reduce env variants (scalar- and Vec-accumulator), four sites each (worker env type+extract, caller env type+fill). Result: brute_force 2.15 -> 0.08 s; bfs_sieve 60 -> 0.08 s; par==seq on #1/#5/#28/#133; par_codegen 121 + codegen 1605 + memory_sanitizer 262 green; new regression test test_reduce_worker_captures_array_by_pointer_not_inline.

Cross-module enum payload-variant patterns/constructors failed name resolution. collect_enum registers every variant as an EnumVariant symbol in the defining module's scope, but collect_import bound only the enum's TYPE name on import -- not its variants. So an UNqualified tuple/struct-variant pattern or constructor over an IMPORTED enum (`match t { Identifier(name) => .. }`, `B(3)`) hit the resolver's eager head lookup and errored E0100 'undefined name', while the identical code against a LOCAL enum resolved (variant in scope) and a QUALIFIED `Enum.Variant` worked (head is the imported type). Nullary arms escaped because they parse as Binding (resolver defers; typechecker reinterprets against the scrutinee via path.last()). Surfaced by splitting the self-hosted lexer single->multi-file (render's `Identifier(name)`/`Text(t)` arms over imported Token/InterpPart). FIXED 2b2c9acf: collect_import now mirrors collect_enum for imported enums -- register_imported_enum_variants enumerates the imported enum's variants from the program tree and binds each in the importer's scope, so imported enums resolve identically to local ones; genuine typos still error on the lookup miss. Verified: tests/module_graph grammar matrix (variant kind x qualified/unqualified x cross-module + unknown-variant guard) + selfhost split oracle green.

An imported user type did not shadow a same-named baked-prelude type, though a LOCAL definition did. collect_import_origins pulls imported struct/enum/trait defs into the per-module env but skipped binding when env.{structs,enums,traits} already contained the name -- an explicit 'stdlib bakeds win over imports' policy. So `import x.Span; Span { .. }` resolved to the prelude's tracing `Span` {name,span_id,parent_id,fields} -> E0201/E0203 field errors, while a local `struct Span {..}` shadowed fine. defining_stdlib_origin couldn't distinguish prelude from local here because the always-on STDLIB_PROGRAMS bake never sets it (only the gated path does). Surfaced by the self-hosting lexer split (`import span.Span`). FIXED 2b2c9acf: an import now yields only to a genuine CURRENT-MODULE definition (tested against the module's own items) and shadows a prelude entry, exactly as a local def does. Known follow-on facet (not yet fixed): an imported struct's field TYPES are re-lowered in the importer's namespace, so a transitively-used type must also be imported by the consumer (selfhost main.kara imports span.Span so SpannedToken.span: Span lowers correctly). Verified: tests/module_graph prelude-collision + local-shadow baselines + selfhost split oracle green.

Qualified struct construction `module.Type { .. }` failed to parse ('Expected Semicolon, found LeftBrace'), even though `module.Type` type annotations and `module.fn()` calls already parsed+resolved -- a consistency gap surfaced when the self-hosting lexer split tried `span.Span {}` before B-2026-06-15-5's import-shadow fix landed. parse_identifier_expr only rooted a path when the FIRST segment was uppercase; a lowercase module segment fell to the postfix loop, which read `.Type` as field access then choked on `{`. FIXED 5867dfe6 (parser): root a path for lowercase-module + uppercase-type WHEN immediately followed by `{` -- the brace guard is load-bearing, since without it primitive associated-constant access (`i64.MAX`/`f64.NAN`: lowercase primitive + uppercase const, no brace) misparses as a path (regression caught by the full suite). (typecheck): resolve_qualified_struct_info resolves the qualified struct path via the defining module so `module.Type {}` type-checks (the type isn't bound by bare name when only the module is imported). Verified: tests/module_graph xmod_qualified_struct_construction_parses_and_checks + full suite green.

An imported struct's field TYPES were re-lowered in the IMPORTER's namespace, so a field whose type the consumer did not import by name resolved to a same-named prelude type. E.g. consumer imports `SpannedTok` (whose `span: Span` is defined via another module that imports geo.Span) and reads `t.span.offset` -> `Span` resolved to the prelude tracing `Span` {name,span_id,parent_id,fields} -> E0201 'no field offset'. The documented follow-on facet of B-2026-06-15-5. FIXED 5867dfe6: collect_import_origins now transitively registers the struct/enum types an imported aggregate's fields/variant-payloads reference, resolved from the DEFINING module via resolve_type_origin_in_module (which follows that module's own imports of ANY visibility -- module::canonical_origin only walks `pub` re-export chains, so a private `import geo.Span` in the defining module was invisible to it). Worklist + visited set bound the walk over recursive/shared types; a name the current module defines itself is left untouched (local wins). Proven end-to-end: selfhost main.kara dropped its `import span.Span` workaround and the differential oracle stays green. Verified: tests/module_graph xmod_imported_struct_field_type_resolves_transitively + full suite green.

Canonical binary search `while lo < hi { let mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2; nums[mid] }` kept its `nums[mid]` bounds check under -O2, leaving kata #34 1.58x behind equal-safety Rust (298 vs 189ms; overflow tax zero here, so pure codegen; get_unchecked=205ms pins the check as ~85% of the gap). Folding `mid < len` needs the RELATIONAL invariant `mid < hi`, which interval-based CVP/LVI can't derive, and `mid = extractvalue(sadd.with.overflow)` is opaque to its range pass. FIXED 9b36be5d: codegen emits `assume(mid >= lo)` + `assume(mid < hi)` at the midpoint binding under a strict `lo < hi` guard (control_flow_bce.rs binsearch_guard_pair/expr_is_midpoint/try_emit_binsearch_midpoint_assumes; binsearch_guard_stack in compile_while). Both facts locally sound from the midpoint form + dominating guard (floor (hi-lo)/2 in [0,d-1] => lo<=mid<hi); AOT overflow trapping makes no-wrap hold on defined execs. One extra default<O1> pass gated on emission completes the fold (single -Ox doesn't, callee-optimize-then-inline ordering); prelude has no midpoint search so non-binsearch programs pay nothing. #34 search 298->~215ms. Tests: tests/codegen.rs test_ir_binsearch_midpoint_emits_assumes + test_ir_non_midpoint_binding_no_binsearch_assume + test_e2e_binsearch_midpoint_assume_is_sound; full --features llvm suite green.

Indexing a `ref Array[T,N]` parameter fails codegen with 'Index operator applied to non-array type'. A `ref`/`mut ref Array` param's slot LLVM type is `ptr` (the borrow), not `[N x T]`, and the param is in no name-keyed index registry, so compile_index (src/codegen/collections.rs) falls past the tensor/slice/map/soa/vec routes to the generic tail whose ArrayType/VectorType branches can't match a ptr -> error. The `ref Vec[T]` form is handled by an explicit vec_elem_types name-keyed route (collections.rs ~972) precisely because its ref slot is also ptr; `ref Array` has no analogous route. Local fixed arrays (slot.ty=ArrayType) and `Slice[T]` params both index fine, so the idiomatic borrowed-view form is Slice (the kata's bench uses it; not a workaround -- same form as #34/#35 nums). Repro: `fn g(xs: ref Array[i64,4]) -> i64 { xs[0] }` builds-fails, runs fine (run_program bypasses codegen). Suggested fix: mirror the ref-Vec route -- detect a ref/mut-ref Array param via its recorded TypeExpr, load the pointer from the slot and GEP through `[N x T]`, for BOTH index-read and index-store (assignment) paths.

Unbounded ~100 B/connection memory leak in the spawn/structured-concurrency model under a long-lived accept loop (the CANONICAL server shape: loop { tg.spawn(|| handle(conn)) }, exactly examples/ws_idle_holder/src/main.kara). Surfaced by a 1M-connection Windows IOCP soak (docs/spikes/windows-iocp-scale-investigation.md): a TaskGroup.spawn echo server's RSS climbs ~5 MB/50k conns, perfectly linear, while OS handle count stays flat — it's heap (the per-task KaracTaskHandle, + a KaracParkSlot on the coro-spawn path the blocking-handler accept loop lowers through), not an OS handle. The serial inline server (no spawn) is flat over the full 1M; both TaskGroup.spawn AND free spawn leak identically. Root cause (runtime/src/scheduler.rs): karac_runtime_taskgroup_register pushes each child handle onto KaracTaskGroupHandle.children (Mutex<Vec<*mut KaracTaskHandle>>), freed ONLY by karac_runtime_taskgroup_join_and_free at the group's scope exit — which never happens in a server's infinite accept loop, so the Vec + every child grow without bound; free spawn passes group_ptr=None so the discarded handle is orphaned (never registered, never freed — src/codegen/task_group.rs). Platform-agnostic (the scheduler code is not cfg-gated; affects Linux/macOS too — Windows just surfaced it). NOT trivially fixed: eager-freeing a completed registered child is a use-after-free if the user retained+joins its handle (join on a registered child waits+copies but does not free — group is sole freer, B-2026-06-09-1), so a safe reap must be detached-gated (codegen marks discarded spawn handles detached). Fix design in the tracker: detached:AtomicBool on KaracTaskHandle + karac_runtime_task_detach FFI; codegen marks discarded spawn/tg.spawn handles detached; taskgroup_register sweeps+frees terminal detached children (terminal = state!=PENDING non-coro, or park slot done==true coro — the slot exposes done:Mutex<bool>), bounding the Vec to live children; free spawn detached self-frees on completion. Open: the fix is memory-ownership surgery on the async hot path and must clear the Linux ASAN+LSan gate (scripts/lsan-local.sh) — the authoritative leak gate per B-2026-06-14-31/-32 — which the Windows validation box cannot run. FIXED 2026-06-17 (849030b6): detached-gated eager-reap landed. KaracTaskHandle gained detached/reaped flags + karac_runtime_task_detach FFI; codegen marks a discarded spawn/tg.spawn handle detached (pending_spawn_detach in compile_stmt -> lower_spawn_shared); karac_runtime_taskgroup_register sweeps+frees detached terminal children (UAF-safe terminal peek: notify_mutex for non-coro, new karac_runtime_park_slot_done for coro), bounding children to ~live; free-spawn non-coro worker self-reaps on completion with the detach path claiming the free exactly once under notify_mutex. Retained .join()able handles are never detached, preserving the structured wait + B-2026-06-09-1 sole-freer invariants. Verified green on the full Linux ASAN+LSan suite (263/263, scripts/lsan-local.sh): runtime unit regression taskgroup_register_reaps_detached_completed_children (asserts the children Vec stays bounded — fails pre-fix) + detached_free_spawn_self_reaps_after_completion (both reap orderings) + memory_sanitizer E2E asan_discarded_taskgroup_spawn_loop_eager_reap_no_double_free (guards the sweep<->join_and_free double-free/UAF hazard). RESIDUAL: free-spawn + coro (ws_echo_freespawn) self-reap is deferred to B-2026-06-17-3 (needs dispatcher signal-path surgery); the canonical server shape here (tg.spawn, examples/ws_idle_holder) is fully closed.

Residual of B-2026-06-17-2: a discarded FREE `spawn(|| handle(conn))` whose closure tail is a coroutine-compiled blocking handler (the ws_echo_freespawn shape) still leaks its KaracTaskHandle + KaracParkSlot. The B-2026-06-17-2 fix closes the canonical `tg.spawn` server shape (group register-time sweep reaps detached terminal children, incl. coro children via park_slot_done) and free-spawn NON-coro (the pool worker self-reaps after storing terminal state). But a free-spawn CORO handle has no group to sweep it, and the ramp-worker returns the instant the coroutine suspends — completion is the dispatcher signalling the bound slot later, so the worker can't self-reap. karac_runtime_task_detach therefore only flags a free-spawn coro handle; nothing frees it. Fix needs dispatcher-side surgery: a signal-or-reap path (bind the detached free-spawn handle to its slot; on the coroutine's completion slot-signal, if the bound handle is detached+unregistered, free handle+slot instead of just signalling — UAF-safe because a detached free-spawn handle has no joiner). Touches runtime/src/event_loop.rs (KaracParkSlot signal path) + src/codegen/coro.rs (route coro finish through the reap-aware signal). Must clear scripts/lsan-local.sh. Lower severity than the parent: tg.spawn is the documented/canonical server shape; free-spawn is the secondary variant. FIXED 2026-06-17 (69a03439): slot-armed self-reap. KaracParkSlot gained a type-erased `reap` pointer; karac_runtime_task_detach for a free-spawn coro handle calls karac_runtime_park_slot_arm_reap (under the slot `done` lock: either the coroutine already completed -> detach frees now, or store the handle for completion to free), and karac_runtime_park_slot_signal reads the armed handle under that lock and frees handle+slot via karac_runtime_reap_detached_coro_handle after releasing it. The `done` lock makes the arm/complete handshake claim the free exactly once; sound because signal is the slot's last use on every completion path (the codegen CoroContext.slot/cancel-edge invariant). For every non-armed slot (reap==null) signal is byte-for-byte unchanged. Tests: runtime unit detached_free_spawn_coro_reaps_when_{already_complete_at_detach,completion_follows_detach} (both orderings) + coro_e2e coroutine_discarded_free_spawn_self_reaps_under_asan over a real loopback connection with a 2s barrier so Linux LSan is a deterministic regression. Green: full coro_e2e (28), that E2E under Linux LSan, and the memory_sanitizer suite 263/263 (shared signal path unregressed). NOTE: writing the E2E surfaced a separate, orthogonal anomaly — moving a channel Sender into a free-spawn coroutine closes the channel before the send lands (recv got 0); sidestepped here with a sleep barrier; SUBSEQUENTLY FIXED the same day by 691117f6 (the coroutine owns its moved channel-end params, closing the channel only after the send), logged retroactively as B-2026-06-17-9.

Passing a `mut ref T` binding to a `ref T` parameter is a `karac run` warning (the interpreter accepts it) but a `karac build` HARD ERROR: typecheck 'expected ref T, found mut ref T' at the call argument, so a program that RUNS fails to BUILD -- the run/build severity divergence is the defect. The safe widening (reborrow a mut-ref arg down to a ref param) should be accepted on both paths. Repro: `fn peek(xs: ref Vec[i64]) -> i64 { xs[0] }  fn poke(xs: mut ref Vec[i64]) -> i64 { xs[0]=9i64; peek(xs) }` -- run prints 9 with a warning, build errors at the `peek(xs)` call. Surfaced by kata:37's plain-backtracking solver, whose read-only is_safe(board) is called from the mutating search go(board: mut ref Vec[Vec[i64]]). Sidestepped (not worked around) by declaring is_safe's board param `mut ref`: it only reads; the mut is a borrow-mode formality forced by the missing downgrade. Suggested fix: in the typechecker call-argument mode check, accept mut-ref where ref is expected (implicit reborrow) identically under interp and codegen so run==build.

`mut ref T` params already carry LLVM `noalias` (emit_param_alias_attrs), but that rested on an exclusive-borrow guarantee NOT enforced at call sites until B-2026-06-17-6 -- so the pre-existing noalias was latently UNSOUND (f(mut v,mut v) compiled). B-2026-06-17-6 (1e0fe5ea) closes that, making mut-ref noalias sound; kata:37's solver written in its natural `mut ref Array[i64,N]` form (enabled by B-2026-06-17-1) now gets it. CORRECTION: the earlier claim that noalias is the source of the ~1.37x gap to Rust was WRONG. Direct measurement (switch kata:37 from mut Slice to the noalias-carrying mut ref Array): 265.7ms -> 249ms, only ~1.5%. Kara now EDGES OUT plain C (249 vs 255ms; Kara has noalias, the C bench's plain int64_t* does not) and trails Rust by 1.34x. Since Kara and Rust BOTH now carry noalias, that residual is bounds-check elision (B-2026-06-17-7), not aliasing. RESIDUAL (low priority, ~1.5%): `mut Slice[T]` params still don't get noalias -- a mut Slice is a by-value {ptr,len} aggregate param, so the data pointer is a struct field, not the param; marking it needs !alias.scope/!noalias metadata on the slice loads/stores, a separate slice-disjointness lowering. mut ref is the path that matters and is done.

Exclusive-borrow rule not enforced at call sites: `f(mut v, mut v)` (two simultaneous `mut ref` exclusive borrows of the SAME binding) and `f(mut v, v)` (exclusive + shared of the same binding) both compile, violating design.md's exclusive-borrow rule (a `mut ref T` is allowed only when no other borrow, shared or exclusive, of T is active -- the load-bearing soundness pin; split_at_mut is the sanctioned disjoint-partition path). The ownership checker accepts the aliasing under BOTH `karac run` and `karac build`. Worse, codegen then DIVERGES from the interpreter on the aliased case -- a latent miscompile: `fn f(x: mut ref Vec[i64], y: mut ref Vec[i64]) { x.push(1); y.push(2); println(x.len()); println(y.len()) }` with `f(mut v, mut v)` prints len(x)=2,len(y)=2 under `karac run` (true aliasing) but len(x)=1,len(y)=2 under `karac build` (codegen passes the Vec {ptr,len,cap} header by value per borrow, so x's len is stale). This is the BLOCKER for B-2026-06-17-5 (noalias): exclusive-borrow enforcement is the precondition that makes mut-param noalias sound (exactly the invariant Rust's &mut relies on). Surfaced by kata:37 while investigating the noalias gap. Fix: enforce the exclusive-borrow rule in the ownership checker -- reject a second active shared/exclusive borrow of a binding already exclusively borrowed at a call argument.

SOLVED (root cause identified + fixed): kata:37's solver ran ~1.34x behind Rust, and the earlier recharacterization left the cause 'unidentified'. IR/asm-level isolation of `go` pinned it: Rust FULLY UNROLLED the hot candidate loop `while d <= 9 { .. if go(..) ..; d = d + 1 }` while karac's default<O2> pipeline left it ROLLED -- and that single difference was the entire gap. Proven end-to-end: (1) C mirror + `#pragma clang loop unroll(full)` on the candidate loop lands EXACTLY on Rust (189.8ms) with 9 recursive bls; (2) rustc with -unroll-threshold=0 drops Rust back onto Kara/C at ~255ms (rolled). clang's cost model DECLINES this unroll (recursive call + early exit look expensive) even at -O3 -funroll-loops; rustc's LLVM config takes it. So Kara+C were at the correct 'rolled' floor; Rust uniquely unrolled. NB the prior bounds-check-elision hypothesis (B-2026-06-17-7 v1) and noalias hypothesis (B-2026-06-17-5) were both correctly refuted -- the cause was loop unrolling all along. FIX (1de4eb1e): emit `llvm.loop.unroll.full` metadata on the back-edge of small, constant-upper-bounded counted while loops (v < K / v <= K with a constant-step induction var, K <= 32), so LLVM's full unroller fires. Advisory-SAFE: unrolling is semantics-preserving and LLVM ignores the hint when it can't prove a small constant trip count. karac's first llvm.loop metadata emission (self-referential loop-id via temporary-node + RAUW through llvm-sys). RESULT: kata:37 Kara 254 -> 183ms, now 1.03-1.04x AHEAD of Rust (Kara carries noalias + no fixed-Array bounds checks; Rust's &mut [i64] slices still bounds-check). Cross-kata win: kata:36's 9-cell validator loops 192 -> 66ms (2.88x). Binary-search/permutation katas (variable-bounded loops) unchanged -- the gate is narrow by design. Tests: E2E rolled-vs-unrolled byte-identical output + IR gate (eligible loop carries the hint, large-bound loop does not). codegen 1611 / ownership 318 / typechecker 1692 green.

WASM `spawn`/`TaskGroup` builds fail at link: `undefined symbol: karac_runtime_task_detach`. B-2026-06-17-2's detached-eager-reap fix made codegen emit a `karac_runtime_task_detach` call for every discarded `spawn`/`tg.spawn` handle (pending_spawn_detach -> lower_spawn_shared), but the symbol is defined ONLY in the native scheduler (runtime/src/scheduler.rs, gated `net`). The two wasm schedulers -- seq_scheduler.rs (sequential, libkarac_runtime_wasm.a) and wasm_threads_scheduler.rs (wasm-threads, libkarac_runtime_wasm_threads.a) -- never got the extern, so any wasm program using tg.spawn or a discarded free spawn fails at wasm-ld: `undefined symbol: karac_runtime_task_detach` (repeated per call site). Repro: `karac build --target=wasm_wasi <prog>` (or --target=wasm_browser) on a program with `tg.spawn(|| ...)`. Reproduced by tests/cli.rs::wasm_wasi_spawn_taskgroup_sequential_e2e and wasm_threads_spawn_join_runs_on_worker_pool_e2e (currently failing on main for this reason; the native codegen E2E suite is unaffected since the native archive defines the symbol). Found while running the --features llvm suite during kata:37 work -- orthogonal to that change (a missing-symbol link error cannot come from a frontend change). Fix: add a `karac_runtime_task_detach` extern to seq_scheduler.rs and wasm_threads_scheduler.rs (the sequential one can be a no-op or a slot-flag set, matching the wasm reap model), OR gate codegen's task_detach emission so it is skipped on targets whose scheduler does not export it. The per-archive 'exactly one scheduler exports karac_runtime_*' invariant (CLAUDE.md) means the symbol must be added to each wasm scheduler, not linked from the native one. FIXED 2026-06-17 (ab920b23): karac_runtime_task_detach defined in both wasm schedulers -- seq frees an unregistered discarded spawn (registered = group's, sole-freer); wasm-threads ports the native non-coro reap (detached/reaped flags + worker self-reap + detach handshake under notify_mutex). Native-runnable unit tests (free-spawn reap both orderings + registered no-op) per scheduler; the two cli.rs wasm spawn tests pass. RESIDUAL: wasm-threads register-time sweep (B-2026-06-17-2 infinite-group leak-bounding) not ported -- finite groups leak-free; wasm LSan gate is CI.

A Sender/Receiver moved into a non-blocking (coroutine) free `spawn` (or `tg.spawn`) was dropped by the spawn WRAPPER at ramp-return time instead of by the coroutine, so the channel CLOSED before the resumed coroutine ran its `send` -- `rx.recv()` then observed the closed-channel sentinel 0 instead of the sent value. Miscompile (wrong program output), not a leak. Surfaced while writing B-2026-06-17-3's free-spawn-coro reap E2E (the channel-barrier shape), where it was sidestepped with a sleep barrier and flagged 'worth its own investigation'; this entry IS that investigation, retroactively logged. It turned out to have been FIXED the SAME DAY by 691117f6, which post-dates the B-2026-06-17-3 ledger note (69a03439) AND the docs/spikes/windows-iocp-scale-investigation.md follow-up text -- both of which still described it as open until this cleanup. Root cause: the spawn wrapper only ramps the coroutine (which parks on accept) and returns; draining its channel-end cleanup frame (drain_top_frame_with_emit) closed the channel at ramp-return, before the coroutine's later resume ran tx.send. Fix (691117f6): a coroutine-compiled fn registers its owned Sender/Receiver params via track_channel_var so it closes the channel only at body-end completion (after the send) and on the per-park destroy/cancel edge (both via emit_cleanup_action_at, so no leak); call_dispatch.rs/method_call.rs additionally call suppress_channel_drop_for_var to remove the wrapper/caller's early DropChannelEnd at the move site (load-bearing for the spawn-wrapper path; also fixes a latent double-close on a synchronous inline-drive coro caller). Mirrors the owned-user-Drop coroutine-param ownership transfer (DropChannelEnd is a sibling of UserDrop). Covers free spawn and tg.spawn coro via the shared param-lowering path (closure captures lower to owned params). Regression: tests/coro_e2e.rs coroutine_free_spawn_channel_send_lands_before_close (CHANNEL_SPAWN_SRC: free spawn of a coro handler that sends 7 over a captured Sender; asserts recv sees 7, never 0; fails pre-fix, passes post-fix), an active #[test] under #![cfg(feature=llvm)] so it runs in the codegen-e2e CI job. Platform-agnostic (coroutine channel-end lowering is not cfg-gated).

`s.chars().collect()` into a `Vec[char]` failed codegen ('no handler for method collect on non-identifier receiver'), though it always RAN fine under the interpreter -- a run/build divergence. Codegen has no general iterator/`collect` lowering: the chars-iterator value is unsupported (`Vec/String method 'chars' is not yet supported in codegen`), and `collect` on a non-identifier receiver (here the `.chars()` call) falls through to the dispatch-fail error. So the idiomatic O(1)-indexed-char-access form `let v: Vec[char] = s.chars().collect()` -- documented in examples/leetcode/valid_palindrome.kara as THE pattern for a loop needing s[i]/s[j] -- built-failed. Surfaced by kata:38 (count-and-say): the indexed two-pointer run-grouping solver (count_and_say_indexed.kara) needs exactly this form. FIX (e272ed42): detect `<string>.chars().collect()` in compile_method_call and lower it to the already-supported `for c in <string>.chars() { v.push(c) }` build -- synthesize that block (reusing the `.chars()` call verbatim as the loop iterable) and compile it. The `Vec[char]` annotation makes the let-binding handler register the element type at codegen time (no typechecker dependency), so push dispatches; the block's move-out hands the caller the fresh Vec exactly as a `fn() -> Vec[char]` would. No new low-level Vec/iterator codegen -- it reuses the tested for-chars + push + block-return paths. Test e2e_chars_collect_to_vec_char_codegen (owned String + ref String receiver). Full codegen suite 1612 / ownership 318 / typechecker 1692 / interpreter 937 green.

`for c in <String>` mistyped the loop variable as `String` (owned) / `ref String` (borrowed) instead of `char` -- a typechecker/codegen MISMATCH, since codegen's `compile_for_string_chars` already binds the decoded codepoint as `char`. So `for c in s { c.is_alphabetic() }` warned under `karac run` but HARD-ERRORED under `karac build` ('no method <char-method> on type String') the moment `c` was used as a char. Root cause: `element_type_of` (typechecker for-loop element inference, lowering.rs) had no `Str` arm, so String fell through to `_ => ty.clone()`. Surfaced by kata:38 work via karac's own examples/leetcode/valid_palindrome.kara (documents `for c in s` as char iteration; did not build). FIX (a658f238): peel any ref/mut-ref and map underlying `Str` to `char` at the top of `element_type_of` (chars are Copy scalars returned by value, not `ref char`), so owned + borrowed String iteration both bind `char` and the existing char predicates (is_alphabetic/is_numeric/...) dispatch. Test e2e_for_char_in_string_binds_char_codegen. codegen 1614 / typechecker 1692 / ownership 318 / interpreter 937 green.

`s.char_at(i)` (O(n) Unicode-aware i-th char -> Option[char]) and `s.char_count()` (O(n) scalar count -> i64) on String are UNIMPLEMENTED end-to-end: the typechecker rejects them ('no method on type String' -- not in the stdlib_seq.rs String method table, though a comment there already references `s.char_at(i)`), and they do not function under the interpreter either (a program calling them produces no output under `karac run`). design.md documents char_at/char_count as the O(n) Unicode-aware access pair (vs `s.bytes()[i]` for O(1) byte access), and karac's own examples/leetcode/valid_palindrome.kara uses them as its central pattern -- so that example currently FAILS `karac build` and is non-functional under `run`. Needs: typecheck signatures + interpreter impl + codegen + (likely) a runtime decode-to-index helper. Surfaced by kata:38 String-API probing (the kata itself uses `.chars().collect()` + indexing instead, so it is unblocked). FIXED (96cd5015): wired across all four phases -- typecheck (stdlib_seq.rs: char_count -> i64, char_at(int) -> Option[char]), interpret (method_call_seq.rs: chars().count() / chars().nth(i)), codegen (vec_method.rs: char_at branches Some(char)/None via build_option_some_via_phis like String.find's Option[i64]; char_count calls the runtime counter; externs declared in codegen.rs), runtime (karac_runtime_string_char_count / _char_at, UTF-8 decoders over the String's (ptr,len) header). char_at is O(n) UNICODE-SCALAR indexing, not byte indexing: 'hello'-with-an-acute-e is 6 bytes / 5 scalars and char_at(1) is the acute-e; past-the-end and negative indices return None. valid_palindrome.kara now builds and runs. Tests test_e2e_string_char_at_and_count + test_string_char_at_and_count_interpreter (ASCII + Latin-1 + CJK). codegen 1630 / interpreter 939 / typechecker 1692 / ASAN 270 green; fmt + native + both wasm-arm clippy clean.

`char.is_uppercase()` / `char.is_lowercase()` have no codegen handler ('no handler for method is_uppercase on variable c'), while the sibling char-classification predicates is_alphabetic / is_numeric / is_alphanumeric / is_whitespace ARE wired (typecheck at expr_method_call.rs + codegen via karac_runtime_char_is_* helpers). So programs classifying upper/lower case build-fail where the other predicates build fine. Fix is bounded: add is_uppercase/is_lowercase to the typecheck predicate set + the codegen match in method_call.rs + two runtime helpers (karac_runtime_char_is_uppercase/lowercase), mirroring the existing four. Surfaced by kata:38 via valid_palindrome.kara (`c.is_uppercase()`); the for-loop char-typing fix B-2026-06-18-2 removed the upstream 'on type String' error, exposing this codegen gap underneath. FIXED (45ecfdcb): wired exactly as the existing four siblings -- typecheck char-predicate set (expr_method_call.rs), interpreter arms (interpreter/method_call.rs), codegen dispatch + extern decls (codegen/method_call.rs + codegen.rs), and runtime helpers karac_runtime_char_is_uppercase/lowercase (runtime/lib.rs). NOTE the bug was broader than 'no codegen handler': the typechecker did not type the call as Bool, so `karac run` ALSO panicked (Unit condition) -- fixed across typecheck + interpret + codegen + runtime. Unicode-aware (U+00C4 upper, U+00DF lower). Tests test_e2e_char_case_predicates + test_char_case_predicates_interpreter. codegen 1629 / interpreter 938 / typechecker 1692 / ASAN 269 green.

`s.chars()` as a STANDALONE value (bound to a variable, e.g. `let it = s.chars(); let v: Vec[char] = it.collect();`) is unsupported in codegen ('Vec/String method chars is not yet supported in codegen'). The direct idiom `s.chars().collect()` is now lowered (B-2026-06-18-1, e272ed42) by pattern-matching the chain, but materializing the chars-iterator as a first-class value requires a real iterator value representation in codegen, which does not exist. Low priority: the direct `.chars().collect()` idiom (the documented O(1)-indexed-access pattern) works, and `for c in s.chars()` works; binding the iterator to a name is rare. Surfaced alongside B-2026-06-18-1 in kata:38 probing. FIXED (0ccea67e): codegen's faithful representation of an eager char-iterator is a Vec[char] snapshot. compile_method_call materializes a STANDALONE <string>.chars() by reusing the .chars().collect() lowering (for c in s.chars() { push }); the let-binding handler registers `let it = s.chars()` as Vec[char]; and <it>.collect() (collect only typechecks on an iterator, so a Vec-typed receiver is always a materialized iterator) returns a clone of the snapshot. This reaches FULL PARITY with the direct s.chars() for the supported ops (collect, for c in it, len/index); iterator ADAPTOR chains (it.map(f).collect()) remain unsupported -- but identically for the direct s.chars().map(f).collect() chain, so that is a separate pre-existing iterator-adaptor codegen gap, not part of this bug. Tests e2e_chars_iterator_bound_to_variable_codegen + interpreter parity + asan_chars_bound_iterator_collect_clone_no_leak_no_double_free. codegen 1631 / interpreter 940 / typechecker 1692 / ASAN 273 green.

`String.push(char)` lowered to a `karac_string_encode_char` CALL + a variable-length `build_memcpy` -- which LLVM lowers to a libc `memmove` call even to copy ONE byte. On string-build workloads this dominated: profiling kata:38 (count-and-say) showed memmove/memcpy ~40% and encode ~20% of the hot time, the bulk of a 2.66x gap to C (which stores bytes in place). Found by the 'is the gap optimizable?' dig: an isolated chars()-iteration micro-bench runs at C speed (1.01x), refuting an earlier guess that the gap was the `for c in s.chars()` decode; `sample` of the binary then pinned the cost to push's encode+memmove. FIX (1bb11108): compute the UTF-8 length inline (compares+selects, no call) and add an ASCII fast-path -- a codepoint < 0x80 is its own single byte, stored directly with one i8 write, skipping both the encode call and the memcpy; the rare multibyte path still encodes+memcpy's 1-4 bytes; growth geometry unchanged. RESULT: kata:38 65 -> 46ms (1.40x), gap to C 2.66x -> 1.82x, byte-identical output; memmove 324 -> 36 samples, encode eliminated. Benefits ALL String-building codegen. Soundness test e2e_string_push_ascii_fastpath_and_multibyte (1/2/3/4-byte + ASCII interleaved vs interpreter). codegen 1615 / memory_sanitizer ASAN 263 / typechecker 1692 / ownership 318 / interpreter 937 green. Remaining kata:38 headroom (read-side chars() decode fast-path + per-step allocation reuse) identified, not pursued in this change.

`for c in s.chars()` called `karac_string_decode_char` PER CHARACTER to decode one UTF-8 scalar. After the push(char) ASCII fast-path landed (B-2026-06-18-6), this read-side decode became the top non-allocation cost on string-iteration workloads (kata:38 re-profile: decode ~207 samples). FIX (89760340): the symmetric read-side ASCII fast-path -- peek the byte at the current offset; if < 0x80 it is a complete 1-byte scalar, so set codepoint=byte and advance offset by 1 INLINE (no call); multibyte (>= 0x80) takes the slow runtime call, which decodes + returns the new offset as before; the two paths merge at a phi for the offset. The byte peek is in bounds (loop condition guarantees cur_off < len). RESULT: kata:38 (with push fast-path) 46 -> 39ms; combined with B-2026-06-18-6 the kata is 65 -> 39ms (gap to C 2.66x -> 1.58x, to Rust 2.04x -> 1.23x), byte-identical output; decode drops out of the profile entirely, leaving allocation churn as the sole residual (builder-buffer reuse the next, deeper move -- not pursued). Speeds all `for c in s.chars()` iteration. Soundness test e2e_for_chars_decode_ascii_fastpath_and_multibyte (codepoint sum over interleaved 1/2/3/4-byte + ASCII vs interpreter). codegen 1617 / memory_sanitizer ASAN 264 / typechecker 1692 / ownership 318 / interpreter 937 green.

A heap value (String/Vec[T]) moved into a tg.spawn/spawn closure INSIDE A LOOP was freed twice (use-after-free / double-free): the spawn-capture move-suppression in lower_spawn_shared (src/codegen/task_group.rs) removed the parent's user-Drop (suppress_user_drop_for_var) and channel-end (suppress_channel_drop_for_var) cleanups but NOT its FreeVecBuffer. The {data,len,cap} header is bitwise-copied into the spawned task's heap capture env (the task now owns the buffer and frees it at completion), yet the parent ALSO freed it at loop-body scope exit -- racing the task that still reads it. A single non-loop spawn masks it because the TaskGroup join at the enclosing scope precedes the parent free; a loop drains each iteration's frame first. Affects both the coroutine and non-coroutine spawn paths (shared lower_spawn_shared capture loop). NOT cfg-gated -> platform-agnostic. Surfaced by the Relay dogfood (examples/relay/relay.kara) slice 2 round-robin load balancer: each accepted connection clones the next upstream address (String) out of a Vec and moves it into a per-connection tg.spawn handler in the accept loop; pre-fix the handler's TcpStream.connect received a freed/garbage address and the proxy returned empty responses for every request. Note: the bug was initially MIS-diagnosed as an ASAN-invisible 'deeper race' because grep/line-counting detectors miscounted concurrent unsynchronized println() output interleaving as memory corruption; a corruption-only detector (task compares its captured String to known content, prints only on mismatch) showed 0/960 corruption post-fix on both paths, and a 30-way concurrent Relay load test returned 30/30 balanced 10/10/10. Fix (6972ffb3): suppress_vec_buffer_drop_for_var (src/codegen/runtime.rs), a sibling of the user-Drop/channel-end suppressors, removes the parent's FreeVecBuffer by ALLOCA match (type-agnostic -- a String binding's slot type is not always the canonical vec-struct type), called in lower_spawn_shared's capture loop. Same family as B-2026-06-17-9 (a moved-into-spawn value freed by the wrong owner) but for plain heap collections rather than channel ends, and exposed by loop scope rather than a single spawn. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_taskgroup_spawn_heap_capture_in_loop_{coro,noncoro}_no_uaf (6 handlers each capture a cloned heap String in a loop and compare the captured content -- ASAN-poisoned read if freed; silent on match, so a regression shows as ASAN UAF or a CORRUPT line). Green: ASAN suite 263, coro_e2e 30, fmt + clippy clean. Leak coverage (parent over-suppression -> orphaned buffer) is the Linux LSan CI gate's job; macOS ASAN verifies the double-free/UAF only.

`<expr>.clone()` on a `ref`/`mut ref` (BORROWED) collection receiver mis-built into a shallow ALIAS sharing the source buffer, while the interpreter always deep-copied -- a run/build divergence. `try_compile_clone` (src/codegen/entry_chains.rs) passed the receiver variable's raw alloca pointer (`src_slot.ptr`) to the per-type clone fn. For a borrowed param the alloca holds a pointer TO the value (`**Vec` / `**String`), so the clone copied the {data,len,cap} header out of the POINTER's own bits, yielding a Vec/String whose data ptr aliased the source buffer; a subsequent mutation of the source (the backtracking `path.pop()`) then drained the 'snapshot'. Built binaries emitted the right COUNT of snapshots but each was empty/garbage; `karac run` was always correct. Surfaced by kata:39 (combination-sum): all three backtracking solvers do `out.push(path.clone())` where `path` is a `mut ref Vec[i64]` -- so the headline solver was build-broken (right number of combinations, all `[]`).
