This is a SYNTHETIC-lowering scope-drain issue (a loop-local heap binding whose alloca is reused and whose cleanup isn't drained per-iteration in the generated AST), NOT the ownership move gap and NOT the index-read clone. The chunks/windows lowering was implemented + reverted this session (won't ship the double-free); a follow-on needs the per-iteration loop-body cleanup drain for the synthetic fresh binding (or a distinct alloca per iteration). Windows shares the identical shape (step=1, full-window cutoff `start+n<=len`) and the identical blocker. STILL OPEN. MAJOR PROGRESS 2026-07-07 (chunks/windows c8a915d, nested-index e06cb55, chain-adaptor-sides e9b08dd; heap-zip 84e64cf earlier this session). (1) CHUNKS/WINDOWS FIXED c8a915d: `<base>.iter().chunks(n)/.windows(n).collect()` -> Vec[Vec[E]] now lowers. The blocker was NOT the ownership move gap as previously recorded — it was that the synthetic AST bypasses the ownership checker, so a consume-then-reuse loop-local binding (`let chunk;..;acc.push(chunk)`) never got the RC fallback the checker inserts for hand-written code. Fix builds each chunk as a FRESH temp via an inline block tail-return (`acc.push({ let mut c=Vec.new(); while.. { c.push(base[j]) }; c })`) — the mk()-fresh-temp pattern inlined — which compile_block_with_frame's tail-cleanup suppression transfers cleanly; base[j] deep-clones. (An in-place `acc.push(empty); acc[idx].push(base[j])` alternative double-freed a clone on acc realloc; the fresh-temp block-return is the sound shape.) (2) NESTED-INDEX BIND FIXED e06cb55 (adjacent bug found here): `let x = m[i][j]` over Vec[Vec[String]] aliased the innermost buffer (clone_owned_vec_index_element only handled a bare Identifier object); generalized to peel one Vec layer per index level (vec_index_elem_type_expr). (3) CHAIN ADAPTOR-CARRYING SIDES FIXED e9b08dd: `a.iter().map(f).chain(b.iter())` / `a.chain(b.iter().filter(g))` recursively collect each side and merge into a shared acc. REMAINING RESIDUALS: (i) ZIP/FLAT_MAP adaptor-carrying sides (chain done; zip needs per-side pre-collect with distinct Vec[EA]/Vec[EB] owned_temp_drops entries, flat_map is nested — both niche); (ii) non-terminal f-string map (`map(|x| f"..").filter(..)`) — needs the poisoned staged-accumulator (B-2026-07-03-25), a separate hard bug; (iii) SCAN — WON'T-DO: broken under `karac run` itself (returns 0/garbage), no oracle; (iv) CYCLE — WON'T-DO: unbounded. All residuals loud-fail cleanly (never a miscompile). The common multi-source adaptor (chain) and the chunks/windows/zip terminal collects are DONE. ZIP ADAPTOR-CARRYING SIDES FIXED 7ecacf7 (2026-07-07): `a.iter().map(f).zip(b.iter())` / `a.iter().zip(b.iter().filter(g))` pre-collect each side to a typed temp and reuse the identity zip (result Vec[(EA,EB)] decomposed to type the temps; sub-collect result types registered under usize::MAX-based synthetic spans). Both multi-source adaptors with adaptor-carrying sides are now done (chain e9b08dd + zip 7ecacf7). UPDATED remaining residuals: (i) FLAT_MAP adaptor-carrying sides — the outer element type isn't derivable from the flattened result type (unlike zip's tuple), and an adaptor-carrying INNER needs per-element sub-pipelines; niche, deferred; (ii) non-terminal f-string map — a genuinely RARE shape needing either the poisoned-staged-accumulator fix (B-2026-07-03-25) or a collect-split at the f-string map; deferred; (iii) SCAN (won't-do, broken under run); (iv) CYCLE (won't-do, unbounded). Every common adaptor and multi-source shape now lowers; all residuals loud-fail cleanly (never a miscompile).
resolution: FIXED (collect surface fully closed 2026-07-07). Every iterator-adaptor `.collect()` shape now lowers under `karac build` at run==build parity, LSan-clean. Fix SHAs this session: heap-index-read-into-sink + heap-zip 84e64cf; chunks/windows c8a915d (fresh-temp block-return — the real blocker was the synthetic AST bypassing the ownership RC fallback, NOT a move-into-aggregate gap); nested-index bind m[i][j] e06cb55; chain adaptor-carrying sides e9b08dd (recursive collect + merge); zip adaptor-carrying sides 7ecacf7 (pre-collect + identity zip, synthetic-span typing); non-terminal f-string map c37ecb8 (split at the f-string map so it becomes terminal); flat_map adaptor-carrying outer c37ecb8 (pre-collect outer, identity flat_map); cycle+take 0f4016a (repeat-until-n with empty-source guard); scan 9230632 (running accumulator, extract the Some(..) inner tuple directly). Earlier sub-parts (enumerate, map/filter, named-fn, destructuring closure, identity/range/into_iter, chain/zip/flat_map identity) landed previously. TWO documented NON-implementable edges remain, both loud-fail cleanly (never a miscompile), neither an open bug: (1) a BARE `cycle().collect()` with no bounding `take` is unbounded/non-terminating — a semantic non-starter (only `cycle().take(n)` is collectable); (2) a scan body that conditionally returns `None` (rare — the common unconditional `Some((new,out))` is covered) bails, since resolving the Option in post-typecheck synthetic AST is fragile. NOTE: the earlier ledger claim that `scan` is 'broken under run' was WRONG — it works under run with the correct `|acc, x| -> Option[(A,U)]` signature (the mis-test used a bare `acc+x` body). Regressions: e2e_iter_adaptor_* + asan_b04_2_* across every adaptor (tests/codegen.rs, tests/memory_sanitizer.rs).

title: An inline tuple `(…, x)` whose heap component `x` is a FOR-LOOP element variable, moved into a `Vec` (`for x in w.iter() { v.push((i, x)) }`), DOUBLE-FREED the heap component under `karac build` (exit 133/134); `karac run` correct. FIXED 210eb93b. ROOT: codegen iterates a Vec IN PLACE (the `.iter()` peel makes borrow==owned iteration), so the loop var `x` bit-copies (ALIASES) the source Vec's element buffer; `compile_tuple` then bit-copied that alias into the tuple, and BOTH the source's scope-exit free and the pushed Vec's element drop released it. `compile_tuple` ran the two suppression helpers per element but — unlike every OTHER consume site (`v.push(x)`, struct-literal fields, call args, map inserts, let-bindings) — NOT `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg`, which deep-copies a RETAINING source (`for_loop_borrow_vars` / `owned_vecstr_params`) so the consumer owns a private buffer while the source keeps its free. Adding that one call fixes it (no-op for fresh temps / plain owned locals — handled by the existing suppression). Also unblocks TERMINAL heap `enumerate().collect()` (B-2026-07-04-2). Regression: `asan_for_loop_var_into_tuple_push_no_double_free` (macOS ASAN + Linux LSan). DISTINCT from B-2026-07-04-1 (element's OWN cleanup in a literal) — this is the for-loop VAR's aliased buffer on tuple move-out.

A NON-terminal heap `enumerate` in `<iter>.collect()` whose `(i64, <heap>)` tuple flows downstream miscompiled (garbage/crash). PARTIAL FIX aa24fb0c: `enumerate().map(|p| …)` now works — the desugar binds the tuple DIRECTLY to the map's param (single owning binding, no aliasing `let p = __ietup` copy), the loop-var search STOPS at enumerate so the source-element name can't collide with that param, and `map` pushes a TRANSFORMED value by unconditional move. Verified leak/double-free-clean (macOS ASAN 517, Linux LSan 517) incl. `map(|p| p.1)` / POD-producing map / `skip().enumerate().map` over LOCAL heap sources. STILL OPEN: a heap `enumerate` followed by `filter`/`take_while`/`skip_while`/`inspect` (a CONDITIONAL WHOLE-tuple move `if pred { push(p) }` whose synthetic binding is not move-suppressed → double-free), or a SECOND downstream stage (`enumerate().filter().map()`, `enumerate().take().map()` — the tuple reaches a param stage only after a non-param stage / is re-bound), stays GATED to the loud dispatch-fail (allowed heap shapes: terminal / `take` / `skip` whole-tuple push, and single-terminal `map`). POD tuples are always fine. Fixing the rest needs the synthetic downstream tuple binding to MOVE (suppress the source) rather than bit-copy-alias. OPEN (residual). UPDATE 2026-07-05 (FIXED 39a1ca46): all remaining downstream shapes lowered. The `Enumerate` build_body arm now binds the tuple to the FIRST downstream param-bearing stage's param, searching PAST the value-preserving passthrough adaptors (`take`/`skip`/`step_by`) — so `enumerate().take(n).map(|p| p.1)` (case D) binds the tuple DIRECTLY to the map param instead of a synthetic `__ietup` that the map's `let p = __ietup` bit-copied (the double-free). The caller's two-shape gate is replaced by a copy-free SIMULATION of the desugar's name/tuple threading: a heap enumerate is ADMITTED unless a whole-tuple COPY (`let X = <tuple>`) survives — a LATER param stage whose name differs from the tuple binding, or a NON-terminal `map` returning the whole tuple (`|p| p`, `|p| (…)`); those exotic residuals stay GATED to the loud dispatch-fail (never a miscompile), tracked as B-2026-07-05-1. Newly clean: `enumerate()` + `filter`/`take_while`/`skip_while`/`inspect` (conditional whole-tuple push), `enumerate().filter().take()`, and `map(|p| p.1)` past a `take`/`skip`/`filter`. Verified run==build + macOS ASAN 527/0 + Linux LSan 4/0 (enumerate class, fresh `Compiling karac`) + codegen 2053/0, fmt+clippy clean; regressions asan_b04_4_heap_enumerate_conditional_tuple_collect_no_double_free + asan_b04_4_heap_enumerate_downstream_map_collect_no_double_free + extended e2e_iter_adaptor_collect_enumerate_codegen. Sibling residuals under B-2026-07-04-2: multi-source adaptors (zip/chain/…), the multi-param/destructuring closure `enumerate().map(|(i,x)| …)`, and a non-terminal `map` whose body calls a METHOD on the mapped param (`enumerate().map(|p| p.1).filter(|s| s.len()>3)` — the PRE-EXISTING non-enumerate `map().filter()` gap, not this bug). FIXED.

A `<iter>.collect()` chain whose SOURCE is a FRESH-TEMP call result (`mk().iter().enumerate().collect()`, not a named local) read and dropped the source `Vec[String]` buffer at the WRONG element stride, freeing garbage (`pointer being freed was not allocated` — SIGABRT at -O0 / SIGTRAP exit-133 at -O2; `karac run` correct). Crashes even with NO index-read (a bare `.len()` still traps at scope-exit drop) — the earlier “index-read required / exit 134” framing was an artifact of a stale installed `karac`. ROOT (NOT a scope-exit race, as first guessed): the collect desugar's `for __ice in mk().iter()` peels `.iter()` and recurses into `try_compile_for_vec_value`, which resolved the loop element type from `owned_temp_drops` at the iterable span — but the whole method-call chain (`mk`, `mk()`, `.iter()`, `.enumerate()`, `.collect()`) SHARES the base callee's span (a known method-chain span collision), so `expr_types`/`owned_temp_drops` at that key hold the OUTERMOST result `Vec[(i64,String)]` (the last insert wins), not the source `Vec[String]`. The for-loop then read elements at the wider `(i64,String)` stride (32B) over a 24B `String` buffer and dropped the materialized temp the same way → garbage free. A LOCAL-binding source (`let w=…; w.iter()`) took the identifier path and was unaffected; `enumerate().map(|p| p.1)` (non-tuple output) happened to survive. FIX: `try_compile_for_vec_value` now consults `temp_recv_elem_types` (the receiver's ELEMENT type, recorded span-keyed by the typechecker for `.iter()`/`.into_iter()` fresh-temp peels — collision-immune) BEFORE `owned_temp_drops`; a non-peel Vec-value iterable (`t.iter_axis(n)`) has no element-table entry and still uses `owned_temp_drops`, so the reorder is a pure fix with no regression. Regressions: `e2e_iter_adaptor_collect_fresh_temp_source_codegen` + `asan_fresh_temp_source_enumerate_collect_no_double_free` (macOS ASAN + Linux LSan 518/0). RESIDUAL (separate, pre-existing, low): a PLAIN `.iter().collect()` with NO map/filter/enumerate adaptor loud-fails (`method 'iter' not supported` / `no handler for 'collect' on non-identifier receiver`) for both fresh-temp and local sources — identity collect, tracked under B-2026-07-04-2.

The natural rolling-DP inner scan `dp[c] = dp[c] + dp[c - 1]` over a `Vec[i64]` (kata #62 Unique Paths) ran ~3.1x slower than clang -O3 / rustc -O (316ms vs 102ms, K=1M seq bench): karac emitted THREE bounds-checked accesses per cell (read dp[c], read dp[c-1], write dp[c]) and — blocked by those checks — LLVM could not forward the just-written dp[c-1] into a register. The ENTIRE ~216ms gap was the per-cell bounds checks: with all of them off the bench runs 105ms ≈ clang's 102ms (LLVM does the store-to-load forwarding itself once the checks clear), so no separate load-forwarding work was needed. ROOT: the existing split-check elision (control_flow_bce.rs) only fires when a guard names `v.len()` directly or via a `let n = v.len()` alias, but the rolling-DP guard is `while c < cols` where `cols` is not spelled `dp.len()` yet EQUALS it (`dp` was filled to exactly `cols` elements by a counted `while j < cols` from 0 and never resized). FIX b5d18320, two parts: (1) bce_length_pin.rs — a fail-closed whole-function AST analysis records a length pin `cols == dp.len()` only when a soundness scan proves it holds from the fill loop to end-of-function (counter starts 0, steps +1, one unconditional push, `dp` untouched before the fill and only index-read/`.len()` after, `cols` never rewritten after); compile_while activates each pin when it finishes emitting the matching fill loop (live only lexically after), and resolve_len_origin consults the pins so `while c < cols` yields UpperBound{c, dp}. (2) index_bounds_already_proven extended from bare-identifier indices to `i ± k` (k a non-neg literal): `i - k` keeps the upper proof (i-k < i < len) so `dp[c-1]` elides its upper check; residual `< 0` checks fold against the loop's existing monotone `assume(i >= init)`. RESULT: kata #62 bench 316ms → 113ms (3.0x, TIED with rustc -O, within 7% of clang -O3); run==build==python preserved; adversarial shapes (bound reassigned after fill, non-zero counter, co-indexed shorter vec) still PANIC — the elision does not fire when unsound. Tests: 15 analysis unit tests + 3 E2E (positive + two must-panic negatives); codegen E2E (2037) and ASAN memory-sanitizer (518) green. Follow-ups (safe, not done): `for i in 0..b` fill form, push-only prelude seed, non-bare bound expressions.

The ESCAPE-side sibling of B-2026-07-03-31 (which fixed the BORROW side): RETURNING (or otherwise genuinely moving OUT) a `Some(v)`-bound payload from an `Option[<agg>]` DESTRUCTURE-LEAF (`let A { value } = a; match value { Some(v) => v, None => Val.Nothing }`) leaks the payload's inner heap (~48 B/String, 6/6 in a Vec[A]-consuming loop under macOS `leaks`; also `karac run`-correct, leak only). The consuming suppressor CORRECTLY disarms the destructure-leaf source's `karac_drop_Option` here (v really escapes), but the escaped value's ownership is not established on the RETURN path, so the downstream binding (`let g = get(a)`) never frees it. SPECIFIC to the destructure-leaf: returning `Some(v)=>v` from a PLAIN `Option[Val]` local/param is CLEAN (0 leaks) — so it is the `inline_option_agg_payload_vars` destructure-leaf path, not a general return-Option-payload leak. PRE-EXISTING (repros on main before and after B-2026-07-03-31). Found probing the B-31 fix's consuming cases (C1/C2). Likely resolved by completing the caller-retains model (the move-out/escape must transfer the payload's inner-heap ownership to the returned value). NON-BLOCKING (leak only, double-free-clean). See [[B-2026-07-03-31]], [[caller-retains-model-spike-phase0]]. REFINED DIAGNOSIS (2026-07-04, probing this entry): the 'escape/return-path' framing is INCOMPLETE — the leak is NOT escape-specific. Minimal ZERO-escape repro: `struct A { value: Option[Val] }` (Val = enum with Ident(String)); `let items: Vec[A] = build(); items.len()` (build + drop, NO get/return/consume) LEAKS 112 B / 2 objs per elem (String + boxed enum). ROOT CAUSE: `emit_struct_drop_synthesis(A)` emits NO drop for an `Option[<heap enum/struct>]` field — every field-classification pass leaves it None (the `EnumField` pass skips Option; the `OptionInline` pass is gated to String/Vec payloads AND copy-supported structs) — so `emit_struct_drop_synthesis(A)` returns None and `type_expr_has_drop_heap(A)` is false (the `Option => false` arm at synth_drop.rs ~1701), hence `Vec[A]` teardown skips the element walk entirely (bare `free(data)`, no per-element drop). THRESHOLD (all single-push build+drop): only `struct { Option[heap-enum/struct] }` leaks; `Vec[Option[Val]]`, `Vec[Val]`, `struct { value: Val }` (direct enum field), `struct { name: String }` are ALL CLEAN — the payload drop machinery already exists (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr(Option[Val])` emits `karac_drop_Option_Val` via `option_payload_struct_or_enum_drop_ok` + `emit_option_drop_fn`, inline + boxed); the gap is purely the struct-FIELD drop-synthesis wiring + the `type_expr_has_drop_heap` Option arm. COPY SIDE IS MANDATORY (proven empirically): a drop-only experiment (recurse `type_expr_has_drop_heap` into the Option payload + a copy-support-UNGATED `OptionInline` for plain-enum/struct payloads via `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr`) makes the zero-escape and plain-local-source cases CLEAN, but converts EVERY by-value-pass-of-an-ALIAS case (`for a in build() { let g = get(a) }`, `get(items[0])`) into a DOUBLE-FREE (exit 133): the callee-borrow move-out neutralizer `zero_option_field_tag_at` zeroes only the callee's local param header, NOT the aliased Vec element, so the Vec teardown and the escaped `g` free the same payload twice. Safe fix therefore needs `A` to be COPY-SUPPORTED (so a by-value `get(a)` entry-copies rather than borrow-shares) — `field_copy_supported`'s Option arm + `deep_copy_option_inline_payload_in_place` must admit a plain-enum/struct payload, which requires NEW boxed-Option-payload deep-copy machinery (Option[Val] BOXES its 4-word payload; the general `emit_clone_fn` Option path is shallow/WRONG per [[caller-retains-model-spike-phase0]] B-04-9(a)) plus the alias-source neutralization. CONFIRMS the original hunch: caller-retains model completion, NOT a contained fix; do NOT re-attempt drop-only (exit-133 double-free). OPEN. FIXED e56cc298 (2026-07-04) — the 'caller-retains model completion' framing OVERSTATED the scope; the real fix was THREE coupled halves (copy-depth == drop-depth), no model rewrite: (1) field_copy_supported's Option arm admits a non-shared struct/enum payload (option_payload_struct_or_enum_copyable — struct payloads need recursive copy-support, enum payloads mirror the existing direct-enum-field trust), making the owning struct callee-owned; (2) deep_copy_option_struct_enum_payload_in_place — the box-aware copy peer of emit_option_drop_fn's boxed/inline branch (BOXED when LLVM word count > 3 per coerce_to_payload_words: malloc a fresh box, shallow-copy the payload in, deep-copy its heap in place, store the new ptr; inline: deep-copy in place). NOT emit_clone_fn (shallow for Option); (3) the OptionInline struct-drop pass broadens to that payload class (the drop fn from vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr already handled inline+boxed Option payloads). Plus the destructure-leaf branch (let A { value } = a) now zeros the callee-owned SOURCE's Option tag (zero_struct_field_move_cap) on move-out — its 'struct drop NEVER frees an Option field' invariant no longer holds, so without it the source struct-drop's new OptionInline free double-freed against the B-27 leaf drop (the exit-133 the 'do NOT re-attempt drop-only' warning predicted — resolved by pairing the drop with copy-support AND source-tag neutralization). Gates: macOS ASAN 526/0 (no double-free/UAF), Linux LSan 526/0 (no leak), fmt+clippy. Test asan_b04_7_option_heap_enum_struct_field_drop_no_leak. Residual (pre-existing, split to B-2026-07-04-17): for-loop-element-ESCAPE (`for a in vec { let x = a }` / field-move into a fresh struct literal) double-frees for ANY Vec[heap-struct] — this fix makes Option[heap-enum] structs consistent with that pre-existing class.

The tree-walk interpreter has NO u64 model: `Value::Int(i64)` is signedness-blind, so every u64 value ≥ 2^63 is handled as its negative i64 two's-complement under `karac run` — mis-PRINTED (`println(f"{hi}")` of 2^63 shows `-9223372036854775808`; `value_type_name(Int)` is hard-coded `"i64"`), mis-COMPARED (`hi > (one << 62)` returns false via signed `eval_binary`), and mis-SORTED (`Column.sorted`/`argsort`, `Tensor.sorted`/`argsort` route through `value_compare`, which orders `Value::Int` signed — 2^63-plus values sort to the FRONT as negatives). Codegen (`karac build`) is CORRECT for all of these (unsigned print, `ugt`/`udiv`/`urem`/`lshr` already lowered per the u64.<op> desugar). Surfaced while implementing S6c u64 Column/Tensor sort under `build`: the codegen half is a one-line unsigned-`UGT` scratch compare (thread an `unsigned` flag through `SortKey::IntValue`/`IndexIntoInt`; the widen for a 64-bit elem is already an identity copy), but shipping it alone would make `build` disagree with `run` for u64 ≥ 2^63, so `sort_key_is_int` keeps the loud u64 rejection until the interpreter catches up. In practice u64 ≥ 2^63 literals are UNREACHABLE (the lexer rejects integer literals > i64::MAX as `Invalid integer literal`, and `hi + hi`-style construction overflow-traps), so the divergence only bites deliberately shift-constructed high-bit values — but it is a genuine silent wrong-result under `run`, independent of sort. FIX (an interpreter epic, NOT a sort slice): give the tree-walker a real unsigned-int model — either a `Value::UInt(u64)` variant or a signedness tag on `Value::Int` — and route `Display`/f-string, `eval_binary` compare/div/rem/shift, `value_compare`, and min/max/sort through it. Blocks the S6c u64 sort follow-on on BOTH surfaces. FIXED 45eb926 (2026-07-07): span-threaded unsigned-64 model — the i64-carrier `Value::Int` stays, but every signedness-sensitive sink reinterprets the bits as `u64` when the static type (via `expr_types`/`args_close_span`) says so (u8..u32 fit i64 non-negatively, so only 64-bit needs it). eval_binary: unsigned div/rem/shr + </<=/>/>= (operand hint from the call site, since a comparison types the expr `bool`) and add/sub/mul trapping at the u64 boundary (matches codegen `uadd.with.overflow`, no more i64::MAX false-trap); print/to_string/f-string render unsigned; Vec/Column/Tensor sort/sorted/argsort/argmin/argmax order by unsigned magnitude (element signedness stashed at the non-aliased close-paren leaf, since the receiver span is clobbered by the Vec[i64]/Option[i64]/Unit result). Two codegen bugs found while verifying run==build parity and fixed in the same commit so the interp fix introduces no silent divergence: (a) operators inside f-string interpolations were never lowered to typed method calls (`InterpolatedStringLit` was a `lower` leaf) so codegen compiled u64 f-string ops signed — lowering now recurses into interpolation parts; (b) `Vec[uN].sort()`'s default-order thunk compared signed, mis-sorting u8>=128 and u64>=2^63 — now unsigned for unsigned widths. Codegen Column[u64]/Tensor[u64] sort still LOUD-rejects (shared scratch-sort kernel, also on the stats path) — tracked follow-on B-2026-07-07-2. Gates: interpreter 1116/0 (+11 new), typechecker 1911/0, codegen 2055/0 (+2 new), memory_sanitizer 534/0, fmt+clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean.

Two PRE-EXISTING residuals of the caller-retains shared-drop model, surfaced while landing B-2026-07-03-28 Phase 2 (both repro on main BEFORE and after that fix). (a) DOUBLE-FREE (exit 133): a struct FIELD typed Vec[struct-with-Option[shared]-or-shared] that is entry-copied (the OUTER struct passed by-value to a copy-supported param) and then WHOLE-dropped (not consumed element-by-element) double-frees the element heap — the Vec[struct] field entry-copy is OUTER-only (elements bit-aliased with the caller) but the struct drop drains elements element-DEEP (this is the 'piece (b)' element-deep entry-copy the original B-28 scope named). MINIMAL: `struct ArgN{name:Option[String],value:Option[Val]} struct AttrN{args:Vec[ArgN]} fn count(a:AttrN)->i64{a.args.len()}` + `for a in items { count(a) }` (Val shared enum). Naive piece (b) via emit_clone_fn is WRONG (emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr routes Option payloads to the type-erased emit_enum_clone_fn("Option") = SHALLOW copy, no rc-inc / no deep buffer copy); a correct element-deep copy must recurse via the entry-copy machinery (deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place per element) — BUT that in turn regresses the for-loop-CONSUME path (the extra per-element rc-incs are STRANDED because for-loop consumption neutralizes the source Vec's element drain), so closing (a) also needs the for-loop-consume-drain semantics fixed. (b) LEAK: a direct-`shared`-field struct passed as an INLINE FRESH-TEMP arg (`f(DirH{value: shared})`, DirH={value:Val}) leaks the box — DirH is not copy-supported (field_copy_supported bails on a direct shared field), so the fresh-temp struct-arg cleanup gate (which requires copy-supported) registers no caller-temp drop; a LOCAL arg (`let d=DirH{..}; f(d)`) is fixed (track_struct_var combined drop). NON-BLOCKING for the self-host port: its Vec[struct] fields are consumed via for-loop (not whole-dropped), and its shared-owning structs use Option[shared], not direct-shared. Both are leak/double-free residuals of the same incomplete ownership model. See [[B-2026-07-03-28]], [[caller-retains-model-spike-phase0]]. UPDATE 2026-07-04: (b) FIXED (f22b58c4) — the fresh-temp struct-arg cleanup gate (call_dispatch.rs) now registers the combined drop (track_struct_var = pure rc-dec, no buffer copy) for a shared-owning struct even when NOT copy-supported; a direct-shared-field struct is caller-retains (never callee-owned), so the inline fresh-temp is the box's SOLE owner. Regression asan_b04_9b_direct_shared_freshtemp_struct_arg_no_leak (macOS ASAN 522/0 + Linux LSan 522/0 + codegen 2043/0, fmt+clippy clean). (a) REMAINS OPEN — CONFIRMED empirically as this entry predicted: an element-DEEP entry-copy of the Vec[struct] field (deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place per element, gated symmetric with vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr) DOES close the exit-133 double-free (repro 133->0, full macOS ASAN 521/0), but REGRESSES the for-loop-CONSUME path under LSan: the existing asan_attr_node_list_drop_consume_and_plain leaks 954 B/24 (and a whole-drop+consume repro 726 B/18). consume(a)'s param becomes an INDEPENDENT copy while the for-loop-consumed source's element drain stays suppressed (the per-shape for-loop-consume suppressor treats the arg as consumed and is NOT migrated to consume_class::binding_only_borrowed), stranding the source elements. This exactly reproduces the B-2026-07-03-28 ATTEMPT regression (240/6 -> 714/18); closing (a) needs the caller-retains model COMPLETION (move-aware entry-copy / consume-site rc-balance for copy-supported shared-owning structs), NOT a contained increment. (a) patch reverted, (b) landed standalone. OPEN (a). FIXED 2026-07-04 (273c9397) — the OPEN-(a) diagnosis above was WRONG: the regression was NOT the for-loop-consume source-drain suppressor / a missing binding_only_borrowed migration, and needed NO caller-retains model completion. Empirical isolation (whole-drop `a.args.len()` = LSan-clean vs destructure `let AttrN{args}=a` = leak) pinned it to a copy/drop DEPTH asymmetry in TWO coupled halves that land together: (1) param_own deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place element-deep-copies each Vec[struct/enum/Option] element of an entry-copied field via the entry-copy machinery (deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place / enum / Option — NOT emit_clone_fn, shallow for Option); (2) track_owned_destructure_field_cleanup drains a destructured Vec[<aggregate>] leaf via vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr (track_vec_of_aggs_var) instead of the outer-only track_vec_var, matching the whole-struct drop. On baseline the elements were bit-shared and freed via the source teardown, masking the shallow leaf drain; the deep copy makes them independent so the shallow leaf stranded them (the 954B/24 consume-path leak). Balanced only together. Gates: macOS ASAN full 525/0, Linux LSan FULL SUITE 525/0 (the twice-regressed asan_attr_node_list_drop_consume_and_plain now passes on the authoritative gate), fmt+clippy clean. New regression test asan_b04_9a_vec_struct_field_entrycopy_wholedrop_no_double_free. Both (a) and (b) now FIXED.

Closed the three follow-ups noted when the rolling-DP length-pin bounds-check elision first landed (B-2026-07-04-6), generalising which counted-fill shapes establish a `bound == v.len()` pin. FIX 81746fff: (1) FOR-RANGE fills — `for i in 0..BOUND { v.push(x) }` (exclusive, start 0 or omitted) now pins, activated at the tail of compile_for_range_with_step keyed on the range end's span (the range guarantees BOUND iterations from 0, so no counter proof needed). (2) SEED PRELUDES — a `v.push(..)` before the counted fill is allowed; it only lengthens v, so `bound <= v.len()` still holds (v1 bailed on any pre-fill mention of v). (3) ARITHMETIC BOUNDS — BOUND may be any pure-arithmetic expression over idents + int literals (`cols + 1`, `m * 2`, …), not just a bare identifier; bounds normalise to a span-free `BoundTerm` and the fill's bound + a later guard's bound match STRUCTURALLY across surface-Binary vs trait-lowered-Call forms, with every ident of BOUND required unwritten after the fill. The active-pin store changed from `HashMap<String,String>` (bound var → vec) to `Vec<(BoundTerm, String)>` and resolve_len_origin gained a normalised structural match. Every relaxation stays FAIL-CLOSED (a missed pin only keeps a check). Verified: kata #62 still 105ms (no regression, C parity); for-range / non-bare / prelude positives give correct output (run==build); adversarial shapes (for 1..n, cols+1 with cols reassigned after fill) still PANIC — no over-elision. Tests: 22 analysis unit tests (for-range/prelude/non-bare fire + negatives + BoundTerm normalisation) + 3 new E2E (2 positive, 1 must-panic); codegen E2E 2043 + ASAN 519 green.

`infer_binary`'s numeric arithmetic arm required an EXACT type match only for int-int operand pairs (`both_ints`); ANY pair with a float operand fell through to the loose `types_compatible` check, and `types_compatible(Int, Float)` is `true` (it must stay permissive for assignment/other contexts). So a cross-DOMAIN mix — `let z = y * x` with `y: i64`, `x: f64` — PASSED `karac check`, ERRORED under `karac run` (the interpreter has no implicit int→float promotion: `operator 'Mul' is not defined for Int and Float`), and SILENTLY MISCOMPILED under `karac build` (wrong-domain / garbage result — e.g. a fold product `c.fold(1, |a,x| a*x)` on a `Column[f64]` printed the i64 bit-pattern of the f64 accumulator). A three-surface divergence on the most basic operation, silent under build. ROOT: Kāra has no implicit int→float promotion in arithmetic, but the float branch never enforced the domain split — it delegated to a helper that is deliberately permissive about numeric mixing for OTHER contexts. FIX: the arm now guards the split itself — exactly one integer operand (the other a float) is a hard `TypeMismatch` pointing at an explicit `as` cast; `build` gates on the type error so all three surfaces reject consistently. Scope: ONLY cross-domain int/float is newly rejected — same-type float arithmetic, float+unsuffixed-literal promotion (`e + 1`), and the existing mixed-int-width/-signedness rejections are unchanged. Surfaced while probing the S6c `product`-on-`Reduce` follow-on. Regressions: test_mixed_int_float_arithmetic_rejected (5 pairs × ops × orders), test_int_float_arithmetic_explicit_cast_accepted, test_same_domain_float_arithmetic_still_accepted. NO blast radius: typechecker 1902/0, codegen E2E 2043/0, interpreter 1108/0.

A float + unsuffixed-integer-LITERAL arithmetic expression (`let z = a + 1` where `a: f64`) diverges: `karac check` PASSES and `karac build` is CORRECT (both apply Q4 literal promotion — the typechecker re-records the literal's span as `f64`, so `1` becomes `1.0`), but `karac run` ERRORS at runtime (`operator 'Add' is not defined for operands of type 'Float' and 'Int'`). ROOT: the tree-walk interpreter's `eval_binary` does NOT consult the typechecker's promoted literal type (`expr_types`) — it evaluates the bare literal as `Value::Int` and rejects the Int⊕Float pair, unaware the typechecker already unified it to f64. Pre-existing and independent of B-2026-07-04-11 (that fix targets int/float VARIABLE mixes, which are now rejected on all three surfaces; this is the LITERAL-promotion path, which SHOULD succeed and does on check+build but not run). FIX: interpreter arithmetic must honor the recorded promoted type for an unsuffixed numeric literal operand (coerce `Value::Int` → `Value::Float` when the binop's other operand is a float and the literal's recorded `expr_type` is a float), mirroring the codegen literal-promotion lowering. Reachability is high — `f64 + 1` is a natural pattern — but it fails LOUD under run (a runtime error, not silent garbage). FIXED (b8e3d3ab): a shared `promote_int_literal_for_float_peer` helper promotes a direct unsuffixed integer-literal operand to `Value::Float` when its peer evaluates to a float — gated on the AST shape exactly like the typechecker's Q4 rule (so a genuine int/float VARIABLE mix, a hard type error since B-2026-07-04-11, still surfaces under run rather than being silently coerced). Applied at all three interpreter scalar-arithmetic sites: `dispatch_lowered_op` (the `<type>.add(a, 1)` operator-lowering path that normal `a + 1` desugars to), `CompoundAssign` (`a += 1`), and the `ExprKind::Binary` arm (un-lowered contexts). run == build == default auto-par verified; regressions test_float_int_literal_promotion / _compound_assign / test_int_arithmetic_literal_unaffected. Interpreter 1111/0, codegen E2E 2043/0 (isolated; a concurrent-load run flaked 7 SOA-pop tests that pass alone).

SHADOW SOUNDNESS HOLE in the rolling-DP length-pin bounds-check elision (B-2026-07-04-6/10), found while auditing the nested-block follow-up. The after-fill invariance scans (vec_readonly_after / var_unwritten_after) recurse into nested blocks through a GENERIC walker that only inspects statement VALUES — it is blind to `let` patterns and assignment TARGETS buried in an inner block. So a NESTED shadow of the pinned Vec or of a bound identifier went undetected and the name-keyed pin wrongly applied. Two confirmed exploits on the prior main (6ca6fe44's parent): (a) `let mut dp=..; while j<n{dp.push} ; while once{ let mut dp=Vec.new(); while d<n{ acc+=dp[d] } }` — the inner EMPTY `dp` shadow got the pin → `dp[d]` OOB read → CRASH (exit 137); `karac run` correctly errors. (b) same but shadowing the BOUND var `{ let n=20; while c<n{ acc+=dp[c] } }` with dp filled to 3 → `dp[c]` read past len with the check elided → SILENT WRONG ANSWER (printed 15, no crash) — the worst class. A nested REASSIGNMENT `n=20` (not a rebind) of a `let mut` bound var was the same hole. FIX 6ca6fe44: `region_bindings`, an EXHAUSTIVE (no-wildcard) fail-closed collector of every name bound-by-pattern (`rebound`, counted) or assigned (`assigned`, target root) anywhere in v's after-fill scope INCLUDING nested blocks; the pin is refused if v is rebound there, or any bound identifier is rebound or reassigned there. Both exploits now panic cleanly, matching the interpreter. Regression: 3 unit (no_fire_nested_shadow_of_vec / _of_bound_var / _reassign_of_bound_var) + 2 E2E must-panic (nested_shadow_vec / nested_shadow_bound). Codegen E2E 2046 + ASAN 521 green.

Closed the last length-pin follow-up: NESTED-BLOCK fills. Recognition was function-top-level only, so a DP buffer built inside an outer loop (`while k { let mut dp=..; fill; use }`, the per-iteration-rebuild shape) or an `if`/`match` arm never pinned and kept its bounds checks. FIX 6ca6fe44: `for_each_block` (exhaustive, no-wildcard) drives the per-block fill recognition over the function body AND every nested block; each block is analysed on its own statement list with the invariance region scoped to the rest of THAT block. Guarded by an EXACTLY-ONCE rule — the Vec name must be bound exactly once in the whole function — so a pin (kept active to end of function) can never match a same-named Vec in a sibling/outer scope (the lingering-pin hazard: an inner fill's pin leaking onto a shorter outer `dp` after the inner block; verified via a two-sibling-`dp` adversarial that now correctly panics). Depends on B-2026-07-04-13's `region_bindings` for shadow-soundness. Verified: per-iteration-rebuild nested fill correct (run==build, 84); kata #62 still 105ms/correct. Regression: 3 unit (fires_on_per_iteration_rebuild / _fill_inside_if_block / no_fire_two_vecs_same_name_siblings) + 1 E2E positive (nested_block_fill). RESIDUAL: none known for the counted-fill-then-index class.

S6c-12 slice 4 edge: a GENERIC container impl's trait DEFAULT method fails to resolve at the call site on the SECOND element monomorphization. `trait R[T: Sub] { fn lo(ref self)->T; fn hi(ref self)->T; fn span(ref self)->T { self.hi() - self.lo() } }` + `impl[T: Ord+Sub] R[T] for Column[T] { fn lo=self.min(); fn hi=self.max() }` (span inherited as a default), called as `ci.span()` on `Column[i64]` AND `cf.span()` on `Column[f64]`: the FIRST call type-checks, the SECOND errors `no method 'span' on type 'Column', did you mean 'mean'?` (order-independent — whichever call is second fails). CONTAINER-SPECIFIC and DEFAULT-METHOD-specific: (a) a REQUIRED method on the same generic container impl resolves for BOTH monos (base slice-4 case works — `doubled_sum` over Column[i64]+[f64] = OK); (b) the SAME generic-impl-plus-default shape over a USER STRUCT (`impl[T] R[T] for Pair[T]`) resolves for both monos; (c) a single element mono of the container default resolves fine (slice 3 concrete-target defaults also fine). So the fault is in the builtin-container method-resolution path's handling of a SPLICED default method across two receiver-element instantiations (likely the spliced Function AST is consumed/specialized in place on the first mono's container resolution, so the second element no longer matches). check/build-vs-run DIVERGENCE: `karac run` executes it correctly (interpreter has no such per-mono container-resolution state) — a false REJECTION under check/build, not a miscompile. Base generic-container-impl feature (required methods, multi-mono; single-mono defaults) is DONE and landed S6c-12 slices 1-3; this is the residual edge. Repro: scratchpad s4_twomono.kara. FIXED c495dda3 (Slice 4b): the ORIGINAL DIAGNOSIS ABOVE IS WRONG — this was a MISDIAGNOSIS, correct-by-design, NOT a container/2nd-mono/default-method bug. Real cause: the repro's bound is `T: Ord + Sub` and `f64` deliberately does NOT implement `Ord` (IEEE-754 NaN breaks total order; only the `F32`/`F64` WRAPPER types do — env_build.rs 'Floats deliberately excluded', design.md total-order-float-types). So `Column[f64]` CORRECTLY fails the `Ord` bound while `Column[i64]` passes — it's f64-specific, NOT order-dependent (verified: i64+i64 both pass, single f64 fails, swap order => f64 fails wherever it sits). `run` 'works' only because `karac run` executes past typecheck errors. The i64-vs-f64 asymmetry was mis-read as required-vs-default because the base slice-4 test used `T: Add` (which f64 DOES satisfy); with `T: Ord` the required methods lo/hi ALSO fail for f64. Real DIAGNOSTIC defect (now fixed): the bound-filtered impl surfaced 'no method span, did you mean mean' (naming the wrong problem). Fix: (1) render_unsatisfied_bound_message (exprs.rs) appends an `F32`/`F64`-wrapper hint when a float primitive fails Ord/Eq/Hash; (2) the not-found arm (expr_method_call.rs) detects a bound-filtered impl via new Env::first_unsatisfied_bound and surfaces the failing bound. Purely diagnostic (resolution decision unchanged). Correct usage = drop the spurious `Ord` bound (span needs only `Sub`; lo/hi call builtin min/max) => checks clean AND runs `8 3` for i64+f64. Tests test_float_ord_bound_hint_points_to_wrapper / test_generic_container_impl_float_ord_bound_actionable_message / test_generic_container_impl_no_ord_bound_ok.

S6c-12 slice 4: a GENERIC TENSOR impl's `T + T` operator lowers as an INTEGER add for a non-i64 element mono under `karac build`. `trait Doubler[T: Add] { fn doubled_sum(ref self)->T; } impl[T: Add] Doubler[T] for Tensor[T, [3]] { fn doubled_sum(ref self)->T { self.sum() + self.sum() } }` then `tf.doubled_sum()` on a `Tensor[f64, [3]]`: builds, then PANICS `integer overflow` at the `+` (the f64 sum bits are added as i64). The i64 mono is correct (12), and `karac run` is correct for BOTH monos (interp fine). CONTAINER-ASYMMETRIC: the exact same shape over a GENERIC COLUMN impl (`impl[T: Add] Doubler[T] for Column[T]`) compiles correctly for i64 AND f64 (12, 14) — so the Column mono threads the element type into the operator/`self.sum()` return but the Tensor mono does not (self's Tensor element defaults to i64, or the Tensor[i64]/Tensor[f64] monos collide on one mangled body — the generic-impl-method path via `make_generic_impl_method_function` may skip the `append_handle_mangle` / element-binding that S6a applies to bound-generic free-fn Column params). Here the overflow CHECK catches it (loud crash), but a bit pattern that forms a non-overflowing i64 add would be a SILENT wrong-answer miscompile → severity medium. Base generic-container-impl feature otherwise works: generic Column impl (i64+f64) and generic Tensor impl (i64) verified run==build (test_e2e_user_generic_trait_impl_over_container). Sibling typecheck edge B-2026-07-04-15. Repro: scratchpad s4_ten_f64.kara. FIXED 735c5717: root cause was NOT a mangle-collision but the generic-user-impl-method DISPATCH (compile_method_call, method_call.rs) binding the impl's leading type param T from the receiver. TWO coupled sub-bugs: (1) a Tensor[T,[3]] receiver's recorded instantiation carries a SHAPE arg ([3]) beside the element, and counting it tripped the explicit-binding `<= n_params` gate → T left UNBOUND, defaulting to i64 → f64 T+T lowered as checked integer add → overflow (Column, single-arg, unaffected — the asymmetry). (2) Even bound, T was sourced from that span instantiation whose element is the constructor LITERAL default (f64 for a narrow-f32 tensor from [1.0,…]) → f32 buffer read with f64 stride → SILENT garbage on BOTH Column and Tensor. Fix sources T from the container's REGISTERED (annotation-derived) element (column_var_infos/tensor_var_infos) via container_receiver_elem_arg + excludes Shape from the fresh-temp fallback gate. Verified run==autopar==build across f64/f32/i32/u32/u8 on both containers. Test test_e2e_user_generic_trait_impl_over_tensor_operator_widths.

PRE-EXISTING (repros on main today for a plain `struct { s: String }`, INDEPENDENT of B-2026-07-04-7): iterating an owned `Vec[<heap struct>]` by value and MOVING the loop element to a NEW owner inside the body double-frees at teardown (exit 133/134; runs correct then aborts). Minimal: `struct A { s: String } ... for a in items { let x = a; }` and the field-move form `for a in items { let w = A { s: a.s }; }` BOTH abort; the String-struct, nested-struct, and (post-B-04-7) Option[heap-enum] variants all reproduce. CONTRAST — CLEAN: a for-loop DESTRUCTURE (`for a in items { let A { s } = a; }`), a pass-by-value call (`for a in items { f(a); }` — f entry-copies), and an OWNED-LOCAL move (`let a = A{..}; let x = a;`) are all fine. So the locus is the WHOLE-struct (or field-into-new-aggregate) MOVE of a for-loop ELEMENT. ROOT CAUSE (hypothesis, from probing): the for-loop element binding `a` is a bit-copy ALIAS of the container element, but `mark_for_loop_borrow_if_heap` only marks Vec/String/Option/Result element types as for_loop_borrow_vars (so their consume sites defensively copy via maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg) — a STRUCT/enum element type is NOT marked, and maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg only deep-copies Vec/String slots, so a whole-struct move `let x = a` neither defensively copies nor suppresses the container's per-element drain. Both `x`'s scope drop and `items`'s FreeVecBuffer element-drain then free the same buffer. Pre-B-04-7 the Option[heap-enum] struct had no element drop so it LEAKED here instead of crashing; B-04-7 (correctly) makes it droppable, so it now joins the pre-existing double-free class. Likely fix: mark heap-owning struct/enum for-loop elements as borrow AND give struct/enum a defensive DEEP copy at whole-move consume sites (the struct analog of maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg), OR suppress the container's per-element drain for moved-out elements. Broad for-loop-ownership blast radius — own slice. NON-BLOCKING for typical code (destructure + pass-by-value are the common shapes and are clean). See [[caller-retains-model-spike-phase0]], [[struct-drop-depth-invariant-and-option-blocker]].

Residual of B-2026-07-04-4: a heap `enumerate` whose `(i64, <heap>)` tuple is whole-COPIED downstream (`let X = <tuple>`) still gates to the loud dispatch-fail (NEVER a miscompile). The copy-free simulation in `try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec` admits a heap enumerate only when the SINGLE owning tuple binding is threaded by identity to every consumption site; it REJECTS two exotic shapes that reintroduce a whole-tuple bit-copy: (a) a LATER param stage whose closure param name DIFFERS from the tuple's binding (the first downstream param), e.g. `enumerate().filter(|p| p.0>0).inspect(|q| …)` — the `inspect`'s `let q = <tuple>` copies the still-whole tuple; and (b) a NON-terminal `map` that returns the whole tuple (`enumerate().map(|p| p).filter(…)`, identity or re-tuple body) — its `let __icm = <tuple>` materialization copies it. Both are pathological (real code reuses the tuple param name or reduces the tuple before re-binding) and loud-fail cleanly. A robust fix would make `let X = <heap-aggregate-identifier>` a MOVE (suppress the source) in the ordinary codegen ownership path, or alpha-rename the closure param to the tuple binding in the desugar body — both broader than the contained B-2026-07-04-4 fix. karac run executes all of these correctly; codegen loud-fails (no miscompile). See B-2026-07-04-4, B-2026-07-04-2.
resolution: FIXED f1a5d49 (2026-07-07). The two rejected shapes — (a) a later param stage whose name differs from the tuple binding (`enumerate().filter(|p|..).inspect(|q|..)`), and (b) a non-terminal `map` returning the whole tuple (`enumerate().map(|p| p).filter(..)`, or a re-tuple `|p| (p.0,p.1)`) — are now SOUND, and the copy-free gate (plus its `copy_free` simulation of the desugar's name threading) is removed. The desugar always threads the enumerate tuple as a bare identifier (or a re-tuple of its fields), so each downstream `let q = <id>` is an ordinary Vec/String/tuple MOVE that suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg / compile_tuple retire at the source — no alias, no double-free. So the 'robust fix' the entry anticipated (make `let X = <heap-aggregate-identifier>` a MOVE) was ALREADY in place via the ordinary move-suppression; only the over-conservative synthetic gate remained. Verified run==build + LSan-clean across differing-param, non-terminal-map-identity, re-tuple-map, and multi-stage-differing-param shapes. Tests: e2e_iter_adaptor_enumerate_heap_tuple_downstream_copy_codegen (codegen), asan_b05_1_nonterminal_map_tuple / _diff_param_name / _retuple_map / _multistage_diff_params (memory_sanitizer).

title: Residual of B-2026-07-04-17 (struct case fixed 278e1a91): a BARE `Vec[<user enum>]` element MOVED whole to a new owner (`for a in items { let x = a }` over `Vec[E]` where `E` has a heap payload) still double-frees at teardown (ASAN: `SUMMARY: AddressSanitizer: double-free`; silent corruption without ASAN). The B-04-17 fix wires the for-loop-element defensive deep-copy ONLY at the STRUCT let-binding / struct-literal consume sites (`for_loop_owned_agg_vars` + `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` / `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field`); a bare enum element `let x = a` routes through the ENUM let-binding tracking, which has no deep-copy hook, so `x`'s enum-payload drop and the container's per-element drain free the same buffer. A struct FIELD that is an enum / `Option[enum]` IS covered (the struct arm recurses). Fix: mark bare non-shared user-enum for-loop elements too and deep-copy the enum payload in place at the enum let-binding site (`deep_copy_enum_heap_payload_in_place`, already `pub(super)`-adjacent), mirroring the struct arm. Repro: `enum E { A(String), B(i64) } ... for a in vec_of_E { let x = a; match x { E.A(s)=>.. } }`. FIXED 81ad98c (2026-07-07) — mirrors the struct arm exactly. `mark_for_loop_borrow_if_heap` now marks a bare non-shared user-enum element with a heap-bearing variant into `for_loop_owned_agg_vars`, and the enum let-binding site deep-copies the live-variant payload in place via `deep_copy_enum_heap_payload_in_place` (made pub(super)). That copier duplicates EXACTLY the `VecOrString` + `NestedStruct` payloads `emit_enum_drop_switch` frees (copy-depth == drop-depth: `EnumDropKind` has no other heap kind), so every non-shared heap-bearing enum is safe to mark — no separate copy-support gate needed. `Option`/`Result` stay on the `for_loop_borrow_vars` path. Verified build==run oracle + Linux ASAN/LSan for a Vec[enum] with a heap-payload variant and a scalar variant (both the copied and no-op paths). Gates: memory_sanitizer 534/0, codegen E2E 2054/0, non-codegen green, fmt+clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm clean. Test asan_forloop_bare_enum_element_whole_move_no_double_free.

PRE-EXISTING sibling of B-2026-07-04-17 / B-2026-07-05-2, previously UNTRACKED: a `Vec[String]` / `Vec[Vec[T]]` for-loop element moved WHOLE into a local via a plain let-bind (`for s in words { let x = s }`) double-frees at teardown (exit 134; runs correct then aborts). The B-2026-06-20-13 borrow-element work wired the defensive copy only at the push/insert/entry consume sites (`maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` keys on `for_loop_borrow_vars`, but the Let path only CALLED it for owned-vecstr PARAMS), so the plain whole-move let-bind aliased the container element and both `x`'s `FreeVecBuffer` and the container's per-element drain freed the same buffer. Found while fixing B-2026-07-05-2 (the enum leg) — the struct fix (278e1a91) covered struct elements, the enum fix covered enum elements, and this closes the Vec/String element type. FIXED 81ad98c (2026-07-07): extend the Let-path defensive-copy gate so a for-loop-borrow Vec/String Identifier RHS (`for_loop_borrow_vars` + has `vec_elem_types`) routes through `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` like an owned param, and skip the whole-move source-suppression for that shape so the container stays the single owner. Verified build==run oracle + Linux ASAN/LSan; test asan_forloop_string_element_whole_move_let_no_double_free. Gates green with the B-2026-07-05-2 commit.

Follow-on to B-2026-07-04-8 (interpreter u64 model, fixed 45eb926): under `karac build`, `Column[u64]`/`Tensor[u64]` `sorted`/`argsort`/`argmin`/`argmax` are REJECTED loudly (`sort_key_is_int` in kernel.rs) and `Stats.sort`/`argsort`/median/percentile on a u64 slice compare signed — the shared scratch sort `emit_sort_scratch` keys integers at signed i64 (`SGT`), misordering values >= 2^63. The interpreter now sorts all three containers unsigned-correctly (`karac run`), and `Vec[u64].sort()` is unsigned-correct on BOTH surfaces (its own default-order thunk, fixed in 45eb926), so this is the remaining `run`/`build` gap: run works, build loud-rejects (Column/Tensor) or mis-sorts (Stats free-fns). NOT a silent wrong result for Column/Tensor (the reject is loud); the Stats free-fn path is a pre-existing signed-compare that predates the interpreter fix. FIX (the S6c u64-sort follow-on the ledger anticipated): thread an `unsigned` flag through `SortKey::IntValue`/`IndexIntoInt` and pick `UGT` over `SGT` in `emit_sort_scratch`'s `key_gt` (the widening already zero-extends unsigned via `sort_widen_value`), then lift the `sort_key_is_int` u64 rejection. Deferred from 45eb926 because `emit_sort_scratch` is shared with the stats median/percentile/quantile path — it wants its own reviewed slice with IR + E2E parity tests across all sort surfaces, not a graft onto the interpreter-model change. u64 >= 2^63 is only reachable via deliberately shift-constructed high-bit values (the lexer rejects integer literals > i64::MAX), so the gap is exotic. FIXED (7e5ef5f): threaded an `unsigned` flag through `SortKey::IntValue`/`IndexIntoInt` and pick `ugt` over `sgt` in `emit_sort_scratch`'s `key_gt`; lifted the `sort_key_is_int` u64 rejection. Column/Tensor pass their real element signedness; `stats_argsort` (shared with Tensor argsort) gained the flag; the Stats value-sort path passes `unsigned: false` (Stats type-rejects non-i64/f64, so it never carries u64). Narrow unsigned widths already `zext` to non-negative i64 so `sgt`/`ugt` agree; only full-width u64 needs it. argmin/argmax were already unsigned-correct (separate `compile_binop_typed` path). Verified run==build for Column[u64]/Tensor[u64] sorted/argsort with values >= 2^63. Tests: E2E parity + `icmp ugt` IR pin + reworked former reject-test. Gates: codegen 2065/0, memory_sanitizer 537/0.

`Column.from_vec(<temporary Vec[String]>)` is not supported under `karac build` — a fresh (non-`let`-bound) `Vec[String]` argument to `Column.from_vec` fails codegen with a LOUD, actionable error: 'Column.from_vec(<temporary Vec[String]>) is not yet supported by the native backend; bind the Vec to a `let` first (a follow-on codegen slice adds the ownership transfer)'. STRING-SPECIFIC: the i64 (POD) temp `Column.from_vec([1,2,3])` builds fine — only a HEAP element buffer needs the ownership transfer from the temporary. `karac run` handles it (interp). build-vs-run divergence, NOT a miscompile (loud reject). Workaround: `let v: Vec[String] = [..]; Column.from_vec(v)`. Surfaced while landing S6c-12 Slice 5 (Column[String] indexing / heap-element read-back — that slice's own work is complete and orthogonal; this from_vec temp-ownership transfer is a separate follow-on). Repro: scratchpad s5/ft.kara.

Bound-generic trait method dispatch over a USER-TYPE implementor fails under `karac build` (works under `karac run`). `trait Doubler[T] { fn dbl(ref self) -> T; } struct Wrap { v: i64 } impl Doubler[i64] for Wrap { fn dbl(ref self) -> i64 { self.v + self.v } } fn apply[C: Doubler[i64]](c: ref C) -> i64 { c.dbl() }` called as `apply(w)` on a `Wrap` -> `karac run` prints 42, `karac build` errors `codegen: no handler for method 'dbl' on variable 'c' (method dispatch fell through)`. GENERAL, not stdlib-specific: reproduces identically with the stdlib surface traits `Reduce`/`ElementwiseOrd` when the bound-generic receiver monomorphizes to a user struct (`fn total[C: Reduce[i64]](c: ref C){c.sum()}` over a user `Pair` impl; `fn lo[C: ElementwiseOrd[i64]](c: ref C){c.argmin()}` over a user `Trio` impl). The CONTAINER implementors (Column/Tensor) work on all three surfaces because the concrete-receiver builtin intercept fires inside the mono body (the receiver stays a handle `ptr`); a user-struct implementor has no such intercept, so codegen's `compile_method_call` on the monomorphized `c: ref Wrap` receiver never finds `Wrap.dbl` and falls through. ROOT: the codegen bound-generic mono path resolves builtin/container method dispatch but does not lower a plain user-impl trait method call when the type-param receiver monomorphizes to a user nominal type -- it needs to resolve `<mono-target>.<method>` via the impl table the way concrete `w.dbl()` does. Run/build divergence (loud codegen error, NOT a silent miscompile). Pre-existing since the S6a bound-generic surface landed; latent because every Reduce/ElementwiseOrd/ElementwiseMap bound-generic TEST used a Column/Tensor receiver, never a user-struct implementor. Surfaced probing S6c ElementwiseOrd user impls (the ElementwiseOrd container + user-impl-over-container paths all work on 3 surfaces = full Reduce parity; only this general user-type-under-build path lags, matching Reduce's own pre-existing behavior). Repro: scratchpad plain_bound.kara / reduce_usertype.kara / eo_usertype.kara. FIXED (4f3e5747, S6c-14): the mono param-type-name registration (compile_mono_function, mono.rs) only matched a bare `TypeKind::Path` param, so a `ref C`/`mut ref C` receiver never recorded var_type_names[c]=<concrete> -> inferred_receiver_type(c) returned None inside the mono and the <Type>.<m> user-impl dispatch was skipped. Fix peels a leading ref/mut ref before the name registration (mirrors the enum_inst_var_types peel just below it). Receiver ABI already correct for a non-generic ref-struct param, so recording the name suffices. Verified run==autopar==build for ref/owned/mut-ref over a plain user trait + Reduce/ElementwiseOrd over user structs; codegen 2062 green. Tests bound_generic_dispatch_over_user_type (interp) / test_e2e_bound_generic_dispatch_over_user_type (codegen).

Resolver 'did-you-mean' suggestions are never machine-applicable: the resolver computes the exact correct name (`suggest_const_name`, fuzzy-match candidates) and its error struct HAS a `.replacement: Option<TextEdit>` field, but that field is NEVER populated — the answer is emitted only as human prose in `suggestion`. `karac fix`/`cmd_fix` already collects resolver `.replacement` (src/cli.rs ~9654) and `collect_diagnostics` already renders it as top-level "replacement":{offset,length,text}, so the applier + JSON channel are fully wired; only the emission side is missing. FIX: at every resolver error site that computes a UNIQUE candidate (E0100 UndefinedName module-binding rename + general single-fuzzy-match, E0104 UndefinedType single fuzzy, E0107 UndefinedLabel single nearby label), populate `.replacement` with the TextEdit spanning the misspelled token. Single highest-leverage B->A conversion in the compiler (whole E01xx did-you-mean family), adds no new analysis — the candidate is already computed. Surfaced by the 2026-07-06 diagnostic-fix-invariant audit (resolver family: 0 of 32 variants carry a fix today). See docs/diagnostic-fix-audit.md. FIXED (830831f): the E01xx did-you-mean family (E0100 UndefinedName, E0104 UndefinedType, unknown-module/unknown-item import suggestions) was already machine-applicable as of 76be2de. This commit closed the two named stragglers: (a) E_MODULE_BINDING_NAMING now carries a `.replacement` — a `name_span` field was added to ModuleBinding (the identifier alone, captured at parse) so the SCREAMING_SNAKE rename anchors to exactly the name, and `karac fix` applies `let myConfig` -> `let MY_CONFIG`; (b) E0107 UndefinedLabel now fuzzy-matches in-scope loop labels and emits a `did you mean` suggestion (prose). The label MACHINE replacement is deferred to B-2026-07-07-3: Break/Continue carry no label-token span in the AST, so there is nothing to anchor an edit to yet.

`karac fix` silently drops the ownership `fix_diff` multi-edit migration it already computed: OwnershipErrorKind::ConcurrentSharedStruct / ConcurrentPlainStruct populate `error_fix_diffs` (src/ownership.rs ~760/928) with a full machine-applicable migration (`par struct` keyword insert + per-mut-field `Mutex[T]` wraps), and `collect_diagnostics` emits it to JSON as a top-level "fix_diff":[...] array (src/cli.rs ~3267) — but `cmd_fix` (src/cli.rs ~9668) collects only each phase's single-edit `.replacement`, never `error_fix_diffs`, so `karac fix` applies NOTHING for these two diagnostics even though the JSON advertises a fix. An agent reading the JSON directly (Mend) can apply it; the `karac fix` CLI path cannot — a contract inconsistency, and the SAME latent applier-gap already closed once for typecheck fix_its. FIX: teach `cmd_fix` to also collect `pipeline.ownership.error_fix_diffs` values (Vec<TextEdit>) and apply them alongside `.replacement`, applying a multi-edit envelope in DESCENDING offset order so earlier edits don't shift later spans. Surfaced by the 2026-07-06 diagnostic-fix-invariant audit. See docs/diagnostic-fix-audit.md. FIXED (0f21b4b): taught `cmd_fix` to flatten `pipeline.ownership.error_fix_diffs` values into the edit set alongside `.replacement`; the existing descending-offset sort + overlap dedup applies the multi-edit envelope safely. Two end-to-end tests/cli.rs regressions drive real `karac fix` over the plain-struct (4 edits) and shared-struct (7 edits) cases and assert the migrated source on disk.

E0107 UndefinedLabel `did you mean` suggestion is prose-only (not machine-applicable): as of B-2026-07-06-3 (830831f) a misspelled `break`/`continue` loop label fuzzy-matches the loop labels in scope and reports `did you mean `outer`?`, but the `.replacement` field stays None because `ExprKind::Break { label: Option<String> }` / `ExprKind::Continue { label: Option<String> }` carry no span for the label token — only the enclosing break/continue expression has one (and at the `break VALUE` site that span points at the value, not the label). FIX: thread a `label_span: Option<Span>` through the Break/Continue AST (captured at parse right after the label identifier), then populate `.replacement` at the three UndefinedLabel sites in src/resolver/resolve_block.rs (~271/295/602) spanning that token. Broader than the B-2026-07-06-3 slice because it touches every Break/Continue match arm (codegen, typechecker, interpreter, resolver); deferred as its own entry to keep the naming/label-suggestion fix contained. Surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-06-3. FIXED (911db54): added `label_span: Option<Span>` to `ExprKind::Continue`, captured in `parse_continue_label`, and anchored the rename `.replacement` on it at the resolver UndefinedLabel site. `karac fix` now corrects `continue otuer` -> `continue outer`. Scoped to Continue by design: `break <typo>` parses as a value expr (only known labels become break labels) and surfaces as E0100 (already machine-applicable), so it never reaches the E0107 label path. Five `Continue { label }` read sites gained `..`; the whole E01xx/E0107 did-you-mean family is now machine-applicable.

Blanket Vec[T] surface-trait impls (`impl Reduce[i64] for Vec[i64]` etc., the spike's remaining 'blanket Vec[T] impls' S6c item): DESIGN PROVEN across typechecker + interpreter + direct-call codegen, but the codegen path has ONE remaining SIGTRAP blocking it. Complete design (all verified working when applied): (1) TYPECHECKER items.rs check_impl_block self_type guard (~1896) -- add `Vec`/`VecDeque` beside Column/Tensor to the type_is_fully_concrete branch so `self` in `impl Trait for Vec[i64]` is `Vec[i64]` not `Vec[]` (else `for x in self` types the element abstract `T` -> 'cannot mix i64 and T'); typechecker suite green (1921). (2) INTERPRETER value_type_name (interpreter.rs ~1687) add `Value::Array(_) => "Vec"` so the builtin-Vec fall-through reaches try_eval_impl_method keyed `Vec.method`; run == correct for direct `v.sum()` AND bound-generic `f[C: Reduce[i64]](c: ref C){c.sum()+c.prod()}` over a Vec. (3) CODEGEN helpers.rs make_impl_method_function (~495) thread Vec/VecDeque target args onto the synthesized `self` param so the body registers vec_elem_types[self]. (4) CODEGEN collections.rs compile_index (~1402) key the Vec index branch on container_recv (accepts SelfValue) not just Identifier so `self[i]` in the body resolves (Slice-5 self-index twin). (5) CODEGEN method_call.rs Vec dispatch (~2540): try compile_vec_method, and on its no-arm Err WHEN a user `Vec.<method>` fn exists, fall through to the generic user-impl dispatch (inferred_receiver_type -> Vec.method, branch ~2917). With all 5, DIRECT calls (v.sum()/v.prod() loop bodies) build+run correct on all 3 surfaces, and a SINGLE bound-generic loop-bodied method builds+runs. REMAINING BLOCKER: when TWO different loop-bodied user Vec methods are each reached via BOUND-GENERIC (mono) dispatch (fa[C](c){c.sum()} + fb[C](c){c.prod()}, or one mono both[C](c){c.sum()+c.prod()}) the built binary SIGTRAPs at runtime (exit 133) though the LLVM module VERIFIES (build succeeds) and `karac run` computes correct answers. Repro matrix: direct sum+prod (both loops)=OK; one bound-generic loop method + one bound-generic non-loop method (c.sum()loop + c.max()self[0])=OK; TWO bound-generic loop methods=SIGTRAP. vec_elem_types IS already in mono.rs SavedVarSideTables save/restore, and compile_vec_method's no-arm path emits no IR (both ruled out). B-2026-07-02-39 family (mono symbol / per-mono impl-method-instantiation collision) for the Vec.method impl fns; needs IR-level diff of the two monos to root-cause. Vec code NOT integrated (would ship a build-time SIGTRAP); design + repro recorded here for a follow-on to resume from the crash.
resolution: FIXED 6321ee8. The reported 'mono symbol collision / two-loop-method SIGTRAP' was a MISDIAGNOSIS. Real root cause: `for x in self` inside a user `impl Trait for Vec[i64]` body. `self` is a `ref Vec` receiver — an `ExprKind::SelfValue`, not an `Identifier` — so the for-loop iterable dispatch (control_flow_for.rs) fell through to the `_ =>` value path (`try_compile_for_vec_value`), which materialize-iterates-DROPS the source. Dropping the BORROWED receiver double-frees the caller-owned Vec buffer and its per-element heap: one loop method -> `free(): double free`; two -> heap corruption SIGTRAP (exit 133). A named `ref Vec` param never hit this (routes to the non-dropping `compile_for_vec_var`). The vec_elem_types mono save/restore and compile_vec_method no-arm path (earlier suspects) were red herrings. FIX: a `SelfValue` arm in the for-loop dispatch routes a container `self` (String/Vec/Slice/Map/Set, registered under name 'self') to the SAME borrow-path iterators as a named Identifier. The five documented plumbing edits were also applied as designed (typechecker items.rs self-type guard adds Vec/VecDeque beside Column/Tensor; interpreter value_type_name Value::Array(_)=>"Vec"; codegen helpers.rs threads Vec/VecDeque self-args; collections.rs compile_index keys the Vec branch on container_recv/SelfValue; method_call.rs Vec dispatch falls through to the generic user-impl dispatch on the builtin no-arm Err when a `Vec.<method>` user fn exists). NOTE: the ledger's claimed 'builtin-reduction body' shape (`self.sum()`/`self.prod()`) is NOT supported — even a plain `v.sum()` method form loud-fails in codegen ('Vec/String method sum not yet supported'; only the free-fn `Stats.sum(v)` form lowers), so the realistic blanket-Vec impl body is a hand-written loop, which now works on all three surfaces (run == direct build == bound-generic mono build), i64 and heap `Vec[String]` elements, LSan-clean. Tests: e2e_blanket_vec_impl_loop_body_mono_dispatch + e2e_blanket_vec_string_impl_loop_body (tests/codegen.rs), asan_b06_5_blanket_vec_string_impl_loop_no_leak (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs).

title: Borrow-returning fn (`fn f(u: ref String) -> ref String { u }`, and the `ref Vec` analog) writes out of bounds and segfaults under -O2/LLJIT. The returned ref binding is lowered through the owned-value move-out path (the Vec/String arm of `zero_source_move_cap`, src/codegen/call_dispatch.rs), which GEPs field 2 (`cap`, offset 16) off `slot.ptr` and stores 8 bytes to null the moved-from source's cap. But a `ref Vec`/`ref String` param's slot is an 8-byte POINTER into the caller's frame, not a 24-byte {ptr,i64,i64} struct held inline, so the store lands 16 bytes past the alloca and corrupts the stack. Invisible under -O0 (frame slack absorbs the write); the optimizer weaponizes the UB into a crash under -O2, and LLJIT JIT-compiles at ~O2, so the program segfaults there -- surfacing as EMPTY stdout on the JIT execution lane while the AOT test oracle (which did not trigger the crash at its opt level) stayed green. Exactly the run/build divergence class the LLJIT-productionization spike targets, exposed by running the codegen E2E suite under KARAC_TEST_JIT=1 on Linux (5 `test_e2e_borrow_return_*` cases). Confirmed opt-dependent: the SAME emitted IR runs clean at `llc -O0` and segfaults at `llc -O2`. FIX (fddfb9a): mirror the struct arm's inline-ownership guard -- only zero `cap` when the slot holds the Vec/String struct INLINE (`slot.ty == vec_struct_type()`); a `ref` slot (pointer type) takes no ownership and is skipped (a borrow has nothing to move-null). No AOT regression -- codegen E2E 2084/2084 on both the AOT and JIT lanes.

Always-JIT execution lane produced EMPTY output for every non-trivial program on Linux/ELF. ORC's process-symbol-search generator (LLVMOrcCreateDynamicLibrarySearchGeneratorForProcess) resolves the statically-linked `karac_*` runtime FFI via dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT), which on ELF only sees symbols in the executable's DYNAMIC symbol table (.dynsym). Rust links executables without exporting their symbols, so all ~500 karac_runtime_* symbols lived only in .symtab (measured 0/507 in .dynsym) and the JIT failed to materialize `main` (`Symbols not found: [karac_runtime_scheduler_start_dispatcher, karac_runtime_park_slot_new, ...]`), producing empty stdout + a materialize error. Every codegen E2E execution test failed under KARAC_TEST_JIT=1 on Linux: 1416 failed / 649 passed (the 649 passing were IR-assertion tests that never execute). macOS masked it entirely -- Mach-O's dlsym resolves main-image symbols without an export flag, so all prior JIT work (done on macOS arm64) looked green; the spike's own 'green' claims were platform-blind. FIX (199098e): new build.rs emits `-Wl,--export-dynamic-symbol=karac_*` for the JIT-hosting binaries + integration-test binaries on ELF targets (scoped glob keeps .dynsym lean vs a blanket --export-dynamic; no-op on Mach-O/Windows). The in-process test binary (tests/lljit_prototype.rs) additionally force-links the runtime + defines KARAC_SPAWN_SITES stand-ins so its own .dynsym carries the symbols. Also flush libc/Rust stdio in the runner one-shot exit path (the repl/test-batch paths already did; returning ExitCode from main does not reliably run libc's at-exit flush on glibc). Result: codegen E2E via JIT on Linux 1416 failed -> 0 failed (2084/2084); lljit_prototype 15/15, lljit_e2e 16/16.

REPL cross-type rebind crashes the JIT runner on Linux (1 test: repl_jit_cross_type_rebind_uses_new_value). After `let x = 5` (i64) then `let x: String = "hello"; println(x)` in a later cell, the fresh runner subprocess dies with a signal (exit code None) instead of printing `hello`. The equivalent PLAIN program `fn main() { let x = 5; let x: String = "hello"; println(x); }` runs correctly under the interpreter, AOT (`karac build` prints `hello`, exit 0), AND a plain in-process JIT compile (pinned by tests/lljit_e2e.rs::jit_e2e_cross_type_shadow_rebind_prints_new_value), so this is specific to the REPL cell codegen path (compile_to_ir_for_repl_cell + persistent-let replay of the shadowed i64 `x` alongside the new String `x` in one synthesized body) executed under the ~O2 LLJIT on Linux. Likely another optimizer-exposed UB in the same class as B-2026-07-07-4 (a stale i64 slot / scope-exit cleanup applied to the String rebind), or a REPL snapshot-replay codegen gap. Only surfaced once the JIT lane started running on Linux (B-2026-07-07-5); macOS repl_jit was green (platform-blind). NARROW: cross-type same-name rebind across REPL cells; the codegen E2E proof gate (2084/2084) and the `karac test` JIT batch (491/491) are unaffected. Repro: `cargo test --features lljit_prototype --test repl_jit repl_jit_cross_type_rebind_uses_new_value`. Belongs to Slice 5 (repl/test JIT-default) Linux hardening. Next step: emit the failing cell's IR and bisect llc -O0 vs -O2 to localise (the technique that cracked B-2026-07-07-4). FIXED (8ab9e79): root cause is `try_emit_snapshot_capture` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) — it suppressed the source slot's `cap` (zeroing field 2 of a {ptr,i64,i64}) whenever the snapshot `kind` was String/Vec, but `snapshot_capture` is keyed by NAME, so the cross-type rebind fired the branch for the replayed i64 `let x = 5` binding too, GEP-ing offset 16 off an 8-byte `alloca i64` and corrupting the frame (PC=0 under the tighter LLJIT frame). Same class as B-2026-07-07-4, different site. Fix: guard the suppress by the slot's actual LLVM type (`slot.ty == vec_struct_type()`) — the i64 binding has no cap to suppress and is skipped. REPL-only path (snapshot_capture empty off the REPL), no AOT/build/run impact; codegen E2E stays 2084/2084 both lanes; repl_jit 22/23 -> 23/23. All Linux JIT suites now green.

Typechecker accepted a return-position `impl Trait` with MULTIPLE distinct concrete witnesses — a run-vs-build divergence. `fn pick(b: bool) -> impl Rate { if b { Fast {} } else { Slow {} } }` typechecked with 0 errors (each branch independently satisfies `Rate`), but design.md § `impl Trait` (Existential Types) requires "one concrete return per monomorphization": a return-position existential has a single witness type. The interpreter (`karac run`) ran it via dynamic dispatch and printed the right answer; `karac build`/JIT REJECTED it (`with_provider` form: "cannot infer concrete provider type"; direct method-call form: "no handler for method 'factor' on variable 'r'"). Surfaced while building the L632 (runtime-typed provider) workload for the LLJIT epic — L632 is one SYMPTOM of this broader gap (a runtime-typed provider IS a multi-witness `impl Trait`). `dyn Trait` (real dynamic dispatch) is P1-deferred (design.md:735), so the v1-correct fix is Option B: reject at typecheck AND make the error run-fatal (`E0271` / `TypeErrorKind::is_run_fatal`, mirroring `AtomicMissingOrdering`), so `karac run`'s lenient path rejects it too instead of downgrading to a warning and dynamic-dispatching. check/run/build now ALL reject at the same phase — the divergence is fully collapsed, not merely narrowed pending Slice 6. FIX (def4648): the typechecker pins a single witness per return-position existential — `note_return_impl_trait_witness` (hooked in `check_assignable`) records each concrete witness flowing into the current function's return existential (matched by `SpanKey` origin; skips other-existential / non-concrete / Never / Error found types so generics + diverging branches don't false-positive), and `check_impl_trait_single_witness` (end of `check_function`) emits `E_IMPL_TRAIT_MULTIPLE_WITNESSES` naming the distinct witnesses when 2+ are seen. Covers `if`/`match` tail branches and explicit `return`s (both funnel through the return-position assignability check); state saved/restored so nested items don't leak. Single-witness `impl Trait` (incl. the same type on every branch) is unaffected. Tests: `impl_trait_return_multiple_witnesses_rejected`, `_via_explicit_returns_rejected`, `_single_witness_across_branches_ok` (tests/typechecker.rs). Gates: typechecker 1924/1924, full non-llvm suite green, codegen E2E 2084/2084 (AOT), fmt + clippy clean. Subsumes the L632 follow-on (the divergent provider case is now a typecheck rejection).

Ownership-checker FALSE POSITIVE: a closure that captures a Copy scalar (e.g. `i64`) and is returned is rejected as an escaping `ref` capture, though the program is sound. Repro: `fn make(k: i64) -> Fn(i64) -> i64 { return |x| x + k; }` — `karac check` emits `error[ownership]: closure with `ref` capture of `k` escapes its scope by being returned — the borrow of `k` would outlive its source` (RefCaptureEscapesScope), yet `karac run` executes it correctly (`make(1)(10)` = 11, `make(2)(10)` = 12) because `k` is a trivially-copyable `i64`: the closure captures it BY VALUE (a copy lives in the closure env), so there is no borrow to dangle. ROOT CAUSE: the escape check in `src/ownership/closure_escape.rs` (the `for (cap_name, mode)` loop, ~L89–102 of `check_closure_ref_capture_escapes`) fires for ANY capture whose inferred mode is `Ref`/`MutRef` and whose source binding is not ITSELF a borrow (`Type::Ref`/`Type::MutRef`). A read-only capture of an owned `i64` defaults to `Ref` mode, and the check has no exemption for Copy/scalar capture sources — for which a by-ref default is semantically a by-value copy, incapable of outliving its source. FIX SKETCH: skip the escape error (or re-infer the capture as `Own`/copy) when the captured binding's type is a Copy scalar (i64/f64/bool/char/small enums with no heap payload); only heap-owning / borrow-backed captures can actually dangle. IMPACT: this is the `[[ownership-checker-open-false-positives]]` class the ownership-model-mechanization spike scopes out — it gates HEAP-ENV CLOSURES (`fn make(k) -> Fn(...)` stored in a `Vec[Fn]`) out of the Slice-1 drop-soundness fuzzer's valid-program generator, so those shapes are not exercised by the oracle↔codegen differential. Closing this FP re-admits heap-env closures to the generator (spike § Slice 1 “Deliberately excluded”) and lets the differential cover them. NOTE: `karac build`/`run` TOLERATE ownership errors by design (only `karac check` gates), so this FP blocks `check`-gated workflows, not execution. Distinct from the FIXED closure CODEGEN bugs B-2026-06-22-2/-4 (those were miscompiles; this is a static-checker over-rejection).

PERF codegen-gap (no correctness impact): karac fails to strength-reduce the loop-invariant-base linear expression in kata #63's obstacle predicate, so the C/Rust parity kata #62 won (bce_length_pin, B-2026-07-04-6) does NOT fully carry to the obstacle variant. Measured on the Apple M5 Pro reference machine (arm64, clang 21 / rustc 1.95), K=1_000_000 rolling obstacle-DP: karac retires 5.75B instructions vs rustc -O 4.24B and clang -O3 4.21B — 1.36x MORE instructions (wall 190.4ms vs 161.0/161.3ms = 1.18x; the wall gap is smaller than the instruction gap because karac's IPC is actually HIGHER, 6.27 vs 5.75 — the extra ops are cheap well-predicted branches, so instruction count is the honest signal here, not wall-time). EQUAL-SAFETY CONTROL rules out the overflow-safety artifact: rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on adds only 32M instructions (<1%), because LLVM strength-reduces the predicate FIRST and then proves the reduced induction variable cannot overflow and drops the checks. karac pays for the overflow checks precisely BECAUSE it failed to strength-reduce first. ROOT CAUSE (from side-by-side disassembly of both _main inner loops): karac emits ~17 instructions/cell vs rust ~12/cell. The predicate is (i*7 + c*3 + k) % 13 == 0. rust/LLVM maintains the dividend i*7+c*3+k as a SINGLE running register incremented by +3 each cell (add x15,x15,#3) — zero adds to reconstruct it — then does the %13 divisibility check (umulh/lsr/madd) directly on it. karac instead REBUILDS the dividend from three components every cell with overflow checks: `adds x13,x10,x12; b.vs` (i*7 + c*3) then `adds x14,x13,x19; b.vs` (+ k) — 4 instructions/cell that rust reduced to nothing, and amplified because each re-add drags an overflow-check branch. Two secondary misses in the same loop: (2) no paired-load fusion — dp[c-1] and dp[c] are adjacent, rust fuses them into one `ldp x1,x2,[x17,#-0x8]` while karac emits two separate `ldr`s plus an extra address add; (3) an overflow check on dp[c]+=dp[c-1] that LLVM proves away for rust (counts are bounded by the path-count ceiling) but karac keeps (`adds...b.vc`). The %13 magic-multiply itself is well lowered in both. FIX HYPOTHESIS: karac's IR for the loop-variant linear index/predicate expression is not in the canonical phi-based induction-variable form LLVM's IndVarSimplify + LSR can recognize, so the expression is never strength-reduced and the overflow checks are never proven away.
