Direction: lower loop-variant linear integer subexpressions as phi-carried induction variables (and/or emit nsw adds / order the checked-arith lowering after LLVM canonicalization) so LSR can fold the re-adds and the no-overflow proof can drop the checks — the same class of frontend-IR-shape fix as bce_length_pin was for bounds checks. NEXT STEP: dump karac's -emit-llvm for unique_paths_ii.kara, diff the inner-loop IR against rustc's, confirm the missing induction-variable canonicalization, and check whether emitting the predicate dividend as an incremented phi lets LLVM reduce+prove it. NOTE: this replaced the kata's earlier cloud-container snapshot (x86-64) where karac read as 0.95x rust — that parity was an x86/container artifact and does not hold on the M5 corpus machine; the kata README's Benchmarks tables + parity prose still quote the stale container numbers and need an M5 refresh (separate follow-on). Evidence + full instruction/cycle table live in the kata README bench section. Repro: cd kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/63-unique-paths-ii/bench && ./bench.sh (then /usr/bin/time -l on target/unique_paths_ii_kara vs _c / the rust bins). FIXED (ac06b64b): the dominant cause — the strength-reduction miss (sub-issue 1) — is resolved by broadening karac's monotone-counter `llvm.assume(x >= init)` emission from index-only counters to ALL non-poisoned monotone counters (src/codegen/control_flow_bce.rs: drop the `index_used` gate + dead `collect_index_idents`). The obstacle predicate's `i`/`k` counters were used only in arithmetic, not as array indices, so they never got the non-negativity fact; without it LLVM couldn't prove `i*7+c*3+k` in-range and kept 2 `sadd.with.overflow` per cell, which blocked reassociation (hoisting invariant `i*7+k`) and forced signed `srem`. With the assume, the inner loop now matches rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on EXACTLY: one plain `add nuw nsw` + `urem`; overflow checks 5->3 (remaining 3 = `dp[c]+=dp[c-1]` + hash sink, which rust keeps too — equal-safety parity). Measured M5 seq: instructions 5.75B->5.19B, wall 190.4->178.8ms (1.18x->1.11x vs rust), sigma 10.9->1.1ms (the eliminated branches were the variance source). CORRECTION to the original diagnosis: sub-issue (3) `dp[c]+=dp[c-1]` overflow check was NOT a gap — rust-ofl keeps it too; it only vanishes under rust's wrapping release build. Sub-issue (2) paired-load fusion is a minor LLVM-backend residual. The remaining 1.11x wall gap is a SEPARATE Vec-fill/alloc idiom gap (kata's `Vec.new()`+push-loop with capacity-doubling reallocs vs rust's `vec![0;n]` single alloc_zeroed) — opened as [[B-2026-07-08-7]]. General fix: helps every loop whose monotone counter feeds arithmetic, not just kata #63. Gates: codegen E2E 2086/2086, par_codegen 153/153, fmt+clippy clean; kata sink 583461340 identical under run/build/KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0.
investigation: 2026-07-08 reproduction attempt (cloud x86-64 container, LLVM 18): NOT REPRODUCED on x86-64 or any arm64 cost-model LLVM 18 can target (cortex-a710/x2, apple-m1/m2/m3 — the closest proxy to M5, which postdates LLVM 18). Method: dumped karac's pre-opt LLVM IR for two faithful reconstructions of the hot loop — (1) the synthetic obstacle predicate (i*7+c*3+k)%13==0 + rolling 1-D DP dp[c]+=dp[c-1], and (2) a flattened 2-D obstacle grid grid[i*n+c] — and ran karac's exact pipeline (opt -O2 -> llc, same LLVM 18 karac links). Findings, consistent across ALL modeled arm64 cores incl. apple-m3: the linear dividend/flat-index is STRENGTH-REDUCED to a single running induction-variable register (sub/add of the constant stride per cell, used directly in the %13 madd — NO rebuild-from-3-components), the loop-invariant part (i*7+k / i*n) is HOISTED to the outer-loop preheader, and the overflow-check branches on the linear expression are PROVEN AWAY (all nsw, no trap edges). Measured ~9-12 instr/cell, at parity with rust — the 17-instr/cell defect described above did not appear. So the ledger's root cause (checked arithmetic defeating LSR/SCEV) is already handled: LLVM's O2 pipeline recovers the IV form from karac's current checked-arith IR; the fix hypothesis is effectively already realized. The only surviving overflow check is on dp[c]+dp[c-1] (memory-loaded values, genuinely unprovable without value-range info — rust with overflow-checks=on keeps it too; ledger point 3's 'rust proves it away' only holds for grid dimensions small enough that LLVM can bound the sum, which it cannot do in general). RESIDUAL: the gap is either (a) a stale measurement predating a prior IV/nsw codegen improvement, or (b) apple-M5-microarchitecture-specific LSR cost-model behavior that LLVM 18 cannot model. LEFT OPEN pending a re-run of kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/63-unique-paths-ii/bench/bench.sh with the CURRENT karac on the M5 corpus machine; if instr/cell is now ~12, close as already-resolved. No speculative codegen change pushed — the current x86/modeled-arm64 output is already optimal and a pre-reduction change risks regressing it.

title: [FIXED] Raw-pointer instance methods (`.offset` / `.read` / `.write`) are spec'd (design.md § raw pointers, L3319/L3337) but unimplemented in codegen: `p.offset(i)` fails with 'no handler for method offset on variable p', and `.write` on a non-identifier receiver (a chained `p.offset(i).write(v)`) fails with 'no handler for method write on non-identifier receiver'. Blocks the sound *manual* FFI ownership-handoff round-trip (allocate a raw buffer in Kāra, fill it via pointer writes, hand the pointer to C, free via a Kāra export) — the alternative to the auto-boxing/auto-destructor sugar for additive-interop Slice 4. Also `p[i] = x` index-store on a raw pointer is rejected ('Index store on non-array type'). Fix: add dispatcher arms in `compile_method_call` for the inherent pointer methods on `*const T`/`*mut T` (offset/read/write/read_unaligned/write_volatile), including chained (non-identifier) receivers. Found while building the producer-mode FFI handoff; `forget` (the handoff primitive) shipped, but the round-trip needs these.

fix: FIXED (LLJIT Slice 6c prereq). ROOT CAUSE: `compile_for` (src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs) peeled `.iter()`/`.into_iter()`/`.chars()`/`.bytes()`/`.step_by()` off the for-loop iterable but had NO arm for `.enumerate()`, so `for (i,v) in xs.iter().enumerate()` landed on the dispatcher's silent `_ =>` skip-body arm — the loop body was never emitted, so EVERY outer-variable mutation inside an enumerate loop was lost under codegen (not Map-specific: a plain `sum = sum + v` accumulator also stayed 0). NARROWED via: plain `.iter()` loop persists (len=3), enumerate loop does not (len=0); a plain-i64 `sum`/`count` accumulator in an enumerate loop reads 0 vs the interpreter's 60/5. FIX: added an `.enumerate()` arm to `compile_for` that recognises a 2-tuple pattern over an indexable receiver (`for_receiver_is_indexable`: Vec/Slice/array var), stashes the index sub-pattern in a new `Codegen.enumerate_index_pattern` field, and recurses on the inner `.iter()` receiver with the ELEMENT sub-pattern. The underlying `compile_for_{vec,slice,array}_var` loops already carry the storage index as their induction variable `cur` — which IS the enumerate index — so each now calls `bind_enumerate_index(cur)` right after binding the element, which `take()`s the stashed pattern (so nested loops don't inherit it) and binds it. Codegen-only: the tree-walk interpreter was already correct and is untouched. VERIFIED: examples/leetcode/two_sum.kara now prints `Found: nums[0]+nums[1]=9` under interp, AOT, and JIT (was `No solution` under AOT/JIT); full codegen suite 2086/2086, memory_sanitizer 557/0, new regression test `e2e_enumerate_loop_body_and_outer_mutations_execute` in tests/codegen.rs. COVERAGE: `.iter().enumerate()` over a Vec/Slice/array VARIABLE and over a struct FIELD (`obj.field.iter().enumerate()`, via the field-iter synth path) are both fixed. Still falling through to the prior skip (unchanged, rarer, follow-on): enumerate over array-LITERALS, index receivers, and Map/Set/String receivers.
title: SILENT WRONG OUTPUT (exit 0): under codegen (both `karac build` AOT and the Slice-6b `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1` JIT path), a `Map` insertion performed inside a `match m.get(k) { ... None => { m.insert(...) } }` arm WITHIN A LOOP is lost across iterations — a later iteration's `m.get()` misses the key that was inserted, so the canonical get-then-insert-in-loop Map idiom silently produces wrong results. `examples/leetcode/two_sum.kara` is the flagship case: the tree-walk INTERPRETER prints the correct `Found: nums[0]+nums[1]=9` (2+7=9), but the compiled binary AND the JIT print `No solution found` for every case, at exit 0. This is a genuine interpreter-vs-codegen divergence — surfaced by LLJIT Slice 6b routing `karac run` onto the same codegen as `karac build` (lenient interpreter-run had hidden it; the epic exists to expose exactly this class). AOT and JIT agree, so the defect is codegen-side, NOT JIT-specific. MINIMAL REPRO (mapiter.kara): `let mut seen: Map[i64,i64] = Map.new(); for (i, num) in [2,7,11,15].iter().enumerate() { let complement = 9 - num; match seen.get(complement) { Some(j) => println(f"pair {j},{i}"), None => { let _ = seen.insert(num, i); } } } println("done");` — interp prints `pair 0,1` then `done`; AOT prints only `done`. NARROWING (each tested interp==AOT except the last): (a) `m.insert(k,v)` then `m.get(k)` with NO loop and NO match -> WORKS (got 42); (b) insert in a PLAIN loop `for k in 0..3 { m.insert(k, k*10) }` then get OUTSIDE -> WORKS (got 10); (c) insert inside a `match m.get(5) { None => { m.insert(5,99) } }` arm with NO loop, then get again -> WORKS (post 99); (d) insert inside a `match m.get(k)` None-arm INSIDE a loop, get in the next iteration -> FAILS (miss). So the trigger is specifically loop + match-on-get + insert-in-arm together — likely the `match seen.get(complement)` scrutinee holds a borrow/temporary of the map (or a snapshot of its buffer pointer) across the arm, and the `seen.insert` in the None arm mutates through a path codegen doesn't reflect back into the loop-carried map slot (stale header/length, or the get's temporary aliases a pre-insert buffer that a realloc-on-insert leaves dangling). SEVERITY high: exit 0 with wrong output is the worst failure class and this is a textbook idiom. NEXT STEP: dump codegen IR for mapiter.kara, inspect how `match m.get(k)` lowers the scrutinee temporary vs the loop-carried map slot, and whether `insert`'s potential realloc updates the slot the next iteration's `get` reads. Repro files were in scratch during the Slice-6b verification; reconstruct from the snippet above.

FIXED (both legs). A function that returns an owned, heap-owning STRUCT param leaked the arg buffer under `karac build`. NON-GENERIC leg (776169c): the caller's return-passthrough guard (`call_arg_flows_into_return`, B-2026-07-01-7) skipped the arg-temp drop when the callee CAN return the param, on the premise the callee FORWARDS the buffer — but a copy-supported heap struct param is ENTRY-COPIED at the callee (`make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` -> `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place`), so the callee returns an INDEPENDENT copy and the original moved-in buffer (caller already suppressed its cleanup as a move) is orphaned. Fix: `arg_is_entry_copied_heap_struct` (call_dispatch.rs) mirrors the callee's entry-copy predicate; on the passthrough path the caller still registers the struct drop for an entry-copied arg (forwarded params — bare String/Vec, Map/shared/non-copyable Option, user-Drop via a Call arg — unaffected, preserving the B-2026-07-01-7 double-drop guard). GENERIC/MONO leg (this commit): the monomorphized call path had NO ownership handling — `compile_mono_function` (mono.rs) registered no owned-aggregate param drop (unlike `compile_function`) so a non-returned owned heap-struct param was never freed, and `compile_generic_call` did no caller-side arg-temp registration (unlike `compile_call`), so std.cmp `min`/`max`/`clamp[T: Ord]` over a heap Ord type leaked every arg buffer. Fix brings the mono path to ownership parity: (A) `compile_mono_function`'s param prologue calls `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` for bare (non-ref) struct/enum params at the CONCRETE monomorph type (from `var_type_names`) — the returned param is move-suppressed by the existing `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return`; (B) `compile_generic_call` registers the caller's arg-temp drop (`track_inline_owned_aggregate_arg`) gated by `!call_arg_flows_into_return || arg_is_entry_copied_heap_struct`, the same rule as `compile_call`. A+B are paired (the callee returns an independent copy, so the caller's original is orphaned without B). Ground-truthed balanced via a malloc interposer: generic `min(Name,Name)` = 4 mallocs / 4 frees; `gid`/`gpick` balanced. Validation: memory_sanitizer 559 (incl. the three new regression tests: asan_return_owned_heap_struct_param_no_leak, asan_generic_fn_returns_owned_heap_struct_param_no_leak, asan_std_cmp_min_max_clamp_heap_ord_no_leak) + codegen 2088 + par_codegen + lljit_e2e + all non-llvm phase suites green; NO double-free regressions. The par cooperative-cancel tests (test_e2e_par_branch_(err)defer_fires_on_cooperative_cancel) are pre-existing timing-flaky under parallel load — they flake on BASE too (3/4) and the affected program's binary is byte-identical with/without this change, so unrelated. SECONDARY (still open, separate): the ownership checker treats a generic trait-method value arg as a MOVE not a borrow — a user-written generic `min` (`match a.cmp(b) {...}`) is rejected at `karac check`; the baked stdlib min/max/clamp compile only because baked stdlib bypasses ownershipcheck. That is a checker-precision gap, not a codegen leak, and does not affect the shipped stdlib functions.

fix: this commit (characterization — no code change; Linux glibc shows karac already wins, macOS-only platform-allocator artifact, allocator surgery unwarranted)
title: PERF codegen-gap (no correctness impact): karac's `Vec.new()` + push-loop fill does not lower to a single sized zeroed allocation the way rust's `vec![0; n]` does — surfaced as the residual gap after B-2026-07-08-3 fixed kata #63's obstacle-predicate strength-reduction. On M5 seq, kata #63 is 1.11x wall / 1.23x instructions (5.19B vs rust 4.24B) behind AFTER the predicate loop reached exact rust parity, and the remaining delta localizes to the per-call dp-buffer construction. The kata builds dp via `let mut dp = Vec.new(); while j < cols { dp.push(0); j+=1 }`; at -O2 this lowers to an empty alloc (cap 4) plus a fill loop that, per element, tests `needs_grow` (j==cap) and on hit doubles capacity via `umax(2j,4)` and reallocs — so for the swept n in [2,33] each of the 1,000,000 calls does ~log2(n) reallocs (cap 4->8->16->32) + n grow-checks + n stores. Rust's `vec![0; cols]` is one `__rust_alloc_zeroed` (calloc) + zero fill, no per-element grow check, no reallocs. Same allocation-traffic class as the known OPEN Vec/String alloc gap (#405, ~2.7x rust). FIX DIRECTIONS (not yet chosen): (a) recognize the `Vec.new()` + counted `push(const)` loop idiom and rewrite to `with_capacity(n)` + memset (frontend peephole); (b) teach `push` codegen / the growth policy to presize when the loop trip count is a known SCEV; or (c) provide/lower a `Vec.filled(n, x)` builtin the kata + mirrors can call, matching rust's `vec![x; n]`. NOTE the kata<->mirror idiom asymmetry: the kara source uses a push-loop while the rust/c/go mirrors use sized fills, so this also affects benchmark apples-to-apples — worth a mirror/idiom review when fixing. Cross-ref [[B-2026-07-08-3]] (the now-fixed predicate half). Repro: cd kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/63-unique-paths-ii/bench && ./bench.sh; compare instructions-retired of target/unique_paths_ii_kara vs the rust bin (the predicate loop is now identical, so the delta is the fill). REOPENED 2026-07-08 (revert 3a4e6135): the 546a8c2 fix was REVERTED — bisect-confirmed it REGRESSED its own target kata #63 by +8.4% instructions (5.19B->5.62B, deterministic; neutral on #64). The fill peephole rewrote #63's `Vec.new()+push(0)` dp buffer to `Vec.filled`, and that construction change broke the obstacle-predicate BCE/strength-reduction that B-2026-07-08-3 (ac06b64b) installed. CORRECTED DIAGNOSIS (the original reallocs premise was wrong): (1) reallocs are NOT the gap — `with_capacity` presizing gives zero improvement; (2) kāra's `Vec.new()+push-loop` codegen is EXCELLENT — 3.7x FASTER than rust's own manual push-loop (52 vs 194 ms on a 2M-iter fill+sum), so kāra's Vec is not deficient; (3) `Vec.filled` and the push-loop have NEAR-EQUAL instruction counts (~1.3B), and LLVM's LoopIdiomRecognize already converts kāra's fill store-loop to memset at -O2 (verified in IR — an explicit memset emit is redundant, no-op); (4) the ONLY reason rust wins #63 is its mirror uses `vec![0;n]` (`__rust_alloc_zeroed`/calloc) while the kata uses a push-loop — a benchmark idiom asymmetry, not a kāra codegen deficiency; (5) the true residual (kāra 53 ms / 1.32B vs rust 44 ms / 1.30B on the fill+sum micro, NEAR-EQUAL instructions but ~1.2x wall) is ALLOCATOR/MEMORY-bound (IPC) — rust's alloc_zeroed + allocator throughput vs kāra's malloc/free churn on 2M tiny per-call buffers — i.e. the [[#405]] Vec/String alloc class, NOT fill codegen. NET: the peephole addressed the wrong root cause AND regressed the target kata, so it is reverted; `Vec.filled` (the method) and the B-2026-07-08-10 stride test are retained. PROPER FIX (if pursued): profile the small-per-call-buffer alloc/free path (bump/arena allocator or per-call buffer reuse); LOW priority — it is a ~1.2x memory-bound gap on a micro-pattern, and kāra's Vec codegen itself is already strong. Any future fill peephole MUST A/B instructions on #63 (not just fill-only microbenchmarks) to avoid re-breaking the scan BCE. SLICE 2026-07-09 (safe half of fix-direction (c), NO auto-peephole): added a `calloc`-backed zeroed-alloc runtime entry point `karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic(count, size)` (runtime/src/alloc.rs) + the `__karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic64` wasm i64-dims shim (wasm_alloc.rs), and taught `build_vec_filled` (src/codegen/collections.rs) to lower an explicit `Vec.filled(n, 0)` — any statically all-zero-bits fill of a trivially-copyable element — to it, SKIPPING the malloc + runtime fill loop entirely. This matches rust's `vec![0; n]` (`__rust_alloc_zeroed`/calloc, OS-lazily-zeroed pages), closing the specific rust advantage the corrected diagnosis pinned in points (4)/(5) for the zeroed-fill idiom. Gated STRICTLY on a compile-time zero (int/bool/float all-zero-bits), so it never rewrites anyone's `Vec.new()+push` construction (unlike the reverted 546a8c2 auto-peephole) and therefore cannot re-break #63's obstacle-predicate BCE. IMPORTANT — this does NOT close the kata-#63 residual: #63 must keep its push-loop dp buffer (for the BCE), so its remaining gap is still the allocator-bound tiny-per-call-buffer churn (the LOW-priority [[#405]] Vec/String arena/reuse work), untouched here. Verified: runtime unit tests (calloc non-null + fully zeroed + degenerate 0-dims), IR (zero -> `karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic`, no `filled.body`; non-zero -> keeps the fill loop, regression guard), E2E all-zeros readback, ASAN calloc buffer freed once (no leak/UAF), both wasm clippy arms green. Status stays OPEN pending the #405 allocator work. RE-VERIFIED 2026-07-08 (current main, fresh archives): #63 push-loop = 5.19B instructions (1.22x vs rust-ofl 4.26B; 1.11x wall) — the ac06b64b baseline HOLDS, NO regression. Independently re-confirmed on current karac that the `Vec.filled(cols,0)` construction REGRESSES #63 to 5.60B (breaks the obstacle-predicate BCE), so the push-loop stays the optimal shape and there is NO codegen win left to grab; the 1.22x residual is genuinely allocator/memory-bound (~1M tiny per-call dp buffers) = the broad #405 class. TAKE (recommendation): not worth #63-scoped allocator surgery; the fix belongs to a dedicated, corpus-validated #405 allocation-throughput slice — prefer a runtime small-object size-class free-list (Linux-LSan-gated) over escape-analysis->alloca. Two MEASUREMENT/ENV gotchas surfaced and fixed while taking this: (a) `karac build -o PATH` IGNORES -o and emits ./<stem> to CWD, so measuring a pre-existing target/ binary silently reads a STALE artifact (a Vec.filled-era 5.60B build here) and fabricates a phantom regression — a 5-build git-bisect chased it before catch; always rebuild to a clean dir and verify the sink. (b) installed archives at ~/.local/lib + ~/.cargo/lib were STALE (missing karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic from 6d068281), so EVERY Vec.filled(n,0) program failed to link with the installed toolchain — rebuilt lean+full and refreshed both install dirs + target/release; verified a Vec.filled program links via default resolution again.
resolution: FIXED for concrete 64-bit-element (i64/u64/usize) Vecs (546a8c2): a new pre-size rewrite `fill_to_filled` (src/presize.rs) collapses `Vec.new()` + an EXACT counted `push(<integer literal>)` fill (both `for I in 0..BOUND` and `while IV<BOUND` with IV proven 0/step+1) to a single `Vec.filled(BOUND, LIT)` — fix direction (c), driven from the frontend peephole of direction (a). At -O2 this lowers to `alloc + memset` (the `vec![0;n]` shape): NO per-element grow check (the residual the `with_capacity` pre-size left — its self-referential capacity phi defeats SCEV), NO reallocs, and because `Vec.filled` sets len==BOUND directly, LLVM then proves idx<len and elides the downstream DP-loop bounds checks itself (subsuming the bce length-pin for this shape). Verified on x86-64 + LLVM-18-modeled arm64 (apple-m3): rolling-DP `dp[c]=dp[c]+dp[c-1]` inner loop drops from 6 pre-opt bounds checks + grow check to ZERO. Correctness checked interp==compiled across for/while/counter-used-after(fixup)/small-bound shapes. SCOPE: the rewrite fires only when the element type resolves to a concrete 64-bit int — narrow elements (Vec[i32] etc.) are EXCLUDED because `Vec.filled` itself mis-sizes narrow buffers (the compiled i64 literal vs i32 element stride) — that latent codegen bug is filed as B-2026-07-08-10; and UN-ANNOTATED bindings (element still a type variable at the `Vec.new()` initializer) fall back to the correct `with_capacity` path. So an un-annotated kata `dp` needs a `: Vec[i64]` annotation (or the B-2026-07-08-10 fix) to reach the memset lowering. M5 re-run of the kata bench recommended to confirm the wall/instruction delta closes for the annotated form. RESOLVED 2026-07-10 (Linux x86-64 / glibc measurement — the AUTHORITATIVE CI-gate platform): the gap DOES NOT EXIST on Linux; it was an Apple-M5/macOS-allocator artifact, not a karac codegen or allocator deficiency, so NO fix is warranted. Deterministic callgrind Ir + min-of-9 wall on this box (kata #63, the exact bench source, 1M-iter sweep): karac 5.186B Ir / 584 ms; rust -O 5.549B Ir / 745 ms; rust -O3 -Ctarget-cpu=native 5.261B Ir / 656 ms. karac wins on BOTH instructions and wall at EVERY rust opt level (the ledger's prior '1.22x behind, 5.19B vs rust 4.24B' was M5/arm64/macOS — the 4.24B is unreachable on glibc). callgrind function breakdown shows WHY the sign flips: karac's allocator cost is malloc 45M + _int_free 54M ~= 99M Ir (1.9%), served from glibc's per-thread TCACHE fast path; rust's vec![0;n] -> calloc bypasses tcache into _int_malloc 110M + calloc 68M + _int_free / _int_free_merge_chunk / _int_free_maybe_consolidate ~140M ~= 320M Ir (5.8%). So on glibc the allocator is karac's ADVANTAGE, the inverse of macOS libmalloc (nano + lazy-zeroed pages) where calloc is cheap. A matched-idiom Vec fill+sum micro (BOTH sides Vec::new()+push-loop, 2M iters) is even starker: karac 63 ms vs rust 259 ms = 4.1x FASTER on Linux, confirming the entry's own note that karac's push-loop Vec codegen is excellent (not the deficient side). DECISION: do NOT build the proposed thread-local small-object free-list. It would give ZERO Linux benefit (karac malloc is already 0.87% via tcache), risk REGRESSING that tcache fast path, and at best help a macOS micro-pattern that (a) is a ~1.1x platform-allocator gap on ONE idiom, (b) is already matched by the landed Vec.filled(n,0)->calloc lowering wherever a static zero fill applies, and (c) cannot be validated on the Linux memory-sanitizer/bench CI gate. Severity downgraded high->low and status closed as a platform-allocator characterization: there is no actionable codegen/allocator work on the authoritative gate. Reproduce: kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/63-unique-paths-ii/bench, build the .kara and .rs, `valgrind --tool=callgrind` for Ir + min-of-9 `time` for wall on any glibc host.

source: example:leetcode/valid_parentheses + LLJIT Slice 6c run-JIT blast-radius sweep
title: CORRECTED ROOT CAUSE + kata fixed. Original report ('escaped f-string braces {{}} render wrong') was WRONG about the mechanism: Kāra's f-string brace escape is BACKSLASH — `\{` / `\}` — NOT the Python/Rust `{{` / `}}`. `\{\}` renders literal `{}` correctly in BOTH interp and codegen (verified). A bare `{` always opens an interpolation, so `f"a{{}}b"` parses as `a` + interpolate(`{}`) + `b`, where `{}` is an empty BLOCK expression of type unit — the kata author used the wrong (Python-convention) escape. examples/leetcode/valid_parentheses.kara used `{{}}`/`{{[]}}` expecting literal braces; FIXED to `\{\}`/`\{[]\}` (now interp == AOT == the Expected-output comment). RESIDUAL REAL BUG (low severity, degenerate — reachable only by deliberately interpolating a unit value, e.g. `f"{ {} }"` or `f"{()}"`): displaying UNIT diverges between backends — the interpreter renders `()`, codegen renders `0`. Neither is clearly 'right' (displaying unit is nonsensical). RECOMMENDED FIX: make a unit-typed f-string interpolation a TYPE ERROR (unit is not Display) in the typechecker — closes the divergence at the source for both backends and rejects the meaningless case; alternatively make codegen render unit as `()` to match interp. Not an LLJIT regression; predates the epic. Downgraded to low severity now that the only real-world occurrence (the kata) is fixed.
resolution: 2026-07-08 FIXED (the residual unit-Display divergence). `type_supports_display` (src/typechecker/derives.rs) listed `Type::Unit => true`, so a unit-typed f-string interpolation slipped past the interpolation Display check (src/typechecker/exprs.rs) and then rendered DIFFERENTLY per backend — interpreter `()`, codegen `0`. Changed to `Type::Unit => false`: unit is not Display (Rust has no `Display for ()` either), so the existing check now rejects it at typecheck with `type '()' does not implement Display; cannot interpolate in f-string` for BOTH the explicit `f"{()}"` and the degenerate `f"a{{}}b"` (where `{}` parses as an empty unit block). This closes the run-vs-build divergence at the source and turns the meaningless case into a clear error across every Display context (f-string / println / to_string). The kata (valid_parentheses) was already corrected to the backslash escape `\{`/`\}` earlier. Regression test: tests/typechecker.rs::test_unit_is_not_display_in_fstring. Full suite green after the change (nothing legitimately displayed unit): typechecker 1926, lib 863, interpreter 469, codegen 2097, clippy both cfgs + fmt clean.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (unit Display)

source: example:leetcode/lru_cache + LLJIT Slice 6c run-JIT blast-radius sweep
title: CODEGEN GAP (blocks Slice 6c): built-in `Option[T]` / `Result[T,E]` values have NO Display support under codegen — neither in an f-string (`f"{opt}"`) NOR via `println(opt)` — while the interpreter renders them correctly (`Some(7)` / `None` / `Ok(..)` / `Err(..)`). `karac build`/JIT abort with `error: codegen failed: Display of a struct ... is supported when the argument is a variable or field access ... bind ... to a `let` first (user-struct Display, subtask-5 follow-on)`. IMPORTANT — that error message is MISLEADING for this case: binding to a `let` does NOT help. Verified: `let x = get(1); f"{x}"` (Option via variable) fails identically to `f"{get(1)}"` (Option via call result); `println(x)` fails too. The message is accurate only for USER structs/enums (which do render via var/field), not for the built-in Option/Result enums, which no Display arm handles — so an Option value (lowered to a tagged struct) falls through `fstr_render_part` (src/codegen/runtime.rs ~6417) and the `println` struct-arg path (~method_call.rs) to the generic struct-value error. ROOT CAUSE: `src/codegen/synth_display.rs` synthesizes Display fns for structs / Vec / Map / Set (`emit_display_fn_for_type`, `emit_vec_display_body`, `emit_map_display_fn`, `emit_set_display_fn`) but has NO Option/Result arm, and `expr_user_enum_name{,_any}` only recognize USER enums, so the built-in Option/Result enums route nowhere. FIX (a bounded codegen slice, ~150-250 lines + tests): add `emit_option_display` / `emit_result_display` (or a general built-in-enum Display) that loads the discriminant/tag, branches None -> literal "None" and Some -> "Some(" + <recurse: the payload's own per-type Display fn> + ")" (and Ok/Err similarly), reusing the existing per-type Display-fn cache for the payload; then route Option/Result exprs to it from BOTH `fstr_render_part` and the `println` struct-arg path BEFORE they hit the generic struct-value error. TEST MATRIX: Option[i64], Option[String], Option[user-struct], Some/None, Result[T,E] Ok/Err, and a nested Option[Option[i64]] / Vec[Option[i64]]. Also fix the misleading error message. This is one of the ~16 codegen gaps the Slice-6c blast-radius sweep surfaced (blocks lru_cache and any program that prints an Option/Result); closing it is prerequisite work for the 6c JIT-default flip. Not an LLJIT regression — a pre-existing codegen-vs-interpreter gap the epic's run->codegen routing exposed.
investigation: 2026-07-08 deep dive (traced the full Display path): the general enum-Display synthesizer `emit_enum_display_fn` (src/codegen/synth_display.rs) ALREADY handles Unit/Tuple/Struct variants and recurses into each payload field via `emit_enum_field_display` -> the field type's own Display fn — so Some(T)/None and Ok(T)/Err(E) are structurally covered. The blocker is TYPE PLUMBING, not the render logic: `expr_user_enum_name_any` explicitly excludes Option/Result (`tn != "Option" && tn != "Result"`) because they are GENERIC built-ins — the variant def is `Some(T)`, so `emit_enum_field_display` would call `llvm_type_for_type_expr(T)` on the unsubstituted generic `T`, not the concrete payload type. To render `Option[i64]` vs `Option[String]` the Display fn must know the CONCRETE payload TypeExpr, and codegen has no ready accessor: it consumes purpose-built side-tables derived from `TypeCheckResult.expr_types` in the LOWERING pass (e.g. `var_elem_type_exprs` for Vec element types), and there is NO Option/Result-payload-TypeExpr table. That is why even the VARIABLE case (`let x = get(1); f"{x}"`) fails, not just call results. CONCRETE IMPLEMENTATION PATH (a real cross-cutting slice, ~2-3 files): (1) in the lowering pass, populate a new side-table from expr_types mapping Option/Result exprs (by var name AND by span) to concrete payload/ok/err TypeExprs; (2) add `emit_option_display_te(payload_te)` / `emit_result_display_te(ok_te, err_te)` modeled on `emit_vec_display_fn_te` (concrete-type-keyed cache; load tag word 0; None -> "None", Some -> "Some(" + payload Display + ")"; Ok/Err similarly); (3) route Option/Result exprs to them from `fstr_render_part` (runtime.rs ~6360-6428) and the `println` struct-arg path BEFORE the generic struct-value error, spilling non-place values to an alloca (as `render_user_enum_display` already does). Reuse `Self::option_payload_te` (param_own.rs:1257) to split `Option[T]`. The render machinery is done; the work is the payload-TypeExpr plumbing through lowering. Also fix the misleading 'bind to a let first' error message — the let does not help for Option/Result.
resolution: 2026-07-08 FIXED for the variable/place case (inline-primitive payloads). Implemented the payload-TypeExpr plumbing the investigation called for: (1) typechecker `record_pattern_binding_surface_types` (src/typechecker/patterns.rs) now records Option/Result let-binding surface types into pattern_binding_inner_types, mirroring Map/Set; (2) codegen let-path (src/codegen/stmts.rs) routes those through `register_var_from_type_expr`, which (src/codegen/types_lowering.rs) populates two new side-tables `var_option_payload_te` / `var_result_payload_te` (src/codegen.rs) via `Self::option_payload_te` / new `Self::result_payload_tes` (src/codegen/param_own.rs); (3) new `emit_option_display_te` / `emit_result_display_te` (src/codegen/synth_display.rs) load the tag word, branch None->"None"/Some->"Some("+payload+")" (Ok/Err likewise), reusing `emit_enum_field_display` for the payload; (4) both `try_compile_collection_display` (f-string path) and `compile_print` (println path, src/codegen/control_flow.rs) dispatch to them. Payloads are guarded by `is_inline_displayable_payload` (primitives + String/str/char/bool only) to avoid invalid IR on compound (boxed/wide-struct) payloads, which the 4-i64 aggregate reload cannot reconstruct — those still fall through to the (now-accurate) struct-Display error. Regression test: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_option_result_display_matches_interpreter (Option[i64]/Option[String]/Result[i64,String] via both f-string and println, interp==AOT). FOLLOW-ON (still open, tracked here): (a) the CALL-RESULT case `f"{cache.get(1)}"` / `println(cache.get(1))` is NOT yet supported — needs a span-keyed (not just var-name-keyed) payload-TypeExpr lookup so a bare call expr can find its concrete payload type; lru_cache still can't print an inline `cache.get(N)` (and has a separate pre-existing all-None logic bug). (b) compound (non-inline) payloads: Option[user-struct-with-Display] etc. still error. The 'bind to a let first' hint is now ACCURATE for Option/Result primitive payloads (binding does help). | 2026-07-08 FOLLOW-UP: the CALL-RESULT case is now ALSO fixed (`f"{get(1)}"` / `println(get(1))` with no intervening `let`). Added a span-addressed payload table `Program.display_option_result_types` (forwarded from `TypeCheckResult.expr_types` in the lowering pass, filtered to `Type::Named{name:"Option"|"Result"}`) → codegen side-table `display_option_result_types` → new `try_compile_option_result_display(expr)` (src/codegen/synth_display.rs) which splits the payload (same `is_inline_displayable_payload` guard), spills the non-place call value to an alloca (mirrors `render_user_enum_display`), and renders via `emit_option_display_te`/`emit_result_display_te`. Dispatched from both `fstr_render_part` (src/codegen/runtime.rs) and `compile_print` (src/codegen/control_flow.rs) after the variable-case arms. Also swapped into the stdlib-body span-table swap. Regression test: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_option_result_call_result_display_matches_interpreter. STILL OPEN follow-on: compound (non-inline) payloads (Option[user-struct-with-Display] etc.). NOTE: `examples/leetcode/lru_cache.kara` still does not build — but NOT for a Display reason anymore: it hit a SEPARATE pre-existing codegen bug (a struct with a `Map` field constructed inside an associated `new()` emits invalid IR) filed as B-2026-07-08-12, plus a pre-existing interpreter all-None logic bug. The Display gap this entry tracks is closed.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Display)

title: SILENT WRONG OUTPUT (exit 0): `Vec.filled(n, val)` (and the `Vec[val; n]` repeat literal, which shares `build_vec_filled`) mis-sizes the buffer for a NARROW (sub-64-bit) element type. `build_vec_filled` (src/codegen/collections.rs) sizes the allocation and the fill stride from `val.get_type()` — the COMPILED value type — not the destination element type. A bare integer literal compiles to i64, so `let v: Vec[i32] = Vec.filled(5, 7)` allocates + fills at 8-byte (i64) stride while every `v[i]` read GEPs at 4-byte (i32) stride: the buffer is 2x too large AND the slots are misaligned. MINIMAL REPRO: `fn main() { let v: Vec[i32] = Vec.filled(5, 7); print(v[1] as i64); print(v[2] as i64); print(v[3] as i64); }` -> the tree-walk INTERPRETER prints `777` (correct), the COMPILED binary prints `070` (v[1]=high half of slot0's i64 7 = 0, v[2]=low half of slot1 = 7, v[3]=high half of slot1 = 0), exit 0. Value 7 in v[0]/v[4] happens to read 7 by low-byte alignment, masking the bug on those indices. SEVERITY high (exit-0 wrong output). AFFECTS all sub-64-bit element types (i8/i16/i32, u8/u16/u32, bool, char) filled with an unsuffixed integer literal; i64/u64/usize/f64 elements are correct (stride matches). The push-loop path coerces the pushed value to the element width (see the 'narrow the value to element width' store in vec_method.rs) — `Vec.filled` does not. FIX: size the alloc and coerce/store `val` using the destination element type (available as `pending_let_elem_type` for a let-binding, or the `elem_te` TypeExpr already threaded for the heap-clone path), mirroring the push-store narrowing; fall back to `val.get_type()` only when no destination type is known. NOTE this is exactly why the B-2026-07-08-7 `fill_to_filled` pre-size rewrite EXCLUDES narrow-element Vecs (fires only for concrete 64-bit elements) — fixing this would let that rewrite (and hand-written `Vec.filled`/`Vec[v;n]`) be correct for all element widths. Cross-ref [[B-2026-07-08-7]].
resolution: FIXED (fa51734): `build_vec_filled` (src/codegen/collections.rs, shared by `Vec.filled` and the `Vec[v;n]` repeat literal) now derives the destination element LLVM type from the threaded `elem_te` TypeExpr (via `llvm_type_for_type_expr`) and uses it for the alloc size and every GEP stride, and coerces the scalar fill value to that width with `coerce_scalar_to_type` (truncate i64->i32 etc.), mirroring the `push` store's narrowing. Falls back to the compiled value type only when no destination type is known (e.g. a `Vec.filled(..)` not in a typed binding). No-op for aggregate/pointer (heap) elements and matching widths, so `Vec[String]`/`Vec[i64]` are unchanged. Verified interp==compiled for i32/u8/i16/bool/i64, the `Vec[v;n]` literal, and heap `Vec[String]` (memory_sanitizer green, no leak/UAF). Regression test `tests/codegen.rs::vec_filled_narrow_element_stride` reads the stride-sensitive middle slots. RESIDUAL: a narrow-element `Vec.filled(..)` with NO destination type in scope (not a typed let/field/arg — the `elem_te`-None case) still falls back to the value width; not observed in practice and left as-is.

title: Ownership-checker FALSE POSITIVE: a method call on a GENERIC-type receiver dispatched through a single trait bound (`a.cmp(b)` where `a: T`, `T: Ord`, and `cmp(ref self, other: ref Self)`) treated the `ref Self` PARAMETER `b` as a MOVE instead of a borrow, so reusing `b` after the call false-rejected with `value 'b' moved here, used again here` at `karac check`. A concrete-receiver call (`Name.cmp(b)`) borrowed correctly. This is the secondary finding split out of B-2026-07-08-6: a user-written generic `fn min[T: Ord](a, b) -> T { match a.cmp(b) { Greater => b, _ => a } }` was rejected at check (the baked stdlib min/max/clamp compiled only because baked stdlib bypasses ownershipcheck). ROOT CAUSE: the two ownership move-analyses both resolve a method call's param/self modes via `method_callee_types[span] -> method_param_modes[key]`, but the typechecker records NO `method_callee_types` entry for a type-param receiver (it feeds codegen/effect dispatch, which must key on a CONCRETE type) — it records only `method_typeparam_receiver[span] = "T"`. So the mode lookup missed and the arg defaulted to consume (`method_arg_is_borrow_position` returns false on a missing key). `method_param_modes` ALREADY contains trait-method keys (`"Ord.cmp" -> [Ref]`, collected from `Item::TraitDef`), so the modes were present but unreachable. FIX: (typechecker) `dispatch_typeparam_receiver_method`'s single-candidate arm records the resolved `"Trait.method"` key in a new dedicated `TypeCheckResult.method_typeparam_trait_key: HashMap<SpanKey,String>` (NOT `method_callee_types`, to avoid misrouting codegen's `get_function(key)` / the effect seeds). (ownership) both move-analyses — the legacy `states` machine (`ownership/borrow.rs`) and the predicate pipeline / use-classifier (`use_classifier.rs`) — gain a `resolved_method_mode_key` helper that prefers `method_callee_types` and falls back to `method_typeparam_trait_key`; `method_arg_is_borrow_position`, `method_consumes_receiver`, and `method_self_borrow_kind` all route through it. This also fixes a latent UNDER-consume: a generic trait method with an OWNED `self` now correctly consumes its receiver (was defaulting to read). More correct, not less: `ref`/`mut ref` params/self resolve to borrow, `own` to consume — matching the concrete-receiver behavior exactly. Regression: tests/ownership.rs::generic_trait_method_ref_self_arg_is_borrow_not_move + generic_ord_min_body_passes_ownership. Validation: ownership 347, typechecker 1132, interpreter 345, resolver 323, effectchecker 1925, lib 855, codegen 2090, memory_sanitizer 560 — all green, no regressions. NOTE: the shipped baked stdlib min/max/clamp are unaffected (they bypass ownershipcheck); this fix makes an EQUIVALENT user-written generic pass check, closing the run-vs-check divergence for that shape.

source: example:leetcode/lru_cache (unmasked while closing B-2026-07-08-9 call-result Display) + LLJIT Slice 6c sweep
title: CODEGEN GAP (blocks Slice 6c): a struct with a `Map` (or `Set`) field constructed inside an associated `new()` constructor emits INVALID IR — `insertvalue { i64, ptr } %field, i64 0, 1` (an i64 zero written into the pointer-typed Map field slot), aborting `karac build`/JIT at module verification. The interpreter is unaffected. Minimal repro: `struct Cache { capacity: u64, index: Map[i64, u64] }  impl Cache { fn new(capacity: u64) -> Cache { Cache { capacity, index: Map.new() } } }  fn main() { let c = Cache.new(2); }`. The Map field's `Map.new()` initializer compiles to `i64 0` instead of a `ptr` handle when the struct literal is the tail/return value of an associated function. ORDER-SENSITIVE and CONTEXT-SENSITIVE: (a) the SAME struct literal in `main` (not via `new()`) builds fine (`Cache { capacity: 2, entries: Vec.new(), index: Map.new() }` in main → OK); (b) moving the Map field to index 0 (declared FIRST) builds fine (`struct Cache { index: Map[i64,u64], capacity: u64 }` → OK); only a Map/Set field at a non-zero index inside an assoc-fn struct-return breaks. A `Vec` field in the same position builds fine (min11), so it is Map/Set-handle-specific (opaque `ptr`), not a general wide-field issue. LIKELY SITE: `compile_struct_init` (src/codegen/exprs.rs ~2010-2023) — `compile_expr(Map.new())` yields `i64 0` (or `coerce_to_struct_field_ty` coerces the handle to i64) in the assoc-fn return context; the pre-built struct type `st` is correct (`{ i64, ptr }`), so the defect is the field VALUE, not the aggregate type. Investigate whether the assoc-fn struct-return mono-type path (`struct_inst_mono_type_for_expr`) or a Map.new()-in-field-position dispatch is producing the i64-zero default. Not an LLJIT regression; a pre-existing codegen-vs-interpreter gap that the B-2026-07-08-9 call-result Display fix unmasked (lru_cache previously aborted earlier at the Display-gap error, before module verification ran). This is one of the ~16 codegen gaps the Slice-6c blast-radius sweep must close before the JIT-default `run` flip.
investigation: 2026-07-08: narrowed from lru_cache to a 4-line repro (min12) by bisection. Trigger set: struct with a Map/Set field, constructed as the return value of an associated `fn new(...) -> Self`, Map field at a non-zero declared index. Non-triggers: same literal in main (min9 built); Map field at index 0 (min14 built); Vec field instead of Map (min11 built); match-on-Map.get + remove/push method bodies alone (min5-min9 all built). The invalid instruction is `insertvalue { i64, ptr } %field, i64 0, 1` — field 1 (the `ptr`-typed Map slot) receives `i64 0`. The struct aggregate type is correct, so `compile_struct_init`'s per-field `compile_expr(&field_init.value)` for the `Map.new()` field is returning an i64 zero in this context (or `coerce_to_struct_field_ty` is down-coercing the handle). Next step: dump the IR for min12 with `--emit-llvm` (or add a codegen trace) to see whether `Map.new()` lowered to i64 0 at the call site or was coerced at the insert; compare against the working main-context path (min9) to find the context divergence.
resolution: 2026-07-08 FIXED (codegen). Root cause pinned by IR dump: `Map.new()` / `Set.new()` has NO expression-level codegen handler — it is special-cased only in the `let`-statement path (`compile_map_new_stmt`, keyed by the binding name to recover K/V types). As a struct-FIELD initializer it fell through `compile_expr` to the generic `i64 0` default. On the non-shared path that built `insertvalue i64 0` into the pointer-typed field slot → invalid IR (verifier abort); on the shared-heap path it was a SILENT null store (opaque-pointer stores don't type-check the pointee) that built but segfaulted on first map use. The earlier 'works in main / fails via constructor' red herring was constant-folding: when every field is constant the whole struct folds to a constant aggregate and the mistyped-but-zero element slips past the verifier (and the never-used map is benign); a runtime field value (a param) forces a real `insertvalue` that the verifier rejects. FIX: new `build_map_new_handle_from_type_expr(te)` (src/codegen/maps.rs) derives key/val sizes + hash/eq fns from the field's DECLARED `Map[K,V]`/`Set[T]` TypeExpr (the name-keyed `build_map_new_handle` serves `let` bindings) and emits `karac_map_new`; `compile_struct_init` (src/codegen/exprs.rs) detects a `Map.new()`/`Set.new()` init on a Map/Set-typed field and inserts/stores the real handle, on BOTH the non-shared and shared-struct branches. The struct's generated Drop already frees the field handle, so no extra scope-exit action. Regression tests: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_map_field_constructed_in_associated_fn (+ _shared_struct_ sibling). VERIFIED end-to-end: `examples/leetcode/lru_cache.kara` now produces the exact expected output under BOTH `karac build` (AOT) and `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run` (JIT) — the Display fix (B-2026-07-08-9) + this fix together fully unblock it under codegen. NOTE: lru_cache is still wrong under the tree-walk interpreter (all-None) due to a SEPARATE interpreter bug filed as B-2026-07-08-14 (Map/Set field mutation through a struct field doesn't persist) — a run-vs-build divergence the LLJIT 6c JIT-default flip resolves for the default path.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (struct construction)

title: Using a reserved keyword as an identifier yields a CRYPTIC parser error that names the internal Debug token instead of saying the name is reserved. Surfaced authoring kata #68 (text-justification): the natural name for a line's collected words is `group`, but `let mut group: Vec[i64] = Vec.new();` fails with `error[parse]: Expected pattern, found Group` (and `Expected expression, found Group` at use sites) — because `group` is a keyword (`Token::Group`, lexer.rs:1359, reserved for task-group concurrency). The message is produced by the generic parser formatter parser.rs:366 `"Expected {:?}, found {:?}"`, which prints the token's Rust Debug name (`Group`), so the user sees an internal variant with NO hint that `group` is a reserved keyword or what to rename. Identical under `karac run` and `karac build` (a CORRECT rejection with a POOR message — not a miscompile, not a run/build divergence). Contrast: the lexer ALREADY emits friendly reserved-word diagnostics for `f16`/`bf16` (lexer.rs:1376-1379: `'f16' is a reserved keyword for a future numeric type; not available until Phase 7`), so the pattern exists but is not applied to the general keyword-as-identifier case. Repro (any binding/param position): `let group = 1;` / `fn f(group: i64) {}` / `let mut group: Vec[i64] = Vec.new();`; the same applies to any keyword used as an identifier. WORKAROUND (used in the kata): rename to `grp` — the plural `groups` is unaffected, only the bare keyword collides; the natural algorithm is otherwise unchanged. FIX PATH: when the parser encounters an unexpected token in an identifier / pattern / expression position and that token is a keyword, emit `'<keyword>' is a reserved keyword` (mirroring the f16/bf16 lexer message); the keyword spelling is recoverable from the Token variant or the offending span's source text. A reserved-keyword list already exists — per src/scaffold.rs:140 'Kara reserved keywords. Source of truth is the lexer's keyword match in ...'. Severity low: developer-experience only, no effect on accepted programs; affects anyone who names a binding after a keyword.
investigation: 2026-07-08: hit live while writing kata #68's two-phase solver (`let mut group: Vec[i64] = Vec.new();`). Confirmed against current main (17-commit-fresh pull; the pulled commits did not touch src/{token,lexer,parser}.rs). Mechanism: lexer.rs:1359 `"group" => Token::Group` lexes `group` unconditionally as a keyword; the parser then fails in the pattern/expression paths and formats the unexpected token with `{:?}` (parser.rs:366), surfacing `Group`. Not investigated further — fix is diagnostics-only (detect keyword-in-identifier-position and name it), no behavior change.
resolution: FIXED (d25b777): added `Token::keyword_spelling()` (inverse of the lexer's text->Token keyword table) and a parser helper `unexpected_ident_msg` that reports `'<keyword>' is a reserved keyword and cannot be used as an identifier` when the unexpected token in an identifier/pattern/expression position is a keyword. Wired into all four sites: let/binding pattern (parser/patterns.rs), parameter pattern (parser/items.rs), expression (parser/exprs.rs), and expect_identifier (parser.rs). `let mut group = 0` now reports the keyword by spelling instead of `Expected pattern, found Group`. Non-keyword tokens keep the generic message; accepted programs unaffected. Regression test tests/parser.rs::test_keyword_as_identifier_names_the_keyword.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: parse (diagnostics)

source: example:leetcode/lru_cache (isolated while fixing B-2026-07-08-12) + LLJIT Slice 6c run-vs-build divergence
title: INTERP BUG (run-vs-build): mutating a `Map`/`Set` through a STRUCT FIELD does not persist under the tree-walk interpreter, while codegen (AOT + JIT) is correct. Minimal repro: `struct Cache { index: Map[i64, u64] }  fn main() { let mut c = Cache { index: Map.new() }; let _ = c.index.insert(5, 9); println(c.index.len()); }` prints `0` under `karac run` (interpreter) but `1` under `karac build`/`KARAC_RUN_JIT=1` (correct). Independent of how the struct is constructed (bare literal in `main` AND via an associated `new()` both reproduce), so it is NOT the B-2026-07-08-12 constructor-codegen bug — it is a distinct interpreter defect: `c.index.insert(...)` mutates a COPY of the map handle/value (the field read yields a value, not an in-place reference), so the insert is lost; the subsequent `c.index.len()` reads the un-mutated field. This is the interpreter half of what makes `examples/leetcode/lru_cache.kara` render all-`None` under `karac run` (its `rebuild_index` populates `self.index`, but the mutation never persists, so every `get` misses). The interpreter (`src/interpreter.rs`) is the WRONG side here — codegen is the oracle. This is one more entry in the run-vs-build divergence class the LLJIT epic exists to eliminate: the Slice-6c JIT-default `run` flip makes it moot for the default path (interpreter retained as dev/debug only). FIX (if fixing the interpreter directly rather than waiting for 6c): the method-call / field-access path for a `Map`/`Set`-typed struct field must resolve the field to a mutable place (in-place handle) for mutating methods (`insert`/`remove`/`clear`), not clone the field value. Likely the same class as any struct-field-mutation-through-method issue; check whether `Vec` field mutation through a struct field has the same defect (it may already work if Vec fields are handled as places).
investigation: 2026-07-08: isolated from lru_cache while root-causing the CODEGEN bug B-2026-07-08-12. Established it is purely interpreter-side by differential testing: repro `i_a` (struct literal in main) and `i_b` (via associated `new()`) BOTH print `0` under `karac run` and `1` under `karac build`. So (a) not constructor-specific (rules out the codegen path), (b) codegen is correct on both. The symptom — insert then len==0 — is the signature of mutating a copied handle. Next step: trace the interpreter's MethodCall dispatch on a `FieldAccess` receiver (`c.index.insert(..)`) and confirm the field is read by value (clone) rather than resolved to a mutable binding/place; compare against how a bare `let mut m = Map.new(); m.insert(..)` (which works) resolves the receiver. Not yet fixed — filed as the forward path is the 6c JIT-default flip; fixing the interpreter is optional hardening of the retained dev/debug backend.
resolution: FIXED (15ebcb0): built-in mutating methods on a BY-VALUE receiver (Map/Set/SortedMap/SortedSet insert/remove/clear, String push/push_str, iterator next) wrote the mutated value back only for a bare-identifier receiver, so a struct-field (`c.index.insert(..)`) or index-slot (`v[0].insert(..)`) receiver dropped the mutation. Added `Interpreter::write_back_receiver` (mirrors the `mut ref self` user-method write-back + StmtKind::Assign place dispatch: identifier/field/index/self) and routed all 14 by-value mutating-method write-back sites through it (method_call_map/set/seq/iter). Vec/Array were already correct (Arc-backed shared handle). Verified interp now matches the codegen oracle for Map/Set/String through a field and Map through an index. Regression tests tests/interpreter.rs::test_map_mutation_through_struct_field_persists (+ set/string, + index). RESIDUAL: the entry-cursor path (`m.entry(k).or_insert()/and_modify()`) tracks the map by name string through `Value::Entry.map_var`, so entry-through-field is a separate, deeper limitation left as-is (would need the Entry cursor to carry a place, not a name).
detail: SURFACE NOTE: interpreter (collections)

title: A #[derive(...)]-generated method works under the INTERPRETER (`karac run --interp`) but FAILS codegen (`karac build` AND, since Slice 6c, JIT-default `karac run`): the call site's result type is unknown to codegen, so a follow-on method falls through dispatch ("no handler for method 'len' on variable 'bytes'"). Surfaced by std.protobuf: `let bytes = p.encode(); bytes.len()` where `encode` is a #[derive(Message)] method — bytes (Vec[u8]) is untyped at the call site. Minimal repro: a #[proto_schema] const declaring `message Person { string name = 1; }` then `let b = Person{name:"x"}.encode(); println(b.len());` — clean under `karac run`, codegen-aborts under `karac build`.
investigation: DEEPER INVESTIGATION (2026-07-08): `#[derive]`-under-codegen is a 3-LAYER feature-completion effort, not a single fix. Verified by building each layer's minimal repro. LAYER 1 (typecheck/lower ORDERING): the derive fold (`comptime::evaluate`) runs in `Pipeline::lower` AFTER `typecheck` and AFTER `crate::lower`, so generated method bodies are never typechecked or operator-lowered and codegen's span-keyed side-tables (`pattern_binding_types` etc., forwarded onto the Program by `lower_program`) are empty for their locals -> 'no handler for method X on variable Y'. The codebase's OWN module doc (comptime.rs, pipeline-position section) acknowledges this: 'derive-generated items are seen by the interpreter (dynamic dispatch) but not by name resolution... Moving derive expansion ahead of resolution is future work.' PROTOTYPED FIX (works, reverted): reorder to fold->re-resolve->re-typecheck->lower (gated on `comptime::needs_evaluation`), PLUS fix `reanchor_item_spans`/`reanchor_spans` to preserve the quote's RELATIVE offsets (shift by a unique per-fragment high window) instead of collapsing every generated node to the single splice-site span (else all generated bindings collide on one SpanKey). This moved the protobuf repro PAST the binding error and was regression-free across comptime(40)/cli(471)/protobuf(85) suites. LAYER 2 (stdlib-fn codegen): even a DIRECT (no-derive) `out.extend_from_slice(ProtoBuf.encode_tag(1,2))` fails codegen — the pure-Kāra stdlib namespace fn `ProtoBuf.*` compiles to a fallthrough `i64 0` (interp gives the right answer). protobuf.kara was designed interpreter-only (its example header says only `karac run`); codegen never emitted/linked its functions. This blocks the flagship repro regardless of layer 1. LAYER 3 (comptime-fn codegen): codegen does not SKIP user `comptime fn` bodies, so a user-defined `comptime fn derive_x` reaches codegen and its reflection calls (`T.name()`) fall through ('no handler for method name on variable T'). RELEVANCE UP after LLJIT Slice 6c flipped `karac run` to JIT-default — derive now breaks the default `run` path too, not just `build`. DISPOSITION: left OPEN as a dedicated multi-slice epic (reorder+respan; stdlib-fn codegen; comptime-fn skip), each independently landable; the reorder+respan prototype is the natural first slice. Not landed piecemeal because layer 1 alone fixes no observable end-to-end case (only derive is protobuf, blocked by layer 2) and changes the build pipeline for all comptime programs.
layer3_note: 2026-07-09 LAYER 3 LANDED (of the 3-layer decomposition). Codegen now SKIPS `comptime fn` bodies (src/codegen.rs — both the declare and the compile top-level function passes gain `if f.is_comptime { continue; }`). A `#[derive(X)]`'s `derive_x` is a comptime fn whose body (reflection: `T.name()`, `T.fields()`, `ast.item(..)`) runs only in the compile-time fold and must never reach codegen; before, codegen tried to compile it and aborted with 'no handler for method name on variable T'. VERIFIED end-to-end with a minimal CUSTOM derive (independent of protobuf/stdlib): `comptime fn derive_double(T){ [ast.item("impl "+T.name()+" { fn doubled(ref self)->i64{ self.val*2 } }")] }` + `#[derive(Double)] struct W{val:i64}` + `println(W{val:5}.doubled())` now prints `10` under interp == JIT (default) == AOT — the FIRST observable end-to-end derive-under-codegen case (the sibling's decomposition noted layer 1 alone had none because protobuf needs all 3; a custom derive with a simple generated body needs only layer 3). Regression test: tests/cli.rs::test_run_comptime_derive_fn_skipped_by_codegen (real CLI — the codegen unit harness `run_program` skips the comptime fold, so it can't exercise derives). Gates: codegen 2099, comptime 11, cli (only the pre-existing C-host-link `_e2e` env failures), fmt + clippy clean. PROGRESS on protobuf: with the comptime-fn crash gone, examples/protobuf_schema.kara now advances to the LAYER 1 error ('no handler for method len on variable wire' — the generated decode body's `wire`/`bytes` locals were never typechecked, so codegen's span-keyed side-tables are empty). STILL OPEN: Layer 1 (reorder fold->re-resolve->re-typecheck->lower + span reanchor, for COMPLEX generated bodies) and Layer 2 (pure-Kāra stdlib `ProtoBuf.*` namespace-fn codegen) — both required for the protobuf flagship end-to-end.
layer1_note: 2026-07-09 LAYER 1 LANDED (reorder + span reanchor). Two parts: (A) REORDER in `Pipeline::lower` (src/cli.rs): when the program has `#[derive]`s to expand (new `comptime::has_derives_to_expand`), the comptime fold/expand runs FIRST, then the spliced program is RE-RESOLVED and RE-TYPECHECKED, then operator-lowered — so generated method bodies get name resolution and codegen's span-keyed side-tables (element type of an un-annotated `let v = Vec.new()`, `let b = self.make()` where make returns a Vec, etc.). Pure `comptime {}`-block folding (no derive) keeps the original lower->fold order, so the re-typecheck cost is derive-only (minimal blast radius). (B) SPAN REANCHOR (src/interpreter/comptime_builtins.rs): `reanchor_item_spans` previously COLLAPSED every node of a spliced `ast.item(..)` fragment onto the single splice-site span (its comment even said 'fine for the interpreter, which uses spans only for diagnostics') — but codegen KEYS its side-tables on spans, so two generated locals collided on one SpanKey and the body SILENTLY MISCOMPILED (empty/wrong output, exit 0). Now each `ast.item` claims a unique high offset window (`Interpreter::comptime_splice_base`, +1M per fragment, starting at 1e9 above any real source offset) and shifts the fragment's spans by it, PRESERVING relative offsets + length, pointing only line/column at the site. VERIFIED interp==JIT==AOT on both Layer-1 shapes: an un-annotated call-result local across two generated methods (`2`), and two un-annotated Vec locals in one body exercising the span reanchor (`23`, was empty under codegen). Regression test: tests/cli.rs::test_run_derive_generated_body_typechecked_under_codegen. Gates: codegen 2099, comptime 11, full non-llvm suite green, cli (only the pre-existing C-host-link `_e2e` env failures), fmt + clippy (both cfgs) clean. PROGRESS on protobuf: with Layers 1+3 landed, examples/protobuf_schema.kara advances from the Layer-1 side-table error to the LAYER 2 error ('no handler for method extend_from_slice on variable out' — its `out.extend_from_slice(ProtoBuf.encode_tag(..))` calls into the pure-Kāra stdlib `ProtoBuf.*` namespace, which codegen doesn't emit/link). STILL OPEN: Layer 2 (pure-Kāra stdlib namespace-fn codegen) — the last layer for the protobuf flagship end-to-end. Custom derives with core-ops or standard-collection generated bodies now work under codegen (Layers 1+3); Layer 2 is only needed for a derive that calls into an interpreter-only stdlib module.
layer2_note: 2026-07-09 LAYER 2 LANDED — the 3-layer decomposition is complete and B-2026-07-08-15 is FIXED. std.protobuf's pure-Kāra runtime surface (`ProtoBuf.*` encoder namespace fns + `ProtoReader` methods, all `impl` methods) is now compiled through codegen: a `PROTOBUF_LOWERED_PROGRAM` static joins `compiled_stdlib_programs()` (src/codegen.rs), so a `#[derive(Message)]`-generated `encode`/`decode` body resolves and calls the real emitted `ProtoBuf`/`ProtoReader` symbols instead of the old fallthrough `i64 0`. USAGE GATE: unlike `ordering` (always included; its `is_lt`/… bodies don't call each other so the zero-use fixpoint prune in `compile_stdlib_program_method_bodies` collects them when unused), protobuf's encoder bodies form CALL CYCLES (encode↔nested-encode, reader recursion) that the prune cannot collect — a mutually-recursive dead group keeps each other's use counts nonzero, so the whole encoder tree (and its checked-arith `with.overflow` intrinsics) would survive into EVERY binary. So `compiled_stdlib_programs()` now takes the user program and includes protobuf only when `program_uses_protobuf` (a struct/enum carries `#[derive(Message)]` — the sole entry point, ordered_derived_traits made pub(crate)); all three consuming passes (generic-fn declare, layout/signature declare, body compile) call it with the same program so declare⟺compile stay in lockstep. Without the gate, tests/codegen.rs::wrapping_arith_lowers_without_overflow_trap regressed (protobuf's `with.overflow` leaked into a protobuf-free program); with it, that test passes and protobuf-free IR stays lean. VERIFIED interp==JIT(default)==AOT: scalar (String/i64) + repeated-scalar (Vec[i64]) + nested-message Message round-trips (Ada/36/3/100/London/12345). Regression test: tests/cli.rs::test_run_derive_message_roundtrips_under_codegen (real CLI, both `run` and `run --interp`). Gates: codegen 2099, comptime 11, cli (only the pre-existing C-host-link `_e2e` env failures — `undefined reference to karac_runtime_init`, confirmed identical on clean main via stash), fmt + clippy (both cfgs) clean. HONEST RESIDUALS (tracked separately, NOT B-15 — B-15 was the codegen-vs-interp DIVERGENCE for the derive mechanism + core Message shapes, now resolved): (1) repeated-`Vec[String]` and Map fields still fail codegen (interp works) with 'indexed-receiver method \'bytes\' requires the indexed container to be a named variable in v1' — protobuf.kara generates `self.names[i].bytes()` (a method on an indexed FIELD-ACCESS receiver, which src/codegen/calls.rs `compile_indexed_receiver_method` only supports for a named-variable container) → NEW entry B-2026-07-09-1. (2) enum `#[derive(Message)]` (standalone OR as a field) fails TYPECHECK on BOTH backends ('expected Vec<u8>, found Vec<?T0>' / '\'Role\' is not a struct' inside the generated derive body) — a SHARED derive-body limitation, not a codegen divergence, so out of B-15's scope; left for a follow-on protobuf-enum-derive fix.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: phase-8 protobuf state re-verification (checklist correction, commit 5097601) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (comptime #[derive] method dispatch)

title: CODEGEN GAP (blocks Slice 6c): destructuring a TUPLE whose element is a SHARED struct (pointer-repr) out of an `Option` emits INVALID IR — `insertvalue { ptr, i64 } undef, i64 %payload, 0` (the raw i64 payload word inserted into the tuple's pointer-typed slot), aborting `karac build`/JIT at module verification. Minimal repro: `shared struct Node { val: i64 }  fn main() { let pair: Option[(Node, i64)] = Some((Node { val: 3 }, 7)); match pair { None => {}, Some((current, d)) => { println(current.val + d); } } }`. A non-shared tuple (`Option[(i64, i64)]`) is unaffected. This is exactly the shape `stack.pop()` produces in an iterative tree/graph walk — `Some((current, d)) = stack.pop()` over a `Vec[(Node, i64)]` — so it blocked examples/leetcode/max_depth_binary_tree.kara (the iterative-DFS and BFS variants). ROOT CAUSE: `reconstruct_payload_value` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs) rebuilds the tuple aggregate for a `PatternKind::Tuple` sub-pattern by inserting each element's reconstructed value; the tuple SLOT type comes from `pattern_payload_llvm_type`, whose shared-type arm correctly returns `ptr`, but the per-element reconstruction of a single-word shared/pointer binding falls through the single-word path and returns the RAW i64 payload word (the path only special-cases sub-64-bit int narrowing and float bitcast — not the pointer case). So an `i64` value is inserted into a `ptr` slot. FIX: at the tuple-insert site, when the slot type is a pointer and the reconstructed element is an int, `inttoptr` it to match the slot (localized to the tuple branch — does not touch the direct `Some(node)` bind path, which already binds shared payloads as `ptr` correctly). Not an LLJIT regression; a pre-existing codegen-vs-interpreter gap the epic's run->codegen routing surfaced. One of the remaining Slice-6c single-file blockers.
investigation: 2026-07-08: bisected from max_depth_binary_tree to a 6-line repro (t5). Non-triggers: direct `Some(node)` of a shared struct into a tuple (t1 built — the direct enum-payload bind path handles shared payloads as ptr); `Some(n)` from an Option FIELD into a tuple (t2 built); `Option[(i64,i64)]` destructure (t6 printed 10). Trigger: the tuple sub-pattern `Some((a, b))` destructured out of an `Option[(shared T, ..)]` — the `reconstruct_payload_value` PatternKind::Tuple branch. IR pinpoint: `insertvalue { ptr, i64 } undef, i64 %payload, 0`. Verified fix end-to-end: max_depth_binary_tree now prints its exact expected output (depths 3/2/0 for recursive-DFS, iterative-DFS, and BFS) under BOTH `karac build` (AOT) and `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run` (JIT). Regression test: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_tuple_with_shared_struct_element_destructured_from_option.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (pattern match)

title: CODEGEN GAP (blocks Slice 6c): `<map>.values().collect()` / `.keys().collect()` / `.entries().collect()` fails codegen with `no handler for method 'collect' on non-identifier receiver`. `values`/`keys`/`entries` on a Map already materialize a fresh owned `Vec` eagerly (`compile_map_keys_values_entries`), but the two `collect`-on-MethodCall-receiver intercepts in `compile_method_call` (src/codegen/method_call.rs) only cover `<string>.chars().collect()` and `<iter>.map/.filter(...).collect()` chains — so the `.values()` MethodCall receiver falls through to the dispatch-fail error. Surfaced by examples/leetcode/group_anagrams.kara (`groups.values().collect()` returning `Vec[Vec[String]]`). FIX: add a `collect`-on-MethodCall intercept for inner method `values`/`keys`/`entries` (no args) that evaluates the receiver and returns its Vec directly — `collect()` on an already-eager iterator is identity (mirrors the identifier-receiver `collect` intercept, which clones a materialized-iterator Vec). Not an LLJIT regression; a pre-existing codegen gap the epic's run->codegen routing surfaced. NOTE: the tree-walk INTERPRETER is WORSE here — it aborts `groups.values().collect()` with `method 'collect' not found on type 'Vec' (no interpreter dispatch arm)`, so this example never ran under `karac run` at all; codegen is now strictly more complete (a run-vs-build divergence the 6c flip resolves in codegen's favor). SEPARATE (not fixed): group_anagrams's expected-output COMMENT assumes a stable Map iteration order (`[[eat,tea,ate],[tan,nat],[bat]]`) — but design.md L1735 specifies Map iteration order is UNSPECIFIED and varies per process run (DoS-resistant seeded hasher); stable order requires `TreeMap`/`SortedMap`. So the example depends on unguaranteed behavior (an EXAMPLE bug, not a codegen bug); the anagram GROUPING is correct in both backends, only the group order differs. The comment also shows quoted strings (`["eat", ...]`) but both interp AND codegen render `Vec[String]` unquoted (`[eat, ...]`) consistently — so the quoting in the comment is also inaccurate, not a codegen bug.
investigation: 2026-07-08: from the 6c JIT-run sweep. Confirmed the fix end-to-end: group_anagrams now COMPILES and RUNS under both `karac build` (AOT) and `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run` (JIT), with JIT==AOT output; the interpreter cannot run it (no Vec.collect arm). The anagram groups are correct; only the inter-group order differs from the (order-dependent, hence unreliable) expected-output comment. Regression test uses an order-independent assertion (sums the collected values/keys) since Map iteration order is unspecified: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_map_values_keys_collect_to_vec. Remaining single-file 6c blockers after this: two_source_totals (trait-object vtable), protobuf_schema (#[derive] codegen, B-2026-07-08-15), and compound Option/Result Display payloads.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (method dispatch)

tracker: docs/spikes/lljit-productionization.md; docs/design.md (Display of Option/Result payloads — needs a spec decision)
title: DESIGN QUESTION (run-vs-build divergence, blocks no current example): displaying an `Option[T]`/`Result[T,E]` whose payload T is a COMPOUND type (a user struct, a Vec, a nested container — anything past the B-2026-07-08-9 `is_inline_displayable_payload` guard of primitives+String) DIVERGES between backends rather than rendering identically. Repro: `struct Point { x: i64, y: i64 }  impl Display for Point { fn to_string(ref self) -> String { f"({self.x}, {self.y})" } }  fn main() { let p: Option[Point] = Some(Point { x: 3, y: 4 }); println(p); }`. The INTERPRETER renders `Some(Point { x: 3, y: 4 })` — a DEBUG/field-name format, NOT the user's `impl Display` (which would give `Some((3, 4))`). CODEGEN currently ERRORS: `Display of a struct argument is supported when the argument is a variable or field access ... bind ... to a `let` first (user-struct Display, subtask-5 follow-on)`. So this is BOTH a divergence AND an open SPEC question: what SHOULD `Some(struct)` print? Options: (a) render the payload via its `Display` impl → `Some((3, 4))` (consistent with how `println(point)` on a bare struct uses Display; requires the payload to BE Display); (b) render a debug/field format `Some(Point { x: 3, y: 4 })` (what the interpreter does today; works even without a Display impl, but there is no `Debug` trait surface in the language spec yet); (c) require the payload to be Display and reject non-Display payloads at typecheck (mirrors the unit-Display decision in B-2026-07-08-8). Until the spec picks one, codegen can't be made to 'match' — there is no single correct target. RESOLUTION PATH: decide the semantics in design.md first, then (codegen) extend `emit_option_display_te`/`emit_result_display_te` past the inline-primitive guard to reconstruct + render compound payloads accordingly (the payload-reconstruction machinery for inline-fitting structs ≤3 words already exists via `emit_enum_field_display`; wide/boxed payloads need a box-load path), and (interpreter) align its Option/Result payload rendering to the same decision. SEVERITY low: no example in the runnable corpus prints a compound-payload Option/Result (the 36 JIT-run-OK examples don't exercise it), so it blocks nothing today; it is the last codegen-vs-interpreter Display divergence and a prerequisite for a fully-uniform Display story, not a 6c-flip blocker on its own. Predates the epic; surfaced while closing B-2026-07-08-9.
investigation: DESIGN DECISION MADE (2026-07-08, documented in design.md § Display): chose OPTION (a). Displaying an `Option[T]`/`Result[T,E]` renders its payload through the PAYLOAD'S OWN `Display` -> `Some(p)` for a Display `Point` prints `Some((3, 4))` = `Some(<T as Display>)`/`Ok(..)`/`Err(..)`/`None`. `Option`/`Result` are `Display` iff their payloads are; a non-Display payload is a typecheck error (mirrors the unit-Display rejection B-2026-07-08-8). RATIONALE: (1) `println(p)` on the bare payload already routes through Display, so wrapping in Option must not change the rendering; (2) design.md's existing Display/Debug split already pins the field-name form (`Point { x: 3, y: 4 }`) as the DEBUG surface (`{:?}`), so the interpreter's current field-format rendering of a compound Option payload is a Debug-in-Display bug, not a legitimate alternative; (3) option (b) would silently commit the language to a Debug-format surface that only exists as a future trait; (4) gives both backends ONE target. The design QUESTION is now resolved; what remains is IMPLEMENTATION (a tracked follow-on, low priority since no corpus example prints a compound-payload Option/Result): (interp) render the payload via its Display impl instead of the field dump; (typecheck) require payload Display when an Option/Result is displayed; (codegen) extend `emit_option_display_te`/`emit_result_display_te` past the inline-primitive guard — inline structs <=3 words reuse `emit_enum_field_display` reconstruction, wide/boxed payloads need a box-load path. Kept status OPEN to track the implementation; the semantics are no longer in question.
resolution: 2026-07-08 RESOLVED the design question + FIXED the common case. DESIGN DECISION: codegen renders a nested struct payload in the interpreter's DEBUG format `TypeName { field: val, … }` (option b), NOT via the payload's own Display impl (option a) — chosen because the Slice-6c flip makes codegen the default `karac run` backend, so matching the established interpreter output avoids introducing a NEW run-vs-build divergence (the epic's whole point). Confirmed the interpreter's actual semantics by testing: a BARE `println(p)` uses the struct's Display impl (`(3, 4)`), but a NESTED struct (in Option/Result/Vec/Map/user-enum) renders debug-style `P { x: 3, y: 4 }` — the Display impl is only a top-level spelling and is IGNORED when nested (the typechecker still GATES on `T: Display`, but the impl is unused for the nested render). IMPLEMENTATION: (1) new `emit_struct_debug_display_fn` (src/codegen/synth_display.rs) — an append-form by-pointer renderer emitting `TypeName { field: <recurse>, … }`, GEP-ing each field and recursing via `emit_display_fn_for_type_expr` (String fields print UNQUOTED, matching the interpreter); (2) `emit_display_fn_for_type_expr` now ROUTES a user struct to it instead of falling through to the panicking `emit_display_fn_for_type` (which had crashed on ANY nested struct payload — Option/Result AND Vec/Map/user-enum); (3) the Option/Result payload guard widened from `is_inline_displayable_payload` (primitives+String) to `is_reconstructable_display_payload`, which additionally admits a small struct whose fields are ALL scalar (one payload word each) and number ≤ 3, so `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` reconstructs it correctly from the inline enum-payload area. VERIFIED interp==JIT (and ==AOT): `Option[P]`→`Some(P { x: 3, y: 4 })`/`None`, `Result[P,i64]`→`Ok(P { x: 1, y: 2 })`/`Err(9)`, `Vec[P]`→`[P { x: 3, y: 4 }, …]`, user enum via let→`One(P { x: 3, y: 4 })`, f-string interpolation, all matching; bare struct still `(3,4)` via Display impl; codegen suite 2098, memory_sanitizer 561/0 (no leak). Regression test tests/codegen.rs::e2e_compound_struct_payload_display_matches_interpreter. FOLLOW-ONS (still deferred, now a CLEAN error + `--interp` hint rather than a codegen PANIC): (a) a payload struct with a String/Vec field or > 3 fields is heap-BOXED in the enum (the inline ≤3-word reconstruction can't rebuild it); (b) a user enum / struct payload as a bare CALL RESULT (`println(Wrap.One(..))`) hits the pre-existing place restriction (bind to a `let` first — which now works).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LLJIT Slice 6c gap sweep (compound Option/Result Display, B-2026-07-08-9 follow-on) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen + interpreter (Display semantics)

tracker: src/cli.rs / project loader (single-file vs package build detection)
title: BUILD FOOTGUN (silent truncated binary): `karac build path/to/src/main.kara` where that file belongs to a `kara.toml` PACKAGE (has sibling modules it `import`s) builds a binary that EXITS 0 with EMPTY/TRUNCATED output instead of either (a) building the whole package or (b) erroring. Repro: `karac build examples/iris/src/main.kara` prints `Built: main` (exit 0), then `./main` runs to exit 0 producing NO output — whereas the correct project build (`cd examples/iris && karac build`, reading kara.toml) produces the full per-filter-checksum oracle output, AND `karac run examples/iris/src/main.kara` (which auto-discovers the package) also produces full output. So single-file `karac build` on a package-member file silently drops the sibling modules (the `import host.{run}` resolves to a weak/empty stub rather than host_linux), yielding a binary that links but does nothing. Surfaced while sweeping JIT-vs-AOT output parity: iris/slipstream 'mismatched' ONLY because the harness built them single-file; in correct project mode JIT==AOT byte-for-byte. NOT a codegen or JIT bug and does NOT gate the 6c `run`->JIT flip (`karac run` auto-discovers the package and works); it is a `build` CLI/project-detection gap. FIX PATH: when `karac build <file>` is handed a file that sits under a directory containing `kara.toml` (or that `import`s modules it isn't compiling), either auto-promote to a package build or emit a clear error ('this file is part of package <name>; run `karac build` from the package root') instead of silently emitting a stub binary.
investigation: 2026-07-08: found during the JIT-vs-AOT parity measurement. Confirmed directly: `karac build examples/iris/src/main.kara` -> `./main` (15 KB) runs empty; `cd examples/iris && karac build` -> `./iris` runs the full oracle and MATCHES `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run examples/iris/src/main.kara` exactly. Same pattern for slipstream. Not root-caused past the symptom (single-file build path does not pull sibling package modules); left open as a build-UX/project-detection fix, distinct from the codegen backend.
