resolution: 2026-07-08 FIXED (refuse-with-guidance gate). Confirmed the deeper cause: single-file `karac build`/`check` does NOT error on an unresolvable local-module import; `import host.{run}` with no `host` module reports 'All checks passed', so a package member built single-file links against stubbed sibling calls and runs empty. FIX: `cmd_build` (src/cli.rs) calls `package_member_build_refusal(filename)` which, when the file lives under a `kara.toml` package's `src/` dir (via `manifest::discover_project_root` + canonicalized under-`src/` check), refuses with an actionable message ('… source file of the package at <root> … build the whole package instead: cd <root> && karac build (or karac run <file>)') instead of emitting a truncated binary. Gated to `<root>/src/` so a standalone script near a manifest is unaffected. VERIFIED: `karac build examples/iris/src/main.kara` refuses; standalone leetcode files still build. Regression: tests/cli.rs::test_single_file_build_refuses_package_member. Auto-promoting to a project build (matching run's discovery) left as a follow-on.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LLJIT Slice 6c JIT-vs-AOT parity sweep (build-mode footgun) — SURFACE NOTE: cli (build / project loader)

title: RUN-vs-BUILD divergence: `karac build` (PROJECT mode) fails to resolve a cross-module ASSOCIATED FUNCTION that `karac run` resolves fine. `examples/parallax` and `examples/parallax_lite` (both `kara.toml` packages, 6 and N modules): `cd examples/parallax && karac build` reports `error[E0200]: no associated function 'new' on type 'InMemoryUserDB'` (and the same for InMemoryOrderDB / InMemoryNotifDB / InMemoryRecommendDB) and emits NO binary — while `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run examples/parallax/src/main.kara` AND plain `karac run ...` both resolve `InMemoryUserDB.new()` (defined in a sibling module, e.g. providers.kara) and run to completion ('loaded top recommendation ... got dashboard'). So the project-build module-resolution pass cannot see a sibling module's `impl`-block associated function that the run/JIT path CAN. SYMPTOM confirmed; ROOT CAUSE not yet pinned — likely a module-ordering / impl-block-registration difference between the project-build resolver pass and the run/interpreter loader (the run path may register all modules' impls before resolving main, while project-build resolves main against an incompletely-populated assoc-fn table). Independent of the JIT backend and does NOT gate the 6c `run`->JIT flip (run works); it is a `build`/resolver bug. FIX PATH: ensure project-mode build registers every module's `impl`-block associated functions into the global assoc-fn table BEFORE resolving call sites, matching the run path's ordering; add a cross-module `Type.assoc()` resolution regression test at the project-build level.
investigation: 2026-07-08: found during the JIT-vs-AOT parity sweep (parallax/parallax_lite were the two 'aot-build-failed but jit-ran' entries). Confirmed the symptom in project mode (`cd examples/parallax && karac build` -> 4x E0200, no binary; `karac run` -> full output). Not root-caused; recorded as a run-vs-build resolver divergence for follow-up, distinct from codegen/JIT. Sibling of B-2026-07-08-19 (both are `build`/project-loader gaps surfaced by the same parity sweep).
resolution: 2026-07-08 FIXED. Root cause: project-mode `karac build` runs a PER-MODULE typecheck pass (`typecheck_modules` in src/cli.rs) BEFORE the merged super-program codegen; each module is typechecked against a fresh env built from its OWN items only. The typechecker's `collect_import_origins` (src/typechecker/env_build.rs) pulled an imported item's DEFINITION into the per-module env for StructDef/EnumDef/TraitDef/TraitAlias/MarkerTrait but NOT the type's `impl` blocks. So `import providers.{InMemoryUserDB}` carried the struct but not its `impl InMemoryUserDB { pub fn new }`, and `InMemoryUserDB.new()` failed E0200 'no associated function'. `karac run` typechecks the merged super-program (all impls present), so it never hit this. FIX: `collect_import_origins` now also collects, for each non-aliased imported type, the `impl` blocks in the type's defining module whose target is that type, and registers them via `env_add_impl` (skipping any the current module shadows locally). Aliased imports left as a known limitation. VERIFIED: examples/parallax + parallax_lite build in project mode AND the AOT binary matches `KARAC_RUN_JIT=1 karac run` exactly. Regression: tests/cli.rs::test_build_project_resolves_imported_type_associated_fn. Suite green (typechecker 1926, cli 471, codegen 2098, clippy both cfgs + fmt clean).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LLJIT Slice 6c JIT-vs-AOT parity sweep (project-build vs run divergence) — SURFACE NOTE: resolver / project build

title: SHARED RC-DROP freed scalar Map/Set keys as pointers. `emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn` (MapOrSet arm) and `emit_shared_enum_rc_drop_fn` (Map/HashMap/Set payload arm) in src/codegen/synth_drop.rs hardcoded `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(handle, 1, 1)` on the (false) assumption the runtime no-ops a non-heap side via a `cap==0` guard. For a populated `Map[i64, u64]` the runtime instead reads offset-16 of an 8-byte scalar key as a bogus `cap` and, when non-zero, frees offset-0 (the key VALUE, e.g. `7`) as a pointer -> heap corruption (`free(0x7)`). The runtime's `free_heap_field` docstring (runtime/src/map.rs:129) explicitly says 'never call this on a scalar field'; the correct `(drop_key,drop_val)` derivation via `map_drop_flags` (a side is 1 ONLY when its type lowers to the 24-byte Vec/String struct) already existed and was applied to the NON-shared struct arm (line 1475) and the tuple arm (`emit_tuple_elem_drops`, B-2026-06-13-19) but the two SHARED arms were missed. Latent until B-2026-07-08-12 (Map.new() in a constructor) began storing a REAL map handle where a null handle previously made the drop a no-op. AOT masked the crash (the OOB `cap` read happens to be <=0 in the release-archive heap layout, so `free` is skipped) while the JIT (debug-runtime heap layout) crashed -> a genuine run==build divergence, exactly what the LLJIT parity CI leg exists to catch. FIX: both shared arms now compute `(dk,dv)` from the field/payload `Map[K,V]`/`Set[T]` TypeExpr via `map_drop_flags` (scalar map -> `(0,0)`), matching the struct/tuple arms.
investigation: 2026-07-08: root-caused from the karalang/kara CI `Codegen E2E via LLJIT (run==build parity)` failure on main (the sole red job after the windows-ci git_fetch fix). Reproduced locally: the shared-struct test passes under AOT but the JIT dispatch (`KARAC_TEST_JIT=1`) segfaults with empty stdout. gdb: `__karac_rc_drop_Cache -> karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(_,1,1) -> free_stored_key -> free(mem=0x7)`. Emitted IR confirmed identical `1,1` under both AOT and JIT (ownership arg irrelevant), proving codegen, not the harness, was wrong. Regression tests (JIT+AOT): tests/codegen.rs::e2e_shared_struct_map_field_constructed_in_associated_fn (existing, now guarded by the parity leg) and e2e_shared_enum_map_payload_drops_without_freeing_scalar_keys (new). Gates: codegen 2098/2098 (AOT and JIT parity), memory_sanitizer 560/0, fmt + clippy (llvm --all-targets) clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: CI codegen-e2e LLJIT run==build parity leg (windows-ci follow-up) + tests/codegen.rs::e2e_shared_struct_map_field_constructed_in_associated_fn — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (shared-struct / shared-enum RC drop synthesis)

title: SHARED-ENUM match-binding that MOVES a heap payload out double-frees it. A `shared enum` whose variant carries an owning heap payload (e.g. `Full(Map[i64, u64])`), matched with a binding that moves the payload out (`match s { Store.Full(m) => m.len(), ... }`), frees the payload twice: once via the moved binding `m`'s scope-exit cleanup and once via the enum's generated RC drop (the move is not suppressed in the enum-drop path). Segfaults under BOTH AOT and JIT (`free(mem=<heap ptr>)` inside `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` from `main`). Distinct from B-2026-07-08-21 (that was the drop FLAGS; this is drop SUPPRESSION on a moved-out payload). The construct-and-drop-without-move shape works (regression-tested). REPRO: `shared enum Store { Empty, Full(Map[i64,u64]) } fn build()->Store{ let mut m=Map.new(); let _=m.insert(5,9); Store.Full(m) } fn main(){ let s=build(); match s { Store.Full(m)=>{ println(m.len()); } Store.Empty=>{ println(0); } } }`. Likely needs the match-arm move to zero/forget the enum payload slot so the enum RC drop skips it (analogous to the tuple move-suppression dual `zero_tuple_elem_cap_at`).
investigation: 2026-07-08: found while adding a JIT regression for B-2026-07-08-21. Isolated to the move-out: the same enum constructed and dropped WITHOUT moving the payload out of a match binding runs clean (AOT+JIT), so the drop-flags fix is correct; the double-free is specific to the match-binding move path. Not yet fixed.
resolution: FIXED (f06e1cf): extended `suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out` (control_flow_match.rs) to zero the Map/Set HANDLE word in the shared-enum box when a match arm moves the payload out. The box's `__karac_rc_drop_<E>` frees the Map/Set handle unconditionally via `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` (no `cap>0` guard, unlike Vec/String), and Map/Set aren't in `EnumDropKind` (freed by field TYPE in `emit_shared_enum_field_drop`), so the pre-existing suppressor (which only zeroed VecOrString cap words) never touched them -> double-free (moved binding + box rc-drop), segfault under AOT+JIT. Detect a Map/Set payload field by TypeExpr (last path segment in Map/HashMap/Set/HashSet) at each consumed position and store 0 into its handle word; the runtime free no-ops on a null handle, so the moved binding frees it exactly once. Non-consuming arms (`Full(_)`/`Empty`) suppress nothing and rc-drop frees normally (no leak). ASAN regression test tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_shared_enum_map_payload_move_out_no_double_free loops move-out + no-move-drop.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: spun off while fixing B-2026-07-08-21 — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (shared-enum match-binding move-out)

title: PARSER: a SINGLE-FIELD shorthand struct literal `P { a }` in a value position was misparsed as `P` followed by a block `{ a }`, producing a spurious `expected 'P', found 'i64'` (tail/return position) or `Expected Semicolon, found LeftBrace` (let-RHS). `looks_like_struct_literal` (src/parser/exprs.rs) only recognized `{ ident :` (explicit field), `{ ident ,` (>=2-field shorthand), `{ }` (empty), and `{ ..` (spread) as struct literals — it deliberately EXCLUDED `{ ident }` to avoid misparsing an `if`/`while` condition body (`if a > b { x }` must read `b`'s value then the branch body `{ x }`). But that blanket exclusion also broke every legitimate single-field shorthand in a VALUE position: `fn mk(a: i64) -> P { P { a } }`, `let p = P { a };`, `take(P { a })`. Two-field `P { a, b }` (comma disambiguates) and explicit `P { a: v }` (colon disambiguates) worked; only the single-field shorthand form was affected. Reproduces identically under `karac run`, `karac check`, and `karac build` (a pure parse issue, not a run-vs-build split). Surfaced writing the B-2026-07-08-20 project-build regression test (a `Widget { id }` constructor body). FIX: introduced a `no_struct_literal` parser restriction flag (set only while parsing an `if`/`while`/`for` CONDITION, cleared inside any delimited sub-expression — parens, call args, array literals), and accept `{ ident }` as a single-field struct literal EXCEPT when that flag is set. So value-position single-field literals parse, while `if DEBUG { … }` still reads `DEBUG` + body block. A `match` scrutinee is intentionally NOT restricted (Kāra allows an unparenthesized struct-literal scrutinee `match P { x: 1 } { … }`). Also incidentally makes single-field-shorthand struct-literal MATCH SCRUTINEES and nested-in-condition struct literals (`while has(P { x }) { … }`) parse. Regression tests: tests/parser.rs::test_single_field_shorthand_struct_literal + ::test_single_field_brace_stays_block_in_condition.
investigation: 2026-07-08: bisected the trigger across variants — single-field shorthand in tail position and let-RHS both fail (typecheck 'expected P found i64' and parse 'Expected Semicolon' respectively); explicit `{a: v}` and two-field `{a, b}` pass; local-var vs param makes no difference. Confirmed single-file (not multi-module) via `karac check`. Root-caused to the `looks_like_struct_literal` heuristic's intentional `{ ident }` exclusion (its own comment named the `if a > b { x }` ambiguity). Fixed with a proper condition-scoped restriction flag rather than the blanket exclusion. Verified end-to-end: interp == JIT on a single-field-shorthand + constructor program (7, 41); parser 748, codegen 2098, full non-llvm suite + clippy (both cfgs) + fmt all green; the no-regression guards (if DEBUG / while RUN / match scrutinee) pass.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: isolated while writing the B-2026-07-08-20 regression test — SURFACE NOTE: parser (struct literal)

title: PERF codegen-gap (no correctness impact): kāra's stock `default<O2>` pass pipeline leaves LLVM RUNTIME loop unrolling OFF, so small counted loops with a runtime trip count stay rolled — one op + back-edge per iteration — where `rustc -O` (which enables runtime unrolling by default) unrolls them. Surfaced by kata #70 climbing-stairs, a two-counter Fibonacci recurrence `next = a + b` run K=30M times over a swept n in [1,45]. EQUAL-SAFETY measurement (M5, seq; bench.sh gained a `rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on` row this pass): kāra 210.3 ms vs checked-Rust 111.3 ms = kāra 1.89x BEHIND. The tell is that Rust's OVERFLOW-CHECKED build is 1.52x FASTER than its own wrapping build (111.3 vs 169.6 ms) — enabling overflow checks hands LLVM the nsw facts that trip its runtime unroller, which unrolls the fib loop 4x (disasm: four `adds` per back-edge + epilogue vs one). kāra emits the same checked `adds` but never unrolls (confirmed: `opt -passes=default<O2>` AND `default<O3>` both decline; the loop IS unrollable — forcing `loop-unroll<O2;partial;runtime>` unrolls it). So the gap is NOT overflow-check cost and NOT (as #70's container-pass README guessed) clang's freedom to transform — it is specifically kāra not runtime-unrolling a counted loop that Rust does. Against the WRAPPING natives (c 170.4, rust -O 169.6) kāra's 210.3 is 1.24x behind = overflow-check tax (apples-to-oranges) + this unroll gap. ISOLATED FIX PROVEN: run kāra's pre-opt IR through `default<O2>,function(loop-unroll<O2;partial;runtime>,instcombine,simplifycfg)`, llc, link — #70 goes 190->114 ms (equal-safety parity with rust-ofl) at ZERO binary-size cost (34104 B both). BUT a BLUNT GLOBAL FLIP of that pipeline string was built (worktree runtime-unroll-pipeline) and CORPUS-VALIDATED, and REJECTED: it is a wash-to-negative. Wall-time A/B (installed ac06b64b vs unroll build): #70 -21% (win), but #1 two-sum +8% and #63 unique-paths-ii +12% SLOWER (real regressions — the #63 regression partially undoes B-2026-07-08-3); instruction-count A/B also swung both ways (#91 -31.7%, #66 -8.4% wins; #1 +12.8%, #63 +8.3% losses). The append-after-pipeline placement is also suboptimal (karac build only reached 173 ms on #70 vs opt+llc's 114 ms, because passes appended after `default<O2>` don't get the full re-optimization rustc gets running unrolling INSIDE the pipeline; inkwell's run_passes takes only a pipeline string and does not expose PipelineTuningOptions / the `-unroll-runtime` cl::opt, so unrolling can't be enabled mid-pipeline without llvm-sys FFI). PROPER FIX (scoped, not yet done): a profitability-gated runtime unroll — either enable runtime unrolling INSIDE `default<O2>` via a PipelineTuningOptions/llvm-sys path (so unrolled bodies are re-optimized), tuned with a conservative cost threshold, OR a targeted frontend unroll for small-trip-count counted loops — then validate with a FULL-CORPUS wall-time sweep (all 80 katas: perf + binary size), since a global unroll trades wins for regressions and must not net-regress the corpus. NOTE the workload is unroll-heuristic-sensitive (rust-wrap vs rust-ofl differ 1.5x purely on unrolling), a fragile micro-loop per BENCHMARKS.md's optimizer-distortion pitfall — but the gap it exposes (kāra doesn't runtime-unroll) is real and general. Repro: cd kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/70-climbing-stairs/bench && ./bench.sh (equal-safety row now present); the kāra row trails the rust-ofl row 1.89x. DIAGNOSIS COMPLETE (2026-07-08, still open — fix not yet implemented). Ran the regression diagnosis (why does unrolling regress #1/#63 while helping #70/#66/#91). Three findings, all measured: (1) The earlier blunt-flip regression (#1 -8%, #63 -12% wall) was an ARTIFACT OF PASS PLACEMENT, not fundamental to unrolling. The blunt flip APPENDED `function(loop-unroll<O2;partial;runtime>)` AFTER `default<O2>`, so unrolled memory-bound loops (#63's dp[c]+=dp[c-1] scan) were never re-optimized -> code bloat + broken BCE. Running runtime unrolling IN-PIPELINE instead (`opt -passes=default<O2> -unroll-runtime=true`, i.e. the cl::opt the in-pipeline LoopUnrollPass reads) does NOT regress #63 — it unrolls harmlessly and the post-unroll passes clean it up (measured: #63 5.171B -> 5.156B, neutral/marginally faster). (2) BUT in-pipeline unroll (any threshold) will NOT unroll #70's scalar Fibonacci loop — LLVM 18's cost model declines the loop-carried scalar recurrence, so the cl::opt path gives ZERO #70 win. rustc gets #70's 4x unroll because rustc 1.95 ships a newer/more-aggressive LLVM unroller than kāra's LLVM 18. (3) The distinguishing feature: unrolling WINS on scalar-recurrence counted loops (pure register ops, no memory — #70 `next=a+b`) and REGRESSES on memory-bound array loops (#63 dp scan) when forced without cleanup. VALIDATED FIX PATH (measured, sound by construction): emit per-loop `!llvm.loop.unroll.count N` metadata on FRONTEND-IDENTIFIED unroll-favorable loops (small counted loop, scalar-recurrence body, no memory access). Confirmed by hand-adding `!{!"llvm.loop.unroll.count", i32 4}` to #70's fib latch and re-running `default<O2>`: #70 4.634B -> 3.737B instructions, 1.38x FASTER, in-pipeline (cleaned), sink identical — and because the metadata is PER-LOOP, #63's loops (no metadata) are untouched (zero regression by construction). NOTE the softer `!"llvm.loop.unroll.enable"` is INSUFFICIENT (cost model still declines #70, sadd stays 3); forced `unroll.count` is required, which is why the frontend must GATE which loops get it (forcing all counted loops would re-hit #63's dp scan). IMPLEMENTATION (not done): a frontend loop-shape analysis (counted `while`/`for`, loop-carried scalar phi recurrence, body free of loads/stores/calls) that attaches the `!llvm.loop` unroll-count metadata on the latch branch; then A/B instructions on #1/#63 (must stay flat) and #70/#66/#91 (should win) + a corpus wall+size sweep. This supersedes the rejected blunt-flip approach. Cross-ref [[B-2026-07-08-3]] (#63's BCE that the append broke). FIXED (05c01077): implemented the validated per-loop-metadata fix. karac now emits `!llvm.loop.unroll.count 4` on counted `while` loops with a pure-scalar body (the Fibonacci-recurrence shape) via a new `while_loop_wants_partial_unroll` gate + `attach_unroll_count_metadata` (src/codegen/control_flow_bce.rs, wired in compile_while). The gate is fail-closed: guard_counter_var (a counter upper-bounded in the guard, runtime bound OK) + body_top_level_constant_step (counted) + body_is_scalar_only (every stmt/expr is a literal, identifier, Unary/Binary, or a lowered `<prim>::<op>()` operator call — NO Index/Call/MethodCall/FieldAccess). That scalar-only check is what keeps the unroll OFF memory-bound loops (#63's dp scan), so the blunt-flip regression class cannot recur. Measured on M5 seq (installed karac A/B): #70 climbing-stairs 1.54x faster (-19.4% instructions), sink identical; #63/#1/#66/#69 FLAT (+/-0.1%); binary size delta 0 on all (scalar loop bodies are tiny). Full-unroll (small constant trip, unroll.full) still takes precedence. Gates: codegen E2E 2098/2098 (new IR regression test test_ir_partial_unroll_metadata_gated_on_scalar_recurrence_body asserts the hint fires on a scalar `while i<=n` and NOT on a memory-bound `while c<64 { s+=v[c] }`), fmt + clippy clean. CONSERVATIVE FIRST CUT: memory-access loops that DID win under the blunt flip (#66 plus-one carry scan -8%, #91 decode-ways -32%) are excluded here (they have array indexing) — a follow-on could widen the gate to memory-bound loops IF done in-pipeline with re-optimization (the diagnosis showed in-pipeline unroll keeps #63 neutral), but the scalar-only cut captures the clean win with zero regression risk and zero bloat.

A method call on an INDEXED FIELD-ACCESS receiver — `self.names[i].bytes()` — works under the INTERPRETER but FAILS codegen with 'indexed-receiver method \'bytes\' requires the indexed container to be a named variable in v1 (got non-identifier inner expression)'. src/codegen/method_call.rs routes an `ExprKind::Index` receiver to `compile_indexed_receiver_method` (src/codegen/calls.rs), which requires the indexed container (`inner`) to be a bare `Identifier` (a named local like `xs[i].m()`); a FIELD-ACCESS container (`self.names[i].m()`) falls through to the error. Surfaced by std.protobuf `#[derive(Message)]` on repeated-`Vec[String]` and Map fields: derive_message generates `self.<field>[i].bytes()` for the length-delimited encode loop (protobuf.kara ~lines 497-515), so those Message shapes round-trip under `karac run --interp` but codegen-abort under JIT-default `karac run` / `karac build`. Scalar + repeated-scalar (Vec[i64]) + nested-message Messages are unaffected (they don't hit the indexed-string path) and round-trip under all three backends (B-2026-07-08-15 Layer 2).
investigation: 2026-07-09: isolated from the B-15 Layer 2 landing. Minimal repro: `#[derive(Message)] struct Tags { names: Vec[String] }` + `let t = Tags{names:["a","bb","ccc"]}; println(Tags.decode(t.encode()).names.len());` — prints `3` under `karac run --interp`, codegen-aborts with the indexed-receiver error under default `karac run`. FIX DIRECTION (not yet done): generalize `compile_indexed_receiver_method` to accept a field-access (or any place-expression) container — compile the container place, GEP the element, then dispatch the method on it — rather than requiring a named-variable inner. Alternatively (narrower), hoist `self.<field>[i]` into a temp local in the derive-generated body so the existing named-variable path applies. Lower severity than B-15: the common Message shapes work; only repeated-String and Map are blocked.
resolution: 2026-07-09 FIXED — two codegen sub-gaps, both closed generally (not protobuf-specific). (1) INDEXED FIELD-ACCESS CONTAINER (`self.names[i].bytes()`): `compile_indexed_receiver_method` (src/codegen/calls.rs) now hoists a FieldAccess container to a synth Vec/Slice identifier before the existing named-variable path — resolve the field via `lower_field_access_ptr` (normalising `self`/SelfValue), register a synth binding (`register_var_from_type_expr` populates its vec/elem tables + slot), rewrite `inner` to that synth, and tear the synth down alongside the element synth. (2) TUPLE-ELEMENT METHOD (`e.0.bytes()`, what a Map field's `for e0 in self.<field>.entries()` encode loop generates — `.entries()` yields an owned `Vec[(K,V)]`, so `e0` is a tuple element): `try_compile_tuple_index_receiver_method` (src/codegen/calls.rs) was gated to Map/Set tuple elements; relaxed to also route String elements through the same synth-elem-ptr dispatch (a read-only alias, no drop). That needed `place_chain_tuple_tes` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs) to resolve a bare tuple-typed identifier — added an Identifier arm consulting `tuple_var_elem_tes` (made pub(super)) — AND `register_var_from_type_expr` to populate `tuple_var_elem_type_names` for a Tuple TypeExpr (new arm), so a `Vec[(K,V)]` for-loop element binding records its element type names. VERIFIED interp==JIT==AOT: repeated-`Vec[String]` Message round-trips (`Tags{names:[a,bb,ccc]}` → len 3, [1]=bb), Map Message round-trips (`Counts{tally}` → len + get), plain-tuple `e.0.bytes()`, and `for kv in m.entries() { kv.0.bytes() }`. Regression tests: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_tuple_element_string_method (plain tuple + map-entries, no derive) and tests/cli.rs::test_run_derive_message_repeated_string_and_map_roundtrip (full derive round-trip, both backends). Gates: codegen 2098, comptime 11, cli derive tests green, fmt + clippy (both cfgs) clean. NOTE: enum `#[derive(Message)]` (standalone or as a field) is still blocked on BOTH backends by a derive-body typecheck limitation (`Vec<?T0>`), tracked in B-2026-07-08-15's layer2_note — orthogonal to this codegen fix.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: B-2026-07-08-15 Layer 2 residual — repeated-String / Map protobuf Message fields — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (indexed-receiver method dispatch)

fix: 991d3e2 (Slice 1 arm64 params) + fa18029 (Slice 2 arm64 returns) + 6a6294f (Slice 3a arm64 >16B indirect params) + c3a6820 (Slice 3b arm64 >16B sret returns) + bc6a78c (Slice 3c x86-64 >16B byval/sret)
title: AArch64 (Apple silicon) ABI divergence: a `#[repr(C)]` struct passed BY VALUE across the C export boundary is mislowered on arm64, silently returning wrong data. Surfaced by the macOS-arm64 CI job (codegen-e2e-macos), test_build_crate_type_staticlib_links_from_c_e2e: a C host calls `stats_mean(Stats{ sum: f64 = 30.0, count: i64 = 4 })` on a Kara `pub extern "C" fn stats_mean(s: Stats) -> f64` (Stats = `#[repr(C)] { f64 sum; i64 count }`); it returns 0.00 instead of 7.50. x86-64 CI + local x86-64 give 7.50. ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED (not just suspected): Kara codegen emits the struct param as a RAW LLVM struct-by-value type (`llvm_param_type` -> `{double, i64}`) and relies on LLVM's BACKEND default aggregate lowering, whereas a real C frontend does explicit per-target ABI CLASSIFICATION in the IR. Evidence: `clang -emit-llvm` on the identical `double stats_mean(struct Stats s)` coerces the param DIFFERENTLY per target — x86-64: `define double @stats_mean(double %0, i64 %1)` (SysV: eightbyte0=SSE->xmm0, eightbyte1=INTEGER->rdi); aarch64: `define double @stats_mean([2 x i64] %0)` (AAPCS: non-HFA <=16B composite -> [2 x i64] in x0/x1). Kara's raw `{double,i64}` param happens to lower to xmm0+rdi on x86-64 (matches clang by luck; confirmed by disasm: `cvtsi2sd %rdi,%xmm1; divsd %xmm1,%xmm0` = count in rdi, sum in xmm0), but on aarch64 LLVM's backend default for `{double,i64}` byval does NOT match clang's `[2 x i64]` coercion, so `sum` lands in the wrong register class. THE FIX is a per-target aggregate ABI classifier for `#[repr(C)]` struct params AND returns (the same machinery clang/rustc carry): x86-64 SysV eightbyte classification (INTEGER vs SSE) and AArch64 AAPCS (HFA -> [N x floatty] in v-regs; non-HFA <=16B -> [ceil(size/8) x i64]; >16B -> indirect byval pointer), reconstructing the struct in the callee prologue. x86-64 already emits the SysV-correct lowering via the backend default, so a fix can be additive on the aarch64 path (target-gated) to avoid x86-64 regression. SCOPE: repr(C) struct-by-value params/returns (transparent-struct producer path). repr(C) ENUMS are UNAFFECTED and CONFIRMED green on arm64 (integer-word tag/payload; both enum E2E round-trips pass on codegen-e2e-macos), as are auto-boxed Vec/String returns and scalar params. WORKAROUND for producers today: pass structs by raw pointer (`*const T`), which is target-agnostic. QUARANTINE: codegen-e2e-macos drops `test_build_crate_type` from its arm64 filter (comment points here); the test still runs on x86-64. Repro: clang -target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu -O1 -S -emit-llvm on the C signature vs Kara's raw struct param. FIX (Slice 1 — params) LANDED: codegen now coerces exported `#[repr(C)]` struct params per AAPCS on arm64 (`arm64_repr_c_struct_coercion`: HFA -> [N x fp]; non-HFA <=8B -> i64, <=16B -> [2 x i64]; >16B -> rejected with a pass-by-pointer message, not silently miscompiled). The body prologue reconstructs the struct from the coerced value (store+reload, identical 16B layout). x86-64 is byte-identical (coercion is arm64+export-gated via `target_is_aarch64`). Internal Kara calls to such an export are rejected on arm64 (boxed-export pattern). VERIFIED LOCALLY without arm64 hardware: tests/abi_repr_c_struct.rs forces the arch (KARAC_FORCE_TARGET_ARCH) and asserts the emitted LLVM param signatures EQUAL clang's arm64 coercions for 6 shapes (identical IR => identical ABI); CONFIRMED ON REAL ARM64 HARDWARE (2026-07-09): the un-quarantined stats_mean struct-by-value test passes green on the codegen-e2e-macos (Apple silicon) job, and the forced-arch signature-match step is green on Linux — params are done end-to-end. REMAINING (open): repr(C) struct-by-value RETURNS (still raw on arm64), and >16B-by-value params (rejected, not lowered indirect) — Slice 2. SLICE 2 (returns) LANDED: codegen now also coerces exported `#[repr(C)]` struct RETURNS per AAPCS on arm64 (`arm64_repr_c_struct_return_coercion`: non-HFA <=16B -> i64/[2 x i64]; HFA -> raw struct, which LLVM already returns in v-regs matching clang; >16B -> rejected with an out-param message, sret is a further follow-up). Return sites reinterpret the struct value into the coerced register type (reinterpret_value_as). Signature-match verified vs clang for returns (tests/abi_repr_c_struct.rs: [2 x i64], i64, raw-HFA), + a C-host execution test (test_build_repr_c_struct_return_from_c_e2e: make_stats/make_vec2 return structs by value, C reads 30.0/4/1.5/2.5) added to the arm64 CI filter. With this, the silent-miscompile bug is ELIMINATED for all register-passed `#[repr(C)]` struct params AND returns; only >16B by-value remains, and it is a LOUD rejection (pass-by-pointer / out-param), never a miscompile — a bounded enhancement (indirect/sret lowering), not an open correctness hole. Pending CI hardware confirm of the return execution test on codegen-e2e-macos. ✅ FIXED + HARDWARE-CONFIRMED (2026-07-09): both the un-quarantined struct-PARAM test (stats_mean) and the new struct-RETURN test (make_stats/make_vec2) pass green on the codegen-e2e-macos (real Apple silicon) job, and the forced-arch signature-match step is green on Linux. The silent-miscompile is eliminated for every register-passed `#[repr(C)]` struct param and return. The ONLY remaining case is >16B by-value (indirect params / sret returns), which is a LOUD compile-time rejection with a pass-by-pointer / out-param workaround — a bounded future enhancement, NOT an open correctness hole. Two permanent CI gates guard the fix: the Linux signature-match (instant, no arm64 hw) and the macOS-arm64 execution E2E. FULLY CLOSED (2026-07-09, Slice 3): the larger-than-16B by-value case is no longer a rejection - it is lowered faithfully on BOTH targets. AArch64: >16B non-HFA param -> plain indirect ptr to a caller copy, >16B return -> sret (x8) [Slices 3a/3b, signature-matched vs clang]. Building the paired C-host execution test then surfaced that x86-64 had the SAME >16B bug but as a SILENT MISCOMPILE (never a rejection - that only ever lived on the arm64 path): SysV MEMORY class needs ptr byval(%Struct) params + sret returns, which the raw-struct lowering did not match (clang oracle: i64 @f(ptr byval(...)) / void @f(ptr sret(...)) vs Kara's old raw {i64,i64,i64}). Slice 3c closes it. Verified with REAL x86-64 execution (test_build_repr_c_struct_big_byval_sret_from_c_e2e: a C host calls make/sum3/roundtrip on a 24-byte struct by value, reads back 10 11 12 33 15 38) + native x86-64 signature-match tests; the E2E is in the arm64 CI producer filter so it doubles as the 3a/3b real-execution proof on Apple silicon + Linux arm64. NO remaining by-value correctness gap for register- or memory-class #[repr(C)] structs on either CI-covered target.

The interactive JIT-default `karac repl` SILENTLY DROPPED all cell stdout: a `println(1 + 1);` cell printed nothing to the terminal (while `karac repl --interp` printed `2`). The persistent `karac_jit_runner --repl-mode` subprocess pipes the cell's stdout (`Stdio::piped()`) and returns it over a framed protocol; `Session::run_cell_via_jit` forwarded those bytes only on the `capture=true` path (kernel/tests) and returned `Vec::new()` on the `capture=false` INTERACTIVE path — and `run_with_wrapper`'s Ok-arm never prints `stdout` anyway (the tree-walk interpreter writes straight to the process stdout, so the interp path needs no forwarding). Net: after the Slice-5 JIT-default flip made the JIT the default REPL backend, every interactive `println` cell produced no visible output — the flagship interactive surface was mute under its own default backend. Undetected because the entire `tests/repl_jit.rs` suite drives `Session::evaluate_cell_captured` (`capture=true`), which returns the bytes to the caller and never exercises the interactive forward.
investigation: 2026-07-09: found while measuring the REPL cold-start number (the last LLJIT-spike acceptance remainder) — a `println` smoke test over piped stdin showed only the banner. FIX (src/repl.rs::run_cell_via_jit): on the `capture=false` path, write the runner-captured `stdout` bytes to `std::io::stdout()` (and any clean-cell `stderr` to `std::io::stderr()`) in the `Completed` arm, and `partial_stdout` + the fault diagnostic in the `RunnerDied` arm. Regression test (the gap the capture-only suite missed): tests/repl_jit.rs::repl_jit_interactive_subprocess_prints_cell_output — spawns the REAL `karac repl` binary over piped stdin (the interactive path) and asserts the two cells' `42`/`hi` reach stdout under the JIT banner. Verified interp==JIT for multi-cell sessions with a persistent `let`. Gates: repl_jit 26, repl 136, fmt + clippy (llvm) clean. Follow-on (separate): a cell that PANICS (`emit_panic`'s exit(1)) still loses its own stdout/panic text because the runner dies before framing its capture buffer — tracked as B-2026-07-09-4.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LLJIT spike cold-start measurement — interactive `karac repl` smoke test — SURFACE NOTE: repl (JIT cell output forwarding)

title: A REPL cell that PANICS under the JIT loses ALL of its own output — both the text it printed before the fault AND the panic message itself. The `karac_jit_runner --repl-mode` protocol runs the cell with stdout/stderr CAPTURED into buffers, then writes a `result <id> <exit> <stdout_len> <stderr_len>` frame followed by the buffered bytes. But a fault trips codegen's `emit_panic`, which calls `exit(1)` — the runner process dies BEFORE it writes the frame, so the capture buffers (holding both the pre-fault prints and the panic text) are never transmitted. The client sees stdout close → `CellResult::RunnerDied` with EMPTY `partial_stdout`/`runner_stderr`. Repro: `println(222); println(v[5]); println(333);` on an empty `Vec` prints neither `222` nor the OOB panic — only a generic 'runner died mid-cell' note (added by B-2026-07-09-3 so the fault is at least visible). Contrast `karac run`, whose one-shot JIT subprocess INHERITS stdio, so its panic text reaches the terminal (that path is correct).
investigation: 2026-07-09: isolated while fixing B-2026-07-09-3, then FIXED. Root cause is the capture-then-frame ordering in `--repl-mode`: emit_panic's exit(1) bypasses the framing write. Considered (b) inherit-stdio (breaks the per-cell framing the kernel/value-snapshot path needs) and (c) fork-per-cell (breaks cross-cell value-snapshot persistence — the cell's global writes would land in the dying child). Chose (a): `emit_panic` lowers to libc `exit` (src/codegen.rs:3342), which RUNS atexit handlers, and the dup2 redirect means the tempfiles ALREADY hold the pre-fault prints + the `panic …` printf at fault time — the only missing step is transmitting them. FIX: the runner installs a `libc::atexit` handler (`salvage_faulted_cell_output` in src/bin/karac_jit_runner.rs) armed for the duration of each cell's `call_main` (via `capture_via_redirect(Some(id), …)`, storing the saved parent-pipe fd + tempfile paths + cell id in atomics/Mutex). On a fault it flushes, reads the tempfiles, and writes a NEW `faulted <id> <exit> <so> <se>\n<bytes>` frame to the saved pipe fd before the process dies; disarmed the instant call_main returns so a clean cell salvages nothing. Client (jit_runner_client.rs) recognizes `faulted` and maps it onto `CellResult::RunnerDied{partial_stdout, runner_stderr}`, so the existing run_cell_via_jit RunnerDied arm surfaces the salvaged output (and suppresses the now-redundant 'runner died' note when real output was salvaged) and the client still re-spawns. VERIFIED interactively: `println(111); println(v[5]); println(999);` now prints `111` then `panic: vec index out of bounds` (999 correctly skipped — execution halted at the fault), and a later cell `println(444)` still runs (re-spawn works); contract violations show `panic: contract violated: requires clause`. Matches `karac run`/AOT (panic → stdout). Regression tests: tests/karac_jit_runner_repl.rs::repl_runner_salvages_output_when_cell_calls_exit (protocol-level: asserts the `faulted` frame shape) and tests/repl_jit.rs::repl_jit_interactive_subprocess_surfaces_panic_and_recovers (real `karac repl` binary over piped stdin). Gates: repl_jit 27, karac_jit_runner_repl 3, repl 136, lljit_e2e 16, fmt + clippy (both cfgs) clean. NOTE: the one-shot `karac run`/`--test-batch` paths are untouched (run inherits stdio; test-batch reads parent-known tempfiles).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: B-2026-07-09-3 follow-on — panicking REPL cell loses its output — SURFACE NOTE: repl (karac_jit_runner --repl-mode protocol)

title: `#[derive(Message)]` on a BARE ENUM (`#[derive(Message)] enum Role { Guest, Member, Admin }` + `r.encode()` / `Role.decode(..)`) failed with confusing GENERATED-BODY typecheck errors on BOTH backends — `'Role' is not a struct` (the derived ctor `Role { … }`) and `expected 'Vec<u8>', found 'Vec<?T0>'` (an un-inferred empty `Vec.new()` encode, because an enum has no `T.fields()` to push). `derive_message` is documented and written for a STRUCT (a proto3 message; protobuf.kara L316/L806, checklist Slice 2 'on a struct of proto3 fields'); a proto3 enum is a FIELD type, not a top-level message. Standalone enum-Message is not a proto3 concept, is not emitted by the `.proto` schema path (which emits proto enums as plain `enum` + uses them as struct fields), and never worked on any backend — so this was a missing-guard / bad-diagnostic bug, NOT a lost feature.
investigation: 2026-07-09: surfaced as the last residual of the B-2026-07-08-15 Layer 2 protobuf-under-codegen work (flagged in its layer2_note). Confirmed enum-as-a-FIELD works interp==JIT==AOT (`struct User { name: String, role: Role }` round-trips, encoding `role` as a varint of the variant's 0-based declaration index — `derive_enum_field_roundtrip` and 4 sibling tests), and the `.proto` schema path emits enums WITHOUT `#[derive(Message)]` (runtime/stdlib/protobuf.kara L1599/L1712), so only a directly-annotated bare enum hit the gap. FIX: `derive_message` now guards `T.is_enum()` at the top and raises an actionable `compiler.error` — names the type, states `#[derive(Message)]` applies to a struct, and directs the user to declare the enum WITHOUT the derive and use it as a struct field (proto3 enum), or an enum-with-payload field (proto3 oneof) — then early-returns an empty `Vec[Item]` so no struct-shaped body is generated. Both backends now emit the same clear `E_COMPTIME_ERROR` instead of the two internal errors. Regression test: tests/protobuf_derive.rs::derive_message_on_bare_enum_errors (asserts the diagnostic via the `comptime_diags` harness). Gates: protobuf_derive 62, protobuf_proto 24, comptime 11, fmt + clippy (both cfgs) clean; enum-field + struct-message round-trips unregressed. Not a codegen change (derive_message is a comptime fn, skipped by codegen per B-2026-07-08-15 Layer 3) — protobuf.kara is baked into karac via include_str!, so it required a karac rebuild, not a runtime-archive rebuild.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: B-2026-07-08-15 layer2_note residual — enum #[derive(Message)] — SURFACE NOTE: stdlib (std.protobuf derive_message)

tracker: src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs::bind_pattern_values_via_ptr (TupleVariant arm struct-payload bail) + pattern_binds_struct_payload helper; regression tests/codegen.rs::borrowed_option_struct_payload_field_read_is_correct
title: Matching a BORROWED `Option[struct]` (`ref` / `mut ref` parameter) and reading a field of the `Some(n)` payload silently returned 0 — a wrong-answer miscompile independent of `shared`/RC. Repro: `struct P { val: i64 }  fn getval(o: ref Option[P]) -> i64 { match o { None => 999, Some(n) => n.val } }` returns 0, not the real val (owned `Option[P]` is correct). Also hits `mut ref` and shared-struct payloads (`ref Option[shared Node]`), so any read-only recursive tree/list walk that borrows its node — the natural, idiomatic form — got garbage. Root cause: a single-field struct flattens to one i64 payload word, so the via-ptr `TupleVariant` fast path's `first_word_is_primitive` / `ok_single_word` checks passed and bound `n` as a ref-to-i64 LEAF (the payload word's raw type) rather than a ref to struct `P`. The arm body's `n.field` access then could not resolve against the scalar-typed binding and collapsed to a hardcoded 0 in the arm's result (`phi i64 [999,...], [0,...]`). The owned path never hit this: its value-source `reconstruct_payload_value` consults `pattern_binding_types` (`binding_is_struct`) and rebuilds the `{i64}` struct aggregate at the correct type.
investigation: 2026-07-09: reduced from #98 to a ~10-line repro, then bisected the trigger with a matrix — owned `Option[struct]` OK, `ref Node` (borrow the struct directly, no Option) OK, but `ref Option[struct]` and `mut ref Option[struct]` both read 0; `ref Option[i64]` is a separate typecheck error (payload binds as `ref i64`), not this bug. Dumped the `@getval` IR for owned-vs-ref via a throwaway `compile_to_ir` test: the ref arm GEPs the correct payload address (`%Some.pl.0.ptr = gep {i64,i64,i64,i64}, %o.ref.ptr, 0, 1`) and even loads it, but binds `n` at type i64, so the body's `n.val` feeds a constant 0 into the merge phi; the owned arm reconstructs `{i64}` and feeds `%val`. FIX: in `bind_pattern_values_via_ptr`'s `TupleVariant` arm, before the primitive-word fast path, bail (`return Ok(None)`) when any positional sub-pattern binds a struct-typed name — new helper `pattern_binds_struct_payload` mirrors the owned path's `binding_is_struct` (a `Binding` whose `pattern_binding_types` entry is in `struct_types` OR `shared_types`). This defers struct/shared-struct payloads to the value-source path, which already reconstructs the aggregate at the correct type (the same path the owned case uses). Multi-word structs already bailed via the existing `!ok_single_word && !ok_padded_primitive` guard; this closes the single-word-struct gap the LLVM-word-type heuristic could not distinguish from a genuine `Option[i64]`. PERF NOTE: the #98 'optimization' that surfaced this is a mirage — with the miscompile fixed, the borrowed `is_valid` retires ~7.20B instructions vs ~7.14B for the by-value original (marginally SLOWER), because the fix routes the borrowed struct payload through the same copy/reconstruct work as by-value. The buggy version's apparent 1.15x speedup was the miscompile skipping correct binding+RC work. #98 therefore stays by-value; no kata change. A genuine win would require true borrow-without-clone binding of struct/shared payloads in the via-ptr path (bind `n` as a ref at the struct's own type, and for shared alias the RC handle without inc/dec) — tracked as a separate perf enhancement, not this correctness fix. Read-only correctness is complete; struct-payload write-through under `mut ref` (the copy-shim does not alias back to the caller) is the same future enhancement. VERIFIED: 4-case regression test (ref/mut-ref plain struct, ref shared-struct recursive walk, owned control) runs real binaries and passes; the #98 mixed valid/invalid harness now matches the Python oracle byte-for-byte under the borrowed `is_valid`. Gates: codegen 2100, interpreter 1135, ownership 347, resolver 323, typechecker 1926, fmt + clippy (--all-targets, --features llvm) clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while investigating a LeetCode #98 (validate-BST) codegen 'optimization': switching the read-only `is_valid` to borrow its node (`ref Option[TreeNode]`) — the idiomatic shape every native mirror uses — produced a SILENT WRONG ANSWER (all trees validated as true). The bench sink couldn't catch it (its `build` only makes valid BSTs, so always-true hashes identically); the kata's mixed valid/invalid harness did.

title: Silent unchecked implicit integer conversions at binding boundaries (let-annotation, function argument, function return) — inconsistent with the strict mixed-integer ARITHMETIC rule, and value-lossy. The typechecker rejects mixed-integer arithmetic with E0200 ("cannot mix integer types 'u64' and 'i64' in arithmetic ... cast explicitly with `as`"), but SILENTLY accepts an integer of one type where a differently-typed integer is annotated/expected at every assignment-like site — across BOTH signedness and width, including sign-flip and out-of-range narrowing — with no `as` and no diagnostic. The annotation sets the static type but no conversion/range-check is emitted, so the runtime value is the source's and the static type can lie. Repros (all `karac check` CLEAN, interp==compiled so this is a typechecker-level permissiveness, not a codegen divergence): (1) `let b: u64 = a;` where `let a: i64 = -5;` -> prints 18446744073709551611 (2^64-5, sign-flip uncaught). (2) `let c: u64 = -5i64;` -> same (negative literal into unsigned, silently). (3) `let small: u32 = big;` where `let big: i64 = 5000000000;` -> prints 5000000000, a value OUTSIDE u32's range and NOT truncated (a real u32 would be 705032704) — the `u32` annotation is effectively ignored, no truncation, no error. (4) same silent acceptance at a call boundary (`fn f(x: u64)` called with an i64 arg) and at a return boundary (`fn f() -> u64 { <i64 value> }`). CONTRAST: the explicit `as` cast is correct — `big as u32` truncates to 705032704 (interp==compiled). So the hole is specifically the IMPLICIT boundary coercion, not the cast machinery. WHY IT MATTERS: the explicit-cast discipline the language advertises (and enforces in arithmetic) is bypassed at let/arg/return, so silent sign changes and out-of-range/narrowing conversions pass typecheck and the static type gives a false guarantee — exactly the class of bug `as` is meant to force the author to acknowledge. LOAD-BEARING CAVEAT (why this needs a design decision, not a unilateral strict-mode flip): `String.len()`/`Vec.len()`/`Slice.len()` all return i64 (src/typechecker/stdlib_seq.rs:156/739/821/1109), and existing corpus code assigns those i64 lengths to u64 bindings/fields relying on exactly this implicit coercion — e.g. examples/word_count.kara `WordCount { total: u64 = words.len(), unique: u64 = ...len() }`. Making all binding-site integer coercions strict would break that code broadly. OPTIONS (not yet chosen): (a) require `as` at binding boundaries too (most consistent; needs a corpus migration + possibly reconsidering whether len() should be u64); (b) allow only same-signedness widening implicitly and reject narrowing + sign-change (rejects repros 1-3, but i64->u64 is a sign change so word_count would still need `as`); (c) at minimum, reject a statically-known out-of-range or negative literal into a narrower/unsigned type (`-5i64 -> u64`, `5000000000i64 -> u32`) while leaving variable-to-variable widening — catches the smoking-gun literals with the least churn. Found during a correctness dogfooding pass authoring examples/mend/examples/grade_histogram (a u64 loop counter over an i64 `.len()` tripped E0200, which led here). Repro dir: examples/mend/examples/grade_histogram/ (notes.md). Filed rather than fixed because the corpus dependency makes this a language-design decision (which boundaries require `as`, and whether len() should be u64), not a mechanical typechecker patch. PARTIAL FIX 2026-07-09 (option (c) — literal boundary range-check): the statically-detectable holes are now closed. A SUFFIXED integer literal at a coercion boundary (let-annotation / fn-arg / return / struct-field / match-arm) is now range-checked against the CONTEXTUAL type, not just its own suffix — so `let x: u64 = -5i64` (E0200 negative-into-unsigned), `let x: u32 = 5_000_000_000i64` and `let x: u8 = 300i64` (E0200 out-of-range) are rejected, matching the unsuffixed twins the B-2026-07-02-7 check already caught. Implemented in src/typechecker/exprs.rs: a `suffixed_int_literal_value` companion to `unsuffixed_int_literal_value`, plus a second block in `check_expr` that runs the existing `check_int_literal_fits` against the expected type (emits ONLY when the value does not fit, so an in-range coercion like `5i64 -> u64` is left alone; returns early to avoid double-emitting with the synthesis-time own-suffix check). Gates: full non-llvm suite + 2103 codegen E2E + new tests/typechecker.rs::{test_suffixed_int_literal_rejected_at_narrowing_boundary (asserts the error AND a single diagnostic), test_suffixed_int_literal_in_range_coercion_still_admitted}; corpus scan of 168 .kara files shows ZERO new rejections; fmt + clippy clean. STILL OPEN (the design-decision half): VARIABLE-to-variable implicit integer coercion at these same boundaries stays permissive — `let b: u64 = a` (a: i64), `let s: u32 = big` (big: i64 = 5e9, out of range but only known at runtime), and fn-arg/return with variable operands are still silently accepted. Catching those requires either a strict `as`-at-boundaries rule (option (a)) — which needs the len()-returns-i64 corpus migration (word_count.kara's `total: u64 = words.len()`) and/or reconsidering whether len() should be u64 — or a runtime range check. Left as the parked language-design call; the literal cases were the concrete soundness smoking guns and are fixed. FIXED 2026-07-09 (variable half — design decision (B), widening-only implicit int coercion): the remaining runtime-only hole is closed. A NON-literal integer value at a check-mode coercion boundary (let / arg / return / struct-field — all funnel through check_expr) must now WIDEN; a narrowing or sign-changing coercion (`let s: u32 = big_i64`, `u64 = v.len()` where len() returns i64, any signed→unsigned, u64→i64) is a type error naming the required `as`. Widening stays implicit (i32→i64, u8→u32, u8→i16 strictly-wider unsigned→signed). Implemented in src/typechecker/types.rs (`int_signed_width` + `int_coercion_is_widening`, the (B) predicate) and src/typechecker/exprs.rs (`check_int_widening_coercion`, called in check_expr before check_assignable; skips literals — value-checked already, so `let a: u64 = 5i64` stays fine — and non-concrete/float types). NOTE on the bundled len()→usize migration: it proved UNNECESSARY. Empirical blast radius of the widening rule across the whole corpus was ONE site (examples/leetcode/course_schedule.kara: `for i in 0..num_courses` typed the index i64 against a u64 bound, then passed it to a u64 param — a genuine latent i64→u64 the rule surfaced; fixed by `0u64..num_courses`), and ZERO in the Rust test suite. len() into an unsigned slot barely occurs; len() is overwhelmingly used in i64 comparisons/arithmetic, which the rule does not touch — so migrating len()→usize would have ADDED churn (every `0..v.len()` range / comparison) rather than absorbed it, for a semantic nicety orthogonal to this bug. Left as an optional standalone follow-up. Gates: full non-llvm suite (7572) + codegen E2E (2103) + par_codegen (153) + cli (516) green; new tests/typechecker.rs::{test_variable_narrowing_or_signchange_coercion_requires_as, test_variable_widening_coercion_still_implicit, test_in_range_int_literal_coercion_still_admitted}; corpus scan shows zero remaining widening errors; fmt + clippy clean.

fix: 4c90993d (classifier + signature-match tests + failed Stage 4 Windows CI job attempt) + <this commit> (drop the infeasible Windows execution leg; Linux forced-arch signature-match is the correctness gate)
tracker: src/codegen/driver.rs (native_target_is_windows_x86_64) + src/codegen/types_lowering.rs (win_x64_repr_c_struct_param_class / win_x64_repr_c_struct_return_class) + .github/workflows/ci.yml (codegen-e2e Linux job: KARAC_FORCE_TARGET_ARCH=windows_x86_64 signature-match step)
title: Windows x64 `#[repr(C)]` struct-by-value ABI is unhandled — the raw-struct lowering does not match the Microsoft x64 calling convention, a latent silent-miscompile for any `pub extern "C" fn` taking/returning a `#[repr(C)]` struct by value on a Windows target. Discovered while implementing B-2026-07-09-2 Slice 3c (SysV x86-64 >16B byval/sret). The Microsoft x64 convention differs from SysV: an aggregate whose size is not 1/2/4/8 bytes (i.e. 3/5/6/7 or >8 bytes) is passed/returned BY REFERENCE (a pointer to a caller-allocated copy), NOT in registers and NOT SysV-`byval`-on-stack; only power-of-2 sizes up to 8 bytes go in a single register. So neither the raw-struct lowering NOR the SysV byval/sret path (B-2026-07-09-2 Slice 3c) is correct on Windows. To avoid applying an unverified SysV convention there, `native_target_is_x86_64()` excludes the `*-windows-*` triple, so Windows keeps its (also-unverified) raw-struct lowering rather than a wrong SysV one — no regression vs the prior state, but the gap is now explicit. FIX LANDED (2026-07-09): a Windows-x64 aggregate ABI classifier is now in codegen. Params: 1/2/4/8-byte structs → `Coerced(iN)` (single integer register); every other size → `Indirect` as a plain `ptr` (NO `byval` attribute — the Microsoft convention is caller-owned copy passed by address, which a plain `ptr` captures; SysV's `byval` exists to model callee-side copy semantics Windows doesn't need). Returns: same 1/2/4/8-byte rule → `Coerced(iN)` in RAX; everything else → `Sret`. Wired into `functions.rs` alongside the existing AArch64 (AAPCS) and x86-64 SysV branches through the new `target_is_windows_x86_64` gate (SysV `target_is_x86_64` stays deliberately Windows-excluded, so the SysV `byval` application never fires on Windows). Internal Kara calls to a Windows-classified export are rejected with the broadened boxed-export message. VERIFIED LOCALLY without a Windows LLVM 18 install: signature-match tests in `tests/abi_repr_c_struct.rs` under `KARAC_FORCE_TARGET_ARCH=windows_x86_64` cover the six key shapes (single i32/i64 register coercion, {i32,i32} 8B → i64, 16B and >16B indirect ptr, all-float NON-HFA fallback, small/big returns, and the combined sret+indirect-ptr param case) — identical IR lowers identically through LLVM, so signature match = ABI match; the same test file uses this trick for arm64 successfully (B-2026-07-09-2). NATIVE WINDOWS CI LEG (Stage 4) DEFERRED (2026-07-10): the `codegen-e2e-windows` job attempted in PR #1 ran three times and hit a hard upstream ecosystem gap on the third — `llvm-sys 181` requires `llvm-config.exe` at `<PREFIX>/bin/`, and the official LLVM Windows NSIS installer (and every downstream: Chocolatey `llvm`, `KyleMayes/install-llvm-action`, direct download) OMITS it. In-tree probe confirmed: extracted LLVM 18.1.8 Windows tree contains `clang-*.exe`, `lld*.exe`, headers, and libs, but no `llvm-config*`. Alternatives (shim script, MSYS2 MinGW LLVM, from-source LLVM) each add long-term cost that outweighs a nice-to-have execution proof, so the leg is dropped from the CI matrix and the roadmap Tier 5 entry is updated to name the deferral. Reinstate once LLVM ships `llvm-config.exe` on Windows, an accepted community redistribution includes it, or someone signs up to maintain a shim. LOW-ish priority note stays true: Windows producer output (.lib/.dll) was previously unverified end-to-end, so no user relied on Windows struct-by-value exports before this fix.
resolution: Classifier + signature-match tests landed in 4c90993d and remain the correctness gate. The Linux `codegen-e2e` job runs `cargo test --features llvm --test abi_repr_c_struct` under `KARAC_FORCE_TARGET_ARCH=windows_x86_64` on every push and PR to main; identical IR lowers identically through LLVM so a signature match is an ABI match (same forced-arch trick that carries arm64 correctness from Linux). Verified green across three PR runs (29066039424, 29066527100, 29067120775 — all failed only at the deferred native Windows execution leg, never at the signature-match step). Native Windows hardware confirmation is deferred pending the upstream LLVM Windows `llvm-config.exe` gap being closed; the Windows classifier is complete + validated on every merge without it.

title: The diverging primitive `panic(msg)` is recognized by the typechecker (diverging list, typechecker/exprs.rs:752 & expr_method_call.rs:41) and by the effectchecker (`panics` effect set, effectchecker.rs:591) but is NOT registered in `PRELUDE_FUNCTIONS` (src/prelude.rs:1369), so the resolver rejects every call site with `error[resolve]: undefined name 'panic'`. It also has no codegen lowering (`compile_diverge`, call_dispatch.rs, handles only `todo`/`unreachable`) and no interpreter arm (`eval_builtin_diverge`, interpreter/eval_call.rs). Net: `panic("msg")` is entirely unusable today; the diverging-primitive need is met by `todo()`/`unreachable()` and by a failing `assert`. Surfaced while implementing `#[track_caller]` slice 6 (docs/implementation_checklist/phase-5-diagnostics.md), which lists `panic()` as an annotation target; that sub-item is MOOT until `panic()` is wired end-to-end. FIX (not done): add `panic` to PRELUDE_FUNCTIONS, a `compile_diverge` arm that calls `emit_panic` with the user message, and a matching `eval_builtin_diverge` arm — mirroring `todo`/`unreachable`. Once lowered inline like the others, it inherits `#[track_caller]` redirection for free (no attribute plumbing needed).
resolution: Fixed 2026-07-10. Wired `panic` end-to-end mirroring `todo`/`unreachable`: added `"panic"` to `PRELUDE_FUNCTIONS` (src/prelude.rs) so the resolver accepts call sites; a dispatch + `compile_diverge` arm (src/codegen/call_dispatch.rs) that `emit_panic`s the message then terminates the block with `unreachable`; and a dispatch + `eval_builtin_diverge` arm (src/interpreter/eval_call.rs + builtin.rs). Design choice: unlike todo/unreachable (which prefix their default, e.g. `not yet implemented: <msg>`), `panic("msg")` surfaces the user message VERBATIM since it is the explicit user-facing form; bare `panic()` falls back to the `explicit panic` default. Both backends compose the message identically. Being lowered inline, it inherits `#[track_caller]` redirection for free. Tests: interpreter `test_panic_records_runtime_error_verbatim` + `test_panic_no_arg_uses_default_message`; codegen `test_ir_panic_tail_emits_unreachable_not_ret` + `test_e2e_panic_prints_message_verbatim`. The phase-5 track_caller slice-6 sub-item that listed `panic()` as an emitter is now un-mooted.

title: `Result::unwrap_err()` / `Result::expect_err()` (the Err-extracting, Ok-panicking variants) have NO codegen dispatcher arm: `karac build` fails with `codegen failed: no handler for method 'unwrap_err' ... (method dispatch fell through)`. The Ok-extracting forms `Result::unwrap`/`Result::expect` ARE lowered inline (src/codegen/calls.rs ~L1371, panic on the Err tag), but the `_err` mirror variants were never added to the Result method dispatch (src/codegen/method_call.rs lists Option/Result methods without them). Surfaced while implementing `#[track_caller]` slice 6 (docs/implementation_checklist/phase-5-diagnostics.md), which lists both as annotation targets; that sub-item is MOOT until the codegen arms land. FIX (not done): add `unwrap_err`/`expect_err` arms in the Result method path that extract the Err payload and `emit_panic` on the Ok tag (the tag==1/Ok mirror of unwrap/expect). Because the panic lowers inline like unwrap, it inherits `#[track_caller]` redirection automatically. Verify interpreter parity (`karac run`) at the same time.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-10 (this commit): added `unwrap_err` / `expect_err` across all four layers — typechecker (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs: the two unwrap-family gates + a method-aware inner-type pick — `Result[T,E]` -> E, the SECOND type arg, for the `_err` variants — and the return-type arm), codegen dispatch gate (src/codegen/method_call.rs), the extract-or-panic lowering (src/codegen/calls.rs: `is_err_variant` panics on the Ok tag (tag==1) and reconstitutes the Err payload from the SAME overlaid payload words, inner type E; inherits `#[track_caller]` like unwrap), and interpreter parity (src/interpreter/method_call_optres.rs: mirror arms extracting Err / panicking on Ok). `karac build` no longer fails. Gates: codegen 2121 (+1 E2E test_e2e_result_unwrap_err_expect_err), full non-llvm suite, fmt + clippy clean; interp==compiled on scalar Err, fresh-temp heap `Err(String)`, and expect_err. NOTE: a LET-BOUND heap-payload Result (`let r = f(); r.unwrap_err()` / `r.unwrap()`) double-frees — but that is a PRE-EXISTING family-wide bug affecting unwrap/expect too (the extracted payload aliases the let-bound Result's buffer and its scope-exit drop frees it again), NOT introduced here; fresh-temp receivers and non-heap payloads (the common cases) work. Filed separately as B-2026-07-10-2.

fix: this commit (took fix direction (a): route generic `T.default()` in a mono to the concrete type). Three-part wiring: (1) `type_satisfies_bound` (src/typechecker/exprs.rs) gained a `Default` arm delegating to `type_supports_default`, so the `T: Default` bound discharges for `#[derive(Default)]`/primitive concrete args instead of falling through to the empty impl-table and rejecting every arg; (2) `compile_assoc_call` (src/codegen/assoc_call.rs) resolves `type_name` through `type_subst_names` at entry so `T.default()` keys on the concrete `S.default` (derived inherent fn) inside the mono, plus a `primitive_default_value` helper emits the correctly-typed zero for i64/f64/bool/char/String (the old i64-0 fallthrough zeroed only 8 of String's 24 bytes — a double-free); (3) the interpreter's `fn_param_mut_ref_flags` + a `primitive_default_value` intercept (src/interpreter.rs, eval_call.rs) close the interpreter legs for baked-stdlib `mut ref` write-back forwarding and primitive `T.default()`. Also fixed a latent ASAN-harness gap: `run_under_asan` skipped `desugar_program`, so any derive-dependent program miscompiled under it (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs). std.mem `take` now ships as a real generic source body. Tests: interpreter test_std_mem_take, codegen e2e_std_mem_take_codegen, asan_std_mem_take_heap_no_leak_no_double_free, typechecker test_default_bound_{satisfied_by_derive_and_primitive,rejected_for_non_default_type}.
title: Generic `T.default()` under a `T: Default` bound does NOT monomorphize in codegen (it runs correctly in the interpreter). `Default` is a DERIVE-ONLY builtin — `#[derive(Default)]` synthesizes a concrete INHERENT `impl Type { fn default() -> Type }` (src/desugar.rs synthesize_default_impls), there is NO `trait Default { fn default() }` declaration. Concrete `S.default()` compiles + runs correctly; but a generic body `fn make_default[T: Default]() -> T { T.default() }` monomorphized to `S` emits a PLACEHOLDER `i64 0` for the `T.default()` call instead of a call to `S.default()`, so `karac build` fails module verification: 'Function return type does not match operand type of return inst: ret i64 0 { i64 }'. `karac run` (interpreter) executes the same program correctly (prints the default). So the generic typeparam-assoc-fn dispatch (`T.method()` where `T: SomeBound`) does not resolve `default` to the concrete synthesized inherent assoc fn in the mono — likely the same family as the ownership generic-trait-method gap (B-2026-07-08-11) but for CODEGEN assoc-fn dispatch, and complicated by `Default` having no real trait method to route through (find_trait_method('Default','default') returns None; the interpreter is lenient and resolves it at runtime via the concrete instantiation). BLOCKS std.mem `take` (roadmap Phase 8): `take[T: Default](dest: mut ref T) -> T { replace(dest, T.default()) }` as a source wrapper compiles+runs in the interpreter but fails `karac build`, so `take` is deferred (swap/replace shipped as intrinsics). FIX DIRECTIONS: (a) route a generic `T.default()` in a mono to the concrete `<ConcreteT>.default()` synthesized inherent fn (needs the typeparam-assoc-fn codegen dispatch to consult the derive-synthesized impls at the resolved type); or (b) introduce a real baked `trait Default { fn default() -> Self }` and have the derive emit `impl Default for T` so normal trait-method mono dispatch applies (larger, but removes the derive-only special-case) — this also unblocks `take` as a plain source body. Repro: `#[derive(Default)] struct S { x: i64 } fn md[T: Default]() -> T { T.default() } fn main() { let s: S = md(); println(f"{s.x}"); }` — `karac run` prints 0, `karac build` fails verification.

Niche-optimized `Option[shared T]` value stored into a CONVENTIONAL 4-word field slot crashed codegen — and the crash was INVISIBLE because all three selfhost PARSER oracles were vacuously skipping on it. A niche-ABI `Option[shared T]` value is a bare pointer (null=None); a niche-returning fn like the self-hosted parser's `parse_else() -> Option[Expr]` yields one, and `let else_branch = parse_else(); IfExpr { else_branch, .. }` stores it into `IfExpr.else_branch`, a CONVENTIONAL `{tag,w0,0,0}` slot (IfExpr is a non-`shared` node struct, so its `Option[shared]` field is not niche-laid-out). `emit_rc_inc_for_captured_option` (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) assumed a struct value and panicked at `val.into_struct_value()`; with that guarded, the raw pointer was `insertvalue`'d into a 4xi64 aggregate -> LLVM 'Invalid InsertValueInst'. Pre-existing since before the 2026-07-09 pull window (bisect: every tested commit back to 5a5cbca is bad; the regressing commit predates it). MASKING BUG (test integrity): tests/selfhost_parser{,_types,_items}.rs classified only `error[`/`codegen failed`/`parse error`/`Module verification failed` as a compile error; a Rust PANIC matched none, so `!bin.exists()` fell through to the 'no llvm / missing runtime archive' SKIP and the oracle reported a vacuous 'ok'. Result: the ENTIRE parser port (slices 1-3c) was differentially UNVERIFIED for weeks while CI stayed green. FIX (706a71e): (1) `emit_rc_inc_for_captured_option` handles the niche pointer/int representation (null-guarded rc-inc) as well as the conventional struct; (2) new `widen_niche_option_ptr_to_field` normalizes a niche pointer to `{tag, w0, 0, 0}` at both struct-field-init sites (shared-struct GEP-store branch + non-shared insertvalue branch), a no-op when the value is already conventional; (3) all four selfhost oracles now treat a compiler panic (`panicked at`) or signal-kill (`status.code().is_none()`) as a HARD FAILURE, never a skip. Gates: codegen 2106/0, memory_sanitizer 562/0, clippy + fmt clean; the 7 existing `option_shared_niche` tests stay green.
resolution: Fixed the niche->conventional boundary conversion at struct-field-init + hardened the oracle skip-classification. NOTE: with the build panic gone the parser now COMPILES but crashes at runtime on basic inputs (B-2026-07-09-12) — the three parser oracles are #[ignore]'d pending that fix, so full differential re-verification is still owed.

title: Self-hosted parser BUILDS (after B-2026-07-09-11) but SEGFAULTS / heap-corrupts at RUNTIME on control-flow expressions. NARROWED 2026-07-09 (valgrind): the crash class is a for-loop over an owned `Vec[shared T]` (or a plain `Vec[struct/enum-with-shared-field]`) that CONSUMES each element by matching the shared enum and MOVING a heap child OUT of a NestedStruct payload. Minimal repro (double-free, valgrind-clean once fixed): `struct Id { name: String, span: i64 } shared enum Expr { Ident(Id) } fn render_expr(e: Expr) -> String { match e { Ident(n) => { let Id { name, span } = n; name } } } fn go(v: Vec[Expr]) -> String { let mut o = "".to_string(); for e in v { o.push_str(render_expr(e)); } o }` — returning the extracted `name` double-frees it against the shared box's `__karac_rc_drop_Expr` recursion into the `Id` NestedStruct payload. `Vec[String]` and `Vec[struct{String}]` for-loops are CLEAN; the bug is specific to a shared-handle element / shared NestedStruct payload whose extracted heap CHILD is moved out. ROOT: `suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs) zeros a moved-out `VecOrString` / `Map`/`Set` payload field IN THE BOX so the rc-drop skips it, but DELIBERATELY excludes `NestedStruct` — correct for the common case (a NestedStruct binding is a shallow VIEW; the box retains ownership and frees it, the binding does not) but WRONG when the binding's heap child is further moved out (then both the returned child and the box free it). TRAP (verified 2026-07-09): naively extending the suppressor to also zero NestedStruct fields fixes this repro but REGRESSES 17 memory_sanitizer tests (asan_match_byvalue_shared_enum_bind_without_consume, asan_struct_wrapped_*, asan_vec_of_shared_enum_*, …) into LEAKS — the shallow-view cases where the box legitimately owns the children. The correct fix needs an OWN-vs-SHARE distinction (does the arm move a heap child OUT of the nested-struct binding?), i.e. deep move-analysis, OR a deep-clone of the nested-struct payload at the shared-enum match bind (perf cost). ADDITIONALLY: the real parser's `{ a; }` render shows a DISTINCT corruption (the ExprStmt's `expr` reads back as `(error @1:1)` — a use-after-free during render, not just a drop-time double-free) that the minimal repro does NOT reproduce (needs the full 19-variant 152-byte Expr layout and/or the parse-side ownership), so there is >=1 further nested-drop bug beyond the one above. NEXT: decide the own-vs-share fix design; reproduce the render-corruption variant; then un-ignore selfhost_parser/_types/_items (keyed to this id).
resolution: OPEN. Investigated deeply 2026-07-09: precise minimal repro + root cause (shared-enum NestedStruct payload move-out double-free) identified; a naive suppressor extension regressed 17 asan tests and was reverted. Needs a careful own-vs-share design (move-analysis or deep-clone-on-bind), plus a second nested-drop bug (render use-after-free) still to reproduce. The 3 parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d. DEEPER ANALYSIS 2026-07-10 (fix design settled, NOT yet implemented): the double-free needs a heap child to be MOVED OUT of the shared-enum NestedStruct payload binding (repro F returns the extracted String); the single-value case is clean, Vec-sourced (for-loop OR v[i] index) is not, and `Vec[String]`/direct-`VecOrString`-payload for-loops are clean (only NestedStruct payloads whose extracted CHILD escapes). The shared-enum NestedStruct binding is a VIEW BY DESIGN: `pattern_binding.rs` ~L402 skips `track_struct_var` when `pattern_binding_scrutinee_is_shared_enum` (B-2026-06-14-31) because the binding aliases the RC box's inline buffer and the box's rc-drop is the sole owner — this is SOUND FOR rc>1 (you cannot move out of a shared box). Therefore my reverted 'suppress the box + let the binding own' idea is UNSOUND for genuinely shared (rc>1) boxes (it would strand the other references) and ALSO leaked (17 asan regressions) because the binding registered no drop. The SOUND fix is DEEP-CLONE-ON-BIND: when a shared-enum match arm binds a NestedStruct payload, deep-clone the struct into the binding (via the existing `emit_struct_clone_fn`) and register its `track_struct_var` drop, leaving the box UNTOUCHED (box frees its own original; binding frees its clone). Sound for any rc. TRADEOFFS to weigh before implementing: (a) a deep clone per shared-enum NestedStruct match — the parser matches AST nodes constantly, and cloning a node whose payload holds a `Vec`/recursive child is potentially expensive and exercises the clone path on complex nested structures; (b) it changes deliberate load-bearing design (B-2026-06-14-31) with existing regression tests. A more surgical alternative is CLONE-ON-EXTRACT-FROM-VIEW (clone only the specific heap field when it is destructured out of a shared-view struct binding) — cheaper but needs the destructure site to know the binding is a shared-enum-payload view (no per-binding flag exists today; only the per-match `pattern_binding_scrutinee_is_shared_enum`). RECOMMENDATION: implement deep-clone-on-bind gated hard on the full memory_sanitizer suite; fall back to clone-on-extract if the clone cost/regressions bite. BLOCKER on the deep-clone-on-bind approach found 2026-07-10: the clone infrastructure (emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr / emit_struct_clone_fn, src/codegen/clone_drop.rs) does NOT rc-inc a SHARED field — clone_drop.rs:~102 falls through to emit_primitive_clone_fn (a bare `*dst=*src` pointer copy) for shared/RC types. So deep-clone-on-bind is correct only for shared-field-FREE payload structs (repro F's `Id { name: String, span }` works), but would DOUBLE-FREE for the parser's real AST node structs (Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr] }, ExprStmt { expr: Expr }, …) which carry shared `Expr` fields the clone would alias without an rc bump. CONCLUSION: B-2026-07-09-12 is a multi-PART compiler feature, not a single patch — it needs EITHER (a) escape analysis (clone a heap leaf only when it escapes the arm), OR (b) a corrected deep-clone that rc-incs shared struct fields PLUS deep-clone-on-bind + drop registration; AND separately the distinct render use-after-free (the `{ a; }` `(error @1:1)` corruption, not reproduced by the minimal repro). All in load-bearing drop code that regressed 17 asan tests on the first touch. RECOMMENDATION: schedule as a focused, multi-commit task with fresh context; do not rush. The 3 parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d against this id; selfhost_lexer stays green; main is known-good. PARTIAL FIX 2026-07-10 (copy-supported payload sub-class — deep-clone-on-bind): the minimal double-free repro (repro F: a `Vec[shared enum]` for-loop whose arm destructures a NestedStruct payload and MOVES a heap child out — `let Id { name, span } = n; name`) is FIXED for payloads whose heap is fully outer-buffer duplicable. Mechanism: at the shared-enum match bind (src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs, the `!pattern_binding_is_borrow` struct arm) the reconstructed payload VIEW is upgraded to an OWNED deep clone IN PLACE via `emit_struct_clone_fn` + `track_struct_var`, leaving the RC box's original untouched (box frees original, binding frees clone — sound for any refcount). GATED on a NEW, purpose-built predicate `struct_clone_fully_duplicates` (src/codegen/param_own.rs), STRICTLY narrower than the existing `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct`: it admits ONLY String / primitive / `Vec`|`VecDeque` of a clone-duplicable element / nested such struct, and BAILS on any shared / `Option[shared]` / `Vec[shared]` / enum / Map / Set / Slice field. The distinction is load-bearing: `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct` describes what the deep-copy-ON-ENTRY path (`deep_copy_*_in_place`, which rc-incs shared elements) can duplicate, whereas the CLONE path (`emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr`) shallow-copies a shared handle with NO refcount bump. Gates: full memory_sanitizer suite GREEN (574, +1 new: asan_shared_enum_struct_payload_child_moveout_no_double_free — String-payload + Vec[i64]-payload move-out, looped x20 under LSan); codegen 2116, par_codegen 155, fmt + clippy clean; repro F interp==compiled==`helloworld`. STILL OPEN (the hard half): the self-hosted parser STILL CRASHES at runtime (`malloc(): unaligned tcache chunk detected`) because its real AST nodes (`Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr] }`, `CallExpr { args: Vec[Expr] }`, `Wrap { items: Vec[Expr] }`, …) carry `Option[shared]` / `Vec[shared]` / bare-shared fields, which `struct_clone_fully_duplicates` correctly EXCLUDES — so those payloads keep the read-only-sound VIEW behavior and a move-out of THEIR heap children is still a double-free. Fixing that half requires a refcount-correct, niche-aware deep clone in the clone infra, which was ATTEMPTED THIS SESSION and REVERTED after empirically confirming TWO concrete failure modes (both asan-caught): (1) making a shared-handle clone rc-inc DOUBLE-COUNTS against the existing `v[i]` `share_bare_shared_ctor_payload` inc → LEAK (23712 bytes / 988 allocs in asan_vec_shared_elem_into_some_returned); (2) a shallow clone of a niche-optimized `Option[shared]` payload SEGVs (the enum-clone path assumes a conventional {tag,words} layout — the B-2026-07-09-11 niche subtlety). So the clone-infra fix must (a) reconcile with the existing per-site rc-inc machinery to avoid double-inc, and (b) handle niche `Option[shared]` + `Vec[shared]` element clones. AND the distinct render use-after-free (the `{ a; }` ExprStmt.expr → `(error @1:1)` corruption) remains unreproduced/unfixed. The 3 parser oracles STAY #[ignore]'d against this id; selfhost_lexer stays green; main is known-good. SECOND ATTEMPT 2026-07-10 (wider deep-copy-on-bind — ATTEMPTED + REVERTED, 4 asan regressions): tried to extend coverage to the parser's real nodes by routing the bind clone through the deep-copy-ON-ENTRY machinery (`deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place`) instead of the clone path, adding the two missing arms it needs — (1) a bare-`shared` field arm in `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field` (rc-INC the box handle) and (2) a `Vec[shared]` element arm in `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place` (rc-INC each element, the 'distinct residual' the old comment named) — plus a wider bind predicate `shared_enum_payload_bind_duplicates` (= `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct` + bare-shared) and switching the bind to `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` + `track_struct_var`. The bare-shared arm was correctly CONTAINED (the param path gates on `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct`, which bails on bare-shared, so the arm is inert there). BUT the full memory_sanitizer suite regressed 4 tests, REVERTED: (a) DOUBLE-FREE on asan_shared_enum_boxed_struct_payload_moveout — the HEAP-BOXED payload bind case (`Blk(Block)` where Block is wider than the payload area) has its own boxed-payload drop machinery that deep-copy-on-bind double-counts against (the arm is read-only + extracts `b.tail`); (b,c) SEGV on asan_struct_wrapped_deep_build/_vec_field (`Vec[Expr]` = `Vec[shared]`) — the per-element rc-inc + outer-buffer copy for a `Vec[shared]` field MISLOWERS/segfaults, so the copy↔drop symmetry for `Vec[shared]` is NOT the naive 'rc-inc each 8-byte handle' (the element layout / boxed-vs-inline distinction matters); (d) LEAK (2300 B / 50 allocs) on asan_vec_of_struct_shared_and_option_field_consumed — the `Vec[shared]` element-inc arm ALSO fires on the PARAM path (`field_copy_supported` admits `Vec => true` incl `Vec[shared]`, so a copy-supported struct with a `Vec[shared]` field is callee-owned-copied), adding an unbalanced inc. TAKEAWAYS for the next attempt: the bare-shared field arm is sound + contained and can be reused; the TWO real blockers are (i) `Vec[shared]` needs a correct element copy that matches its actual drop (investigate the whole-Vec `Vec[shared]` drop path before assuming per-element rc-inc — the SEGV says the assumption is wrong), and (ii) the heap-BOXED shared-enum payload bind case is a distinct sub-problem from the inline one and must be gated out or handled separately. The render use-after-free remains untouched. Landed this session: ONLY the copy-supported half (commit ec23bc1); the wider attempt was reverted to keep main green. 3 parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d.
