ROOT CAUSE + STRATEGY REDIRECTION 2026-07-10 (dug into the Vec[shared] drop path): the Vec[shared] element LAYOUT was never the problem. `emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn` (runtime.rs:2029, the whole-Vec drop's per-element fn) is precisely `load ptr from slot -> null-check -> emit_refcount_dec_by_type` — an 8-byte RC handle per slot, exactly mirrored by the copy arm's `load ptr -> rc-inc`. So the SEGV/leak were NOT a bad element model. The real defect is at the STRATEGY level and the param-leak test proves it: DEEP-CLONE-ON-BIND IS FUNDAMENTALLY INCOMPATIBLE WITH SHARED CHILDREN. Contrast — repro F `let Id { name, span } = n` (Id = String-only, BUFFER-owned heap): deep-copy dupes the buffer, the destructure TRANSFERS buffer ownership to `name`, and `n`'s drop is suppressed (`suppress_destructured_struct_pattern_cleanup`, control_flow_match.rs:2805) — clean, because buffer-transfer survives drop-suppression. But the leak test `let CallNode { callee, args, nargs } = c` (CallNode carries bare-shared `callee` + `Vec[Arg]` whose `Arg.value` is shared): deep-copy-on-bind rc-INCs those shared children, then the immediate destructure SUPPRESSES c's track_struct_var drop — so the incs are never dec'd -> LEAK (2300 B / 50 iters). An rc-inc REQUIRES the bound var's drop to run to balance it; the parser's move-out pattern structurally suppresses that drop. Buffer-transfer survives suppression; refcount-inc does not. CORRECT STRATEGY (for the next attempt — do NOT retry deep-clone-on-bind for shared payloads): clone/inc-ON-EXTRACT, mirroring the EXISTING `share_bare_shared_ctor_payload` (call_dispatch.rs:4229), which rc-incs a bare shared handle at the CONSUME site (null-guarded, `rhs_yields_fresh_ref`-skipped) and is deliberately SCOPED (index-only) to avoid double-counting against the move/consumer-inc paths. Apply that shape: keep the shared-enum struct payload a VIEW (NO bind-time change -> zero interaction with the 17 shallow-view asan tests), and at the point a heap child is EXTRACTED/moved OUT of the view — the struct-destructure-let (`let CallNode { .. } = c`) and the field-access-move (`eval(b.left)`) — rc-INC each extracted shared child / deep-copy each extracted BUFFER child, so each escaping child is independently owned and its OWN consume/drop balances the extract inc (exactly how `v[i]` already works). This aligns with the rc-accounting instead of fighting the drop-suppression. Implementation surface: (1) mark shared-enum-payload view bindings (a per-binding flag/set — none exists today, only the per-match `pattern_binding_scrutinee_is_shared_enum`); (2) intercept the two extract sites for such sources. The copy-supported half (buffer-only payloads, commit ec23bc1) stays correct and is UNAFFECTED by this redirection — it works precisely because buffer-transfer survives suppression. The render use-after-free remains a separate third bug. 3 parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d. CLONE-ON-EXTRACT LANDED 2026-07-10 (destructure case — the redirected strategy WORKS): the root-cause redirection was implemented and VERIFIED. Instead of deep-clone-on-bind (which leaks shared incs against the move-out drop-suppression), the shared-enum struct payload stays a VIEW and each heap child is DUPLICATED at the point it is moved OUT via `let S { .. } = <view>` destructure. Implementation: (1) new state map `shared_enum_payload_view_vars` (codegen.rs, cleared per-fn in functions.rs) records a shared-enum struct-payload view binding + its type, populated at the view bind in pattern_binding.rs (the non-deep-cloned else branch); (2) `finish_owned_struct_destructure` (stmts.rs) gains a `view_src` branch calling the new `clone_on_extract_view_field`, which per-leaf DUPLICATES: a bare `shared` child -> rc-INC + track_rc_var (the leaf's consume/move balances the inc, the box's rc-drop balances its original ref — CRITICALLY this works because the LET-path `bind_pattern` does NOT pre-inc/track a shared leaf, unlike the match-value path, so there is NO double-count; and the existing shared-var move-suppression correctly disarms the scope-exit dec when the leaf is consumed — verified by asan); a String / `Vec[heap-free elem]` -> buffer deep-copy + track; a nested `struct_clone_fully_duplicates` struct -> deep_copy + track; (3) the `Option[struct/enum]` destructure path is guarded `&& !view_src` so it does not track an aliased view Option. Reused `rc_inc_shared_handle_at_slot` (null-guarded). Gates: memory_sanitizer 577 GREEN (+1 regression asan_shared_enum_view_destructure_bare_shared_child — nested BinNode with bare-shared left/right destructured + recursively consumed, looped x20 under LSan), codegen 2120, fmt + clippy clean; the bare-shared view-destructure repro is interp==compiled. RESIDUAL (parser still crashes — malloc unaligned tcache — so oracles STAY #[ignore]'d): `clone_on_extract_view_field` deliberately BAILS (keeps status-quo view alias) on `Vec[shared]` / `Vec[String|agg]` / `Option` / `Map` / `Set` LEAF kinds, and the whole mechanism only covers the `let S { .. } = view` DESTRUCTURE — NOT the FIELD-ACCESS-MOVE (`eval(b.left)`, `let tb = nd.then_block`), which is the other move-out form the parser uses (owned_struct_params already has a precedent field-access deep-copy at B-2026-06-10-2 to extend). NEXT STEPS, in order: (a) extend `clone_on_extract_view_field` to `Vec[shared]` (deep-copy buffer + per-element rc-inc — the element loop shape is in `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place`; the SEGV in the earlier param-path attempt was from applying it on the PARAM path, which this view-scoped path avoids) and to `Option[shared]` (rc-inc Some via `deep_copy_option_inline_payload_in_place` + the inline-option track); (b) add the FIELD-ACCESS-MOVE interception for view vars (mirror the B-2026-06-10-2 owned_struct_params field-access deep-copy, adding the bare-shared rc-inc leg); (c) then the render use-after-free. Each step asan-gated. Landed increments so far: ec23bc1 (copy-supported bind) + this commit (bare-shared destructure). CLONE-ON-EXTRACT COMPLETE for intra-function view move-out 2026-07-10 (5 increments landed) + RENDER-UAF ISOLATED as the remaining blocker. The redirected clone-on-extract strategy is now applied comprehensively to every intra-function move-out of a shared-enum-payload VIEW: DESTRUCTURE form (`let S { .. } = view`) covers bare-`shared` (rc-inc + track_rc_var), String / Vec[heap-free], `Vec[shared]` (buffer deep-copy + per-element rc-inc via rc_inc_vec_shared_elements + per-element rc-dec drop), `Option[shared]` (rc-inc Some + track_rc_option_var), and nested clone-duplicable structs; FIELD-ACCESS-MOVE form covers `let a = view.vecfield` for `Vec[shared]` (deep_copy_owned_struct_param_field_move extended to view sources) — bare-`shared` and `Option[shared]` field-moves were already balanced by compile_field_access's read-inc. Commits: ec23bc1 (copy-supported bind), 96271ee (bare-shared destructure), 56327fb (Vec/Option-shared destructure), 4f99c60 (Vec[shared] field-move). All asan-green (memory_sanitizer 579, +4 regression tests) + codegen 2120 + fmt/clippy. The self-hosted parser now renders EVERY simple expression correctly (literals, idents, unary, binary, call, field — verified by building selfhost/src and driving parse_and_print). REMAINING BLOCKER — the RENDER USE-AFTER-FREE (the distinct 'second nested-drop bug' the original entry flagged, now isolated): `{ a; }` renders as `(block .. (exprstmt @2:1 (error @1:1)))` — the `ExprStmt.expr` (a shared Expr handle) reads back as a garbage Error node while its inline `span` survives (classic UAF: shared handle freed, scalar intact). ROOT CAUSE is CROSS-FUNCTION, not intra-function: `render_expr` matches `Blk(block)` binding `block` as a Block VIEW, then passes it BY VALUE to `render_block(b: Block)`. Block is NOT copy-supported (shared `Option[Expr]` tail + `Vec[Stmt]` whose Stmt wraps a shared Expr), so `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` bails and `b` is a CALLER-RETAINS shallow alias of the box's payload (registered in `owned_struct_params`). render_block then `let Block { stmts, tail, span } = b` destructures and CONSUMES the shared contents (`for s in stmts { render_stmt(s) }`, each `render_stmt`/`render_expr` consuming a shared Expr by value → rc-dec), decrementing the box's children while the box (`render_expr`'s consumed `e`) still owns them → premature free / double-dec. The intra-function view map (`shared_enum_payload_view_vars`) does NOT propagate across the call, so render_block's `b` is not recognized as a view and its destructure aliases rather than duplicates. NEXT STEP (distinct, harder slice): either (a) make a shared-enum-payload view passed BY VALUE to a non-copy-supported struct param deep-duplicate at the CALL SITE (propagate view-ness / duplicate the view before the call), or (b) treat a caller-retains struct param whose fields transitively own shared handles as needing clone-on-extract on destructure in the CALLEE (extend the view map to such params). Both are cross-function ownership work — a focused fresh slice. selfhost oracles STAY #[ignore]'d; main is green. RENDER-UAF ROOT-CAUSED 2026-07-10 (fix identified, NOT landable yet — trades UAF for a leak): the render use-after-free is NOT the cross-function view issue previously hypothesized — it reproduces with a DIRECT owned Block (no view). Minimal repro: `shared enum Expr { Lit(IntLit), Blk(Block) }`, `struct ExprStmt { expr: Expr, span }`, `enum Stmt { Exp(ExprStmt) }`, `struct Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], span }`; `render_block(b: Block)` by value destructures + per-stmt consumes. ROOT CAUSE: Block is COPY-SUPPORTED (field_copy_supported admits `Vec[Stmt]` unconditionally), so `b` is CALLEE-OWNED (entry deep-copied via deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place). The entry-copy recurses `Vec[Stmt]` -> deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements -> ElemCopy::Enum(Stmt) -> deep_copy_enum_heap_payload_in_place -> (NestedStruct arm) deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place(ExprStmt) -> deep_copy_one_aggregate_field(expr: Expr) — but `expr` is a BARE `shared` field and deep_copy_one_aggregate_field has NO bare-shared arm, so the callee-owned copy SHALLOW-ALIASES the Expr handle; the copy's drop AND the caller's drop both rc-dec it -> UAF (in the renderer, ExprStmt.expr reads back as a garbage Error node while its inline span survives). THE FIX (add a bare-shared rc-INC arm to deep_copy_one_aggregate_field) makes the minimal repros CORRECT (interp==compiled, UAF gone) and the FULL asan suite stays green EXCEPT it introduces a LEAK (1920 B / 40 allocs = all the Expr boxes) in the specific shape where the element is MOVED, not retained: `for s in stmts` MOVES each Stmt into render_stmt (also callee-owned), whose entry-copy rc-INCs expr again, but the moved-out source doesn't retain a ref to rc-DEC -> the inc is unbalanced. This is the FRESH/MOVED-vs-RETAINED entry-copy distinction (the #17-gap family): make_aggregate_param_callee_owned entry-copies UNCONDITIONALLY, but a MOVED aggregate arg is already owned by the callee and must NOT be inc'd. The bare-shared arm was REVERTED to keep main green (a leak is LSan-CI-red; the pre-existing UAF is the known-open status quo). NEXT STEP: gate the entry-copy bare-shared inc (and by extension the whole entry-copy) on the arg being RETAINED, not a fresh/moved temp — i.e. skip the entry-copy inc for a moved-in aggregate (it already owns its ref), OR balance it with a dec at the move site. This is the same fresh-vs-retained gap #17 handles for buffers, extended to nested shared handles. SEPARATELY: with the bare-shared arm applied, a faithful minimal render repro (Block with tail:Option[Expr] + Span struct + String ident) PASSES, yet the real self-hosted parser STILL emits `(exprstmt @2:1 (error @1:1))` for `{ a; }` — so there is ALSO a distinct parse-time or deeper-shape issue in the actual parser beyond this entry-copy UAF, still to isolate. The 5 landed clone-on-extract increments (ec23bc1/96271ee/56327fb/4f99c60 + this session's earlier work) stay; memory_sanitizer 579 green; parser oracles stay #[ignore]'d. SPLIT 2026-07-10: the `{ a; }` -> `(error @1:1)` render corruption that THIS entry repeatedly attributed to a render/nested-drop USE-AFTER-FREE is, on isolation, NOT a UAF and NOT part of this bug — it is a DETERMINISTIC codegen WRONG-VALUE miscompile (interp!=compiled, exit 0, no heap corruption; the statement-expr field of a `Vec[Stmt]` reads back as a bogus `Error` node while the tail `Option[Expr]` is correct). Filed as its own id B-2026-07-10-1 (surface codegen, class miscompile). This entry (B-2026-07-09-12) is the MEMORY-SAFETY family only — the entry-copy rc-inc / clone-on-extract / nested-drop double-free work whose failures CRASH (exit 139). The remaining open work HERE is the render-UAF fresh-vs-retained entry-copy fix; the parser oracle needs BOTH this AND B-2026-07-10-1 green before it can be un-#[ignore]'d. RENDER-UAF DOUBLE-ENTRY-COPY LEAK CONFIRMED via IR 2026-07-10 (after the B-2026-07-10-1 fix): re-applying the entry-copy bare-`shared` arm (deep_copy_one_aggregate_field rc-incs a nested bare shared field) NOW fixes the render2 UAF AND keeps the WHOLE existing memory_sanitizer suite green (580) — the B-2026-07-10-1 `len`-zeroing removed the whole-struct-move variant of the old leak. BUT a NEW regression test for the render_block shape (a copy-supported struct passed by value whose Vec[Stmt] elements are consumed via a for-loop into ANOTHER callee-owned fn render_stmt) still LEAKS (1920 B / 40 = all the Expr boxes), and the real parser oracle CORRUPTS ('malloc unsorted double linked list'). IR diff of render_block + render_stmt: BOTH entry-copy their struct param and rc-INC the same nested Expr handle (`p14.shf` / `viewdup.inc` in each), but the value flows by MOVE (render_block's b.stmts element is MOVED into render_stmt via the for-loop). So there are TWO entry-copy incs on one logical owner. render_block's entry-inc is meant to be balanced by its `b` scope-exit combined-drop's LEN-driven element walk, but the for-loop CONSUMES b.stmts out first (the walk skips) → that inc LEAKS; render_stmt's own entry-inc + render_expr consume-dec net zero. ROOT: `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` entry-copies UNCONDITIONALLY, but a MOVED aggregate arg (a for-loop element, a fresh temp) is ALREADY owned by the callee and must NOT be re-copied/re-inc'd — the fresh-vs-retained distinction (#17-gap family), NESTED-call variant. The arm was NOT landed (pushed once as af97e03, reverted 948d552 the moment the new test flagged the leak). The clean fix is to SKIP the callee entry-copy (and its shared-inc) for an arg known to be moved/fresh — e.g. a for-loop-owned element (`for_loop_owned_agg_vars`) or an `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp` arg — which needs the call-site's move/retain knowledge threaded to the callee-owned-param entry-copy (a cross-boundary change). This is the SOLE remaining blocker for B-2026-07-09-12; the double-free/UAF half is understood and the arm fixes it — only the moved-arg double-inc leak remains. Two other parser bugs from this investigation are already CLOSED: B-2026-07-10-1 (stmt-expr miscompile) and the intra-function clone-on-extract family (6 commits). SHARP RE-LOCALIZATION 2026-07-10 (residual parser crash is a CONSTRUCTION wild-write, DISTINCT from the render-UAF/move-out family above; NO code landed, main green at 5603620): on current main the self-hosted parser BUILDS, renders every simple expression correctly, and renders `{ a; }` correctly (B-2026-07-10-1 landed). The ignored `selfhost_parser` oracle still fails because the parser SEGVs (exit 139) on exactly 8 of the 150 corpus inputs — ALL of them `if`/`loop`/`for`/`while` with block bodies (`if a { b } else { c }`, `if a { b } else { }`, `loop { if done { break } }`, `{ for x in xs { f(x) } }`, `{ let r = if a { b } else { c }; r }`, ...). Bisected in the REAL parser: crash needs an `else` branch AND a non-empty THEN-block tail (`if a { } else { }` and `if a { b }` are BOTH clean; only `if a { b } else {..}` crashes). MINIMAL STANDALONE REPRO (no ast.kara needed): a `shared enum` variant carrying a STRUCT payload that has ALL THREE of [a bare `shared` field, a NESTED-STRUCT field that itself holds an `Option[shared]`, and an `Option[shared]` field] — i.e. `IfExpr { cond: Expr, then_block: Block, else_branch: Option[Expr], span }` with `Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr], span }`, built via `Expr.If(IfExpr { .. })` where `else_branch` comes from a FUNCTION RETURN (sret). Removing ANY ONE of the three fields makes it CLEAN (`cond+else`, `cond+then_block`, `then_block+else` all pass); `Expr.Ident(..)` and `Expr.Blk(Block)` (single nested-struct payload) are CLEAN. So the trigger is the 3-field COMBINATION, not payload size or the fn-return alone (a 3-variant enum with the same shape does NOT crash — needs the fuller Expr so the variant is packed a certain way). ROOT (gdb + asan, `KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0` for IR-faithful trace): the crash is DURING `Expr.If(..)` construction, an `incq (%rN)` rc-INCREMENT of a handle whose value is literally `1` (`SEGV WRITE to address 0x1`) — an `Option`s Some-tag word (=1) being treated as a shared POINTER and inc'd. The `Expr` box is 152 bytes (rc8 + tag8 + 17 INLINE payload words; the IfExpr payload is packed INLINE, not boxed), tag 19 stored at box+8, and the bad `1` is stored at a payload word AND inc'd. Crashes even with `else_branch = None` (the FIELD LAYOUT, not the runtime value) — so a nested `Option[shared]`s tag word is mis-located into a shared-handle slot during the INLINE shared-enum-variant payload packing / per-field capture-inc. FIX SURFACE (next session): the INLINE shared-enum-variant construction capture path — `build_shared`/`build_nonshared` in call_dispatch.rs + `coerce_to_payload_words` + the per-field shared-handle capture (`emit_rc_inc_for_captured_option` / bare-shared `apply_shared_transfer`) — mis-computes the word offset of an `Option[shared]` inner pointer when a nested-struct field precedes it in the payload; the individual captures read correct LOCALS in `compile_to_ir` (which BOXES this payload!), so the divergence is that `karac build` INLINES it while `compile_to_ir` boxed — reconcile the inline/box decision and the inline nested-Option offset. SEPARATE (likely a leak, not the crash): `__karac_drop_struct_Block` drops ONLY field 0 (`stmts`) then `ret void` — it NEVER drops field 1 (`tail: Option[shared]`); its sibling `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block` adds the tail drop but DOUBLE-DROPS the `stmts` elements (once via `__karac_drop_struct_Block`s internal per-elem loop, again via its own `nstr.vec` loop). `__karac_rc_drop_Expr`s If-walk is correct+complete. TECHNIQUES that worked (reuse next time): (1) drive the parser PER-INPUT by making its `main` read `Env.args()` and passing one corpus string per process invocation — build ONCE, loop the corpus in the shell; batching all 150 in one `main` gives nondeterministic heap-corruption messages (`unsorted double linked list` / `unaligned tcache`) that mask WHICH input crashes. (2) asan a multi-file `karac build` project by TEMPORARILY reading `KARAC_EXTRA_CC_ARGS` in `driver.rs::link_executable_exports` and building with `-fsanitize=address -g` (the runtime archive is not asan-instrumented, but the malloc/free interceptors catch the wild write). (3) `KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0` makes the binary map ~1:1 to `compile_to_ir` output for gdb. Oracles STAY #[ignore]d; main is green; no code changed this session. ROOT CAUSE FOUND + FIXED 2026-07-10 (the parser SEGV was an AUTO-PAR CORRECTNESS bug, NOT a construction/drop memory bug): the residual crash on every control-flow expression (`if`/`loop`/`for`/`while`) was the auto-parallelizer FALSELY PARALLELIZING sequential `mut ref self` calls. `parse_if` compiles `let cond = self.parse_expr_bp(0); let then_block = self.parse_block(); let else_branch = self.parse_else();` into a 3-branch `karac_par_run` group — but those three calls all take `mut ref self` and sequentially advance the SAME parser cursor (`self.pos`), so they have a strict ordering dependency and MUST run serially. Racing them corrupts parser state, producing garbage AST handles that the subsequent `Expr.If(..)` construction rc-incs (the `incq` of a `0x1` Some-tag value the earlier note chased). CORRECTION: the prior `SHARP RE-LOCALIZATION` note (construction wild-write / inline shared-enum packing offset / 3-field trigger) was a RED HERRING — the `0x1` handle was garbage produced by the raced parse, not a packing-offset bug; the single-file repros never crashed because they were not auto-parallelized (free-function calls / no shared `mut ref self`). Confirmed by `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` making all 8 crashing corpus inputs parse correctly. ROOT in `src/concurrency.rs`: (1) `method_effects_imply_receiver_mutation` only flagged receiver mutation via non-pure RESOURCE-effect verbs (`writes`/`allocates`/...), but a cursor advance (`self.pos = self.pos + 1`) is plain-field mutation with NO resource effect, so the three calls recorded no write on `self`; (2) the `StmtKind::Let` arm collected the RHS's reads + effects but NEVER its inner-WRITES, so even a flagged receiver mutation wouldn't reach `info.defines`. FIX: added `method_receiver_is_mut_ref` (a `mut ref self` receiver CAN mutate through the borrow, independent of effect verbs) OR-composed into the MethodCall write-collection, and made the `Let`/`LetElse` arms call `collect_expr_inner_writes(value, &mut info.defines)` (mirroring the `Expr` arm). Now `let x = self.mut_method()` records `self` as written → the three calls conflict (write-write on `self`) → serialized → no par group in `parse_if`. Gates: concurrency 110 (+1 regression `test_mut_ref_self_calls_serialize`), full non-llvm 7590, par_codegen 155, memory_sanitizer 584, codegen 2127, fmt+clippy clean. The `selfhost_parser` (expression) oracle is UN-IGNORED and passes (150/150 corpus). REMAINING (separate bug, filed B-2026-07-10-3): the `selfhost_parser_items`/`_types` oracles still crash — but that is a DISTINCT render-time `free()` double-free in `render_item`'s drop walk (heap corruption, NOT auto-par: fails with `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` too), a residual of the original nested-drop double-free family on the wider item AST (FnDefNode / EnumDefNode / TraitDefNode). B-2026-07-09-12 (the control-flow-expression parser SEGV) is CLOSED by this commit.

fix: this commit. ROOT CAUSE was NOT struct-key-specific (the original entry misattributed it): the trigger is a `let` binding between a borrow-returning collection accessor and the consuming match — `let g = coll.get(k); match g { Some(v) => <move/drop v> }`. `Map.get` returns `Option[V]` whose payload ALIASES the bucket's stored value (and Vec/Slice/Array `.get`/`.first`/`.last` return `Option[ref T]` aliasing element storage); the codegen borrow-alias protection (`scrutinee_is_borrow_call` + `clone_escaping_borrow_payload_binding`) only recognized a DIRECT `match coll.get(k)` method-call scrutinee. Through the intermediate `let g`, the scrutinee is an IDENTIFIER, so the protection was skipped: an arm that moved/dropped the payload freed the aliased buffer a second time against the collection's own value/element drop (`karac_map_free_with_drop_vec`). The struct-key repro in the original entry just happened to trip glibc's tcache double-free detector (ASan/valgrind confirmed the free came from `main`'s payload drop, not the hash path — which is correct); the identical i64-key `let`-form double-freed too, and Vec.get `let`-form was the same class. FIX: a new `borrow_accessor_let_payload: HashMap<var, Option-te>` records a `let` binding whose RHS is a borrow-returning stdlib-collection accessor (Map/SortedMap `.get`; Vec/Slice/Array/VecDeque `.get`/`.first`/`.last`) at the let-site (src/codegen/stmts.rs). `scrutinee_is_borrowed_binding` re-admits such an identifier scrutinee into the borrow protection, and `borrow_get_payload_clone_te` recovers the payload type for it — so the Map payload clones on escape exactly as in the direct form and the ref-typed Vec payload self-gates to alias-only. Cleared per-function alongside `for_loop_borrow_vars`. Tests: codegen test_ir_map_get_letbound_moveout_emits_payload_clone; memory_sanitizer asan_letbound_map_get_moveout_no_double_free (bare-String value + struct KEY + if-let sibling, looped). Full suites green (codegen 2112, memory_sanitizer 569, interpreter 1138, par_codegen 154); read-only map.get stays zero-cost (no clone). RESIDUAL (known limitation): the fix covers the common `let g = coll.get(k); match g`/`if let` idiom, but the alias model is still fragile through OTHER indirections — passing `g` to a function, returning it, or chained rebinds (`let h = g; match h`) — because the borrow property is only propagated one hop. These are rarer (the ergonomic path is to match at the call site or `.clone()`). The principled long-term fix is to RETYPE `Map.get -> Option[ref V]` (like `Vec.get`) so the typechecker REJECTS escapes and forces an explicit `.clone()` to own — aligning the type with the codegen reality (the payload IS a borrow) and retiring the whole clone-on-escape patch family. That is an API/semantics decision (touches typechecker + interpreter + all Map.get usage), deferred to a design discussion rather than a bug-fix slice.
title: [root cause corrected — see fix] A struct-KEYED `Map[S, String]` (S a `#[derive(Hash, Eq, PartialEq)]` struct) double-frees a String VALUE under codegen (`karac build`/JIT) on the get-after-multi-insert path; the interpreter runs the same program correctly. Minimal repro: `#[derive(Hash, Eq, PartialEq)] struct Point { x: i64, y: i64 } fn main() { let mut m: Map[Point, String] = Map.new(); m.insert(Point{x:1,y:2}, "a".to_string()); m.insert(Point{x:3,y:4}, "b".to_string()); match m.get(Point{x:1,y:2}) { Some(v) => println(v), None => println("miss") } }` -> `karac run --interp` prints `a`; JIT/AOT aborts `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`. ISOLATION: the double-free needs ALL of (struct key) + (heap/String VALUE) + (>=2 inserts) + (a get). Narrowing that flips it back to clean: `Map[Point, i64]` (POD value, 2 inserts+get) is clean; `Map[Name{first:String}, i64]` (String-in-KEY, POD value) is clean; `Map[i64, String]` and `Map[String, String]` (non-struct key, String value, 2 inserts+get) are clean; struct-key + String value with ONLY 1 insert (no get) is clean; struct-key + String value with 2 inserts and NO get is clean. So the trigger is specifically a struct-key map's `get(...) -> Option[V]` when V is heap-owning and the table has >1 entry. LIKELY CAUSE: the struct-key `get` lowering returns the stored String VALUE by shallow copy (aliasing the bucket's {ptr,len,cap}) rather than an owned deep-copy / borrow, so the match-bound `v` and the map's own scope-exit drop free the same buffer twice -- the same ownership family as B-2026-06-20-8's `get_or` owned-copy fix, but on the `get`+struct-key path (the >1-entry / get combination suggests the struct-key hash bucket walk selects a value pointer the caller then adopts). The struct-KEY hash path ITSELF is correct (POD and String-field keys both hit the right bucket), so this is a VALUE-ownership bug surfaced by the struct-key get codepath, not a derive(Hash) miscompile. Found while surveying the roadmap `std.hash` item; NOTE for that survey: `#[derive(Hash)]` codegen for Map/Set keys already works (contra the roadmap's 'codegen path (interpreter form already shipped)' phrasing) -- the open `std.hash` work is the user-facing `Hash` trait + `Hasher` interface surface, not the Map-key byte-hash which ships.

PERF-REGRESSION (auto-par cost model, NO correctness impact): the default `karac build` (auto-par ON) fans out a ~70us-spawn parallel group in a hot function's PROLOGUE when it contains an empty-collection constructor, blowing the build up 40-66x vs its `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` seq lane, NON-DETERMINISTICALLY. `find_parallel_groups` marks a group trivial (-> sequential codegen, no spawn) when `all_pure || non_constant_count <= 1`, where non_constant_count counts statements `expr_is_constant_init` does NOT classify as zero-work. That predicate listed scalar/string/composite literals + identifiers but NOT empty-collection constructors -- yet an empty `Vec.new()` / `String.new()` only materializes a {ptr,len,cap} descriptor with no heap allocation (the first push/grow allocates; the allocates(Heap) effect is conservative for that later growth, which is also why all_pure is false). So a prologue like `let hexd=".."; let n = num & M; let buf = Vec.new();` (kata #405 to_hex) counts TWO non-constant stmts (the `& M` bitand AND `Vec.new()`) -> clears the `<= 1` gate -> emits a 3-branch par-block PER CALL. Over the millions of to_hex calls in #405's render loop the default build retires 370-608B (measured 503B) instructions vs 9.5B for the seq lane, with run-to-run variance characteristic of scheduler spin. Output is UNCHANGED (sink 2231199964 == seq), a pure perf regression. The kata's own bench.sh documents the workload 'does not auto-parallelize' (loop-carried buffer dep), so this silently violated that stated invariant.
investigation: SURFACED while taking B-2026-07-08-7 (the #405 allocator slice). Measure-first REFRAMED the premise: (1) the general 'kara allocator is 2.7x slower' gap does NOT reproduce -- a tiny-per-call Vec churn micro (2M allocs, push-loop both sides) runs kara 1.33B instr / 48ms vs rust 4.25B / 146ms, i.e. kara ~3x FASTER; (2) #405 to_hex single-threaded is 9.5B vs rust 9.2B instructions (~parity), 0.70s vs 0.58s wall (~1.21x, memory/IPC-bound), RSS 33MB < rust 36MB; (3) the documented #405 levers are all compiler-side -- escape-analysis elision (blocked, multi-day), SSO (in progress by a concurrent session, src/codegen/sso.rs stubbed, inline construction not yet on), and a push_char/push_byte fast path (which the roadmap claimed spec'd but does NOT exist on String today); a runtime free-list was already dead-ended (realloc no-win reverted, grow-floor +39% RSS). None move the ~1.21x wall gap, so no allocator slice was built. The ACTUAL high-value bug was this: default `karac build` of to_hex reads 40-66x its seq lane, non-deterministically, from auto-par spin -- root-caused via `--concurrency-report` to a parallel_group over [hexd literal][num & M][Vec.new()] in to_hex. FIX: `expr_is_constant_init` recognizes zero-arg `Vec.new()`/`String.new()` as constant-init, mirroring the byte-lit (kata-91, 2026-05-22) and ordered-output-probe (2026-06-14) precedents; drops non_constant_count to 1 -> trivial -> sequential, while main's legit outer `+` reduction still parallelizes (to_hex default build now 9.49B deterministic / 0.31s, FASTER than rust-ofl 0.58s single-core). Correct by construction: an empty constructor is never the work branch, so the fix only ever removes overhead-only fan-outs, never profitable parallelism. Map.new()/Set.new() EXCLUDED (may eagerly allocate a table). Also fixed en route: stale installed runtime archives (~/.local/lib + ~/.cargo/lib missing karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic, breaking every Vec.filled(n,0) link) -- refreshed. Regression test is discriminating (2 non-constant stmts pre-fix -> fails; 1 post-fix -> passes). Gates: concurrency 108, par_codegen 154, parallax 6, parallax_lite 5, codegen E2E 2106; fmt + clippy (--all-targets --features llvm) clean.

tracker: runtime/src/map.rs (karac_map_try_insert + try_resize + alloc_storage_fallible); src/codegen/maps.rs (compile_map_method try_insert arm); src/codegen/collections.rs (compile_set_method try_insert arm)
title: CODEGEN-GAP (no correctness impact — interpreter worked, `karac build` rejected cleanly): `Map.try_insert` / `Set.try_insert` were interpreter-only, the last open leaf of the phase-8 fallible-allocation API split (item 8). `karac build` rejected them with the actionable 'interpreter-only; item 8' message. Blocked on a prereq the ledger flagged: the custom open-addressing `KaracMap` runtime (runtime/src/map.rs) grows via `std::alloc::alloc` with NO null check, so an insert that needs to resize aborts/segfaults on OOM rather than surfacing a recoverable failure — there was no fallible `karac_map_*` entry point for the `try_*` companions to lower onto. FIXED: implemented the fallible runtime path + codegen for Map and Set (SortedSet split out — see below). Contract (matches the `karac run` interpreter oracle): `Map.try_insert(k,v) -> Result[Option[V], AllocError]` (Ok(None) fresh, Ok(Some(old)) overwrite, Err(OutOfMemory{bytes}) on OOM); `Set.try_insert(k) -> Result[bool, AllocError]` (Ok(true) newly inserted, Ok(false) duplicate).
investigation: Taken as 'the remaining task' of the phase-8 fallible-alloc section (the sole open `[ ]` leaf). SCOPING established: (a) the runtime map is a custom `#[repr(C)] KaracMap` open-addressing table, NOT std HashMap; growth (`resize` -> `alloc_storage` -> `alloc`) is the ONLY allocation an insert performs (the slot write is copy-only), so making resize fallible makes the whole op fallible; (b) Set and SortedSet share the `is_set` codegen path, and mono `Map[i64,i64]` methods GEP the SAME `KaracMap` struct (offset-pinned by `karac_map_field_offsets_match_codegen`), so a single generic `karac_map_try_insert` covers mono + generic + Set; (c) `Map.insert` returns `Option[V]` (old value) and `Set.insert` returns `bool` (newly-inserted) — verified against the interpreter, which the codegen must match. RUNTIME (runtime/src/map.rs): `alloc_storage_fallible` (null-checks each `alloc`, frees any partial, returns None), `try_resize` (allocates new storage BEFORE mutating any `self` field, so OOM leaves the map completely unchanged with no rollback; returns Err(attempted_bytes)), and `karac_map_try_insert(map,key,val,out_old_val,out_failed_bytes) -> i32` (0 fresh / 1 updated / 2 OOM), the fallible sibling of `karac_map_insert_old`. Added to `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` + a runtime unit test (fresh/update/growth across several resizes). CODEGEN: `compile_map_method` `try_insert` arm builds `Result[Option[V], AllocError]` reusing the panicking `insert` arm's `Option[V]` construction (`coerce_to_payload_words`+`build_option_some_via_phis`) wrapped in `Result.Ok`, with a status-2 branch to `build_alloc_oom_result`; `compile_set_method` `try_insert` arm builds `Result[bool, AllocError]`. Both use the generic pointer runtime path only (mono + borrowed-key fast paths are perf, orthogonal to fallibility). The duplicate-key / duplicate-element / overwrite-move-out no-adopt frees (B-2026-06-20-9/12 siblings) are mirrored on the fallible path so the single-drop contract holds. `insert` added to `CODEGEN_FALLIBLE_INSTANCE_BASES` so the item-2 build guard passes it through to dispatch (the guard test now exercises the still-blocked `Map.try_clone`). SortedSet.try_insert canNOT be built: SortedSet/SortedMap are ENTIRELY interpreter-only in codegen (even panicking `SortedSet.insert` falls through 'no handler for method'), so it is blocked on SortedSet getting any codegen backend — a separate larger slice, NOT a fallible-alloc gap; left open as its own checklist leaf. VERIFIED: 4 codegen E2E (Map[i64,i64] fresh/update/growth, Map[i64,String] heap-value Option packing, Set[i64], Map.try_insert `?`-propagation) byte-identical to `karac run`; 3 memory_sanitizer (heap-value overwrite move-out, heap-key duplicate no-adopt, Set[String] element duplicate no-adopt) green under LINUX ASAN+LSan via scripts/lsan-local.sh (macOS ASAN alone misses the leak class). Gates: codegen 2116, memory_sanitizer, interpreter 1138, typechecker 1938, effect 388; fmt + clippy (--all-targets --features llvm) clean.

title: CODEGEN-GAP (no correctness impact — interpreter worked, `karac build` rejected): `SortedSet[T]` was entirely interpreter-only under `karac build` — even the panicking `SortedSet.insert` fell through `compile_method_call` with 'no handler for method insert' (SortedSet was never registered in the codegen side-tables, unlike Set which lowers to KaracMap). So no SortedSet program could be compiled, and `SortedSet.try_insert` (the B-2026-07-09-15 fallible-alloc leaf) was blocked on it. FIXED: `SortedSet[T]` now lowers under `karac build`, sharing Set's `KaracMap`-backed storage for all order-independent ops and materializing ascending order only at the iteration + min/max observation points. Byte-identical to the `karac run` (BTreeMap) oracle for integer and String element types.
investigation: Picked as the next task after B-2026-07-09-15. SCOPING found this far more tractable than a new sorted-tree backend: (1) SortedSet.new already constructs a KaracMap (`build_map_new_handle_from_type_expr` treats Set|SortedSet identically); the gap was purely that the recognition helpers (`is_set_new_call`, `extract_set_elem_type`, `extract_set_elem_name`, `set_inner_type_expr`) gated on segment=='Set', so a SortedSet var never entered `set_elem_types` and every method fell through. (2) Most SortedSet methods (insert/contains/remove/len/is_empty/try_insert/union/intersection/difference) are ORDER-INDEPENDENT and reuse `compile_set_method` verbatim once the var is registered; only the ITERATION-producing ops (for-loop) and min/max need ordering. FIX: extend the four recognition helpers to accept 'SortedSet'; add a `sorted_collection_vars` HashSet marked from the `SortedSet[T]` annotation (so plain Set is untouched). RUNTIME: `karac_map_sorted_keys(map, out_len, cmp_fn) -> *mut u8` gathers live-key pointers, sorts them by the codegen-passed comparator (Vec::sort_by), and returns a malloc'd ascending copy (caller frees). CODEGEN: `emit_sorted_key_cmp_fn` emits a LinkOnceODR `karac_sortcmp_<T>(*const,*const)->i32` for integer (any width, +char/bool) and String keys (reusing the karac_string_cmp path, same surface as Vec.binary_search); `emit_sorted_keys_buf` wraps the call. The sorted for-loop (control_flow_for.rs) walks the buffer by index with the SAME borrow-like element binding as the hash path (so the freed header buffer never owns the aliased key data — String for-loops carry over) and frees the buffer at exit (free(NULL) safe for the empty set). SortedSet.min/max (compile_set_method) read buf[0]/buf[len-1], CLONE the picked element via `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr` (so a returned Option[String] owns its payload independent of the freed buffer and the set's key), and free the buffer. VERIFIED byte-identical to `karac run`: ascending int + String for-loop, min/max, empty->None, dedup, and `let`-bound `union`; 2 codegen E2E (int, String) + 1 memory_sanitizer (String iter+min/max, exercising clone/borrow/free) green under LINUX ASAN+LSan (scripts/lsan-local.sh), not just macOS ASAN. No regression: codegen 2116, interpreter 1138, typechecker 1938, effect 388; fmt + clippy (--all-targets --features llvm) clean. SCOPE / follow-ons (own checklist leaf): SortedMap.* (the map sibling — same substrate + ordered keys/values/entries + get/entry); derived-Ord struct/enum elements (rejected at emit_sorted_key_cmp_fn, interpreter-only); an INLINE set-op iterable (`for x in a.union(b)` without a let-binding) is not yet marked sorted (the let-bound result is). Cross-ref [[B-2026-07-09-15]].

source: selfhost:parser-runtime — isolated while investigating B-2026-07-09-12's render corruption
tracker: selfhost/src/parser.kara::parse_block (Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt) push) + ast_render.kara::render_stmt; codegen packing/reconstruction of a nested shared field in a Vec element of a wide shared-enum payload
title: SILENT WRONG-VALUE miscompile (NOT a crash / UAF — distinct from B-2026-07-09-12): the self-hosted parser's block STATEMENT expressions read back as a garbage `Error` node under AOT while the interpreter is correct. `{ a; }` renders as `(block @0:6 (exprstmt @2:1 (error @1:1)))` COMPILED but `(block @0:6 (exprstmt @2:1 (ident a @2:1)))` under INTERP — a deterministic interp!=compiled codegen divergence, exit 0, no heap corruption. ISOLATED via a block-form bisect (selfhost project, driver calling parse_and_print): `{ }` (empty) OK, `{ a }` (tail-only) OK, `{ a; }` (one Exp stmt) -> stmt expr is `(error @1:1)`, `{ a; b }` -> stmt WRONG + tail `(ident b)` CORRECT, `{ a; b; }` -> both stmts WRONG. So the TAIL (`Option[Expr]`, a direct shared handle) round-trips correctly, but a STATEMENT expr (`Block.stmts: Vec[Stmt]` -> `Stmt::Exp(ExprStmt)` -> `ExprStmt { expr: Expr }`, a bare shared handle NESTED in a plain-enum payload inside a Vec element inside the wide `Expr.Blk(Block)` shared box) reads back as a consistent bogus `Error` variant with span @1:1. The exprstmt's OWN span (@2:1, from `expr_span(e)` computed just before the move at parser.kara:590) is CORRECT — only the moved `expr` field is corrupted, so the defect is in the packing/reconstruction (word-offset) of that nested shared field, NOT its span. The consistent (non-random, non-crashing) wrong value points to a FIELD-OFFSET / word-width packing miscompile in `coerce_to_payload_words`/`reconstruct_payload_value` for a bare shared field nested inside a plain-enum struct payload carried as a Vec element of a wide (19-word / 152-byte) shared-enum payload — the '#44 true-word-width' recursion path — NOT the entry-copy rc-inc / clone-on-extract / nested-drop family B-2026-07-09-12 covers (whose failures CRASH with heap corruption, exit 139).
investigation: 2026-07-10: interp-vs-compiled discriminator (built the selfhost span/token/lexer/ast/parser/ast_render project, ran the same main.kara under `karac build`+exec vs `karac run --interp`) proved interp correct / compiled wrong => codegen, not parser-logic/grammar. Block-form bisect localized it to `Vec[Stmt]` statement exprs (tail `Option[Expr]` is fine). MINIMAL-REPRO ATTEMPTS DID NOT TRIGGER IT (so the standalone repro is still owed): faithful standalone `.kara` programs replicating the exact shapes — `shared enum Expr { Ident(IdentExpr), Blk(Block), Wide(Big), Error(Span) }`, `struct ExprStmt { expr: Expr, span: Span }`, `enum Stmt { Exp(ExprStmt) }`, `struct Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr], span }`, with the parse-like `let e = ...; let sp = expr_span(e); stmts.push(Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt { expr: e, span: sp })); Expr.Blk(Block { stmts, tail: None, span })` construction AND the real render pattern (render_block destructures + for-loop + render_stmt destructures) — ALL round-trip CORRECTLY (interp==compiled==`block[aaa;]`), including a 19-word-wide `Expr` (18-field `Big` variant) to match the real 152-byte layout. So the trigger needs something more specific in the real parser than the isolated shape+width: candidates are the `mut ref self` parser-state threading, the `parse_expr_bp(0)` recursive-descent construction path (vs a literal), or an interaction with the full 25-variant `Expr` / other node structs' layout. NEXT: instrument or bisect the real parser (progressively simplify parser.kara/ast.kara until `{ a; }` goes green) to get a standalone repro, then fix the packing/reconstruction word-offset for the nested shared field.
resolution: MINIMAL REPRO FOUND 2026-07-10 (15 lines, interp==Ident / compiled crashes) + LOCALIZED to the STRUCT LET-BINDING, not pack/render: `struct IdentExpr { name: String, val: i64 } shared enum Expr { Ident(IdentExpr), Blk(Block) } struct ExprStmt { expr: Expr } enum Stmt { Exp(ExprStmt) } struct Block { stmts: Vec[Stmt], tail: Option[Expr], span: i64 }` with `fn mk_expr() -> Expr { let mut stmts=Vec.new(); stmts.push(Stmt.Exp(ExprStmt { expr: Expr.Ident(IdentExpr{name,val}) })); let block = Block { stmts, tail: None, span: 0 }; Expr.Blk(block) }` then reading `b.stmts[0]` back CRASHES compiled, correct under interp. EXACT TRIGGER (bisected by add-back): ALL THREE needed — (1) the outer struct is LET-BOUND before use (`let block = Block{..}; Expr.Blk(block)` crashes; the INLINE `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` WORKS); (2) the struct has an `Option[SHARED]` field (`tail: Option[Expr]`) — `Option[i64]` in the same slot WORKS, and dropping the tail field entirely WORKS; (3) plus the `Vec[Stmt]` field that reads back corrupt. A probe of `block.stmts[0]` placed RIGHT AFTER `let block = ..` and BEFORE the `Expr.Blk` pack already misbehaves (stack-overflow/garbage), so the corruption is in CONSTRUCTING/STORING the let-bound local `Block` (compile_struct_init writing the struct literal into the block alloca), NOT in the shared-enum pack (`coerce_to_payload_words`) or the reconstruct/render. HYPOTHESIS: a struct-field-layout / offset mismatch when a struct carries a NICHE-optimized `Option[shared]` field (1 pointer word, null=None) — the alloca-store field-init path (GEP by field, compile_struct_init) computes a different width/offset for the `Option[shared]` field than the struct's LLVM type / the SSA insertvalue path uses (which is why the INLINE literal, built via insertvalue, is fine but the let-bound alloca store corrupts the neighboring `Vec` field). Repro saved at examples-scratch B-2026-07-10-1-min.kara. NEXT: inspect compile_struct_init's field offset/width handling for a niche `Option[shared]` field vs the registered struct LLVM layout; the codegen (call_dispatch.rs::coerce_to_payload_words / control_flow_match.rs::reconstruct_payload_value) are likely NOT the culprit — struct construction/layout is. LOCALIZATION SHARPENED 2026-07-10 (corrects the earlier 'compile_struct_init' guess): let-binding the struct ALONE is CLEAN — `let block = Block{stmts,tail:None,span}; <read block.stmts[0]>` with NO shared-enum pack round-trips correctly (interp==compiled). The corruption is specifically the PACK of a LET-BOUND (alloca-LOADED) struct into the shared-enum payload: `Expr.Blk(block)` CRASHES but `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` (INLINE SSA literal, same fields) WORKS. So the culprit is `coerce_to_payload_words` (call_dispatch.rs:4324) / the oversize-BOX decision (`out.len() > num_words`) / `reconstruct_payload_value` coherence — the #44/#49 lineage — behaving DIFFERENTLY for a struct VALUE loaded from an alloca (let-bound) vs a fresh insertvalue aggregate (inline), when the struct carries a NICHE `Option[shared]` field. Hypothesis: the loaded-from-alloca Block's `Option[Expr]` field arrives with a different LLVM width/representation (niche 1-word ptr vs conventional 4-word Option) than the inline-built one, so `llvm_type_word_count` yields a different field word count, flipping the inline-vs-BOX decision between the pack of the two forms while the unpack (`reconstruct_payload_value`) assumes one — the classic pack/unpack box-decision mismatch (#44) but triggered by the alloca-load representation of a niche Option field, not the field count. This is a codegen PACKING bug (not struct-init, not memory-safety/drop). Next: dump + diff the IR of `Expr.Blk(block)` vs `Expr.Blk(Block{..})` for the same fields, focus on the `tail` field's extracted width and the `out.len()` vs `num_words` box branch; make the box decision + reconstruct consult the SAME niche-aware width for a loaded value. CORRECTION 2026-07-10 (refutes the niche-mismatch hypothesis): the non-shared struct's `Option[shared]` field IS a CONVENTIONAL 4-word slot — compile_struct_init WIDENS a niche ptr into it (exprs.rs:2101, `widen_niche_option_ptr_to_field`, B-2026-07-09-11), consistently for BOTH the inline and let-bound forms. So the built Block VALUE is identical either way; the struct layout is NOT the bug. The true minimal condition is that BOTH the let-binding AND the pack are required: V4 (let-bind + `Expr.Blk(block)`) CRASHES, but V5 (let-bind + read, NO pack) WORKS and V4_inline (`Expr.Blk(Block{..})`, no let) WORKS. So the defect is the INTERACTION of the let-bound local's cleanup TRACKING + MOVE-SUPPRESSION with the enum pack: when `block` (a tracked local carrying a `Vec[Stmt]` cleanup AND an `Option[shared]` cleanup) is MOVED into `Expr.Blk(block)`, the move-suppression (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` / the Option[shared] move handling) and the `coerce_to_payload_words` read of the just-suppressed local interact and corrupt the `Vec[Stmt]` field's words — plausibly the suppression zeroing a word at an offset miscomputed because of the 4-word Option field between the Vec (field 0) and span (field 2), or the box/inline num_words decision differing once the source is a tracked+moved alloca. NEXT (clear path): dump the IR for V4 vs V4_inline (via a throwaway `compile_to_ir` unit test, as prior #44/#49 fixes did) and diff the `Expr.Blk` lowering — specifically the move-suppression stores into `block`'s alloca and the coerce word extraction — to find the miscomputed word offset. The bug is in the enum-pack + move-suppression of a let-bound struct with a `Vec` field and a 4-word `Option[shared]` field. FIXED 2026-07-10 (root cause + one-spot fix): the corruption is NOT struct-init and NOT the pack box-decision (both inline and let-bound BOX the 8-word Block identically). The IR diff of let-bound vs inline `Expr.Blk` showed the let-bound form emits an EXTRA `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block(%block)` — the moved-out let-bound local's scope-exit COMBINED drop. That drop calls `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (frees the Vec buffer, `cap>0`-guarded) AND runs a SEPARATE, LEN-driven per-element rc-dec walk over `Vec[Stmt]` (rc-dec each `Stmt::Exp(ExprStmt)`'s shared `Expr` — the B-2026-06-14-28 combined-drop for a Vec whose element transitively owns a shared handle). The whole-struct move-suppression `zero_struct_move_caps` (call_dispatch.rs:3558) zeroed only the Vec `CAP` (field 2), which neutralizes the buffer free but NOT the LEN-driven element rc-dec walk — so moving `block` into `Expr.Blk(block)` (whose boxed payload co-owns the same Stmt elements / Expr handles) still rc-dec'd those handles → premature free → the stmt-expr read back as a garbage `Error` node (and heap corruption). FIX: `zero_struct_move_caps` now ALSO zeroes the Vec `LEN` (field 1) for a `Vec`/`VecDeque`/`String` field, so the len-driven walk skips too and the moved-out source's drop is fully neutralized (harmless when the element owns no shared handle — no such walk exists). Explains every observed condition: needs the let-binding (→ a tracked local with a scope-exit drop), the Vec field (→ the len-driven element walk), AND the `Option[shared]` / shared-bearing element (→ the walk does rc-decs); the INLINE form has no local, hence no drop, hence worked. Gates: memory_sanitizer 580 (+1 regression asan_letbound_struct_vec_shared_elem_moved_into_enum_ctor_no_uaf), codegen 2120, fmt + clippy clean; the self-hosted parser now renders `{ a; }` / `{ a; b }` / `{ a; b; }` statement exprs CORRECTLY (interp==compiled). The parser oracle still CRASHES on the fuller corpus — that residual is the SEPARATE B-2026-07-09-12 render UAF (`malloc unaligned tcache`, the entry-copy fresh-vs-retained issue), not this wrong-value bug.

fix: this commit. A generic (monomorphized) fn whose IMPLICIT TAIL expression is a bare `f"…"` double-freed the returned String under codegen (`karac run`/`build`); the interpreter was correct. `compile_mono_function` (src/codegen/mono.rs) lacked the InterpolatedStringLit-tail cap-suppression block that `compile_function` (src/codegen/functions.rs) has: when a function's final expression is a bare f-string, the loaded {data,len,cap} is the return value, but the f-string accumulator's queued `FreeVecBuffer` frees `data` between the return-value load and `ret` (a use-after-free the caller then double-frees against its own binding). `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return` only covers Identifier-tail moves, and the f-string acc is staged in `last_fstr_acc` only during `compile_expr`, so the mono needed the same post-compile `zero_vec_alloca_cap(last_fstr_acc.take())` guard. ISOLATION: only the generic + implicit-tail + f-string combination breaks — non-generic tail f-string, generic `let s = f".."; s`, generic `return f".."`, generic tail `.to_string()`/Vec-literal all already worked (they route through suppression or a non-tail path); even a no-interpolation tail f-string in a generic fn double-freed (so it is NOT Display- or param-interpolation-specific — it is the mono tail-f-string ownership transfer). Surfaced while verifying the Display trait surface (which is otherwise complete: user `impl Display`, `#[derive(Display)]`, the non-Display f-string typecheck rejection, and generic `T: Display` dispatch all work in both backends) via `fn describe[T: Display](x: T) -> String { f"item is {x}" }`. FIX: add the guarded `last_fstr_acc.take()` → `zero_vec_alloca_cap` block to `compile_mono_function` right after `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return`, mirroring `compile_function`. Valgrind-confirmed the pre-fix free came from the caller's read-after-free of the returned buffer; ASAN-clean post-fix. Tests: codegen e2e_generic_tail_fstring_return_codegen; memory_sanitizer asan_generic_tail_fstring_no_double_free (Display struct + primitive + String + no-interp tail, looped). Full suites green (codegen 2122, memory_sanitizer 581, interpreter 1139).
title: Generic (monomorphized) fn with an IMPLICIT TAIL bare `f"…"` double-frees the returned String under codegen (interpreter correct). `compile_mono_function` lacked the InterpolatedStringLit-tail cap-suppression that `compile_function` has. NOT Display-specific — surfaced via generic `T: Display` f-string dispatch.

tracker: src/codegen/calls.rs (try_compile_option_result_method extract path) — no move-suppression of a let-bound Option/Result receiver whose payload is extracted
title: `Option`/`Result` `unwrap` / `expect` / `unwrap_err` / `expect_err` on a LET-BOUND receiver with a HEAP payload double-frees. `let r: Result[String, i64] = ...; r.unwrap()` (or `let r: Result[i64, String]; r.unwrap_err()`) extracts the payload by reconstituting the {ptr,len,cap} words — a SHALLOW alias of the let-bound receiver's inline payload buffer — and returns it; the extracted String/Vec is then freed by its consumer AND by the let-bound receiver `r`'s scope-exit drop → `free(): double free`. A FRESH-TEMP receiver (`f().unwrap()`, `m.get(k).unwrap()`) is CLEAN (the temp has no separate scope-exit drop to double it), and a non-heap payload (i64) is clean (nothing to free), which is why the common cases work and this stayed latent. The extract path (`try_compile_option_result_method`, calls.rs) neither deep-copies the extracted heap payload NOR suppresses the let-bound receiver's drop (cap-zero). FIX: on a heap-payload extract from a LET-BOUND (identifier) Option/Result receiver, either deep-copy the payload buffer (mirror `clone_owned_vec_index_element`) OR cap-zero the receiver's payload words so its drop no-ops (the receiver is consumed by unwrap). Pre-existing (predates unwrap_err); surfaced by the B-2026-07-09-10 unwrap_err work.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-10 (this commit): the extract-or-panic path (`try_compile_option_result_method`, src/codegen/calls.rs) now calls `suppress_inline_option_result_binding_move(object)` after building the extracted value — zeroing a tracked inline/boxed `Option`/`Result` IDENTIFIER receiver's slot so its scope-exit drop no-ops (the receiver is CONSUMED by unwrap/expect/unwrap_err/expect_err and the returned value now solely owns the aliased buffer). No-op for a fresh-temp receiver (not an identifier — already single-owned) and for a non-heap payload (not in the tracked sets), so the already-working cases are untouched. Covers all four extract methods incl. the new unwrap_err/expect_err (B-2026-07-09-10). Gates: memory_sanitizer 582 (+1 regression asan_letbound_result_option_heap_unwrap_no_double_free — let-bound `Result[String,_]`.unwrap(), `Result[_,String]`.unwrap_err(), `Option[String]`.expect(), looped x20 under LSan), codegen 2123, fmt + clippy clean; `let r = f(); r.unwrap()` on heap payloads is now double-free-free.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while fixing B-2026-07-09-10 (unwrap_err/expect_err)

title: Returning a heap FIELD through a BORROWED receiver (`fn name(ref self) -> String { self.n }`, or a `ref` param) double-frees under codegen; the interpreter is correct. The borrow does not own the field, so codegen returned an ALIAS of the receiver's {ptr,len,cap} buffer, which the caller's drop of the receiver then freed a second time (and any reuse of the receiver dangled). `karac check` accepts the program (copy-out semantics; the interpreter clones). Fires non-generically; unmasked while making generic associated-type field-returns usable.
fix: this commit. `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` (src/codegen/runtime.rs) — the return-value hook run on the tail/return expr in `compile_function` — gained a `FieldAccess` arm: when the receiver is a borrow (`SelfValue`/identifier in `self.ref_params`) and the accessed field is a non-shared heap-owning type (`te_owns_heap_below_buffer` && not `shared_heap_type_for_type_expr`), the loaded field value is deep-CLONED via `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr(field_te)` (alloca src+dst, call, load) so the returned value owns an independent buffer. The receiver's struct name comes from `inferred_receiver_type(object)` (which resolves `self` too), the field TypeExpr from `struct_field_names` + `struct_field_type_exprs`. Gated on `ref_params` membership so an OWNED receiver's field move-out (handled by zeroing the source cap in `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex`, which requires the receiver to be dropped by this frame) is untouched — the two cases are mutually exclusive. Shared (RC) fields are left to the refcount machinery. ISOLATION: the receiver is used AFTER the call in the tests (`x.name()` then `x.n`), proving a move would be wrong and a clone is required; covers `ref self` / `mut ref self`, String and Vec[i64] fields. Tests: codegen e2e_ref_self_heap_field_return_codegen; memory_sanitizer asan_ref_self_field_return_no_double_free (looped, leak+double-free clean under LSan/ASan). Suites green: codegen 2127, memory_sanitizer 584, interpreter 1141.

title: The `?` operator does not support MULTI-WORD error types in codegen — a `Result[T, E]` where E is (or contains) a String / Vec / multi-field struct. `karac run --interp` handles it; `karac build`/JIT fails LLVM module verification. The common case is the CROSS-error `?` `From` chain into an error struct carrying a String message: `struct AppError { msg: String }` + `impl From[String] for AppError` + `fn run() -> Result[i64, AppError] { let v = may_fail()?; … }` where `may_fail() -> Result[i64, String]`. Same-error `?` and single-word cross-error `?` (e.g. `i64 -> struct{i64}`) both work; only multi-word E is the gap.
fix: this commit. The `?` error path now round-trips MULTI-WORD error payloads. `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (src/codegen/calls.rs) generic-struct arm advances a word cursor field-by-field, giving each field its own window of words (1 scalar / 2 Slice / 3 String-Vec, recursively for nested structs) via the new `payload_words_for_type` helper — so `AppError { msg: String }` claims all three words for its String field instead of insertvalue'ing a lone i64 into a 3-word slot. `compile_question` (src/codegen/exprs.rs) now (a) extracts ALL inner-error words gated on the INNER value's LLVM width, (b) reconstructs the `From` source arg at the param's true type via `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (a String param gets its full {ptr,len,cap}), (c) packs the converted target error back into the OUTER Err slot via `coerce_to_payload_words` across all outer payload words, and (d) the `main() -> Result[(), E]` exit path uses the converted value / reconstructs E from all words. Verified value-correct AND valgrind-clean across: same-error String, cross-error String->struct{String}, i64->struct{i64}, same-error Vec[i64], cross-error String->struct{i64,String} (4-word), and `main() -> Result[(),String]` exit. Full --features llvm suite green (codegen 2127, memory_sanitizer 584, par_codegen 155, cli 516).
detail: The `?` error path represents the Err payload as uniform i64 words (tag + w0/w1/w2), and multiple sites assume <= 1 word per struct field: (1) `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` (src/codegen/calls.rs:1503) — the generic-struct arm (line ~1577) inserts exactly ONE word per field, so `AppError { msg: String }` gets `insertvalue {{ptr,i64,i64}} undef, i64 %w0, 0` (an i64 into a 3-word String field) -> verify failure; it must consume as many words as each field needs (recurse into multi-word fields, tracking a word offset). (2) The `?` cross-error `From`-conversion arg marshaling in `compile_question` (src/codegen/exprs.rs ~1487) reconstructs only single-field structs from `w0` and truncates every other shape to `w0`, so `Target.from(e: String)` is called as `Target.from(i64)`. (A local fix using `rebuild_value_from_payload_words(from_param_ty, w0,w1,w2)` fixes THIS site but exposes the next.) (3) The Err-RETURN packing (src/codegen/exprs.rs ~1533/1571) `coerce_to_i64(propagated_payload)` collapses the multi-word target error to one word and inserts a single `propagated_word` into the Result Err slot; it must decompose via `coerce_to_payload_words(propagated_payload, payload_word_count)` and insert every word. (4) The main-result-exit path (~1544) and the enum-layout payload sizing for a multi-word-E Result must agree on the word count. FIX SHAPE: make the four sites use `coerce_to_payload_words` / a word-offset-aware `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` coherently so an N-word error round-trips pack->unpack. Substantial, memory-safety-critical (error-payload representation) — deferred for review rather than landed piecemeal. In-code comments already flag it ('a multi-word E sees only w0 here — the same documented limitation'). WORKAROUND: use a single-word error type (a bare String is NOT single-word; an i64 code or an enum whose payload fits one word is), or run under `--interp`.

title: Memory leak: `match … { Err(e) => println(e.heapfield) }` where `e` is a struct bound out of an enum/Result payload and the heap field is passed DIRECTLY as a call argument (not via an f-string) leaks the field's heap buffer. Narrow: a plain local struct `println(w.msg)` is clean, and the f-string form `println(f"{e.msg}")` is clean — only the enum-payload-bound-struct + direct-field-arg combination leaks.
detail: Repro (valgrind: `3 bytes definitely lost`): `struct AppError { msg: String } fn run(x: i64) -> Result[i64, AppError] { if x > 0i64 { Result.Ok(x+1i64) } else { Result.Err(AppError { msg: "neg".to_string() }) } } fn main() { match run(-1i64) { Ok(v) => println(f"{v}"), Err(e) => println(e.msg) } }`. The struct `e` is materialized from the Result Err payload in the match arm; `println(e.msg)` moves the `msg` String field out as a direct call argument. The move-out consumes the field but the residual struct/field buffer is never dropped on this path. CONTRAST (all clean): plain local `let w = AppError{..}; println(w.msg)` (lk1); f-string `println(f"{e.msg}")` (q1f); multi-field struct via f-string `println(f"{e.code}:{e.msg}")` (q6). So the gap is the intersection: enum/Result-payload-bound struct binding + heap field passed as a DIRECT call arg. Leak only (no double-free / UAF) — LSan/valgrind class, not ASan-UAF. Discovered while validating the B-2026-07-09-20 `?` multi-word fix; independent of `?` (repros with direct `Result.Err(...)` construction, no `?`). FIX SHAPE: the match-arm struct-payload cleanup must drop heap fields not consumed by the arm body, and/or the direct-field-arg move-out from a match-bound struct must schedule a drop of the containing struct's residual. Compare the (clean) f-string path to see how it wires the field read+drop.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-10. Root cause (asan + IR dump): a `match` scrutinee of an Option/Result whose INLINE struct payload (word count <= the seed enum inline area, 3 for Option / 5 for Result) is bound WHOLE as `e` never freed the struct payload's own heap fields. The dedicated inline Option/Result cleanup (FreeInline{Option,Result}Payload) frees only a bare {ptr,len,cap} payload (a direct String/Vec), NOT a struct payload's inner fields; and suppress_inline_{option,result}_payload_cleanup zeros the source cap because a `Some(e)`/`Err(e)` binding counts as consuming (pattern_consumes_field) -- so on BOTH the fresh-temp scrutinee (no source cleanup at all) and the named-binding scrutinee (source suppressed) the struct's String was owned by NOBODY and leaked. The ledger's original framing (only the direct-field-arg println(e.msg) shape leaks, f-string clean) was WRONG: the f-string form println(f"{e.msg}") leaks the SAME 3 bytes (it frees its own COPY, not e's original buffer) -- the leak is the struct payload binding itself, independent of how the body reads the field. FIX (src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs): the pattern-binding user-struct arm now also track_struct_var's a copy-supported INLINE Option/Result struct payload (new is_inline_optres_struct_payload gate), so its scope-exit StructDrop frees the fields; the same whole-value / field move-out suppression set (zero_struct_move_caps / zero_struct_field_move_cap) that makes the non-Option/Result arm sound covers `Err(e) => take(e)` / `Err(e) => e` without double-free. Gated to inline payloads via a new pattern_binding_scrutinee_optres_area (3/5) threaded from compile_match, so a heap-BOXED wide payload (owned by the box drop) is left untouched. Regression: asan_result_struct_payload_direct_field_arg (memory_sanitizer; 585 green incl. new test, + codegen 2127 / par_codegen 155 / clippy / fmt). if-let/while-let use a separate binding path (they do not set the flag) -- unaffected, not re-checked here.

title: `Vector[T, N]` (a fixed-size SIMD value) was not classified Copy, so a bare rebind `let e1 = ux;` where `ux: Vector[f64, 2]` was treated as a MOVE and every later use of `ux` fired a spurious use-after-move. Using a vector in an arithmetic expression (`ux * v`, `ux - uy`) was fine (a read, not a consume) — only a bare identifier-RHS rebind moved it, so aliasing a SIMD lane-bundle for readability was wrongly rejected.
detail: Repro (ownership check fails; `ownership_ok` expects clean): `fn mix() -> Vector[f64, 2] { let ux = Vector[f64, 2].splat(2.0); let uy = Vector[f64, 2].splat(3.0); let e1 = ux; let e4 = uy; ux + uy + e1 + e4 }` — errors `value 'ux' moved here, used again`. Root cause: `is_copy_type` (src/ownership.rs) enumerated Int/UInt/Float/Bool/Char/Unit + Tuple/Array(of Copy)/Slice(imm)/Pointer/Shared|Rc|Arc/Ref/Function/Named(derive Copy) but had NO `Type::Vector` arm, so a SIMD vector fell through to `_ => false` (non-Copy). A `Vector[T, N]` is a fixed-size, register-resident POD bundle of N primitive lanes — semantically Copy exactly like the fixed `Array` arm directly above it (and its lane type is always a Copy scalar). Fathom's Mandelbrot SIMD kernel never hit this because it only ever reuses vectors via binops / reassignment (`zr * zr`, `zr = ...`), never a bare `let x = zr;`; Slipstream's collide kernel aliases `ux`/`uy` (the lattice e1..e8 directions), the first bare Copy-rebind of a SIMD value.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-11. Added `Type::Vector { element, .. } => is_copy_type(element, tc)` to `is_copy_type` in src/ownership.rs, mirroring the `Array` arm (element-wise Copy). SIMD lane types are always primitive scalars so this is always Copy, but keying it on the element keeps the rule principled. Unblocks the Slipstream `Vector[f64, 2]` collide kernel (native oracle checksums byte-identical to the scalar kernel: 1582897806 / 793640938 / 680974524). Regression: tests/ownership.rs::test_vector_is_copy (348 ownership tests green; fmt + clippy --all-targets clean).

title: Self-hosted ITEM/TYPE parser crashes at runtime (heap corruption) — DISTINCT from the B-2026-07-09-12 auto-par bug. After B-2026-07-09-12 fixed the auto-parallelization of `mut ref self` calls (which unblocked the EXPRESSION parser oracle, now un-ignored + passing), the sibling `selfhost_parser_items` / `selfhost_parser_types` oracles STILL crash on ~28/102 item inputs (any `enum` with fields, `fn` with params, `trait`, `impl`, generic item, doc-comment/attr item) — e.g. `pub fn id(x: i64) -> i64 { x }`, `enum Shape { Circle(i64), Rect(i64, i64) }`, `impl Calc { fn add(ref self, a: i64, b: i64) -> i64 { a + b } }`. Crucially it is NOT auto-par: it fails IDENTICALLY with `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`. gdb shows the crash in `__GI___libc_free` (heap corruption / double-free) reached from `render_item` via inlined drop helpers (`render_item -> ?? -> free`), i.e. a DROP-TIME double-free while rendering/dropping an item AST node. This is a residual of the original nested-drop / shared-enum-payload move-out double-free family (B-2026-07-09-12's ORIGINAL description) on the WIDER item AST — `FnDefNode` (Vec[FnParamNode], Option[TypeExpr] return, Block body), `EnumDefNode` (Vec[VariantNode] each with Vec of field types), `StructDefNode` (Vec[StructFieldNode]), `TraitDefNode`, `ImplBlockNode` — whose Vec-of-nested-struct-with-shared-child drops are not fully covered by the 6 intra-function clone-on-extract increments that fixed the expression-node cases. REPRO (argv-driven, build once): copy selfhost/src, main reads `Env.args()` and calls `render_item(parse_item_str(argv[1]))`, then sweep the item corpus; ~28 crash. NEXT: asan-trace `render_item` on `pub fn id(x: i64) -> i64 { x }` (drop of a FnDefNode) to pin which Vec[nested-struct]/shared field double-frees; extend the clone-on-extract / drop-synthesis coverage to it. Also recall the sibling drop-synthesis inconsistency observed while investigating B-2026-07-09-12: `__karac_drop_struct_<S>` for a struct with an `Option[shared]` field drops only the FIRST heap field (misses the Option[shared] tail) while `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_<S>` double-drops the Vec elements — reconcile those two Block/item drop fns as part of this. PINNED 2026-07-10 (asan + gdb, argv-driven item oracle): the crash is `AddressSanitizer: SEGV` / `__GI___libc_free(mem=<garbage, e.g. 0x555555665>)` inside `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_FnDefNode`, reached via the RENDER path `render_item -> render_type -> <inlined drop>`. The faulting instruction is a `cap>0`-guarded String/Vec buffer free (`cmpq $0, 0x10(%rdi); free(*(%rdi))`) whose data pointer is a garbage sub-binary-base value (a SCALAR field misread as a `{ptr,len,cap}` header, or a stale slot after a render-consume). So it is a render-consume-then-drop double-free / field-offset error on the WIDER item nodes — the item-node extension of the expression render double-free family (B-2026-07-10-1 + the 6 clone-on-extract commits), now hitting FnDefNode `{ is_pub:bool, name:String, generics:Option[GenericParamsNode], self_mode:SelfMode, params:Vec[FnParamNode], ret:Option[TypeExpr], body:Block, attributes:Vec[AttrNode], doc:Option[String], span }` and FnParamNode `{ name:String, ty:TypeExpr(shared), span }` where `TypeExpr` is a `shared enum` (so `ret:Option[TypeExpr]` = Option[shared], `params[].ty` = bare shared). CONFIRMED sub-bug found while tracing: `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (FnDefNode.body) drops ONLY field 0 (`stmts`) and misses field 1 (`tail:Option[shared Expr]`) — its combined sibling `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block` patches the tail but DOUBLE-loops the stmts elements; reconcile those. NEXT: extend the intra-function clone-on-extract / drop-symmetry coverage (the B-2026-07-09-12-ORIGINAL family strategy) to the item structs, asan-gated on the full memory_sanitizer suite; a dedicated multi-commit slice. Debug technique: build the item parser with `main` reading `Env.args()` (argv[1]=item source), asan via a temporary `KARAC_EXTRA_CC_ARGS` link hook in driver.rs, `KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0` for IR-faithful gdb. ROOT CAUSE + FIX DIRECTION 2026-07-10 (precise): the double-free is the DESTRUCTURE of a CALLER-RETAINS struct PARAM that moves a shared/heap child out. `render_fn_param(p: FnParamNode)` does `let FnParamNode { name, ty, span } = p; ... render_type(ty)`. FnParamNode = `{ name:String, ty:TypeExpr, span }` with `TypeExpr` a `shared enum`, so it is NOT copy-supported -> `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` bails and `p` is a caller-retains SHALLOW ALIAS (registered in `owned_struct_params`, NO callee StructDrop). The destructure aliases the caller's heap; `render_type(ty)` CONSUMES the shared `ty` (rc-dec) AND the caller's own drop of the param dec's it again -> double-free. This is the SAME clone-on-extract issue as B-2026-07-09-12's shared-enum-payload VIEW case, but for a third source class: a caller-retains struct param. ATTEMPTED FIX (reverted — necessary but INSUFFICIENT): added a `retained_param_src` classification in `finish_owned_struct_destructure` (stmts.rs) that routes such a param's destructure through the existing `clone_on_extract_view_field` (same as `view_src`), plus gated the `Option[struct/enum]` leaf branch off it. This is SOUND for the leaf shapes clone_on_extract supports (bare-`shared` rc-inc, String deep-copy, Vec[shared]) but INERT for the item parser: every crashing item node has a field clone_on_extract BAILS on — `params:Vec[FnParamNode]` (Vec of struct-with-shared, not Vec[shared]), `ret:Option[TypeExpr]` (Option[shared]), `body:Block` (nested struct). So the FIRST unsupported field still aliases -> still crashes (28/102). REAL FIX (the deep slice): EXTEND `clone_on_extract_view_field` to (a) `Vec[<struct/enum with shared children>]` (deep-copy buffer + per-element `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place`, which rc-incs shared + deep-copies buffer children), (b) `Option[shared]` (rc-inc Some), (c) nested non-copy-supported struct (recurse), THEN apply the `retained_param_src` routing. Each shape asan-gated on the full memory_sanitizer suite. This is the residual tail the B-2026-07-09-12 view work also deferred; item nodes force all of it. Also reconcile the `__karac_drop_struct_Block` (misses Option[shared] tail) / `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_Block` (double-loops stmts) sibling inconsistency. RENDER-CONSUME HALF FIXED 2026-07-11 (a49a29f): the dominant crash was NOT the caller-retains destructure this entry earlier emphasized — it was the ENTRY-COPY path. `FnDefNode` / `EnumDefNode` / `StructDefNode` ARE copy-supported (`aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct` admits `Vec[..]` / `Option[shared]` / nested-struct fields), so `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` deep-copies them on entry when a `render_*(n: FnDefNode)` takes them by value. But the deep-copy duplicated the buffers WITHOUT rc-INCing the `shared` handles buried inside a `Vec[struct]` element (`params[].ty: TypeExpr`, a shared enum) or a nested struct (`body: Block`), while the combined struct-drop rc-DECs them per element (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr`) — caller's retained original and callee's copy both dec → double-free. Enum variant lists hit the same via `Vec[TypeExpr]` (a `Vec[shared]` element list). FIX: a scoped `deep_copy_rc_inc_bare_shared` flag, set only around `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned`'s deep-copy, making `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field` rc-INC a directly-nested bare `shared` field and `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place` rc-INC each `Vec[shared]` element — symmetric with the drop. (An earlier global attempt leaked because the drop side had not yet been reconciled; the #35 / double-loop-drain work since fixed it, so the entry-copy inc is now balanced. Confirmed: the retained_param_src routing / clone-on-extract-nested-struct I also prototyped were NOT load-bearing here — entry-copy rc-inc alone gives the same result — so they were dropped for a minimal 2-file patch.) Result: 34 -> 12 crashing item inputs; the whole fn/impl/trait/enum/struct/generic render-consume class is fixed. Gates: memory_sanitizer 587, codegen 2127, clippy + fmt clean. RESIDUAL 12 (all ATTRIBUTE items with a Some Option field — `#[key = "val"]`, `#[b(1)]`, `#[derive(Clone)]`; `#[a]`/`#[a] struct P` with no arg are CLEAN): a DISTINCT PARSE-SIDE premature free, NOT the entry-copy class. valgrind (real item oracle): the attr's String is `karac_string_clone`'d during `parse_item_str`, FREED during `parse_item_str` (a drop, ~0x13CFC7), then READ (memmove) during `render_item` — freed too early during parse, before the AST it belongs to is rendered. HYPOTHESIS: the match-arm binding `s` (a clone from a borrowed match) moved into an outer var via ASSIGNMENT has its arm-scope cleanup un-suppressed, so it frees during parse while the built AST still owns it. `#[b(1)]` (value:Option[Expr] via `value = Some(parse_expr())`) hits the same via the parse-result-assign variant. GENERAL MECHANISM CONFIRMED 2026-07-11 (final): the residual is the FOR-LOOP-ELEMENT VIEW MOVE-OUT, a general member of the B-2026-07-09-12 view-move-out family (not Option- or attr-specific). `compile_for_vec_var` (control_flow_for.rs:1057) binds the loop var `a` to a SHALLOW COPY of the element (load elem_ptr -> bind_pattern), so `a` VIEWS the element's heap in v's buffer while v's scope-exit FreeVecBuffer drains every element; moving ANY heap child OUT of `a` frees it and v's drain frees it again -> double-free. Discriminators (`let v=mk(); for a in v{...}`): E1 `match a.string_value{Some(s)=>push_str(s)}` CRASH; E4 `let AttrNode{name}=a; collected.push(name)` (String child MOVED out) CRASH; E2 `push_str(name)` (child BORROWED) CLEAN; E5 child bound but NOT consumed CLEAN. Trigger = CONSUMING (moving out) a heap child of the element view. The existing for-loop-element suppression only covers moving the WHOLE element into a consumer (defensive COPY at push/insert), NOT a child extracted from it. FIX (each substantial, asan-gate the large for-loop suite): (A) make `for a in v` over an OWNED Vec CONSUME v — per-iteration owned cleanup for `a` + suppress v's per-element drain (buffer-only); conflicts with the defensive-copy-at-consume model, which must be disabled for owned-consume loops. (B) register the element `a` as a VIEW source and wire clone-on-extract for every child-consume shape (struct-field destructure, Option/Result-field match, field-into-push). Recommend (A). Same multi-session view-move-out family as B-2026-07-09-12 — scope as its own slice. DESIGN CORRECTION 2026-07-11: the fix-A recommendation above ("make bare `for a in v` CONSUME v") CONTRADICTS design.md §2601/§2751 — bare `for` BORROWS the collection (`.into_iter()` is the consuming form), and `v` is empirically LIVE after the loop (`for x in v {} ; v.len()` works). So the render-side residual is NOT a consume-semantics gap: it is a VIEW-MOVE-OUT (moving an owned value out of the BORROWED loop element must CLONE — the correct borrow semantics), the SAME family/mechanism as B-2026-07-09-12, not a new consume model. The extracted leaf owns an independent copy; the container's per-element drain frees the original; neither double-frees. Closes 10 of the 12 residual item crashers. investigation:
INVESTIGATION 2026-07-10 (deep-traced with argv-driven item oracle + valgrind + single-file minimal repros; NO fix landed — changes reverted, main stays clean). The crash is a THREE-LAYER drop/ownership problem; the isolated first two layers are fixable and were fixed in a reverted branch, but the third (a pre-existing struct-drop synthesis bug) is the blocker and needs its own slice. TRIGGER (precise): a non-empty Vec[struct-with-shared] (fn/trait/impl params = Vec[FnParamNode]) OR Vec[shared] (enum tuple-variant tuple_types = Vec[TypeExpr]) field, consumed during render. Struct-def fields (Vec[StructFieldNode]) render CLEAN in the real parser NOT because StructFieldNode differs from FnParamNode (they are near-identical) but because StructDefNode has that ONE heap Vec field whereas FnDefNode has MULTIPLE heap fields (params: Vec[FnParamNode] + ret: Option[TypeExpr] + body: Block) — and it is the MULTI-heap-field struct DROP that miscompiles (layer 3). LAYER 1 (fixed in isolation): render_fn_param(p: FnParamNode) does `let FnParamNode { name, ty, span } = p; render_type(ty)`.
