When `p` is a CALLER-RETAINS struct param (owned_struct_params) the moved-out bare-shared `ty` (rc-dec'd by render_type) double-frees against the caller's own drop. FIX: a `retained_param_src` classification in finish_owned_struct_destructure (stmts.rs) routing such a param's destructure through clone_on_extract_view_field (same as the shared-enum-payload VIEW case) — its bare-shared rc-inc / String deep-copy / Option[shared] arms suffice. Verified clean (minimal repros mp, mf, min). LAYER 2 (fixed in isolation): FnDefNode/FnParamNode are actually COPY-SUPPORTED (field_copy_supported, param_own.rs ~293, returns true for `Vec`/`String` UNCONDITIONALLY without checking the element carries shared), so they are ENTRY-COPIED (make_aggregate_param_callee_owned), NOT caller-retains. The entry-copy of a Vec[struct-with-shared] field (deep_copy_one_aggregate_field Vec arm -> deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place -> per-element deep_copy_struct_heap_fields) copies each element's String but does NOT rc-inc its bare-shared GRANDCHILD (deep_copy_one_aggregate_field has no bare-shared arm — deliberately, since a DIRECT bare-shared param field must not be rc-inc'd there: the reverted moved-arg leak, B-2026-07-09-12). So the callee's independent copy aliases the caller's ty box and both drops free it. FIX: a `clone_on_extract_deep_copy_shared` flag enabling a bare-shared rc-inc arm in deep_copy_one_aggregate_field, set ONLY while DESCENDING INTO Vec[agg] elements (a shared grandchild buried in a Vec has no caller inc/dec path — only the element's __karac_vec_elem_full_drop_<S> rc-decs it — so the copy MUST rc-inc it; the top-level direct-field arm stays off). Verified clean in isolation (mfd_p = FnDefNode{name,params}; a = params unused; b = params consumed via count()). LAYER 3 (THE BLOCKER, NOT fixed): a struct with BOTH a Vec[agg] field AND an Option[shared] field mis-drops. Isolator `comb.kara`: struct FnDefNode { name: String, params: Vec[FnParamNode], ret: Option[TypeExpr], span } consumed with ret UNUSED still throws 11 valgrind invalid-frees, while {name,params} alone (mfd_p) and {name,ret} alone (mfd_r) are BOTH clean. So __karac_drop_struct_FnDefNode (or its interaction with the entry-copy of two heap fields) double-frees when a Vec[agg] and an Option[shared] field coexist — the same sibling drop-synthesis inconsistency already noted in this entry (drop_struct drops only the first heap field / vec_elem_full_drop double-loops). NEXT SESSION: fix emit_struct_drop_synthesis for a multi-heap-field struct FIRST (make {Vec[agg], Option[shared]} coexist drop each field exactly once), THEN re-land layers 1+2. Minimal isolator to gate on: comb.kara above (build single-file, valgrind, expect 0 errors). DEBUG LESSON: an E0401 (a fn-local enum variant `BareShared(StructType<'ctx>)` cannot carry the outer impl lifetime) silently FAILED two `cargo build` runs, so a stale binary was tested for several iterations — always confirm `Finished` (not just exit 0 of a backgrounded build) and, when in doubt, `strings target/release/karac | grep <new-symbol>` before trusting a behavioral result.

=== 2026-07-11 (session cont.) — RC-FALLBACK ROOT CAUSE FOUND + FIXED (a06a9e3), residual re-scoped ===
The doc-comment / attribute crashers turned out to be a DISTINCT root cause from the render-consume nested-drop family above — NOT a drop-synthesis gap at all, but a MISCOMPILE of `match`-then-reassign on a loop-carried Option/enum local. Reduction (all valgrind-confirmed): the 5-crasher item corpus (90 `/// a point\npub struct Q`, 97/100/102 doc+attr, 101 `#[rc_budget(max:5)]`) bisected to a ~11-line free-function repro with NO parser, NO tokens, NO heap: `fn collect() -> Option[i64] { let mut buf=None; let mut i=0; loop { if i>=1 break; i+=1; let has = match buf { Some(prev)=>true, None=>false }; buf = Some(7); } buf }` SIGSEGVs. Discriminators: needs the loop (straight-line clean), needs the payload BINDING `Some(prev)` (wildcard `Some(_)` / `is_some()` clean), needs the reassign after the match (no-reassign clean). ROOT CAUSE (from real IR with ownership loaded, then objdump): the ownership RC-fallback predicate boxes `buf` (auto-RC), so its slot is a `{refcount, value}` box POINTER; reads deref correctly but the whole-value assignment `buf = Some(..)` stores the 4-word Option struct straight into the 8-byte pointer slot (`store {i64,i64,i64,i64} into ptr %buf`), smashing it + 24 bytes of stack — the reload then treats the tag (0x1) as a pointer (`mov 0x9,%rbx` / `decq 0x1` / `free(0x1)` in the optimized asm). The existing code ALREADY skips boxing for `Option[shared T]` for exactly this slot-smash reason (stmts.rs:3041 comment); loop-carried plain-Option/enum slipped through. WHY the predicate fires: formal `rc_candidates` needs dominance-incomparable (C=match-in-loop-body, U=return-in-exit); the loop back-edge gives the exit an entry-predecessor so the in-loop reassign block does not *dominate* the exit, and the pure-dominance `reassign_kills` is (per its own doc) vacuous for the RC shape — misses the reassign every real C->U path threads. FIX: `reassign_kills_by_reachability` (rc_predicate.rs) — a block that rebinds the binding kills the value on every path threading it, so kill iff U is reachable from C in the FULL cfg yet unreachable once reassigning blocks are removed. Full-reachability guard preserves genuine branch-divergent / ClosureCapture / ContainerStore RC (their C,U carry no intervening reassign / aren't in a forward relation). Updated the now-outdated `rc_predicate_unaffected_by_reassign_kill` unit test (renamed `same_branch_reassign_after_consume_kills_rc_witness`, +companion `branch_divergent_consume_without_reassign_still_fires_rc`). Gates: fmt+clippy clean, ownership 348 / rc_predicate_parity 35 / rc_predicate 39 / codegen 2130 / memory_sanitizer 587 all green. IMPACT: item corpus 5 crashers -> 1; all doc-comment + `#[attr="str"]` cases now valgrind-clean.
RESIDUAL (still open, 1 corpus crasher — entry 101 `#[rc_budget(max:5)]` and, under valgrind, every attribute-with-ARGS / string_value case): a token-VIEW `Option[String]` double-freed through ENTRY-COPY render-consume. Reduction `vw2.kara`: `P.take(mut ref self) -> String { match self.toks[i].token { Str(n)=>n, .. } }` returns a payload that (for the Option consumer, unlike Vec.push which defensively copies) aliases the token buffer; wrapped `Some(nm)`, stored in a struct, passed BY VALUE through `render_item`/`render_node` (callee-owned entry-copy) which shallow-copies the `Option[String]` field (the `type_expr_has_drop_heap`==false-for-Option caveat) -> two owners -> the 39-byte payload freed twice. KEY DISCRIMINATORS: needs BOTH the token-view origin (fresh `.to_string()` = clean: ec1/ec2) AND the entry-copy consumption (match-in-main, no entry-copy = clean: vw3/vw4). This is the render-consume/clone-on-extract tail of this bug on `Option[heap]` fields, NOT the RC-fallback miscompile. NEXT: extend entry-copy deep-copy (make_aggregate_param_callee_owned / deep_copy_one_aggregate_field) and/or finish_owned_struct_destructure to duplicate an `Option[inline-heap]` field (mirror the `view_src`/clone_on_extract_view_field path already there for shared-enum-payload views), gated so it stays symmetric with the Option-inline drop; re-gate with the full asan suite (prior Option-drop attempts regressed 4 asan tests — see this entry's history). Isolator: vw2.kara (build single-file, valgrind, expect 0).

=== 2026-07-11 (session cont. 2) — SECOND ROOT CAUSE FIXED (0cb4456); residual narrowed to 5 attribute-ARG cases ===
After the RC-fallback fix (a06a9e3) took the item corpus from 5 plain-run crashers to 1, a valgrind sweep showed the remaining failures are a SECOND, independent double-free: an inline-heap `Option[String]`/`Option[Vec]` binding MOVED BY VALUE into a user function that OWNS+frees it was freed twice — the callee's param consumption frees the `Some` payload AND the caller's scope-exit `FreeInlineOptionPayload` also fires. Root cause: `String`/`Vec` by-value params use the CALLER-RETAINS model (callee deep-copies on consume, never frees — `owned_vecstr_params`), but an inline-heap `Option` param is CALLEE-OWNED, so the caller must relinquish it on the move exactly as the enum-variant-ctor / struct-literal field-init paths already do via `suppress_inline_option_result_binding_move`. That suppressor was wired for those two move destinations but NOT for a by-value call argument. FIX (0cb4456): call it in the user-call arg loop, gated OUT of a borrowed position (`borrow_skip`) and a return-passthrough (`call_arg_flows_into_return`); it self-guards on the arg being a tracked inline/boxed Option/Result local. Minimal repro `vw7.kara`: `P.take(mut ref self)->String` matches a payload out of `self.toks[i].token` (the token IS neutralized — take is correct), returns it OWNED, caller wraps `Some(nm)` and passes by value to `consume(sv: Option[String])` → double-free; `consume` returning `out` vs the fresh-literal source both matter (vw7 crashes, vwclean/ec2 clean). Gates: fmt+clippy clean, codegen 2134, memory_sanitizer 588 (Linux/LSan) green — no regression; corpus latent-UAF down from EVERY attribute case to 5, and all `#[attr = "str"]` string_value cases now clean.
RESIDUAL (still open — 5 attribute-ARG corpus entries: 89 `#[derive(Clone, Debug)]`, 91 `#[repr(C)]`, 94 `#[cfg(test)]`, 98 `#[a] #[b(1)]`, 101 `#[rc_budget(max: 5)]` — all carry a parenthesized `args` list): a DOUBLY-nested for-loop-element aliasing double-free of `AttrArgNode.name: Option[String]`. Path: `render_meta`'s `for a in attributes { render_attr(a) }` → `render_attr` `let AttrNode { .. args .. } = a` (destructures a `Vec[AttrArgNode]` field OUT of the for-loop element `a`) → `for arg in args { render_attr_arg(arg) }`. Bisection (real selfhost, items-proj): STUBBING `render_attr_arg` to `{ "".to_string() }` (drop `arg`, no destructure, no consume) STILL crashes → the double-free is the `for arg in args` element-move-into-a-consuming-fn against the container's per-element drain, where `args` itself is a Vec field destructured out of a for-loop element. NOT reproducible in isolation despite many attempts (fe1/fe2/sf1 — local `Vec[struct{Option[String]}]`, doubly-nested `Vec[Outer{inner:Vec[Inner{Option[String]}]}]`, struct-field-Option — all CLEAN or LEAK, never the crash): the faithful trigger additionally needs the strings to originate from parser token move-outs (`karac_string_clone` alloc, freed by a drop with a bogus 0xFF..7F caller) AND the `AttrArgNode.value: Option[Expr]` (Option[shared]) sibling field. FIX AREA (localized, not yet landed): `deep_copy_for_loop_agg_element_move` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) handles a for-loop element consumed as `let x = a` (whole-move) and `A { f: a.g }` (struct-literal field-init) but NOT (a) a for-loop element passed as a CALL ARGUMENT, nor (b) a `Vec[agg]` FIELD destructured out of the element then element-drained — either the destructured Vec field must be deep-copied or the container's per-element drain neutralized. HIGH REGRESSION RISK (this entry's history: prior Option-drop attempts regressed 4 asan tests); land only with the full memory_sanitizer suite green. Isolator to keep hunting a standalone repro: the items-proj harness (copy selfhost/src, driver reads Env SEL, `SEL='#[rc_budget(max: 5)] struct Budgeted { n: i64 }' valgrind ./parse`).

=== 2026-07-11 (session cont. 3) — RESIDUAL DEEP-DIVE: pinpointed to parse-side entry-copy aliasing of Option[String] nested in Vec[AttrNode] (fix still open) ===
Full IR trace of the last 5 crashers (all attribute-ARG cases: 89/91/94/98/101) via a temporary pre-opt IR dump on the real selfhost (items-proj harness). Findings, layer by layer, all CONFIRMED from IR:
- render_item entry-copies `item` (Item is a 27-word INLINE enum, payload not boxed); `match item { Struct(n) }` destructures. `finish_owned_struct_destructure` fires with callee_owned_src=Some for ALL three levels (StructDefNode←n, AttrNode←a, AttrArgNode←arg — confirmed via a KARAC_DBG_DESTR probe), and the source fields ARE neutralized: the struct-arm destructure zeros n's `attributes` cap (`sfld.move.cap1799`), so n's `__karac_drop_struct_StructDefNode` skips attributes; `__karac_drop_Item`'s `drop.Struct` arm is EMPTY (item never drops the Struct payload). So neither n's drop nor item's drop double-drains attributes — the render/destructure side is CORRECT.
- The drop synthesis is also correct: `__karac_drop_struct_AttrNode` drops path(Vec[String]) + args(Vec[AttrArgNode] via `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_AttrArgNode`) + string_value(Option[String]); `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_AttrArgNode` = `__karac_drop_struct_AttrArgNode` (drops name:Option[String] ONCE) + one rc-dec of value:Option[Expr](shared). name freed once, value rc-dec'd once. No double there.
- ROOT (from the O0 symbolized double-free trace): the "max" (AttrArgNode.name) buffer is a `karac_string_clone` DEEP-COPY that is FREED from within the `parse_item_str` call tree (scope-exit of a parser temporary), while the RETURNED Item still aliases it — so render_item's later read is a use-after-free and the drop double-frees. i.e. it is a PARSE-SIDE entry-copy/move-suppression aliasing: `attrs: Vec[AttrNode]` is threaded BY VALUE through the parser (`parse_attr_arg`→`parse_attribute`→`parse_attributes`→`parse_struct_def(…, attrs, doc)`), and somewhere in that chain the deep-copy/drop of the DEEPLY-nested `AttrNode.args[j].name: Option[String]` (a Vec[struct]-inside-Vec[struct]-with-Option[String], alongside the sibling `value: Option[Expr]` shared field) is asymmetric — the returned node aliases a temporary's clone that the temporary's drop frees. NOT reproducible standalone despite ~15 attempts (fe1/fe2/sf1/mp1/mp2/vw* — local Vec, doubly-nested Vec[Outer{Vec[Inner{Option[String]}]}], match-payload-destructure, +Option[shared] sibling — all CLEAN or LEAK, never the crash): the faithful trigger needs the strings to originate from parser token move-outs AND the exact multi-level parser node-passing. FIX AREA (localized to the deep-copy/drop symmetry, not landed — HIGH regression risk, prior Option-drop attempts regressed 4 asan tests): the entry-copy of a `Vec[AttrNode]` param and the AttrNode/AttrArgNode drop must agree on ownership of the nested `Option[String]` when the struct ALSO carries an `Option[shared]` sibling — either the parser node-passing needs the same by-value move-suppression my 0cb4456 fix added for a direct `Option` arg (but for a Vec[struct-with-Option] arg / struct-literal field), or `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` for a struct with coexisting {Vec[agg], Option[String], Option[shared]} fields is not fully symmetric with its synthesized drop. Isolator remains the items-proj harness; `SEL='#[rc_budget(max: 5)] struct Budgeted { n: i64 }' valgrind ./parse`. TWO fixes DID land this session (a06a9e3 RC-fallback, 0cb4456 direct-Option-arg), taking the corpus from 5 plain-run crashers to 1 and clearing all doc-comment/string_value cases.

=== 2026-07-11 — NEXT-AGENT HANDOVER (start here) ===
STATE: 2 of the 3 root causes in this bug are FIXED + shipped (a06a9e3 RC-fallback slot-smash, 0cb4456 direct-Option-arg double-free) → item corpus 5 plain-run crashers → 1, all doc-comment/string_value cases clean. ONE residual remains (5 attribute-ARG cases under valgrind, 1 hard-crash: entry 101 `#[rc_budget(max: 5)] struct Budgeted { n: i64 }`). Read the three prior `session cont.` sections above for the full IR trace before touching code.
DO THIS FIRST — do NOT attempt the fix blind. Crack a faithful minimal repro. Every synthetic attempt so far (fe1/fe2/sf1/mp1/mp2/vw*) LEAKS or PASSES, never double-frees — because they omit the trigger's two essential ingredients, which the deep-dive proved are BOTH required:
  (1) the `Option[String]` payload must ORIGINATE from a parser token move-out (`match self.tokens[i].token { Ident(n) => n }` / `StringLiteral(s) => s`, producing a `karac_string_clone`), NOT a fresh `.to_string()`;
  (2) the node must be threaded BY VALUE through the multi-level parser call chain `parse_attr_arg → parse_attribute → parse_attributes → parse_struct_def(…, attrs, doc)`, i.e. a `Vec[AttrNode]` (each AttrNode owns `args: Vec[AttrArgNode]`, each AttrArgNode owns `name: Option[String]` alongside a sibling `value: Option[Expr]` SHARED field) passed as a by-value param and stored into the returned struct.
Build the repro by MINING the real parser: start from selfhost/src/parser.kara's `parse_attr_arg`/`parse_attribute`/`parse_struct_def` and delete unrelated fields/paths until it stops crashing, rather than growing a synthetic up (growing-up has failed ~15 times). Confirm with `SEL='#[rc_budget(max: 5)] struct Budgeted { n: i64 }' valgrind ./parse` in the items-proj harness (copy selfhost/src/*, Env-driven driver).
THEN the fix (localized, HIGH regression risk — prior Option-drop attempts regressed 4 asan tests): the entry-copy (deep-copy) and the synthesized drop of a struct carrying COEXISTING {Vec[agg], Option[String], Option[shared]} fields must agree on ownership of the nested `Option[String]` when the node is passed by value. Candidate spots: `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` / `deep_copy_vec_aggregate_elements_in_place` (src/codegen/param_own.rs) symmetry with `emit_struct_drop_synthesis` / `__karac_vec_elem_full_drop_*`; OR extend the by-value move-suppression that 0cb4456 added for a direct `Option` call-arg to the `Vec[struct-with-Option]` arg / struct-literal-field-store shape. GATE on the FULL memory_sanitizer suite (Linux/LSan) + codegen + the un-ignored selfhost_parser_items/_types oracles before committing. Un-ignore `tests/selfhost_parser_items.rs` + `tests/selfhost_parser_types.rs` (both `#[ignore = "B-2026-07-10-4: ..."]`) once green — those are the acceptance gate.

source: kata:76-minimum-window-substring (byte-indexed count table `need[c]`/`have[c]` where `c: u8` from `String.bytes()`)
title: Indexing a Vec[T]/Slice[T]/Array[T,N] (read OR write) with a NARROWER-than-i64 integer index — e.g. a `u8` from `String.bytes()` used directly as a count-table index, the natural sliding-window / counting-sort idiom — emitted an INVALID bounds-check ICmp: codegen built `icmp uge i8 %idx, i64 %len` WITHOUT widening the index to the length's i64 type, so LLVM module verification failed ('Both operands to ICmp instruction are not of the same type!') and `karac build`/JIT aborted. The TREE-WALK INTERPRETER handled the same code (implicit widen), so it was a run==build DIVERGENCE (interp passed, codegen failed). Repro: `fn main(){ let b="A".bytes(); let c=b[0i64]; let mut v:Vec[i64]=Vec.new(); let mut i=0i64; while i<128i64 {v.push(i); i=i+1i64;} println(f"{v[c]}"); }` — interp printed 65, `karac build` failed verification. ROOT CAUSE: the ~12 Vec/Slice/Array/SoA index-lowering sites in src/codegen/calls.rs and src/codegen/collections.rs (plus tensor.rs) took the index at its own int width (`compile_expr(index)?.into_int_value()`) and compared it against the i64 length directly. The Column index path already went through `coerce_to_i64` (so column indexing with a u8 worked) — the Vec/Slice/Array paths did not. FIX: route every index through the existing `coerce_to_i64` helper (zext when narrower, trunc when wider) before the bounds `icmp` and the GEP, at all sites (calls.rs 3, collections.rs 9, tensor.rs 1). Regression test locks a u8-indexed Vec[i64] read AND write. Found by dogfooding kata-katas #76 Minimum Window Substring.

kāra is the SLOWEST of five compiled mirrors on kata #76 minimum-window-substring (M5 AND x86 container) — a two-pointer sliding-window bounds-check-elision gap. M5 seq lane (--warmup 5 --runs 30, all 99.7% CPU, single-threaded): go 265.3 < c 282.7 < rust -O 285.7 < rust_ovf (rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on) 312.3 < KARA 351.0 ms. So kāra 1.24x behind the C floor, 1.23x behind rust -O, and 1.12x behind EQUAL-SAFETY rust_ovf — both overflow-checked, so that 1.12x is a pure NON-overflow codegen gap. Container was worse (kara 789.6 vs c 510.6 = 1.55x C); the M5 narrowed it but did NOT reorder — unlike #73/#74 (which went kāra-last->1st/2nd on M5), kāra stays LAST here. EVIDENCE (warm, /usr/bin/time -l): instructions retired kāra 12.457B vs C 7.576B (1.64x) vs rust_ovf 11.141B (1.12x); cycles kāra 1.557B vs C 1.293B (1.20x, matches the wall ratio); IPC kāra 8.00 — the HIGHEST of the three (C 5.86, rust_ovf 7.79). So the gap is instruction COUNT, not pipelining: many CHEAP instructions retired fast, i.e. surviving bounds-checks, not slow ones. Binary 33.4 KiB (C parity), runtime RSS 1.4 MiB (C parity), compile 89 ms (~rustc) — the whole gap is the runtime hot loop. HOT LOOP (bench/minimum_window_substring.kara fn min_window): `while r < n { let cr = s[r]; have[cr]+=1; ...; while formed==required { let cl = s[l]; have[cl]-=1; ...; l = l + 1 } r = r + 1 }` with n = s.len() and s an unmutated `ref Vec[i64]`. DIAGNOSIS (leading hypothesis, and it is exactly a class this investigation already documents): the surviving check is `s[l]` — the window-left pointer `l` is incremented ONLY inside the conditional `while formed==required` shrink loop, so it is a CONDITIONALLY-UPDATED MONOTONE variable, precisely the class bce_monotonic_assume.md § Diagnostic pins as unfoldable (kata #26 `nums[k]`, kata #88 `nums1[i]`): its phi is not an AddRec and is self-referential, so SCEV gives up and LVI widens to overdefined, and LLVM cannot derive the RELATIONAL invariant l <= r < n on any pass ordering. This is the sliding-window sibling of the midpoint idiom (B-2026-06-16-1) — same relational-assume shape, different derivation. `s[r]` (r unconditionally ++'d) MAY also survive if the `let n = s.len()` capture blocks the AddRec==len proof. The count tables `have[cr]`/`need[cr]`/`have[cl]`/`need[cl]` are VALUE-indexed (cr=s[r] in the 4-symbol alphabet {0,1,2,3} but typed unconstrained i64), so they are bounds-checked in BOTH kāra and Rust and are NOT the differentiator — though a value-range narrowing (if karac tracked the alphabet bound) is a secondary opportunity. FIX DIRECTION: extend src/codegen/control_flow_bce.rs's relational-assume machinery (the binsearch_guard_pair / try_emit_binsearch_midpoint_assumes path that landed the midpoint pair) to the two-pointer shrink idiom — for a var `l` conditionally incremented under a guard inside a loop dominated by `l <= r` and `r < n`, emit `assume(l < n)` (and/or `assume(l <= r)`) at the `s[l]` use, gated on emission + soundness-checked identically (AOT overflow trapping makes the no-wrap precondition hold on all defined executions). CONFIRMATION NEEDED (first step of the fix slice, NOT yet done): otool/opt-dump min_window's post-inline IR (methodology in bce_monotonic_assume.md § Diagnostic), enumerate which of s[r]/s[l]/have[]/need[] icmp+cold-panic checks survive default<O2>, and verify injected assumes fold them (Experiment A/B style). ORACLE / repro: the kata itself — sink 487341820, kara-katas/leetcode/1-100/76-minimum-window-substring/bench/results.json (M5), dual-run reproducible (kāra 351.0 +/- 7.7 ms, tight). Not a correctness bug; perf codegen-gap. Relatives: B-2026-06-16-1 (midpoint idiom, landed), and the #26/#88 conditionally-monotone diagnostic rows in docs/investigations/bce_monotonic_assume.md. PHASE-0 CONFIRMATION 2026-07-10 (DIAGNOSIS REVERSED — the two-pointer BCE fix direction above is RETRACTED as UNSOUND; owner-approved to correct the record, not ship it). Did the IR confirmation this entry demanded BEFORE coding, via an ir_for() codegen-IR probe on a minimal two-pointer repro (`while r<n { acc+=s[r]; while acc>K { acc-=s[l]; l+=1 } r+=1 }`): (1) s[r]'s UPPER check is compile-time ELIDED via the `r<n` guard (len_alias ties n->s), leaving only a neg-check that the EXISTING `r.mono.fact` (r>=0) monotone assume folds. s[l]'s FULL `icmp uge l, %v.len` SURVIVES — but its upper bound `l<n` holds ONLY through the algorithm's SEMANTIC invariant (l<=r, maintained by `formed==required`), which is NOT syntactically provable: the minimal repro GENUINELY OOBs when acc stays >K, so the check is SOUNDLY REQUIRED. Emitting `assume(l<n)`/`assume(l<=r)` would MISCOMPILE valid programs (inject UB). Unlike the midpoint idiom (mid=lo+(hi-lo)/2, LOCALLY provable from the dominating guard), the two-pointer upper bound is a loop invariant no syntactic assume can supply. AND rust_ovf bounds-checks s[l] identically (it cannot prove l<n either), so s[l] is NOT the kara-vs-Rust differentiator. (2) The count-table DATA-STRUCTURE hypothesis is ALSO disproven: kara's bench mirror uses Vec[i64] need/have while Rust uses [i64;4] fixed arrays (raw-IR probe: Vec index = 8 len-loads / 30 bounds-cmps / 12 oob vs Array 1 / 10 / 8), BUT rebuilding the kara mirror with Array[i64,4] count tables barely moved the instruction count — 12.457B -> 12.453B (0.03%), sink 487341820 preserved — because the Vec `len` is LOOP-INVARIANT so LLVM hoists it and the pre-opt difference vanishes post-O2. So NEITHER named cause is the real cost. (3) The ~1.12x gap (kara 12.46B vs rust_ovf 11.14B instructions retired; IPC kara 8.00 highest of the three; ~4-5 extra instrs per outer iteration) is REAL and reproducible but DIFFUSE — not localized to a single eliminable check. RE-SCOPED: stays an OPEN perf gap, but the two-pointer-BCE fix direction is WITHDRAWN. The one un-ruled-out SOUND candidate: whether the monotone lower-bound assumes (emit_monotone_assumes' `x>=init`) fold their neg-checks under a single default<O2> or need the gated extra-pass trigger the midpoint fix uses (`binsearch_assume_emitted`, currently binsearch-only) — needs LLVM `opt -passes='default<O2>'` on the post-inline IR to confirm (opt not available locally this session). If it is a phase-ordering miss, extending the extra-pass trigger to monotone-assume emission is a small SOUND fix (folds s[r]'s neg-check) but does NOT touch s[l] and likely closes only part of the gap. NEXT (if pursued): install LLVM opt, diff post-O2 IR of kara vs rust_ovf min_window inner loops to pin the residual instrs; the s[l] check and any genuinely-required overflow checks are NOT eliminable. Method: `cargo test --features llvm --test codegen <probe>` with ir_for() gives raw codegen IR. OPT-CONFIRMED 2026-07-10 (LLVM 18.1.8 opt, phase-ordering lead RULED OUT — investigation COMPLETE, no sound fix exists; severity -> low). Ran `opt -passes='default<O2>'` on the raw codegen IR of the minimal two-pointer repro with `scan` marked `noinline` (so its `ref Vec` param stays opaque — the un-annotated repro const-folds away because main passes a literal vector, a probe trap worth noting). Result, stable across one AND two O2 runs (so NOT phase-ordering): s[r] has ZERO surviving checks — its upper bound is compile-time elided via the `r<n` guard and its neg-check folds via the existing `r>=0` monotone assume under a SINGLE O2 (the post-inline `getelementptr i64, ptr %v.data, i64 %r.032` in while.body has no guard). Exactly ONE check survives: the `vidx.oob` on `s[l]` (`%l.126`) in the inner shrink loop — the soundly-required one (l<n unprovable). So the 'monotone-assume needs the midpoint extra-pass' hypothesis is FALSE: the monotone assumes already fold everything foldable under one O2. CONCLUSION: every lead is now exhausted — s[l] BCE (unsound, Rust checks it too), count-table Vec-vs-array (optimizer-erased, 0.03%), and s[r] phase-ordering (already folds) are all ruled out. There is NO localized, sound codegen fix for this kata; the residual ~1.12x (kara 12.46B vs rust_ovf 11.14B instrs) is DIFFUSE codegen quality (overflow-check placement / instruction selection on the arithmetic-heavy inner loop), not one eliminable check. Kept OPEN as an honest small perf gap but NOT PURSUING further — no action item remains. If ever revisited, the only remaining angle is whole-inner-loop instruction-sequence diffing (kara vs rustc) for the arithmetic, which is a general codegen-quality effort, not a #76-specific bug. GENERALIZED 2026-07-10 (the gap is instruction DENSITY, visible only when throughput-bound — confirmed on #77/#78). Instruction-count comparison across the compute-bound backtracking siblings shows the SAME density gap as #76, just latency-hidden. Kata #78 subsets (warm, /usr/bin/time -l): kara 3.110B instructions vs C 2.392B (1.30x) vs rust_ovf 2.908B (1.07x) vs rust 2.573B — yet CYCLES are all within ~1% (kara 2.508B, C 2.514B, rust_ovf 2.539B, rust 2.532B), so wall-time is a dead heat (kara 2nd of five, AT the C floor, ahead of both Rust builds). #77 combinations is the same shape (5-way dead heat). The discriminator is IPC / whether the loop is throughput- or latency-bound: on #76 (a tight scan, kara IPC 8.00, throughput-bound) the extra instructions have nowhere to hide and show as the 1.24x wall-time loss; on #77/#78 (recursion, kara IPC ~1.2, latency/stall-bound on call-return + path push/pop memory traffic — even C is IPC 0.95) the extra instructions execute in the stall shadow for free, so equal cycles -> parity. So the accurate characterization is NOT '#76-specific gap' but 'kara's codegen emits ~1.1-1.3x the instruction count of C/rustc generally (bounds checks, overflow checks, less-tight instruction selection; e.g. #78's kara `while p<plen { path[p] }` indexed fold vs Rust's iterator), and it costs wall-time ONLY on throughput-bound loops'. The lever, if ever pursued, is general codegen density (broad overflow-check elision on provably-safe ops + tighter selection), which would lift the throughput-bound cases (#76) and is invisible on latency-bound ones (#77/#78) — a general codegen-maturity effort, not a per-kata fix. No new action; this sharpens the scope. Sinks all correct, so it is density not miscompilation (kara does MORE work, not less). TWO FLAVORS 2026-07-10 (#81 adds an IPC/scheduling flavor, distinct from density). Kata #81 (search-rotated-sorted-array-II, a cache-hot duplicate-aware binary search) puts kāra LAST of five on the M5 (c 137.8 < go 148.0 < rust 167.3 < rust_ovf 203.4 < kara 219.9 ms; 1.60x C, 1.08x equal-safety rust_ovf). But unlike #76/#79 this gap is NOT instruction density: kāra retires 3.554G instructions to rust_ovf's 3.540G (identical, 1.004x), yet burns 1.10x the cycles at a LOWER IPC (kara 3.55 vs rust_ovf 3.89). So the same emitted work schedules ~10% worse on the M5 pipeline — an instruction-SELECTION/SCHEDULING quality gap, not a count gap. (Ruled out the tempting 'nums[mid] bounds check survives because the guard is `lo <= hi` not the strict `lo < hi` the midpoint BCE needs' hypothesis: if kāra kept an extra check rust_ovf elides, kāra's instruction count would exceed rust_ovf's — it does not. Confirming via the instruction count BEFORE claiming, per this entry's own #76 reversal.) Also note overflow checks genuinely cost on #81 (rust_ovf 203.4 vs rust 167.3 = 1.22x; midpoint arithmetic + rolling hash on the hot path), unlike the free-overflow katas. So B-2026-07-10-5 now covers TWO diffuse codegen-quality sub-flavors, both throughput-visible / latency-hidden: (a) instruction DENSITY — kāra emits ~1.1-1.75x C/rustc's instruction count (#76/#79/#80); (b) instruction SCHEDULING — kāra emits the SAME count but ~10% lower IPC (#81). Neither has a single lever; the general remedy is codegen maturity (tighter selection + scheduling + broad overflow-check elision on provably-safe ops). No per-kata fix. #87 CORROBORATION 2026-07-11 (density flavor (a) on RECURSION, throughput-exposed; plus a kata-code caveat that refines the Vec-vs-array finding). Kata #87 scramble-string, the O(n^4) top-down MEMOISED recursion, on the production-representative x86-64 Linux container: at EQUAL SAFETY kara 826.6 ms vs rust_ovf 643.8 vs c 679.5 (kara 1.28x rust_ovf / 1.22x C). Callgrind (K=400, identical sink 183450647628): C 22.2M instrs, rust_ovf 26.7M (1.20x), kara 28.7M (1.29x C, 1.08x rust_ovf) — squarely in this entry's documented 1.1-1.3x density band. Static disasm of the hot `scramble`: kara 14 `jo` (overflow) + 10 `jae` (bounds) = 24 safety branches vs rust_ovf 10 + 5 = 15 (LLVM elides ~half rustc's bounds + ~4 overflow checks that kara's survive); computing memo[key] from (i1*n+i2)*(n+1)+len emits 4 `jo` + 1 `jae` for one indexed read. REFINES this entry two ways: (1) the '#77/#78 recursion is latency-bound so density is hidden -> parity' claim is NOT universal — #87's memoised recursion is throughput-exposed enough (tight per-call char-count loop + memo-index arithmetic between calls) that the SAME density gap costs ~1.14x wall, i.e. recursion CAN lose, IPC-dependent. (2) The '#76 Vec-vs-array count table was optimizer-erased (0.03%)' conclusion does NOT generalize: #87's char-count table written as `Vec.new()`+push is allocated PER-CALL INSIDE the recursion (not loop-invariant like #76's), so it is NOT erased — rewriting it as `Array[i64,26]` (matching C/Rust stack arrays) dropped instrs 32.9M->28.7M and seq wall ~9% (810->736 ms), sink preserved. LESSON for the density measurements: a fixed-size scratch buffer must be a stack `Array`, not a `Vec`, or the per-call heap alloc inflates the gap and is mistaken for codegen density. Kata #87 fixed to Array[i64,26] accordingly. Still no new action item — confirms the general codegen-density scope; no per-kata fix. INDEPENDENT REPRO 2026-07-12 (x86-64 Linux container, clang/opt/callgrind 18.1.3 -- fresh toolchain, CONFIRMS no-bounded-fix and SHARPENS the overflow-check evidence). Re-audited from scratch on the new environment. (A) Overflow-check parity with EQUAL-SAFETY C: built a representative two-pointer + flat-index kara repro (`while r<n { acc+=s[r]; while acc>k {acc-=s[l]; l+=1} best+=(r*n+l)*(n+1)+r; r+=1 }`) and the byte-equivalent C at `clang -O2 -ftrapv`; identical sink. Static disasm: kara's inlined loop emits EXACTLY the same 6 `jo` overflow branches as clang -ftrapv (the only extra is the one soundly-required `s[l]` bounds check; C is unchecked). On a constant-bounded loop (`while i<1000 { sum += i*2; sum += i+1; i+=1 }`) kara and clang BOTH SCEV-elide every induction-variable overflow check (`i*2`, `i+1`, `i=i+1` -> plain lea/add, no jo) and BOTH keep jo only on the unbounded `sum` accumulator. So kara's overflow-check ELISION already matches clang -ftrapv -- the 'broad overflow-check elision on provably-safe ops' lever this entry kept naming is ALREADY DONE by LLVM for kara; not a deficiency. CAVEAT refining the #87 evidence: raw cross-compiler `jo`-COUNTS are confounded by UNROLL FACTOR (kara unrolled the constant-bound loop x4, clang x2 -> 7 vs 6 jo for identical per-iteration checks), so the #87 'kara 14 jo vs rust_ovf 10' static count OVERSTATES any elision gap; the dynamic callgrind Ir is the trustworthy signal. (B) Dynamic Ir reproduces the band: amplified loop-dominated repro (2.5M inner iters, 99.25% Ir in the inlined scan): kara 46.6M vs C -ftrapv 41.5M (1.12x, EXACTLY this entry's equal-safety band) vs C -O2-unsafe 29.0M (1.61x). The 5.1M / 1.12x over equal-safety C is ~2 instr/iter = the required `s[l]` bounds check + diffuse selection, NOT any eliminable overflow check. NET: every sound lever independently re-confirmed done-or-unsound on fresh tooling; investigation stays CLOSED-no-fix, severity low. The only remaining axis is general instruction-selection maturity (unroll/scheduling), not a localized kara bug. DENSITY EFFORT 2026-07-12 (x86 container, callgrind Ir; owner-directed general density pass — CORRECTS this entry's framing). Added a KARAC_NO_AUTOPAR escape hatch (cli.rs, 1f39fea) so sequential lowering can be measured in isolation, then measured the representative two-pointer scan and the count-table (have[cr]/need[cr]) pattern SEQUENTIALLY vs equal-safety rustc. FINDINGS: (1) auto-par overhead is NEGLIGIBLE here — seq 46.56M vs auto-par 46.61M Ir (~0.1%); the reduce-worker the scan lowers into is a single sequential partition, so the earlier 1.12x callgrind number was NOT auto-par overhead. (2) At EQUAL SAFETY on x86, kara BEATS rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on on instruction count: two-pointer scan kara 46.56M vs rust_ovf 51.65M (kara 0.90x, 10% FEWER); count-table kara 36.12M vs rust_ovf 37.02M (kara 0.976x). vs UNSAFE C -O2 -ftrapv (no bounds checks) kara is 1.089x, the entire delta being the memory-safety bounds check on the unprovable-relational s[l] (l<=r not syntactically provable — same check rust_ovf keeps). (3) ASM CONFIRMATION of kara's sequential scan loop: s[r] loaded ONCE and reused for both acc+= and best+= (CSE'd like C's r10), indexed addressing (rax,rdi,8), loop-counter inc with NO overflow check (elided like clang), s[r] upper-bound check elided via the r<n guard; the only structural extra vs C is the required s[l] bounds check. Also confirmed opt -O2 fully CSEs a doubly-emitted s[r] read (one bounds check/load/panic-site survive). CONCLUSION: kara x86 sequential codegen DENSITY is not behind — it is 2-10% AHEAD of equal-safety rustc on these loops; every named sound lever (overflow-check elision, counter-overflow elision, index-load CSE, single-load reuse, indexed addressing, provable-bounds elision) is already done. The original 'kara 1.12x behind rust_ovf' gap is therefore aarch64/M5-specific instruction SELECTION/SCHEDULING (consistent with this entry's own #81 'same instruction count, ~10% lower IPC' note), NOT x86 instruction density, and the residual vs C is memory safety (unsound to remove). No x86 density fix exists to make; the honest remaining axis is aarch64 scheduling/selection maturity, out of reach in this x86 container. FIXED 2026-07-15 (2fa6513) — ROOT CAUSE FOUND AND CORRECTED, and it was NONE of the axes this entry investigated: not BCE (the s[l] check is soundly required and rust_ovf keeps it too), not phase ordering, not Vec-vs-array, not scheduling — karac simply NEVER COMMUNICATED THE VEC-LENGTH BOUND to LLVM. rustc folds len-derived overflow checks because slice lengths are capped by the Layout <= isize::MAX allocator contract; karac's len loads carried no such fact, so at EQUAL safety the emitted loop kept (a) a per-iteration overflow-FLAG re-check (cbnz) on the hoisted n+1 and (b) a full checked add PLUS a duplicate increment for l+1 under the dominating l <u len bounds check — exactly +1 instr/outer-iter and +2 instrs/inner-iter, the documented 1.12x instructions-retired deficit vs rust_ovf. FIX (three legs): (1) runtime/src/alloc.rs gains KARAC_MAX_ALLOC_BYTES = 2^61-1 — a ceiling in karac_alloc_fallible/karac_realloc_or_panic/karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic that is unreachable on real hardware (VA <= 2^57) and exists purely to make len <= cap <= cap*elem_size < 2^61 a PROVABLE invariant of every live Vec/String; (2) codegen annotates Vec len loads with !range [0, 2^61) at .len(), both emit_split_bounds_check len loads, and the for-in bound (annotate_len_load_range; zero-sized elements — legal via struct E {} — are structurally detected and skipped; slice sites skipped: Array-backed slices are not allocator-bounded); (3) checked_alloc_bytes (umul.with.overflow -> panic 'capacity overflow') at every user-controlled count*elem_size site (with_capacity/try_with_capacity/Vec.filled/ String.repeat) — which ALSO closes a latent MEMORY-SAFETY hole this investigation exposed: Vec.with_capacity((1<<61)+1) of an 8-byte element previously WRAPPED the byte multiply to an 8-byte allocation while recording cap = 2^61+1, a heap overflow on the first pushes; it now panics up front with a source location. EVIDENCE (cross-ISA static diff, in-container LLVM 18 opt/llc — the 'only remaining angle' this entry named, done statically on x86 by retargeting karac's IR to arm64-apple-macosx -mcpu=apple-m1): #76 minimal two-pointer repro outer/inner loop 23/10 instructions BEFORE -> 22/8 AFTER = EXACT structural parity with rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on (22/8, same csel bounds-merge, same single unchecked increments); x86-64 whole-function 70 -> 61. Sink preserved (500497157161, AOT = JIT = interp), valgrind clean. Tests: ir_vec_len_load_carries_range_metadata / ir_zero_sized_elem_vec_len_has_no_range_metadata / e2e_with_capacity_count_overflow_panics + 2 runtime alloc units; codegen 2382 / par_codegen 159 / memory_sanitizer 716 green; fmt + clippy (native + both wasm cfgs) clean; all four runtime archives rebuilt. RESIDUALS: (i) M5 wall-time re-bench of kata #76 is owner-side (this container is x86; static instruction parity predicts the 1.12x equal-safety deficit closes; the ~1.24x-vs-C residual is the soundly-required s[l] check C does not pay); (ii) the #81 flavor-(b) same-count/lower-IPC scheduling gap is NOT addressed — remains documented-no-lever; (iii) slice len loads unannotated pending a type-level Array size cap; (iv) interp reports capacity overflow as a raw Rust panic (diagnostics polish).

Turbofish-inferred raw-pointer binding loses its pointee in codegen: `let p = ptr.null[u8](); unsafe { p.read() }` fails with 'no handler for method read on variable p', while the annotated form `let p: *const u8 = ptr.null()` works. Root cause: a turbofish constructor's `*const T` return does not thread T=u8 into the binding's type as a clean `Type::Pointer` reaching the read's receiver `obj_ty`, so the typechecker's `Type::Pointer` method arm never records `pointer_method_receiver_pointees` and codegen's `raw_pointer_pointee_types` has no entry. Pre-existing (reproduces on 5931619, before the E_RAW_POINTER_UNRESOLVED_POINTEE slice). Surfaced because the E_RAW diagnostic recommends `ptr.null[u8]()` as a fix-it — the annotation fix-it is the reliable one until this lands. Typechecks fine (no type error); codegen-only. FIX DIRECTION: register the pointee for an inferred (non-annotated) pointer let-binding from its inferred `Type::Pointer` type, or ensure the turbofish return-type substitution yields a `Type::Pointer` at the binding's use-site occurrences. RESOLVED 2026-07-11 (8c4a32f): ptr.null[u8]() parses as Call{Index{FieldAccess{ptr,null},u8}} (indexing/turbofish ambiguity like size_of[T]()); neither typecheck's explicit-generic-args route nor codegen's pointer-intrinsic route recognized it, so T never bound (*const T -> Type::Error, no pointee, value stored i64). FIX both phases: expr_call.rs routes the Index/2-seg-Path dotted-builtin turbofish through infer_explicit_generic_args_call; call_dispatch.rs mirrors ptr.null() lowering via compile_ptr_module_call. Test raw_pointer_turbofish_binding_is_null; codegen 2140, typechecker 1962. NOTE interpreter never implemented raw-pointer construction (pre-existing AOT-only limitation).
resolution: [renumbered from B-2026-07-11-1 on 2026-07-17: duplicate id from a concurrent-session collision]

title: An explicit `par { }` block's top-level `let` bindings did not ESCAPE the block: `par { let a = fa(); let b = fb(); } /* use a, b */` failed with `error[resolve]: undefined name 'a'` after the block. The join barrier is meant to make each branch's result live in the ENCLOSING scope (the shape examples/db_pipeline::execute_pair and design.md's structured-concurrency model rely on); the previous design only exposed branch bindings to an optional tail expression INSIDE the block (`par { …; (a, b) }`). ROOT CAUSE: every phase gave the par block a private scope for its branch bindings that was popped at block end — resolver `resolve_par_block` (re-)defined the join bindings into an intermediate block scope, typechecker routed `ExprKind::Par` through `infer_block` (pushes/pops a scope), interpreter `eval_par_block` pushed/popped a merge scope, and codegen `compile_par_block` only materialized return slots for names read by `block.final_expr` (so a binding consumed AFTER the block, invisible to the tail-expr read set, got no slot). FIX (B-2026-07-11-3): the join barrier now hoists each branch's top-level `let` into the ENCLOSING scope across all four phases — resolver defines join bindings into the enclosing scope (no private block scope), typechecker checks branch stmts in the enclosing local_scope, interpreter merges branch vars into the current scope, and codegen gives EVERY top-level branch binding a return slot bound into the surrounding scope. Mirrors the auto-parallelizer, whose grouped `let`s are ordinary enclosing-scope locals already (that's why auto-par never had this bug and why the hard parts — slot liveness / drop across the join barrier — were already solved). Sibling isolation (B-2026-07-02-5) is preserved: each branch RHS still resolves in its own child scope and a sibling read still gets the cross-branch diagnostic (message updated to note bindings are usable after the block, not only in a tail expr). Both the escape form and the tail form work. Verified: resolver/typechecker/interpreter/ownership/effectchecker/concurrency suites, par_codegen (E2E escape test), codegen 2131, and an ASAN test asserting escaped heap (String) owners drop exactly once (no leak / double-free). Found by the docs/book audit; unblocks examples/db_pipeline.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: docs/book audit 2026-06-13; examples/db_pipeline/src/executor.kara::execute_pair; phase-6-runtime.md open item

title: `spawn(|| work())` — a closure-literal thunk with NO LHS annotation — failed type inference with `cannot infer type parameter 'T'; add a type annotation`. design.md:707 specifies `spawn[T](f: OnceFn() -> T) -> TaskHandle[T]`, so `T` must be inferable from the closure's return type. ROOT CAUSE: in `check_call_args_with_substitution_full` (the generic free-function call inference), pass 1 SKIPS closure args (`arg_tys.push(None)`) because closures are checked in pass 2 with param-type pushdown — but pass 2 checked each closure against the resolved slot and never unified the closure's INFERRED type back into the instantiated slot. So a type param appearing ONLY inside a closure param's type (`spawn[T](OnceFn() -> T)`, where `T` is fixed solely by the thunk's return, with no other argument to bind it) stayed an unsolved metavar — the return `TaskHandle[T]` came back with `T` unbound and the `let` binding tripped the unsolved-type-param diagnostic. The named-fn form `spawn(make_int)` worked (a fn ref is a NON-closure arg, inferred + unified in pass 1) and any LHS-annotated form worked (the annotation pins `T` from the LHS), which is why every existing spawn test passed and the closure-thunk gap slipped. FIX (B-2026-07-11-4): pass 2's closure branch now unifies the closure's inferred type into the un-resolved slot via `unify_types` (whose `Fn`→`OnceFn` cross arm descends into the return type), binding `T` from the closure body; a no-op when `T` was already solved from another argument. Both the free `spawn(|| …)` and method `tg.spawn(|| …)` forms now infer without an annotation; `spawn(work())` (a non-thunk VALUE) stays rejected because `spawn` wants a thunk. Verified: typechecker suite + two new closure-thunk inference tests (i64 and String returns), spawn_e2e, codegen 2131. Found by the docs/book audit; makes docs/book/src/ch14-concurrency.md compiler-accurate.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: docs/book audit 2026-06-13; docs/book/src/ch14-concurrency.md; phase-6-runtime.md open item

title: Matching a `ref`-scrutinee enum and using a non-i64-word payload (String / bool / narrow int / float) miscompiles: the via-ptr fast path binds the leaf directly at the i64 payload WORD, so `*binding` / a method call deref-loads the wrong shape. A String payload → codegen PANIC `into_struct_value` "Found IntValue … expected the StructValue variant" (`%s.deref = load i64`) at the `.push_str(s)` dispatch; a bool payload → module-verify `Branch condition is not 'i1' type! br i64 %b.deref`.
detail: Minimal repro (panics on `karac build` AND `karac run` JIT): `enum E { S(String), N(i64) } fn render(e: ref E, out: mut ref String) { match e { S(s) => { out.push_str(s); } N(n) => { out.push_str("num"); } } }`. Root cause: `bind_pattern_values_via_ptr`'s `TupleVariant` arm (`src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs`) took a `ok_padded_primitive` fast path (meant for `Option[i64]`'s over-wide 3-word slot) whenever the FIRST payload LLVM word was primitive — but every heap aggregate flattens to `{ptr:i64,…}` so its first word IS i64, and narrow scalars sit in an i64 slot too. It then `emit_ref_leaf_binding_at_ptr(s, field_ptr, field_ty=i64)` bound the leaf as a `ref i64`. The value-source (copy-shim) path already reconstructs payloads at their true type (narrowing bool→i1, rebuilding String/Vec), so the fix is to DEFER to it.
source: dogfood:examples/json.kara (recursive JSON enum, `match v { Num(n) => out.push_str(n), Bool(b) => if *b … }`)
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-11. In the via-ptr `TupleVariant` arm, added `declared_mismatches_word`: look up the leaf binding's recorded surface type (`pattern_binding_types`) → `llvm_type_for_name`, and if it isn't the i64 payload word, `return Ok(None)` to defer to the value-source path. Covers String/Vec (struct ≠ i64), bool (i1), narrow ints (i8/i16/i32), char (i32), f64 — while `Option[i64]` (name "i64" == i64 word) keeps the fast path. Type-info-absent bindings keep the legacy heuristic (no regression). Generalizes the B-2026-07-09-6 struct-payload guard to every non-word payload. Regression tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_ref_enum_string_and_bool_payload; 2130 codegen + 587 asan green.

title: A `Vec[struct]` bound as an enum payload loses its element TypeExpr, so `for e in entries { e.field }` binds the element without a struct type and the field read returns garbage (`i64 0`) → codegen PANIC at the `push_str(e.field)` dispatch. A `Vec[i64]` enum payload iterates fine (primitive elements need no field GEP), and a plain `let es: Vec[Ent]` local field-access works — only the intersection (Vec-of-struct AS an enum payload) breaks.
detail: Minimal repro: `enum J { N, Obj(Vec[Ent]) } struct Ent { key: String } fn ser(v: ref J, out: mut ref String) { match v { N => {out.push_str("n");} Obj(entries) => { for e in entries { out.push_str(e.key); } } } }`. Root cause: the value-source enum-payload binding (`src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs`, the `"Vec"|"VecDeque"` arm) registered `vec_elem_types[name]` (the LLVM element type — enough for a for-loop over a `Vec[i64]` payload) but NOT `var_elem_type_exprs[name]` (the element TypeExpr that struct-field GEP resolves through). A plain local gets both via `register_var_from_type_expr`; the payload path got only the former, so a struct element had no TypeExpr and `e.field` fell through to an i64 read.
source: dogfood:examples/json.kara (`Obj(entries: Vec[Entry]) => for e in entries { escape_into(e.key, out); ser_into(e.val, out) }`)
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-11. The `"Vec"|"VecDeque"` payload-binding arm now also `self.var_elem_type_exprs.insert(name, inner_te.clone())` alongside `vec_elem_types`, so the for-loop element carries the struct TypeExpr and `e.field` GEPs correctly — identical to a plain `let es: Vec[Ent]`. Regression tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_ref_enum_vec_struct_payload_field_access; 2130 codegen + 587 asan green.

title: `?` on a `Result[<concrete user enum>, E]` truncates the multi-word Ok payload to its first word: `let v = pv()?` where `pv() -> Result[Json, String]` and `Json` is a wide (>1-word) enum yields `v` typed as a bare `i64` (the tag/first word), not the full enum aggregate. A later use (`Entry { val: v }`, method call) then mis-types it → module-verify `Invalid InsertValueInst operands! insertvalue { …, {i64,i64,i64,i64} } …, i64 %v, 1` (an i64 inserted where the 4-word enum struct is expected). Explicit `match pv() { Ok(x) => x, Err(e) => return … }` works — ONLY the `?` sugar truncates.
detail: Minimal repro (`karac build` / JIT fail; interpreter fine): `enum Json { Null, Str(String), Obj(Vec[Entry]) } struct Entry { key: String, val: Json } fn pv() -> Result[Json, String] { Result.Ok(Json.Null) } fn po() -> Result[Json, String] { let mut es: Vec[Entry] = Vec.new(); let v = pv()?; es.push(Entry { key: "k", val: v }); Result.Ok(Json.Obj(es)) }`. ROOT CAUSE (precise): `reconstruct_question_ok_payload` (`src/codegen/exprs.rs`) rebuilds a multi-word Ok payload only when its type is found in `string_typed_exprs` (String) or `enum_inst_type_exprs` (generic `Named`); otherwise it `return Ok(w0)` — truncating to one word. `enum_inst_type_exprs` is populated in `src/lowering.rs:~498` with a `Type::Named { args } if !args.is_empty()` filter, which EXCLUDES a concrete (arg-less) user enum like `Json`. So `?` on `Result[Json, …]` can't find `Json`'s type and truncates. String/Vec[T]/generic payloads work because they land in one of the two tables. FIX DIRECTION: give `reconstruct_question_ok_payload` the concrete-enum Ok payload type — either a dedicated `?`-Ok-payload TypeExpr table (populated from `tc.expr_types` at the `?` span, which already holds the unwrapped type per the fn's own comment), or broaden the `enum_inst_type_exprs` filter to include arg-less enums (verify the other consumer — the Some-payload heap-vs-word decision — is unaffected). The failure-path (`Err`) already reconstructs wide payloads via `rebuild_value_from_payload_words`; the Ok-path needs the same type resolution for concrete enums.
source: dogfood:examples/json.kara (`let v = self.parse_value()?; entries.push(Entry { key: k, val: v })` in parse_object)
tracker: examples/json.kara (blocks the JIT/build path; interpreter-verified via KARAC_RUN_JIT=0)
investigation: Minimally reduced from the full JSON parser through ~10 repros: eliminated recursion, ref-vs-value match, Vec-of-enum, Vec-of-struct field access (those were B-2026-07-11-6, fixed), Result-Ok binding via match (works), and struct-construction-with-enum-field-from-identifier (works). The ONLY trigger is the `?` operator on a Result whose Ok type is a concrete multi-word enum. Interpreter (tree-walk) is correct throughout.
resolution: FIXED 2026-07-11. Two-part fix. (1) TYPE: added a lowering-derived `concrete_named_type_exprs` table (src/lowering.rs) — the arg-less-Named complement of `enum_inst_type_exprs` — keyed by span, consumed ONLY by `reconstruct_question_ok_payload` (src/codegen/exprs.rs) so it can resolve a concrete user enum/struct Ok-payload's type that the generic-only table dropped. (2) WORD SPAN: an enum's LLVM layout is flat i64 words (`{i64 tag, i64…}`), so reconstruct now FLAT-COPIES all the enum's words verbatim from the Result's payload fields, sidestepping `rebuild_value_from_payload_words`'s 3-word cap (which truncated a 4-word heap-bearing enum, dropping `cap` -> invalid free). Memory-safe (ASAN green) for borrow, move-out, and wide tuple-variant payloads. examples/json.kara now `karac build`s to a native binary matching the oracle byte-for-byte. Regressions: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_question_on_result_concrete_enum + tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_question_on_result_heap_enum_no_leak_or_double_free.

title: [NOT A BUG — REVERTED, misdiagnosis] The interpreter prints an `Error return trace:` block to output for an error that was HANDLED (caught by a `match`/`if let`), not just for one that escapes to the top. A program that uses `?` internally and handles every error (e.g. `match parse(x) { Ok(v) => …, Err(e) => println(e) }`) still dumped a stale `?`-propagation trace at exit, polluting program output.
detail: Repro: any program with a `fn helper() -> Result[T,E]` that a caller invokes with `?`, whose error is ultimately caught by a `match`, and where no LATER successful `?` runs. `cmd_run` (`src/cli.rs:~5005`) printed the `?`-error-return trace whenever the ring buffer was non-empty at exit, unconditionally. The buffer (`error_trace`) gets a frame pushed per `?` that re-propagates an `Err` (`eval_expr.rs` Question arm) and is CLEARED only by a later successful `?` (Ok/Some) — never when a propagated `Err` is caught by a match. So a handled `?`-error left stale frames that printed as an `Error return trace`. Only the LAST `?`-propagated error's frames survive (each Ok-`?` clears), which is why it looked intermittent (only errors reaching the top via `?` with no trailing Ok-`?` showed a trace).
source: dogfood:examples/json.kara (its 3 error round-trips all handled via `match parse(x) { Err(e) => … }`, yet the last printed a trace)
resolution: REVERTED 2026-07-11 — MISDIAGNOSIS, not a bug. The `?`-error-return trace is written to STDERR (never stdout) and INTENTIONALLY prints for a HANDLED error too: a debug diagnostic recording the last error's propagation path, cleared only on a subsequent successful `?`. tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_question_trace_single_frame_on_err exercises a `?`-error caught by a `match` and asserts the trace IS present. The originally-committed interpreter gate (cli.rs) + a codegen main-exit-clear were both reverted; examples/json.kara's stdout oracle was always clean (2>/dev/null), so there was never a real problem. Lesson: the trace lives on stderr and is handling-agnostic by design.

title: RC-fallback false-positive on a loop accumulator moved out via an early `return`: an accumulator built in a loop and returned from a branch inside that loop (`loop { items.push(parse()?); if done() { return Result.Ok(Json.Arr(items)); } }`) fires a `perf[rc-fallback]` on `items`/`out`/`entries`. The ownership analysis treats the loop-branch `return` (consume) and the fall-through `push` (use) as a potential reuse-after-consume, though the `return` is terminal and the two are mutually exclusive.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: ergonomics — Repro: `fn f() -> Result[Json,String] { let mut items = Vec.new(); loop { items.push(parse()?); if done() { return Result.Ok(Json.Arr(items)); } } }` -> `perf[rc-fallback] ... 'items' (direct re-use after consume)`. The RC fallback is CORRECT-but-suboptimal (the program runs), so this is an analysis-precision / diagnostic-quality item, not a miscompile: a value moved on a terminal early-return path and mutated on the fall-through path of the same loop is single-owner and needs no RC. FIX DIRECTION: teach the RC-fallback pass that a move at a loop-body `return` is terminal, so it doesn't alias the fall-through mutation. NOTE: the original filing ALSO claimed 'no Iterator.collect()' -- that was WRONG (author error: called `.len()` directly on the `Iterator`, never tried `.collect()`). `Iterator[T].collect() -> Vec[T]` is fully implemented (typechecker src/typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs, interpreter + codegen); `s.chars().collect()` and `(0..n).collect()` both work. examples/json.kara now uses it directly, dropping the unnecessary `to_chars` helper.
tracker: backlog (ownership RC-fallback precision on loop early-return)

title: Pushing an empty `Vec.new()` into a `Vec[Vec[i64]]` does not infer the inner element type from the receiver/method signature — `out.push(Vec.new())` where `out: Vec[Vec[i64]]` fails typecheck with "expected 'Vec<i64>', found 'Vec<?T0>'", requiring an explicit `let empty: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); out.push(empty)`. The expected argument type of `push` (Vec[i64]) is statically known, so a more complete bidirectional inference would propagate it into the empty `Vec.new()` literal (Rust infers the analogous `v.push(Vec::new())`). LOW severity / ergonomics only: it is a CLEAN, actionable typecheck error (not a cryptic message, not a run/build divergence), and the one-line type annotation is idiomatic rather than a contortion — so the kata was NOT routed around, just annotated. Surfaced while adding the Gosper's-hack bitmask variant, whose k==0 case pushes the single empty combination; the DFS solvers never hit it because their empty combination comes from `path.clone()` of an already-typed empty `path`. REPRO: `fn main(){ let mut out: Vec[Vec[i64]] = Vec.new(); out.push(Vec.new()); println(f"{out.len()}"); }`. FIX DIRECTION: thread the expected type from the call argument position into the `Vec.new()` associated-call inference (expected-type propagation for empty container constructors in argument position), then the annotation becomes unnecessary. FIXED 2026-07-11 (this commit): the `push` special-case arm in `src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs` inferred the argument in synthesis mode, then unified the receiver element type (`Vec[i64]`) with the arg (`Vec[?T0]`) — correctly binding `?T0 = i64` — but only TOP-LEVEL-resolved (`resolve_type_var_top`) before the assignability check, so the arg's NESTED `?T0` stayed stale and `check_assignable(Vec[i64], Vec[?T0])` reported a spurious `expected 'Vec<i64>', found 'Vec<?T0>'`. FIX: deep-resolve BOTH the receiver element type and the arg type through the substitutions the unify just populated (`resolve_type_vars` with empty name-maps) before `check_assignable` — mirroring how the general generic-call path (`check_call_args_with_substitution_full` pass 2) resolves slots. The empty-constructor push now infers without the `let empty: Vec[i64] = Vec.new()` annotation, and codegen builds+runs correctly (`out.len()` == 1). This reuses the unify's binding rather than threading an expected type into `Vec.new()`'s associated-call inference (the original FIX DIRECTION) — same user-visible result, smaller blast radius; the scalar `v.push(5)` receiver-var pinning path is preserved. Test: tests/typechecker.rs::test_push_empty_vec_new_into_nested_vec_infers_element.

RUN-vs-BUILD divergence: `karac run` (JIT-default) cannot execute a `gpu.dispatch` program. `#[gpu] fn double(x: f32) -> f32 { x*2.0 }` + `gpu.dispatch(double, buf)` runs correctly two ways — `karac build` (auto-selects libkarac_runtime_gpu.a, links Metal, dispatches on the real GPU) and `karac run` with `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0` (tree-walk interpreter, element-wise CPU) — both byte-identical `[1,2,3,4]f32 -> 2 4 6 8`. But the DEFAULT `karac run` (JIT, since the Slice-6c JIT-default flip) fails: `JIT session error: Symbols not found: [ _karac_runtime_gpu_map ]` then `lookup_address("main"): Failed to materialize symbols`. The in-process JIT runner's runtime rlib is not built with the opt-in `gpu` feature, so the dispatch symbol is absent and `main` cannot be materialized.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: GPU slice-0 spine live-verification on Metal (Slipstream GPU-status audit; docs/dogfooding.md + phase-10-targets GPU-LBM breakdown) — Repro (macOS/Metal, 2026-07-10): gpu_slice0.kara = `#[gpu] fn double(x: f32) -> f32 { x * 2.0 }` + `fn main(){ let buf: Vec[f32]=[1.0,2.0,3.0,4.0]; let out=gpu.dispatch(double,buf); for v in out { println(f"{v}") } }`. `karac build gpu_slice0.kara && ./gpu_slice0` -> `2 4 6 8` (otool shows Metal.framework). `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0 karac run` -> `2 4 6 8`. Default `karac run` -> `Symbols not found: [ _karac_runtime_gpu_map ]`. ROOT CAUSE: the `gpu` feature (wgpu/Metal backend, libkarac_runtime_gpu.a) is opt-in and NOT enabled for the JIT runner's runtime rlib, so the LLJIT dlsym generator cannot resolve `_karac_runtime_gpu_map`. The Slice-6c JIT-default flip collides with the slice-0 GPU spine (docs/spikes/gpu-wgsl-slice0.md). WORKAROUND: `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0` (interp CPU fallback) or `karac build` the program. FIX OPTIONS: (a) feature-gate the gpu runtime symbols into the JIT runner rlib + add to `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` (cf. the env.set FFI keep-list gotcha in the JIT-default work); or (b) route `#[gpu]`/`gpu.dispatch` programs in `karac run` to the tree-walk interpreter regardless of KARAC_RUN_JIT. Does NOT affect the shipped AOT path or the interpreter; scoped to the experimental gpu.dispatch surface.
tracker: docs/implementation_checklist/phase-10-targets.md Track C (GPU); memory project_gpu_dispatch_jit_gap; relates to project_jit_default_flip_blocked (__preserve_no_mangle_symbols keep-list)
resolution: Fixed in 05d72ed via FIX OPTION (b): `cmd_run` (src/cli.rs) now detects a `#[gpu]` kernel (`program_declares_gpu_kernel` — any top-level `#[gpu] fn` or `#[gpu]` impl method) and routes such a program to the tree-walk interpreter (element-wise CPU) instead of the LLJIT, regardless of the Slice-6c JIT-default. The interpreter needs no GPU backend, so `karac run gpu_slice0.kara` now prints `2 4 6 8` like `karac build` and `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0`. Chose (b) over (a) [feature-gate the gpu symbols into the JIT runner rlib] because (a) drags the heavy wgpu/naga/objc2 dep tree (+~4.5 MB) into every JIT run for an experimental surface, whereas (b) is a contained driver-side gate. Kernel detection is a sound superset of detecting an actual `gpu.dispatch` call (a `#[gpu]` fn is only reachable through dispatch; the interpreter runs every program, so a declared-but-never-dispatched kernel routes there harmlessly). Non-GPU programs stay JIT-default; the `--interp` / `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0` escape hatches still work. Reproduced on x86-64 Linux (no GPU needed — the interpreter path is CPU) and verified all three run forms agree. Regression: `tests/cli.rs::test_run_gpu_dispatch_routes_to_interpreter`. The AOT `karac build` GPU path is untouched.

source: dogfood:gpu_gate1 (surfaced building the CG-4 struct-SoA gpu.dispatch demo / GPU-GATE-1)
title: Reading a field from a LITERAL-constructed SoA binding segfaults under codegen. `let world: Vec[Particle] = [Particle{..}, Particle{..}]` with `layout world: Vec[Particle] { group gp { pos } group gv { vel } }`, then `world[1].pos` (any SoA field/index read) → SIGSEGV (exit 139) in the compiled binary; the tree-walk interpreter (KARAC_RUN_JIT=0) computes it correctly (run-vs-build divergence + memory unsafety on a valid program). Narrowly isolated: SoA-literal + `world.len()` works; PUSH-built SoA + `world[1].pos` works; plain AoS (no layout) literal + read works — ONLY literal-construction × SoA field/index read crashes. Likely the SoA collection-literal builder allocates the SoA vec header (ptr-per-group + len/cap) but does not allocate/populate the per-group backing-array pointers the way the push/index-store path does, so the read derefs a garbage/uninitialized group pointer. RESOLVED 2026-07-11 (e86942e): SoA let-binding path (stmts.rs) handled only Vec.new()/with_capacity and layout-returning calls, so a collection-literal RHS fell through to AoS construction — the AoS {ptr,len,cap} header was stored into the SoA slot and every later world[i].field read decoded it as the multi-group SoA struct -> garbage group-ptr deref -> SIGSEGV. FIX: soa_literal_elems detects a [..]/Vec[..] RHS and compile_soa_let_from_literal (exprs.rs) builds an empty SoA header then replays each element through the compile_soa_method push decomposition. Test test_e2e_soa_literal_construction_field_read; codegen 2140.
detail: Repro (macOS, `karac build`): `struct Particle { pos: f32, vel: f32 }` · `layout world: Vec[Particle] { group gp { pos } group gv { vel } }` · `fn main(){ let world: Vec[Particle] = [Particle{pos:0.0,vel:1.0}, Particle{pos:5.0,vel:2.0}]; println(f"{world[1].pos}"); }` → exit 139. Diagnostic matrix (same struct+layout): (A) literal + `world.len()` → 2 (ok — header allocated); (B) `let mut world = Vec.new(); world.push(..); world.push(..); world[1].pos` → 5 (ok — push builds group buffers); (C) no layout block, AoS literal + `world[1].pos` → 5 (ok). Failing intersection = SoA-literal-construction × SoA-field-read. PRE-EXISTING SoA collection codegen (src/codegen/collections.rs / declarations.rs); NOT touched by the CG-4 GPU work that surfaced it (that diff adds only gpu.dispatch code — the repro uses no gpu.dispatch). Blocks the literal-init variant of the GPU-GATE-1 kata; the gate kata builds `world` via push instead. FIX DIRECTION: make the SoA collection-literal builder allocate + populate each group's backing array from the literal elements (scattering each field into its group), exactly as the SoA push/index-store path does — diff literal-construction lowering vs push in collections.rs. Verify under guardmalloc/ASan (garbage-pointer deref).
tracker: docs/implementation_checklist/phase-10-targets.md (CG-4 / GPU-GATE-1 gate kata builds `world` via push pending this); src/codegen/collections.rs SoA literal construction

title: A function whose value is a top-level `if`-expression with FLOAT type miscompiled: `fn relu(x: f32) -> f32 { if x > 0.0 { x } else { 0.0 } }` failed codegen with module-verify error "Function return type does not match operand type of return inst! ret i64 0 / float". `compile_if`'s value phi-merge (src/codegen/control_flow.rs) built a phi only when both branch LLVM types agreed, and `unify_int_branch_widths` reconciled only INTEGER width artifacts. A float literal (`0.0`) lowers at the default f64 width; beside an f32 sibling branch (a param / typed expr) the phi operands disagreed (f32 vs f64), the merge fell through to the `i64 0` placeholder, and the fn tail emitted `ret i64 0` against its `float` return type. The i64/int if-return worked (int harmonizer); float-specific. Any float `if`-return with a literal branch was affected — not gpu-specific.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: GPU-LBM-4 (surfaced allowing `if` in #[gpu] scalar kernels; the bug is general) — Repro (`karac build`, no gpu): `fn relu(x: f32) -> f32 { if x > 0.0 { x } else { 0.0 } } fn main(){ println(f"{relu(3.0)}"); println(f"{relu(-2.0)}"); }` → codegen error (i64 control: `fn pick(x:i64)->i64{ if x>0 {x} else {0} }` compiled + printed 3/0 fine). ROOT CAUSE: compile_if's `(false,false)` (neither arm diverges) branch built the phi under `tv.get_type()==ev.get_type()` after `unify_int_branch_widths`, which is int-only; a float f32-vs-f64 mismatch fell to the const-`i64 0` placeholder (same fall-through the int-width fix addressed for self-hosting #7). FIX (514ee12c): added `unify_float_branch_widths` + `fptrunc_branch_value_in_pred` (sibling of the int helpers) — fptrunc the f64 literal branch down to the sibling f32 in its predecessor before the phi. Sound: the typechecker unified both branches to one Kāra float type, so the f64 side is always the literal-default artifact (an f64-returning fn never hits the mismatch, its literals are already f64), and the truncation is value-preserving. Regression tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_float_if_return_phi_width; full codegen suite 2138 passed 0 failed (no if/match/float regression). Surfaced by GPU-LBM-4 enabling scalar `#[gpu]` control flow (`if x>0 {x} else {0}` → WGSL `select`), whose #[gpu] fn is also codegen'd as a regular LLVM function and hit this.
tracker: src/codegen/control_flow.rs compile_if / unify_float_branch_widths; float sibling of the int-width fix (self-hosting #7)

title: `.push()` (and other Vec/String methods) unsupported on a non-identifier receiver — a nested place expr like `self.scopes[i].names.push(x)` / `o.inners[0].xs.push(v)` fails codegen
detail: A method call whose receiver is a NESTED place expression (index-then-field, `o.inners[0].xs`) falls through method dispatch: `codegen: no handler for method 'push' on non-identifier receiver (add a dispatcher arm in compile_method_call)`. `self.field.push(x)` (one-level field receiver, e.g. `Parser.loop_labels.push`) works; `self.field[i].field.push(x)` does not. LOUD error (not silent), so it fails the build rather than miscompiling. MINIMAL REPRO: `struct Inner { xs: Vec[i64] } struct Outer { inners: Vec[Inner] } fn main() { let mut o = Outer { inners: Vec.new() }; o.inners.push(Inner { xs: Vec.new() }); o.inners[0].xs.push(5); println(o.inners[0].xs.len()); }` => build error. Surfaced by the phase-12 Resolver port Slice 1 (`selfhost/src/resolver.kara`), which wanted a nested `Vec[Scope]{ names: Vec[NameBinding] }` and had to be restructured to a FLAT `binds: Vec[NameBinding]` tagged with scope id so every `.push` lands on a single `self.field` vec. FIX AREA: `compile_method_call` — the receiver-place lowering handles Identifier and (some) field receivers but not a general place expression; extend it to compute the receiver pointer via the place-chain walker (the same one field/index assignment uses) before dispatching Vec/String builtin methods.
resolution: Fixed in fc50a89. ROOT CAUSE: `lower_field_access_ptr`'s `Index` arm (src/codegen/calls.rs) required the indexed container to be a bare `Identifier`; a FieldAccess container (`o.inners` in `o.inners[i].xs.push`) returned `Ok(None)`, so the receiver-pointer resolution fell through to the non-identifier-receiver dispatch error. FIX: extend the `Index` arm to hoist a FieldAccess container to a synthetic Vec identifier before indexing — resolve the field to its storage pointer + declared element registries (recursively, so the base may itself be a place chain), register a synth binding via `register_var_from_type_expr`, index that, then tear the synth down via the new shared `unregister_synth_container` helper. Sibling of the B-2026-07-09-1 FieldAccess-container hoist in `compile_indexed_receiver_method`. The field pointer aliases the parent storage so the mutation writes back through the whole chain (verified by read-back). Works for plain- and shared-struct elements and a `mut ref self` root, and generalizes to deeper index+field chains (`o.a[i].b[j].c.push`); pure double-index `a[i][j].method` stays rejected (MR5/FR5, a distinct path). Full codegen (2144) + selfhost oracles green; regression tests `codegen::test_e2e_nested_receiver_push_index_then_field`, `codegen::test_e2e_nested_receiver_push_shared_struct_element`, and ASAN/LSan `memory_sanitizer::asan_nested_receiver_push_string_field_no_leak`. The resolver's flat-`binds` workaround can now be replaced with the natural nested `Vec[Scope]{ names: Vec[NameBinding] }` if desired.

title: SILENT: `match ref_struct.field { Some(g) => ... }` on an Option/enum FIELD of a `ref`-bound struct binds the payload as an EMPTY/zeroed shallow copy — its Vec/heap fields read empty
detail: Matching an Option (or enum) payload OUT OF A FIELD of a `ref`-bound struct (`fn f(h: ref Holder) { match h.w { Some(g) => for x in g.items {..} } }`) binds `g` as a zeroed/shallow copy: `g.items` (a `Vec` field) reads LENGTH 0 even when the source has elements. SILENT wrong result (no error) — the dangerous kind. DISCRIMINATOR: matching a `ref Option[T]` PARAM directly (`fn f(w: ref Option[Wrap]) { match w { Some(g) => .. } }`) is CORRECT; the bug is specific to a FIELD-of-a-ref scrutinee (`h.w`). REPRO A (BUG, prints 0, want 3): `struct Wrap { items: Vec[i64] } struct Holder { w: Option[Wrap] } fn count(h: ref Holder) -> i64 { let mut c = 0; match h.w { Some(g) => { for x in g.items { c = c + 1; } } None => {} } c } fn main() { let mut items: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); items.push(1); items.push(2); items.push(3); let h = Holder { w: Some(Wrap{items:items}) }; println(count(h)); }`. REPRO B (CLEAN, prints 3): same but `count(w: ref Option[Wrap])` matching the param directly. Surfaced by the phase-12 Resolver Slice 1: `match f.generics { Some(g) => for p in g.params {..} }` over `f: ref FnDefNode` silently skipped every generic param (T undefined, no dup). WORKAROUND used in resolver.kara: walk the whole AST BY VALUE and destructure (the ast_render idiom) so the scrutinee is an owned value, never a field-of-ref. FIX AREA: the match-scrutinee lowering for a field-access place through a `ref` — the payload extraction (`bind_pattern_values` / `reconstruct_payload_value`) reads the field's payload words from a wrong/zeroed base when the scrutinee root is a borrow + field-access; likely the same place-chain-through-ref base-pointer gap as B-2026-07-11-11. Because it is SILENT it can miscompile arbitrary user code, not just selfhost — high severity despite low current blast radius.
resolution: Two independent gaps, both fixed in af0cc9d. ROOT CAUSE (not the base-pointer gap hypothesised in FIX AREA): (1) `typechecker/fields.rs infer_field_access` returned `Type::Error` for a field read through a `ref`/`mut ref` STRUCT — only unions were peeled; the struct-through-ref path was deliberately dark (`self`-receivers dodged it by being typed as the bare value, so only `ref`/`mut ref` PARAMS hit it). With the scrutinee typed Error, the `Some(g)` payload binding never recorded its surface type, so codegen's `pattern_payload_word_count` fell to the 1-word default and truncated the 3-word `Wrap` (Vec) to its data pointer — `g.items` read len 0. FIX: peel the borrow for any `Named`/`Shared` inner and resolve the field's declared by-value type (generic-arg substitution was already borrow-aware via `field_type_with_receiver_args`). (2) Once sized correctly, the value-path payload binding aliases the borrowed buffer but got an owned scope-exit drop -> double-free against the caller's storage. FIX: `control_flow_match.rs scrutinee_is_readonly_borrowed_place` classifies a field/element read rooted at a `ref`/for-borrow/borrow-accessor binding as a borrow (drop suppressed) ONLY when no arm moves a payload out — escaping payloads (`match self.toks[i].tok { Id(s) => s }`) stay on the owned move-out path, which the borrow clone-on-escape net does not cover for user enums. Full codegen (2138) + memory_sanitizer (593, ASAN/LSan) + all 4 selfhost oracles green; regression test `codegen::match_scrutinee_field_of_ref_struct_payload_binding` covers both the non-escaping (prints 3) and escaping (prints hello) shapes. B-2026-07-11-11 (nested-receiver `.push()` on `a.b[i].c`) is a DISTINCT method-dispatch gap, still open — this fix surfaces it (`o.inners[0].xs.push(5)` now type-resolves but hits 'no handler for method push on non-identifier receiver').

title: `String.chars()` random-access/length ergonomics (the gap-1 half of B-2026-07-11-9, which was marked fixed for gap 2 — the rc-fallback — only). Materialization and random access already worked by this point: `s.chars().collect()` yields a `Vec[char]` (codegen materializes chars eagerly to a `Vec[char]`, B-2026-06-18-1/-5) with native `.len()` and `[i]` indexing. The remaining holes were the iterator-LENGTH conveniences: `s.chars().count()` TYPECHECKED but FAILED codegen with 'no handler for method count on non-identifier receiver' (the `count` terminal had a typechecker arm but no codegen arm for a method-chain receiver), and `s.chars().len()` was REJECTED at typecheck with 'no method len on type Iterator' (only `count` was a recognized terminal) — even though codegen's len/is_empty method-chain intercept would have serviced it. FIX (B-2026-07-11-13): (1) codegen — add `count` to the len/is_empty method-chain intercept in `src/codegen/method_call.rs` (both the Vec-struct and slice-header receiver arms), so `<chars-materialized Vec>.count()` extracts the Vec's `len` field (count == len for the eager snapshot); (2) typechecker — accept `len` as an alias for the `count` terminal on `Iterator` (`src/typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs`), both returning i64; (3) interpreter — alias `len` to `count` in the iterator terminal dispatch (`src/interpreter/method_call_iter.rs`, drain-and-count). All three phases now agree: `s.chars().count()` and `s.chars().len()` return the char count under `karac check`/`build`/`run`, empty string → 0, and `v.iter().count()` on a plain Vec is unaffected. Verified E2E (codegen build+run and interpreter both print 5/5/0). No hand-rolled `Vec[char]` materialization loop is needed for random-access char parsing anymore. Requested by the owner; found via the B-2026-07-11-9 JSON dogfood.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: B-2026-07-11-9 gap 1 (JSON dogfood: random-access char parsing); closes the chars-ergonomics remainder that stayed unfixed when B-2026-07-11-9 was marked fixed for gap 2 only

title: LEAK: a user method on a FRESH-TEMP shared-struct receiver (`make().count()`) leaks the RC box + heap fields under LSan — a regression on `main`, the pinned test `memory_sanitizer::asan_freshtemp_shared_struct_method_no_double_free` fails (288 bytes / 3 allocations)
detail: `tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_freshtemp_shared_struct_method_no_double_free` FAILS under Linux LSan: `make().count()` in a loop (where `make() -> Bag`, `shared struct Bag { items: Vec[String] }`, `count(self) -> i64`) leaks 288 bytes / 3 allocations (the RC box + the two Vec[String] field buffers).
