The fresh-temp materializes into `__urecv_tmp` and is meant to be drop-tracked as ONE scope-exit RcDec (`track_rc_var`) that drives rc->0 and frees the box + fields — but that dec is NOT firing (or the temp isn't tracked), so the box leaks each iteration. REGRESSION, not new: BISECTED to the origin/main range 21a9e8c..2877a73 (fails at 2877a73, i.e. BEFORE any of this session's B-2026-07-11-11/-12 work — confirmed by checking out 2877a73 clean and reproducing). Prime suspect: `687df6c fix(ownership): don't RC-fallback an accumulator moved out via a terminal return (B-2026-07-11-9 gap 2)` — an RC-fallback change that plausibly dropped the fresh-temp shared-receiver's RcDec tracking; needs confirmation by reverting/bisecting that commit against this test. IMPACT: the Linux-CI `memory-sanitizer` gate is RED on current `main` (cd2f6b7+). REPRO: run `cargo test --features llvm --test memory_sanitizer asan_freshtemp_shared_struct_method_no_double_free`. Not caused by and orthogonal to the nested-receiver / field-access-through-ref fixes filed same day; filed on discovery per the never-route-around-it rule while fixing B-2026-07-11-11.
resolution: Fixed in fbf97df. ROOT CAUSE (bisected to 2877a73 'String.chars().count()/.len() ergonomics', NOT the suspected ownership commit 687df6c — that one passes): 2877a73 added `count` to the non-identifier-receiver `len`/`is_empty` method-chain intercept in `src/codegen/method_call.rs`. That intercept speculatively `compile_expr(object)`s the receiver, then services it only when the value is a Vec/String/slice struct. For a `make().count()` receiver whose result is a user struct/enum (here the `shared struct Bag` with a user `fn count(self)`), the value is a handle/pointer — neither a Vec struct nor a slice header — so the intercept fell through WITHOUT freeing the just-materialized temp, and the real user-method dispatch re-evaluated `object`, leaking the discarded RC box + its heap fields (288 B / 3 allocs over the 3-iteration loop). Latent for `len`/`is_empty` since the intercept was first added; `count` newly collided with a common user method name. FIX: gate the intercept on the receiver's type NOT declaring its own `len`/`is_empty`/`count` method — when `dispatch_key` names an emitted `<Type>.<method>` fn (or a generic one), defer to user-method dispatch instead of speculatively compiling the receiver. Iterator/collection chains (`s.chars().count()`, `make_vec().len()`) declare no such fn, so they still take the intercept (the B-2026-07-11-9 feature is unchanged: chars count/len still 5/5/0). Bonus: a user `make().len()` now returns the user method's value instead of a mis-read collection length. Full codegen (2145) + memory_sanitizer (594, ASAN/LSan) green; regression `codegen::e2e_user_len_family_method_on_freshtemp_dispatches_to_user` locks the dispatch value and the pinned ASAN gate `asan_freshtemp_shared_struct_method_no_double_free` now passes.

title: LEAK: `with_capacity(n)` with a runtime `n == 0` orphaned a 1-byte heap buffer. `karac_alloc_or_panic(0)` deliberately normalizes `0 -> 1` and returns a real, non-null buffer (a non-null result is its success signal, alloc.rs), but the zero-capacity constructor stamped `cap = 0` into the collection aggregate `{data, len=0, cap=0}`. The load-bearing `cap > 0 <=> owned heap` drop convention (clone_drop.rs: String SSO / static-literal rodata views / borrowed slices all use `cap == 0` to mean DO-NOT-FREE) then SKIPPED freeing that non-null buffer at scope exit -> a 1-byte leak per construction. Latent for any explicit `Vec.with_capacity(0)` / `String.with_capacity(0)`, but the presize.rs pass (phase-7-codegen 7.3 lever #1) makes it COMMON: it rewrites `let mut v = Vec.new(); while i < k { v.push(..) }` to `Vec.with_capacity(k)`, so any counted-fill loop whose bound evaluates to 0 at runtime (the VM's `run(prog, 0)` with no local slots) leaked once per call. LeakSanitizer on Linux CI (memory-sanitizer job) is the authoritative gate; a local macOS asan run misses it (no LSan). FIX (B-2026-07-11-15): in src/codegen/assoc_call.rs, guard the buffer allocation for all zero-capacity constructors so a zero byte-count yields a NULL data pointer with no malloc, making `with_capacity(0)` bit-identical to `Vec.new()` (`{null, 0, 0}`) — the drop then no-ops and the first push grows from null (`realloc(null, _) == malloc`). New helper `with_capacity_buffer_or_null` handles the panicking `Vec`/`VecDeque`/`String.with_capacity`; new helper `fallible_with_capacity_buffer` handles `try_with_capacity`, where the zero case must resolve to `Result.Ok({null,0,0})` and NOT a spurious `Err(OutOfMemory)` (a null buffer is only OOM when an allocation was actually attempted). For a compile-time-constant nonzero capacity LLVM folds the `== 0` test away, so the common `with_capacity(<literal>)` keeps its single unconditional allocation. Verified ASAN/LSan-clean: the VM example, the narrowed presize repro, and a dedicated regression exercising all three constructors at zero cap plus the nonzero grow-after path. Found via the vm.kara dogfood (feedback_katas_are_bug_finders).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: vm.kara dogfood (stack-based bytecode VM): `run(prog_arith(), 0)` — a program run with zero local slots — leaked one byte per call under LeakSanitizer. Narrowed to `let mut v = Vec.new(); while i < n { v.push(..) }` with a runtime `n == 0`.

title: Module-level `let` binding with a COMPUTED / cross-referencing initializer (e.g. `let DOUBLED: i64 = COUNT * 2;` referencing another module binding, or any non-literal/non-composite RHS) is not emitted by codegen: `module_binding_init` (src/codegen/module_bindings.rs) returns None for an Identifier/BinaryOp RHS, so the binding is silently skipped and a later read fails with `codegen: Undefined variable 'DOUBLED'`. Only literal + composite (Tuple/Array/Repeat/Struct/enum-unit) initializers are handled today (the module_binding_init doc explicitly defers computed shapes to 'slice 10 or a future widening'). The INTERPRETER now evaluates these correctly (B-fixed 2026-07-11: register_items ModuleBinding arm), so run succeeds (prints 84) while build errors — a LOUD run-vs-build divergence (build refuses to compile; not a silent miscompile). FIX DIRECTION: const-fold a module-binding initializer that references already-declared module globals — evaluate the RHS against the const values of prior bindings (a small const-evaluator over the module-binding dependency order), OR route computed initializers through the existing `finalize_module_binding_static_init` runtime static-init pass. Surfaced while closing the module-level-let interpreter run-parity gap; the literal + composite + `let mut`-reassignment surface now has full run==build parity.
fix: this commit — computed module-binding initializers routed through __karac_static_init (declare zero placeholder global + compile_expr the initializer in the prologue, storing before main). Annotated computed/cross-referencing inits (let DOUBLED: i64 = COUNT * 2) now run==build. Follow-up (same day): UN-annotated computed inits (let DOUBLED = COUNT * 2) also closed — lowering threads the typechecker's inferred value-expr type into a new Program.module_binding_types side-table (keyed by binding name), and declare_module_bindings sizes the placeholder global from it when there is no `: TYPE`. The typechecker stays the single source of truth for the type (codegen never re-infers). Both i64 chains and an i32 binding are covered by test_e2e_modbind_computed_unannotated_initializer + interpreter parity. Module-binding run==build parity is now COMPLETE (literal, composite, let mut, annotated-computed, un-annotated-computed).

title: `fold(init, |acc, x| body)` on a fused iterator chain had no codegen terminal. The sequential iterator engine in `src/codegen/method_call.rs` (`try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec`) only supports `collect` as a terminal; a chain ending in `fold` (`v.iter().map(f).filter(p).fold(0, |a,x| a+x)`) fell through the whole dispatcher to the loud 'no handler for method \'fold\' on non-identifier receiver' error, so ANY `fold` on an iterator chain failed `karac build` and the default JIT `karac run` (the interpreter handled it — a run-vs-build divergence). FIX (B-2026-07-11-17): new `try_compile_iter_chain_fold`, wired into `compile_method_call` next to the `collect` entry, gated on an iterator-chain receiver (a MethodCall or Range — `Column`/`Tensor.fold` on a variable receiver is intercepted earlier by `try_compile_column_method`, so it never reaches here). It reuses the collect desugar's map/filter element-threading (bind-or-elide when the current element already IS the stage param) but emits an ACCUMULATE sink instead of a `push`: it peels the map/filter adaptors down to a base source the `for`-loop already iterates correctly (`X.iter()`/`iter_mut`/`into_iter`/`chars`/`bytes`/`keys`/`values`, or a range) and builds `{ let mut __facc = init; for <elem> in <base> { <filter as if, map as let-rebind>; let <acc_p> = __facc; __facc = <fold_body> }; __facc }`, compiled via `compile_expr`. Materializing no intermediate Vec sidesteps the fused chain's element-type recovery problem (codegen has no lowering-derived side-table entry for a SYNTHETIC `collect`). Fails CLOSED (Ok(None) -> the loud dispatch error, never a silent wrong answer) for any shape it can't lower: a non-map/filter adaptor in the chain (enumerate/take/zip/scan), a non-single-Binding closure, or a base that is itself an unrecognized adaptor MethodCall. Verified interp==JIT==native across bare-iter/map/filter/filter+map/if-body-max/nonzero-init/empty-source/range folds, and ASAN/LSan-clean over heap-String-field records. Found via the pipeline.kara dogfood (feedback_katas_are_bug_finders). The broader iterator-terminal gaps this dogfood also surfaced (for-over-chain silent miscompile; sum/reduce/for_each/any/all) are B-2026-07-11-18 and B-2026-07-11-19.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: pipeline.kara dogfood (functional log-analytics pipeline): every aggregate query (`iter().filter(..).map(..).fold(init, |acc,x| ..)`) failed `karac build`/JIT while the interpreter ran fine.

title: SILENT MISCOMPILE: `for x in <iter-chain-with-map/filter>` iterates ZERO times in codegen (interpreter iterates correctly) — wrong answer, no error.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found while building the pipeline.kara dogfood + fixing B-2026-07-11-17 (fold): probing `for x in <iter-chain>` shapes. — `for x in v.iter().map(|x| x*10) { s = s + x }` and `for x in v.iter().filter(|x| x>2) { .. }` run the loop body ZERO times under `karac build` / the default JIT `karac run`, silently producing the pre-loop state (e.g. sum stays 0), while the tree-walk interpreter iterates correctly. No diagnostic. `compile_for` (src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs) has explicit peel arms for `.iter()`/`.into_iter()`/`.chars()`/`.bytes()`/`.enumerate()` and ranges — each added to fix a PAST silent-miscompile of the same shape (the arm comments cite chars, bytes, indexed-Vec[String]) — but a `.map(..)`/`.filter(..)` adaptor iterable has NO arm and falls through to the dispatcher's silent `_ =>` arm, so the body never runs. This is the dangerous class (silent wrong result). WORKS: bare `for x in v.iter()`, `for x in v` by-value, `for (i,x) in v.iter().enumerate()`, and the MATERIALIZED form `for x in v.iter().map(..).collect()` (routing through the working `collect` engine). REPRO (prints 0, want 100): `fn main(){ let v: Vec[i64] = [1,2,3,4]; let mut s = 0; for x in v.iter().map(|x| x*10) { s = s + x; } println(f"{s}"); }`. SUGGESTED FIX: add `compile_for` arm(s) that route a map/filter (fusible-adaptor) iterable through the same fused desugar the collect engine uses, with the loop body as the sink — the same generalization B-2026-07-11-17's fold terminal wants (both need 'fused chain with a custom per-element sink'; a shared, sink-parameterized fusion engine would fix both and subsume the fold desugar). Filed on discovery per the never-route-around-it rule; the pipeline.kara dogfood avoids the shape (uses `.collect()` then `for p in slow`).
resolution:
Fixed. `compile_for` (src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs) now routes a `map`/`filter` adaptor iterable through the SAME map/filter fusion as the fold terminal (B-2026-07-11-17), with the USER'S loop body as the per-element sink. The fold fix's front half was extracted into two shared helpers in src/codegen/method_call.rs: `peel_fused_map_filter_chain` (peels map/filter down to a for-iterable base source, validating it — fail-closed on any other shape) and `build_fused_chain_body` (threads the element through the adaptors — `if ` for filter, `let

= ` for map, with bind-or-elide — ending in a caller-supplied sink closure). `try_compile_for_iter_chain` uses both with a sink that binds the user pattern to the adapted element and inlines the user body, emitting `for in { ; let = ; }` compiled via compile_expr. The user body inlines into the base for-loop, so `break`/`continue`/labels target it correctly. Gated in compile_for on the outermost method being `map`/`filter` (empty-adaptor / bare-source iterables keep their existing arms). Verified interp == JIT across map / filter / filter+map / struct-element / nested / break / continue, and ASAN/LSan-clean over Vec[String] map/filter chains. Full codegen (2152) + memory_sanitizer (598) green; regressions test_e2e_for_over_iter_chain + asan_for_over_iter_chain_heap_elems_no_leak. This is the sink-parameterized fusion generalization proposed in the original filing; it subsumes the fold desugar (both now share peel + build_fused_chain_body).

title: FIXED (last gap c964b29). Rollup of the sequential-iterator terminal/adaptor codegen+typecheck gaps the pipeline.kara dogfood surfaced. All tracked sub-items now build == run (interp == JIT == AOT): sum / reduce / for_each (typecheck+interp+codegen), any / all codegen terminals, `|_|` wildcard closure params in a chain, `let it = v.iter()` materialized-iter-let codegen, and `reduce` codegen (was deferred) — these landed across earlier slices. The LAST gap, `count()` as a fused-chain terminal (`v.iter().count()` / `.filter(...).count()` / `.map(...).count()` failed under codegen — the receiver was caught by the materialized-collection `len`/`count` intercept which tried to compile the chain as a Vec and fell over on the `map`/`filter` adaptor, while the interpreter ran it), is fixed in c964b29: an iterator-chain `count` arm before that intercept desugars to `fold(0, |acc, _| acc + 1)` via the shared peel, failing closed so `s.chars().count()` still uses the intercept. Verified across bare/filter/map/filter+map chains, empty source, `range().count`, and `chars().count()`; valgrind-clean. Test: `test_e2e_iter_chain_count_terminal`. (Out of this rollup's scope: other terminals — `min`/`max`/`product`/`find`/`position`/`nth`/`last` — are separate future work, not tracked here.)
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced while building pipeline.kara + fixing fold (B-2026-07-11-17): probing the sequential iterator terminal/adaptor support matrix. — A tracking rollup of the remaining sequential-iterator gaps the pipeline.kara dogfood surfaced (fold itself is fixed in B-2026-07-11-17; the for-over-chain silent miscompile is B-2026-07-11-18). Each is independently fixable; grouped here to avoid scattering one-line entries. (1) TYPECHECK rejects `v.iter().sum()`, `.reduce(|a,x| ..)`, `.for_each(|x| ..)` with 'no method X on type Iterator' — not in the accepted terminal set in src/typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs (which DOES list all/any/collect/count/fold/map/filter/…). (2) `v.iter().any(|x| x>3)` / `.all(|x| x>0)` TYPECHECK but hit the loud codegen 'no handler for method any/all on non-identifier receiver' — no codegen terminal (interpreter runs them). (3) A `|_|` wildcard closure param in a chain (`v.iter().map(|_| 7).collect()`) makes the fused-chain codegen bail, surfacing as 'no handler for method collect on non-identifier receiver' — a named param works. (4) `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` fails codegen with 'Vec/String method iter is not yet supported in codegen' — `iter` only works FUSED into a chain, not materialized to a `Value::Iterator` variable (interpreter supports it). All four are codegen/typecheck completeness gaps, not miscompiles (loud failures except none are silent). Most naturally fixed alongside the sink-parameterized fusion engine proposed in B-2026-07-11-18: `sum`=fold(+), `reduce`=fold-first, `for_each`/`any`/`all` are sink variants. `count` on a chain already works via the len/is_empty/count intercept.
progress: any/all FIXED (this thread, 71a34fa): the short-circuit boolean terminals now have codegen terminals (src/codegen/method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_any_all`), reusing the shared map/filter fusion (peel_fused_map_filter_chain + build_fused_chain_body, B-2026-07-11-17/-18) with a short-circuit sink (result seeded false/true, flipped + `break` on the deciding element). Typecheck + interpreter already accepted them, so this was codegen-only. Verified interp==JIT across any/all true/false, empty-source edges (any->false, all->true), adapters-before-terminal (filter+any, map+all), heap-String elements, and ranges; ASAN-clean over a map->heap-String chain that short-circuits mid-stream. Tests: codegen::test_e2e_iter_chain_any_all_terminal, memory_sanitizer::asan_iter_chain_any_all_short_circuit_no_leak. STILL OPEN: (a) sum/reduce/for_each rejected at typecheck (not in the Iterator method set in src/typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs) AND unsupported in the interpreter (method_call_iter.rs only has fold/any/all/count/collect) — each needs a 3-phase add (typecheck + interpreter + a codegen sink: sum=fold(+), for_each=for-loop, reduce=fold with Option result); (b) `|_|` wildcard closure param makes the fused-chain codegen bail; (c) `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` (iter materialized to a Value::Iterator variable) unsupported in codegen. || `|_|` WILDCARD PARAMS FIXED (bd709ac): the collect/fold/any/all fused-chain codegen required a `PatternKind::Binding` and bailed on a `_` param (`map(|_| 7).collect()` -> loud 'no handler'; the interpreter already accepted it — a run-vs-build divergence). Codegen now binds a `_` to a fresh throwaway name in both the map/filter ADAPTERS (try_compile_iter_adaptor_collect_to_vec + peel_fused_map_filter_chain) and the TERMINAL closures (shared `closure_param_name` helper — covers `fold(0, |a, _| a + 1)` count, `any(|_| ..)`). Verified interp==JIT across adapter + terminal wildcards; test codegen::test_e2e_iter_chain_wildcard_closure_param. || `for_each` RECLASSIFIED: not merely a 3-phase add — its natural use `for_each(|x| acc = acc + x)` is exactly the capture-MUTATING shape that diverges under the unimplemented `mut ref` closure capture (B-2026-07-11-23). Deferred to that fix. || STILL OPEN: sum/reduce (typecheck+interp+codegen; sum needs a typed zero from the element type, reduce an Option result seeded by the first element), and `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` (iter materialized to a Value::Iterator variable) in codegen. || `sum` FIXED (this thread): the numeric `sum()` terminal now lands across all three surfaces. TYPECHECK (src/typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs `"sum"` arm) accepts it, validates a numeric element (is_numeric || type_param_has_numeric_bound; a String element is rejected with 'requires a numeric element type'), records the yielded element TypeExpr span-keyed in a new `iter_terminal_elem_types` side-table (typechecker -> ast::Program -> codegen, wired through lowering.rs like `temp_recv_elem_types`), and returns the element type. INTERPRETER (method_call_iter.rs `"sum"` arm) drains the iterator, adding each element to an accumulator seeded from the first (empty -> Int(0)). CODEGEN (src/codegen/method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_sum`) desugars to the fold engine with a synthesized `(0 as <elem>)` cast init (a width-correct zero for every numeric type incl. isize/usize/f32/f64 without an IntSuffix spelling) and an `acc + x` body, reusing the shared map/filter fusion; fails closed (Ok(None) -> loud dispatch error) when the element type wasn't recorded. Verified interp==JIT==AOT across iter().sum, map().sum, filter().sum, filter().map().sum, f64 sum (zero must be 0.0), empty (0), and range sum. Tests: codegen::test_e2e_iter_chain_sum_terminal, interpreter::test_iter_sum_terminal, typechecker::test_iter_sum_rejects_non_numeric_element. STILL OPEN: `reduce` (Option result seeded by the first element; the general-element case needs a non-numeric-safe accumulator seed, unlike sum's typed zero) and `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` (iter materialized to a Value::Iterator variable) in codegen. || `reduce` LANDED interp-first (this thread): TYPECHECK (stdlib_iter.rs `"reduce"` arm) accepts `reduce(f: Fn(A,A)->A) -> Option[A]` (was rejected as 'no method reduce'), records the element TypeExpr in `iter_terminal_elem_types`. INTERPRETER (method_call_iter.rs `"reduce"` arm) folds with the first element as the seed, returns Some(acc) / None (empty). Verified under `karac run`: sum-reduce 10, max-reduce 4, empty None, map().reduce 20. CODEGEN is INTENTIONALLY DEFERRED with a loud, actionable error (method_call.rs: '`Iterator.reduce()` is not yet supported under karac build … re-run with --interp, or use .fold(init, f)') rather than the generic no-handler fall-through: its `Option[A]` result would need a synthetic accumulator + per-element match/Some/None whose Option layout depends on typechecker-supplied payload sizing the fused desugar can't rederive without a typecheck pass on the synthetic nodes (unlike `sum`, whose desugar is pure arithmetic). No silent wrong answer. Tests: interpreter::test_iter_reduce_terminal, typechecker::test_iter_reduce_accepts_and_yields_option. STILL OPEN: `reduce` CODEGEN (needs an Option-layout-aware terminal or a typecheck pass over synthetic desugar nodes), and `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` (iter materialized to a Value::Iterator variable) in codegen. || `for_each` LANDED across all three surfaces (this thread, now unblocked by the B-2026-07-11-23 mut-ref capture fix): TYPECHECK (stdlib_iter.rs `"for_each"` arm) accepts `for_each(f: Fn(T) -> ()) -> ()` (param seeded from T, return inferred freely like `map`, result discarded). INTERPRETER (method_call_iter.rs `"for_each"` arm) drains the iterator invoking `f` per element for side effects, returns unit; a capture-mutating body now propagates via the Rule 2 shared-cell capture. CODEGEN (method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_for_each`) desugars to a `for` loop over the peeled base with the closure body INLINED as the loop body (reusing peel_fused_map_filter_chain + build_fused_chain_body), yielding unit — the body inlines so capture-mutation propagates and no closure value is built (the stored-mut-ref-closure refusal never applies). Verified interp==JIT==AOT across bare for_each (capture-accumulate 10), map().for_each (30), filter().for_each (6), and range for_each (10). Gates: fmt, clippy, non-llvm, codegen (2198) green. Tests: interpreter::test_iter_for_each_terminal, codegen::test_e2e_iter_chain_for_each_terminal. STILL OPEN on B-19: `reduce` CODEGEN and materialized `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` in codegen. || `reduce` CODEGEN landed for SCALAR elements (this thread): `try_compile_iter_chain_reduce` (src/codegen/method_call.rs) desugars a scalar `reduce(|a,x| body)` to an `Option[A]` accumulator seeded `None`, folded per element via a synthetic `match __racc { None => Some(x), Some(a) => Some(body) }` — the type-erased 4-word Option layout lets the synthetic `Some(...)`/`None` construction (coerce_to_payload_words) and the tag-dispatched match (a bare `None` binding is recognized as a unit variant by tag) compile with NO typecheck pass over the nodes; the `Option[A]` accumulator annotation supplies A for the `Some(acc)` payload binding. Two AST-shape gotchas resolved empirically: the `Some` ctor callee must be a bare `Identifier` (a `Path` form miscompiled to all-None), and the element must be trivially-copyable. HEAP-element reduce (String/Vec/struct) is GATED OUT (`is_trivially_copyable_te`) — its synthetic `Some(acc) => Some(f(acc,x))` double-frees the payload (extracted acc consumed by f AND the old accumulator dropped); it falls through to the loud `--interp` deferral (interpreter runs it correctly). Verified interp==JIT==AOT for scalar reduce (sum 10, max 4, empty None, map().reduce 20, filter().reduce 9); heap reduce interp a-b-c / build loud-defers (no crash, no silent wrong). Gates: fmt, clippy, non-llvm, codegen (2202) green. Test: codegen::test_e2e_iter_chain_reduce_scalar_terminal. STILL OPEN on B-19: heap-element `reduce` codegen (payload rc-accounting in the synthetic match) and materialized `let it = v.iter(); it.fold(..)` in codegen. || MATERIALIZED-ITERATOR bindings LANDED (this thread): `let it = v.iter(); it.<terminal|adaptor>(..)` / `for x in it` now compile. Codegen has no runtime iterator value, so a `let it = <chain>` whose RHS is a fusable `.iter()/.iter_mut()/.into_iter()` chain is NOT codegen'd — it records the (recursively-inlined) chain expr (`iter_let_bindings`, src/codegen.rs) and skips (src/codegen/stmts.rs Let arm). Each use then inlines the chain via `substitute_iter_let_receiver` — at method calls (`compile_method_call` top: `it.fold(..)` -> `v.iter().fold(..)`) and at for-loops (`compile_for` top: `for x in it` -> `for x in v.iter()`), so the existing fused terminals/adaptors/for-paths handle them. Chained lets compose (`let it5 = it4.filter(g)` records `v.iter().filter(g)`). SOUND GATE: intercept only when the RHS is typechecker-typed `Iterator` (new `iterator_typed_exprs` span-set: typechecker expr_types -> ast::Program -> codegen via lowering.rs, sibling of `string_typed_exprs`) AND the peeled base is a genuine iter method — this is what stops a collection-returning `.iter()` (`Column.iter() -> Vec[Option[T]]`, the eager `chars()`/`bytes()` materializations, bare ranges) from being mis-inlined (two such regressions in the syntactic-only draft: `Column.iter()` then `all.len()`, and `s.bytes()` then `b[0]` — both fixed by the type gate). Unsupported uses (stateful `it.next()`, materialized-range terminal, passing `it` to a fn) fail closed (loud `--interp`), never silent-wrong. Verified interp==JIT==AOT: fold/sum/for_each over a materialized iter, adaptor-in-let, chained filter-let, for-over-materialized-iter; `Column.iter()`+len and `bytes()`+index compile unchanged. Gates: fmt, clippy, non-llvm, codegen (2205) green. Tests: codegen::{test_e2e_materialized_iterator_binding, test_e2e_collection_returning_iter_not_mis_materialized}. STILL OPEN on B-19: heap-element `reduce` codegen (payload rc-accounting in the synthetic match) — the last leg; scalar reduce, sum, for_each, and materialized iterators all land.

fix: gpu_wgsl::reachable_helpers: add Index/MethodCall/Cast arms to calls_in and a StmtKind::Let arm to calls_in_block so #[gpu] helper calls in those AST positions are gathered into the reachable-helper set.
title: GPU helper-call gathering (reachable_helpers, GPU-LBM-5) misses #[gpu] helper calls inside Index / let-RHS / MethodCall / Cast positions, so a valid GPU kernel calling a helper there fails codegen with a misleading 'unsupported call in a GPU kernel body' error.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced building the GPU-SLIP-2c f32 LBM `stream` kernel on Metal: `let c = coll[g_idx(x,y,w)]` and `coll[g_idx(x-1,y,w)].f1` place the `g_idx` #[gpu] helper call inside an Index expression (and a let RHS). — reachable_helpers walks a #[gpu] kernel/helper body to gather the transitively-reachable helpers to emit as WGSL fns; its calls_in only traversed Call/Binary/Unary/If/Block/StructLiteral/FieldAccess, and calls_in_block only visited StmtKind::Expr statements. So a helper call sitting in an Index (`buf[idx(...)]`), a let-binding RHS (`let c = buf[idx(...)]`), a method receiver/args, or a cast operand was never collected, and lower_call then rejected it with 'unsupported call ... only arithmetic/comparison operators and #[gpu] helper functions are supported' even though the helper existed. Latent since GPU-LBM-5 (helpers) — prior helper-using kernels only called helpers in struct-field-init and if-condition positions, which were covered. The LBM stream kernel is the first to index a buffer by a helper result. Fix adds the missing Index/MethodCall/Cast traversal arms and the StmtKind::Let arm. Regression test: gpu_wgsl::gathers_helper_in_index_and_let_rhs. Not a miscompile (loud codegen error), so no wrong-output risk; a completeness gap that blocked a valid kernel.

title: SILENT: reusing an owned `Option[shared struct]` value across two by-value consuming calls whose callee CLONES the matched subtree double-frees under codegen (JIT+AOT) — heap corruption, no diagnostic; interpreter correct
detail: Passing the SAME owned `Option[shared struct]` binding by value to a recursive consumer TWICE, where the consumer pattern-matches the payload and CONSTRUCTS new nodes referencing the matched subtree's fields (a deep clone), corrupts the heap under both JIT and AOT (`malloc(): unaligned tcache chunk detected` — glibc double-free/write-after-free detector). The interpreter (`karac run --interp`) produces the CORRECT result, so it is a run/build divergence. NO diagnostic is emitted — the RC-fallback pass does not detect that the reused owned handle needs an inc before the first consume, so the first call frees the underlying nodes and the second call double-frees them. MINIMAL REPRO (build crashes, interp prints `nodes: 4`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn clone_offset(node: Option[Node], delta: i64) -> Option[Node] { match node { None => None, Some(n) => Some(Node { val: n.val + delta, left: clone_offset(n.left, delta), right: clone_offset(n.right, delta) }) } } fn count_nodes(node: Option[Node]) -> i64 { match node { None => 0i64, Some(n) => 1i64 + count_nodes(n.left) + count_nodes(n.right) } } fn main() { let orig = Some(Node { val: 1, left: Some(Node { val: 2, left: None, right: None }), right: None }); let c1 = clone_offset(orig, 10i64); let c2 = clone_offset(orig, 20i64); println(f"nodes: {count_nodes(c1) + count_nodes(c2)}"); }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (1) a SINGLE clone_offset(orig,..) call with no reuse is CLEAN (build == interp). (2) Reusing `orig` twice but with a READ-ONLY consumer (`count_nodes(orig); count_nodes(orig);` — matches+recurses but constructs nothing) is CLEAN. (3) The same nested-Vec BST-shape builder that SHARES matched handles directly (`left: lefts[a]` instead of `clone_offset(lefts[a],..)`) is CLEAN. So the trigger is the conjunction: reuse-after-consume of an owned Option[shared] handle AND a callee that reconstructs nodes from the matched payload. FIX AREA: the ownership/RC-fallback pass fails to flag the reused owned shared-struct binding at the first by-value call site (no N0503), so codegen emits a move (free at callee scope end) instead of an inc-then-move; the callee's match-arm drop of the owned `node` param then frees storage the caller still references for the second call. Likely interacts with the recursive-clone drop accounting (the callee both consumes `node` and returns fresh nodes). Because it is SILENT and corrupts the heap it can miscompile arbitrary user code — high severity.
source: kata:leetcode-95-unique-bst-ii (generate_trees_dp.kara, bottom-up shape-DP with value offset)
resolution: Fixed for the LOCAL binding manifestation (`let orig = Some(<shared struct literal>)`): codegen's let-binding `shared_option_info` resolution gained a case (e) that registers a fresh `Some(<shared>)` RHS into the caller-retains model (`track_rc_option_var` -> `var_option_shared_heap`), so a per-pass arg-site inc (`share_option_shared_ref_for_arg`) balances the callee's exit dec and a scope-exit dec balances the literal's rc==1. src/codegen/stmts.rs. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_fresh_some_shared_reused_across_consuming_calls_no_double_free. NOTE: the sibling manifestation where the reused value is a Vec-of-Vec ELEMENT (`clone_offset(shapes[i][j], ..)`) under container growth is a DISTINCT, still-open defect tracked as B-2026-07-11-24.

title: DOUBLE-FREE: `for it in vec { match it { Variant(payload) => ... } }` over a Vec of a heap-bearing user STRUCT/ENUM double-frees the payload — the loop element is in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars`, which the match borrow classification never consulted
detail: Iterating a `Vec[E]` (E a non-shared user enum, or a struct, with a heap-bearing variant/field) and matching the loop element DIRECTLY — `for it in items { match it { A(x) => read x.field } }` — double-frees the payload's heap (String buffer / inner Vec) at scope exit: `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`. ROOT CAUSE: a heap-bearing user struct/enum for-loop element is registered in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars` (the deep-copy-on-whole-move / callee-entry-copy model, `types_lowering.rs::mark_for_loop_borrow_if_heap`), NOT `for_loop_borrow_vars`. `scrutinee_is_borrowed_binding` (control_flow_match.rs) only consulted `for_loop_borrow_vars`, so `match it` was NOT classed as a borrow; the struct payload `x` bound out of the borrowed element registered its own scope-exit drop, which then double-freed against the container's per-element drop when the Vec unwound. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) a SCALAR-payload enum element (`Vec[enum{A(i64)}]`) is fine (no buffer to alias); (b) `for a in items { let x = a; match x { ... } }` (whole-move COPY first) is fine — `let x = a` deep-copies via the owned-agg model, so x owns its heap. Only the DIRECT match on the borrowed loop element hit it. Sibling of B-2026-07-11-12 (borrowed-scrutinee struct-payload double-free) but for the for-loop-owned-agg element, which #12 did not cover. REPRO (double-frees, want 35 2): `struct Named { name: String, tags: Vec[i64] } enum Item { A(Named), B } fn total(items: ref Vec[Item]) -> i64 { let mut n = 0; for it in items { match it { A(x) => { n = n + x.name.len(); for t in x.tags { n = n + t; } } B => {} } } n } fn main() { let mut v: Vec[Item] = Vec.new(); let mut tg: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); tg.push(10); tg.push(20); v.push(Item.A(Named{name:"hello", tags:tg})); v.push(Item.B); println(total(v)); println(v.len()); }`. FIX: `scrutinee_is_readonly_owned_agg_loop_var` — classify a bare-identifier scrutinee in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars` as a borrow (suppressing the payload drop) when no arm moves a payload out (the same escape guard as #12's `scrutinee_is_readonly_borrowed_place`, factored into the shared `no_arm_payload_escapes`). Escaping payloads stay on the owned whole-move path. Surfaced while starting the phase-12 program-level resolver two-pass (which iterates `for it in ref items { match it { Func(f) => define(f.name) } }` — the natural self-hosted-compiler shape), so this unblocks that slice. Regression tests: codegen `for_loop_enum_element_direct_match_payload_read`, ASAN/LSan `asan_for_loop_owned_agg_elem_direct_match_no_double_free`.
resolution: Fixed in 6dbe1fb. A heap-bearing user struct/enum for-loop element lives in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars` (deep-copy-on-whole-move), which `scrutinee_is_borrowed_binding` never consulted, so a direct `match it { A(x) => ... }` gave the borrowed-element payload its own drop -> double-free against the container's per-element drop. Added `scrutinee_is_readonly_owned_agg_loop_var` (borrow-classify such a scrutinee when no arm escapes a payload; shared escape guard `no_arm_payload_escapes` with B-2026-07-11-12's place variant). Full codegen (2154) + memory_sanitizer (600) green; regressions `codegen::for_loop_enum_element_direct_match_payload_read` + ASAN `asan_for_loop_owned_agg_elem_direct_match_no_double_free`. (Renumbered -20->-21->-22 through two mid-flight id collisions with concurrently-landed remote ledger entries.)

title: `mut ref` closure capture (mutation of a captured mutable local) is unimplemented: a closure that writes a captured name mutates a SNAPSHOT, not the outer binding. Stored closures drop the mutation in BOTH interp and codegen; the inlined iterator terminals (fold/any/all) DIVERGE — codegen inlines (mutates outer = design-correct) while the interpreter snapshots.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced adding `for_each` to the iterator terminals (B-2026-07-11-19): a `for_each(|x| c = c + x)` capture-mutating closure diverged interp vs build, tracing to how closures capture mutable locals. — design.md Rule 2 / Rule 2½ (§ Closures: parameter modes, capture, and escape): a bare closure that MUTATES a captured name captures it by `mut ref` (read → ref, mutate → mut ref, consume → own; `own |x|` is the explicit by-value form). So `|x| c = c + x` should mutate the outer `c`. But closures currently capture by VALUE (the interpreter's `env.snapshot()` per the design's 'Tree-walk interpreter (Phase 4) note', and codegen's stored-closure env is likewise a snapshot), so the mutation lands on a copy and is lost. TWO OBSERVABLE FORMS: (1) STORED closure — `fn main(){ let mut c:i64=0; let f=|x:i64|{c=c+x}; f(3i64); f(4i64); println(f"{c}") }` prints 0 in BOTH `karac run` and `karac build` (consistent, but WRONG per the mut-ref-capture design; should be 7). (2) ITERATOR-TERMINAL DIVERGENCE — the fold/any/all codegen terminals (B-2026-07-11-17/-19) INLINE the closure body into the fused loop, giving it live outer access (mutation propagates = design-correct), while the interpreter invokes the closure via invoke_function_value over a captured snapshot (mutation lost). So `let mut c=0; v.iter().fold(0,|a,x|{c=c+1; a+x}); c` (and the `any`/`all` analogues) print the mutated count under `karac build` but 0 under `karac run` — a RUN-vs-BUILD divergence. It only bites the unusual capture-MUTATING closure; idiomatic fold (accumulate via the `acc` param) / any / all (pure predicates) don't capture-mutate, so their shipped tests agree on both engines. IMPACT: (a) `mut ref` closure capture is a missing v1 language feature; (b) the terminal divergence is a latent run-vs-build hazard for capture-mutating closures. BLOCKS: a clean `for_each` — its most natural use is `for_each(|x| total = total + x)`, EXACTLY the divergent capture-mutation shape, so `for_each` was reverted (kept out of typecheck + interpreter + codegen) pending this fix rather than shipping a terminal whose common case diverges. Once `mut ref` capture propagates in the interpreter (the env-snapshot must share, not copy, mut-ref-captured slots), for_each lands trivially (codegen = a for-loop over the chain, already correct) and fold/any/all become consistent for capture-mutating closures too. FIX AREA: interpreter closure environment — mut-ref-captured names must alias the outer cell (the ownership/typechecker pass already classifies capture modes per Rule 2), not be snapshot-copied; codegen's non-inlined (stored) closure env needs the same for form (1). The typechecker/ownership capture-mode classification is the authoritative input both backends should honor.
resolution: PARTIAL (this thread) — the run-vs-build DIVERGENCE is resolved and `mut ref` capture is implemented in the interpreter (the semantic reference); codegen support for a STORED mut-ref closure remains (now refused LOUDLY, never silently wrong). INTERPRETER: a bare closure that MUTATES a captured name now infers `mut ref` capture per design.md Rule 2 — eval of `ExprKind::Closure` computes free_idents (scope-correct) INTERSECT assigned-target roots and promotes exactly those outer slots to `Value::SharedCell` via `wrap_capture` (reusing the pre-existing explicit-`mut ref` machinery). Read-only bare captures keep by-value/snapshot semantics (unchanged). Fixes form (1) interp (`|x|{c=c+x}` -> 7) and resolves form (2): the fold/any/all capture-mutation now agrees interp==codegen (count 3). The assigned-roots AST walk (`assign_target_root` + `collect_assigned_roots_{expr,block}`) was hoisted to `src/ast.rs` so both backends share it. CODEGEN: closures capture BY VALUE into the env struct (no by-reference/mut-ref support — even EXPLICIT `mut ref |...|` silently returned 0), so `compile_closure` now REFUSES a stored closure whose body assigns a captured free var with an actionable error (use --interp, or thread state through params/return) — preventing a silent interp-vs-build divergence. The inlined fold/any/all/sum terminals are UNAFFECTED (they inline the body, never build a closure value) and stay correct. Verified: cap1 interp 7 / build loud-error; cap1e explicit-mut-ref build loud-error; cap2 fold interp==JIT 6/3. Gates: fmt, clippy, non-llvm, codegen (2193) green. Tests: interpreter::{test_closure_bare_mutating_capture_propagates, test_closure_capture_mutation_in_fold_no_run_build_divergence, test_closure_readonly_capture_unaffected}; codegen::{test_e2e_iter_fold_capture_mutation_inlined, test_stored_mutating_closure_rejected_in_codegen}. STILL OPEN: codegen BY-REFERENCE env capture (make a stored mut-ref closure actually work under `karac build` — needs the captured slot heap-boxed and the body to load/store through it, plus the escape-soundness guard so a by-ref capture can't outlive its frame). UNBLOCKS `for_each` (B-2026-07-11-19): the interpreter now propagates capture mutation, so `for_each(|x| acc = acc + x)` no longer diverges — it can land (typecheck + interp + a codegen for-loop sink) as a follow-up. FULLY FIXED 2026-07-12 (d123c06) — the STILL-OPEN codegen by-reference env capture now lands for NON-escaping stored mut-ref closures. IMPLEMENTATION (src/codegen/closures.rs compile_closure): a captured name the body ASSIGNS (mutref_caps = free_vars INTERSECT collect_assigned_roots_expr(body)) is captured BY REFERENCE — its env slot type becomes `ptr` (not the value type), the CREATION site stores the outer slot's ADDRESS (slot.ptr) instead of a loaded value, and the BODY-unpack registers the var's VarSlot directly at that extracted pointer (no local alloca copy) so every read/write in the body loads/stores THROUGH the outer binding. Matches the interpreter's SharedCell aliasing: `|x|{c=c+x}` over f(3);f(4) => 7 on both engines. SOUNDNESS gate = `!is_heap_env`: a by-ref capture is valid only while the outer slot outlives the closure (non-escaping). reject_escaping_capturing_closure (compile_function) already rejects every non-return escape (struct field / collection store / id chain) and a return-tail escape sets is_heap_env, so a closure reaching compile_closure with is_heap_env==false provably does not escape its frame. An ESCAPING mut-ref closure is still REFUSED loudly (would dangle -> UAF) with an updated message. mutref_caps forces the per-name env layout (the per-path struct-field-precise layout doesn't model a ptr-to-root slot); mixed by-value + by-ref captures in one closure work (each free var independently value-or-ptr). Inlined fold/any/all/sum unaffected (never build a closure value). VERIFIED interp==build + valgrind-clean across single/mixed/multi-capture, i64/bool/String(heap) captures, and a closure passed to an invoking fn (apply(g,n)); and that returning / struct-storing / collection-storing a mut-ref closure stays refused. Two orthogonal PRE-EXISTING issues surfaced but NOT in scope: (1) a `Vec[Fn]`-local closure buffer is not freed at scope exit (identical 64B leak for a read-only-capture closure — a Vec-of-closures drop gap); (2) the ownership checker false-positives 'value g moved then used again' when a HEAP-capturing (String) closure is called twice (codegen/run ignore ownership, so String mut-ref capture still runs correct). Gates: codegen 2241, memory_sanitizer 650, par_codegen 159, non-llvm full, clippy(llvm+no-llvm)+fmt green. Tests: codegen::{test_stored_mutating_closure_by_ref_capture, test_escaping_mutating_closure_still_rejected_in_codegen} (replaced the old rejection test) + memory_sanitizer::asan_closure_mut_ref_capture_no_leak. UNBLOCKS for_each (B-2026-07-11-19): both engines now propagate capture mutation.

title: Passing a `Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]]` ELEMENT by value to a consuming (cloning) callee corrupts the heap when the outer Vec GROWS in a loop — silent wrong answer at small sizes, double-free crash at larger; interpreter correct
detail: Sibling of B-2026-07-11-21 (which fixed the local `let x = Some(<shared literal>)` manifestation). Here the reused owned `Option[shared]` value is a nested-Vec element `shapes[i][j]` (a `Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]]` double-index) passed BY VALUE to a recursive consumer that clones the matched subtree, inside a loop that GROWS the outer Vec (`shapes.push(cur)`) and re-reads earlier entries. Codegen (JIT+AOT) mis-accounts the element's refcount: the consuming callee's `Option[shared]` param decs at exit but no call-site retain fires for an Index-shaped arg rooted in a grown nested Vec, so the element the container still owns is over-decremented. Manifests progressively by size: interpreter is correct at every size; codegen is correct at n=1, SILENTLY WRONG at n=2 (`build_all(2)` node-sum 6 vs the correct 4 — extra/aliased nodes counted), and CRASHES at n>=3 (`malloc(): unaligned fastbin/tcache chunk`). DISCRIMINATORS: (1) a SINGLE-level Vec index reused across two consuming clone calls (`clone_offset(vec[0], ..)` twice, `vec: Vec[Option[shared]]`) is CLEAN — the single-Vec index-arg already gets a retain. (2) Reading the inner Vec by value first (`let lefts = shapes[i]; clone_offset(lefts[a], ..)`) vs direct double-index (`clone_offset(shapes[i][a], ..)`) BOTH crash — the intermediate `let` is not the trigger. (3) SHARING the element instead of cloning (`cur.push(Some(Node{ left: shapes[i][a], .. }))`, a non-consuming read) is CLEAN. (4) A straight-line single inner read (`let inner = shapes[0]; two clones of inner[0]`) is CLEAN — the outer-Vec GROWTH across loop iterations is the differentiator. So the trigger conjunction is: nested `Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]]` + outer growth in a loop + a by-value element pass to a CONSUMING callee. MINIMAL REPRO (interp `trees: 14 nodesum: 56`; codegen crashes at build_all(4), silently wrong at build_all(2)): the shape-DP `build_all` below. FIX AREA: the call-site retain coverage (`share_option_shared_ref_for_arg` handles Identifier args, `share_option_shared_field_ref_for_arg` handles `obj.field`) has no arm for an Index-into-nested-Vec arg carrying `Option[shared]`; and/or the Vec-growth realloc of a `Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]]` drops inner-Vec element handles it should keep. REPRO: `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn clone_offset(node: Option[Node], delta: i64) -> Option[Node] { match node { None => None, Some(n) => Some(Node { val: n.val + delta, left: clone_offset(n.left, delta), right: clone_offset(n.right, delta) }) } } fn build_all(n: i64) -> Vec[Option[Node]] { let mut shapes: Vec[Vec[Option[Node]]] = Vec.new(); let mut base: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); base.push(None); shapes.push(base); let mut k = 1i64; while k <= n { let mut cur: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); let mut r = 1i64; while r <= k { let lefts = shapes[r - 1i64]; let rights = shapes[k - r]; let mut a = 0i64; while a < lefts.len() { let mut b = 0i64; while b < rights.len() { let left = clone_offset(lefts[a], 0i64); let right = clone_offset(rights[b], r); cur.push(Some(Node { val: r, left: left, right: right })); b = b + 1i64; } a = a + 1i64; } r = r + 1i64; } shapes.push(cur); k = k + 1i64; } shapes[n] }
source: kata:leetcode-95-unique-bst-ii (generate_trees_dp.kara, bottom-up shape-DP with value offset)
resolution: PARTIAL. The general leg — borrow-eliding a `let r = v[i]` binding whose HEAP element `r[j]` is passed by value to a consuming callee (shallow-alias double-free) — is FIXED in d2bb92e (src/codegen/borrow_elision.rs: an `r[idx]`/`v[idx]` call/method argument of a non-trivially-copyable element type disqualifies the borrow, forcing the deep clone; element-copyability oracle threaded from codegen's param + annotated-let declared types). Verified clean for Vec[Vec[String]] element consume (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_vecvec_heap_element_consumed_by_value_no_double_free) and it makes the #95 shape-DP correct for n<=3. The Vec-of-Vec-of-Option[SHARED] specific case has TWO further residual layers, tracked as B-2026-07-11-29: (3) at n>=4 specific clone combinations produce malformed trees (extra nodes), (4) the force-cloned Vec[Option[shared]] binding's scope-exit drop leaks the retained element handles (LSan). Also note the sibling local-binding manifestation B-2026-07-11-21 (fixed 43e1354).

title: SILENT MISCOMPILE: a GENERIC struct's ASSOCIATED function returning a struct (`impl[T] W[T] { fn make(x: T) -> W[T] { W{v:x} } }` called `W.make(7)`) returned a ZEROED struct — native printed 0 / OOM'd on a `Vec[T]` field; the interpreter was correct.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Generics dogfood probe (a generic `Stack[T]`/`W[T]` container): `let s: Stack[i64] = Stack.new(); s.push(..)` OOM'd under `karac build`/JIT while the interpreter was correct. — `W.make(7)` for a generic `impl[T] W[T]` compiled to a hard-coded `i64 0` that the `let w: W[i64] = ...` binding read as a zeroed struct (a `Vec[T]` field then held a garbage `{ptr,len,cap}` header, so a subsequent `push` reallocated against garbage -> `panic: out of memory` / corruption). ROOT CAUSE (dispatch, not layout/ABI): the impl-method declaration pass (src/codegen.rs) registers a generic impl method ONLY into `generic_fns` (keyed `"W.make"`), NOT as a concrete module function — a bare `W.make` would get the all-`i64` default. But the associated-call dispatcher `compile_assoc_call` (src/codegen/assoc_call.rs) only resolves CONCRETE `module.get_function("W.make")` names and has NO `generic_fns` / monomorphization path, so the call matched no arm and fell through to that method's `Ok(const 0)` tail (the same silent-`const 0` trap as B-2026-07-08-25's `T.default()`). The generic FREE-fn equivalent `fn make[T](x)->W[T]` worked (its 1-segment dispatch checks `generic_fns` -> `compile_generic_call`), as did a NON-generic assoc fn (a real module function) and generic DIRECT construction (`W{v:7}` inline). Only the generic-ASSOCIATED path lacked the mono route (residual gap left by B-2026-07-03-23, which wired `generic_fns` into the METHOD-call and struct-literal paths but never the associated-call path). FIX (B-2026-07-11-25): in the 2-segment `Path` dispatch (src/codegen/call_dispatch.rs), before falling through to `compile_assoc_call`, check `generic_fns.contains_key("W.make")` and route to `compile_generic_call` — the same monomorphization a generic free fn / instance method uses; `infer_type_args` binds the type params from the call (a no-arg `S.new()` binds from the typechecker's recorded per-call type args, i.e. the `let` annotation). `make_generic_impl_method_function` already synthesizes the `generic_fns` entry correctly for a no-receiver static fn (it prepends `self` only when the method declares one). Keyed by the LITERAL segment name, so a `T.assoc()` inside a monomorph (segment is the param name `T`, never a registered key) is unaffected and keeps its `compile_assoc_call` type-subst remap. Verified interp==JIT==native across: arg-inferred `T` (`W.make(7)`), return-annotation `T` (`S.new()` no args), a `Vec[T]` field via push/len (the original OOM shape), a `String` instantiation, a method on the returned value, a two-type-param generic (`P.of(a,b)`), and two distinct `i64` monomorphs. Full codegen (2157) + memory_sanitizer (601) green; ASAN/LSan-clean over a String-element `S.new()` built and dropped in a loop. Found via a generics dogfood probe (feedback_katas_are_bug_finders).

title: A fresh-temp ENUM scrutinee whose type has a user `impl Drop` SILENTLY SKIPPED that Drop in `if let` / `while let` / `let…else` / `match` — the user-defined cleanup side effects (unlock / close / log / resource release) never ran, even though the SAME enum in a plain `let s = make()` binding runs it. Not a leak of memory only (the class the sibling scrutinee-temp-scope item, phase-6 line 38, is deferred for) — a correctness gap: `impl Drop for E { fn drop(mut ref self){ println("D") } }` + `if let E.V(x) = make() { … }` printed nothing for the Drop. Found while scoping line 38 (the drop-timing polish): a probe with a user-`Drop` enum vs. a plain `let` isolated it. ROOT CAUSE: `materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs, from the item-B pattern-arm unbound-heap-field-drop work) only materialized + tracked a fresh-temp enum scrutinee when a variant had a HEAP-bearing payload (`has_droppable`), and even then registered ONLY the compiler's field cleanup (`track_enum_var` / `emit_enum_drop_switch`) — never the user `impl Drop` wrapper. So an all-scalar user-Drop enum returned None (never materialized) and its Drop was skipped; a heap-bearing one freed its fields but still skipped the user body. The interpreter had the same gap (bound `let` ran the Drop, a scrutinee did not). FIX (B-2026-07-11-26): (1) codegen — materialize the scrutinee when EITHER a heap field OR a user Drop needs to fire (`drop_method_keys.contains(enum)`), and register `track_user_drop_var` (the `karac_drop_<E>` wrapper, user body) in addition to the heap-field cleanup, ordered so the user body fires FIRST and the field free LAST (LIFO drain). The enum user-drop wrapper runs only the body (its field-cleanup handoff, `emit_struct_drop_synthesis`, is struct-only), so the two registrations don't overlap → no double-free (verified: bound move-out, unbound field, and miss edge all ASAN-clean). (2) interpreter — a new `freshtemp_scrutinee_user_drop_type` (typed via `typecheck_result.expr_types`, gated on `drop_method_keys`) runs the user Drop body on the scrutinee at the four sites, snapshotting the value so it fires on the match edge too (Rust-managed values → no double-free). The user Drop now RUNS in every construct under `check`/`build`/`run`. **Residuals (documented, narrower):** (a) the while-let MISS-edge scrutinee runs its heap-field free but not the user body in both backends; (b) the if-let/match drop-TIMING still differs (codegen at enclosing-scope exit, interpreter at statement/arm end) — that IS the deferred line-38 slice-3 'scrutinee sub-frame' timing polish, distinct from this correctness fix. Gates green: fmt, clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm, codegen 2157, memory_sanitizer 600 (ASAN/LSan clean), interpreter 1152, full non-llvm suite; +5 tests across the three phases.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: scoping probe of phase-6-runtime.md line 38 (if-let/while-let/let-else scrutinee-temp scope) — surfaced a distinct correctness bug next door to the deferred timing polish

fix: codegen/exprs.rs + stmts.rs: bind/assign a gpu.dispatch result into a SoA `layout` variable via AoS->SoA scatter (compile_soa_let_from_gpu_dispatch / compile_soa_assign_from_gpu_dispatch, sharing soa_scatter_aos_into + the factored soa_push_value), instead of storing the AoS {ptr,len,cap} header raw into the multi-group SoA slot.
title: A gpu.dispatch result bound/assigned to a SoA `layout` variable SIGSEGVs: compile_gpu_dispatch_soa returns a standard AoS Vec {ptr,len,cap}, but a layout-bound variable is physically the multi-group SoA struct — the SoA-let/assign dispatch only recognized Vec.new/user-call/literal RHS, so gpu.dispatch fell through and the 3-field AoS header was stored raw into the N-group SoA slot (field reads decode it with SoA striding -> garbage; a re-dispatch reads len/cap as group pointers -> crash). This blocked chaining collide->stream on the GPU (the double-buffered substep).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced building the GPU-SLIP-3 multi-step GPU substep: chaining `let coll = gpu.dispatch(collide, grid); grid = gpu.dispatch(stream, coll, ...)` needs the intermediate to have a `layout` (for the 2nd dispatch's SoA typecheck), but binding a dispatch result into a layout variable SIGSEGV'd. — The SoA let-init (stmts.rs) dispatches on the RHS expression kind: Vec.new()->compile_soa_new, a user Call returning Vec[E]->compile_soa_let_from_call (which monomorphizes the callee to RETURN SoA), a collection literal->compile_soa_let_from_literal (B-2026-07-10-7). gpu.dispatch is a MethodCall on the `gpu` ambient and matched none, so its AoS result (compile_gpu_dispatch_soa wraps the runtime's interleaved buffer as a standard {ptr,len,cap} Vec) was stored raw into the multi-group SoA alloca. Reading coll[i].field then GEP'd field k of the AoS header as group-k's pointer (garbage), and a second gpu.dispatch(stream, coll) read len/cap as SoA group pointers and crashed in memmove uploading the buffer. Verified: even a SINGLE dispatch into a layout variable crashes (not chaining-specific); a user fn returning a push-built Vec into the same layout variable works (the backward-mono path handles it). Fix: gpu.dispatch can't be monomorphized to return SoA (runtime call), so scatter its AoS result -- factor soa_push_value out of compile_soa_method's push arm (the grow+store write half), then compile_soa_let_from_gpu_dispatch builds an empty SoA header + loops soa_push_value(aos[i]) + frees the AoS carrier; compile_soa_assign_from_gpu_dispatch is the reassignment sibling (frees the old groups, resets the header, scatters) for the substep double-buffer move `grid = gpu.dispatch(stream, coll, ...)`. Both go through new SoA-let/assign arms gated on is_gpu_dispatch_call. Validated on Metal (M5 Pro): chain [11,21]->[22,42]; full LBM substep x6 on 32x16 matches CPU f32 to 2.68e-7 with mass conserved + bounded peak speed; macOS `leaks --atExit` on the substep: 0 leaks. Existing SoA push E2E tests validate the push refactor; the scatter itself is Metal-only (no headless GPU in CI).

title: Two monomorph void-return miscompiles: (a) a generic VOID fn whose body TAIL is a statement-position `if`/`while` emitted `ret i64 0` in a void LLVM fn; (b) a bare `return;` inside a void mono compiled inline in `main` emitted `ret i32 0` (main's exit-code sig) — both fail module verification ('non-void return in Function of void return type').
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Generic-container dogfood probe (a `Heap[T: Ord]` with `sift`-style void helpers): a generic VOID fn whose body's tail is an `if` (`fn cmp_swap[T: Ord](..) { if xs[i] > xs[j] { .. } }`) failed `karac build`/JIT while the interpreter was correct. — Two independent gaps in `compile_mono_function` (src/codegen/mono.rs), both making a generic VOID monomorph emit a NON-void return that fails LLVM verification; the interpreter is correct, so both are run-vs-build divergences (loud, caught at build). The generic `T: Ord` comparison was a RED HERRING — `fn f[T](x: T) { if true { } }` (no compare, no bound) triggers (a). (a) TAIL-IF RET-VALUE: a statement-position `if`/`while` yields a default `i64 0` from `compile_block` (control_flow.rs's placeholder), and the mono return-emission (mono.rs ~1606) ret-ed that value WITHOUT the `fn_returns_void` guard the non-generic `compile_function` has (functions.rs ~1744, which discards the placeholder and emits `ret void`). So a void mono with a tail `if`/`if-else`/`if {return;}` emitted `ret i64 0`. It is the uncovered sibling of the same-site narrow-width return fix (B-2026-07-03-N added `coerce_to_current_ret_type` for `-> u8` monos but not the void discard). FIX: add the `fn_returns_void` guard at the mono return-emission — a void mono `ret void`s regardless of the tail's compiled value (mono uses the conventional by-value ABI, no sret/niche/box here, so a void LLVM return type is always a true unit fn). (b) STALE current_fn_name: `compile_mono_function` set `current_fn` but NOT `current_fn_name`, so a mono compiled INLINE inside `main` inherited `current_fn_name == "main"`. A valueless `return;` (exprs.rs) checks `current_fn_name == "main"` to emit `ret i32 0` (main's C-ABI exit-code sig) vs `ret void` — so a bare `return;` in a void generic helper called from main mis-emitted `ret i32 0`. FIX: set `current_fn_name = func.name` in `compile_mono_function` (mirroring `compile_function`), saved/restored by `compile_generic_call` around the inline body. Both blocked generic void helpers with early returns (heap `sift_down`, in-place sort passes). Verified interp==JIT==native across: tail `if`/`if-else`/`if {return;}`, the `T: Ord` compare-and-swap shape, a bare `return;` in a mono called from main AND from another mono, a recursive generic void fn with a return, plus value-returning and narrow-`u8` monos and `return;` in `main` itself (unchanged). Full codegen (2158) + memory_sanitizer (602) + non-codegen green. Found via a generic-container dogfood probe (feedback_katas_are_bug_finders).

title: Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]] deep-clone + consume + grow: force-cloned inner Vec's scope-exit drop LEAKS retained element handles, and at larger sizes specific clone combinations produce MALFORMED trees (extra nodes); interpreter correct
detail: Residual layers of B-2026-07-11-24 remaining after its borrow-elision leg was fixed (d2bb92e). The unique-BST-II bottom-up shape-DP over Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]] (kata #95 generate_trees_dp.kara) diverges from the interpreter on codegen. Two ENTANGLED codegen defects, both root-caused to minimal repros. LAYER 3 (corruption, use-after-free): reusing a Vec[Option[shared]] ELEMENT as a direct consuming call argument, TWICE, with each result captured into a fresh Some(Node{..}) node, mis-frees. MINIMAL REPRO (interp `sum: 6`; codegen `sum: 8`, all three of cur[0]/cur[1]/src[0] alias the same corrupted tree `(1 (20 (31 (32))))` where 20 is the SECOND call's DELTA leaking as a node value): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn clone_offset(node: Option[Node], d: i64) -> Option[Node] { match node { None => None, Some(n) => Some(Node { val: n.val+d, left: clone_offset(n.left,d), right: clone_offset(n.right,d) }) } } fn main() { let mut src: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); src.push(Some(Node{val:1, left:Some(Node{val:2,left:None,right:None}), right:None})); let mut cur: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); let l0 = clone_offset(src[0],10); cur.push(Some(Node{val:0,left:l0,right:None})); let l1 = clone_offset(src[0],20); cur.push(Some(Node{val:1,left:l1,right:None})); /* count cur[0]+cur[1] = 6 */ }`. The niche Vec-element read loads the inner pointer WITHOUT an inc; the callee's param RcDecOption over-decrements the element the container still owns, freeing it prematurely; a later malloc reuses the slot and the second `src[0]` read returns the freshly-pushed node (delta-offset), so the second clone_offset operates on the wrong tree. WORKAROUND that makes it correct: bind the element first (`let s = src[0]; clone_offset(s, ..)`) — clone_owned_vec_index_element deep-clones/retains `s`. Also clean if the clone result is pushed DIRECTLY (no Some(Node{..}) wrap) or on a single (non-reused) call. ATTEMPTED FIX (share_option_shared_index_ref_for_arg — inc the loaded inner of a v[i] Option[shared] arg, mirroring the FieldAccess companion): FIXES the corruption (kata correct for n=1..7 across run/build/autopar0/interp/python) BUT INTRODUCES LAYER-4 LEAKS — bugIdx (`out.push(clone_offset(vec[0],10))` reused) goes from valgrind-clean to 96 B leaked. Root of the entanglement: clone_offset's Option[shared] param is treated as BORROWED (no exit dec) in some call contexts and CONSUMING (RcDecOption) in others, so a blanket caller-side inc over-counts where the callee does not dec. LAYER 4 (leak, pervasive): the shared-node capture + drop accounting leaks independently of layer 3 — even `let s = src[0]; cur.push(Some(Node{left:s}))` (share, no clone) and the direct-clone bugIdx leak under valgrind on this compiler; the Some(Node{shared field}) capt-inc and the Vec[Option[shared]] / node scope-exit drop do not fully balance the retained handles. FIX AREA: needs a precise dec-flow model for an Option[shared] param passed from a Vec-index read (decide borrow vs consume per call site, and make the caller inc iff the callee decs), PLUS completing the shared-node capture/drop so retained handles are released. The two must be fixed together — fixing corruption alone (caller inc) leaks; the interpreter is correct throughout. Systemically under-supported area (nested collections of RC/shared with clone+consume+capture+grow).
source: kata:leetcode-95-unique-bst-ii (generate_trees_dp.kara, bottom-up shape-DP with value offset)
resolution: FIXED (106efc1). Five distinct RC-accounting gaps in the caller-retains model for Option[shared] values read from Vec elements, all closed together so the #95 shape-DP is byte-identical across interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par and LSan/valgrind-clean. (3-corruption) added share_option_shared_index_ref_for_arg, the Index companion to the Identifier/FieldAccess arg retains, so a v[i] Option[shared] consuming-call arg incs the loaded inner (the niche read loads without inc; the callee param RcDecOption otherwise over-decs the element the container still owns). (4-nested-drop-leak) te_recursive_drop_fully_supported now accepts an Option[shared T] payload, so a Vec[Vec[Option[shared]]] scope-exit drop routes to the strictly-recursive emit_vec_drop_fn -> emit_option_drop_fn (tag-guarded rc-dec) instead of the one-level buffer-only fast path that leaked every inner shared node. (let-reuse) stmts.rs case (f) registers a `let s = v[i]` Option[shared] binding into var_option_shared_heap (the retain-clone already applied its +1) so subsequent by-value passes get the arg-inc and a scope-exit dec. (push-move) the Vec.push builtin now emits share_option_shared_ref_for_arg for a moved Option[shared] binding (co-ownership inc: push stores the ref while the source keeps its own under reference semantics), closing the pre-existing out.push(orig) UAF this cluster surfaced. (field-share) the struct-literal field capture-inc is skipped for an already-retain-cloned v[i] field value (maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg's karac_clone_Option_Node inc IS the co-ownership inc; a second capture-inc double-counted with no matching dec -> leak). Earlier concern that layers 3+4 were entangled (the index-arg inc introducing leaks) was itself a symptom of gaps 2/5, not a real coupling: with the recursive drop and the field/binding accounting in place the index-arg inc is clean. Five regression tests in tests/memory_sanitizer.rs (asan_vec_option_shared_index_reused_across_consuming_calls_no_uaf, asan_vecvec_option_shared_scope_exit_drop_no_leak, asan_let_bound_vec_option_shared_reused_no_uaf, asan_push_bound_option_shared_binding_no_uaf, asan_struct_field_shares_vec_option_shared_index_no_leak). The interpreter was correct throughout every layer.

tracker: tests/safety_design.rs::adversarial_escape_via_borrowed_collection_local (#[ignore]d, asserts the desired rejection; auto-enables when fixed); docs/implementation_checklist/phase-9-verification.md
title: FIXED (84061d3). Borrow-return source pinning was gated on the return TYPE being top-level `ref T` / `mut ref T` / `StringSlice`, so an OWNED container whose element/payload is a borrow (`-> Vec[ref T]`, `-> Option[ref T]`, `-> Result[ref T, E]`, tuple-with-ref) never entered the pass and a local-sourced element escaped un-pinned (design.md Feature 4 Part 3: such a container is a borrowed collection bounded by every element source, so a local element dangles like a `-> ref Struct` field). FIX: `check_borrowed_collection_pinning` (ref_return.rs), entered when the return type stores a `ref` in an element/payload position (`type_expr_has_ref_element`), reuses the existing `classify_borrow_return` leaf classifier on each element source. CONSERVATIVE: rejects only a clear `DanglingSource` (local/temp/literal); a borrowable source (`ref` param / `ref self` / ref-local) or any untraceable form is ACCEPTED, so no currently-valid program is newly rejected. Traces two witness shapes: a returned container identifier whose `.push(arg)` sources are classified, and a directly returned `Some(arg)` / `Ok(arg)` borrow-payload wrapper. The `#[ignore]`d adversarial witness `adversarial_escape_via_borrowed_collection_local` is un-ignored and passes; the param-sourced accept controls (Vec + Option) stay accepted; plain `Vec[i64]` unaffected. All suites green (ownership 350, safety 33, typechecker 2020, codegen 2232, memory_sanitizer 632) — no false positives. RESIDUAL: only the `.push()` insert form and bare `Some`/`Ok` wrappers are traced; a qualified `Option.Some(local)`, `insert`/`extend`, or an element stored via indexing is conservatively accepted (miss, not a false reject) — extend the source collector as those shapes appear.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced building the phase-9 borrow-model adversarial soundness corpus (tests/safety_design.rs). A `-> Vec[ref String]` (and `-> Option[ref String]`) whose element borrows an owned LOCAL passes all checks, whereas the structurally-equivalent `-> ref Holder` borrowed-struct escape is correctly rejected as BorrowReturnNotSourcePinned{DanglingSource}. — src/ownership/ref_return.rs::check_ref_return_source_pinning gates the whole source-pinning pass on the return TYPE being `TypeKind::Ref | TypeKind::MutRef` (plus StringSlice): `if !is_ref_ret && !is_slice_ret { return; }`, with an explicit code comment 'Tier-1: plain ref T / mut ref T (+ StringSlice) returns only. Borrows nested in generic wrappers (Option[ref T]) are a follow-on.' So an OWNED container whose element/payload type is `ref T` (`Vec[ref T]`, `Option[ref T]`, `Result[ref T, E]`, `(ref T, ref U)`) is never entered, and an element/payload sourced from a local escapes. NOT an immediate use-after-free today: `v.push(local)` MOVES the owned local into the vector (ownership analysis reports `local` as moved after the push), so the buffer travels with the returned vec and nothing dangles at the return point (interpreter and — for the moved case — codegen keep it alive; a2d derefs the returned element and prints correctly on the interpreter). The unsoundness the design warns about is latent: the `ref` element TYPE becomes a lie (the collection actually OWNS its elements), the borrowed-collection scope bound ('bounded by the shortest-lived element source', design.md Part 3) is not enforced, and any future construction path that stores a genuine non-owning borrow into such a container (rather than moving) would produce a real dangling element with no static guard. FIX SHAPE (deferred): extend the return-type gate to also fire when the return type structurally CONTAINS a `ref` in an element/payload position, and trace each such nested borrow's source through the same classify_borrow_return machinery the struct-field path (classify_borrow_return_struct) already uses per field. Sibling of the completed B-2026-06-07-5 borrowed-struct-return work, which pinned `-> ref Struct` field sources; this is the same idea for `-> Wrapper[ref T]`. Adversarial corpus keeps a green #[ignore]d witness that asserts the desired rejection so the fix auto-verifies.

title: A generic struct instance method mis-inferred its type param `T` (mangled `$i64`, defaulted) when `T` appeared ONLY nested inside a container field (`xs: Vec[T]`): `add(x: T)` failed module verification (String arg to i64 param); `get()->T` / `pop()->Option[T]` returned i64-shaped GARBAGE (silent). All non-i64 T affected; a direct-`T` field made it work.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Generic-container dogfood (a `Heap[String]`): every method mis-monomorphized when the element type param appeared only nested in the `Vec[T]` field. — For `impl[T] H[T] { fn add(mut ref self, x: T) {..} }` called on `h: H[String]`, codegen's instance-method dispatch (src/codegen/method_call.rs) synthesizes an EXPLICIT impl-`T` binding from the receiver's RECORDED struct instantiation and passes it to `compile_generic_call`. But the typechecker's `infer_struct_literal` solves a generic struct's type args only from FIELD VALUES (src/typechecker/fields.rs) — `Vec.new()` leaves the element unconstrained, so `T` is never solved and the literal freezes as the bare `H[TypeParam(T)]`, NOT `H[String]`. That bare `T` (a) lowers to the `i64` unknown-name default AND (b) UNCONDITIONALLY OVERRIDES (compile_generic_call's explicit-args loop, `insert` not `or_insert`) the correct `T=String` that `infer_type_args` bound from the `x: T` arg. Result: mangle `add$i64`, an i64-width `x` param, a String arg → LLVM verifier error. For a method with no `T`-typed arg (`get()->T`, `pop()->Option[T]`, `swap`) there is nothing to rescue the binding, so `T` silently defaults to i64 and the method reads/returns the element with the wrong width → GARBAGE (silent miscompile). `H[i64]` 'works' only because i64 IS the default; all non-i64 T (String/f64/bool) fail. A `struct W[T]{v:T}` (direct-`T` field) works because `infer_struct_literal` solves `T` from the field value → concrete instantiation. The free-fn equivalent works (no receiver-synthesized override). Residual gap from B-2026-07-03-23 (which wired the receiver-instantiation binding but only for field-value-solvable `T`). FIX (B-2026-07-11-31), two complementary codegen changes: (1) method_call.rs — DROP bare, still-unsolved impl type params from the receiver `explicit` prefix (truncate at the first bare param, preserving positional zip), so a concrete method ARG rescues `T` via `infer_type_args` (fixes `add`); (2) stmts.rs + expr_ops.rs — seed the receiver var's instantiation from the concrete `let` ANNOTATION (`H[String]`) via a new `is_generic_named_struct_type_expr` (the struct sibling of the enum recorder), not the frozen RHS, so a method with NO `T`-arg (`get`/`pop`/`swap`) recovers concrete `T` too. Verified interp==JIT==native across String/f64/i64 through add / get(->T) / pop(->Option[T]) / len, and a full `Heap[String]` heapsort (byte-identical). Full codegen (2166) + memory_sanitizer + non-codegen green. Found via the heap.kara generic-container dogfood (feedback_katas_are_bug_finders). NOTE: a `Heap[String]` that SWAPS elements by index still trips a SEPARATE, general double-free (B-2026-07-11-32) unrelated to this type-inference fix.

title: DOUBLE-FREE: an index-based element swap of a NON-COPY `Vec` element (`let t = v[i]; v[i] = v[j]; v[j] = t;` over `Vec[String]`) aliases the heap buffers and double-frees at scope exit. Output is correct (values read before the free), but ASAN / a native run aborts (`free(): double free detected`). NON-generic — a plain `Vec[String]` triggers it; not specific to the generic heap.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced ASAN-testing a `Heap[String]` (B-2026-07-11-31 dogfood): the heap's index-based `swap` double-frees non-Copy elements. — The classic swap idiom `let t = v[i]; v[i] = v[j]; v[j] = t;` over a `Vec[T]` of a NON-COPY `T` (`String`, `Vec`, heap struct) is memory-unsafe under codegen. An index READ of a non-Copy element (`let t = v[i]`) copies the `{ptr,len,cap}` HEADER (aliasing the same heap buffer) rather than moving/cloning; the subsequent index ASSIGNS then leave two slots (or a slot and `t`) pointing at the same buffer, so scope-exit drops free it twice. REPRO (native prints the correct `bb aa cc` then aborts `free(): double free detected`): `fn main(){ let mut v: Vec[String] = [f"aa", f"bb", f"cc"]; let t = v[0]; v[0] = v[1]; v[1] = t; println(v[0]); println(v[1]); println(v[2]); }`. The interpreter is memory-safe (value semantics). WORKS for Copy `T` (`Vec[i64]` swap is a true value copy — no aliasing), which is why the shipped `Heap[i64]` heapsort dogfood (examples/heap.kara) is ASAN-clean. IMPACT: any in-place sort / heap / partition over non-Copy elements written with the natural index-swap is a silent double-free (correct output masks it until ASAN / a hardened allocator aborts). LIKELY FIX AREA: the index-READ of a non-Copy element into a `let` binding must either MOVE (and the source slot must not be dropped, since it's immediately overwritten) or CLONE; and index-ASSIGN must drop-old / not-alias. A dedicated `Vec.swap(i, j)` builtin that swaps headers without a user-visible copy would also resolve the idiom. Filed on discovery per the never-route-around-it rule; examples/heap.kara stays `Copy`-element (i64) until this lands.
resolution: FIXED for the whole NON-generic surface. Root cause: an index-read of a non-Copy element in ASSIGNMENT-RHS position only loaded the {ptr,len,cap} header (compile_vec_index), unlike the Let arm which deep-clones (clone_owned_vec_index_element) — so the destination and the source element co-owned the buffer and double-freed at scope exit. Four coordinated codegen changes: (1) src/codegen/stmts.rs StmtKind::Assign — route the RHS through clone_owned_vec_index_element (deep-clone `s = v[i]` / `v[i] = v[j]` / `self.xs[i] = self.xs[j]`; no-op for every non-index RHS), and OR `rhs_is_heap_vec_index` into the Identifier-target eager-free so the overwritten LHS isn't leaked; (2) src/codegen/collections.rs vec_index_elem_type_expr — added the FieldAccess-object arm (`self.xs[i]`, `h.items[i]`) with monomorph type-param substitution so field-rooted index reads clone too (previously only bare `v[i]`; `let t = self.xs[i]` double-freed); (3) src/codegen/stmts.rs index-target suppression — generalised from Identifier-object to also fire for a field-rooted target whose element is a heap {ptr,len,cap} (String/Vec), so a named-binding RHS `self.xs[j] = t` is move-suppressed; (4) src/codegen/stmts.rs index/field-target arms — zero the staged f-string acc cap (generalising the Identifier + SoA handling) so `v[i] = f"…"` / `p.name = f"…"` don't double-free the moved acc buffer. clone_owned_vec_index_element also guards on the read value's LLVM type matching the element clone it emits (defends against a mis-typed read — see B-2026-07-11-35). Verified interp==JIT==native across bare-Vec and struct-field swaps of String/Vec elements plus the f-string-into-projection and index-read-into-var shapes; 5 new ASAN/LSan cases + 1 codegen E2E; full codegen (2168) + memory_sanitizer (611) + non-codegen green. NOTE: a GENERIC container's field-rooted index read (`Heap[String]` `self.xs[i]`) still reads garbage (element mis-resolved to the i64 default) — a distinct monomorph read bug, B-2026-07-11-35; examples/heap.kara stays i64-element until it lands.

title: Vec[Option[shared]] element drop leaked the shared payloads (buffer-only cleanup) — kata-23 merge-k-lists
detail: A `Vec[shared struct]` or `Vec[Option[shared struct]]` local frees its DATA BUFFER at scope exit but never rc-dec's the shared-struct ELEMENTS, so every element node leaks. Minimal repros (macOS `leaks` detects both — the nodes are genuinely unreferenced at exit): c5 `let mut v: Vec[ListNode] = Vec.new(); v.push(n1); /*drop*/` -> leaks n1; c4 `let mut v: Vec[Option[ListNode]] = Vec.new(); v.push(Some(n1)); v.push(Some(n2));` -> leaks both. A single `Option[shared]` local drops fine (0 leaks), and a bare shared chain built without a Vec drops fine — the leak is specific to the Vec-element path. ROOT CAUSE: the Vec-local cleanup registers `track_vec_var` (buffer-only) instead of the element-aware `track_vec_of_aggs_var`; the dispatch helper `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` (src/codegen/runtime.rs:1681) handles direct shared elements (line 1746, `emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn`) but its `Option` arm (lines 1700-1713) returns None for a SHARED-struct payload (it only accepts inline String/Vec payloads or value struct/enum, not `Option[shared]`), so `Vec[Option[shared]]` falls through to buffer-only drop. The primary `let v = Vec.new()` path (materialize_owned_temp / the let-site track_vec_var) also doesn't route direct `Vec[shared]` through the element-aware drop. REGRESSION: the LSan gate test asan_owned_vec_param_let_move_interval_merge (kata-23 merge-k-lists) was added PASSING 2026-06-07 (9e261565) and is GOOD there under macOS leaks; it is BAD by HEAD~180 and all points since — so main's memory-sanitizer CI job is likely RED.
