This is why fixing either alone regresses: +DEFECT2-only (add field retain -> rc2) makes field+drain LEAK (parent dec only = 1 of 2); +DEFECT1-only (add pop dec) makes field+drain UAF (pop dec + parent dec = 2 of rc1). BOTH must land together. TARGET (co-ownership): field-push retains (+1, rc2); EVERY removal path decs exactly once — Vec residual-drop (already does) AND pop-result-consume (needs the nested-dec transfer). Then all four cases balance. FIX SITES: (1) vec_method.rs ~1990 add `share_option_shared_field_ref_for_arg(&args[0].value, elem_val)` beside the Identifier retain; (2) the harder one — register a nested `Option[Option[shared]]` dec on the pop RESULT (route pop's result binding/temporary through the same nested-drop registration a fresh `Some(Some(..))` binding gets, rather than the flat single-Option `track_rc_option_var`). Same systemically under-supported surface as B-2026-07-11-29 (a full prior session); DEFECT 1 is a genuine separate pop-ownership gap, so this is a multi-site RC slice, not a one-liner. No partial landed — a one-sided fix regresses field+drain (confirmed above), and on this memory-safety surface a regressing partial is worse than the documented UAF-with-workaround (drain the worklist fully / avoid residual elements). COMPLETION 2026-07-12 (fba468e). The fix marked here as 744ca1c was the RETAIN-ONLY half (DEFECT 2, field-push `share_option_shared_field_ref_for_arg`): it closes the field+residual USE-AFTER-FREE but, exactly as this entry's investigation predicted, LEAVES the DRAIN-path LEAK (DEFECT 1) — and in fact a retain-alone lands the field+drain case as a leak (rc 2, only the parent field decs). fba468e adds DEFECT 1: `Vec[Option[shared]].pop()` returns `Option[Option[shared]]` whose heap-BOXED inner-Option payload was freed without rc-deccing the node (box-drop inner_drop_fn=None, since the `inner_struct_name` derivation only covers user structs, not a nested Option[shared]). New `track_boxed_enum_var_with_inner_drop` + shared `option_shared_payload_element_drop` give the box-drop an inner `Option[T]` element drop (`emit_option_drop_fn`, null-guarded rc-dec) for nested-Option[shared] payloads — wired into the fresh-temp match scrutinee, the typed let-binding, AND `untyped_let_boxed_enum_te` (extended to recover `vec.pop()`'s `Option[element]` result type so an untyped `let x = vec.pop()` gets the box-drop at all). With both halves every removal path (residual Vec drop AND pop-consume) decs exactly once against the retained +1: all four shapes (fresh/field push x residual/drain) + bind-then-match + discard-unmatched pop + the kata #100 paired-stack same-tree solver are UAF- and leak-free under ASAN/LSan (3 new regression tests alongside the sibling's residual-only one). The SEPARATE index- extraction leak (`dst.push(src[0])`) is B-2026-07-12-21, still open. Gates: codegen 2230, memory_sanitizer 634, interpreter 1177, clippy + fmt clean.
tracker: FIXED (744ca1c): Vec.push handler now calls `share_option_shared_field_ref_for_arg` (the FieldAccess sibling of the Identifier retain it already had), inc'ing the field-read inner so the container holds an independent +1 — balancing the Vec per-element drop and the source node's own drop. Manifestation had evolved from the originally-filed LEAK to a use-after-free (an intervening change made the Vec per-element drop release residuals, turning the missing-retain under-count into a double-release). Fresh `push(Some(..))` unaffected (owns its sole +1). Tests: memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_field_read_option_shared_push_no_leak_or_uaf, codegen.rs::test_e2e_field_read_option_shared_push_correct. NOTE: the DIRECT index-push sibling (`dst.push(v[i])`) has a separate pre-existing leak — see B-2026-07-12-21.

title: The auto-parallelizer LOST a `mut ref self` method's mutation (silent wrong answer) when the call was nested in an f-string interpolation (`println(f"{b.push_len(x)}")`) or a match scrutinee (`match b.take()`). The concurrency dependency scan `collect_expr_inner_writes` (src/concurrency.rs) did not recurse into `InterpolatedStringLit`, the `Match`/`If`/`While` scrutinee/condition, or most value-expression forms, so the nested `MethodCall` never reached the receiver-write logic. The consecutive statements were then classed INDEPENDENT and parallelized via `karac_par_run`, capturing the receiver BY VALUE into the par env — so each push/pop landed in a discarded copy and was lost. interp (and `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0`) correct; JIT+native wrong and identical. Sibling of B-2026-07-09-12 (auto-par of `mut ref self` calls).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a generic Stack[T] / Box push-pop): interp!=build on a mut-ref-self mutating method called inside an f-string / match. — SURFACE NOTE: concurrency+codegen — REPRO (interp `1 2 final 2`; build/JIT `1 1 final 0`): `struct Box { items: Vec[i64] }  impl Box { fn push_len(mut ref self, x: i64) -> i64 { self.items.push(x); self.items.len() } }  fn main() { let mut b = Box { items: Vec.new() }; println(f"{b.push_len(10)}"); println(f"{b.push_len(20)}"); println(f"final {b.items.len()}"); }`. Also the match-scrutinee shape (`fn take(mut ref self)->Option[i64]{self.items.pop()}  ...  match b.take() { Some(n)=>.., None=>.. }; b.items.len()`). DISCRIMINATORS: a `let a = b.push_len(10)` binding, a discarded statement `b.push_len(10);`, and a `.to_string()` chain arg (`println(b.push_len(10).to_string())`) ALL work — only the f-string-interpolation and match-scrutinee sub-expression positions lose the mutation. A SCALAR-field mutation (`self.n = self.n + 1`, a field-Assign in the method body) via f-string also happens to survive here, but the fix is uniform (it does not depend on field kind). IR EVIDENCE: `Box.push_len`'s body correctly mutates `*self` in place and returns the reloaded len; `@main` passes the real `ptr %b` to both calls with no reset — the HARNESS IR (auto-par OFF) is entirely correct. The REAL build/JIT IR (captured from the JIT temp .ll) has `%__par_branches`/`%__par_env` and `%b1 = load {...}, ptr %b` — the three println statements are dispatched as parallel branches, each capturing `b` BY VALUE, so the pushes mutate discarded copies. `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` makes every case correct — the decisive confirmation.
resolution: FIXED (0ec5b8d). The auto-par data-dependency scan `collect_expr_inner_writes` (src/concurrency.rs) records a WRITE on a method call's receiver root when the method is `mut ref self` / carries a mutating effect (`method_receiver_is_mut_ref` / `method_effects_imply_receiver_mutation`), so two statements that both mutate `b` serialize instead of racing. But the scan only reached the `MethodCall` arm through the block/branch arms — it had NO arm for `InterpolatedStringLit` (fell to `_ => {}`), SKIPPED the `Match` scrutinee (walked arms only) and the `If`/`While` CONDITION and `IfLet`/`WhileLet` scrutinee, and dropped every other value-expression form (`Binary`/`Unary`/`Index`/`FieldAccess`/`Cast`/`Question`/`Range`/`Tuple`/`ArrayLiteral`/`MapLiteral`/`StructLiteral`/`Pipe`/`OptionalChain`/`Return`/`Break`/`Lock`/`Try`, …). So a mutating call nested in any of those positions recorded no receiver write, the statements were classed independent, and the auto-parallelizer raced them with the receiver captured by value into the par env — the mutation lost. FIX: broaden the recursion so the scan descends into f-strings, the match/if/while scrutinee+condition, and every value-expression form, reaching the `MethodCall`/`Call` arms wherever a mutating call hides. SAFE by construction: a write is recorded ONLY for a genuinely mutating call (the receiver-mode / mut-arg gates are unchanged), so broader recursion only ever ADDS serialization — it can never introduce a race, and a purely-read-only nested call still parallelizes. VERIFIED: interp(oracle)==JIT==native across the f-string push-loop, the match-scrutinee pop, and a generic Stack drain; full par_codegen (158) + codegen (2204) + memory_sanitizer (625, LSan) + non-codegen (7807) suites green; fmt+clippy clean. NOTE why it escaped: the plain codegen E2E harness (`tests/codegen.rs::run_program`) compiles with the concurrency analysis = None, so NO E2E test ever ran auto-par; the new guard uses `tests/par_codegen.rs::run_program` which threads `Some(&analysis)`.

tracker: minimal repros below; loud (a type error, not a silent miscompile). Discriminators isolate it to a generic METHOD + `.pop()` on a `Vec[T]` FIELD whose result flows into an inferring (non-checking) position.
title: Inside a GENERIC method (`impl[T] Box[T]`), the result of `self.items.pop()` on a `Vec[T]` FIELD is INFERRED as `Option[Option[T]]` (one extra Option layer) when it flows into a position without a pushed expected type — a `Some(...)` argument or an un-annotated `let`. The DIRECT tail return (`fn take(mut ref self)->Option[T]{self.items.pop()}`) checks fine (the expected `Option[T]` unifies it away), and the non-generic `Vec[i64]` sibling infers `Option[i64]` correctly. So `fn pop(mut ref self)->Option[T]{ if .. {return None;} Some(self.items.pop()) }` is rejected: `expected Option<T>, found Option<Option<Option<T>>>` (the double-nested infer + the outer Some = triple).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a generic Stack[T] over String): `Some(self.items.pop())` in a generic method reported a type with an EXTRA Option layer. — REPRO A (rejected): `struct Box[T]{items:Vec[T]}  impl[T] Box[T]{ fn dbl(mut ref self)->Option[Option[T]]{ Some(self.items.pop()) } }` -> `expected 'Option<Option<T>>', found 'Option<Option<Option<T>>>'`. REPRO B (also rejected, so binding-through-a-local does not help): `fn dbl(mut ref self)->Option[Option[T]]{ let x = self.items.pop(); Some(x) }` -> same triple. DISCRIMINATORS (all CLEAN): (1) direct return `fn take(mut ref self)->Option[T]{ self.items.pop() }`; (2) non-generic `impl Box{ fn dbl(mut ref self)->Option[Option[i64]]{ Some(self.items.pop()) } }`; (3) generic FREE fn `fn take[T](v: mut ref Vec[T])->Option[T]{v.pop()}` + `fn wrap[T](v: mut ref Vec[T])->Option[Option[T]]{Some(take(v))}`; (4) generic method with a `Vec[T]` PARAM (not self.items): `fn f(ref self, v: mut ref Vec[T])->Option[Option[T]]{Some(v.pop())}`; (5) `Some(o)` where `o: Option[T]` is a param; (6) annotated `let x: Option[T] = self.items.pop();`. So the bug requires: GENERIC method + `.pop()` on a `self.<field>` `Vec[T]` (a generic-struct field access) + result in an INFERRING position (Some-arg / un-annotated let). LIKELY AREA: `infer_method_call` for a `Vec[T]`-returning-`Option[T]` builtin on a generic-struct FIELD receiver over-applies the element->Option wrap (the field's `Vec[T]` element `T` is itself resolved as `Option[T]` in the inference-only path), where the field-receiver element resolution differs from the param-receiver path (discriminator 4 is clean). Not a codegen/run bug (loud type error); route: fix the field-receiver generic element inference OR make `Some(...)`/let push the checking direction. Filed on discovery; the workaround is to return `self.items.pop()` directly (already `Option[T]`) rather than re-wrapping. INVESTIGATION 2026-07-12: narrowed the extra-Option-layer source. Instrumenting the typechecker's pop arm (expr_method_call.rs ~3076) shows `self.items.pop()` is handled CORRECTLY: obj_ty = `Vec[TypeParam(T)]` and the arm returns `Option[TypeParam(T)]` (single, called exactly once). So the field access + pop return are RIGHT. Further, `fn take(mut ref self)->Option[T]{ let x = self.items.pop(); x }` checks CLEAN — the let-bound local `x` is Option[T] and returns fine. The extra layer is introduced by the `Some(x)` CONSTRUCTOR inference specifically when `x` is the pop-derived local: `Some(self.items.pop())` and `let x=self.items.pop(); Some(x)` both yield the triple, while discriminator (5) `Some(o)` for a PARAM `o: Option[T]` is clean. So the fix locus is NOT the pop/field-access typing (both correct) but the Some/enum-variant constructor's argument-type inference for a locally-bound Option[T] whose element is a generic param inside a generic method — it appears to re-wrap the arg once. Look at how the enum-ctor call path recovers the arg's type (likely re-inferring vs reading the recorded type) under the generic-method env, in an inferring (no pushed-expected) position. FIXED 2026-07-12 (237e27b). Root cause: the GAT-only `substitute_type_params` pass in `check_call_args_with_substitution_full` (exprs.rs ~1199, added for slice-8c to rewrite an `AssocProjection.param` string that `resolve_type_vars` can't touch) ran UNCONDITIONALLY over the ALREADY-resolved return type. In a generic method the enum constructor's own generic param and the enclosing method's are BOTH literally "T", so the solved substitution {"T": Option[T]} was re-applied to the already-correct ret = Option[Option[T]], rewriting the inner T a second time -> Option[Option[Option[T]]] (the triple). Confirmed via a KARAC_DBG_CTOR probe: before_subst = Option[Option[T]] (resolve_type_vars already correct), solutions = {"T": Option[T]}, after = Option[Option[Option[T]]]. The field-vs-param divergence the discriminators flagged is a red herring at the representation level: the self.items field-receiver pop carries the arg as Option[TypeParam("T")] (a real type param, which the name-based substitution matches) while the param-receiver pop carries Option[Named("T")] (which the TypeParam-only substitution skips) — so the param case dodged the double-apply. FIX: gate the substitute_type_params pass on the return type actually containing an AssocProjection (new free fn `type_contains_assoc_projection`). resolve_type_vars already fully resolves plain type params, so over a projection-free type the pass is at best a no-op and at worst this double-application; the projection-param rewrite is the only thing it uniquely provides, so gating on projection-presence preserves GAT behavior exactly (all GAT/projection typecheck tests stay green) while eliminating the over-nesting. Verified interp == JIT == AOT on the realistic early-return + Some(field-pop) Stack[T] shape (got 20 / empty -> outer none). Tests: test_generic_method_some_over_field_pop_no_extra_option_layer (typechecker.rs, 4 shapes incl. the clean direct-return discriminator) + test_e2e_generic_method_some_over_field_pop (codegen.rs, runtime oracle). Gates green: typechecker 2014, codegen 2222, interpreter 1173, clippy/fmt clean.

title: A match ARM inside a very large recursive match-method that declares ~4+ heap-typed (Vec) locals corrupts memory (segfault / double-free / spurious vec-index-out-of-bounds); moving the arm body into its own method is clean
detail: In the self-hosted typechecker's `check_expr` (a large `match` over the `Expr` enum, ~20 arms, recursive via `self.check_expr`), giving ONE arm four or more heap-typed local `Vec` bindings (e.g. `hit`/`extra_off`/`extra_len`/`vbad_off`/`vbad_len`, all `Vec[i64]`) plus the loops that populate and drain them causes runtime memory corruption: 3 such Vecs run clean, a 4th produces `panic: vec index out of bounds` then SIGSEGV, and a 5th produces `free(): double free detected` (repeated per struct-literal evaluated). The logic is correct (verified: the same code emits the right diagnostics in the 3-Vec form). DISCRIMINATORS: (a) the SAME buffer-and-drain logic in a dedicated method `check_struct_lit(mut ref self, n: StructLitExpr) -> ExprInfo` (arm becomes `StructLit(n) => self.check_struct_lit(n)`) is fully CLEAN with all 5 Vecs — this is the applied workaround; (b) a synthetic standalone function with a 40-arm `match` and one arm carrying the same 5 `Vec[i64]` locals + drain loops does NOT reproduce (exits cleanly), so a small function frame is fine; (c) a small standalone function with the buffer/consume/scan pattern (1–3 Vecs) is clean. The trigger is the combination of MANY heap locals in a single arm of an already-large (many-arm, recursive) match method — all arms' allocas share one stack frame, so the aggregate local/drop count in `check_expr` appears to cross a codegen threshold where per-arm Vec drops corrupt. Points at the entry-block alloca aggregation / drop-flag emission for large functions rather than any one construct. NO tiny standalone repro yet (only reproduces at the real `check_expr` scale). WORKAROUND (reliable): extract any Vec-heavy match arm into its own `fn` so it gets a fresh, small frame. FIX AREA: codegen frame/drop-slot handling for very large functions (drop-flag or alloca layout) — needs a scaled repro to bisect. FIXED 2026-07-12 (588265f). ROOT CAUSE (the whole 'many heap locals / large function' framing was INCIDENTAL). Reduced from selfhost check_expr to a 15-line standalone: `shared enum E { A(i64), B(i64) }  fn chk(e: E) -> i64 { match e { A(n) => n, B(m) => { let mut e = 0; let mut i = 0; loop { if i>=3 {break;} e=e+i; i=i+1; } m+e } } }` SEGFAULTS at O2 and O0. Real trigger: a SINGLE same-named local (`let mut e = 0`) SHADOWING the by-value shared-enum/shared-struct scrutinee PARAMETER `e` inside a match arm. Vec count, arm count, recursion all ruled out by build-checked reduction (earlier 'NARM=0 crashes' etc. were stale-binary artifacts from build failures masked by >/dev/null). IR: param slot `%e = alloca ptr`, shadow `%e11 = alloca i64`; the param's scope-exit RC-dec emitted `load ptr, ptr %e11` — it reloaded the pointer BY NAME from variables["e"], which the shadow had repointed at the i64 alloca, so the dec walked an integer-as-pointer. WHY THE WORKAROUND WORKED: check_struct_lit(mut ref self, n: StructLitExpr)'s param is `n`, and its drain counter `let mut e` shadowed nothing named `e` — inlining into check_expr(e: Expr) is what introduced the shadow. FIX (src/codegen/runtime.rs emit_cleanup_action): reload-by-name exists to follow a REASSIGNMENT (keeps the slot pointer-typed); a shadow of a different type does not. Gate the reload on slot.ty.is_pointer_type(), else fall back to the pointer captured at registration (for a never-reassigned param, the incoming object %0). Applied to RcDec, FreeClusterWalk, FreeSharedElided (RcDecOption already keys on its captured option_slot). Verified: minimal + full-inlined-selfhost run clean (exact-correct diagnostics), valgrind 0-errors, both regression tests segfault at O0+O2 pre-fix. Gates: codegen 2235, memory_sanitizer 636, par_codegen 159, selfhost_typechecker 1, non-llvm full, clippy(llvm+no-llvm)+fmt green. Tests: test_e2e_shared_scrutinee_shadowed_by_local_no_corruption (codegen.rs) + asan_shared_scrutinee_shadowed_by_local (memory_sanitizer.rs), non-recursive to isolate from the recursive-payload leak B-2026-07-12-25.
source: selfhost:typechecker.kara check_expr StructLit arm (Phase 12 TypeChecker Slice 11 — MissingField)
resolution: 588265f — pointer-type gate on RC-cleanup reload-by-name; a non-pointer same-named shadow no longer redirects a shared binding's scope-exit drop. [renumbered from B-2026-07-12-6 on 2026-07-17: duplicate id from a concurrent-session collision]

tracker: raw-pointer construction (`ptr.const`/`ptr.mut`) does not mark the rooted binding as address-taken → mem2reg promotion → stale read-through-pointer for ref-param roots
title: A volatile read through `ptr.const(param.field)` does NOT observe a prior volatile write through `ptr.mut(param.field)` to the SAME field, IN THE SAME FUNCTION, when `param` is a `mut ref` struct parameter (reads the stale pre-write value); the write DOES reach real memory (a caller reading the same field back through a raw pointer sees it). The identical shape on an OWNED LOCAL is correct. Root: `ptr.const`/`ptr.mut` emit no address-taken/escape marker on the rooted binding, so mem2reg promotes a `ref`-param's local slot and the two independent pointer derivations read a promoted (stale) SSA value instead of shared memory.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Dogfooding while extending `ptr.const`/`ptr.mut` codegen to field/index/tuple place shapes (phase-11 embedded MMIO slice): verifying the new field-place addresses against a `mut ref` struct parameter surfaced a stale intra-function read-back. — The place-shape address computation itself is CORRECT — verified by address deltas (`ptr.mut(r.f) - ptr.const(r.f) == 0`; field-1 minus field-0 == 4) and by cross-function visibility: a `volatile_write` through `ptr.mut(param.control)` inside a `fn bump(param: mut ref Reg)` IS observed by the caller reading `volatile_read(ptr.const(r.control))` back in `main` (prints 777). The bug is purely a same-function read-back staleness for a `mut ref` PARAMETER root. MINIMAL REPRO (prints `20` then `20`; both should be `777`): `struct Reg { status: i32, control: i32 } fn bump(r: mut ref Reg) -> i32 { unsafe { let pw: *mut i32 = ptr.mut(r.control); volatile_write(pw, 777); let pr: *const i32 = ptr.const(r.control); volatile_read(pr) } } fn main() { let mut r: Reg = Reg { status: 10, control: 20 }; let inside = bump(mut r); println(inside); unsafe { let pr: *const i32 = ptr.const(r.control); println(volatile_read(pr)); } }`. Note: inserting a `ptr.const(r.control)` in `main` BEFORE the `bump` call (address-taking the caller's binding) makes the caller's post-call read-back return 777 — evidence the root is missing escape/address-taken marking, not a wrong GEP. NOT NEW to the field-place slice: the identifier-root path shows the sibling symptom (a direct `println(cell)` after `volatile_write(ptr.mut(cell), 99)` reads the stale `10`); the field-place extension merely made the ref-param variant reproducible. The OWNED-LOCAL same-function round-trip is correct and IS covered by tests (tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_ptr_const_mut_place_shapes_roundtrip + test_e2e_volatile_read_write_roundtrip). FIX DIRECTION: at `ptr.const`/`ptr.mut` construction (`ptr_place_addr` / `compile_ptr_module_call`), mark the rooted binding's alloca as address-taken / non-promotable so mem2reg cannot lift it, OR attach pointer-aliasing metadata (the `!provenance` refinement already tracked as a follow-up in the strict-provenance ptr comments). Deferred as a separate slice — it is orthogonal to place-shape support and touches the pointer ABI's escape model. INVESTIGATION 2026-07-12: the CODEGEN staleness no longer reproduces on main HEAD — the exact repro now prints the correct `777` then `777` under `karac run` (JIT), deterministic across runs. NOT this session's authorship (verified: none of this session's commits touch the raw-pointer place / alias-metadata path); resolved upstream on 2026-07-12 (candidates: 5b13343 `ptr.const/ptr.mut over field/tuple-index/Vec-index places`, or the volatile-intrinsic maturation). Owner: please confirm and close. SEPARATE ISSUE surfaced while verifying: the INTERPRETER (`KARAC_RUN_JIT=0`) cannot run this program at all — it panics with an `internal error: entered unreachable code: variable 'ptr' not found` at eval_expr.rs:129 because it has no dispatch for the `ptr.mut`/`ptr.const`/`volatile_read`/`volatile_write` MMIO intrinsics (codegen-only today). A compiler should emit a clean 'unsupported in interpreter' diagnostic, not an unreachable!-panic; worth a dedicated low-sev ledger entry for the interpreter MMIO-intrinsic gap. FIXED 2026-07-12 (4a72185). The prior INVESTIGATION note's 'resolved upstream' conclusion was WRONG — it only tested the JIT (`karac run`, -O0). Testing AOT (`karac build`, optimized) showed the bug STILL LIVE: AOT printed the stale 20/20 while JIT printed 777/777. ROOT CAUSE (the original escape/mem2reg-promotion diagnosis was a RED HERRING — the address computation is correct): `compile_volatile_write` stored the value with its OWN LLVM type, not `dst`'s POINTEE type. An integer literal defaults to i64 in codegen, so `volatile_write(pw /* *mut i32 */, 777)` emitted `store volatile i64 777` through an i32* — an 8-byte store into a 4-byte field. Unoptimized IR (confirmed via an ir_for probe): `store volatile i64 777, ptr %pw1` paired with `load volatile i32, ptr %pr4`. Two problems: (a) it wrote 4 bytes past the field, and (b) the width mismatch between the i64 store and the i32 load defeated the optimizer's volatile load-forwarding under -O, so the same-function read-back value-numbered back to the pre-write value 20. JIT at -O0 doesn't run that pass, so it happened to read the correct low 4 bytes (777) — hence the JIT-vs-AOT split. FIX: coerce the written value to `dst`'s recorded pointee type (the same `raw_pointer_pointee_types` table `compile_volatile_read` consults, keyed on the dst expr span) via the existing `coerce_scalar_to_type` before the volatile store, so the store width matches the pointee and the paired load, and the two accesses forward correctly. Now interp / JIT / AOT all agree (777/777); the owned-local and place-shape volatile round-trip tests are unaffected. SECOND HALF (the interpreter MMIO-intrinsic gap the note flagged): the tree-walk interpreter has no raw-pointer model, so `ptr.const`/`ptr.mut`/`volatile_read`/`volatile_write` panicked with `unreachable!("variable 'ptr' not found")` (the `ptr` receiver eval'd as an unbound identifier). Now they record a clean structured runtime error naming the intrinsic + the `karac build` / non-`--interp` workaround (intercepted in eval_method_call for `ptr.*` and eval_call for `volatile_*`, both guarded on the name not being a user binding). Tests: test_e2e_volatile_write_narrow_field_ref_param_roundtrip (codegen.rs — exercises the AOT path via run_program, both in-function and cross-function read-back) + test_mmio_intrinsics_clean_error_not_panic (interpreter.rs). Gates green: codegen 2228, interpreter 1175, memory_sanitizer 631, clippy + fmt clean.

tracker: minimal repros below; LOUD (module verification failure -> codegen refuses and prints the `--interp` fallback hint, not a silent miscompile). Interp is correct. Inverse-sibling of B-2026-07-11-28 (a VOID generic mono emitting `ret i64 0`); this is a NON-void generic mono emitting `ret void`.
title: A GENERIC (monomorphized) function `fn f[T](..) -> i64` whose body TAIL is a bare `loop { .. return <value>; .. }` (a divergent loop that exits only via `return`/`break`) fails module verification under `karac build`/JIT: `Function return type does not match operand type of return inst!  ret void   i64` — the mono emits a spurious `ret void` at the loop's unreachable fall-through in an i64-returning function. The NON-generic sibling compiles fine, and a generic `while`+tail-return or `loop{..break;}`+tail-return also compiles fine. interp runs correctly (falls back via the printed hint).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a generic `total_area[T: Shape](xs: ref Vec[T])` summing via `loop { if i>=len { return t; } .. }`): interp runs, `karac build`/JIT fails to compile. — MINIMAL REPRO (interp prints `5`; JIT/native fail codegen): `fn f[T](x: T) -> i64 { loop { return 5; } }  fn main() { println(f"{f(99)}"); }`. Also `fn f[T](x: T) -> i64 { let mut i = 0; loop { if i >= 3 { return i; } i = i + 1; } }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (1) NON-generic `fn f(x: i64) -> i64 { loop { return 5; } }` compiles + runs (CLEAN) — generic-only. (2) generic `while i < n { .. }` + tail return compiles (CLEAN) — the bare `loop {}` tail is the trigger. (3) generic `loop { if c { break; } .. } ; i` (break + a real tail expression after the loop) compiles (CLEAN) — the loop must be the DIVERGENT tail whose only exits are `return`. The `loop {}` expression's type is `Never`; `compile_mono_function`'s tail handling (src/codegen/mono.rs, the terminator/`ret` emission after `compile_block`) appends a fall-through `ret void` for the unreachable post-loop path instead of leaving the block terminated (or emitting the function's declared i64 return / `unreachable`). The non-generic `compile_function` path handles this (its terminator check sees the loop already left a terminator, or coerces). Same region as B-2026-07-11-28 (mono void-return handling), OPPOSITE symptom: there a void mono emitted `ret i64 0`; here an i64 mono emits `ret void`. Not a run bug (loud verifier reject); route: in the mono tail-return emission, skip the fall-through `ret` when the body's tail is a divergent (`Never`) `loop` that already terminated every reachable path, or emit `unreachable` for the dead fall-through instead of `ret void`.

tracker: minimal repros below. SILENT miscompile (interp != build, no error). Interp is correct (tests/interpreter.rs::test_match_with_guard covers guards on the interp side). No codegen/par E2E test appears to exercise a match-arm guard — the same untested-harness gap that hid B-2026-07-12-5.
title: A `match` ARM GUARD (`p if cond => ..`) is SILENTLY IGNORED under codegen (`karac build` / JIT): the arm fires whenever its PATTERN matches, regardless of the `if` condition. So the first arm whose pattern matches wins even when its guard is false. Affects BOTH an irrefutable binding pattern (`x if x < 0`) and a refutable enum pattern (`Some(x) if x > 5`). interp is correct; JIT+native produce identical wrong output. Extremely common construct.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a `classify(n)` with `match n { 0 => .., x if x < 0 => .., x if x < 10 => .., _ => .. }`): interp correct, build/JIT wrong. — MINIMAL REPRO (interp `111 222`; JIT/native `111 111`): `fn f(n: i64) -> i64 { match n { x if x < 0 => 111, _ => 222 } }  fn main() { println(f"{f(-5)}"); println(f"{f(5)}"); }` — codegen returns 111 for `f(5)` (the `x if x < 0` arm fires with x=5 although 5<0 is false). MORE (interp `zero neg small big`; JIT/native `zero neg neg neg`): `fn classify(n:i64)->String{ match n { 0 => f"zero", x if x < 0 => f"neg", x if x < 10 => f"small", _ => f"big" } }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) scalar binding guards `match n { x if x>100 => 1, x if x>10 => 2, x => x }` -> codegen `1 1 1` for 500/50/5 (should be `1 2 5`); (b) ENUM-pattern guard `match o { Some(x) if x>5 => 1, Some(_) => 2, None => 3 }` -> codegen `1 1 3` for Some(10)/Some(2)/None (should be `1 2 3`) — so the guard is ignored for enum patterns too, not just irrefutable bindings. The guard EXPRESSION is not evaluated at all (the arm's pattern-match result alone decides the arm). `src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs` does reference `arm.guard` in some lowering paths (e.g. the Map.get-scrutinee / fresh-temp / enum-drop arms), so the general scalar/enum match-lowering path either never emits the guard test or emits it but does not branch to the next arm on guard-false. SEVERITY high: silent wrong answer for a fundamental, ubiquitous construct. LIKELY long-standing (no codegen E2E covers a guard); please confirm regression-vs-longstanding. FIX AREA: the general match-arm lowering must, after a successful pattern-match, evaluate `arm.guard` and fall through to the next arm's test when it is false (Rust semantics), binding the pattern vars first so the guard can reference them.

tracker: minimal repros below. LOUD (typecheck error `?T0`), consistent interp==build, workaround = annotate the param. Common ergonomic pattern.
title: FIXED (3fd34c0) for the arithmetic-body case. A `let`-bound closure with an UN-annotated numeric param and no pushed `Fn(..)` type left the param an unsolved inference var (`?T0`): `let f = |x| x + 1; f(5)` was rejected (`arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found '?T0'` in the body + `expected '?T0', found 'i64'` at the call). The push side (`xs.iter().map(|x| x + 1)`) and an explicit `|x: i64|` already worked. FIX (two sites, via the existing `substitutions` substrate): (1) `infer_binary` (expr_ops.rs) — for an arithmetic op, when exactly one operand resolves to an unsolved var and the other to a concrete numeric type, solve the var to it and re-resolve; (2) closure inference (exprs.rs) — resolve the param types through the substitutions after the body so the closure types as `Fn(i64) -> i64`, not `?T0` (the call then checks cleanly and codegen gets the right width). Solving in the body suffices; no separate call-site machinery. Verified interp == JIT == AOT for int + float bodies; annotated / push-side cases unchanged. RESIDUAL (still open, rarer): a param with NO body constraint (`let id = |x| x; id(5)`) needs CALL-SITE-driven solving (collect the arg type and propagate to the param + return) — still rejected, unchanged. Tests: `tests/typechecker.rs::test_closure_pullside_param_inferred_from_arith_body`, `tests/interpreter.rs::test_closure_unannotated_param_inferred_from_arith_body`, `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_closure_unannotated_param_inferred_from_body`.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a `let add_base = |x| x + base;` helper): the closure param type is not inferred. — REPRO (rejected): `fn main() { let f = |x| x + 1; println(f"{f(5)}"); }` -> `error[typecheck]: arithmetic operator requires numeric type, found '?T0'` and `expected '?T0', found 'i64'` at `f(5)`. DISCRIMINATORS (CLEAN): (1) `let f = |x: i64| x + 1; f(5)` (annotated param); (2) `xs.iter().map(|x| x + 100)` where the map element type PUSHES `i64` into the closure param. So the gap is purely pull-side inference for a let-bound closure: neither the body operator (`x + 1`, both operands i64 by the literal) nor the later monomorphic call site `f(5)` is used to solve the param metavar. LIKELY AREA: closure type inference records the param as a fresh metavar and only unifies it against a PUSHED expected `Fn(..)` type; it does not (a) propagate the body's operator-operand constraint back to the param, nor (b) collect call-site arg types to solve it. Not a codegen/run bug (loud); workaround is to annotate. Low severity but a very natural pattern (`let f = |x| x * 2`).

tracker: minimal repros below. `karac check` PASSES (false confidence) but BOTH `karac run` (interp) AND `karac build` (codegen) fail loudly. Distinct from the closure-param-inference gap B-2026-07-12-10 (which is an un-annotated-closure typecheck error) — here even a fn-reference or an annotated closure gets past typecheck and then has no runtime.
title: `Option[T].map(f)` and `Result[T,E].map(f)` TYPECHECK CLEAN (`karac check` -> `All checks passed`) but are UNIMPLEMENTED in BOTH the interpreter AND codegen: `karac run` dies with `method 'map' not found on type 'Option'/'Result' (no interpreter dispatch arm)` and `karac build` dies with `no handler for method 'map' ... (method dispatch fell through)`. Affects a fn-reference (`a.map(dbl)`) AND a closure (`a.map(|x: i64| x*2)`). design.md (`self.fetch_profile(user.id).map(Response.ok)`) documents `.map` on Result as intended, so this is typecheck-ahead-of-runtime, not a should-reject.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (Option/Result combinators: `a.map(|x| x*2).unwrap_or(-1)`). — REPRO (check passes; run+build fail): `fn dbl(n:i64)->i64{n*2}  fn main(){ let a: Option[i64] = Some(5); let m = a.map(dbl); println(f"{m.unwrap_or(-1)}"); }`. `karac check` -> `All checks passed`; `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0 karac run` -> `runtime error: method 'map' not found on type 'Option' (no interpreter dispatch arm)`; `karac build`/JIT -> `codegen: no handler for method 'map' on variable 'a'`. Same for `Result[i64,String].map(dbl)` and for a closure arg `a.map(|x: i64| x*2)`. The typechecker has an explicit Option/Result `map` arm (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs ~2322, a `map_receiver` for Option/Result), so it types the call and the chained `.unwrap_or`, but neither runtime has a dispatch arm (interpreter: no Option/Result `map` in method_call_optres.rs; codegen: no arm in `compile_method_call` for Option/Result `map`). SECONDARY: an UN-ANNOTATED closure `a.map(|x| x*2)` additionally fails typecheck with `?T1` (Option.map does not PUSH the element type into the closure param, unlike `Vec.iter().map(|x| ..)` which does — a facet of the closure-param-inference gap B-2026-07-12-10). SEVERITY medium: a very common combinator; `karac check` passing gives false confidence, though run/build fail loudly. FIX: implement Option/Result `.map` (and likely siblings `and_then`/`map_err`/`filter`/`ok_or`) in the interpreter (method_call_optres.rs) and codegen (compile_method_call Option/Result arm), OR gate the typechecker to reject until implemented; ALSO push the element type into the map closure's param. FIXED 2026-07-12 (ba52795): implemented `Option[T].map(f)` / `Result[T,E].map(f)` across all three surfaces — typechecker (dedicated Option/Result map arm: infers the mapper's Fn(T)->R, records source T, returns Option[R]/Result[R,E]), interpreter (apply f to Some/Ok payload, pass None/Err through), and codegen (branch on tag → reconstruct payload → synthesize Some(f(x))/Ok(f(x)) reusing call+ctor codegen → phi with passed-through receiver). Verified interp==JIT==AOT on Option/Result present+absent, a type-changing map (i64->bool), and a chain; valgrind-clean. SCOPE / follow-ons (each deferred LOUDLY, never silent-wrong): (1) fn-reference and ANNOTATED-closure mappers only — an un-annotated `|x| ..` still needs the closure-param-pull inference tracked as B-2026-07-12-10 (the SECONDARY note here); (2) a HEAP payload T (String/Vec/struct) defers to the interpreter under codegen (the reconstructed payload aliases the receiver's buffer — ownership care is a follow-on); (3) `map_err`/`and_then`/`filter`/`ok_or` are natural siblings not yet implemented (fall through loudly in both backends).

tracker: minimal repro below. LOUD (module verification failure -> codegen refuses, prints the `--interp` fallback hint). Interp is correct. A top-level fn returning a closure WITH an explicit `-> Fn(..)` annotation compiles fine — the gap is the un-annotated let-bound outer closure.
title: A CLOSURE whose body is itself a CLOSURE (a closure returning a closure / currying) fails codegen: the OUTER closure's return type is lowered as the default `i64` instead of a closure fat pointer `{ptr, ptr}`, so the mono emits `ret { ptr, ptr } %closure_env` in an `i64`-returning LLVM fn -> `Function return type does not match operand type of return inst!`. interp runs correctly; `karac build`/JIT fails.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a curried adder: `let make_adder = |n| |x| x + n;`). — MINIMAL REPRO (interp prints `15`; JIT/native fail codegen): `fn main() { let make = |n: i64| |x: i64| x + n; let add5 = make(5); println(f"{add5(10)}"); }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (1) a TOP-LEVEL fn returning a closure WITH the return annotation compiles + runs CLEAN: `fn make_adder(n: i64) -> Fn(i64) -> i64 { |x: i64| x + n }` — the explicit `-> Fn(i64)->i64` gives codegen the right return type. (2) a closure with a BLOCK body returning a SCALAR compiles CLEAN (`let f = |n: i64| { n + 1 }`). So the trigger is a let-bound closure whose RETURN VALUE is another closure, with no return-type annotation to override the `i64` default. The typechecker + interpreter both handle it (interp runs; typecheck passes), so codegen's closure lowering is the gap: it must infer/thread the outer closure's return type as the inner closure's `Fn(..)` fat-pointer type (or read it from the typechecker's recorded closure type) instead of defaulting the closure body's return to `i64`. Not a run bug (loud verifier reject). Related family: closures / `Fn(..)`-typed values (B-2026-06-20-1, B-2026-06-21-3). Workaround: hoist to a top-level fn with an explicit `-> Fn(..)` return type. INVESTIGATION 2026-07-12: the return-type inference is necessary but NOT sufficient. Adding a `ExprKind::Closure => closure_value_type()` arm to `infer_closure_return_type` makes the single-use case (`let add5 = make(5); add5(10)`) compile and print 15 correctly, BUT the multi-instance case silently corrupts: `let add5 = make(5); let add10 = make(10); add5(10)` prints 20 (add10's value) and add10(10) prints garbage. The inner closure's env captures the outer closure's param `n` on the OUTER closure's STACK frame; when the outer returns the inner closure the env escapes and must be HEAP-allocated per instance (like the working top-level `fn make_adder(n) -> Fn(..) { |x| x+n }`, which the `fns_returning_heap_env` machinery handles by fn NAME). A nested/anonymous closure returning a closure is not registered there, so both instances alias one reused stack env. Landing the return-type arm ALONE turns the loud verifier-reject into a SILENT WRONG ANSWER — do NOT land it without the escaping-inner-closure heap-env allocation. Requires extending the heap-env escape analysis to anonymous closures whose body is a closure. FIXED 2026-07-12 (d29b6b0). Confirmed the prior investigation's finding: a return-type-only fix is INSUFFICIENT and turns the loud verifier-reject into a silent WRONG ANSWER (multi-instance `make(5)`/`make(10)` aliased one reused stack env). The complete fix is THREE coordinated parts. (1) infer_closure_return_type: a closure body that is itself a closure evaluates to the closure fat-pointer type (`closure_value_type`), not the i64 default — this alone cured the LLVM verifier reject. (2) compile_closure: a new `closure_tail_heap_closure_span` (the currying sibling of `func_tail_heap_closure_span`) detects a closure whose tail is a CAPTURING closure literal and marks that inner span heap-env for the body compile, so the nested compile_closure gives the inner env a per-call RC heap box instead of a stack alloca — each `make(n)` instance owns a distinct env, killing the aliasing. (3) The KEY soundness insight: a curry closure-VALUE binding whose call returns a heap env is semantically identical to a NAMED `fns_returning_heap_env` fn, so it is routed through the SAME central `is_heap_env_producing_call` predicate (new per-function `curry_closure_vars` set, populated by `compute_curry_closure_vars` before the misuse guard runs). This reuses the ENTIRE existing heap-env machinery unchanged — `let f = make(..)` gets a FreeClosureEnv cleanup; a `Vec[Fn]` storing curry closures frees each element's env on drop (the Vec-owner slice); struct / tuple / array owners work; and any not-yet-supported escape (store in a collection LITERAL, capture, unbound `make(..)`) is loudly rejected with E_ESCAPING_CLOSURE_NOT_YET + `--interp` hint, exactly matching the named-fn epic's behavior (verified: the named-fn `let fs = [make_adder(5)]` rejects identically). SUPPORTED shapes (interp == JIT == AOT, leak-free): single- and multi-instance currying, 3-level currying (`|a| |b| |c| a+b+c`), non-capturing inner, float payloads, and Vec.new()+push storage. Leak-freedom proven under valgrind AND LeakSanitizer (the Vec-store case leaked 16 KB / 1000 boxes pre-fix, 0 post-fix). REMAINING (separate slices, all loudly gated today, none silent): storing a curry closure in a COLLECTION LITERAL / struct field, capturing it in another closure, or an unbound direct-chain call `make(5)(10)` — the same escape shapes the named-fn heap-env epic (B-2026-06-22-2) still gates; a heap-CAPTURE inner (String/Vec) is also gated (Slice-2 territory). Tests: test_e2e_closure_returning_closure_currying (codegen.rs, single + multi-instance) + asan_curry_closure_vec_store_no_leak (memory_sanitizer.rs, LSan leak-lock). Gates green: codegen 2223, interpreter 1173, memory_sanitizer 628, par_codegen 159, clippy + fmt clean.

tracker: minimal repros below. SILENT miscompile (interp != build, no error). Interp is correct. Sibling of B-2026-07-12-9 (match arm GUARDS ignored) in the 'non-enum-scrutinee match arm selection broken under codegen' family — same untested-harness gap (no codegen E2E covers a tuple-pattern match).
title: A `match` on a TUPLE scrutinee is not DISCRIMINATED under codegen (`karac build` / JIT): the FIRST tuple-pattern arm always fires regardless of the tuple's element values, so every input maps to the first arm. `match (a, b) { (0,0) => 10, (1,2) => 20, _ => 30 }` returns 10 for ALL inputs. Affects i64 tuples AND bool tuples. interp is correct; JIT+native produce identical wrong output. A match on a SINGLE scalar/bool scrutinee works (scalar literal patterns ARE tested) — only the tuple-element tests are missing.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood (a `quadrant(x,y)` via `match (x>0, y>0) { (true,true)=>1, .. }`). — MINIMAL REPRO (interp `10 20 30`; JIT/native `10 10 10`): `fn f(a:i64,b:i64)->i64{ match (a,b) { (0,0)=>10, (1,2)=>20, _=>30 } }  fn main(){ println(f"{f(0,0)}"); println(f"{f(1,2)}"); println(f"{f(5,5)}"); }`. ALSO bool tuples (interp `1 0 0`; JIT `1 1 1`): `match (a,b) { (true,true)=>1, _=>0 }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) a SINGLE-scalar match `match x>0 { true=>1, false=>2 }` is CLEAN (returns 1/2 correctly) — bool/scalar literal patterns ARE tested; (b) the tuple case fails for BOTH i64 and bool element types. So codegen's tuple-pattern lowering binds/destructures the tuple but does NOT emit the per-element equality tests that select the arm — every tuple 'matches' the first tuple pattern. Sibling of B-2026-07-12-9 (guards silently ignored): both are the general non-enum-scrutinee match-arm-selection path emitting no discriminating test (guard test there, element test here). SEVERITY high: silent wrong answer for a common construct (`match (a, b) { .. }`). No codegen E2E covers a tuple-pattern match (the same harness gap that hid B-2026-07-12-5/-9). FIX AREA: the match lowering for a tuple scrutinee must emit the per-element pattern tests (recursively) and branch to the next arm on any element mismatch, before running the arm body.

title: Explicit `e.to_string()` on a `#[derive(Display)]` enum with PAYLOAD variants (e.g. `Other(String)`) was rejected by the typechecker (an all-unit-only `enum_display` gate) and had NO codegen handler, even though `f"{e}"` interpolation and `println(e)` of the same value already rendered it (`Other(disk full)`) across all backends. The all-unit restriction was stale — it waited on a payload-enum Display renderer that has since landed. FIXED: (1) typechecker `expr_method_call.rs` drops the `.all(Unit)` condition so `.to_string()` types as `String` for payload-carrying derive(Display) enums (`#[display_snake_case]` stays excluded pending its own renderer follow-on); (2) codegen `method_call.rs` adds a payload-enum arm to the `to_string` dispatch rendering via `render_user_enum_display` (the value-driven Display fn f-strings use), returning an owning String — all-unit enums keep the dedicated select-chain, only payload enums reach the new arm. Verified interp == JIT == AOT for identifier + struct-field receivers incl. a heap String payload; valgrind-clean. Tests: `tests/typechecker.rs::test_to_string_on_payload_display_enum_typecheck`, `tests/interpreter.rs::test_enum_display_payload_variants_to_string`, `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_payload_enum_to_string`.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: self-review while dogfooding a `trait Error { message() }` (the `impl Error { message() { self.to_string() } }` pattern)

title: A bare `self.to_string()` / `f"{self}"` inside an impl method failed under codegen (`no handler for method 'to_string' on non-identifier receiver`) while the interpreter rendered it — a build==run divergence blocking `impl Error { fn message(ref self) -> String { self.to_string() } }`. `self` is `ExprKind::SelfValue`, unmatched by the Display name-lookup helpers. FIXED for ENUM receivers (71929b2): `expr_user_enum_name` / `_any` now recognise `SelfValue`, resolving it to the impl's Self type via `var_type_names["self"]`; the `SelfValue` place expr is passed to the render helpers UNCHANGED (ref-aware), NOT normalised to `Identifier("self")` (which double-freed / misread the ref). All-unit + payload enum receivers render identically interp/JIT/AOT; 200-iter loop valgrind+ASAN/LSan clean when the rendered String is consumed directly. STRUCT receivers are split out to B-2026-07-12-17 (a struct `self.to_string()` hits a pre-existing return-position double-free in `compile_struct_display_string`) and stay excluded so the clean error is preserved rather than turned into a double-free. Tests: `tests/interpreter.rs::test_enum_self_to_string_in_impl_method`, `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_enum_self_to_string`, `tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_enum_self_to_string_no_leak`. UPDATE (7c8d383): the STRUCT receiver is now also handled — the return-position double-free B-2026-07-12-17 that forced the struct exclusion is fixed, so codegen recognises a `SelfValue` struct receiver too. Display `self.to_string()` / `f"{self}"` now works for BOTH structs and enums.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: self-review while fixing B-2026-07-12-14 (payload-enum to_string) — Repro: `#[derive(Display)] struct Point { x: i64, y: i64 } impl Point { fn d(ref self) -> String { self.to_string() } }` — typechecks, `karac run` prints `Point { x: 3, y: 4 }`, `karac build`/JIT errors `no handler for method 'to_string' on non-identifier receiver`. Same for an all-unit enum and (post-B-2026-07-12-14) a payload enum. The IDENTIFIER and FIELD-ACCESS receiver forms (`a.to_string()`, `w.e.to_string()`) all work across backends — only the `SelfValue` receiver is unhandled.

tracker: generic methods with by-value narrow-int (i8/i16/i32/u8/u16/u32) params: (1) call-site arg not coerced to the mono's param type -> LLVM verifier hard-fail; (2) generic struct field LAYOUT shared last-writer-wins across instantiations -> silent field mis-sizing
title: Two coupled generic-monomorphization codegen bugs, both blocking `VolatileCell[i32]`. (1) A generic method with a by-value narrow-int type param (`fn set[T](v: T)` mono'd at `T=i32`) declares an `i32` param, but the call site passes the argument as i64 (Kara's narrow-ints-live-in-i64-slots convention), so LLVM module verification HARD-FAILS ("Call parameter type does not match function signature") even for a SINGLE instantiation. (2) A generic struct instantiated at MULTIPLE types shares ONE LLVM field layout via the last-writer-wins `struct_types[base_name]` entry, so a later instantiation's field access reads the wrong width -> silent truncation/garbage; backend-DIVERGENT (interpreter can be correct while build is wrong).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Dogfooding the `VolatileCell[T]` stdlib wrapper (phase-11 embedded MMIO follow-on): `VolatileCell[i32]` exercised generic-method narrow-int codegen and surfaced two coupled monomorphization gaps. — GAP 1 (arg coercion). REPRO (loud-fails at build; interpreter correct): `struct Box[T] { v: T } impl[T] Box[T] { fn new(v: T) -> Box[T] { Box { v: v } } fn set(mut ref self, v: T) { self.v = v; } fn get(ref self) -> T { self.v } } fn main() { let mut b: Box[i32] = Box.new(1); b.set(77); println(b.get()); }` -> `error: codegen failed: Module verification failed: "Call parameter type does not match function signature! i64 77 ... call void @\"Box.set$i32\"(ptr %b, i64 77)"`. The non-generic direct-call path narrows via `coerce_args_to_fn_params` (call_dispatch.rs / method_call.rs user-method arm); the generic mono-call path (`compile_generic_call`, mono.rs ~line 1057-1089) pushes by-value scalar args verbatim with NO coercion. A one-line fix (`self.coerce_args_to_fn_params(func, &mut compiled_args)` before the `build_call`) makes GAP 1 correct for single instantiation (verified: `Box[i16/i32/i64]` alone round-trip correctly on BOTH backends, and `VolatileCell[i32]` then gives 7/42/100). BUT that fix UN-MASKS gap 2 (converts its loud verifier-fail into a silent wrong answer / build-interp divergence), so it must NOT land alone. GAP 2 (struct field-layout collision). The mono mangling IS correct and distinct (`Box.set$i16` vs `Box.set$i32`, confirmed by instrumenting `mangle_mono_name` -> `subst_names` carries i16/i32 correctly). The truncation is in the FIELD store/read: `struct_types["Box"]` is a single shared entry (declarations.rs:535) overwritten per mono, so the mono body's `self.v = v` / `self.v` read resolves the WRONG instantiation's layout. Per-instantiation layouts already exist (`mono_struct_type`, types_lowering.rs:96, B-2026-07-03-23) but the in-mono field access does not consult them for a generic-param-instantiated `self`. REPRO A (both backends wrong -> parity, narrow+narrow): `... let mut a: Box[i16] = Box.new(1); let mut b: Box[i32] = Box.new(1); a.set(30000); b.set(2000000000); println(a.get()); println(b.get());` -> BUILD and INTERP both print `30000` then `37888` (= 2000000000 & 0xFFFF: `b`'s i32 field laid out as i16). REPRO B (DIVERGENT, wide+narrow): `... let mut a: Box[i64]=Box.new(1); let mut b: Box[bool]=Box.new(false); a.set(9000000000); b.set(true); println(a.get()); println(b.get());` -> interp prints `9000000000` then `true` (CORRECT); build prints `9000000000` then `false` (WRONG). Masking: `get`-only / small-value programs (`Box.new(111)` i16 + `Box.new(222)` i32, both `.get()`) print correctly because the values fit the narrower shared layout — the collision only bites when a later instantiation stores a value too wide for the first instantiation's field width. COUPLING: gap 1's fix is correct and desirable (single-instantiation generic narrow-int methods are unusable without it) but is BLOCKED on gap 2 — landing gap 1 alone degrades gap 2 from a loud verifier error to a silent build/interp divergence, violating the interp==build oracle. Land them together: fix gap 2 (route in-mono field access for a generic-param-instantiated receiver through `mono_struct_type` under the active `type_subst`) THEN add gap 1's arg coercion. Both are needed for `VolatileCell[i32]` (the marked follow-on to the phase-11 volatile MMIO intrinsics).

title: FIXED (7c8d383). A struct `.to_string()` (rendered via the synthetic f-string in `compile_struct_display_string`) double-freed its rendered buffer when returned directly from a function (`fn d(p: ref Point) -> String { p.to_string() }` -> `free(): double free detected`; interp correct). ROOT CAUSE: the return handler (functions.rs) zeroed the f-string accumulator's cap — so its `FreeVecBuffer` no-ops and the caller owns the buffer — ONLY when the tail expr was a literal `InterpolatedStringLit`; the `.to_string()` tail, which also stages `last_fstr_acc` (via the synthetic f-string), was missed, so the buffer was freed both at scope exit and by the caller. A let-binding tail was already fine (the let path transfers via `rhs_stages_fstr_acc`). FIX: the return handler now uses that SAME `rhs_stages_fstr_acc` predicate, covering both the `f"…"` and struct-`.to_string()` tail shapes; a `None` acc (user `impl Display` to_string) is a harmless no-op. This also unblocked struct `self.to_string()` / `f"{self}"` (the struct half of B-2026-07-12-15) — codegen's Display helper now recognises a `SelfValue` struct receiver. Verified ref/owned-param + nested + self (inherent & trait) returns render correctly and are valgrind + ASAN/LSan clean. Tests: `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_struct_to_string_returned_from_fn`, `::test_e2e_struct_self_to_string`, `tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_struct_to_string_returned_from_fn_no_double_free`.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: self-review while fixing B-2026-07-12-15 (self.to_string for Display enums)

title: FIXED (acbaf96). Filed as a leak, but actually a MISCOMPILE (build != run): a generic `fn f[E: Display](e: ref E)` monomorphized to a payload `#[derive(Display)]` enum rendered `e` (`println` / f-string) by reading the enum from the `ref` param's SLOT address (a pointer TO the value) instead of the value, printing `Other()` garbage for every input under codegen while the interpreter was correct. The f-string-RETURNED shape (`fn wrap[E: Display](e: ref E) -> String { f"error: {e}" }`) rendered the correct value but LEAKED the render buffer (15 B) — the leak was a downstream symptom of the same wrong address. ROOT CAUSE: `render_user_enum_display` (synth_display.rs) resolved the value pointer as the raw `variables[n].ptr` slot; for a `ref` param that slot holds a pointer to the value, so both the read and the buffer tracking used the wrong address. FIX: resolve via `get_data_ptr`, which loads through a `ref` param (and unwraps an RC-promoted binding) and returns the alloca unchanged for an owned binding — the common `println(local_enum)` / owned cases are unaffected. One root fix resolved BOTH the miscompile and the leak. Independent of `self`/`.to_string()` (reproduces with a plain `ref E` param and `println`). Verified interp == JIT == AOT across owned / non-generic-ref / generic-`ref E` / generic-`mut ref E` receivers, all valgrind + ASAN/LSan clean. Tests: `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_generic_ref_enum_display`, `tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_generic_ref_enum_display_no_leak`.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: self-review while fixing B-2026-07-12-15 (self.to_string for Display enums)

tracker: env-layer `bound_satisfied` only consulted the impl table, so derive-only builtin traits (Copy/Clone/Eq/Ord/Hash/...) — never impl-table entries — were unsatisfiable by primitives at method resolution
title: A bounded generic method (`impl[T: Copy] Cell[T] { fn read(...) }`) was WRONGLY REJECTED on a PRIMITIVE instantiation (`Cell[i32]`) — "`i32` does not implement `Copy`". The env-layer method-resolution gate `bound_satisfied` (`impl_bounds_discharge` + the diagnostic `first_unsatisfied_bound`) only walked the impl table, but the derive-only builtin traits (Copy / Clone / Eq / Ord / Hash / PartialEq / PartialOrd / Debug / Default) are NEVER impl-table entries — a primitive satisfies them structurally — so every primitive instantiation of such a bounded generic method failed to resolve. The richer TypeChecker-layer `type_satisfies_bound` handles them correctly, but the env layer (`TypeEnv`, no access to the `type_supports_*` helpers) did not.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found while building the `VolatileCell[T: Copy]` stdlib wrapper (phase-11 embedded MMIO follow-on): calling a `VolatileCell[i32]` method was rejected because the `T: Copy` bound was reported unsatisfied for `i32`. — REPRO (rejected before fix; `i32` IS Copy): `struct Box[T: Copy] { v: T } impl[T: Copy] Box[T] { fn new(v: T) -> Box[T] { Box { v: v } } fn get(ref self) -> T { self.v } } fn main() { let b: Box[i32] = Box.new(5); let _x: i32 = b.get(); }` -> `error: method 'get' is not callable on this `Box`: trait bound `T: Copy` is not satisfied; `i32` does not implement `Copy``. FIX: `bound_satisfied` (src/typechecker/env.rs) now satisfies the derive-only builtins structurally for primitive scalars BEFORE the impl-table walk, SPLIT BY TRAIT to respect float semantics — `f32`/`f64` satisfy `Copy`/`Clone`/`Debug`/`PartialEq`/`PartialOrd`/`Default` but NOT `Eq`/`Ord`/`Hash` (floats are not totally ordered / bit-equatable), matching the `type_supports_*` helpers and preserving the `f64`-as-`Ord`-key rejection (`test_generic_container_impl_float_ord_bound_actionable_message`, which the initial blanket version broke and the split version restores). A non-primitive, or a float against `Eq`/`Ord`/`Hash`, falls through to the impl-table walk unchanged — named-type `#[derive]` satisfaction at this env gate stays a pre-existing gap (the richer `type_satisfies_bound` covers it elsewhere; not needed for the primitive case this fix targets). This unblocked the sound `VolatileCell[T: Copy]` bound (accepts primitive registers, rejects `VolatileCell[String]`). Tests: tests/typechecker.rs::test_copy_bound_generic_method_callable_on_primitive / test_copy_bound_rejects_non_copy_type / test_volatile_cell_copy_bound_accepts_primitive_rejects_string; regression guard test_generic_container_impl_float_ord_bound_actionable_message.

title: FIXED (86fd7c5). Completes closure-param inference (body side = B-2026-07-12-10). A let-bound closure with an un-annotated param and NO body constraint (`let id = |x| x; id(5)`) left the param an unsolved inference var and the call was rejected. TWO coordinated changes: (1) `infer_call`'s `Type::Function` arm (expr_call.rs) solves a still-unsolved param var from the monomorphic call arg's type, then resolves params+return for the arg-check (closures are monomorphic — one call fixes it; a second call with a different type mismatches; for the identity closure the return shares the param var so it resolves too). (2) `finalize_closure_expr_types` (patterns.rs), at the same finalize point as `finalize_pattern_binding_inner_types`, re-resolves every recorded `Function`/`OnceFunction` type in `expr_types` through the now-final substitutions — the earlier-identified codegen-propagation blocker: the call-site solve lands AFTER the closure literal's `Fn(?T0)->?T0` is recorded, and codegen reads closure signatures from `fn_value_typed_exprs` (built in lowering from `expr_types`), so without this it defaulted the param to i64 (i64 identity built+ran, `String` identity failed module verification — the build!=run gap that forced the first revert). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for i64 + String identity closures and return propagation; String closure valgrind-clean; monomorphic re-use with a second type still mismatches; B-10 / annotated / push-side map unchanged. Tests: typechecker x2, interpreter x1, codegen E2E x1.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: split from B-2026-07-12-10 (closure-param inference) while fixing the arithmetic-body half

tracker: Vec[Option[shared]] element extraction via `v[i]` + re-store leaks the node per op; rc never reaches 0 (needs IR-level rc-inc/dec trace of extract+store+drop)
title: Extracting an `Option[shared]` element from a `Vec[Option[shared]]` via `v[i]` and re-storing it (e.g. `dst.push(src[0])`, or `let x = src[0]; dst.push(x)`) LEAKS the node once per operation (confirmed 32 bytes/iteration over a 200x loop = 6,400 bytes / 200 blocks). The node's rc never reaches 0. Distinct from the field-read push (B-2026-07-12-4, fixed by a retain): this is NOT a push-site retain-count issue and NOT specific to the direct push form — a fundamental RC imbalance in the Vec[Option[shared]] element-EXTRACTION (index-read) + re-store + dual-drop sequence.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found while fixing B-2026-07-12-4 (field-read Option[shared] push): the direct index-read sibling `dst.push(src[0])` leaks independently. — MINIMAL LOOPED REPRO (leaks 6,400 bytes / 200 blocks under valgrind/LSan; prints `2000`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn xfer() -> i64 { let mut src: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); src.push(Some(Node { val: 10, left: None, right: None })); let mut dst: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); dst.push(src[0]); let mut r: i64 = 0; match dst[0] { None => {} Some(nd) => { r = nd.val; } } r } fn main() { let mut i: i64 = 0; let mut t: i64 = 0; while i < 200 { t = t + xfer(); i = i + 1; } println(f"{t}"); }`. INVESTIGATION (2026-07-12, while fixing B-2026-07-12-4): (1) CONFIRMED a REAL per-iteration leak, not main-exit drop elision — an unread Vec in `main` (never read after a push) 'leaks' its whole buffer under valgrind because main-final-scope drops are elided, but the SAME unread Vec in a looped HELPER fn is clean, and this repro loops in a helper and leaks 32 B/iter. Value is correct (`nd.val == 10`); pure leak. (2) NOT a push-site retain-count issue: leaks IDENTICALLY with and without `share_option_shared_index_ref_for_arg` applied at the push (adding the inc just raises the leaked node's rc; a leak is per-block). (3) NOT specific to the direct-push form: the `let x = src[0]; dst.push(x)` form (whose `let` should deep-clone via `clone_owned_vec_index_element`, and whose push hits the Identifier retain) leaks the same 6,400 B / 200 blocks — so the imbalance is in extracting an Option[shared] element from the Vec (index niche read) and giving it a second owner, not in the push arg treatment. (4) NOT the push-handler suppress helpers (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` / `suppress_inline_option_payload_cleanup_for_moved_arg` / map/result siblings) — all early-return on a non-Identifier / non-TupleIndex / non-FieldAccess arg, so none touch the `v[i]` Index arg. FIX AREA: the inline-RC balance of `Vec[Option[shared]]` index-read extraction (`compile_index` niche path) composed with the destination Vec's per-element drop and the source Vec's per-element drop — the extracted element must be given a clean +1 (retain or deep-clone) that the destination drop releases, OR the source must disown it and only the destination drop release. Needs IR-level rc-inc/dec instrumentation (RC ops are emitted INLINE, not via a runtime fn, so a codegen-side trace or `.ll` inspection is required to pin the exact missing/extra dec). SIBLING GAP (unchanged): `Vec.push_front` / VecDeque `try_push*` call NONE of the `share_option_shared_*_for_arg` retains. SHARP ISOLATION (2026-07-12): the leak is NOT the extraction/re-store — it is the MATCH on a DIRECT index-read `Option[shared]` scrutinee. Minimized: (A) `let mut dst: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); dst.push(Some(Node{val:10})); match dst[0] { Some(nd) => r = nd.val, None => {} }` (NO src, NO re-store) LEAKS 16 B/iter. (B) `dst.push(src[0])` with NO match-read is CLEAN. (C) `match o` on a plain `Option[Node]` variable is CLEAN. (D) `let e: Option[Node] = dst[0]; match e { ... }` (let-bind the index-read FIRST) is CLEAN. So the culprit is the FRESH-TEMP match scrutinee for a `vec[i]` Option[shared] index-read. ROOT CAUSE (same class as B-2026-07-12-2 gap 2): `clone_owned_vec_index_element` deep-clones the index element via the concrete `Option[shared Node]` clone fn (rc-INC on the shared payload), but `materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee` (control_flow_match.rs ~4285) resolves the drop from the GENERIC SEEDED `Option` layout — whose `field_drop_kinds` are all `None` — so `has_droppable` is false, no drop is tracked, and the cloned shared payload's retained rc is never released. The let-bound path (D) uses the binding's CONCRETE type drop and releases correctly. FIX DIRECTION: give the fresh-temp scrutinee drop the CONCRETE payload drop-kind (from the scrutinee type / `enum_inst_type_exprs`) instead of the generic seeded layout, AND make the per-arm move suppression (`suppress_destructured_enum_payload_cleanup`) shared-aware so an arm that CONSUMES the payload doesn't double-release. HIGH RISK: this path has ~6 prior double-free bugs (B-2026-06-14-12, #35, #38, …); a mis-balanced release turns the leak into a UAF. Pure leak today (value correct), so not urgent. A low-risk alternative worth exploring: route a direct `match vec[i]` on a shared/heap Option through the proven-clean let-bound path (D) by internally binding the scrutinee. FIXED (ee17020) via that low-risk alternative — NOT the high-risk drop-kind/suppression surgery. The lowering pass (`bind_shared_index_match_scrutinee`, src/lowering.rs) rewrites `match <vec[i]> { arms }` — when the scrutinee is a bare `Index` whose `expr_types` entry is `Option`/`Result` with a (recursively) `shared` payload — into `{ let __karac_msc_‹span› : ‹ScrutTy› = <vec[i]>; match __karac_msc_‹span› { arms } }`. The synthetic binding carries an EXPLICIT type annotation (`TypeChecker::type_to_type_expr` of the scrutinee type) because the typechecker never saw this binding — codegen resolves the concrete payload drop from the annotation, not an `expr_types` key. This turns the leaking direct form into the proven-clean path (D) at the AST level, so the interpreter, JIT, and AOT all inherit the correct behavior and agree (no backend-specific drop surgery). Gate is shared-payload-only: String/Vec/box Option payloads are already balanced through their own concrete layouts (verified `Vec[Option[String]]` direct-index match is clean pre-fix), so no working case is perturbed. Ownership/effect checkers are AST-name-based and accept the synthetic binding exactly as they accept a hand-written `let e = vec[i]; match e` (which passes `karac check`). Verified interp == JIT == AOT (prints 2000) + valgrind/LSan-clean on the minimal repro, the src+dst re-store repro (`dst.push(src[0])` + match), and the full suites (codegen E2E 2234, memory_sanitizer 634, non-llvm). Tests: codegen.rs::test_e2e_direct_index_match_option_shared_correct, memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_direct_index_match_option_shared_no_leak. The high-risk drop-kind + shared-aware-suppression direction remains available if a future case needs the direct-form drop resolved in place, but is unnecessary for this leak. SIBLING GAP (still open, unfixed): `Vec.push_front` / VecDeque `try_push*` call none of the `share_option_shared_*_for_arg` retains — tracked here for the push-side, separate from this match-scrutinee fix.

tracker: fixed same slice; regression guard added (see resolution)
title: `karac run` (JIT / LLJIT) fails with 'Symbols not found: [karac_realloc_or_panic]' on ANY program that GROWS a `Vec` or `String` past its initial capacity. The in-place grow symbol codegen emits (`karac_realloc_or_panic`, src/codegen/vec_method.rs) was missing from the runtime's `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` force-keep list, so the LLJIT process-symbol-search (dlsym) generator can't resolve it and `main` never materializes. AOT (`karac build`) and `karac run --interp` are unaffected; the failure is loud (no silent wrong output). Since `karac run` defaults to JIT, this broke JIT execution for essentially every non-trivial program.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced rebenching kara-katas #101 (symmetric-tree) on the M5 Pro: with karac freshly rebuilt from main, `karac run` (JIT) failed to materialize `main` on the kata (which builds trees into a Vec) while `karac build` (AOT) and `karac run --interp` produced the correct sink 240918324. — The standalone JIT runner (`src/bin/karac_jit_runner.rs`) and the in-process LLJIT test harness both resolve emitted `karac_*` runtime FFI via `dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT)`, which only sees symbols the binary actually links. `runtime/src/lib.rs::__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` force-keeps each such symbol by taking its address through `black_box`, defeating linker DCE (and on Linux driving build.rs's `--export-dynamic-symbol=karac_*` so it lands in `.dynsym`). The list kept `karac_alloc_fallible` + `karac_alloc_or_panic` (the initial fresh malloc) but NOT `karac_realloc_or_panic` — the symbol codegen emits for the IN-PLACE grow of `Vec.push`/`reserve` and the `String` push/grow path (both realloc an existing non-empty buffer). Consequence: the FIRST push into an empty Vec (cap 0 -> initial `karac_alloc_or_panic`) works, but the instant len exceeds capacity the grow calls `karac_realloc_or_panic`, which was DCE'd out of the runner binary, so the LLJIT link-time symbol search fails: `JIT session error: Symbols not found: [ _karac_realloc_or_panic ]` / `lookup_address("main"): Failed to materialize symbols`. REPRO (fail before / pass after): `karac run` on #101 is_symmetric.kara and is_symmetric_serial.kara; minimal `fn main(){ let v:Vec[i64]=Vec.new(); let mut i:i64=0; while i<64 { v.push(i); i=i+1; } }`. The AOT build and `--interp` of the same programs print the correct result, isolating the defect to JIT symbol resolution (not codegen, not semantics). WHY IT SLIPPED: the existing JIT test `lljit_post_w1_vec_push_libc_grow_path` pushes a SINGLE element into an empty Vec — the initial-alloc path that never reallocs — so `karac_realloc_or_panic` had ZERO JIT coverage.
resolution: FIXED. Added `alloc::karac_realloc_or_panic` to the allocation-wrappers `keep!` in `runtime/src/lib.rs::__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` (the symbol is `#[no_mangle] pub extern "C"` in `runtime/src/alloc.rs`, always in the static archive — only the JIT runner's DCE dropped it; AOT was never affected). Regression guard: new in-process test `lljit_post_w1_vec_push_realloc_grow_path` in `tests/lljit_prototype.rs` loops 64 pushes to FORCE at least one realloc grow (the sibling single-push test never reaches it). Verified: `karac run` (JIT) now runs #101 is_symmetric.kara + is_symmetric_serial.kara -> sink 240918324, byte-identical to `--interp` and `karac build`; the minimal Vec-growth repro agrees interp==JIT==1498500. Gates: fmt clean; clippy --all --all-targets --features llvm -D warnings clean; `cargo test --release --features llvm --test lljit_prototype` green for the new + all sibling JIT tests. (The pre-existing macOS-only `lljit_gdb_registration_listener_registers_dwarf_module` failure is unrelated — it asserts the LLVM GDB JIT-descriptor listener fires, a platform/LLVM-build concern with no causal link to symbol preservation; it fails identically on untouched main.) No AOT or interpreter impact.

tracker: a fresh-temp `match <call returning Option[shared]>` scrutinee can leak the node once per match; materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee no-ops for seeded Option (all-None drop kinds), so the owned call-result scrutinee is not released after the match
title: A fresh-temp `match <call-returning-Option[shared]> { Some(n) => <use n> }` LEAKS the node once per match in some shapes (32 B/iter over a loop). Sibling of B-2026-07-12-21 (fixed, index-read scrutinee): both stem from the enum drop-tracking no-op'ing for a seeded Option match scrutinee, but this is the fresh-temp (call-result) shape, NOT covered by the index-read fix (which only touches `v[i]` scrutinees), and it depends on how the callee builds its returned Option.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found during a deep dive on B-2026-07-12-21 (index-read Option[shared] match scrutinee, fixed by a sibling as ee17020): a fresh-temp `match <call>` variant leaks independently of that fix. — REPRO (looped 200x; leaks 6,400 bytes / 200 blocks under valgrind/LSan on current main WITH the B-21 fix ee17020 in place; prints `1400`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn take() -> Option[Node] { let mut src: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); src.push(Some(Node { val: 7, left: None, right: None })); match src[0] { None => None, Some(n) => Some(n) } } fn main() { let mut i: i64 = 0; let mut t: i64 = 0; while i < 200 { match take() { None => {} Some(n) => { t = t + n.val; } } i = i + 1; } println(f"{t}"); }`. DISCRIMINATORS (all looped): (a) `let _d = take();` (discard, no caller match) is CLEAN — take()'s returning arm is balanced, so the leak is purely the caller's `match take()`; (b) `match make() { Some(n) => n.val }` where `make()` returns a FRESH literal `Some(Node{..})` is CLEAN — so it is NOT 'every fresh-temp Option[shared] match leaks'; (c) `let d = take(); match d { … }` (bind-then-match) should be re-checked (a let-bound scrutinee is drop-tracked, likely clean). So the leak is specific to a DIRECT `match <call>` fresh-temp scrutinee whose returned Option derives from a returning match arm (`Some(n) => Some(n)`), not a plain literal. Independent of the B-21 fix — that fix only handles index-read (`v[i]`) scrutinees, not call scrutinees. FIX AREA: `materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee` returns None for a seeded Option (all-None drop kinds), so a fresh-temp OWNED Option[shared] match scrutinee's rc is not released after the match. Because the fresh-literal case (b) is clean, the release must be conditional on the scrutinee actually holding a live +1 the arm didn't move out — needs IR-level rc-inc/dec tracing (RC ops are inline) of the caller's `match <call>` to pin why the returning-arm-derived callee leaks but the literal callee does not (likely an extra inc in the `Some(n) => Some(n)` rebuild inside the callee, or a fresh-temp scrutinee release the literal case gets via move-consume but the rebuilt case does not). RELATED: the fix pattern from B-21 (register a scope-exit RcDecOption for the owned Option[shared] scrutinee, gated to avoid double-release when an arm moves the payload out) is the likely shape. SIBLING GAP (unchanged): Vec.push_front / VecDeque try_push* call none of the share_option_shared_*_for_arg retains. COMPLETION (d9dd2ee): fixed by extending the B-21 lowering rewrite (bind_shared_index_match_scrutinee in src/lowering.rs) from Index-only to Index|Call scrutinees. A direct `match <call returning shared-bearing Option/Result>` is rewritten into `{ let e: <ScrutTy> = <call>; match e {..} }`; the let binding carries the concrete type so its scope-exit cleanup releases the retained +1 (the erased generic Option layout that the direct fresh-temp scrutinee resolved gave all-None drop-kinds → has_droppable=false → no release). Scoped to Index|Call: pop-family MethodCall returns heap-boxed Option[Option[shared]] already balanced by B-2026-07-12-4's fresh-temp boxed-enum drop path, so left untouched. Discriminator (b) `match make()` (bare-literal-return callee) was already clean and now also goes through the rewrite; verified it stays clean (no over-release). Verified interp==JIT==AOT (1400), LSan-clean on the repro + literal discriminator, full suites green (memory_sanitizer 637, codegen E2E 2235, non-llvm, clippy+fmt). Tests: codegen.rs::test_e2e_freshtemp_call_match_option_shared_correct, memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_freshtemp_call_match_option_shared_no_leak + asan_freshtemp_literal_call_match_option_shared_no_double_free. SIBLING GAP (unchanged, still open surface): Vec.push_front / VecDeque try_push* retains, and a non-pop MethodCall returning Option[shared] (e.g. map.get) as a direct match scrutinee is not covered by the Index|Call gate.

tracker: a binding of type Result[shared T, E] that holds a +1 (call return, index-clone, returning-arm rebuild) is never rc-released at scope exit; only a fresh-literal Ok(...) is clean (general enum drop). There is no track_rc_result_var / CleanupAction::RcDecResult — the RC scope-exit machinery is Option-only.
title: A `let d: Result[shared T, E] = f()` (or `= v[i]`, or `match`) LEAKS the payload node once per binding (32 B/iter over a loop). The Option analog is clean (track_rc_option_var queues an RcDecOption scope-exit release); Result has NO such release — only a fresh-literal `Ok(Node{..})` binding is freed (via general enum drop). The B-2026-07-12-21/23 lowering rewrite routes shared-bearing Result `match v[i]` / `match take()` scrutinees into `{ let e: Result[..] = <expr>; match e }`, so all of those inherit this leak.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found during the B-2026-07-12-23 dive (fresh-temp Option[shared] match scrutinee, fixed by a sibling as d9dd2ee). The B-21/B-23 lowering rewrite routes shared-bearing Result match scrutinees into a let-bound form, but that target itself leaks for Result — exposing a deeper gap: Result[shared] has no let-path RC cleanup analogous to Option's track_rc_option_var / RcDecOption. — REPRO A (bindonly, NO match anywhere — the decisive discriminator; leaks 6,400 B / 200 blocks under valgrind on current main, prints `done`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn take() -> Result[Node, i64] { let mut src: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); src.push(Some(Node { val: 7, left: None, right: None })); match src[0] { None => Err(1), Some(n) => Ok(n) } } fn caller() { let d = take(); } fn main() { let mut i: i64 = 0; while i < 200 { caller(); i = i + 1; } println("done"); }`. DISCRIMINATORS (all looped 200x in a helper): (a) REPRO A `let d = take();` (Result, no match) LEAKS 6,400 B; the OPTION analog `let d = take();` (take -> Option[Node]) is CLEAN — so the gap is Result-specific, not an index/match/fresh-temp issue. (b) `let d: Result[Node,i64] = Ok(Node{..});` (fresh LITERAL Ok, no call/index) is CLEAN — a freshly-constructed Ok reaches the general enum-drop path; the leak only bites a Result[shared] value that arrived carrying a +1 from elsewhere (call return / index-clone). (c) `match take()` (Result), `let d = take(); match d` (Result), and direct `match v[0]` over `Vec[Result[Node,i64]]` ALL LEAK 6,400 B — the B-21/B-23 lowering rewrite (bind_shared_index_match_scrutinee, Index|Call, gate is_shared_bearing_option_result which INCLUDES Result) rewrites them into the leaky let-bound form, so routing is correct but the target let-binding has no Result RC release. ROOT CAUSE: the scope-exit RC cleanup machinery is Option-only. `track_rc_option_var` (runtime.rs:1160) queues `CleanupAction::RcDecOption` for `let`/param/pattern bindings of `Option[shared T]` (call sites: stmts.rs:3554 let-binding, stmts.rs:5965 pattern, functions.rs:1676 param). There is NO `track_rc_result_var` and NO `CleanupAction::RcDecResult` (confirmed: `grep -rn 'track_rc_result\|RcDecResult\|var_result_shared' src/codegen/` = 0 hits).
