So a Result[shared] binding gets no scope-exit rc-dec of its Ok payload; the value's +1 strands. The fresh-literal Ok is saved only because it independently reaches the general fresh-enum drop (materialize/track_enum_var), which a call-returned / index-cloned Result value does not. FIX SHAPE: add Result support to the RC scope-exit path — either generalize `track_rc_option_var` + `RcDecOption` to carry the enum name (Option|Result) + Ok/Some tag + payload-word offset (Result's inline area is 5 words vs Option's 3; the payload ptr word offset differs), or add a parallel `track_rc_result_var` + `CleanupAction::RcDecResult`. Wire it into the three call sites that currently gate on Option only (stmts.rs let/pattern, functions.rs param), keyed on the binding's concrete `Result[shared T, E]` annotation. VERIFY with the memory_sanitizer suite (Linux LSan) on REPRO A + the match/index variants, and guard against double-free when the Ok payload is also reached by a returning-arm move-out or the general enum drop (the Option side manages this via move-suppression metadata recorded in track_rc_option_var; the Result path needs the same). SCOPE NOTE: Err(E) where E is itself shared is untested here; the fix should handle a shared payload in either arm or explicitly scope to Ok-shared and note the Err-shared gap. PARTIAL FIX (fe90e41): the MATCH-SCRUTINEE class is now released. `match take()` / `match v[i]` on a shared-bearing `Result` (Ok- or Err-shared) no longer leak — the B-21/B-23 lowering rewrite (bind_shared_temp_match_scrutinee) routes both through a synthetic `__karac_msc_*` let-bound scrutinee, and the new `track_rc_result_var` (src/codegen/runtime.rs) registers a tag-guarded `CleanupAction::RcDecOption` for it (reusing the tag-parameterized action with the Result struct type + Ok/Err tag; `result_arms_shared_type_for_type_expr` in types_lowering.rs resolves the shared arm(s)). Wired at the let-stmt handler (stmts.rs), gated to `__karac_msc_*` synthetic bindings. Verified LSan-clean on match / index / Err-arm / rebuild-arm repros with no double-free (codegen E2E + memory_sanitizer green). RESIDUAL (still OPEN): a USER `let d: Result[shared] = ...` binding — consumed in place (`let d = take(); match d`) or discarded (`let d = take()`) — still leaks. Registering a producer-side dec for USER bindings turns the leak into a DOUBLE-FREE at the verified move-out sites (`return d`, `v.push(d)`, tail-return `{ ...; d }`) because Result has NO move-out coordination: the `var_option_shared_heap`-keyed tail-return inc (stmts.rs:148), consuming-arg (call_dispatch.rs), collection-insert, explicit-return, and reassignment machinery is Option-only. Closing the residual needs a parallel `var_result_shared_arms` map + Result-aware coordination at each move-out site (full Option RC-lifecycle parity) — scoped as a follow-up. PARTIAL FIX 2 (ae75d73): expanded from synthetic-scrutinee-only to ALL NON-ESCAPING bindings via a conservative escape analysis (src/result_escape.rs — a compiler-EXHAUSTIVE AST walk; a binding is non-escaping iff every use is a direct `match` scrutinee, or it is unused; a use inside a closure body is a CAPTURE = escape). Now CLEAN (valgrind/LSan at -O0 AND -O2): `let d = take(); match d` (the idiomatic bind-then-match), `let d = take()` (discard), multiple matches of the same binding, and a returning-arm rebuild through a user binding — all release via track_rc_result_var at scope exit. Escaping bindings are left UNREGISTERED (never a producer-side dec), so NO double-free: return / explicit-return / v.push(d) / struct-field / tuple / reassign / closure-capture all verified badmem-0. RESIDUAL (still OPEN, narrower): a binding that ESCAPES to a consumer that does NOT itself release — `eat(d)` (an owned Result[shared] passed by value; the callee's param is not RC-tracked) and `let e = d` (identifier alias) — still leaks (safe: no double-free). Closing it needs Result function-PARAMETER RC-tracking (the callee owning the dec, mirroring track_rc_option_var at the param site + call-site source suppression) — the consuming-call leg of full Option-parity move-out coordination. Tests: codegen.rs::test_e2e_closure_captured_result_shared_correct; memory_sanitizer.rs::{asan_let_bound_match_result_shared_no_leak, asan_escaping_result_shared_binding_no_double_free}; 7 escape-analysis unit tests in src/result_escape.rs. FIX 3 / RESOLVED (550b22c): closed the consuming-call leg — an owned Result[shared] passed BY VALUE to a consuming fn (`eat(d)`) now releases. The escape analysis is applied to PARAMETERS too (result_escape::nonescaping_param_names): a param consumed in place (only matched) owns the caller's transferred +1 and takes a scope-exit RcDecOption at the param binding site (functions.rs); a FORWARDED param (`eat(r){eat2(r)}`) escapes → unregistered → the terminal consumer's dec is the only one (chain self-balances, no call-site suppression). Borrowed `ref Result` params self-gate off (param.ty is Ref(..), not a Result Path → track_rc_result_var no-ops). Verified valgrind/LSan-clean at -O0 AND -O2: eat(d), eat(take()), the eat(r){eat2(r)} chain; no double-free on ref params or any escape shape. STATUS: the common Result[shared] ownership patterns all release correctly now — `match take()`/`match v[i]` (synthetic scrutinee), `let d = take(); match d`, `let d = take()` (discard), multi-match, returning-arm rebuild, and `eat(d)` by-value consume. REMAINING RESIDUALS (narrow, all leak-only — NEVER a double-free): (a) identifier alias `let e = d` (owned_rhs gate excludes an alias RHS); (b) borrow-arg then later match `peek(d); match d` (a call arg is conservatively an escape — the pure-AST escape walk has no callee param-mode info to tell a `ref`-borrow arg from a move arg); (c) method-consume `r.unwrap()` / `r.is_ok()` (a receiver position, not a match scrutinee). Each needs either param-mode threading into the escape walk (a/b) or recognizing consuming-method receivers as release points (c) — diminishing-returns follow-ups; the leak class this bug filed (scope-exit RC release for Result[shared]) is resolved for all common shapes. Tests: memory_sanitizer.rs::{asan_consuming_call_result_shared_no_leak, asan_forwarded_result_shared_param_no_double_free} + 10 escape-analysis unit tests in src/result_escape.rs. FIX 4 (2da1dff): extended the consume-in-place recognition beyond `match` to `if let` / `while let` / `let…else` scrutinees (all match-sugar), via a shared walk_scrutinee helper. `let d = take(); if let Ok(n) = d { … }` (a common Result-handling shape) now releases; verified LSan-clean at -O0/-O2 incl. an if-let body that moves the payload out (no double-free). while-let/let-else have no Kāra surface syntax today (not in the parser) — handled defensively. Tests: memory_sanitizer.rs::{asan_if_let_result_shared_no_leak, asan_if_let_result_shared_move_out_body_no_double_free}. The (a) borrow-arg-then-match and (c) method-consume residuals remain (leak-only, no double-free). HARNESS (dabcf94): tests/shared_ownership_matrix.rs — a checked cross-product matrix ({source/binding/consume} x {Option, ResultOk, ResultErr}) run under ASAN, classifying each cell Clean/Leak/MemError via build-once-run-twice (LSan on/off). Discovery over 33 cells: ZERO memory errors (no double-free/UAF anywhere across the class), and the frontier is exactly two Result-only leak-only cells — alias (let e = d) and closure_capture — where Option is CLEAN (it has the var_option_shared_heap move-out coordination Result lacks). Closing those two is the only remaining residual and needs Result to gain Option-parity coordination (a distinct, larger change); the matrix now gates against any Clean->Leak regression or any new MemError across the whole shape space.

title: A match arm that binds a `shared enum` payload and passes it BY VALUE into a RECURSIVE self-call leaks the whole RC chain (every node); the same payload passed to a NON-recursive helper, or bound-but-unused, is clean
detail: SOURCE NOTE: derived from B-2026-07-12-6 regression-repro construction (shared enum recursive chk) — Found while building the B-2026-07-12-6 regression repro (distinct bug — that was the shadow RC-dec corruption, now fixed 588265f). MINIMAL: `shared enum E { Leaf(i64), Node(E) }  fn chk(e: E) -> i64 { match e { Leaf(n) => n, Node(x) => chk(x) } }` called in a loop (`chk(Node(Node(Node(Leaf(1)))))`) LEAKS all nodes (valgrind: definitely+indirectly lost == every node, e.g. 400 blocks over 100 iters of a depth-4 chain; ASAN/LSan flags it). DISCRIMINATORS (valgrind-verified): (a) `Node(x) => chk(x)` (bound payload into a RECURSIVE self-call) LEAKS; (b) `Node(x) => depth(x)` where depth is a distinct NON-recursive fn with the same signature is CLEAN; (c) `Node(x) => 0` (payload bound but unused) is CLEAN; (d) plain `let e = Node(Node(Node(Leaf(1))))` dropped at scope exit is CLEAN — the recursive shared-enum DROP itself is fine. So trigger = a match binds a shared-enum payload `x` (rc-inc alias-acquire) AND `x` is moved by value into a recursive self-call. Likely the per-arm payload-binding RcDec is suppressed as a 'move into call' (nothing dec's the alias the callee also holds) OR the recursion's param rc-inc/dec doesn't net to zero on the aliased chain — one un-released ref per level. NOT a crash (sinks correct); a steady leak. FIX AREA: shared-enum match-payload move-into-call RC accounting under self-recursion (move-suppression vs callee-retains). Oracle: valgrind/LSan leak-clean + preserved sink. FIXED 2026-07-12 (c51f5e8). ROOT CAUSE: a PARITY bug in fresh_arg_bare_shared_heap_type (src/codegen/call_dispatch.rs) — the predicate deciding whether the CALLER must register a scope-exit RC-dec for a fresh bare-shared arg temp. It carries a passthrough self-exclusion (`if args.any(|a| fresh_arg_bare_shared_heap_type(a).is_some()) { return None }`) — correct for a FUNCTION chain `g(make())` (g may forward make()'s box, dec'd once at the outer link), but it recursed through a VARIANT CONSTRUCTOR's payload arg too. Since the predicate returns Some iff the arg is NOT itself a fresh-shared temp, `Node(inner)` FLIPPED Some/None each nesting level: `Leaf(1)`->Some, `Node(Leaf)`->None, `Node(Node(Leaf))`->Some, ... So a call `chk(Node^d(Leaf))` registered the caller-side dec only at EVEN d; ODD d registered nothing and leaked the whole chain (valgrind depth sweep: d=1 leak, d=2 clean, d=3 leak, d=4 clean, d=5 leak — every node lost on odd d). IR CONFIRMATION (main): at d=1 no rc_dec/__karac_rc_drop_E of the constructed temp; at d=2 the drop is present. WHY THE DISCRIMINATORS: `Node(x)=>depth(x)` (non-recursive helper) was 'clean' only because the leak is at the caller (main's `chk(Node(...))` arg), not the recursion — but the same arg passed to a recursive vs non-recursive callee leaks identically; the real axis is CONSTRUCTOR NESTING DEPTH PARITY, which the ledger's recursion framing missed. `Node(x)=>0` and plain `let e=Node(..)` were even-depth-equivalent / not-in-arg-position. FIX: a variant constructor is NOT a passthrough — `Node(inner)` MOVES inner into its payload and its recursive __karac_rc_drop frees it, so the outer temp always needs its own caller-side dec regardless of the payload. Skip the passthrough self-exclusion when the arg is a variant constructor (enum_name_of_expr(expr).is_some()). Applies to both the call and method-call arg-drop paths (same predicate). Verified: depths 1-5 all leak-free post-fix, value-correct, valgrind 0-errors + no double-free, and the g(make()) function-passthrough chain unaffected (still balanced). Gates: codegen 2237, memory_sanitizer 640, par_codegen 159, selfhost_typechecker 1, non-llvm full, clippy(llvm+no-llvm)+fmt green. Tests: test_e2e_recursive_shared_enum_arg_correct (codegen.rs) + asan_recursive_shared_enum_arg_no_leak (memory_sanitizer.rs, odd depth-3).
resolution: c51f5e8 — skip the passthrough self-exclusion in fresh_arg_bare_shared_heap_type for a variant constructor; a fresh shared-enum ctor arg now registers its caller-side RC-dec at every nesting depth, not just even.

title: `AlreadySetError[T]` (the `OnceLock`/`OnceCell` `set` error type, a BAKED stdlib struct) is never registered in codegen because baked stdlib structs are not spliced into `program.items` — so every construct-and-read of it hits the `i64` name fall-through and returns garbage. A scalar `OnceLock[i64]` `set().Err(e) => e.rejected` silently returned 0 (interp: correct). A LIVE, UNGATED silent miscompile, independent of the heap-`T` gate (B-2026-07-12-2). FIXED: seed `AlreadySetError`'s field metadata + generic-base `struct_types` placeholder in `seed_builtin_struct_types`, so `mono_struct_type` builds each instantiation and field access / enum-payload binding resolve. Guarded so a user `struct AlreadySetError` overrides it. Fixes scalar construction + recovery; heap-`T` recovery is a separate broader gap (see B-2026-07-12-2). Test: codegen.rs::test_e2e_oncelock_scalar_reject_recovery.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Found deep-diving B-2026-07-12-2's recovery leg: `OnceLock`/`OnceCell` `set().Err(e) => e.rejected` returns garbage under codegen. — ROOT: `runtime/stdlib/once.kara`'s `struct AlreadySetError[=T] { rejected: T }` is baked (signature-registered for typecheck/interp) but NOT in the `Program.items` codegen walks, so `register_struct_metadata` + `build_struct_types` skip it — unlike a user struct (`MyErr[T]`, which works). Field access `e.rejected` then took the `llvm_type_for_name` `i64` fall-through: a bare `let e = AlreadySetError { rejected: 77 }; e.rejected` returned 0 under `karac build`/JIT, 77 under `--interp`. FIX (ef97f7a, `src/codegen/declarations.rs::seed_builtin_struct_types`): seed the four metadata tables `register_struct_metadata` populates — `struct_field_names`=[rejected], `struct_field_type_names`=[Some(T)], `struct_field_type_exprs`=[Path(T)], `struct_generic_params`=[T] — plus a `struct_types` generic-base placeholder `{i64}` (matching what `build_struct_types` builds for a generic user struct's param fall-through; `mono_struct_type` owns the per-instantiation shape). Guarded on `!struct_field_names.contains_key` so a user's own `AlreadySetError` (in `program.items`, seen later by `register_struct_metadata` which overwrites) wins. Verified scalar recovery + direct read both 77/42 (interp==AOT), codegen E2E 2241, clippy+fmt clean. RESIDUAL (separate, still open under B-2026-07-12-2): HEAP recovery — `OnceLock[String]` `Err(e) => e.rejected` — is a broader generic-struct heap-payload recovery gap (the Err-arm payload word-count comes from the generic `{i64}` base = 1 word, truncating a 3-word String; and chained `e.rejected.len()` additionally hits the general `__field_elem_0` method-dispatch gap that fails user `MyErr[String]` recovery LOUDLY). Not fixed here.

tracker: generic-struct heap-payload recovery: mono field-layout recovery for a generically-instantiated user-struct payload bound out of an Option/Result/enum match arm (word count + field GEP); true blocker for B-2026-07-12-2 OnceLock heap-T ungate
title: Moving a heap field OUT of a concretely-instantiated GENERIC user-struct payload bound from an `Option`/`Result` (or user enum) match arm SILENTLY MISCOMPILED: the payload binding's width came from the all-`i64` generic struct BASE (`{i64}` = 1 word), truncating a 3-word `String`/`Vec` field to a single word. `match boom() { Err(e) => println(e.val) }` over `Result[i64, Wrap[String]]` printed a raw pointer word instead of the string, and `e.val.len()` hit the loud `__field_elem_0` method-dispatch gap. A NON-generic struct payload (`MyErr { info: String }`) already worked (resolves via `struct_types` under its concrete name); a plain `let w: Wrap[String]` already worked (the let-site registers `enum_inst_var_types`). ONLY the match-arm payload binding of a *generic* struct instantiation lost the concrete args. This is the true blocker B-2026-07-12-2 named 'heap-value recovery' — once fixed, `OnceLock[String]` `Err(e) => e.rejected` recovery is sound (pending the discard-path re-apply for the leak half). Affected ordinary user code on `main`, independent of OnceLock.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced taking B-2026-07-12-2 (OnceLock heap-T); the 'heap-value recovery blocker' is a GENERAL gap, not once-specific — FIX (6 layers, each gated on `is_generic_named_struct_type_expr` [codegen: `struct_generic_params`] / a built-in-exclusion [typechecker] so BORROW `ref T` and specially-lowered built-ins Vec/Map/Atomic/OnceLock/... stay untouched — the reverted 2026-07-12d attempt regressed 79 codegen tests by firing on the hot Vec/borrow reconstruction path): (1) typechecker `record_pattern_inner_type` (src/typechecker/patterns.rs) records the concrete instantiation `TypeExpr` for a generic user-struct payload binding, EXCLUDING built-ins with dedicated codegen lowering via the new `is_special_lowered_generic_builtin` helper (Vec/VecDeque/Slice/Array/Vector/Map/HashMap/Set/HashSet/String/Atomic/VolatileCell/OnceLock/OnceCell/Tensor/Column/DataFrame/Sender/Receiver/Channel). Without the exclusion the arm's early `return` preempts the Vec/Slice element-type recording and a plain `let v = [1,2,3]` SEGFAULTS (the trap that sank the first cut this session — bisected vs clean main). (2) `pattern_payload_word_count` (control_flow_match.rs) sizes the binding from `llvm_type_word_count(llvm_type_for_type_expr(inner_te))` (mono width) instead of the `struct_types[base]` {i64}=1-word truncation. (3) `pattern_payload_llvm_type` returns the mono aggregate. (4) `reconstruct_payload_value` prefers the mono struct type for `target_ty`. (5) `pattern_binding.rs` registers `enum_inst_var_types[binding] = inner_te` so `.field` GEP resolves the concrete args (mirror of the let-site, B-2026-07-11-31). (6) field GEP + method dispatch resolve automatically once (5) is set (the working `let`-binding proves the machinery; this also closes the loud `__field_elem_0` gap for `e.field.method()`). NO new leak / double-free: recovery (`Err(e) => println(e.rejected)`) frees via the move-out consumer; the source `FreeInlineResultPayload` never handled a struct-wrapping Err (returns None) and the consuming-arm suppressor zeros the source cap; `track_struct_var(base)` stays a no-op on the heap-free base, so no drop double-fires. The discard / bind-unused String-buffer LEAK pre-existed (pre-fix the binding was garbage and leaked identically) and is the SEPARATE drop slice (couples with B-2026-07-12-2 gap 2). VERIFIED: full codegen E2E 2248 passed / 0 failed (prior attempt: 79 failures); memory_sanitizer green (no double-free/UAF); build==run vs interpreter on field-move / field-method / bind-first / multi-param `Pair` / `Option` payload / user-enum payload; borrow guard-rail `test_e2e_vec_get_first_option_ref_t_reread` (Vec.first -> Option[ref String]) preserved; arrays/Vec unaffected. TEST: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_generic_struct_result_payload_recovery. B-2026-07-12-2 depends on this (its heap-`T` ungate). SEPARATE pre-existing bug surfaced (fails identically on clean main, NOT this regression): returning a generic struct BY VALUE through a match arm (`Err(e) => { e }` where the fn returns `Wrap[String]`) fails module verification ("Function return type does not match operand type of return inst! ret i64 0 { { ptr, i64, i64 } }") — a distinct generic-struct return-ABI gap; file its own low-sev entry.

tracker: returning a bound generic-struct payload directly as the fn return value fails module verification
title: A match arm that RETURNS the bound generic-struct payload directly as the function's return value fails LLVM module verification: fn pick() -> Wrap[String] { match r { Ok(_) => Wrap{..}, Err(e) => { e } } } emits 'Function return type does not match operand type of return inst! ret i64 0 { { ptr, i64, i64 } }' - the arm's return path coerces the bound generic-struct payload to a single i64 word instead of the mono aggregate. DISTINCT from B-2026-07-12-27 (which fixed generic-struct payload FIELD recovery, Err(e) => e.field): field access works, but returning the WHOLE binding e as the fn result hits the return-value coercion path. A NON-generic struct payload returned the same way works; constructing+returning a generic struct in each arm (Ok(_) => Wrap{..}) works - only returning the BOUND payload fails. LOUD (module verification error, not a silent miscompile), so build-gated; interp is correct.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced building B-2026-07-12-27 (generic-struct heap-payload recovery) - an adjacent pre-existing gap (fails identically on clean main) — REPRO (fails on clean main AND with B-27 landed): struct Wrap[T] { val: T } fn pick(x: i64) -> Wrap[String] { match (if x>0 {Ok(x)} else {Err(Wrap{val: 'kept'.to_string()})}) { Ok(_) => Wrap{val: 'ok'.to_string()}, Err(e) => { e } } }. Interp prints 'kept'; AOT errors at module verification. HYPOTHESIS: the arm-tail return path for a bound generic-struct payload identifier reconstructs/coerces it to the payload-word ABI (i64) rather than loading the mono aggregate for the sret/by-value return - the return-coercion sibling of B-27's field-GEP recovery. Likely needs the fn-return path to consult enum_inst_var_types / the mono struct type for a returned generic-struct binding. NOT on the OnceLock heap-T critical path (the recovery pattern that matters is Err(e) => use e.field, fixed by B-27). FIXED 2026-07-13 (8c327a4). Root cause was NOT codegen and NOT 'returning the binding' (the ledger's original framing) — it was TYPE INFERENCE on an INLINE `if`/`match` scrutinee. `match (if c { Ok(x) } else { Err(Wrap{..}) })` inferred the scrutinee as `Result[i64, TypeParam("E")]`: the `Ok(x)` constructor leaves its return-only `Err` param unsolved, and the generic call path maps an unsolved metavar back to the rigid declared `TypeParam` (deliberately, so `find_unbound_type_param` can surface an unbound-`T` diagnostic); then `join_branch_types` returned the FIRST branch verbatim when the two branches were merely 'compatible', so the `Err` branch's CONCRETE `Wrap[String]` in that slot was discarded. With the scrutinee frozen at `TypeParam("E")`, the match-arm payload binding fell to the all-`i64` generic base width and codegen emitted `ret i64` against the mono `{ptr,i64,i64}` return (module-verification failure). A CALL scrutinee (`boom()`) or an annotated let/function-body `if` sidesteps it because the concrete type is pinned by the annotation — which is exactly why B-2026-07-12-27's own test (an annotated function body) passed while this inline-scrutinee sibling did not. FIX: `join_branch_types` (src/typechecker.rs) now MERGES two already-compatible branch types position-by-position via a new `merge_branch_types`, preferring a concrete type over an abstract `TypeParam` / unsolved `TypeVar` / `Error` at each leaf and recursing through same-head `Named`/`Tuple`/`Ref` shapes. It runs ONLY on types already deemed compatible, so it can only sharpen (never widen) the join. Once the scrutinee type is concrete the existing B-27 mono-layout recovery handles the whole-binding return (`Err(e) => e`), the field read (`e.val`), and bind-first — no codegen change. Test: test_e2e_generic_struct_payload_recovery_from_if_scrutinee (interp==JIT==AOT). Validation: codegen E2E 2249/0, memory_sanitizer 654/0, non-llvm green (2023 typechecker incl. inference/diagnostic tests), clippy+fmt clean.

tracker: compound index-assign of a shared/Option[shared] Vec element leaks the OVERWRITTEN old value on arm64; add the ARC setter rule (retain-new -> store -> release-old) to compile_vec_index_store for shared elements
title: ARM64-ONLY leak: `work[i] = <place>` on `Vec[Option[shared]]`/`Vec[shared]` (e.g. `work[i] = work[j]`, the kata-23 merge-k `work[i] = merge_two(work[i], work[i+interval])`) orphans the OVERWRITTEN old element with no rc_dec. x86 balances it (CI green); arm64 (macOS `leaks` + arm64-Linux LSan) leaks. Distinct from B-2026-07-11-33 (that fixed the Vec SCOPE-EXIT element drop; this is the OVERWRITE path).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: digging into asan_owned_vec_param_let_move_interval_merge: fails under local arm64 LSan (macOS leaks + colima arm64-Linux) while x86 CI LSan is green (post condition-4 RC-elision slice) — Minimal repro (leaks 1 node on arm64, clean on x86): `shared struct ListNode { val: i64, mut next: Option[ListNode] } fn probe(lists: Vec[Option[ListNode]]) -> Option[ListNode] { let mut work = lists; work[0] = work[1]; work[0] }` driven from a 2-element pool + walk. Making slot0 a 2-node chain -> 2 leaks, confirming it is the OVERWRITTEN old value that leaks (not the RHS node). `work[0] = None` and `work[0] = Some(fresh)` are CLEAN (old value released), so the leak is specific to a place-expression RHS. ROOT CAUSE: compile_vec_index_store (src/codegen/collections.rs ~3649) releases the old element only when it is a Vec-struct buffer (Vec[Vec[T]]/Vec[String], via emit_free_vec_buffer_if_owned); it never applies the ARC setter rule (retain-new -> store -> release-old) for a shared / Option[shared] element, so the overwritten node is orphaned with no rc_dec. The Identifier-target assign HAS this rule (src/codegen/stmts.rs ~4937); the Index-target arm (stmts.rs ~5188) does not. The emitted IR is arch-independent, yet x86 does not leak -- the missing release is BALANCED on x86 via an arch-dependent struct-move/ABI path and UNBALANCED on arm64. FIX: add the setter rule explicitly for shared index elements (aliasing-safe: retain the new value FIRST so work[i]=work[i] / work[i]=work[j] self-alias survives), verified under BOTH arm64 and x86 LSan (double-free hazard). ARCH EVIDENCE: x86 ubuntu CI LSan runs asan_owned_vec_param_let_move_interval_merge and PASSES (run 29214689397: 654 passed/0 failed, 655 tests); arm64 macOS `leaks` (2 leaks/64B, malloc-in-main nodes) and arm64-Linux colima LSan both leak. This VIOLATES the CLAUDE.md 'leaks are architecture-independent' premise -- an arm64-only leak class x86 CI structurally cannot catch (motivated the new arm64 memory-sanitizer CI leg). INTERIM: asan_owned_vec_param_let_move_interval_merge is #[cfg_attr(target_arch=aarch64, ignore)] referencing this id, so the arm64 CI leg is green except for NEW arm64 leaks; un-ignore when fixed. FIX SITE: src/codegen/collections.rs compile_vec_index_store + the Index-assign arm in src/codegen/stmts.rs.
resolution: FIXED 63d618e. Added the refcount-aware overwrite arm to `compile_vec_index_store` (src/codegen/collections.rs): for a `shared` / `Option[shared]` element it SAVES the old element to a temp alloca, applies the retain (see below), STORES the new value, then RELEASES the saved old via the SAME per-element drop the scope-exit Vec drain uses (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` -> `karac_drop_Option_<T>` / `__karac_vec_elem_rc_dec_<T>`), so the plain-vs-atomic rc discipline matches the drain automatically. Retain differs by element shape, VERIFIED in emitted IR: (a) `Option[shared]` -- `clone_owned_vec_index_element` already rc-incs a place RHS (`karac_clone_Option_<T>`, an inline rc_inc) and a fresh `Some(..)`/`Call` carries its own +1, so retain is UPSTREAM and the store site releases-old ONLY; (b) plain `Vec[shared T]` -- the element clone is a SHALLOW pointer copy (`karac_clone_Node` = load/store, NO rc_inc), so the store site retains the new value HERE via `emit_refcount_inc_by_type` when `!rhs_is_fresh` (fresh RHS carries +1). Release-LAST + retain-FIRST keeps a self-alias (`work[i]=work[i]`/`work[i]=work[j]`) safe. `rhs_is_fresh` is threaded through `compile_index_store`. EMPIRICAL CORRECTION to this entry's earlier note: the pre-fix store site released the old value in NO shape -- `work[0]=None` and `work[0]=Some(fresh)` were NOT actually clean (their old value also leaked on arm64); the `zeroinitializer`/fresh store overwrote the slot with no drop (confirmed by dumping the pre-fix IR). The x86-vs-arm64 asymmetry is purely the ABI-dependent balancing the entry describes, not a shape-specific release. SEPARATE latent bug also fixed en passant: plain `Vec[shared T]` place-RHS overwrite was a DOUBLE-FREE + leak on BOTH arches pre-fix (shallow clone + no release => two slots co-own one un-inc'd box). VERIFY: un-ignored asan_owned_vec_param_let_move_interval_merge on aarch64; added asan_vec_option_shared_index_overwrite_place_rhs_no_leak (2-node chain in slot0 -> pre-fix 2 leaks) + asan_vec_plain_shared_index_overwrite_place_rhs_no_leak; x86 memory_sanitizer 654 + codegen 2248 + par_codegen 159 green (asan+LSan), clippy(--all-targets, +/-llvm)+fmt clean. arm64 memory-sanitizer CI leg is the authoritative gate for the leak half (x86 cannot observe the arch-dependent leak).

tracker: FIXED (78a9a4a): tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_owned_string_param_if_branch_return_no_double_free + _match_arm_ + _let_if_ + test_e2e_owned_vec_param_if_branch_return_no_double_free + test_e2e_owned_generic_param_if_branch_return_no_double_free (interp==JIT==native, 5 shapes). Leak gate: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_owned_string_param_if_branch_return_no_leak_or_double_free (200-iter) + asan_owned_vec_param_match_arm_return_no_leak_or_double_free (100-iter, nested-heap). Regression guard: the existing Option-unwrap `match opt { Some(v) => v, None => d }` corpus (v is a payload binding, NOT a param -> must NOT deep-copy) stays green.
title: An owned Vec/String PARAM returned from an `if`/`match`/nested-block BRANCH TAIL double-frees under codegen (AOT/JIT `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`); interpreter correct. The bare-tail return (`fn id(s: String) -> String { s }`) already deep-copies, but the branch-buried leaf (`if c { a } else { b }`, `match c { true => a, false => b }`, `let r = if c { a } else { b }; r`) applied only move-suppression, which is inert for a caller-retained param, so the returned value aliased the caller's arg buffer.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight deep-dogfooding: probing owned-String/Vec param returns through control flow. `fn pick(a: String, b: String) -> String { if a > b { a } else { b } }` printed correctly under `--interp` but aborted under JIT and native. — REPRO (interp prints `banana`; JIT+native abort `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`): `fn pick(a: String, b: String) -> String { if a > b { a } else { b } }  fn main() { println(pick(f"apple", f"banana")); }`. SIBLINGS (all double-freed pre-fix): `match a > b { true => a, false => b }` (match arm); `let r = if a > b { a } else { b }; r` (let-bound if); `if a > b { a } else { { b } }` (nested block tail); `fn choose(a: Vec[String], b: Vec[String], first: bool) -> Vec[String] { if first { a } else { b } }` (nested-heap Vec[String]); `fn pick[T: Ord](a: T, b: T) -> T { if a > b { a } else { b } }` (generic, String monomorph). DISCRIMINATOR: the Option-unwrap shape `match opt { Some(v) => v, None => d }` where `v` is a match PAYLOAD BINDING (a local, not a param) is CLEAN and must stay clean — the fix must not deep-copy it. ROOT: an owned String/Vec param is CALLER-retained (by-value header ABI; the caller that passed the arg frees its buffer). Returning such a param must hand back a DEEP COPY so the callee's result owns an independent buffer, or the caller frees the arg AND the consumer frees the result against one shared `{ptr,len,cap}` buffer. The bare-tail path does this at the function tail (`maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg`, functions.rs), but when the function's `final_expr` is an `if`/`match`/block, that helper sees the whole branch expression (not the leaf identifier) and no-ops. Each branch instead ran only `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` / `suppress_block_tail_cleanup` (zero the source slot `cap`) — the move-out suppressor, which is correct for a LOCAL owned binding being moved into the consumer but INERT for a caller-retained param (the callee never frees the param, so zeroing its slot does nothing) and leaves the branch value aliasing the caller's arg buffer.
resolution: FIXED (78a9a4a). Added `deepcopy_owned_param_branch_tail` (src/codegen/runtime.rs) next to `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg`: given a value-position block/branch tail expr, it recurses through nested `{ … }`/`Seq`/`unsafe` tails to reach the leaf (mirroring `suppress_block_tail_cleanup`'s recursion) and, for an Identifier leaf, delegates to `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` — which deep-copies iff the name is in `owned_vecstr_params` (or `for_loop_borrow_vars`) and otherwise returns the value untouched, so a LOCAL binding keeps its move-out semantics and the Option-unwrap payload-binding case is a no-op. Wired at every value-position branch escape: `compile_if` (both arms), `compile_if_let` (both arms), and the `match` arm tail (control_flow.rs, control_flow_match.rs). The copy is emitted AFTER the branch frame drains and BEFORE the terminating jump to the merge block, so it lands in the branch's predecessor and the phi/merge picks up the fresh buffer; the branch-end bb is captured after the copy so the phi's incoming predecessor is correct. `emit_vecstr_defensive_copy` operates on the already-loaded SSA `{ptr,len,cap}` value (extract_value, not a re-load), so the prior source-`cap` zeroing is irrelevant, and it recurses into nested heap elements (each String of a `Vec[String]`). A nested `else if` recurses through `compile_if`, which deep-copies its own leaves, so it contributes no leaf at the outer site. VERIFIED: interp(oracle)==JIT==native for the if/else, match-arm, let-bound-if, nested-block, generic (String + i64 monomorphs), and nested-heap `Vec[String]` shapes; the then-branch-taken path (returns `a`) checked too. Full codegen (2261) + memory_sanitizer (664, LSan; 200-/100-iteration loops for the new tests) + fmt + clippy(--all-targets) all green. Not arch-specific (a straight malloc+memcpy deep-copy) — the arm64 memory-sanitizer-arm64 leg will cross-check but no arm64-only surface is expected. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_owned_{string,vec,generic}_param_*_return_no_double_free, tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_owned_{string,vec}_param_*_return_no_leak_or_double_free.

title: A bare generic param `x: T` bound to a builtin COLLECTION (String/Vec/VecDeque) misses its owned-param return DEEP-COPY in the monomorph body under two conditions, each a silent double-free (JIT/native `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`; interpreter correct, no diagnostic). LEG A (String, nested): a generic fn forwarding its owned param to another generic call — `fn twice[T](x: T) -> T { id(x) }` — instantiates the callee as the TYPE-ERASED `id$struct` (no `$T_ct_String` axis) which does not deep-copy. LEG B (Vec, even DIRECT): `let w = id(v)` for a `Vec[i64]` local hits the correctly-named `id$struct$T_ct_Vec_i64` symbol, but its BODY still returns the param by alias — a bare-`T` param bound to `Vec[E]` loses the element `E` (subst_names is head-only `"Vec"`) so the body never registers `vec_elem_types[x]`, so `owned_vecstr_params` never gets `x`, so the tail deep-copy is skipped.
detail:
SOURCE NOTE: Overnight deep-dogfooding, probing owned-heap generic combinators after fixing B-2026-07-13-1. `fn twice[T](x: T) -> T { id(x) }` (generic forwarder) and `let w = id(v)` for a `Vec[i64]` local both printed correctly under --interp but double-freed under JIT/native. — TWO entangled legs, same family as B-2026-07-11-35 (generic owned-heap-param). REPROS (interp correct; JIT+native abort `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`):
LEG A (String, nested generic forward): `fn id[T](x: T) -> T { x }  fn twice[T](x: T) -> T { id(x) }  fn main() { println(twice(f"deep")); }`. Also `fn pick[T](a: T, b: T) -> T { id(a) }` and `id(id(x))` inside a generic body.
LEG B (Vec, DIRECT, no nesting): `fn id[T](x: T) -> T { x }  fn main() { let mut v: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); v.push(7); v.push(8); let w = id(v); println(w[1].to_string()); }`.
DISCRIMINATORS (what WORKS, pinning each leg): String direct call `id(f"deep")` and `let a = id(f"deep"); let b = id(a)` are CLEAN (String's element i8 is implicit in the type NAME, so subst_names["T"]="String" fully specifies it and the concrete `id$struct$T_ct_String` body DOES deep-copy — confirmed in IR). Non-generic `fn twice(x: String) -> String { id(x) }` is CLEAN (concrete String arg → concrete callee symbol). Generic `twice[T]` with the inner arg a FRESH LITERAL `id(id(f"lit"))` is CLEAN (literal has a concrete recorded type). Binding the forward (`let y = x; id(y)` is untested but `let a=..;let b=id(a)` works). Bare-return `fn twice[T](x:T)->T { x }` is CLEAN (B-35 leg-3b mono-tail deep-copy at mono.rs:1715 fires on the Identifier tail). Borrow-then-return `{ println(x); x }` CLEAN.
ROOT CAUSE (confirmed by IR dumps of the monomorphs):
LEG A — inside `twice$struct$T_ct_String` the nested `id(x)` call has id's `T` unify with twice's `T` (a rigid outer param); the typechecker's solver drops this self-referential `T -> T` binding, so `call_type_subs` / `call_type_subs_mangle` have NO entry for that call span. In codegen `monomorphize_call` (src/codegen/mono.rs ~463-519), `infer_type_args` then binds id's `T` to the element-erased `{ptr,i64,i64}` LLVM shape and `subst_names` stays empty; `append_collection_type_param_mangle` (mono.rs:281) needs BOTH subst_names[param] in the collision class AND a token from `call_type_subs_mangle` — both missing — so the mangle stays the bare `id$struct`. That erased body compiles with subst_names empty, so x is never registered as owned-vecstr and the return is a bare `ret %x1` (no dcopy). `id$struct` is only ever the target for String/Vec/VecDeque (user structs/enums keep their concrete-name mangle), so it is a genuinely-heap alias-return.
LEG B — even with the correct `id$struct$T_ct_Vec_i64` symbol (the direct call DOES record the `Vec_i64` mangle token), the mono body param prologue (mono.rs ~1604-1635) registers x via `register_var_from_type_expr(x, subst_monomorph_type_params(T))`; subst_names["T"] is head-only `"Vec"` (from `type_to_concrete_or_param_name`, which erases the element), so the resolved TypeExpr is `Vec` with NO element arg → `register_var_from_type_expr` cannot populate `vec_elem_types[x]` → the `owned_vecstr_params.insert` at mono.rs:1634 (gated on `vec_elem_types.contains_key`) is skipped → `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` at the mono tail (1715) no-ops → `ret %x1` (alias). String escapes this because its element is intrinsic to the name; Vec/VecDeque carry an element that head-only subst_names drops. NOTE the element IS available at the call site in `var_elem_type_exprs[arg]` / `vec_elem_types[arg]` — it just isn't threaded into the callee's mono param registration for a bare-`T` (whole-collection) param the way `augment_subst_from_arg_elem_types` threads it for a `ref Vec[T]` CONTAINER param.
FIX DIRECTION (one focused monomorphizer slice, mirrors `mono_handle_param_infos` for Column/Tensor): at `monomorphize_call`, for each bare-generic callee param matched to a collection ARG (identifier in `string_vars` / with `vec_elem_types` + `var_elem_type_exprs`), (1) set subst_names[param]=head (String/Vec/VecDeque) and append the element-aware `$param_ct_<token>` axis (reuse `type_to_mono_mangle_token`'s spelling: String->"String", Vec[i64]->"Vec_i64") so LEG A resolves to the concrete symbol and REUSES the already-correct concrete body; (2) record the arg's element TypeExpr in a new side table keyed by mangled name (sibling of `mono_handle_param_infos`) and consult it in the mono param prologue to register `vec_elem_types[x]` + `owned_vecstr_params` for LEG B. Then both legs' bodies deep-copy at the tail. VERIFY: interp==JIT==native for String/Vec[i64]/Vec[String] under {direct-literal, direct-local, nested-forward, two-param-pick, id(id(x))}; add codegen E2E + memory_sanitizer loops. Deliberately deferred from the B-2026-07-13-1 session (branch-tail fix) as a separate slice: it touches mono SYMBOL mangling + param prologue + element threading (three interacting subsystems with a regression history), too delicate for the same commit. NOT a silent-forever risk — it is loud at runtime (abort), interp is the correct oracle. TRAP (verified during triage — do NOT take this shortcut): a CALLER-side fix (have the generic forwarder deep-copy or `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` its owned collection arg before the nested call) is WRONG. The caller-retains model puts the return deep-copy in the CALLEE (owned_vecstr_params); a caller-side suppress zeroes the arg's source cap so once the callee is correctly fixed to deep-copy, NOBODY frees the source arg buffer -> a LEAK (invisible to macOS ASAN; only Linux LSan catches it). The fix MUST be callee-side (concrete symbol for leg A + element registration in the mono body for leg B), exactly as the FIX DIRECTION above states.
resolution: FIXED (d20a515). Added `type_subst_type_exprs` (src/codegen.rs) — a generic-param -> FULL concrete `TypeExpr` map (element-aware, `T -> Vec[i64]`/`Vec[String]`), the twin of the head-only `type_subst_names`. `resolve_collection_param_substs` (src/codegen/mono.rs) fills it for each bare-`T` param bound WHOLE to a String/Vec/VecDeque IDENTIFIER arg, reading the arg's registered element from the CALLER's live var side-tables (via `arg_collection_head_elem`) BEFORE `take_var_side_tables` clears them; it ALSO back-fills `subst_names` with the head name for the leg-A nested case the typechecker dropped as a self-referential `T->T` binding (`.entry().or_insert`, so a concrete typechecker binding is never overwritten). The frame is threaded into the mono body (saved/restored around `compile_mono_function` exactly like `type_subst_names`, both swap sites — the generic-call path and the layout-mono path) and consulted FIRST by `subst_monomorph_type_params`, so the body's `register_var_from_type_expr(x, ...)` sees the full `Vec[i64]` and populates `vec_elem_types`/`owned_vecstr_params` -> the owned-param return deep-copy fires with the correct element stride (leg B). For the mangle, `append_collection_type_param_mangle` now derives the element-aware token (`collection_param_mangle_token` -> `mono_mangle_token_for_type_expr`, a codegen-side sibling of the typechecker's `type_to_mono_mangle_token`) when the typechecker recorded no per-call token, so a NESTED call reaches the SAME correctly-strided `$T_ct_<token>` symbol as a direct call (leg A) and two distinct element types (`String` then `Vec[i64]` through one forwarder) stay DISTINCT symbols — no erased-body collision (verified by test_e2e_generic_collection_param_distinct_elements_no_symbol_collision). The CALLER-SIDE-SUPPRESS TRAP flagged in the detail was avoided: the fix is entirely callee-side (the mono body deep-copies), so the caller-retains free stays balanced (LSan-clean). VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for String/Vec[i64]/Vec[String] under direct, nested-forward (`twice`/`pick`), `id(id(x))`, two-param, and distinct-element shapes; scalar generics unchanged (head-name path). Full codegen (2296) + memory_sanitizer (673, LSan) green; fmt+clippy(--all-targets) clean. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_generic_forward_owned_string_param_no_double_free + _owned_vec_param_ + _owned_vec_string_param_ + _collection_param_distinct_elements_no_symbol_collision; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_generic_forward_owned_collection_param_no_leak_or_double_free. NOTE: B-2026-07-13-3 (generic-enum payload BIND width — the sibling in this cluster) is NOT covered by this fix (its `match Opt.Yes(v) => v` payload load is a different path than the param prologue) but the `type_subst_type_exprs` infrastructure this adds is the natural lever for it.

title: A GENERIC function whose body is a `match` expression evaluating to a HEAP type `T` (String/Vec), monomorphized, lowers the match VALUE to the i64 const-0 placeholder — emitting `ret i64 0` against the `{ptr,i64,i64}` return type, which fails LLVM module verification (`Function return type does not match operand type of return inst`). LOUD: `karac build`/JIT refuse with the `--interp` hint; the interpreter is correct. The `if`-body sibling COMPILES (a generic `-> T` fn with an `if` returning String is fine), a SCALAR-return match (`-> i64`) compiles, and the NON-generic match-returns-String compiles — only GENERIC + `match`-body + HEAP return trips it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight deep-dogfooding, generic-enum probes. `fn get[T](o: Opt[T], d: T) -> T { match o { Opt.Yes(v) => v, Opt.No => d } }` over `enum Opt[T]{Yes(T),No}` at T=String failed codegen; interp correct. — REPRO (interp prints `def`; `karac build`/JIT fail module verification with `ret i64 0` / `{ ptr, i64, i64 }`): `enum Opt[T] { Yes(T), No }  fn get[T](o: Opt[T], d: T) -> T { match o { Opt.Yes(v) => v, Opt.No => d } }  fn main() { let r: String = get(Opt.No, f"def"); println(r); }`. Also fails with the `Opt.Yes(f"data")` arm taken. DISCRIMINATORS (pin it precisely): (1) SCALAR return works — `get[T](..) -> T` at T=i64 prints `5|99` clean (m4). (2) NON-generic match works — `enum Opt { Yes(String), No }  fn get(o: Opt, d: String) -> String { match o {..} }` prints `data|def` on all three backends (m1). (3) generic `if`-BODY returning String COMPILES — `fn get[T](c: bool, a: T, d: T) -> T { if c { a } else { d } }` at T=String prints `def` under JIT (m7). (4) generic enum with a String PAYLOAD but a SCALAR return works — `fn describe[T](o: Opt[T]) -> i64 { match o { Opt.Yes(v) => 1, Opt.No => 0 } }` prints `1|0` (m8). So the trigger is exactly GENERIC monomorph + `match` expression body + the match's value type is a heap `T`. LIKELY ROOT: in the T=String monomorph, `compile_match`'s arm values disagree on LLVM type — the generic-enum payload bind (`Opt.Yes(v) => v`) and/or the tail value resolve to i64 (the erased/default width) for one arm while the other (`Opt.No => d`, a String param, `{ptr,i64,i64}`) is the vec-struct — so the all-arms-same-type phi check (control_flow_match.rs ~567) fails and the match falls through to the const-`0` i64 PLACEHOLDER; the mono tail then `ret`s that i64 against the `{ptr,i64,i64}` return type. The `if`-body path escapes because `compile_if`'s two-arm width reconciliation + the mono-tail deep-copy path handles the heap type, whereas the N-arm match-merge does not thread the concrete heap type in the monomorph. RELATED CLUSTER (all surfaced this session, all facets of monomorphs not fully threading concrete HEAP types into bodies): B-2026-07-13-2 (bare generic collection param misses the owned-param return deep-copy — runtime double-free) and B-2026-07-11-35 (the original generic-owned-heap epic). A unified fix that carries each generic param's concrete element/heap TypeExpr into the monomorph body (a TypeExpr-level `type_subst` table, per B-2026-07-13-2's fix direction) would likely resolve this match-arm-type mismatch too, since the arm values would then all lower at `{ptr,i64,i64}`. FIX DIRECTION: ensure the generic-monomorph `match`-arm values (payload bind + param/identifier tails) lower at the concrete heap LLVM type so the arm-type-agreement phi builds the `{ptr,i64,i64}` merge instead of the i64 placeholder; add the same heap-type threading the sibling bugs need. VERIFY: interp==JIT==native for `match`-body generic fns returning String / Vec[i64] / Vec[String], each arm taken; a codegen E2E asserting module verification passes. Not a silent miscompile (loud verification failure + `--interp` hint), so lower severity than B-2026-07-13-2. ROOT SHARPENED (3 more discriminators pin it to the generic-enum PAYLOAD BIND, not the return/scrutinee/param): (m9) both arms return the PARAM `d` with NO `v` used — `match o { Opt.Yes(v) => d, Opt.No => d }` — COMPILES and prints `def`; (m10) generic fn matching a SCALAR scrutinee returning String — `fn get[T](n: i64, a: T, d: T) -> T { match n { 0 => a, _ => d } }` — COMPILES; (m11) NON-generic enum with a generic String return — `enum Flag { On, Off }  fn pick[T](f: Flag, a: T, d: T) -> T { match f { Flag.On => a, Flag.Off => d } }` — COMPILES. Only using the bound payload `v` from a GENERIC-enum variant whose payload type is a heap `T` (`Opt.Yes(v) => v`) trips it. So the precise root is: the generic-enum variant payload BINDING (`bind_pattern_values` / the enum-destructure arm) types `v` at the erased/default i64 width (1 word) instead of the concrete `{ptr,i64,i64}` String (3 words) in the T=String monomorph — so the Yes arm value is i64 while the No arm value (`d`, a real String param) is the vec-struct, the arm-type-agreement phi (control_flow_match.rs ~567) fails, and the match falls to the i64 placeholder. NOTE the fix must make the payload EXTRACTION produce the full 3-word String (not merely coerce the type) or the value would be a garbage String (only the ptr word read) — i.e. it needs the monomorph to know Opt[T].Yes's payload is 3 words at T=String, the same concrete-heap-type threading the cluster (B-2026-07-13-2) needs. UPDATE (B-2026-07-13-2 fixed, d20a515): the `type_subst_type_exprs` map + `resolve_collection_param_substs` + `subst_monomorph_type_params` element-aware threading now exist and fix the sibling param-registration bug; this match-arm payload-bind width bug should reuse them — thread the generic-enum variant payload's concrete `TypeExpr` (via the active `type_subst_type_exprs`) into the payload BIND / load so the arm value lowers at `{ptr,i64,i64}` instead of the erased i64, making the arm-type-agreement phi build the vec-struct merge rather than the i64 placeholder. ROOT DEFINITIVELY PINNED (investigated during the B-13-2 fix, deeper than the payload-BIND hypothesis above): the generic enum `Opt[T]`'s payload area is sized for the ERASED generic `T` = 1 word at DECLARE time — `payload_word_count_for_type_expr` (src/codegen/declarations.rs:3367) returns 3 only for a concrete `String`/`Vec` head and falls to the 1-word default for a bare generic `T` (not in shared_types/enum_layouts/struct maps). The enum LLVM type `{ i64 tag, i64 w0 }` is built ONCE, globally, before any monomorphization (declarations.rs:3244-3274), so `Opt[String].Yes`'s String payload (3 words) does not fit the 1-word slot: a SCALAR `T` (1 word) fits (m4 compiles+runs), a HEAP `T` overflows so the arm value loads at i64 while the sibling `d: String` arm is `{ptr,i64,i64}` -> phi type-disagreement -> const-0 placeholder -> `ret i64 0`. Contrast the NON-generic `enum Opt { Yes(String), No }`, whose payload IS sized 3 words at declare (concrete String) — it compiles+runs (m1). PROPER FIX (a dedicated generic-enum-layout slice, NOT a small graft): size the generic enum's payload PER INSTANTIATION (a per-monomorph enum layout keyed by the concrete type args, `Opt$String` = 3-word payload / `Opt$i64` = 1-word — threading the active `type_subst`/`type_subst_type_exprs` through `payload_word_count_for_type_expr` and the layout build so construction, match-bind, and drop all agree), OR uniformly BOX a generic-enum heap/wide payload behind a 1-word pointer with a deref at every use (the `try_compile_enum_variant` wide-payload boxing path, extended to the generic case) — matching how B-2026-07-12-2 gap 3 boxes wide OnceLock payloads. Either is a substantial enum-layout-subsystem change; deferred from the B-13-2 session as too deep to graft. MEDIUM (loud module-verification failure + `--interp` works), so lower priority than the now-fixed silent B-13-2 double-free.
resolution: FIXED (0e7face). Codegen-only. Root (pinned in detail): a generic enum's payload AREA is sized for the ERASED type parameter (1 word) at declare time — the enum LLVM type is built once, globally, before monomorphization — so a heap monomorph (T=String/Vec, 3 words) is stored BOXED at construction (`coerce_to_payload_words` boxes any value wider than its area), but the match-bind sized `v` at the erased 1-word default because the typechecker records nothing for a `Type::TypeParam` binding. The Yes arm then loaded only the box-pointer word (i64) while the No arm's `d: String` was `{ptr,i64,i64}`; the arm-agreement phi disagreed, the match fell to the i64 const-0 placeholder, and the mono tail emitted `ret i64 0` vs the `{ptr,i64,i64}` return type (module-verification failure). FIX: added `mono_payload_binding_type_exprs` (src/codegen.rs) — a span-keyed map of the monomorph-CONCRETE payload TypeExpr for a bare single-segment type-param variant field. `record_mono_generic_enum_payload_types` (src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs) populates it at the match-bind site by looking up the enum's DECLARED payload TypeExpr (`enum_variant_field_type_exprs`) and substituting through the active monomorph substitution (`subst_monomorph_type_params`, the B-2026-07-13-2 lever). `pattern_payload_word_count` / `pattern_payload_llvm_type` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs) consult it FIRST — only when the typechecker recorded no concrete surface type — so the reconstruct triggers the debox unpack (`want > field_words.len()`), `inttoptr`s the box, loads the full `{ptr,i64,i64}`, and the arm value lowers at its true width; the arm-agreement phi builds the vec-struct merge instead of the placeholder. The Binding metadata path (`mono_payload_binding_surface`) folds the same resolved surface type into the String/Vec element-dispatch + `track_vec_var` scope-exit buffer free, so the moved-out payload deep-copies on return and the heap box is freed exactly once. Scope: ONLY a bare-`T` payload field under an active substitution is recorded — `Vec[T]` (already 3 words at declare, no boxing), scalar `T` (fits inline), and every concrete/non-generic enum keep the existing path verbatim (map stays empty). Map cleared per function; consulted only as the typechecker-empty fallback so a concrete recorded type always wins. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for String / Vec[i64] / Vec[String] under both arms, scalar-T + concrete-enum unchanged. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_generic_enum_{heap_payload_match_return,vec_payload_match_return,scalar_payload_unchanged}; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_generic_enum_heap_payload_bind_return_no_leak_or_double_free (LSan, 200 iters, both arms, String+Vec). Full codegen (2301) + memory_sanitizer (676) + par_codegen (159) + non-llvm suite green; fmt + clippy(--all-targets) clean. NOTE: the boxed-heap-payload move-out infrastructure (`track_vec_var` binding + boxed-payload free + move-suppression), previously exercised only by seeded `Option[Wide]`/`Result[Wide]` and fresh-temps, is confirmed correct for a USER generic enum with a param scrutinee once the binding is registered at the concrete heap width. FOLLOW-ON (0d680d7): extended the same debox to USER-STRUCT heap payloads (`enum Opt[T] { Yes(T) }` at `T = struct Box { s: String }`) — the reconstruct's multi-word target-type resolution now consults the mono TypeExpr before the `field_words.len()` shape heuristic, so a 1-field `{ {ptr,i64,i64} }` struct rebuilds at its exact aggregate instead of the flat vec shape; the existing user-struct `track_struct_var` path frees the moved-out struct's inner heap once (LSan-clean). Tests: test_e2e_generic_enum_struct_heap_payload_match_return + asan_generic_enum_struct_heap_payload_bind_no_leak_or_double_free.

title: Calling a method directly on an enum UNIT-VARIANT LITERAL receiver — `Dir.North.code()` — fails on BOTH surfaces (the typechecker ACCEPTS it, but neither the interpreter nor codegen handles it). Interp: `internal: path 'Dir.North.code' has no interpreter evaluation rule (accepted by the typechecker but not wired in the tree-walk interpreter) — please report this`. Codegen: `no handler for method '<m>' on non-identifier receiver (method dispatch fell through; this is a codegen bug — add a dispatcher arm in compile_method_call...)`. Binding to a variable first is a clean workaround, so LOW severity.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight deep-dogfooding, enum-method probes. `Dir.North.code()` (method on a unit-variant literal) failed on BOTH surfaces with self-reported 'please report this' / 'this is a codegen bug' messages. — REPRO (both surfaces fail loudly; the typechecker accepts): `enum Dir { North, South }  impl Dir { fn code(self) -> i64 { match self { Dir.North => 0, Dir.South => 1 } } }  fn main() { println(Dir.North.code().to_string()); }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (n1) binding first WORKS — `let d = Dir.North; d.code()` prints `0`. (n3) a method RESULT bound then called WORKS — `let d = Dir.North; let e = d.flip(); e.code()` prints `1`. So the gap is exactly a method call whose RECEIVER is a bare enum-unit-variant PATH LITERAL (`Dir.North`), used inline without an intervening binding. LIKELY ROOT: `Dir.North.code()` lowers to a Call whose callee is the 3-segment PATH `Dir.North.code` (a qualified path), not a MethodCall with receiver `(Dir.North)` and method `code` — so the interpreter has no eval rule for calling that path and codegen's `compile_method_call` sees a 'non-identifier receiver' it can't dispatch. Contrast: a variable receiver `d.code()` is a proper MethodCall(receiver=Ident(d), method=code). The disambiguation of `EnumType.Variant.method(...)` (an enum-literal receiver) vs a qualified path needs to resolve to a method call on the constructed unit-variant value. FIX AREA: parser/resolver disambiguation of `A.B.method()` where `A.B` names an enum unit variant → MethodCall on the variant value; then the existing interp method-dispatch + codegen `compile_method_call` identifier-receiver paths apply (or materialize the literal receiver into a temp first, mirroring the `let d = Dir.North` workaround). Also a method call on a TUPLE/payload variant literal (`Opt.Yes(x).method()`) is a likely sibling (untested). NOT urgent: loud on both surfaces (no silent divergence) and the bind-to-temp workaround is trivial. FIX ASSESSMENT (examined the existing rewrite during the B-13-2/-3 session): the typechecker's `rewrite_path_call_to_method_call` lowering (src/lowering.rs:1091) already collapses a 2-segment `Call(Path([X, method]))` (flagged in `path_call_method_dispatch`) to `MethodCall(Identifier(X), method)`, but ONLY for `segments.len() == 2`. The enum-literal case is 3 segments (`Path([Dir, North, code])`): a naive extension to `MethodCall(receiver = Path([Dir, North]), code)` does NOT fix codegen, which rejects the enum-literal PATH receiver with the same 'non-identifier receiver' error (only Identifier receivers dispatch). CLEANEST FIX (one lowering change + a typechecker flag, reuses the working `let d = Dir.North; d.code()` path both backends already handle): (1) typechecker — recognize `Call(Path([Enum, Variant, method], args))` where `Enum.Variant` is a UNIT variant (it already TYPES this — the interp error says 'accepted by the typechecker'), and flag the span (new set or a 3-segment arm of `path_call_method_dispatch`); (2) lowering — rewrite the flagged node to a MATERIALIZED block `{ let __recv = Enum.Variant; __recv.method(args) }` (a `Block` with a synthetic `let` binding the enum-literal + a `MethodCall(Identifier(__recv), method)` tail) rather than a Path-receiver MethodCall, so BOTH the interpreter and codegen dispatch through their existing identifier-receiver method paths with no backend change. A TUPLE/payload variant literal receiver (`Opt.Yes(x).method()`) is the same shape (the `let` binds the constructed variant). Multi-phase but contained; deferred from this session as LOW severity (loud on both surfaces, trivial bind-to-temp workaround) behind the two HIGH-severity cluster fixes.
resolution: FIXED (7bd38e2). Two-part, gated to a UNIT variant of a known enum. ROOT: `Dir.North.code()` parses as `Call(Path([Enum,Variant,method]))`; the typechecker accepted it but ran no `infer_method_call`, leaving the expression UNTYPED (no `expr_types[span]`), and neither backend dispatched a method on a bare enum-literal path (interp: no eval rule for the 3-seg path call; codegen: keys instance dispatch on an IDENTIFIER receiver to resolve the type via `var_type_names`). Because it was untyped, even downstream uses (`let z = Dir.North.code()+1; z.to_string()`) failed codegen for lack of a static type. FIX: (1) typechecker infer_call (src/typechecker/expr_call.rs) detects the 3-seg enum-unit-variant-method shape and types it via `infer_method_call` on a synthesized enum-literal receiver, recording the return type; (2) lowering `rewrite_enum_literal_method_call` (src/lowering.rs) materializes the receiver into a fresh type-ANNOTATED local `{ let __recv: Enum = Enum.Variant; __recv.method(args) }` (annotation required — the synthesized binding never reached the typechecker, so codegen needs it to record var_type_names; same block-materialize the shared-index match-scrutinee rewrite uses), reducing the call to the already-working identifier-receiver method call on both backends. Runs after args are lowered (bottom-up). VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for chained `.to_string()`, a method with an arg, a heap (String) return, arithmetic on the result, and a method calling another method on self; payload-variant literals and `module.Sub.fn()` paths untouched (unit-variant gate). Tests: tests/interpreter.rs::test_method_on_enum_unit_variant_literal; tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_method_on_enum_unit_variant_literal. Full non-llvm suite + codegen (2304) + memory_sanitizer (677) green; fmt+clippy(--all-targets) clean.

title: Three composable Tensor limitations block idiomatic numerical-stdlib .kara over generic-dim tensors: (A) a tensor reduction/transform on a NON-IDENTIFIER receiver (method chain, e.g. `a.zip_with(b, f).sum()`) fails codegen with 'no handler for method sum on non-identifier receiver' — try_compile_tensor_reduce (tensor.rs:2794) only matches Identifier/SelfValue receivers, returning None for a MethodCall receiver so dispatch falls through; (B) a generic shape parameter `D` (declared `fn f[D](t: Tensor[f32,[D]])`) is usable in the SIGNATURE but NOT in a function-BODY type annotation — `let p: Tensor[f32,[D]] = ...` fails typecheck with "const expression: 'D' is not a known const"; (C) a `ref Tensor[f32,[D]]` PARAM passed to a tensor method taking `other: ref Tensor` (zip_with) fails typecheck with 'expected Tensor, found ref Tensor'. Each is loud (compile error / --interp hint), interp-correct where reachable.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight Phase-11 numerical-stdlib work (std.embeddings). Probing a generic-dim cosine_similarity over Tensor[f32,[D]] surfaced three composable Tensor limitations that jointly block writing idiomatic numerical stdlib (embeddings / simd kernels) as ordinary .kara over Tensor. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen / typecheck — WORKAROUNDS THAT SUCCEED (pinning each): (A) bind the intermediate to a `let` and call the reduction on the identifier — `let p = a.zip_with(b,|x,y|x*y); p.sum()` compiles+runs correctly (dot([3,4,0],[3,4,0])=25 verified). So chaining is a convenience gap, not a hard block. (B) OMIT the body annotation — `let p = a.zip_with(...)` (inferred) compiles; only the EXPLICIT `Tensor[f32,[D]]` annotation trips the const-D check. So the generic-shape param resolves fine for inference, just not for a re-stated body annotation. (C) OWNED params (`a: Tensor[f32,[D]]`) work with zip_with (verified) — but owned params can't express cosine_similarity, which uses each input TWICE (dot(a,a) for the norm + dot(a,b)) -> a double-move; ref params are required there, and (C) blocks them. So (C) is the actual hard blocker for the embeddings surface. DISCRIMINATORS: generic-dim OWNED-param single-use fns compile+run (`fn total[D](t: Tensor[f32,[D]]) -> f32 { t.sum() }` -> 6; codegen tests already have `fn trace_diag[N](a: Tensor[f64,[N,N]], n: i64)`). Vector[f32,N] arithmetic is fully solid (splat/from_array/`*` -> correct lanes). Tensor.from + zip_with + sum + scalar .sqrt() all work on CONCRETE shapes with identifier receivers. FIX DIRECTIONS: (A) extend try_compile_tensor_reduce (and _transform/_broadcast) to a non-identifier receiver by compiling the receiver expr to a tensor pointer and recovering (elem, dims) from the typechecker's expr type rather than tensor_var_infos[name] — the error message itself says 'add a dispatcher arm'. (B) make body type-annotations resolve an in-scope shape param `D` as a Dim-kinded const (the resolver comment at resolve_refs.rs:117-122 flags shape-literal identifiers as 'not walked until the Dim/Shape kind system lands' — this is that gap). (C) accept a `ref Tensor` argument where a tensor method's `other: ref Tensor` param is expected without demanding an owned Tensor (auto-ref / ref-passthrough in the zip_with/broadcast intercept's arg typing). IMPACT: std.embeddings, std.simd.math kernels, and std.autograd (all Phase-11 numerical, all [->P1]) cannot be written as idiomatic generic-dim .kara until at least (C) — and ideally (A)+(B) for clean code — are fixed. The Tensor VALUE types lower fine (the 2026-07-09 UNBLOCKED note holds); the gap is method-dispatch ergonomics over generic shapes + borrows.
resolution: Gap C (the hard blocker) FIXED (typechecker: zip_with's `other` arg now unwraps a Ref/MutRef before the same-container check, symmetric to the receiver unwrap — so a `ref Tensor`/`ref Column` argument is accepted; tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_tensor_zip_with_ref_param_cosine_similarity). `std.embeddings` 1-D core (dot/l2_norm/cosine_similarity/l2_normalize) ships on top of it. Gaps A (reduce/transform on a non-identifier/chained receiver) and B (shape param D in a body type annotation) remain OPEN but are ergonomic-only with clean workarounds — A: bind the intermediate to an un-annotated `let`; B: omit the body annotation. Fix directions: A codegen span-collision on MethodCall.span==receiver.span; B thread the fn's generic scope into body-annotation resolution (currently &[]).
investigation: UPDATE 2026-07-17 (triage, still OPEN — large 3-leg feature, each leg characterized): LEG C's TYPECHECK is ALREADY FIXED — `fn f[D](a: ref Tensor[f32,[D]], b: ref Tensor[f32,[D]])` calling `a.zip_with(b, f)` now passes `karac check` (the 'expected Tensor, found ref Tensor' ref-peel gap is gone); leg C's only REMAINING failure is the codegen non-identifier-receiver reduce — IDENTICAL to leg A. So A+C collapse to ONE codegen fix. ROOT of A/C (precise): try_compile_tensor_reduce (tensor.rs) resolves the receiver's element/shape by BINDING NAME (tensor_var_infos), returning None for a MethodCall receiver. Extending it to compile the receiver expr and source elem/shape from the per-span tensor_typed_exprs table DOES NOT WORK: `a.zip_with(b,f).sum()` has MethodCall.span==receiver.span all the way down (sum, zip_with, and `a` share ONE span), so expr_types[span] — hence tensor_typed_exprs[span] — holds the OUTERMOST type (the scalar f32 sum result, which the Tensor filter drops), NOT the intermediate zip_with Tensor. The receiver's Tensor element type is UNRECOVERABLE from the span. FIX-PATH (A/C): add a span-collision-immune side-table (typechecker records -> lowering copies -> codegen consumes) mapping the reduction CALL span to the receiver's element TypeExpr (compile_tensor_full_reduce only needs elem + elem_unsigned; the shape is read from the runtime header), exactly the method_callee_types / method_unwrap_inner_types pattern. Then try_compile_tensor_reduce's non-identifier arm compiles the receiver, reduces, and frees the fresh temp (tensor_operand_is_owned_fresh_temp is already true for a zip_with/map chain). LEG B (separate, typecheck): `let p: Tensor[f32,[D]] = ...` inside `fn f[D](...)` emits UndefinedConst ('D is not a known const', typechecker.rs ~2510) — the shape-dim const-eval for a BODY type annotation does not consult the fn's bound generic shape params (the SIGNATURE path does). Fix: thread the enclosing fn's generic shape params into the body-annotation shape-dim resolution scope so a bound `D` resolves to a symbolic dim (Option::None dim) instead of a const lookup. Deferred as a numerical-stdlib feature epic (medium, interp-correct where reachable, loud compile errors) — not a crash/soundness/correctness bug. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (32d6987, LEGS A+C FIXED — bug stays OPEN for leg B): leg A + leg C (which collapsed to one codegen gap — leg C's ref-Tensor typecheck was already fixed) are DONE. `try_compile_tensor_reduce` (src/codegen/tensor.rs) now has a non-identifier-receiver arm: the typechecker records the reduction receiver's ELEMENT TypeExpr at the reduce call span in `temp_recv_elem_types` (span-collision-immune — the receiver's Tensor type is otherwise lost to MethodCall.span==receiver.span), and codegen compiles the receiver, runs the scalar reduction (sum/mean/prod/min/max; shape from the runtime header), and frees the fresh temp. Verified interp/JIT/AOT parity + valgrind-clean across zip_with/map chains, i64+f32, and a generic-[D] fn. Pin: test_e2e_tensor_reduce_on_chain_receiver. LEG B STILL OPEN (generic shape param `[D]` usable in a `let` BODY annotation, not just the signature): the naive fix (thread the fn's generic scope into the let-annotation `lower_type_expr`, items.rs StmtKind::Let, currently `&[]`) WORKS for the tensor `[D]` case but REGRESSES generic TYPE params — it flips them from the `Named{"T"}` body spelling (whose bounds resolve via `enclosing_bounds` in trait/impl default bodies — the pervasive Named-vs-TypeParam duality) to `TypeParam`, breaking operator/method resolution (2 typechecker tests: operator_on_operator_trait_bounded_type_param_admitted, test_ewmap_trait_bound_map_and_zip_return_self). A plain dim param `D` (declared bare `[D]`, used as a dim only in shape positions) can't be distinguished from a type param at check_function time (its dim-ness is USAGE-inferred, not declaration-flagged: not is_const / is_variadic_shape / `: Dim`-bounded), so a simple const/shape-only scope filter doesn't capture it. Fix-path for leg B: either (a) track which generic params appear in shape positions and scope only those into body annotations, or (b) make the body-annotation shape-dim lowering resolve dims WITHOUT converting sibling type params to TypeParam. Deferred to a dedicated attempt. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (c006423, LEG B FIXED — bug now FULLY CLOSED): generic dim param `[D]` now resolves in a function-BODY type annotation (`let p: Tensor[f32,[D]] = ...` inside `fn f[D](...)`). Fix-path (a) from the prior triage: a new save/restored `current_body_dim_scope` (typechecker.rs) holds ONLY the generic params that appear in a shape position `[D]` in the signature (collected by `collect_shape_dim_generic_names`, items.rs — a recursive TypeExpr walk over `GenericArg::Shape` dims), plus any `const`/`...S` params. The two `StmtKind::Let` annotation sites lower with that scope instead of `&[]`, so a shape param resolves while sibling TYPE params keep their bare `Named{"T"}` body spelling (dodging the Named-vs-TypeParam regression that sank the naive full-scope fix — the 2 typechecker tests operator_on_operator_trait_bounded_type_param_admitted and test_ewmap_trait_bound_map_and_zip_return_self both stay green). Pin: tests/typechecker.rs::test_generic_shape_param_in_body_annotation_resolves (scale[D], zeros_like[M,N] shape params + an id_ann[T] type-param `let y: T` coexisting). Full typechecker suite 2054 / codegen 2436 / memory_sanitizer 768 all green. All three legs (A+C in 32d6987, B here) now fixed. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (45c07cb, LEG B FIXED — bug now CLOSED, all three legs done): took fix-path (b) — `lower_shape_literal` (src/typechecker/lowering.rs) recognizes an enclosing generic shape param via `enclosing_bounds` (pre-populated by `collect_param_bounds` with every generic name in scope) in ADDITION to the passed `generic_scope`, so a body-annotation shape-dim `D` (`let p: Tensor[f32, [D]]` inside `fn f[D]`) lowers to a symbolic `ConstParam` instead of falling to the const-evaluator. Deliberately scoped to SHAPE-dim resolution only: the earlier broad attempt (thread the full enclosing scope into the whole `let`-annotation `lower_type_expr`) flipped body type-param spelling from the bare `Named{"T"}` to `TypeParam(T)` and broke the operator-trait-bound arithmetic check (`operator_on_operator_trait_bounded_type_param_admitted`, `test_ewmap_trait_bound_map_and_zip_return_self`), which relies on the `Named` form — the narrow arm leaves type-param spelling untouched. A genuinely unknown dim (`[Q]`, not a bound param) still errors. Tests: typechecker::test_tensor_body_annotation_generic_shape_param_ok + test_tensor_body_annotation_unknown_dim_still_rejected, codegen::test_e2e_tensor_body_annotation_generic_shape_param. Full typechecker (2053) + tensor codegen (65) green.

title: A `let` that SHADOWS an outer variable inside ANY nested scope (plain block, `if`/`else` block, `while` body, `for` body, `match` arm, nested block) LEAKS the shadowed value PAST the scope under codegen — the inner `let` overwrites the outer variable's `variables`-map slot (and per-var metadata) and NOTHING restores the outer binding at scope exit. Silent wrong result (exit 0, no diagnostic): after the scope, reads of the name return the INNER shadow's value instead of the restored outer value. The tree-walk interpreter scopes correctly, so this is a run/build divergence on one of the most common constructs in the language.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight deep-dogfooding, scoping probes. `let x = 11; { let x = 22; } println(x)` printed 22 under JIT/native but 11 under interp. — REPROS (interp correct; JIT==native wrong; all exit 0, silent): (sh1) `let x = 11; { let x = 22; println(x); } println(x);` -> interp `22,11`, codegen `22,22`. (sh2) with an expr `{ let x = x * 10; ... }` -> interp `50,5`, codegen `50,50`. (sh3) shadow in an `if true { let x = 99; }` block -> interp `99,1`, codegen `99,99`. (sh5) shadow in a `while` body -> interp `100`, codegen `1`. (sh6) shadow in a `for i in 0..2 { let x = i*5; }` body -> interp `100`, codegen `5`. (sh7) shadow in a `match` arm `1 => { let x = 7; }` -> interp `7,100`, codegen `7,7`. (sh8) double-nested `let x=1; { let x=2; { let x=3; print } print } print` -> interp `3,2,1`, codegen `3,3,3`. CONTROL (sh4): an inner `let y` with a DIFFERENT name (no shadow) is CLEAN on all backends (`99,1`) — the outer var is untouched because the inner name never collides. So the trigger is exactly a same-NAME `let` in a nested scope. ROOT: codegen's `self.variables` (name -> slot) map is FLAT — it has no lexical-scope stack. A nested `let x` calls `variables.insert("x", inner_slot)` (stmts.rs bind path), overwriting the outer entry; the per-var sidecar metadata is likewise overwritten (the type-changing-shadow dance at stmts.rs ~2050-2066 `take_var_metadata`s the old binding but DISCARDS it in step 3 rather than saving it for scope-exit restore). At scope exit `compile_block_with_frame` / the loop/match frame drains the cleanup frame (heap drops) but never restores the outer name binding, so `variables["x"]` still points at the inner slot for all subsequent (outer-scope) reads. The interpreter pushes/pops an environment per block, so it restores correctly. FIX DESIGN (a focused scoping slice — deliberately deferred from this dogfooding session as too central to graft unsupervised): add a lexical-scope restore stack PARALLEL to `scope_cleanup_actions` (which already pushes a frame at every one of the ~20 nested-scope entry sites). On a `let` that binds name N in a nested scope: if N is currently bound, record `(N, old VarSlot, old VarMetadataSnapshot)` into the CURRENT restore frame BEFORE overwriting (only the FIRST shadow of N per frame needs saving — a same-scope re-`let` of N re-records, and applying restores LIFO makes the outermost-saved binding win); if N is NEW, record a `(N, remove)` marker. On NORMAL scope exit (frame pop in `drain_top_frame_with_emit` and the plain `scope_cleanup_actions.pop()` paths), apply the frame's restores LIFO: re-`insert` the saved slot + `restore_var_metadata`, or remove the name for NEW-name markers. CRITICAL: apply restores ONLY on the frame POP, NOT during `emit_scope_cleanup`/`emit_scope_cleanup_for_error_path` (the return/`?`/error walks emit cleanup actions for all live frames WITHOUT popping — firing restores there would corrupt the variable state for the rest of the function's linear compilation). EXCLUDE the FUNCTION-BODY frame (functions.rs:1241 / mono.rs:1445) from restore-on-exit (its bindings are function-scoped; restoring at function exit is a harmless no-op but the new-name-removal marker must not run there). `VarMetadataSnapshot` (shadow.rs) already snapshots every per-var sidecar map; it needs `#[derive(Clone)]` (all fields are Copy/Clone) OR a non-removing `copy_var_metadata` so the outer binding can be saved WITHOUT removing it (the block's `let x = x + 1` RHS still needs to resolve the outer `x` while it compiles). VERIFY: interp==JIT==native for sh1-sh8 + same-scope type-changing shadow (`let s = 1; let s = s.to_string()` must still work — the existing dance) + shadow-with-heap-types (`let s = f"a"; { let s = f"b"; } use s` must free both buffers exactly once, LSan-clean) + a shadow whose inner scope RETURNS/diverges (the error-path exclusion). Full codegen + memory_sanitizer + non-codegen suites; the huge existing variable-resolution test surface will loudly catch a scoping regression. SEVERITY HIGH: silent wrong result on an everyday construct (nested-scope variable shadowing), no diagnostic, interp-vs-codegen divergence. ADDITIONAL BINDING SITE (z6): the FOR-LOOP INDUCTION VARIABLE itself shadowing an outer binding leaks too — `let i = 999; for i in 0..4 { sum += i; } println(i)` -> interp `999`, codegen `3` (the last loop value). This is the loop-var binding path (control_flow_for.rs), distinct from a body `let`, but the same flat-`variables` root: the loop var overwrites the outer `i` slot and no restore fires at loop exit. CONFIRMED NON-CASES (same session, prove it is shadowing-specific not general scoping): plain MUTATION of an outer var from an inner block (`let mut x=5; { x=99; } x` -> `99` correct on all backends — reassignment touches no binding), and a `mut ref` out-param (`increment(mut count)` x3 -> `3` correct) both work. So the fix's restore logic must fire for every BINDING site that introduces a name in a nested scope (body `let`, for/while loop var, if-let/match pattern binds, closure params), not just block `let`s.
resolution: FIXED (07fe865). Added a whole-environment lexical-scope checkpoint in src/codegen/shadow.rs: `snapshot_var_env` clones every name-keyed variable map (`variables` + the audited per-variable sidecar set that `VarMetadataSnapshot` lists, plus `owned_vecstr_params`/`for_loop_borrow_vars`) at nested-scope ENTRY, and `restore_var_env` reverts it at scope EXIT — so block-local bindings vanish and shadowed outer bindings return with slot AND metadata restored together (a variables-only restore would pair the outer slot with the inner shadow's stale class tags, a worse dispatch-corrupting state). Wired at `compile_block_with_frame` (blocks/if-arms/unsafe — which also already covers `match`-arm BLOCK bodies), the loop / if-let / while-let / labeled-block dispatch in `compile_expr::compile_expr`, and PER-ARM in `compile_match` (snapshot before the arm's pattern bind, revert after the arm's cleanup frame drains — so an arm's payload/body binding leaks neither to a sibling arm that references the outer name nor to the code after the match). CLEANUP-SAFE (the key correctness argument, confirmed by LSan): scope-exit heap drops are queued as CleanupActions keyed by the binding's ALLOCA in `scope_cleanup_actions`, NOT re-derived from these name maps at drain time (shadow.rs module header), so reverting the name maps neither drops a needed cleanup nor resurrects a freed one — it is a pure name-resolution restore. It runs ONLY on normal scope exit AFTER the cleanup frame drains; a mid-scope `return`/`?` takes the `?` early-return before the revert, so a return/error `emit_scope_cleanup` frame-walk (which does not pop the scope) never triggers it.
