A block/if/match VALUE escaping to a consumer is an already-loaded SSA value, unaffected by the revert; the consuming `let`/arm re-derives its own metadata from the typechecker type (regression-guarded: `let s = { let v = Vec.new(); …; v }` still types `s` correctly). VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for block, nested-block, if-block, while-body, for-body, for-loop-var, match-arm-pattern (+ inter-arm sibling), if-let-pattern, and labeled-block shadows, plus a heap-typed String shadow (both buffers free once). Same-scope type-changing shadows (`let s=1; let s=s.to_string()`) unaffected — the existing type-changing-shadow dance + all its tests stay green. Full codegen (2268) + memory_sanitizer (664+, LSan) + non-codegen suites green; fmt + clippy(--all-targets) clean. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_shadow_{nested_block,double_nested_block,if_block,while_body,for_body,for_loop_var,match_arm_pattern,if_let_pattern,labeled_block,heap_typed}_reverts + test_e2e_block_value_escape_with_local_tail_not_broken_by_scope_revert; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_nested_scope_heap_shadow_no_leak_or_double_free (200-iter LSan). NOTE: closure params were already correctly scoped (own function scope); no change needed there.

title: Reading the row sub-tensors returned by `Tensor.iter_axis(n)` double-frees under JIT/native (`free(): double free detected in tcache 2`); the interpreter is correct. The `Vec[Tensor[T,S]]` row views returned by iter_axis appear to ALIAS the parent tensor's data buffer, so dropping both a row view and the parent frees the same allocation twice. `iter_axis(0)` followed only by `.len()` on the result is CLEAN (the count needs no element access) — the abort requires actually reading a row sub-tensor (`let r0 = rows[0]; r0.sum()`).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Overnight Phase-11 std.embeddings work — probing 2-D tensor row iteration for the batched cosine_similarity forms. `m.iter_axis(0)` on a `Tensor[f32,[2,3]]`, then reading the row sub-tensors, aborts under JIT/native; interp is correct. — REPRO (interp prints `2\n6\n15`; `karac run` (JIT) / `karac build` abort with `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`): `fn main() { let m: Tensor[f32, [2, 3]] = Tensor.from([[1.0f32, 2.0f32, 3.0f32], [4.0f32, 5.0f32, 6.0f32]]); let rows = m.iter_axis(0); println(rows.len()); let r0 = rows[0]; println(r0.sum()); let r1 = rows[1]; println(r1.sum()); }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (1) `iter_axis(0)` + `rows.len()` ONLY (no element access) is CLEAN — prints 2, no abort. (2) reshape alone is CLEAN (`flat.reshape([2,3]).sum()` -> 21). (3) the interpreter computes the correct row sums (6, 15) for the failing program — so this is a compiled-backend-only memory bug, not a logic error. So the trigger is specifically: materialize the `Vec[Tensor]` from `iter_axis`, then READ an element sub-tensor and let it (and the parent) drop. LIKELY ROOT: the row sub-tensors iter_axis produces are VIEWS that share the parent tensor's `data` pointer (a slice into the parent's C-order buffer) rather than owning a fresh copy, but they are dropped as if owned — so the parent's `FreeTensor` + each row view's `FreeTensor` free the same buffer. Either the row views must be borrows (no free — the parent owns the buffer) or deep copies (own a fresh buffer). Mirror the StringSlice `cap==0`-guarded no-free borrow model, or the Vec-element deep-copy model, whichever matches iter_axis's intended ownership. IMPACT: blocks every row-wise 2-D tensor workload written as `.kara`, including the batched / Q×N-matrix `std.embeddings` forms (cosine_similarity_batched / cosine_similarity_matrix / dot_batched / top_k — all iterate corpus rows) and any `std.autograd` batch dim. The 1-D embeddings core (shipped) does not touch iter_axis, so it is unaffected. VERIFY when fixed: interp==JIT==native for iter_axis row reads/sums over f32/i64 2-D tensors; a memory_sanitizer case over the repro (Linux LSan for the leak direction if the fix is the borrow model).
resolution: FIXED (0de5fc8). Root: `t.iter_axis(n)` returns a `Vec[Tensor]` of freshly malloc'd sub-tensor blocks (NOT aliases of the parent — each is a memcpy'd copy; the ledger's 'appear to ALIAS the parent' hypothesis was wrong), freed per-element by `track_vec_of_tensors_var`. A `let r = rows[i]` element move-out routes through `clone_owned_vec_index_element`, but the clone dispatcher (`emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr`) had NO Tensor arm — a `Tensor[...]` fell through to `emit_primitive_clone_fn`, a SHALLOW 8-byte pointer copy. So `r` aliased the container's element and its scope-exit `FreeTensor` raced the container's per-element free on the SAME block -> double free (interpreter's value-copy semantics were correct). Confirmed trigger is EXACTLY the `let r = rows[i]` binding: inline `rows[i].sum()`, `for r in rows`, and `rows.len()` are all clean (no aliasing owned binding). FIX: added `emit_tensor_clone_fn` (src/codegen/tensor.rs) — deep-clones a whole tensor heap block `[rank][dims...][data]` with rank+dims read from the header (any rank / dynamic dims work) — and call it DIRECTLY from `clone_owned_vec_index_element` when the element is a Tensor. CRITICAL scoping lesson: a first version added a `Tensor` arm to the GLOBAL `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr` dispatcher, which regressed `asan_tensor_ref_return_borrow_clean` (LSan leak) — some other dispatcher caller (borrowed `ref Tensor` return materialization / struct-field clone) then deep-copied a BORROWED tensor into a block nobody freed. Scoping the deep clone to the index-move site only keeps every other clone caller on its existing behaviour. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for axis 0/1, rank-3->rank-2 reduction, multiple bound row views. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_iter_axis_row_view_bind; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_iter_axis_row_view_bind_no_double_free (LSan, 300 iters). Full codegen (2303) + memory_sanitizer (677) green; fmt+clippy(--all-targets) clean.

title: `String.from(<String>)` returned the source's `{ptr,len,cap}` aggregate UNCHANGED (an ALIAS of its heap buffer) instead of an owned copy. A fresh owned source — an f-string temp `String.from(f"x")` or an owned String binding `String.from(s)` — is then freed BOTH by its own scope-exit cleanup and by the result binding: `free(): double free detected in tcache 2` under JIT/native; the interpreter (value-copy passthrough) is correct. A string-LITERAL / StringSlice source stayed clean by luck (`cap == 0`, its free is a no-op), which is why `String.from("lit")` and `String.from(<literal-bound var>)` never tripped it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find while verifying B-2026-07-13-4 (a `String.from(f"hi")` in a regression probe aborted under JIT). Not a regression from B-13-4 — reproduced on the pre-B-13-4 HEAD via git stash. — REPRO (interp prints `x`; JIT/native abort `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`): `fn main() { let a: String = String.from(f"x"); println(a); }`. Also `let s: String = f"owned{1}"; let a: String = String.from(s); println(a);`. DISCRIMINATORS: `String.from("plain")` (string literal) and `let lit = "plain"; String.from(lit)` are CLEAN — a literal has `cap == 0`, so aliasing it and double-freeing is two no-op frees. Only an owned source (`cap > 0`: f-string temp OR owned String binding) aborts. ROOT: src/codegen/assoc_call.rs, the `type_name == "String" && method == "from"` arm did `return self.compile_expr(&arg.value)` for a String/StringSlice arg — a passthrough returning the arg aggregate unchanged, so the result shares the source's buffer. Both the source's scope-exit `FreeVecBuffer` (String rides Vec's `{ptr,len,cap}` cleanup) and the result binding's fire on the same pointer. The char arm (`From[char] for String`) was already correct (it builds a fresh String via `emit_codepoint_to_utf8` + `build_owned_string_from_parts`); only the String→String passthrough aliased. FIX: build a FRESH owned copy of the source bytes — extract `{data,len}` and `build_owned_string_from_parts(data, len)`, the same fresh-buffer path `.to_string()` uses — matching the interpreter's value-copy semantics and the owning contract of `From` (the result outlives the argument). Each buffer now frees exactly once: the source by its own cleanup, the result by the binding's. Considered but rejected: suppressing the source's cleanup (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg`) — it reaches a NAMED owned binding (fixed `String.from(s)`) but NOT an anonymous f-string TEMP arg (`String.from(f"x")` still aborted), so the copy is the uniform fix. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for f-string temp / owned binding / string literal sources; LSan-clean under a 300-iter churn over all three (no leak of the copied-from source, no double-free).
repro: fn main() { let a: String = String.from(f"x"); println(a); }
resolution: FIXED (257059b). src/codegen/assoc_call.rs `String.from(<String>)` now builds a fresh owned copy (`build_owned_string_from_parts` on the extracted `{data,len}`) instead of returning the aliased source aggregate. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_string_from_owned_source_copies; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_string_from_owned_source_copies_no_double_free (LSan, 300 iters, f-string temp + owned binding + literal sources). Full codegen (2309) + memory_sanitizer (679) green; fmt+clippy(--all-targets) clean.

title: A generic `shared`/`par` struct with a BARE type-parameter field (`shared struct Box[T] { mut v: T }`) instantiated at a HEAP type (`Box[String]`, `Box[Vec[..]]`, `Box[UserStruct]`) silently corrupted on the native/JIT backends. The interpreter (oracle) computes the correct reference-semantics answer; native prints a garbage pointer-as-integer and JIT aborts `free(): invalid next size (fast)`. Root: the shared RC heap layout is built ONCE at declaration (before any instantiation), so a bare `T` field erases to a single `i64` word — but a monomorph like `String` is a 3-word `{ptr,len,cap}` aggregate, and storing it overflows the 1-word slot into the RC box. Scalar instantiations (`Box[i64]`) and concrete/collection-headed fields (`shared struct Box { v: String }`, `shared struct Cell[T] { items: Vec[T] }`) are unaffected — only a bare-type-param field widened past one word corrupts.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the shared/RC surface (reference-semantics aliasing sweep). `shared struct Box[T] { mut v: T }` at `Box[String]` — a generic RC container, a plausible user shape. — REPRO (interp prints `bye`; native prints e.g. `94404845465600`; JIT aborts `free(): invalid next size (fast)`): `shared struct Box[T] { mut v: T }\nfn main() { let a = Box { v: "hi" }; let b = a; b.v = "bye"; println(a.v); }`. Even construct+read alone corrupts (`let a = Box { v: "hi" }; println(a.v);`) — the reassign is not required. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) `Box[i64]` (scalar T, fits the 1-word slot) → CLEAN, all backends `9`. (b) NON-shared `struct Box[T] { v: T }` at `Box[String]` → CLEAN — the owned path monomorphizes the LLVM struct per instantiation (`mono_struct_type` / `struct_inst_mono_type_for_expr`), storing the field at its real 3-word width. (c) concrete `shared struct Box { v: String }` (non-generic) → CLEAN — `llvm_type_for_type_expr(String)` sizes the slot to 3 words at declaration. (d) `shared struct Cell[T] { items: Vec[T] }` → CLEAN — the field head is the concrete `Vec`, laid out as a 3-word slot regardless of the erased element. ROOT: `llvm_type_for_type_expr` documents the assumption at its generic-owned-struct arm — 'Shared/par structs are non-generic at v1 and route through shared_types' — so a bare `T` field falls to `llvm_type_for_name("T")` → the `i64` default (1 word). `declarations.rs::declare` builds `SharedTypeInfo.heap_type` once from those erased field types; both construction (`compile_struct_init` shared branch) and field reads (`compile_field_access` shared arms) GEP through that single global layout, and the RC-drop field classifier (`emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn`) keys off the declared type name (bare `T` → `SharedFieldKind::None`, so a heap monomorph would ALSO leak — never freed). Full support needs a per-monomorph shared heap layout AND a per-monomorph RC-drop classifier threaded through the load-bearing shared machinery (layout, reads, stores, drop, method dispatch) — invasive, with real regression surface across the many existing shared-struct tests, and no in-tree `.kara` uses the shape. FIX (this entry): convert the silent corruption into a LOUD, actionable codegen refusal. At the shared struct-literal store (`src/codegen/exprs.rs::compile_struct_init`, shared branch), compare `llvm_type_word_count(val)` against the erased slot's word count; when the value is WIDER than the slot, return a codegen error naming the struct/field and pointing to `KARAC_RUN_JIT=0` (the interpreter handles the shape correctly). The guard is precise: niche/`Option[shared]` and `Map.new()`/`Set.new()` fields are handled and `continue` before it; concrete same-width fields compare equal; only the erased-generic-heap case is wider-than-slot. FOLLOW-UP (future work, not a regression): full monomorphized generic-shared-struct support (per-instantiation heap layout + drop) so codegen matches the interpreter instead of refusing.
repro:
shared struct Box[T] { mut v: T }
fn main() { let a = Box { v: "hi" }; let b = a; b.v = "bye"; println(a.v); }
resolution: FIXED (07678e8; loud refusal; full support is documented future work). src/codegen/exprs.rs `compile_struct_init` shared branch now rejects a generic `shared`/`par` struct field whose monomorphized value is wider than its erased 1-word slot, with an actionable message (names struct+field, cites B-2026-07-13-9, points to KARAC_RUN_JIT=0) — silent heap corruption becomes a clean compile error. The tree-walk interpreter remains the oracle and handles the shape correctly. Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_generic_shared_struct_heap_field_rejected_in_codegen (asserts the loud refusal) + test_e2e_generic_shared_struct_scalar_field_ok (scalar `Box[i64]` still compiles/runs); tests/interpreter.rs::test_generic_shared_struct_heap_field_aliases (interpreter oracle pins `bye\nbye`). Full codegen (2314) + memory_sanitizer (680) green; shared_ownership_matrix/rc_fallback green; fmt + clippy(--all-targets --features llvm) clean.

title: A chained field read/store through a Vec that is itself a FIELD of a shared struct (`root.kids[i].val`) returned the const-0 placeholder on the READ and SILENTLY DROPPED the STORE on the native/JIT backends; the interpreter (oracle) was correct. The pushed element is the SAME RC object as the original handle, so both are reference-semantics-visible: `root.kids[0].val` read 0 instead of the child's value, and `root.kids[0].val = X` compiled clean but never persisted (a subsequent read saw the stale value). A LOCAL `Vec[shared]` (`let xs = ...; xs[i].val`) and a scalar Vec field (`root.nums[i]`) were unaffected — only a `Vec[shared]` FIELD chained in one expression.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the shared/RC surface — an RC-cycle tree (parent_tree kata shape) read `root.children[0].val` and got 0 on codegen, 2 on the interpreter. — REPRO (interp `2`; JIT/native `0`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut kids: Vec[Node] }\nfn main() { let root = Node { val: 1, kids: Vec.new() }; let c = Node { val: 2, kids: Vec.new() }; root.kids.push(c); println(f"{root.kids[0].val}"); }`. STORE repro (interp `99`; JIT/native `2`): add `root.kids[0].val = 99; println(f"{root.kids[0].val}");`. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) `let first = root.kids[0]; first.val` → CLEAN (binding the element to a local hits the Identifier shared-type arm). (b) LOCAL `Vec[shared]` `xs[i].val` → CLEAN (dedicated identifier-rooted branch). (c) `root.nums[i]` (`Vec[i64]` field, scalar element) → CLEAN. ROOT: `compile_field_access` and `compile_field_store` each had a dedicated indexed-shared-element branch that only fired when the index root was an `Identifier` (`nodes[i].field`). A FieldAccess-rooted index (`root.kids[i]`) failed that `if let Identifier` guard and fell through: the READ reached the generic struct-value tail, where `compile_expr(Index)` yields a `PointerValue` (the correct element handle — `compile_index` mints a synth Vec identifier and recurses, a pure read with no rc_inc), the `StructValue` guard missed, and the access returned the const-0 placeholder; the STORE reached the nested-plain-struct branch, which only handles `struct_types` (non-shared elements), then fell to the no-op `Ok(())` tail. `type_name_of_expr` already resolves an `Index`-with-FieldAccess-root to the element type name. FIX: a dedicated non-identifier `Index`-of-shared-element branch in BOTH `compile_field_access` (read) and `compile_field_store` (store) — resolve the shared element type via `type_name_of_expr`, take the element handle via `compile_expr(Index)` (no inc), then GEP the heap field through `shared_gep_layout` and load / store. Restricted to a non-identifier index root so the identifier branches stay byte-identical. NOTE the leak this surfaced is filed/fixed separately as B-2026-07-13-11 (the shared-struct RC-drop didn't dec `Vec[shared]` field elements). VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for read, store, compound `x=x+k` store, double-chain (`root.kids[0].kids[0].val`), heap (String) element field, and cross-handle aliasing.
repro:
shared struct Node { val: i64, mut kids: Vec[Node] }
fn main() { let root = Node { val: 1, kids: Vec.new() }; let c = Node { val: 2, kids: Vec.new() }; root.kids.push(c); println(f"{root.kids[0].val}"); }
resolution: FIXED (26bb968). src/codegen/expr_ops.rs: dedicated non-identifier Index-of-shared-element branches in compile_field_access (read) + compile_field_store (store) GEP the heap field through the element handle compile_expr already yields (no inc). Tests: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_shared_vec_field_index_field_read_and_store + test_e2e_shared_vec_field_index_heap_field_read; tests/interpreter.rs::test_shared_vec_field_index_field_read_store_aliasing (oracle); LSan gate shared with B-2026-07-13-11's asan test. Full codegen (2318) + memory_sanitizer (681) + par_codegen/spawn_e2e/cross_task_safe/drop_differential/raii_check green; fmt+clippy(--all-targets --features llvm) clean.

title: A `shared struct` (or `par struct`) with a `Vec[shared T]` FIELD leaked every element's RC box on drop. The shared-struct RC-drop's `VecOrString` field arm froze the `{ptr,len,cap}` buffer but NEVER dec'd the shared ELEMENTS the Vec held, so each child pushed into the field leaked one ref (its box never reached refcount 0).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced while LSan-gating the B-2026-07-13-10 fix: the churn test over a `shared struct { kids: Vec[Node] }` leaked ~40-byte Node boxes (~2/iter). Pre-existing and independent of B-13-10 (that fix has zero RC ops) — the read/store work merely exercised the shape under LSan. — REPRO (LSan on Linux; silent at runtime): churn `shared struct Node { mut val: i64, mut kids: Vec[Node] }`, each iter build a root + children, `root.kids.push(child)`, drop at scope exit — `Direct leak of 40*N bytes in N objects` (the Node boxes). ROOT: `emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn`'s `SharedFieldKind::VecOrString` arm (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) freed only the Vec data buffer; it had no element-drain loop. The NON-shared struct-drop peer (`emit_struct_drop_synthesis`'s `FieldDrop::VecOrString`, #35) already drains — it resolves the per-element drop via `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` (which for a shared element returns `emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn`: load the slot's 8-byte RC pointer, null-check, rc-dec) and loops `0..len` calling it before the buffer free. The shared arm simply lacked that loop. FIX: port the element-drain loop into the shared `VecOrString` arm — resolve the per-element drop the same way (no `subst`: generic shared structs are rejected at construction, B-2026-07-13-9), then, inside the `cap > 0` guard and BEFORE the buffer free, loop `0..len` calling the per-element rc-dec on `data + i`. A `String` / `Vec[primitive]` field resolves to `None` (buffer-free alone suffices, unchanged); a self-recursive `Vec[Self]` resolves to the in-progress drop-fn cache entry. RC CYCLES still leak by design (the refcounts never reach 0 — documented in the parent_tree kata); the ACYCLIC case now frees every element. VERIFIED: the churn asan test is LSan-clean; full memory_sanitizer (681) incl. every existing shared/par-struct drop test green (no double-free from over-draining a scalar/String field).
repro:
shared struct Node { mut val: i64, mut kids: Vec[Node] }
fn main() { let mut i = 0; while i < 300 { let root = Node { val: 1, kids: Vec.new() }; let a = Node { val: 10, kids: Vec.new() }; root.kids.push(a); i = i + 1; } println("done"); }
resolution: FIXED (26bb968). src/codegen/synth_drop.rs: emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn's VecOrString arm now drains each live element (vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr → the shared element's emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn) in a 0..len loop before freeing the buffer — the port of the non-shared peer's #35 drain. Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_shared_vec_field_index_field_mutation_no_leak (300-iter churn, LSan-clean). Full memory_sanitizer (681) + codegen (2318) + par_codegen/spawn_e2e/cross_task_safe/drop_differential green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: A `shared struct` (or `par struct`) with a `Map[K, shared V]` (or `shared K`) FIELD leaked the shared K/V boxes on drop. The shared-struct RC-drop's MapOrSet arm called the type-erased `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` (whose `map_drop_flags` are 0 for a shared side, so it never touches the RC handles) but SKIPPED the per-bucket `emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk` that the non-shared struct-drop peer already runs — so one ref leaked per live entry.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Valgrind leak-sweep of shared/RC drop siblings after B-2026-07-13-11 (definitely-lost scaling with iters: 16-byte Node boxes, 2/iter). — REPRO (valgrind `definitely lost: 32*N bytes in 2*N blocks`): churn `shared struct Owner { mut cache: Map[i64, Node] }` (Node `shared struct`), each iter `Owner { cache: Map.new() }`, insert 2 Nodes, drop. ROOT: `emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn`'s `SharedFieldKind::MapOrSet` arm (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) only emitted the runtime `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(handle, dk, dv)` call. For a shared K or V, `map_drop_flags` yields 0 (the side isn't an inline String/Vec the runtime can free), and the runtime is type-erased — it cannot see the RC layout — so the shared boxes were never dec'd. The NON-shared struct-drop peer (`emit_struct_drop_synthesis`'s MapOrSet arm) already handles this: it runs `emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk(handle, heap_ty, is_val)` per shared side BEFORE the runtime free. FIX: port that walk into the shared arm — resolve the field's `Map[K,V]` / `Set[T]` K/V TypeExprs (`map_kv_type_exprs` / `set_inner_type_expr`), and for each side whose type is shared (`shared_heap_type_for_type_expr`), emit the per-bucket rc-dec walk before `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec`. `current_fn` is `drop_fn` at that point so the walk's blocks attach to the drop body. A scalar/String Map side is untouched (no walk; the runtime frees inline heap as before). VERIFIED valgrind definitely-lost 0 on the churn; full memory_sanitizer (683) + codegen (2319) + par_codegen/cross_task_safe/drop_differential green.
resolution: FIXED (5070066). src/codegen/synth_drop.rs: emit_shared_struct_rc_drop_fn's MapOrSet arm now runs emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk for each shared K/V side before the runtime bucket free (port of the non-shared peer). Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_shared_struct_map_shared_value_field_no_leak (LSan churn). Full memory_sanitizer (683) + codegen (2319) green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: A `shared enum` with a `Vec[shared T]` PAYLOAD (`shared enum Tree { Branch(Vec[Node]) }`) leaked the element boxes when the enum was dropped with its payload INTACT (RC → 0 while the Branch still holds the Vec). `emit_shared_enum_field_drop`'s Vec/String arm froze the `{ptr,len,cap}` buffer but never drained the shared ELEMENTS — the exact gap B-2026-07-13-11 fixed on the struct side, unfixed on the enum side.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Same valgrind leak-sweep as B-2026-07-13-12 — the shared-ENUM sibling of the B-2026-07-13-11 struct leak. — REPRO (valgrind, DIRECT-drop path — construct + drop, no match-out): churn `shared enum Tree { Leaf, Branch(Vec[Node]) }` (Node shared), each iter build `Tree.Branch(v)` with 2 Nodes and drop `t` at scope exit — `definitely lost: 32*N bytes in 2*N blocks`. ROOT: `emit_shared_enum_field_drop`'s `Some("Vec"|"VecDeque"|"String")` payload arm (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) freed only the Vec data buffer — no `0..len` element-drain. FIX: port the B-2026-07-13-11 drain into this arm — resolve the per-element drop via `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr(vec_inner_type_expr(te))` (a shared element → `emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn`), then loop `0..len` calling it on `data + i` inside the `cap > 0` guard before the buffer free. `None` (bare String payload / `Vec[primitive]`) drains nothing, unchanged. VERIFIED valgrind definitely-lost 0 on the direct-drop churn; full memory_sanitizer (683) incl. all shared/par enum drop tests green (no double-free). SCOPE NOTE: this fixes the enum-drop-with-payload-intact path. The SEPARATE `match t { Branch(xs) => … }` MATCH-MOVE path — where the payload Vec is bound OUT to a local — still leaks its elements and is filed as B-2026-07-13-14 (a different subsystem: the moved-out binding's scope-exit drain, not the enum drop).
resolution: FIXED (5070066). src/codegen/synth_drop.rs: emit_shared_enum_field_drop's Vec/String arm now drains each live element (the shared element's emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn) in a 0..len loop before the buffer free (port of B-2026-07-13-11). Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_shared_enum_vec_shared_payload_drop_no_leak (LSan churn, direct-drop path). Full memory_sanitizer (683) + codegen (2319) green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: Matching a `shared enum` and binding a `Vec[shared T]` PAYLOAD out (`match t { Branch(xs) => … }`) leaks the shared elements. The bound Vec `xs` frees its buffer at scope exit but never dec's the shared elements it holds — unlike a directly-constructed `let v: Vec[shared]` binding (which drains) and unlike the enum dropped WITHOUT matching (B-2026-07-13-13, drains). The move-out payload binding is not registered with the Vec-of-shared element-drain cleanup.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced while gating B-2026-07-13-13: the direct-drop path (build + drop) is LSan-clean, but the same shape MATCHED (`Branch(xs) => …`) leaks the elements. Isolated: a local `let v: Vec[Node]` drains fine at scope exit, and the no-match enum drop drains fine (B-13-13), so the gap is specific to the match-bound payload. — REPRO (valgrind `definitely lost: 32*N bytes in 2*N blocks`; interp correct): churn `shared enum Tree { Leaf, Branch(Vec[Node]) }` (Node shared), each iter build `Tree.Branch(v)` with 2 Nodes, then `match t { Tree.Leaf => {} Tree.Branch(xs) => { let _ = xs.len(); } }`, drop at scope exit. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) build + drop WITHOUT matching → CLEAN (B-13-13's direct-drop drain). (b) a directly-constructed local `let v: Vec[Node]; v.push(...)` → CLEAN (local Vec[shared] scope-exit drain). Only the MATCH-BOUND payload leaks. LIKELY ROOT (unconfirmed): the match arm binds the `Vec[shared]` payload to a local (`xs`) as a MOVE out of the enum box, but that binding is not registered with the per-element rc-dec scope-exit cleanup a normal `Vec[shared]` `let` gets (`track_vec_of_shared`-style), OR both the enum-drop-after-match and `xs`'s drop treat the other as the owner and neither drains — a move-out double-non-drop of the element refs. The buffer itself is freed once (no double-free — a pure leak). NEXT: trace the shared-enum match-arm payload binding in the match-compilation path (control_flow_match.rs / stmts.rs) and give a moved-out `Vec[shared]` payload binding the same element-drain registration a direct `let v: Vec[shared]` binding receives. Not addressed in the B-13-11/12/13 drop-synthesis commit (different subsystem).
resolution: FIXED (05c1383). src/codegen/pattern_binding.rs `bind_pattern_values`: a `Vec[shared T]` (heap-agg element) payload binding moved out of an enum is now tracked with the element-draining `track_vec_of_aggs_var` (resolving the element's `vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` → `emit_vec_elem_rc_dec_fn` for a shared element) instead of buffer-only `track_vec_var`, at the same `!pattern_binding_is_borrow` single-owner point. Safe by the documented move-out symmetry: every path that lets the binding outlive its source already zeros the source (the shared-enum box's Vec `cap` via `suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out`; the value-enum destructure suppressors), so the source's drain is suppressed exactly as its buffer-free already is — no double-free. `String` / `Vec[scalar]` payloads resolve to `None` and keep the buffer-only path. Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_match_move_vec_shared_enum_payload_no_leak (LSan churn). Verified valgrind definitely-lost 0 on the repro + move-further shapes (return-out, re-move to another local) and 0 errors on a String-payload move-out (no double-free). Full memory_sanitizer (684) + codegen (2320) + par_codegen/cross_task_safe/drop_differential/raii_check green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: A `shared enum` with a `Map[K, shared V]` (or shared K) PAYLOAD (`shared enum Store { Full(Map[i64, Node]) }`) leaked the shared K/V boxes when dropped with the payload intact. `emit_shared_enum_field_drop`'s Map/Set arm released the bucket storage via the type-erased `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` but skipped the per-bucket `emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk` — the exact gap B-2026-07-13-12 fixed on the struct-field side, unfixed on the enum-payload side.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Continued valgrind leak-sweep of shared-container-of-shared drop siblings after B-2026-07-13-12/13/14 — the enum-payload sibling of the struct-field Map leak (B-13-12). — REPRO (valgrind `definitely lost: 32*N bytes in 2*N blocks`; direct-drop path — build + drop, no match-out): churn `shared enum Store { Empty, Full(Map[i64, Node]) }` (Node shared), each iter build `Store.Full(m)` with 2 entries and drop at scope exit. ROOT: `emit_shared_enum_field_drop`'s `Some("Map"|"HashMap"|"Set"|"HashSet")` payload arm (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) only emitted the runtime `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec(handle, dk, dv)` call. `map_drop_flags` yields 0 for a shared side and the runtime is type-erased, so the shared boxes were never dec'd. FIX: port the shared-half walk (identical to the struct-drop MapOrSet arm, B-13-12) — resolve the payload's `Map[K,V]` / `Set[T]` K/V TypeExprs (`map_kv_type_exprs` / `set_inner_type_expr`), and for each shared side (`shared_heap_type_for_type_expr`) emit `emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk` before `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec`. `current_fn` is the enum drop fn so the walk's blocks attach correctly; the walk may split the block, so the subsequent free uses the walk's exit position. Scalar/String Map sides untouched. VERIFIED valgrind definitely-lost 0 + 0 ASAN errors on the churn; a nested `Vec[Vec[shared]]` field and the struct-Map (B-13-12) shape re-checked clean; full memory_sanitizer (685) + codegen (2320) + par_codegen/cross_task_safe/drop_differential/raii_check green; fmt+clippy clean.
resolution: FIXED (9f59f09). src/codegen/synth_drop.rs: emit_shared_enum_field_drop's Map/Set payload arm now runs emit_map_shared_half_rc_dec_walk for each shared K/V side before the runtime bucket free (port of the B-13-12 struct-drop fix). Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_shared_enum_map_shared_value_payload_drop_no_leak (LSan churn). Full memory_sanitizer (685) + codegen (2320) green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: Sending an OWNED heap payload (`Vec`/`String`) through a `Channel` and then `recv`-ing it DOUBLE-FREED on native/JIT. `compile_channel_send` memcpy'd the value's `{ptr,len,cap}` header into the type-erased byte queue but never neutralized the SOURCE binding's scope-exit free — so the source `v` freed the buffer AND the `recv`'d binding (an alias of the same header) freed it again. The interpreter moves the value into the channel (single owner), so it was correct; the mismatch is codegen-only.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the concurrency/auto-par surface — a `Channel[Vec[i64]]` send→recv aborted `free(): double free detected` on JIT/native, `6` on the interpreter. — REPRO (interp `6`; JIT/native `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`): `fn main() { let (tx, rx): (Sender[Vec[i64]], Receiver[Vec[i64]]) = Channel.new(); let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); v.push(2); v.push(3); tx.send(v); let got = rx.recv(); let mut s = 0; let mut i = 0; while i < got.len() { s = s + got[i]; i = i + 1; } println(f"{s}"); }`. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) an owned String payload (`let s = f"owned-{1}"; tx.send(s)`) double-frees identically — a string LITERAL source (`tx.send("hi")`) stayed clean by luck (`cap == 0`, its free is a no-op), masking the bug. (b) send WITHOUT recv (`tx.send(v)`, never `recv`) did NOT double-free — the double-free needs the RECV to mint the second owner. ROOT: `src/codegen/channel.rs::compile_channel_send` spilled the arg to a stack slot and `karac_runtime_channel_send`'d `elem_size` bytes onto the queue, then returned Unit — with NO move-suppression of the source. For a heap payload the queue slot ALIASES the source's buffer; `recv` memcpy's it back out to a fresh binding that is ALSO tracked for a scope-exit free. Two owners, one buffer → double-free (the interpreter, which moves `v` into the channel, is the oracle). FIX: `send` is a MOVE — suppress the source binding's scope-exit cleanup at the send site, mirroring the struct-literal field-capture suppressor set (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` for a named Vec/String, `suppress_map_cleanup_for_tail_identifier` for a moved-in Map/Set handle, `suppress_inline_option_result_binding_move`, and `suppress_fstr_acc_if_moved_out` for an f-string temp). The queue becomes the sole owner until `recv` transfers ownership to the receiver. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for owned-Vec, owned-String, and Vec-recv-then-read; LSan-clean over a 100-iter balanced send→recv→use churn of both Vec[i64] and owned String. KNOWN FOLLOW-UP (B-2026-07-13-17): a payload SENT but never RECEIVED now leaks when the channel drops — the type-erased queue can't free heap payloads on drop. Correct move-ownership exposed the missing drain-on-drop; it is a strictly less severe state than the double-free (a rare-shape leak vs common-case corruption).
repro: fn main() { let (tx, rx): (Sender[Vec[i64]], Receiver[Vec[i64]]) = Channel.new(); let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); tx.send(v); let got = rx.recv(); println(f"{got.len()}"); }
resolution: FIXED (eb8db8d). src/codegen/channel.rs `compile_channel_send` now suppresses the source binding's scope-exit cleanup (Vec/String/Map-Set/inline-Option-Result/fstr) — `send` moves the payload into the queue, so the source no longer frees a buffer the recv'd binding owns. Test: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_channel_send_recv_heap_payload_no_double_free (100-iter balanced Vec+String churn). Follow-up leak filed as B-2026-07-13-17. Full memory_sanitizer (692) + codegen (2332) + concurrency/par_codegen/spawn_e2e/cross_task_safe green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: A heap payload (`Vec`/`String`/…) SENT on a `Channel` but never RECEIVED leaks its buffer when the channel is dropped. The runtime channel is a type-erased byte queue (`runtime/src/channel.rs`) — it memcpy's element bytes and cannot know a slot holds a heap `{ptr,len,cap}`, so its drop frees the queue storage but not the payload buffers still queued. Before B-2026-07-13-16 this was masked (the un-suppressed source freed the buffer, leaving the queue a dangling alias); correct move-ownership now surfaces it as a genuine leak.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Exposed by the B-2026-07-13-16 fix: once `send` correctly MOVES a heap payload into the channel (the source no longer frees it), a payload sent but never received has no owner to free it when the channel drops. — REPRO (valgrind `definitely lost: 32 bytes in 1 block` per unreceived Vec; interp clean): `fn main() { let (tx, rx): (Sender[Vec[i64]], Receiver[Vec[i64]]) = Channel.new(); let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); v.push(2); tx.send(v); println("sent"); }` (never `recv`s). A balanced send→recv program is clean (the receiver owns and frees) — only the send-more-than-recv-then-drop shape leaks. ROOT: `karac_runtime_channel_*` stores/loads raw bytes with no per-element drop; `CleanupAction::DropChannelEnd` refcount-drops the ends and the last drop frees the `KaracChannel`, but the free doesn't walk remaining queued slots as typed heap values. FIX DIRECTION (not yet done — needs a runtime-API change): thread the element's drop fn into the channel (at `Channel.new` from the element type, or a per-send drop pointer) so the channel's drop drains unreceived heap payloads via that fn — the channel analog of the `track_vec_of_aggs_var` / `emit_free_one_map_handle` element-drop machinery the Vec/Map cleanups use. Bounded but touches `runtime/src/channel.rs` + the channel codegen; deferred from the B-13-16 double-free fix (which is the high-severity half). Severity medium: a rare shape (send without matching recv, then drop) and a recoverable leak, not corruption. FIXED 2026-07-14 (768cda1): threaded the element's drop fn into the channel. Runtime (channel.rs): KaracChannel gains an elem_drop AtomicPtr fn-pointer + a karac_runtime_channel_set_elem_drop setter, and a new Drop impl walks any still-queued blobs running the drop fn on each (freeing the payload heap). Received payloads were dequeued (owned by the receiver) so they're not on the queue at drop — no double-free; null drop fn (scalar payload) is a no-op. Codegen (channel.rs): each heap-payload send (te_owns_heap_below_buffer) records the element's karac_drop_<T> fn via the setter; scalar channels never call it. Keep-list (lib.rs) preserves the new symbol for the JIT runner. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean for unreceived / balanced / partial-recv / String / (no-regression) scalar channels; runtime unit tests (drop-drains-only-unreceived, null-noop) + two memory_sanitizer regressions added; codegen (2340) + par_codegen (159) + memory_sanitizer (703) green; fmt + native/wasm(seq+threads) clippy clean.
repro: fn main() { let (tx, rx): (Sender[Vec[i64]], Receiver[Vec[i64]]) = Channel.new(); let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); v.push(2); tx.send(v); println("sent"); }

title: Reassigning a `Vec[shared]` / `Vec[Option[shared]]` local variable (`current = next`) LEAKS the shared elements of the OVERWRITTEN old Vec (x86-visible; the BFS-worklist `current = next` idiom)
detail: Overwriting a `Vec` local whose element type is a shared struct/enum (`Vec[Node]`) or `Vec[Option[shared]]` with `current = next` does NOT rc-dec the elements the OLD `current` still held before dropping it — the old buffer is freed but its per-element release is skipped, so every shared node the overwritten Vec referenced leaks (LSan/valgrind `definitely lost`, x86-64). Distinct from B-2026-07-12-29 (`work[i] = work[j]` INDEX-assign on a shared Vec element, arm64-only, fixed 63d618e): this is a WHOLE-VARIABLE reassignment and reproduces on x86. The scope-exit drop of a `Vec[shared]` correctly releases elements (a pushed-then-dropped Vec is clean); only the ASSIGNMENT-overwrite path misses the old value's element-release. DISCRIMINATOR: pushing a shared node into a `Vec[shared]` and letting it drop at scope exit is CLEAN; adding a single `current = next` overwrite leaks the element the old `current` held. MINIMAL REPRO (96 B leaked = the whole 3-node tree; interp clean): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] } fn main() { let root = Some(Node { val: 1, left: Some(Node { val: 2, left: None, right: None }), right: None }); let mut current: Vec[Node] = Vec.new(); match root { None => {} Some(n) => { current.push(n); } } let mut next: Vec[Node] = Vec.new(); match current[0].left { None => {} Some(l) => { next.push(l); } } current = next; println(f"len: {current.len()}"); }`. CLEAN COUNTERPART: the same program without the `current = next` line. FIX AREA: the Assign arm's Identifier-target path (src/codegen/stmts.rs) must, when the target is a `Vec[shared]` / `Vec[Option[shared]]` local, run the old value's element-releasing drop (`karac_drop_Vec_<elem>` / the emit_option_drop_fn per-element walk that B-2026-07-11-29 wired) BEFORE storing the new Vec — the same old-value-release that a shared FIELD store already performs, applied to a Vec-of-shared variable overwrite. Same systemically-under-supported Vec-of-shared surface as B-2026-07-11-29 / B-2026-07-12-4 / -29. WORKAROUND: use a queue-free formulation (recursive DFS with a depth index; the shipped kata #102 uses DFS-with-depth + level-by-level DFS, both byte-identical across all surfaces and valgrind-clean). Surfaced by kata #102 (binary-tree level-order): the canonical BFS builds each next level and does `current = next`, leaking the whole tree once per traversal. FIXED 2026-07-14 (924cd05): the Assign identifier-target path now runs the OLD value's element-releasing walk before the store when the Vec element carries a non-trivial per-element drop. New helper emit_owned_vec_element_release_on_overwrite reconstructs the exact FreeVecBuffer cleanup action vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr derives for the scope-exit path (rc-dec for a shared struct/enum; tag-guarded karac_drop_Option_<payload> for Option[shared]) and emits it immediately via emit_cleanup_action, then skips the buffer-only free. So a fixed overwrite and a scope-exit drop release a shared element identically and never twice (the moved-in source's own cleanup is cap-zeroed by the paired move-suppression). Scalar/String/plain-Vec elements need no per-element drop so the helper returns false and the cheaper buffer-only free runs unchanged. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean for Vec[shared], Vec[Option[shared]], the full while-BFS level-order loop, and (no regression) scalar + String Vecs; two memory_sanitizer regressions added; codegen (2339) + memory_sanitizer (698) + non-codegen suites green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: `iter().fold(init, |acc, x| ...)` with a HEAP accumulator (`String`; a Vec accumulator hits a separate type-inference wall) double-frees the accumulator buffer on native/JIT. The idiomatic string-join fold is the headline shape. Scalar accumulators (`sum`, numeric fold) are unaffected; a 0-element fold (init returned, no iteration) is clean — the double-free is per-iteration, from the accumulator reassignment.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the iterator-chain surface — `v.iter().fold(String.from(""), |acc,x| f"{acc}-{x}")` (a string JOIN) aborted `free(): double free` on JIT/native, correct on the interpreter. — REPRO (interp `-1-2-3`; JIT/native `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`): `fn main() { let v: Vec[i64] = [1,2,3]; let j = v.iter().fold(String.from(""), |acc, x| f"{acc}-{x}"); println(j); }`. Even a single-element source double-frees; 0 elements is clean; the fold result being discarded still double-frees (so it is the per-iteration reassignment, not the final move-out). CRITICAL DISCRIMINATOR: the HAND-WRITTEN, typechecked equivalent is fully correct AND leak-clean — `let mut acc = String.from(""); for x in v.iter() { acc = f"{acc}-{x}"; } ` (self-referential) and `{ let cur = acc; acc = f"{cur}-{x}"; }` (separate bind) both match the interpreter under valgrind (definitely-lost 0). ROOT: `try_compile_iter_chain_fold` (src/codegen/method_call.rs) desugars the fold in CODEGEN — i.e. AFTER typecheck — into synthetic AST (`let mut __facc = init; for x in base { let acc = __facc; __facc = fold_body }; __facc`) that is then `compile_expr`'d. Those synthetic nodes were never individually typechecked (they all reuse the fold call span), so codegen's ownership/move analysis for the String accumulator lacks the per-node metadata the equivalent hand-written loop has: the accumulator reassignment frees the old buffer while a co-owner (the `acc` bind, or the f-string read of the accumulator) frees it too → double-free. INVESTIGATED — three desugaring restructures all FAILED to fix it, proving the root is the missing metadata, not the statement shape: (a) flat `let acc = __facc; __facc = fold_body` → double-free; (b) nested `__facc = { let acc = __facc; fold_body }` → the double-free becomes an INTERMEDIATE-accumulator LEAK (the middle B_1..B_{n-1} buffers, ~n-1 leaked per fold); (c) self-referential `__facc = fold_body` with the closure's `acc` param used directly as the accumulator var (verified clean HAND-WRITTEN) → still double-frees when synthesized. FIX DIRECTION (not a quick tweak): lower `fold`/`sum`/the fused-iterator terminals BEFORE typecheck (a pre-typecheck desugar or `src/lowering.rs` restructuring that runs early enough) so the synthetic accumulator nodes are typechecked and carry correct String/move metadata — the only way the ownership analysis matches the hand-written loop. Alternatively thread explicit type/move metadata onto the synthesized nodes. WORKAROUND for users today: hand-write the accumulator loop (`let mut acc = ...; for x in <iter> { acc = ... }`) — it compiles correctly. Scalar-accumulator folds (`sum`, numeric fold) are unaffected and stay on the fused path. FIXED 2026-07-14 (763e244): typechecker records the fold accumulator TypeExpr span-keyed (iter_terminal_acc_types), threaded through lowering to codegen; the synthetic accumulator `let` is stamped with that annotation so a heap accumulator registers as a tracked String/Vec, and the desugar now lowers to the self-referential `acc = f"{acc}-{x}"` shape (closure acc param used directly as the accumulator var) — the exact shape a hand-written loop uses — instead of a fresh `__facc` + `let acc = __facc` move that zeroed the cap and defeated the eager-free (the intermediate-leak variant b). The direct form is used only when `acc` can't collide with a receiver name; the rare colliding heap case defers to --interp rather than emit an unsound lowering. Scalar folds (sum/count/numeric) stay on the proven fresh-name path. Two memory_sanitizer regression tests added (string-join fold + fused map+fold); verified double-free-AND-leak-clean under valgrind and ASAN; codegen (2338) + memory_sanitizer (695) + full non-codegen suite green; fmt+clippy clean.
repro: fn main() { let v: Vec[i64] = [1, 2, 3]; let j = v.iter().fold(String.from(""), |acc, x| f"{acc}-{x}"); println(j); }

title: The `?` operator applied to a `Result[Option[T], E]` (an Option NESTED inside the Result's Ok) loses the Option's payload type/value: the extracted `Option[T]` is truncated to its first word and left untyped, so a subsequent `match { Some(s) => … }` on it fails codegen — `Undefined variable 's'` when the arm returns the binding (`Ok(s)`), or `no handler for method 'len' on variable 's'` when it calls a method (`s.len()`). LOUD codegen failure (compile error, not a silent wrong answer or crash); the interpreter handles it. Plain `?` on a scalar-Ok Result, and matching an Option produced any OTHER way, both work — the trigger is `?` on a Result whose Ok payload is itself an Option.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the ?-operator surface — `let opt = inner(n)?` where `inner() -> Result[Option[String], E]`, then `match opt { Some(s) => Ok(s) }`, failed codegen; the interpreter is correct. — REPRO (interp `got-7`; JIT/native `error: codegen failed: Undefined variable 's'`): `enum E { X }\nfn inner(n: i64) -> Result[Option[String], E] { if n < 0 { return Err(E.X); } Ok(Some(f"got-{n}")) }\nfn outer(n: i64) -> Result[String, E] { let opt = inner(n)?; match opt { Some(s) => Ok(s), None => Ok(f"empty") } }`. Method-call variant (`Some(s) => Ok(s.len())`) fails `no handler for method 'len' on variable 's'` instead — so `s` is registered but UNTYPED. DISCRIMINATORS: (a) `?` on `Result[i64, E]` then a SEPARATE `match mk(a) { Some(s) => … }` (Option from an independent call) → CLEAN. (b) inline `match inner(n)? { Msg.Text(s) => … }` over a PLAIN user enum payload → CLEAN. (c) matching an Option produced without `?` (`let opt = mk(n); match opt { Some(s) => … }`) → CLEAN. (d) an explicit annotation `let opt: Option[String] = inner(n)?` does NOT help. Only `?` on `Result[Option[T]]` breaks. ROOT: `reconstruct_question_ok_payload` (src/codegen/exprs.rs) recovers the Ok-payload type from the recorded type at the `?` operand span (the `?` result type == the unwrapped Ok payload). For `Result[Option[String]]?` that payload IS `Option[String]`, but the arm's DEFENSIVE guard (added by B-2026-07-11-7 to avoid rebuilding a mistakenly-recorded Result/Option WRAPPER from 3 payload words) matches any `Option`/`Result`-headed recorded type and returns just `w0` — so a LEGITIMATE `Option[T]` Ok payload is truncated to one word and its type is not threaded to the binding. The subsequent match can't determine the Some payload type, so the `s` binding is unregistered/untyped. The guard cannot currently distinguish 'the Ok payload is legitimately an Option' from 'the wrapper was mistakenly recorded' — both look like `Option[...]` at the span. FIX DIRECTION: give the `?` lowering a way to tell the two apart — e.g. record the `?` result (Ok-payload) type under a DISTINCT key from the wrapper, or carry the wrapper-vs-payload distinction so `reconstruct_question_ok_payload` rebuilds a genuine `Option[T]` payload (and the binding is typed `Option[T]`) while still guarding the wrapper case. WORKAROUND: split the nesting — `match inner(n) { Ok(Some(s)) => …, Ok(None) => …, Err(e) => return Err(e) }` (match the Result directly, no `?`), or return the inner value through a non-Option intermediate. FIXED 2026-07-14 (75dd66c): the typechecker's resolve_question now records the unwrapped Ok/Some payload TypeExpr in a DEDICATED single-write table (question_ok_payload_types), keyed by the `?` span, threaded through lowering to codegen. reconstruct_question_ok_payload consults it FIRST — always the genuine payload, never the span-colliding wrapper — so a legit nested Option[T]/Result[T,E] Ok payload is rebuilt as the real multi-word value (the Option's 4-word type-erased layout via the flat-i64 copy path) instead of truncated to w0. The B-2026-07-11-7 wrapper guard is retained as a fallback for any `?` the dedicated table doesn't cover. Verified interp==JIT==AOT for String/Vec/scalar nested payloads (value-return AND method-call arms), valgrind + ASAN clean; codegen E2E + ASAN regression tests added; codegen (2339) + memory_sanitizer (696) + non-codegen suites green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: A closure with a BLOCK body whose tail is a local built by a collection/String constructor (`|| { let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); v }`) had its return type mis-inferred as `i64`, so the closure fn was declared `-> i64` while its body returned a `{ptr,len,cap}` Vec/String aggregate → LLVM verifier 'Function return type does not match operand type of return inst'. Capture-independent (fails with or without a captured value). A single-EXPRESSION closure body (`|| make()`) was unaffected — it resolved via the module-fn return type.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Fresh dogfood find probing the closure-with-heap surface — `|| { let mut v = Vec.new(); …; v }` failed LLVM module verification; the interpreter is correct. — REPRO (interp `120`; JIT/native `Module verification failed: "Function return type does not match operand type of return inst!  ret { ptr, i64, i64 } %out, i64"`): `fn main() { let base: Vec[i64] = [10,20,30]; let build = || { let mut out = Vec.new(); let mut i = 0; while i < base.len() { out.push(base[i]*2); i = i+1; } out }; let r = build(); println((r[0]+r[1]+r[2]).to_string()); }`. Also fails for a String tail (`|| { let s = String.from("hi"); s }`). DISCRIMINATORS: (a) single-expression body `|| make()` where `make() -> Vec[i64]` → CLEAN (the Call arm resolves the module fn's return type). (b) no capture needed — `|| { let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(1); v }` fails identically. ROOT: `infer_closure_return_type` (src/codegen/closures.rs) computes the closure fn's declared return type from the body's tail BEFORE compiling it. For a block body it resolves a bare-Identifier tail (`out`) to its `let out = Vec.new()` value and infers THAT — but the Call arm only recognized module-fn calls, enum-variant construction, and lowered operator dispatch; a collection/String constructor `Vec.new()` (a 2-segment `Path` callee) fell through to the `i64` fallback. So the declared return type was `i64` while the compiled body returned the real aggregate. This is the exact sibling of the StructLiteral-body arm (B-2026-07-12-2) and the closure-body-is-a-closure arm (B-2026-07-12-12), both added for the same verifier failure on other body shapes. FIX: recognize the collection/String constructors in the Call arm — `Vec`/`VecDeque`/`String`/`CString` `.new`/`.with_capacity`/`.from`/`.from_vec` → the `{ptr,len,cap}` vec_struct_type; `Map`/`HashMap`/`Set`/`HashSet`/`SortedMap`/`SortedSet` constructors → the opaque handle `ptr`. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for a Vec-returning closure (with capture), a String-returning closure, and a String-block-with-reassign closure; leak-clean under valgrind; full codegen (2335) + memory_sanitizer (693) green.
repro: fn main() { let base: Vec[i64] = [10, 20, 30]; let build = || { let mut out = Vec.new(); let mut i = 0; while i < base.len() { out.push(base[i] * 2); i = i + 1; } out }; let r = build(); println((r[0] + r[1] + r[2]).to_string()); }
resolution: FIXED (be02fd6). src/codegen/closures.rs infer_closure_return_type now recognizes collection/String constructors (Vec/VecDeque/String/CString .new/.with_capacity/.from/.from_vec → vec_struct_type; Map/Set family → ptr) in the 2-segment-Path Call arm, so a block-body closure whose tail is `let v = Vec.new(); … v` is declared with the correct heap return type instead of the i64 fallback. Test: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_closure_block_body_returns_heap_collection. Full codegen (2335) + memory_sanitizer (693) green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: `infer_closure_return_type` had further gaps for heap-typed closure tails, each declaring the closure fn `-> i64` against a heap body → LLVM verifier 'return type does not match operand type of return inst': (1) a `match` tail (NO Match arm → i64 default); (2) an `if` returning `Some(String)`/`None` (bare `Some`/`None`/`Ok`/`Err` constructors unrecognized → i64); (3) a tuple tail with a block-local heap element (`(v, 100)` where `v = Vec.new()` — the tail resolution only handled a BARE identifier, not a nested position). The B-2026-07-13-20 fix covered a bare `let v = Vec.new(); … v` tail; these are its immediate siblings.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Probing closure-return siblings immediately after B-2026-07-13-20 — three more heap-tail shapes hit the same LLVM verifier failure. — REPROS (interp correct; JIT/native LLVM verifier failure): (1) `|d: Dir| match d { Dir.N => String.from("north"), Dir.S => String.from("south") }` → `ret {ptr,i64,i64} %matchval, i64`; (2) `|n: i64| if n > 0 { Some(f"pos-{n}") } else { None }` → `ret {i64,i64,i64,i64} %ifval, i64`; (3) `|| { let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(9); (v, 100) }` → `ret {{ptr,i64,i64},i64} %elem, {i64,i64}`. ROOT (src/codegen/closures.rs::infer_closure_return_type): (1) no `ExprKind::Match` arm — a match tail fell to the `_ => i64` default; (2) bare `Some(x)`/`None`/`Ok(x)`/`Err(x)` (an `Identifier`-callee `Call`, or bare `None` identifier) weren't recognized as `Option`/`Result` construction, so they inferred `i64`; (3) the block-tail resolution only re-typed a BARE-identifier tail against the block's `let` bindings, so a block-local referenced from a nested position (a tuple element) stayed at the i64 fallback. FIX: (1) add a `Match` arm inferring from the first arm's body (all arms share a type by typecheck; a block-body arm recurses); (2) map `Some`/`None` → the `Option` enum layout and `Ok`/`Err` → the `Result` layout (type-erased wrapper aggregate, not the payload); (3) generalize the `Block` arm to build an EXTENDED inference scope from all the block's `let` bindings (annotation, else infer the value, seeing earlier lets) and infer the tail under it — so a block-local resolves whether the tail is a bare identifier or a nested position. VERIFIED interp==JIT==native for the match/if-Option/tuple shapes; full codegen (2336) + memory_sanitizer (693) green; fmt+clippy clean.
repro:
enum Dir { N, S }
fn main() { let m = |d: Dir| { match d { Dir.N => String.from("north"), Dir.S => String.from("south") } }; println(m(Dir.N)); let pair = || { let mut v = Vec.new(); v.push(9); (v, 100) }; let (vv, k) = pair(); println((vv[0] + k).to_string()); }
resolution: FIXED (1a621fe). src/codegen/closures.rs infer_closure_return_type: added a Match arm; recognize Some/None → Option layout and Ok/Err → Result layout; generalized the Block arm to extend the inference scope with all block `let` bindings (so a block-local resolves in a nested tail position, not just a bare-identifier tail). Test: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_closure_heap_return_type_siblings (match tail + if-Option tail + tuple-with-block-local-Vec tail). Full codegen (2336) + memory_sanitizer (693) green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: DOUBLE-FREE: a heap payload MOVED out of a for-loop-over-owned-Vec element (via a match arm, into a function-call argument) is freed twice — once by the callee that took ownership, once by the loop element's per-iteration drop, which is NOT suppressed for the moved-out payload. Minimal repro (native + JIT, double free detected in tcache 2): `enum Part { Text(String), Empty }  fn use_str(s: String) -> i64 { s.len() }  fn main() { let mut parts: Vec[Part] = Vec.new(); let mut text = "".to_string(); text.push_str("hello"); parts.push(Part.Text(text)); let mut n = 0; for p in parts { match p { Part.Text(t) => { n = n + use_str(t); } Part.Empty => {} } } println(n); }`. NARROWING (all three isolate the trigger): (a) SINGLE enum value not from a Vec — `let p = Part.Text(text); match p { Text(t) => use_str(t) }` — is CLEAN (the match's `suppress_destructured_enum_payload_cleanup` correctly zeroes the source cap). (b) Vec iteration but the arm only BORROWS — `Text(t) => t.len()` — is CLEAN. (c) Vec iteration + move-into-call — the failing case. So the double-free needs BOTH the for-loop-owned element AND a move of the bound payload into a call: the match-arm cap-suppression that works for a plain `let`-bound scrutinee does not reach the for-loop ELEMENT's per-iteration drop (compile_for's vec-value path in src/codegen/control_flow_for.rs), so the element's `__karac_drop_<E>` re-frees the buffer the callee already freed. REAL-WORLD SHAPE: the self-hosted lexer's `render` (selfhost/src/main.rs) does exactly this — `for p in parts { match p { Text(t) => { line.push_str(escape_for_render(t)); } ... } }` over the `Vec[InterpPart]` of an f-string token — so any f-string with body text (`f"no holes"`, `f"a{x}"`) aborts, failing the selfhost_lexer oracle. Isolated cleanly: without the `render` call lexing is clean; `f""` (empty, no Text part pushed) is clean. FIX DIRECTION: in the match-statement suppression path (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs, the `suppress_destructured_enum_payload_cleanup` site) OR the for-loop element drop registration (control_flow_for.rs vec-value path), ensure a payload consumed by an arm (moved into a call arg — not just into a `let`/return/constructor) zeroes the loop-element source's cap so the per-iteration EnumDrop skips it. Verifiable on x86 (reproduces natively).
investigation: 2026-07-14: CI showed selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer failing on main. Reproduced locally (x86): the compiled Kara lexer aborts with `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`, NOT a token mismatch. Bisected the corpus to f-string interpolation; split lexing vs rendering (drop the render call — clean), then reduced render's `for p in parts { match p { Text(t) => escape_for_render(t) } }` to the 12-line standalone repro above and narrowed with the (a)/(b)/(c) variants. NOT B-2026-07-13-18 (that is `.fold` with a String accumulator; the selfhost lexer uses no `.fold`). Left open: the fix is a real ownership/drop-suppression change in the loop x match interaction, in code under active concurrent development; filing with the minimal repro so it is actionable. Sibling in spirit to B-2026-07-08-22 (shared-enum match-binding move-out double-free) — both are match-arm move-out suppression gaps, this one on the owned-Vec-element (non-shared) path. FIXED 2026-07-14: added `clone_escaping_owned_agg_loop_var_enum` (src/codegen/control_flow_match.rs) — the loop-element sibling of `clone_owned_vec_index_element` (`v[i]`). When a bare `for`-loop element in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars` (a heap-bearing non-shared user enum) is matched AND an arm's payload ESCAPES (`!no_arm_payload_escapes`), deep-copy the element's payload in place (same `deep_copy_enum_heap_payload_in_place` the `let x = p` whole-move path uses) so the arm extracts from an INDEPENDENT buffer and the container's original element is freed exactly once; `did_clone` forces the clone through `materialize_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee` so no-bind arms / unbound fields free the clone (no leak). Read-only matches stay on the borrow path (drop already suppressed) — the escape gate keeps them unchanged. Verified: 12-line minimal repro + all narrowing variants + the self-hosted lexer f-string shape now run clean; memory_sanitizer 693/0 (no leak/UAF), codegen 2336/0 AOT and 2336/0 KARAC_TEST_JIT parity, fmt + clippy (plain + llvm --all-targets) clean. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::e2e_for_loop_enum_element_match_move_payload_no_double_free. NOTE: this un-masks a SEPARATE self-hosted-lexer oracle divergence on `f16`/`bf16` (filed B-2026-07-14-2) — the double-free fix alone does not fully green selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: CI Codegen-E2E: selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer (double-free); isolated from selfhost/src/lexer.rs interpolated_string + main.rs render — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (for-loop element drop x match-arm payload move)

title: SPEC CONTRADICTION on bare `f16`/`bf16`: the Rust seed lexer (src/lexer.rs, keyword_or_ident, explicit comment ~L1373) treats bare `f16`/`bf16` as ORDINARY TYPE-NAME identifiers (like `f32`/`f64`, resolved to `Type::Float` by the typechecker), emitting `IDENT f16`. The self-hosted lexer (selfhost/src/lexer.rs keyword_or_ident L1429-1430) RESERVES them, emitting `Token.Error('f16'/'bf16' is a reserved future numeric type)`. So `selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer` diverges on the corpus entry `"f16 bf16"` (token line 646: Kāra `ERROR` vs Rust `IDENT f16`). design.md is internally inconsistent: L1025 (Reserved float keywords) says 'any identifier named f16 or bf16 is a compile error in v1', but L2296 writes `let w: Tensor[bf16, [768, 768]]` — using `bf16` as a TYPE NAME, which REQUIRES it to lex as an identifier (a reserved Error token can't be a type name). f32/f64 already lex as identifiers and become types via the typechecker; the Rust seed applies the same coherent, forward-compatible rule to f16/bf16, and it is the test's oracle. RESOLUTION (owner decision — NOT made here): most likely make the self-host lexer match the seed (drop the L1429-1430 reservation so f16/bf16 lex as identifiers) AND fix design.md L1025 to say the RESTRICTION is enforced at the type/binding layer in Phase 7, not the lexer — this keeps `Tensor[bf16,…]` lexable today. The alternative (reserve in BOTH lexers) breaks the L2296 type-usage example and the f32/f64 precedent. Not touched here because it is a language-design call in the F16/BF16 feature owner's domain.
investigation: 2026-07-14: found while fixing B-2026-07-14-1. Once the f-string double-free was fixed, selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer runs to completion and reports the FIRST token divergence at `f16`. Confirmed the seed emits IDENT (grep + the test's own diff output) and the self-host reserves (selfhost/src/lexer.rs L1429-1430), and that design.md L1025 contradicts its own L2296 `Tensor[bf16,…]` example. Left OPEN for an owner spec decision; the double-free fix (B-2026-07-14-1) lands independently. FIXED 2026-07-14: resolved in favor of the Rust seed (the test's oracle) + the L2296 type-usage example. (1) selfhost/src/lexer.rs keyword_or_ident — dropped the `f16`/`bf16` reservation arms so they fall through to `Token.Identifier`, exactly like the seed; a bare `f16` now lexes as an identifier and `Tensor[bf16, …]` type annotations parse. (2) design.md § Reserved float keywords (L1025) — rewritten: f16/bf16 are recognized as built-in float TYPE-NAME identifiers now (lexed as identifiers like f32/f64), NOT lexer-reserved keywords; the reduced-precision float TYPES (arithmetic/casts/backend) remain Phase 7, and using the type before then is a typecheck-layer error, not a lexer error. Verified: selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer passes, and selfhost_parser_items / selfhost_resolver / selfhost_typechecker all green (no downstream regression from the lexer now emitting IDENT).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: selfhost_lexer_matches_rust_lexer, unmasked once B-2026-07-14-1's double-free was fixed — SURFACE NOTE: lexer (Rust seed vs self-host) / design.md spec inconsistency

title: ARM64 LEAK: `let a = m.get(k).unwrap()` where m is `Map[_, shared T]` DOUBLE rc-inc's the fetched node. `Map.get` on a shared value rc-inc's the aliased bucket pointer (src/codegen/maps.rs get arm, the 2fe5c718 fix) to hand the caller a fresh +1 ref; the consuming `.unwrap()` let-site then rc-inc'd it AGAIN because `rhs_yields_fresh_ref` classes `.unwrap()`/`.expect()` as non-fresh (correct for a BORROWED Option, whose extracted payload is an alias that needs an inc to own). So each reader bind netted +2 against a single per-iter/scope dec — every fetched node over-retained by 1, leaking the whole map. The codegen has NO arch conditionals on this path, so the over-retain is present on BOTH arches; x86 CI's memory-sanitizer masked it because the test built a reference RING (`node[(i+1)%k]`) that stayed reachable through a stale stack slot at exit (an LSan reachability false-negative), while arm64's LSan reported it. Isolated (all on arm64 LSan): a loop of bare `let a = m.get(i).unwrap(); a.val` (no push, no graph) leaks exactly the N nodes; a CHAIN graph leaks pre-fix and is CLEAN post-fix; a RING / self-loop leaks pre- AND post-fix (an uncollectable RC cycle — a separate, known limitation, not this bug). FIX (this commit): `rhs_yields_fresh_ref` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) now returns TRUE for `<shared-map>.get(k).unwrap()/.expect()` via the new `unwrap_receiver_is_shared_value_map_get` helper — get already delivered the +1, so the let-site receive-inc is skipped, leaving the single get-inc balanced by the single per-iter dec. The regression test's ring was also corrected to a CHAIN (node0->..->node(k-1)) so it exercises the `let a/b = m.get(k).unwrap()` + `neighbors.push(b)` reader shape without conflating it with RC-cannot-collect-cycles. Verified: full memory_sanitizer suite 693/0 on the native arm64 Linux LSan harness (scripts/lsan-local.sh); ASAN detects double-free arch-independently, so a green arm64 run also rules out any over-DEC (double-free) that removing the second inc might have introduced on x86.
investigation: 2026-07-14: CI memory-sanitizer-arm64 red on asan_map_get_shared_value_in_loop_no_alias_collapse (360B/10 allocs, all INDIRECT). Reproduced on native arm64 Linux via scripts/lsan-local.sh (macOS + x86 both mask it — macOS has no LSan; x86 via the ring-reachability false-negative). Pre-opt IR (compile_to_ir) is arch-identical and the in-module RC drop fns survive opt -O2 identically under either triple, ruling out an ABI/optimizer divergence. Read the loop-body IR: TWO rc_inc on the node refcount per iteration (the get arm's + the unwrap let-site's) vs ONE per-iter dec. Bisected with probe tests (only-a / chain / self-loop) to separate the double-inc (real bug, fixed) from the ring cycle (inherent RC leak, test corrected to a chain). Sibling in spirit to B-2026-07-12-29 (an arm64-only RC leak whose x86 balance came from an ABI path) — same 'x86 masks an RC imbalance the arm64 leg catches' class, here a reachability false-negative rather than an ABI dec.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: CI Memory-sanitizer-arm64: asan_map_get_shared_value_in_loop_no_alias_collapse (LeakSanitizer)

title: A recursive value enum whose variant carries the SAME nested/recursive type in TWO payload fields (`enum Expr { Num(i64), Add(Expr, Expr) }`) emitted every rejection diagnostic TWICE — byte-identical (same span, same message). Two independent sites both looped per payload FIELD instead of per offending RELATIONSHIP: (1) the typechecker's `validate_enum_payload_no_nested_enum` (src/typechecker/derives.rs) fired E_ENUM_NESTED_ENUM_PAYLOAD once per nested-enum field, and the message names only `(variant, head)` at `variant.span`, so `Add(Expr, Expr)`'s two `Expr` fields produced two identical diagnostics; (2) the ownership `check_cycles` graph (src/ownership.rs) accumulated one adjacency edge per field, so `Expr`'s neighbor list was `[Expr, Expr]` and the DFS detected — and reported — the self-cycle once per edge, yielding two identical `ownership cycle detected: Expr → Expr` errors. Net user-visible effect: `karac build` on the shape above printed 4 lines for what is 2 distinct problems. (Also confirmed the run/build divergence on this input — `karac run` stops at the typecheck gate and shows only the E_ENUM_NESTED_ENUM_PAYLOAD hint, while `karac build` continues through ownership and appends its cycle error — is documented, intentional design, not a bug: run is execution-focused/short-circuits at the first fatal class, build/check surface the full diagnostic set. Left as-is.) FIX (this commit): (1) derives.rs dedups per distinct nested-enum head within a variant via a `flagged_heads: HashSet<&str>` — `Add(Expr, Expr)` reports Expr once; a variant with DISTINCT nested types (`Add(Expr, Stmt)`) still reports both. (2) ownership.rs dedups each node's outgoing adjacency by target type NAME (first-occurrence order preserved) before the cycle DFS — a cycle through a given type name is one cycle regardless of how many fields realize the back-edge; genuinely distinct neighbor names (distinct cycles) are preserved. Verified: `karac build` on `Add(Expr, Expr)` now prints exactly one E_ENUM_NESTED_ENUM_PAYLOAD + one ownership cycle; `Add(Expr, Stmt)` prints two distinct nested-enum diagnostics + one cycle. Full typechecker + ownership suites green (2035 passed).
investigation: 2026-07-14: found while auditing the run-vs-build diagnostic surface on a recursive-enum reproducer (`enum Expr { Num(i64), Add(Expr, Expr) }`). `karac build` emitted each of the two rejections twice with identical span+text. Traced to two per-field loops that should be per-relationship: derives.rs `validate_enum_payload_no_nested_enum` (field loop, no dedup on the `(variant, head)` the message actually names) and ownership.rs `check_cycles` graph construction (one edge pushed per field, so a two-field self-reference became two adjacency edges and the DFS emitted the cycle per edge). Both fixed at the emission granularity rather than a render-layer dedup, so genuinely-distinct diagnostics that happen to share text at DIFFERENT spans (or distinct nested types at the same span) are unaffected.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (diagnostic-consistency finding on recursive/nested enum rejection) — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker + ownership (diagnostics)

title: The typechecker SILENTLY ACCEPTED any method name on `Option[T]` / `Result[T,E]`, poisoning the call to `Type::Error` (universally assignable) so it typechecked clean and then failed at runtime. The classic trigger is forgetting to unwrap: `Vec.get(i)` returns `Option[T]`, so `grid.get(i).len()` (where `grid: Vec[Vec[i64]]`) calls a Vec method on an Option — the typechecker passed it, then the interpreter PANICKED (`internal error: entered unreachable code: len() receiver was Value::EnumVariant; the typechecker accepted .len() on a type without one`, src/interpreter/method_call_seq.rs:48) and codegen aborted (`no handler for method 'len'`). `opt.push(9)`, `res.push(9)`, `opt.bogus()`, `res.totally_bogus()` all passed typecheck identically. ROOT: the `None` (method-not-found) arm of `infer_method_call` (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs) errors only for user-defined types; ALL prelude types (`prelude::PRELUDE_TYPES`) kept a 'historical silent fall-through' for a 'partially-implicit method surface', returning `Type::Error` instead of `NoMethodFound`. Same silent-poison hole already closed for numeric receivers (B-2026-07-03-5) and user-defined/distinct types. FIX (this commit): carve `Option` and `Result` out of the leniency via `EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE = ["Option", "Result"]` — their method surface is small and EXHAUSTIVELY modelled (every valid method resolves via a dedicated arm or baked impl BEFORE the fall-through), so a method reaching None is genuinely absent and now emits `NoMethodFound` (with an edit-distance 'did you mean' suggestion). SAFETY (measured, not assumed): enumerated the interpreter's full Option/Result arm set (method_call_optres.rs: unwrap, expect, unwrap_err, expect_err, unwrap_or, map, is_some, is_none, is_ok, is_err) and verified every one — plus the impl-routed and_then/ok_or/unwrap_or_else/filter/ok/map/map_err-resolution — resolves in typecheck WITHOUT reaching None, so no working method is rejected; full example sweep (0 newly-broken), full non-llvm suite (954+ across files), full `--features llvm` codegen suite (2340) all green; the only `.kara` uses of the now-rejected names (`.map_err` x12) are in non-compiled `brainstorming/design_studies/` sketches. The other prelude types (`Vec`, `Map`, `Regex`, …) KEEP the silent fall-through (their surface is not fully modelled) — the pre-existing regression gate `test_method_resolution_prelude_silent_fallthrough_preserved_when_method_absent` was re-scoped from `Option` to `Vec` to keep guarding that remaining leniency. Extending the carve-out to more prelude types is future work gated on modelling their full surface.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing nested collections, wrote `grid: Vec[Vec[i64]]` then `grid.get(i).len()`. Interp panicked, JIT+native rejected at codegen — a 3-way agreement that the shape is unsupported, but the REAL bug was upstream: typecheck should have caught `.len()` on the `Option[Vec[i64]]` that `.get` returns. Minimized to `Option[i64].len()` / `.push()` / `.bogus()` all passing `karac check`. Mapped the blast radius (i64/String correctly reject; Option/Result/Vec/bool wrongly accept) and traced to the prelude silent-poison fall-through in the method-not-found arm. Scoped the fix to Option/Result only after proving (interp arm enumeration + full-suite sweep) that their modelled surface is complete, so tightening introduces no false positive.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (nested-collection `grid.get(i).len()` — Vec.get returns Option) — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (method resolution)

title: Several standard `Option`/`Result` combinators are UNIMPLEMENTED across the stack: `map_err`, `map_or`, `map_or_else`, `take`, `err`, `ok` (Result), `flatten`, `get_or_insert`, `and_then`(Result), `or`/`or_else`, `filter`(unmodelled arg). Before B-2026-07-14-5 they silently typecheck-poisoned to `Type::Error` and then failed at runtime (`method 'X' not found on type 'Result' (no interpreter dispatch arm)` for most; a hard `unreachable!` panic for the `Vec`-shaped `.len()`). After B-2026-07-14-5 they are cleanly rejected at compile time with `no method 'X' on type 'Option'/'Result'`. That is the correct interim behavior (honest fail-fast), but these are useful, expected methods — the `brainstorming/design_studies/*.kara` sketches lean on `.map_err(...)?` chains for error plumbing. IMPLEMENT them end-to-end (typechecker signature + interpreter arm in method_call_optres.rs + codegen arm) and add each to the modelled surface. Until then, `?`-operator error mapping must be written the long way (explicit match). PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (57e33a9, 6e0e59d): most of the family is now implemented. NON-CLOSURE batch (`ok`/`err` Result->Option, `or`/`and` select, `ok_or` Option->Result, `flatten` Option un-nest) is DONE across ALL layers (parser weak-keyword for `.or`/`.and`, typechecker, interpreter, codegen) with interp==JIT parity + valgrind-clean (57e33a9). CLOSURE batch (`unwrap_or_else`, `map_or`, `map_or_else`, `map_err`, `and_then`, `or_else`, `filter`) is DONE in the typechecker + interpreter (the oracle), fully usable via `karac run --interp`; its `karac build`/JIT lowering is a follow-up (currently a clear 'use --interp' codegen error, the same shape `map` uses for heap payloads) (6e0e59d). Verified by `test_option_result_combinators` (interp) + `test_e2e_option_result_combinators_nonclosure` (codegen). STILL OPEN: (a) codegen lowering for the closure batch (closure invocation + per-method basic-block management like `map`; needs the error type `E` threaded for the Result absent-branch closures); (b) the MUTATING combinators `take` / `get_or_insert` (need a place-expr receiver, not yet modelled); (c) codegen for heap (String/Vec/struct) payloads across the whole family (gated to scalar payloads under `karac build`, like `map`). NOTE: Option[i64] is not Copy, so these combinators consume `self`/args — a moved receiver reused later is (correctly) an ownership error. CODEGEN PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (ebe8d0a, 5154158, 6a8994d): the closure batch now has `karac build`/JIT lowering too (scalar payloads, like `map`), so these run under the DEFAULT `karac run` (JIT), not just `--interp`: `map_or`/`and_then`/`filter` (payload `T`), `map_err` (records + reconstructs `E`), and the OPTION forms of `unwrap_or_else`/`map_or_else`/`or_else` (no-arg absent closure). All follow `map`'s reconstruct-payload → invoke-closure → branch/phi shape via new helpers `compile_optres_closure_on` / `compile_optres_closure_noarg`; each verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean + stable over a 150-200 iteration loop (the layout-sensitive-crash class). REMAINING codegen gaps (still `--interp`-only, clear error): the RESULT forms of `unwrap_or_else`/`map_or_else`/`or_else` (their absent closure takes the `Err` value `e`, so they need `E` threaded to codegen — a second span-keyed side-table, since the present-payload slot already holds `T`), and HEAP (String/Vec/struct) payloads across the whole family (scalar-gated under `karac build`, exactly as `map` is). STILL fully open: the MUTATING `take`/`get_or_insert` (place-expr receiver, unmodelled). FIXED 2026-07-14 (a900ba9 + bb05537/6e0e59d/ebe8d0a/5154158/6a8994d/e7047c3): EVERY combinator named in this entry is now implemented end-to-end (typechecker signature + interpreter arm in method_call_optres.rs + codegen arm in calls.rs, on the modelled surface): `map_err`, `map_or`, `map_or_else`, `take`, `err`, `ok`, `flatten`, `get_or_insert`, `and_then` (Option AND Result), `or`/`or_else`, `filter` — plus `and` and `unwrap_or_else`. The parser now accepts `.or(...)`/`.and(...)` (weak-keyword method names). The mutating pair is honest about mutation: `take()` yields the current value and writes `None` back through the receiver's place; `get_or_insert(v)` fills a `None` and yields the payload BY VALUE (Kāra models the result as `T`, not Rust's `&mut T` — a mut-ref result needs the deferred place-ref surface); BOTH are seeded receiver-mutating in the effectchecker builtin table (`Option.take`, `Option.get_or_insert`) per the B-2026-07-14-17 standing rule so the auto-par gate serializes them.
