The Result forms of the absent-closure combinators got `E` threaded to codegen via the new `method_unwrap_err_types` side-table (typechecker→lowering→Program→codegen). All verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean; regression tests: interp `test_option_result_combinators`, codegen `test_e2e_option_result_combinators_nonclosure` / `_closure` / `test_e2e_option_take_get_or_insert`. RESIDUAL (shared with the pre-existing `map`, NOT part of this entry's ask): codegen is gated to trivially-copyable (scalar) payloads — heap (String/Vec/struct) payloads run via `--interp` with a clear error; and `take`/`get_or_insert` codegen requires a named Option binding receiver (a temp has no slot to mutate).
investigation: 2026-07-14: while auditing which Option/Result methods reach the typecheck not-found fall-through (to bound the B-2026-07-14-5 tightening), found `map_err`/`map_or`/`take`/`err`/`flatten`/`get_or_insert` all return the interpreter's graceful 'no dispatch arm' runtime error today — genuinely unimplemented, not merely unmodelled. Recorded as a feature-gap backlog item rather than a compiler defect: the tightening makes their absence visible at compile time, which is the right place to surface it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-14-5 (method-resolution audit) — SURFACE NOTE: stdlib / interpreter / codegen (Option/Result method surface)

title: Two for-loop-over-iterator-adaptor shapes SILENTLY SKIPPED the loop body in codegen (ran zero iterations, produced a wrong answer with no error), while the interpreter ran them correctly: (1) `for p in xs.iter().enumerate()` with a SINGLE-var binding (the 2-tuple destructure `for (i, x) in …` IS handled by the enumerate peel in control_flow_for.rs; the single-var Binding pattern fell through it); (2) `for … in xs.iter().zip(ys.iter())` with ANY pattern (zip for-loops have no codegen lowering — only `zip().collect()` does). Both fell through `compile_for`'s dispatch to the `_ =>` 'Unknown iterable — skip body, return unit' catch-all, which returns `const 0` and never emits the body. Probe: `for p in a.iter().enumerate() { s += p.1 }` over [10,20,30] gave interp=60, JIT/native=0; `for (x,y) in b.zip(c) { s += x*y }` gave interp=140, JIT/native=0. FIX (this commit): convert the silent skip into a LOUD compile error when the unhandled iterable is a `.enumerate()` / `.zip()` method call — matching the codebase's existing policy for unsupported zip chains (`zip().map()` already bails loud; tests/codegen.rs e2e_iter_adaptor_zip_identity_collect). The error names the shape, suggests the working `for (i, x)` destructure for enumerate, and points to `--interp` (which handles all these). This eliminates the silent-wrong-answer soundness hole; PROPER support (materialize the tuple for single-var enumerate; lower zip for-loops in lockstep) is tracked as B-2026-07-14-8. Zero blast radius: no test/example/kata used either shape (only the probe did). Not chosen: the body-augmentation desugar (`for (·i,·e) … { let p = (·i,·e); body }`) — it double-drops a heap element (the container loop AND the synthesized tuple both own/free `·e`), so it is unsafe for `Vec[String]` etc.; deferred to the proper fix. Verified: 3 regression tests + full codegen suite 2343 green. [BROADENED in follow-up]: the initial fix covered only `enumerate`/`zip`, but probing found `skip`/`take`/`chain` (and the whole lazy-adaptor family) reach the SAME silent-skip fall-through and miscompiled identically (`for x in v.iter().skip(2).take(3)` gave interp=12/JIT=0; `chain` gave interp=33/JIT=0). The loud-bail set was extended to the full unlowered lazy-adaptor list mirrored from typechecker/stdlib_iter.rs: enumerate, zip, skip, skip_while, take, take_while, chain, step_by(on an Iterator — range step_by stays handled), flat_map, chunks, chunk_by, windows, cycle, scan, peekable, inspect. Handled forms (map/filter fusion, enumerate 2-tuple, RANGE step_by) return before the fall-through and are unaffected. Regression tests updated to a table covering enumerate/zip/take/chain.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing iterator adaptors, `for pair in b.iter().zip(c.iter()) { acc += pair.0 * pair.1 }` gave interp=1400 but JIT/native=0. Bisected: enumerate single-var .0/.1 → JIT skips; enumerate destructure → works; zip single-var AND destructure → both skip; map-producing-tuple single-var → works (iter-chain fusion path). Traced to control_flow_for.rs: the enumerate peel requires a 2-tuple pattern; zip has no peel at all; both reach the `_ =>` silent-skip catch-all. Confirmed body-skip directly with a println side-effect (interp prints 3x, JIT 0x). Chose loud-bail over the risky tuple-materialization desugar.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (enumerate/zip for-loop, interp=oracle vs JIT/native) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (for-loop over iterator adaptor)

title: Proper codegen lowering for the FULL family of iterator for-loop adaptors (enumerate single-var, zip, skip/take/chain, step_by-on-iterator, flat_map, chunks, windows, cycle, scan, peekable, inspect) that B-2026-07-14-7 currently makes bail LOUD (interp handles both): (1) `for p in xs.iter().enumerate()` SINGLE-var binding — needs to materialize the `(index, element)` tuple into `p` so `p.0`/`p.1` work, WITHOUT double-dropping a heap element (the naive `let p = (·i, ·e)` desugar makes the container loop and the tuple both own `·e`); the safe route is a dedicated bind that stores index+element into a tuple alloca and registers `p` as a tuple var with per-iteration ownership handed to the tuple only. (2) `for … in xs.iter().zip(ys.iter())` — needs a lockstep two-source index loop (`while i < min(lenA, lenB)`) binding `(A[i], B[i])` to the pattern, reusing the tuple-materialization + element-drop threading that `zip().collect()` already implements (method_call.rs zip collect arm). Until implemented, the workaround is `for (i, x) in …enumerate()` (destructure) and an explicit index `while` loop for zip, or `--interp`. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (5ec07f8 zip, 83e82e4 enumerate): BOTH priority legs named in this entry are now LOWERED in codegen for the dominant scalar shapes. (1) `for p in xs.iter().enumerate()` SINGLE-var binding — `p` is bound per iteration to a fresh `{i64 index, T element}` tuple struct (`compile_for_enumerate_single_var`), so `p.0`/`p.1` extract via the normal TupleIndex path; exactly the prescribed safe route (ownership handed to the tuple only), with heap elements still bailing loud (the tuple would double-own them). (2) `for (a, b) in xs.iter().zip(ys.iter())` — a lockstep two-source index loop over `0..min(lenA, lenB)` (`compile_for_zip_vec_vars`) binding `A[i]`/`B[i]` to the destructure sub-patterns; named scalar-element Vec sources only, heap elements bail loud. Both verified interp==JIT, stable over 300-400-element loops, valgrind-clean; regressions `test_e2e_for_enumerate_single_var` + `test_e2e_for_zip_two_vecs`; the stale bail-contract cases retargeted to the heap shapes. STILL OPEN (loud bail, `--interp` handles them): skip/skip_while/take/take_while/chain/step_by-on-iterator/flat_map/chunks/chunk_by/windows/cycle/scan/peekable/inspect, plus the HEAP-element legs of enumerate-single-var and zip. SKIP/TAKE LEG 2026-07-14 (a3ca13f): `skip`/`take` window chains over a named scalar Vec are now lowered too (`try_compile_for_skip_take_chain` folds the chain innermost-first into clamped `[start, end)` bounds and runs the by-value index loop; arbitrary nesting order and over-length counts verified interp==JIT; regression test_e2e_for_skip_take_window). Remaining open: take_while/skip_while (predicate forms), chain, step_by-on-iterator, flat_map, chunks/chunk_by/windows, cycle, scan, peekable, inspect, plus the heap-element legs. CHAIN LEG 2026-07-14 (4b4a2f5): `for x in xs.iter().chain(ys.iter())` over two named scalar Vecs is lowered as two sequential index loops sharing one exit block (break in the first leg exits the WHOLE chain; continue per-leg; self-chaining works). With zip + enumerate-single-var + skip/take + chain done, the four adaptors this entry NAMED as priorities are all lowered for the dominant scalar shapes. Remaining open (loud bail, `--interp` handles them): the predicate forms take_while/skip_while, step_by-on-iterator, flat_map, chunks/chunk_by/windows, cycle, scan, peekable, inspect, and the heap-element legs. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (d415269): the PREDICATE legs `take_while`/`skip_while` are now LOWERED — and unlike the index-window legs, they landed in the SHARED fused-chain desugar (`peel_fused_map_filter_chain` + `build_fused_chain_body`, method_call.rs), so every consumer got them at once: for-loops over adaptor chains AND the fold/sum/count/reduce/for_each/any/all fused terminals, composing with map/filter in any order. `take_while` compiles to `if pred { rest } else { break }`; `skip_while` to a hoisted pre-loop latch flag with `if !flag and pred {} else { flag = true; rest }` (short-circuit `and` stops re-evaluating the predicate once latched). HEAP (String) elements work through these legs (the desugar binds elements exactly like the existing filter fusion) — broader than the scalar-gated zip/enumerate/chain/skip-take legs. Verified interp==JIT==AOT (17-case scalar program + heap/label/materialized-binding program), valgrind-clean, 15x stress; regression `test_e2e_for_take_while_skip_while`; the predicate bail-contract case retargeted to `flat_map`. STILL OPEN (loud bail, `--interp` handles them): flat_map/chunks/chunk_by/windows/cycle/scan/peekable/inspect/step_by-on-iterator, plus the HEAP-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain/skip-take. NOTE: lowering these surfaced a PRE-EXISTING interp-vs-codegen closure-capture divergence on lazy adaptors — ledgered as B-2026-07-14-20. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (95e6562): `take`/`skip`/`step_by`/`inspect` are now fused-chain steps in the SHARED peel too — for-loops AND every fused terminal (fold/sum/count/reduce/for_each/any/all) handle them in any composition with map/filter/take_while/skip_while, over scalar and heap elements, Vec/range/chars sources, and materialized bindings; counter arithmetic mirrors the collect engine's stage shapes exactly. The cheap skip/take index-window path and the strided-range `step_by` arm keep dispatch priority for the shapes they cover. Two interp-parity clamp fixes rode along: the index-window fold treated NEGATIVE counts as huge unsigned values (take(-1) yielded EVERYTHING, skip(-1) NOTHING — now signed-clamped to 0 per the interp's n.max(0)), and the collect engine's step_by stride was unclamped (`.step_by(0).collect()` trapped SIGFPE on `% 0` — now clamped to 1 per the interp's n.max(1)). Regression `test_e2e_for_step_by_inspect_take_skip_fused`. STILL OPEN (loud bail, `--interp` handles them): flat_map/chunks/chunk_by/windows/cycle/scan/peekable, plus the HEAP-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (f7130b2): `flat_map` for-loops LOWERED via a nested-loop desugar (`__fml_N: for p in recv { for pat in inner { body' } }`, control_flow_for.rs) — the closure param is the outer loop var, and an exhaustive-match AST walker (`retarget_unlabeled_breaks_*`, no wildcard arm so future AST variants fail the build) retargets unlabeled `break`s in the body onto the synthesized outer label so they exit the WHOLE flat sequence; unlabeled `continue` is naturally correct (next flat element IS the inner loop's next iteration); breaks in nested user loops stay local. Both sides compose with fused chains; heap inner elements work. Labeled flat_map loops bail loud (labeled `continue` means next-flat-element — inexpressible without rewriting user labels). Regression `test_e2e_for_flat_map`; the old flat_map bail case (which actually died at TYPECHECK, never testing the codegen bail) retargeted to the labeled-loop shape. STILL OPEN (loud bail, `--interp` handles them): chunks/chunk_by/windows/cycle/scan/peekable for-loops, flat_map terminals beyond collect (fold/sum/count over flat_map chains), plus the HEAP-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (d68486a): flat_map TERMINALS lowered too — fold/sum/count/reduce/for_each/any/all treat a peel-rejected flat_map receiver (single simple closure param, whitelisted inner, peel-accepted outer) as a ZERO-STEP base; the synthesized `for <elem> in <recv>` then routes through compile_for's nested-loop flat_map desugar. Verified interp==JIT==AOT on all eight terminals + mapped-inner composition, valgrind-clean; regression `test_e2e_flat_map_terminals`. The B-8 remaining tail is now: chunks/chunk_by/windows/cycle/scan/peekable for-loops, adaptors AFTER flat_map in a chain, and the HEAP-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain — all loud-bail to `--interp`. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (01dae34): `cycle` for-loops LOWERED — a restart loop around one full pass of the source chain, with a yielded-flag guard (a pass yielding NOTHING ends the loop, matching the interp's empty-template stop) and per-pass adaptor-state reset (take(2).cycle() == the interp's fresh template clone); unlabeled breaks retarget to the outer label via the flat_map walker, labeled loops bail loud. Verifying parity surfaced B-2026-07-14-22 (the interp for-loop's eager iterator drain HUNG on unbounded cycles — fixed in the same commit, lazy per-iteration pulls). Remaining tail: chunks/chunk_by/windows/scan/peekable for-loops, adaptors AFTER flat_map/cycle in a chain, and the HEAP-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain — all loud-bail to `--interp`. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (ac20c62 scan, 431b69a windows/chunks): three more legs lowered. `scan` for-loops (direct `Some((new, out))` bodies — the collect desugar's shape with the user body as sink; ONE loop, so break/continue/labels are natural and state advances before the body; conditional-None early-stop bodies still bail loud). `windows(k)`/`chunks(k)` for-loops over named scalar Vecs (each group materialized as a FRESH Vec[T] per the interp's per-pull allocation; clamp parity pinned — chunks(0)==chunks(1), iterator-variant windows(0)==windows(1) per the interp's n.max(1) dispatch clamp, k>len yields nothing; auto-par-safe, verified under a parallelized binary). REMAINING tail now: `peekable` (pointless as a bare for-iterable), `chunk_by`, heap-element legs of zip/enumerate-single-var/chain/windows/chunks, adaptors chained AFTER flat_map/cycle/scan/windows/chunks, scan's early-stop body form, and labeled flat_map/cycle loops — all loud-bail to `--interp`. FIXED 2026-07-15 (ff89fcf completes the tail; principal commits d415269/95e6562/f7130b2/d68486a/01dae34/ac20c62/431b69a) — the iterator for-loop adaptor surface named by this entry is now LOWERED end-to-end: map/filter/take_while/skip_while/take/skip/step_by/inspect/peekable as fused steps (for-loops + all eight terminals, any composition); enumerate (destructure + scalar single-var); zip/chain/skip-take windows over named Vecs with ANY element type (loop-borrow marking extends the single-source heap machinery); flat_map/cycle/scan/windows/chunks/chunk_by via structural desugars — labeled loops included (user label on the outer loop, labeled continue renamed to the inner), and all six structural adaptors compose as fused-chain BASES so adaptors chained after them lower too. DECLINED SHAPES (loud bail naming `--interp`, each with rationale): scan's conditional-None early-stop body (typed Option match reconstruction on synthetic AST — the B-16 payload class); enumerate SINGLE-var heap elements (tuple would double-own; the `for (i, x)` destructure form handles heap); zip single-var binding; chain with a non-Vec side; computed-key chunk_by over HEAP elements (the synthesized key binding leaks one buffer per boundary compare — found by valgrind pre-commit, gated instead of shipped); destructuring closure params anywhere in a chain. Known benign divergence: chunk_by re-evaluates the key at both sides of each boundary (interp caches one key/element) — observable only with side-effecting key closures. Regressions: test_e2e_for_adaptor_tail_complete + the per-leg tests; bail contracts retargeted to the declined shapes.
investigation: 2026-07-14: split out of B-2026-07-14-7. The collect-terminal zip lowering already exists and pairs sources element-wise with heap-aware cloning; adapting that lockstep index loop to drive a user body (instead of pushing into an accumulator) is the natural implementation path. Enumerate single-var needs the tuple-alloca bind to avoid the heap double-drop that blocks the trivial desugar.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-14-7 — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (for-loop over iterator adaptor)

title: `for x in xs.iter_mut()` (mutable iteration) is an explicitly-DEFERRED feature (typechecker/lowering.rs: 'the future .iter_mut()') that the typechecker nonetheless ACCEPTS, and the two backends then failed INCONSISTENTLY: the interpreter errors loudly at runtime ('method iter_mut not found on type Vec (no interpreter dispatch arm)'), but CODEGEN SILENTLY SKIPPED the loop body — `for x in v.iter_mut() { *x = *x * 10 }` over [1,2,3] left `v` unchanged and printed the ORIGINAL sum with NO error (JIT/native agreed on the wrong answer; a println side-effect inside the body never fired, proving zero iterations). Root: the `.iter()` for-loop peel in codegen/control_flow_for.rs handles `iter`/`into_iter` but not `iter_mut`, so `for x in v.iter_mut()` fell through to the `_ =>` 'Unknown iterable — skip body' catch-all. FIX (this commit): codegen now bails LOUD on `for … in …iter_mut()` — matching the interpreter's loud failure and the loud-bail policy applied to the other unlowered adaptors (B-2026-07-14-7) — with a message pointing at the supported in-place-mutation form (`for i in 0..xs.len() { xs[i] = … }`, which works correctly on all backends, verified). Both backends now fail on iter_mut instead of one silently miscompiling. Zero blast radius: no Kāra for-loop over iter_mut in any test/example/kata. Proper mutable-iteration support (interp dispatch arm yielding mut refs + codegen mut-ref element binding with `*x = …` write-back) is a feature gap tracked as B-2026-07-14-10.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing in-place mutation, `for x in v.iter_mut() { *x = *x * 10 }` gave interp=runtime-error but JIT/native=unchanged-collection (sum 10 not 100). Added a println side-effect: the body never ran under JIT, confirming a silent loop-skip (same class as the adaptor silent-skips, different method). Confirmed iter_mut is deferred by design (lowering.rs comment) yet typecheck-accepted. Chose codegen loud-bail (consistent with interp) over a premature feature implementation.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (for x in v.iter_mut(), interp vs JIT/native) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (for-loop over iter_mut) + interpreter (iter_mut unimplemented)

title: Implement `for x in xs.iter_mut()` end-to-end: (interp) add an `iter_mut` dispatch arm that yields a mutable reference to each element so `*x = …` writes back into the Vec; (codegen) peel `iter_mut` like `iter` in control_flow_for.rs but bind the loop element as a `mut ref` (a pointer into the Vec's `{ptr,len,cap}` storage at `data + i*stride`) rather than by value, and route `*x = …` deref-assignment to that pointer. The typechecker already types the element as `mut ref T` (lowering.rs). Until then, both backends fail loudly (B-2026-07-14-9) and the workaround is an index loop `for i in 0..xs.len() { xs[i] = … }`. Applies to Vec (and by extension Slice / VecDeque). Cross-check leak/drop behavior for heap element types under ASAN once implemented. PROGRESS 2026-07-14 (7d88499): the INTERPRETER now implements `for x in xs.iter_mut()` — a new `Value::VecSlotRef { storage, index }` (holding the element storage's shared `Arc<RwLock<Vec<Value>>>` handle directly) is yielded per element; `Env::get` auto-derefs it to the live element and `Env::set` writes through to `storage[index]`, so `*x = …` / `*x += 1` land in the live Vec (same choke-point treatment as `MapSlotRef`). The for-loop intercepts a `<vec>.iter_mut()` iterable and materializes the slot-refs instead of value copies. Verified via `test_for_iter_mut` (deref-assign, compound `*x += 1`, second pass, String element); usable now via `karac run --interp`. STILL OPEN: the CODEGEN (`karac build`/JIT) lowering — bind the loop element as a `mut ref` (pointer into `data + i*stride`) and route `*x = …` deref-assignment to it (control_flow_for.rs); it currently bails loud with a message that now points at `--interp`. Heap-element leak/drop behaviour under ASAN to be cross-checked when the codegen leg lands. CODEGEN LEG DONE 2026-07-14 (a9ea16e) — B-10 is now FIXED end-to-end. `karac build`/JIT lowers `for x in xs.iter_mut()` for a named Vec binding with a SCALAR element and a simple loop variable (`compile_for_vec_var_iter_mut`, control_flow_for.rs): the loop variable is bound as a MUT-REF slot pointer into the Vec's storage (`data + i*stride`, restored per iteration) and registered in `entry_slot_ref_vars`, so the existing deref machinery routes `*x` reads, `*x = v` stores, and `*x += 1` compound stores through the live element pointer — writes land in place. Verified interp==JIT (i64 + f64, `*x =` and `*x +=`, two sequential passes), stable 12/12 + valgrind-clean over a 500-element loop, and CORRECT under a default auto-par build with an independent sibling group (the B-2026-07-14-17 hazard class). Regression: codegen `test_e2e_for_iter_mut_scalar` + interp `test_for_iter_mut`. RESIDUAL: heap (String/Vec/struct) elements and destructure patterns bail LOUD to `--interp` (which handles all shapes) — a heap element written through the ref needs the old payload dropped first, the same deferred heap leg the Option/Result combinator codegen shares.
investigation: 2026-07-14: split out of B-2026-07-14-9. The by-value-vs-by-ref element binding already exists for the read `.iter()` path (compile_for_vec_var binds Copy scalars by value); iter_mut needs the pointer-binding variant plus deref-store lowering. The typechecker/lowering already models the `mut ref T` element type, so the work is backend-only.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-14-9 — SURFACE NOTE: interpreter + codegen (iter_mut)

title: `Vec[Vec[T]].get(i).unwrap()` loses the inner `Vec[T]` type in codegen: a subsequent `.len()`/`.get()`/index on the result fails LOUD with `no handler for method 'len' on variable 'row'` (bound) / `on non-identifier receiver` (inline). The interpreter handles it (oracle sum=33). Precisely isolated — these WORK: `g[i].len()` (index), `let row = g[i]; row.len()` (index bind), and even plain `let o: Option[Vec[i64]] = Some(v); o.unwrap().len()` (explicitly-typed Option-of-Vec unwrap). Only the chain where the Option comes FROM `.get(i)` on a nested Vec fails: codegen does not derive that `Vec[Vec[T]].get(i)` yields `Option[Vec[T]]` and thus `.unwrap()` yields a `Vec[T]`, so the binding is never registered in `vec_elem_types` and method dispatch on it falls through. FIX DIRECTION: thread the outer Vec's element type through the `.get()`→`Option[elem]`→`.unwrap()`→`elem` chain when registering the let-binding / inline-receiver type (the typechecker's `expr_types` already carries the correct `Vec[i64]` at the `.unwrap()` span — codegen should consult it to register the Vec, same shape as B-13-2/B-13-10 heap-binding threading). LOUD error, not a miscompile; workaround is the index form `g[i]`. Companion known-limitation seen alongside: `-> ref T` method results (`box.get()`) must be `let`-bound before use (B-2026-06-07-5), also loud. FIXED 2026-07-14 (0910b5f): Vec/Slice `.get`/`.first`/`.last` return `Option[ref elem]` and PACK the element VALUE, so the fix (a) reconstructs the `.unwrap()`/`.expect()` payload at the pointee's VALUE shape by peeling the `ref` when the receiver is a value-packing borrow accessor (calls.rs) and (b) registers the let-binding's collection type from the PEELED element (`borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_heap_inner`, stmts.rs) so `row.len()`/`.get(j)` dispatch, treating the shallow alias as BORROW-ELIDED (no scope-exit drop; the container stays sole owner - no double-free). This is the Vec sibling of the Map `get().unwrap()` B-2026-07-14-15 borrow-elision. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean (no leaks/invalid-reads/double-free) for get/first/last on `Vec[Vec[i64]]` and `Vec[String]` (incl. heap-constructed strings); codegen (2349) + memory_sanitizer (707) green; the large_n sort_by struct-field tests stay green; fmt+clippy clean. Regression tests: codegen `test_e2e_vec_get_first_last_unwrap_heap_element` + ASAN `asan_vec_get_unwrap_heap_element_no_double_free`.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing nested Vec via `.get().unwrap()`. `let row = g.get(i).unwrap(); row.len()` → codegen 'no handler for len on variable row'; `g.get(i).unwrap().len()` inline → 'non-identifier receiver'. Bisected against index form (works) and plain Option[Vec].unwrap() (works), pinpointing the loss to the `Vec[Vec].get()` element-type derivation. Filed as loud feature gap; deprioritized vs silent-miscompile finds.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (nested-collection grid.get(i).unwrap()) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (method-chain element-type threading)

title: A GENERIC function that consumes an owned HEAP param MORE THAN ONCE leaks exactly one heap buffer (the original param), in the AOT/native binary (interpreter is leak-free as oracle). Exhaustively isolated with valgrind (single-threaded, `definitely lost`, authoritative): LEAKS — `fn dup[T](x: T) -> Vec[T] { v.push(x); v.push(x); v }` with T=String (2 bytes 'hi'); `fn repeat[T](x: T, n) { while i<n { v.push(x) } }` (constant 1 block regardless of n≥2 — a 15-char string leaks 15 bytes, confirming it is ONE whole String, i.e. the original param, not a per-iteration leak). CLEAN — the same generic fn pushing x ONCE (x is MOVED, not cloned); the NON-GENERIC `fn dupS(x: String)` double-push; a generic param of STRUCT-with-Vec-field type (the struct arm of make_aggregate_param_callee_owned covers it). So the trigger is precisely {generic monomorph} × {owned builtin-heap param: String/Vec} × {used ≥2 times so it is CLONED per use, never move-consumed}. ROOT (hypothesis, from src reading — not yet IR-confirmed): on the mono path (mono.rs ~1724) an owned Path param routes through `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned`, which returns FALSE for builtin String/Vec (only user struct/enum get the entry-copy + tracked scope-exit drop) — so the generic String param gets NO callee-owned drop. When the param is used once it is move-consumed (its buffer transfers into the sink, balanced); when used ≥2× codegen defensively CLONES it at each push and the ORIGINAL param buffer is neither moved out nor dropped → leaked. The non-generic path avoids the leak (either it move-consumes the last use, or it tracks the String param's drop) — the two must be reconciled. FIX DIRECTIONS (both need ASAN/valgrind + the move-suppression pairing to avoid a DOUBLE-FREE on the move-consumed single-use case): (A) register the builtin-heap param's scope-exit buffer drop (track_vec_var/track_string_var equivalent) on the mono path, relying on the existing move-suppression to disable it when the param IS moved out — mirror whatever the non-generic path does; (B) make the generic param's LAST use a move rather than a clone (matches non-generic, also saves a clone). Must verify against the B-35 family regressions (return-owned-T-param double-free, B-2026-07-13-… ) since this is the same subsystem. NOT a miscompile (values are correct — `w.len()`==n, contents right); a pure leak. FIXED 2026-07-14 (6a51fe9): compile_generic_call now materializes a caller-scope drop for a fresh-heap String TEMP arg (a fn-return / non-binding), mirroring the non-generic compile_call — the mono clones a String param into its owned copy (and again when forwarding to the return), orphaning the caller's temp, which the generic path never dropped. Gated to String via string_typed_exprs (a named-binding arg keeps its own let-drop, excluded by expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp; a Vec[T] param temp move-aliases into the sink so materializing it would double-free). NOT under the flows_into_return passthrough guard, since the mono clones-not-moves the String even when forwarding it. CAVEAT: the filed dup/repeat examples multi-consume the param (v.push(x); v.push(x) / loop push) and are REJECTED by `karac check` (ownership: x moved twice) — so the production-reachable manifestation is the SINGLE-use temp (passthru(mk()), single push-into-container), which this fixes; a Vec[T]-param temp is clean when ownership-valid (the double-free only appeared on the ownership-rejected double-push, behind the check gate). memory_sanitizer regression added over both single-use shapes; interp==JIT + valgrind-clean; codegen (2344) + memory_sanitizer (702) green; fmt+clippy clean.
investigation: 2026-07-14: found probing `repeat[T](x, n)`. valgrind on the native binary: 2 bytes definitely lost for a String repeat; interp leak-free. Bisected: generic-vs-nongeneric (only generic leaks), push-once-vs-twice (only ≥2 uses leak), String-vs-struct-with-heap-field (only builtin-heap String/Vec leaks — struct is covered by make_aggregate_param_callee_owned's struct arm), loop-vs-straightline (both leak, so it is use-count not the loop). Constant 1-block leak scaling with the String's content length pins it to a single un-freed String = the original param. Read param_own.rs + mono.rs: make_aggregate_param_callee_owned returns false for String/Vec, so the mono owned-Path-param entry-copy+drop is a no-op for builtin heap params. Filed rather than fixed: the safe fix must pair callee drop with caller-side move-suppression to avoid converting the leak into a double-free on the single-use (move-consumed) path; high-risk area (the B-35 leg 3b was a return-owned-T-param DOUBLE-FREE), warrants dedicated IR-verified work.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (repeat[T](x, n) generic heap-param multi-push, valgrind) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (generic monomorph owned heap-param drop)

title: A slice pattern (`[]`, `[a, b]`, `[first, .., last]`) matched on a `ref Vec[T]` / `ref Slice[T]` PARAMETER silently mis-dispatched in codegen — the wrong match arm ran, so JIT and native produced WRONG answers (or no output) while the interpreter (oracle) was correct. Isolated exhaustively: LOCAL Vec scrutinee and OWNED-param scrutinee both dispatch correctly; only the `ref`-param scrutinee is broken. Repro: `fn f(v: ref Vec[i64]) -> i64 { match v { [] => 0, _ => 1 } }` on an EMPTY vec gave interp=0 but JIT=1 (never matched `[]`); `match v { [a,b] => a+b, _ => -1 }` on `[3,4]` gave interp=7 but JIT=-1. ROOT: `resolve_slice_source` (src/codegen/control_flow_slice.rs) built the SliceSource by `build_struct_gep(vec_ty, slot.ptr, …)` directly on the variable's alloca. For a by-value/`let` Vec the alloca IS the `{ptr,len,cap}` struct, but a `ref Vec` PARAM's alloca holds a POINTER to the caller's struct — so the GEP read the borrow pointer's own bits as `{ptr,len,cap}`, yielding a GARBAGE length. The slice-pattern length check (compile_slice_pattern_condition) then compared against garbage and took the wrong branch every time. FIX (this commit): deref the borrow once (`build_load(ptr_ty, slot.ptr)`) when the scrutinee name is in `self.ref_params`, before GEPing data/len — the same `ref`-param deref pattern already used for SoA params (collections.rs). Applied to both the Vec and Slice arms. Verified interp≡JIT≡native on `[]`/`[a,b]`/`[first,..,last]` over empty/pair/longer vecs; LOCAL + OWNED-param cases unchanged (no regression); full codegen suite 2345 green. NOTE (separate, NOT fixed here): slice-pattern element bindings type as `ref T` and do not coerce to `T` in an arm body (`[x] => x` where the arm returns `i64` errors 'expected i64, found ref i64') — consistent across all backends (a typecheck/ergonomics gap, not a miscompile); left for a follow-up.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing slice patterns, `fn describe(v: ref Vec[i64]) { match v { [] => .., [x] => .., [first,..,last] => .. } }` returned 'empty' for ALL inputs under JIT and produced no output under native, vs correct interp. Bisected scrutinee kind: LOCAL Vec (works), OWNED param (works), REF param (broken) — pinning the fault to the borrow deref. Read resolve_slice_source: it GEP'd slot.ptr unconditionally; the SoA-param path in collections.rs showed the canonical ref-deref. One-line-per-arm fix; ASAN/codegen-suite clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (slice pattern on ref Vec param, interp=oracle vs JIT/native) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (slice-pattern match on borrowed scrutinee)

title: Two related slice-pattern typechecker ergonomics gaps, both CONSISTENT across interp/JIT/native (so not miscompiles — just friction). (1) BINDING TYPE: an element binding in a slice pattern types as `ref T`, not `T`, and does not coerce in the arm body — `match v { [x] => x, .. }` where the arm must yield `i64` errors `expected 'i64', found 'ref i64'`; the user must write `*x` or rebind. Arithmetic auto-derefs (`[a, b] => a + b` works), so it is inconsistent: `+` derefs but a bare tail/return position does not. Likely the same `ref T` element-binding shape as the for-loop borrow element, which DOES bind Copy scalars by value (lowering.rs `is_borrow_copy_scalar`) — the slice-pattern binder should apply the same Copy-scalar-by-value rule. (2) EXHAUSTIVENESS: `match v { [] => .., [head, ..] => .. }` is genuinely exhaustive (empty + non-empty covers every length) but the checker reports `non-exhaustive match: pattern '_' not covered`, forcing a dead `_` arm. Holds for owned Vec, ref Vec, and Slice scrutinees alike. The slice-pattern exhaustiveness logic does not model `[]` + `[_, ..]` (or `[_, ..]` + `[..,  _]`) as covering the length space. FIX DIRECTION: (1) apply the Copy-scalar-by-value binding rule to slice-pattern leaves (mirror the for-loop element binder); (2) extend match-exhaustiveness for slice patterns to recognize an empty-slice arm plus an open-ended `[head, ..]`/`[.., tail]` arm as total — carefully, since a WRONG widening here would ACCEPT a genuinely non-exhaustive match (soundness), so it must be conservative (only the exact `[] + [_, ..]` / `[_, ..] + []` complementary pair). Deferred rather than rushed for that soundness reason. FIXED 2026-07-14 (ffa33c3): Part 2 (exhaustiveness) — a conservative length-coverage recogniser (slice_patterns_cover_all_lengths, src/exhaustive.rs) accepts a Vec/Slice match whose IRREFUTABLE slice patterns tile all lengths (an open-ended [a..,..] covers [m,inf); every length in 0..m covered by a closed arm), run BEFORE the open-domain wildcard-required fallthrough. Sound: literal/nested-ctor elements don't count, so [head,..] alone / []+[a] / []+[5,..] still report non-exhaustive; operates on the lowered matrix so guards + variant-name element bindings are handled. Part 1 (element binds as ref T in tail position) was ALREADY resolved upstream (all repro variants pass). Regression + soundness tests added; typechecker (2039) + exhaustive suites green.
investigation: 2026-07-14: while fixing B-2026-07-14-13 (slice-pattern-on-ref-Vec miscompile), the reduced repros surfaced these two orthogonal typecheck gaps. Both reproduce identically under `karac check` regardless of backend, so they are front-end (typechecker), not codegen. Filed as ergonomics backlog; the exhaustiveness half is soundness-sensitive so it is explicitly NOT a quick widen.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (slice-pattern ergonomics, surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-14-13) — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (slice-pattern binding types + exhaustiveness)

tracker: none yet — RELATED to B-2026-07-14-11 (.get().unwrap() heap-value family); flagged for the agent in that area
title: CRASH: `let r = m.get(k).unwrap()` on a `Map[K, V]` whose VALUE is a NON-shared heap type (`Vec`, `String`) DOUBLE-FREES under JIT and native (`free(): double free detected in tcache 2`), while the interpreter (oracle) is correct. Binding ALONE triggers it — the result need not be used. Confirmed for `Map[String, Vec[i64]]`, `Map[i64, Vec[i64]]`, and `Map[String, String]`; `Map[String, i64]` (scalar value) is CLEAN; building/dropping the map without the get is CLEAN. ROOT: `map.get(k)` returns `Option[ref V]` (a BORROW of the bucket's value). For a heap value the `.unwrap()` let-binding `r` is registered as an OWNED Vec/String (gets a scope-exit buffer drop) but its `{ptr,len,cap}` is a SHALLOW ALIAS of the map's stored value — so at scope exit BOTH `r`'s drop AND the map's value-drop free the same buffer → double-free. (Scalar values carry no buffer, so no crash; that is why `Map[_,i64]` is clean.) The binding must be treated as a BORROW (no owned drop — the map still owns the buffer) OR deep-cloned. This is the NON-SHARED sibling of B-2026-07-14-3 (which fixed the SHARED-value map-get over-retain via `unwrap_receiver_is_shared_value_map_get` + `rhs_yields_fresh_ref`, stmts.rs) — that fix is shared-specific and does NOT cover a plain heap value. It is ALSO a sibling of B-2026-07-14-11 (`Vec[Vec].get().unwrap()`), but a DISTINCT symptom: B-11 fails to register `r` as a Vec at all → LOUD `no handler for method 'len'` (no crash); THIS registers `r` as a Vec AND an owned drop → silent-until-exit DOUBLE-FREE. FIX DIRECTION: at the `let x = <map>.get(k).unwrap()` binding (stmts.rs), when the map VALUE is a non-shared heap type, register `x` as a BORROW for method dispatch (so `x.len()` still works) but SUPPRESS the scope-exit buffer drop — mirror the borrow-return / `ref`-binding no-drop treatment. Deep-clone is the fallback if a borrow lifetime can't be guaranteed. NOT FIXED by me: this touches the same `.get().unwrap()` let-binding path the agent owning B-2026-07-14-11 is in — flagged for coordination to avoid a conflicting edit to stmts.rs. FIXED 2026-07-14 (51618aa): the `<map>.get(k).unwrap()`/`.expect()` bind on a non-shared Vec/String map value is now recognised (is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get_unwrap, stmts.rs) and treated as BORROW-ELIDED — the same no-owned-drop path a shallow-aliased v[i] element takes: the binding keeps its vec_elem_types registration (so r.len() etc. dispatch on the alias) but takes NO scope-exit buffer drop, so the map stays the sole owner and the double-free is gone. The borrow's lifetime is tied to the map by the ownership checker. Shared-value sibling (B-2026-07-14-3, rc_inc) and scalar values (no buffer) are excluded. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean for Map[String,Vec[i64]], Map[i64,Vec[i64]], Map[String,String], Map[String,Vec[String]], scalar Map[String,i64], and .remove().unwrap() (owned, correctly NOT elided); memory_sanitizer regression added; codegen (2345) + memory_sanitizer (703) green; fmt+clippy clean.
investigation: 2026-07-14: found via a leak-check probe on `Map[String, Vec[i64]]`; interp printed the right answer, JIT/native aborted with a glibc double-free. Bisected: scalar-value map (clean) vs heap-value map (crash); build-only (clean) vs build+get (crash); bind-without-use STILL crashes (so it is the binding's registered drop, not a use). Mapped the family across Vec/String values and both key types. Confirmed distinct from B-11 (loud, not crash) and from B-2026-07-14-3 (shared-value refcount, not a plain-heap double-free). Filed + flagged rather than fixed, per the active two-agent split on the get-unwrap area.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (Map[String, Vec[i64]] get().unwrap(), interp=oracle vs JIT/native) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (map-get heap-value binding ownership)

title: CRASH: `let x = v.get(i).unwrap()` / `v.first().unwrap()` on a `Vec[T]` with a SCALAR element (`i64`) reads invalid memory under JIT/native (`Invalid read of size 1`, empty output — crashes before the value is used) while the interpreter (oracle) prints the correct value. `v.get(i)` returns `Option[ref T]` (a borrow of the element); the consuming `.unwrap()`/`.expect()` on a SCALAR-payload borrowed Option mis-lowers the extraction (the size-1 read suggests the discriminant/byte path is walked off a bad pointer). DISCRIMINATORS (all valgrind-confirmed, x86-64): CRASHES — `v.get(i).unwrap()` and `v.first().unwrap()` with element `i64`. CLEAN — the SAME shape with a `String` element (`v.get(i).unwrap()` on `Vec[String]`); a plain index `v[i]` (0 errors); and `match v.get(i) { Some(x) => .. }` (the match, not unwrap, is clean). So the trigger is precisely {borrowed `Option[ref scalar]` from `Vec.get`/`.first`} x {consumed by `.unwrap()`/`.expect()`}. Distinct from B-2026-07-14-11 (`Vec[Vec].get().unwrap()` loses the inner Vec TYPE -> LOUD `no handler for len`) and B-2026-07-14-15 (`Map` get().unwrap() of a HEAP value -> double-free): this is a SCALAR-element Vec.get()/first().unwrap() -> silent invalid-read crash. NOT YET FIXED. FIX DIRECTION (unconfirmed): the `.unwrap()` lowering for an `Option[ref scalar]` produced by `Vec.get`/`.first` must deref the ref to load the scalar (or the borrowed-Option payload extraction must yield the pointee), not read a byte off the option layout. Common idiom, high severity (crash on a routine access pattern), but users have the `v[i]` / `match` workarounds. UPDATE 2026-07-14 (0910b5f, String leg): the STRING value leg is now FIXED alongside B-2026-07-14-11 - the unwrap reconstruction peels a leading `ref`/`mut ref` to the 3-word `{ptr,len,cap}` Vec/String pointee when the receiver is a value-packing borrow accessor (`get`/`first`/`last`), so a heap String recovers its len/cap (previously collapsed to just the data pointer, working only by luck for a null-terminated literal), AND the let-binding is registered + borrow-elided so `x.len()` dispatches with no double-free. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean for heap `Vec[String]` get/first/last().unwrap(). REMAINING OPEN: only the USER-STRUCT element leg (`Vec[Struct].get(i).unwrap()`), deliberately excluded here because a blanket <=3-word struct peel regressed test_e2e_large_n_sort_by_named_struct_field (some genuine `Option[ref Struct]` payloads hold a real pointer); the struct leg needs producer-aware value-vs-ref packing intent threaded to the struct reconstruction. Severity of the remaining struct leg is silent-wrong (not the original scalar crash). STRUCT LEG FIXED 2026-07-14 (290bfa1): the user-struct element leg is now closed, so B-16 is FULLY FIXED across all element kinds (scalar 10fbd6e, Vec/String 0910b5f, struct 290bfa1). `Vec.get`/`.first`/`.last` PACK the element VALUE, so the unwrap reconstruction now peels the `ref` to ANY StructType pointee under the producer-aware value-packing-accessor gate (calls.rs) — rebuilding the struct value (inline <=3-word AND boxed >3-word) instead of `inttoptr`'ing a bad pointer (which read every field back 0/garbage; the sort_by struct-field E2E tests passed only VACUOUSLY, insensitive to the bad reads). A heap-owning struct binding from this borrow chain shallow-aliases the container's element buffers, so its scope-exit struct-drop is SKIPPED (`is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct`, stmts.rs) — the container's per-element drain stays the sole owner (the struct sibling of the Vec/String `borrow_elided` path); a pure-value struct's drop is a no-op so it needs no suppression. Verified interp==JIT + valgrind-clean (no leaks/invalid-reads/double-free) for pure-value (Point), heap-field (Person{name:String}), and boxed (5-field) structs across get/first/last; the large_n sort_by struct-field tests now read real values and stay green. codegen (2350) + memory_sanitizer (708) green; fmt+clippy clean. Regression tests: codegen test_e2e_vec_get_first_last_unwrap_struct_element + ASAN asan_vec_get_unwrap_struct_with_heap_field_no_double_free. FOLLOW-UP FIX 2026-07-14 (13b3081): the initial struct-leg landing (290bfa1) carried a LATENT layout-sensitive crash — its borrow-elision predicate `is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct` probed a drop-fn SYNTHESISER (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr`) for its Some/None, which moved the LLVM builder mid-let-codegen (a 100-element Vec[Score] sort read via get().unwrap() crashed ~50-100% depending on binary layout; valgrind masked it). The predicate is now pure (`struct_types.contains_key`). Verified stable 25/25 AOT + valgrind-clean; codegen (2352) + memory_sanitizer (708) green.
investigation: INVESTIGATION 2026-07-14 (IR-confirmed root; a naive fix REGRESSES, so left open). Dumped the codegen IR: `Vec.get(i)`/`.first()` return `Option[ref T]` but the get.valid block PACKS THE ELEMENT VALUE into the payload words — `load i64 *elem` -> opt.w0 for a scalar; for a String it loads the {ptr,len,cap} and packs ptrtoint(ptr)->w0, len->w1, cap->w2. The consuming `.unwrap()` (try_compile_option_result_method, calls.rs ~1658) reconstructs at `llvm_type_for_type_expr(inner_te)` where inner_te=`ref T` — and `ref`/`mut ref` ALWAYS lower to `ptr` (types_lowering.rs:356) — so rebuild_value_from_payload_words hits the PointerType arm and `inttoptr`s w0. Scalar: inttoptr(value) = bogus ptr -> `Invalid read` crash on use. String: inttoptr(ptrtoint(data_ptr)) = the data ptr ONLY, DROPPING len/cap (works by luck for a null-terminated string LITERAL; a heap String loses its length). COUPLED with B-2026-07-14-11: even with a correct value, the let-binding is SIZED as a 1-word ptr (bind_pattern_types, patterns.rs:1554 leaves `ref` bindings unrecorded -> `alloca ptr`), so a String value (3 words) would overflow the slot AND `x.len()` fails to dispatch (`no handler for len`) because x isn't registered as a String. ATTEMPTED FIX (reverted): peeling a leading `ref`/`mut ref` from inner_te before reconstruction fixes the scalar crash AND the String value AND makes `v.get(i).unwrap()` chained through a map bind clean (msimple/mmulti) — BUT it REGRESSES test_e2e_large_n_sort_by_named_struct_field: some genuine `Option[ref T]` payloads DO hold a real pointer (the sort_by named-struct comparator path), where inttoptr is correct. So the reconstruction cannot blanket-peel by type — it must know whether THIS producer packed a value (Vec.get/first/last, Map.get-of-nonshared) or a pointer. CORRECT FIX DIRECTION: thread the producing-method / value-vs-ref packing intent to the unwrap (e.g. a span-keyed 'payload is a value not a ref' flag set where Vec.get/first pack the value), OR fix at the SOURCE so `Vec.get(i)` on a value element returns `Option[T]` (value) rather than `Option[ref T]` — and pair it with the B-11 binding-registration so the slot is sized/dispatched as T. Scope: calls.rs unwrap reconstruction + Vec.get/first codegen (collections.rs) + bind_pattern_types (typechecker) — a coupled B-11+B-16 change. Scalar crash confirmed on `let x=v.get(i).unwrap()` and `v.first().unwrap()`; `match v.get(i)` and `v[i]` stay clean. PARTIAL FIX 2026-07-14 (10fbd6e): the SCALAR-element crash leg is fixed — the unwrap reconstruction now peels a leading ref/mut ref when the pointee is a SCALAR (int/float/bool/char), rebuilding the value instead of inttoptr'ing it. Regression-free (no producer packs a scalar's ADDRESS in Option[ref scalar]). The STRUCT/String value legs remain OPEN (they need the coupled reconstruction + binding-registration + borrow-elision change that a blanket peel regressed on the sort_by struct comparator); severity effectively downgraded from crash to silent-wrong for struct/String get().unwrap().
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe, surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-14-15 (Map get().unwrap()) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Iterator/Vec .get(i)/.first().unwrap() on a SCALAR element)

title: `Vec.clear()` (and the in-place mutators `fill` / `swap` / `swap_remove`) were INVISIBLE to the auto-parallelizer's write-dependency gate: the effectchecker builtin seed table (src/effectchecker.rs) seeded push/insert/sort/sort_by/sort_by_key/reverse/pop/remove `allocates(Heap)` but NOT clear/fill/swap/swap_remove, so `method_effects_imply_receiver_mutation("clear")` returned false and the DEFAULT auto-par build treated `a.clear()` as read-only on `a`. This is the exact residual B-2026-07-02-8 flagged in its own note ('any future builtin mutating method must be seeded or it silently races — clear...'). REPRO (memory-safety): two `Vec[i64]`/`Vec[String]` in `main` — `a.push(..)*N; a.clear(); println(a.len()); let b=...; b.push(..); println(b.len())`. The second Vec gives the auto-parallelizer an independent statement group to co-schedule the `a.len()` read with, so `a.clear()` (which frees the buffer, drops the heap elements, and resets the `{ptr,len,cap}` header to `{null,0,0}`) raced its sibling read: at -O0 `a.len()` read the stale pre-clear branch-capture header (printed the un-cleared len every run — 4 instead of 0, 100% deterministic), and at -O2 the scope-exit cleanup for `a` freed the buffer `clear` had ALREADY freed — a DOUBLE-FREE (`free(): double free detected in tcache`, SIGABRT). Single-vec `clear` was correct (no independent group to race into); interpreter was correct (no auto-par); `KARAC_NO_AUTOPAR=1` was correct (gate bypassed). Same missed-serialization class as B-2026-07-02-8 (sort/reverse/pop/remove), B-2026-06-20-16 (map entry writes), B-2026-07-09-12 (parser cursor advances). FIX: seed `Vec.clear` / `Vec.fill` / `Vec.swap` / `Vec.swap_remove` `allocates(Heap)` in the builtin seed table (a conservative non-pure MUTATION marker, not a literal alloc claim — matching the over-approximate pop/remove seeds). The gate matches on the `.{method}` name suffix, so the `Vec.clear` seed also serializes `Map.clear` / `Set.clear`; `Vec.insert` / `Vec.extend` were already covered by the existing `Map.insert` / `Map.extend` suffix matches. Verified: the two-vec repro prints `0` (JIT + -O2 build), no double-free; regression tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_vec_clear_under_autopar_no_double_free (ASan-clean under default auto-par, looped over heap String elements). RESIDUAL (unchanged): any FUTURE builtin in-place mutator must still be seeded here or it silently races — the standing hazard this class keeps re-surfacing.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: Surfaced while implementing Vec.truncate (Phase 8): a single-vec truncate/clear worked, but adding a SECOND Vec to main produced a wrong length at -O0 and a double-free at -O2. KARAC_NO_AUTOPAR=1 made it correct, pinning the cause to auto-parallelization. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (auto-par write-dependency gate)

title: `Tensor.matmul(other)` and `Tensor.transpose()` are PHANTOM methods: the typechecker ACCEPTS them (returns a Tensor type, so `a.matmul(b).sum()` type-checks) but NEITHER backend implements them — `karac run`/interp fails at runtime with `method 'matmul' not found on type 'Tensor' (no interpreter dispatch arm)` and codegen/build fails with `no handler for method 'matmul'`. Both LOUD (not a silent miscompile), but the typechecker's acceptance is misleading. COMPOUNDING: matmul's typechecker RESULT TYPE does not preserve rank — `let c = a.matmul(b); c[0, 0]` is rejected with `index must be an integer or range, found '(i64, i64)'` (2D indexing needs a rank-2 Tensor type; matmul's inferred result is not one), whereas the element-wise (`a + b`) and `reshape` results ARE correctly rank-2-indexable and WORK end-to-end. Confirmed working Tensor methods: `sum`, `mean`, `reshape`, `shape`, `from`, element-wise `+`/`*`, 2D index `m[i, j]`. Confirmed phantom: `matmul`, `transpose`. FIX DIRECTION: implement `matmul` (2D @ 2D → 2D contraction) and `transpose` (axis-permute) in the interpreter (Tensor method dispatch) and codegen (Tensor method arm), and fix matmul's typechecker return type to a rank-2 `Tensor` so its result is indexable — OR, if these are deferred, reject them at typecheck with a clear 'not yet implemented' message (like the numeric/Option tightening) rather than accept-then-fail-at-runtime. Since they are core, clearly-intended tensor kernels (not typos), implementing is preferred over rejecting. FIXED (this commit): implemented end-to-end. (1) TYPECHECKER: routed both through the tensor shape-method intercept (expr_method_call.rs) with new infer_tensor_transpose (result dims = reversed; any rank — rank-1 is the identity, NumPy semantics) and infer_tensor_matmul (rank-2 × rank-2 only in v1, numeric + matching element types, static inner-dim check when both concrete literals; result Tensor[T, [m, n]]) in expr_method_tensor.rs — the result is now properly rank-typed so c[0, 0] indexes. (2) INTERPRETER: eval_tensor_transpose (reversed-axes permute walk) + eval_tensor_matmul (runtime rank/inner-dim guards re-emitted per module policy; triple-loop contraction; integer tensors accumulate in i64, float in f64) in method_call_tensor.rs. (3) CODEGEN: compile_tensor_permute factored into a perm-driven core (compile_tensor_permute_with_perm); transpose derives its static rank from the lowering side-table result entry and delegates; compile_tensor_matmul emits runtime rank-2 + inner-dim guards (emit_tensor_guard), allocs the [m, n] result, and lowers the triple loop with float/int arithmetic by element LLVM type; both wired into try_compile_tensor_transform with the matmul ARG resolved like a receiver (identifier binding slot vs compiled value) and freed when a fresh temp (chained forms like a.matmul(b.transpose())). (4) OWNERSHIP: surface signatures added to runtime/stdlib/tensor.kara (transpose(ref self); matmul(ref self, other: ref Tensor[T, S]) — both operands read, not moved) so the ownership checker models the arg as a borrow; PLUS a use-classifier fix (is_tensor_borrow_arg_method_call): in a CHAIN (a.matmul(b).transpose()) the parser's MethodCall.span == receiver.span aliasing let the outer call clobber the inner's method_callee_types entry, so the mode lookup resolved the inner matmul to Tensor.transpose (zero params) and b false-rejected as moved — name-matched short-circuit (the relational-method precedent) is immune to the collision; covers matmul + the broadcast_* family (same latent bug). Verified: interp≡JIT≡native on square/non-square/int matmul, rank-1/2/3 transpose, chained forms; valgrind + ASAN leak-clean including chained fresh-temp frees; full codegen (2364) + memory_sanitizer (709) + typechecker/interpreter suites green.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing Tensor ops. `Tensor.from(...)` + index + `sum`/`mean`/`reshape`/`shape` + element-wise all work interp≡JIT≡native. `a.matmul(b)[0,0]` typecheck-errored on the INDEX; bisected to find matmul itself is unimplemented (interp 'method not found', codegen 'no handler') and its result type is not rank-2. `transpose` is the same shape. Filed as a Tensor feature gap (loud, not silent), distinct area from the get-unwrap/self-host tracks.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (Tensor.matmul / transpose) — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker + interpreter + codegen (Tensor method surface)

title: The `Regex` feature works in the interpreter (oracle) but is ENTIRELY UNWIRED in codegen — `karac build`/JIT fail LOUD on every Regex operation while `karac run --interp` is correct. Confirmed: `Regex.compile(pat).unwrap()` → codegen `Option/Result method 'unwrap' expected struct receiver, got IntValue i64 0` (the `Result[Regex, RegexError]` Ok payload is an i64 HANDLE, not a struct, and the Option/Result unwrap codegen arm assumes a struct-represented receiver); `.expect()` → same; and even the match path `match Regex.compile(p) { Ok(re) => re.is_match(h) }` → codegen `no handler for method 'is_match' on variable 're'`. So both the Result-of-handle unwrap AND the Regex methods (`is_match`, and by extension `find`/`captures`/`replace`) are absent from codegen. Interp gives `true`/`false` correctly for all. No `Regex` references in src/codegen/ or tests/codegen.rs — it was never ported to the backend. All failures are LOUD (compile errors with the `--interp` hint), NOT silent miscompiles. FIX DIRECTION: wire the Regex runtime handle through codegen — (a) teach the Option/Result unwrap/expect codegen arm to accept a scalar/handle (i64) Ok payload (generalizes beyond Regex: any `Result[<handle-type>, E]`), and (b) add a codegen dispatch arm for the Regex methods (`is_match`/`find`/`captures`/`replace`) calling the same `karac_regex_*` runtime entrypoints the interpreter uses. The unwrap-of-handle-payload half (a) is the more general and higher-value piece. Until then Regex is interp-only.
investigation: 2026-07-14: probing Regex. `Regex.compile('[0-9]+').unwrap().is_match(h)` → interp true/false, codegen 'unwrap expected struct receiver, got i64'. Bisected: the match-without-unwrap path ALSO fails (is_match no handler), and scalar `Result[i64,String].unwrap()` WORKS — so it is specifically the Regex-handle Result payload (i64) for unwrap plus the missing Regex method arms. grep confirms zero Regex in src/codegen. Filed as a codegen feature gap (loud, interp-only). CORRECTION 2026-07-14 (both filed fix-directions rest on FALSE premises; scope re-established, owner-approved to correct the record). (1) The unwrap arm is NOT the problem: `Result[i64,String].unwrap()` works BOTH locally-constructed AND when RETURNED from a fn (`fn make()->Result[i64,String]{Ok(42)}; make().unwrap()` → 42 in codegen). So fix-direction (a) 'teach unwrap to accept a scalar/handle Ok payload' is moot — it already does; the codegen 'expected struct receiver, got i64 0' fires only because the RECEIVER is a null i64 placeholder, because `Regex.compile(...)` is entirely unhandled in codegen and yields `i64 0` (is_null/is_const) as a fall-through. (2) There are NO `karac_regex_*` runtime entrypoints — the interpreter implements Regex IN-PROCESS via the `regex` crate (`use regex::Regex as RustRegex`, `Arc<RustRegex>` stashed as an opaque int in `Value::Struct{name:"Regex"}`); `regex` is a TOP-LEVEL dep only (Cargo.toml L130), NOT in runtime/Cargo.toml. So fix-direction (b) 'call the same karac_regex_* entrypoints the interpreter uses' is impossible — they must be BUILT. REAL SCOPE = a full feature build: (i) add `regex` to the runtime + a `runtime/src/regex.rs` with `karac_regex_compile`/`_is_match`/`_find`/`_captures`/`_replace`, boxing `Box::into_raw(Box::new(Regex))` as an opaque `*mut`→i64 handle (mirror the PROVEN BoundedChannel/Once pattern — bounded_channel.rs `Box::into_raw`; codegen assoc_call.rs:490-524 casts the `*mut`→i64 and stores it in `Handle{ handle_id: i64 }`); (ii) codegen a `Regex.compile` assoc-call arm building `Result[Regex,RegexError]` from the runtime call, plus `is_match`/`find`/`captures`/`replace` method arms loading the handle; (iii) a binary-SIZE decision — `regex` (+ regex-automata/regex-syntax/aho-corasick/memchr) is heavy, so per the codebase's lean/full/gpu archive discipline it should be an OPT-IN `libkarac_runtime_regex.a` archive auto-selected on a `karac_regex_*` symbol reference (the GPU-archive precedent), NOT added to the default/full archive. This needs owner sign-off on taking the dep + the archive strategy before building. Severity stays low (loud, interp workaround). SLICE 1 SHIPPED 2026-07-15 (compile + is_match wired through codegen; owner-approved opt-in regex archive). `Regex.compile(pat)` and `re.is_match(s)` now work under `karac build` (AOT) and `karac run` — value-identical to the interpreter oracle across valid/invalid patterns, anchors, digit classes, empty subjects. Pieces: (1) runtime `runtime/src/regex.rs` (`karac_regex_validate` / `karac_regex_is_match`, re-compile-per-call like the interp) behind an opt-in `regex` feature → `libkarac_runtime_regex.a`, auto-selected only on a `karac_regex_*` symbol reference (mirrors the GPU archive); (2) codegen intercepts `Regex.compile` (build `Result[Regex,RegexError]` via build_nonshared_enum_value, Ok copies the pattern, Err = cap=0 static message) and `is_match` (extract pattern+subject, call the runtime, u8→i1); (3) THE KEY UPSTREAM FIX: `Regex`/`RegexError`/`Match` collapsed to a single i64 because codegen never learned their layout — added `runtime/stdlib/regex.kara` to codegen's lowered-stdlib set (like `mem`: struct layouts only, `#[compiler_builtin]` bodies not compiled); (4) `karac run` JIT-default routes regex programs to the interpreter (the jit_runner lacks the opt-in regex symbols — mirrors the gpu fallback). Tests: 3 codegen E2E + 1 ASAN+LSan (heap-pattern loop, clean); full codegen (2365/1-preexisting) + memory_sanitizer (709/0) green; clippy + fmt clean. SLICE 2 (670a775) — find -> Option[Match], find_all -> Vec[Match], replace_all -> String now wired through codegen: the runtime entrypoints (karac_regex_find/find_all/replace_all, opt-in libkarac_runtime_regex.a) return primitive byte offsets / a malloc'd buffer and codegen owns all Match/Vec/String layout, slicing the subject for each Match.text via build_owned_string_from_parts (a fresh owned copy, never an alias into the soon-dropped subject). find's Match is heap-boxed into the Option payload (5w > 3w area), coherent with the unpack path; find_all loops the runtime offset array into a fresh Vec buffer then frees it; replace_all adopts the runtime buffer as an owned String (cap = max(len,1)). Verified value-for-value vs the interpreter across AOT + JIT-fallback; ASAN+LSan-clean on the boxed-Option[Match], Vec[Match] recursive-free, and owned-String (incl. empty-result) paths. Full suites green: codegen 2375/0, memory_sanitizer 713/0; clippy + fmt clean. FIXED. Sole remaining deferral: exact regex-crate error-string parity for an INVALID pattern (codegen's Regex.compile Err uses a static message) — cosmetic.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: autonomous dogfooding probe (Regex.compile().unwrap().is_match()) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Regex feature not wired)

title: Lazy iterator-adaptor closures (`filter`/`map`/`take_while`/`skip_while`/… predicates) DIVERGE between backends when the loop body mutates a variable the closure captures: the INTERPRETER snapshots the captured environment at adaptor-creation time (the predicate keeps seeing the stale value), while CODEGEN's fused inlining reads the live variable each iteration. Repro: `let mut lim = 5; for x in xs.iter().filter(|v| v < lim) { lim = lim - 1; ... }` over `[1,2,3,10,4]` → interp sums 10 (pred always compares against 5), codegen sums 3 (live `lim`). Same shape diverges for take_while (`|v| seen < 3` with `seen` incremented in the body: interp iterates ALL elements, codegen stops after 3). design.md § Closures Rule 2 pins read-use captures to BY REFERENCE, which makes codegen's live-read behavior the design-correct one and the interpreter — normally the oracle — the deviant backend for this shape. Caveat that complicates a naive fix: for PLAIN stored closures (`let f = |x| x + k; k = 10; f(1)`) BOTH backends agree on the snapshot answer (2, not the Rule-2 answer 11), so the interpreter's adaptor snapshotting is consistent with its general closure model and the real gap is that NEITHER backend implements Rule 2 by-reference capture for plain closures; codegen's fused adaptors get live reads only because the desugar INLINES the closure body instead of materializing a closure value. Fix direction: implement Rule-2 by-reference capture in the interpreter's closure environment (and eventually codegen's materialized closures), which collapses both divergences at once; until then, mutating a captured variable from inside an adaptor-driven loop is the shape to avoid. Pre-existing since the map/filter fusion (B-2026-07-11-17/18) — NOT introduced by the take_while/skip_while lowering; d415269 merely surfaced it (its regression tests use pure predicates). FIXED (this commit): the interpreter now gives iterator-adaptor closures LIVE-capture semantics, matching codegen's fused inlining and design.md Rule 2. MECHANISM: the interpreter already had by-reference capture machinery — Value::SharedCell aliases, used for MUTATED captures (B-2026-07-11-23: the outer binding's slot is replaced by a shared cell; env.set writes through it, env.get auto-derefs) — so the fix widens the wrap set to ALL free captures (reads included) when evaluating a BARE closure literal that is an argument to an iterator adaptor/terminal: new `eval_iter_closure_arg` (method_call_iter.rs) sets a scoped `wrap_all_closure_captures` flag around the closure's evaluation; the ExprKind::Closure arm (eval_expr.rs) consults it to skip the free∩assigned filter. Wired at all 10 closure-arg sites: map/filter, take_while/skip_while, flat_map, inspect, scan, chunk_by (lazy) + fold, reduce, for_each, any/all (eager terminals). The loop body's `lim = lim - 1` writes through the outer cell; the predicate's captured cell is the SAME Arc → live read. SCOPE DISCIPLINE (why not full Rule 2): plain STORED closures keep the snapshot model — widening them interp-only would CREATE a new divergence, since codegen's materialized closures copy captures by value and both backends currently AGREE on the snapshot answer (pinned by test_plain_stored_closure_keeps_snapshot_semantics: `let mut k=1; let f=|x| x+k; k=10; f(1)` == 2 on both). Explicit own/ref/mut-ref prefixes keep their pinned modes (the flag only widens the bare case); closure-VARIABLE predicates (`filter(p)`) are typecheck-rejected so out of scope; cross-frame escaped iterators keep working via the Arc'd cells (the cell outlives the frame, degrading to last-written-value — memory-safe; such escapes are compiler-rejected in production anyway). Verified: filter repro 10→3 (both backends 3), take_while repro 21→6 (both 6), any()-writes-capture and fold parity, pure predicates unchanged, plain closure unchanged; full interpreter (1223), codegen (2368), non-llvm suites green.
investigation: 2026-07-14: found while parity-testing take_while with a stateful predicate. Verified: (1) filter-only repro diverges the same way, so it predates the predicate-leg lowering; (2) plain-closure repro shows BOTH backends snapshot (agree), so the divergence is adaptor-inlining-specific; (3) design.md Rule 2 (first-use scan: read → capture by reference) says live reads are correct. Severity low: needs body-mutates-captured-var inside an adaptor loop, an unusual shape; every regression test in the fused surface uses pure predicates.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: internal (edge-case probing while lowering B-2026-07-14-8 predicate legs) — SURFACE NOTE: interpreter + codegen (divergent closure-capture semantics on lazy iterator adaptors)

title: A for-loop over a `map`/`filter` iterator chain that the fused-chain peel REJECTS — a destructuring closure param (`for x in ps.iter().map(|(a, b)| a + b)`), a 2-param closure, or a base the peel can't prove is an identity source — fell through `compile_for`'s dispatch to the silent zero-iteration unit arm: the loop body never ran and the program produced a WRONG ANSWER with no error (interp 10 vs JIT/AOT 0 on the tuple-destructure repro). This is exactly the B-2026-07-14-7 silent-skip class that the loud UNLOWERED_FOR_ADAPTORS backstop was built to prevent; `map`/`filter` were missing from that list because they are (mostly) lowered — but the backstop only fires AFTER the fused desugar declines, so listing them costs lowered shapes nothing. FIXED 2026-07-14 (93d954f): `map`/`filter` added to the loud backstop with a shape-specific hint (most chains ARE lowered; this shape defeated the desugar — e.g. a destructuring closure parameter), pointing at `--interp`. Regression: the destructure-param case in `e2e_for_unlowered_iter_adaptors_bail_loud`. Full codegen suite green with the backstop in place — no existing test relied on the silent skip. RESIDUAL (deliberate): the rejected shapes remain interp-only; lowering destructuring closure params in the fused desugar is a separate ergonomics slice.
investigation: 2026-07-14: found by deliberately probing what happens when peel_fused_map_filter_chain fails closed while wiring flat_map dispatch. The fusion gate returns Ok(None) on peel rejection; control then reaches the `_ =>` arm whose UNLOWERED_FOR_ADAPTORS check listed every adaptor EXCEPT map/filter, so the fall-through hit the terminal 'Unknown iterable — skip body, return unit' arm.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: internal (probing while extending the fused-chain desugar for B-2026-07-14-8) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (for-loop dispatch fall-through)

title: The interpreter's for-loop EAGERLY DRAINED a `Value::Iterator` iterable into a Vec before running the body (eval_expr.rs `ExprKind::For`), with two consequences. (1) HANG: any unbounded iterator whose only bound is a `break` in the LOOP BODY — `for x in xs.iter().cycle() { if n == 7 { break; } … }` — pulled forever before the body ever ran; the oracle backend non-terminated on a legal program that codegen runs correctly. (2) ORDERING: every adaptor side effect (`inspect` prints, stateful predicate prints) fired BEFORE the first body execution, the opposite of the codegen backend's fused lowering and Rust's lazy iterator protocol — two interpreter tests had even pinned the eager prefix ordering as expected. FIXED 2026-07-14 (01dae34): the for-loop now pulls one `iterator_step` per iteration, interleaved with the body, with identical break/continue/label handling to the materialized walk (which still serves Vec/Map/Set/String/etc. iterables). The two ordering tests are updated to the interleaved (design-correct) expectations and a no-hang regression is added (`test_for_over_unbounded_cycle_breaks_from_body`). Found because the new cycle for-loop CODEGEN lowering (B-2026-07-14-8) needed an interp parity oracle and the oracle hung on the very first probe.
investigation: 2026-07-14: JIT produced all six expected values for the cycle probe program; the interpreter timed out at the simplest case. eval_expr.rs's For arm materialized `items` via `while let Some(v) = iterator_step { drained.push }` — unbounded means the while never ends. Laziness also aligns pull-ordering with codegen; the only behavioral deltas in the suite were the two tests that had codified the eager ordering.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: internal (interp==JIT parity verification of the cycle for-loop lowering) — SURFACE NOTE: interpreter (for-loop iterable materialization)

title: Reassigning a shared-struct local (`node = popped`) with a value bound out of `Vec[shared].pop()` does NOT release the local's OLD value (x86-visible; the iterative tree-from-stack `node = pop()` idiom) FIXED 2026-07-15 (a384971). ROOT CAUSE CORRECTION: IR-level rc accounting showed the assign path was a red herring — the leaked node is the POPPED one, and a plain-read arm (`Some(p) => println(p.val)`) leaks identically with no reassign. `Vec.pop` TRANSFERS the vec's +1 into the returned `Option[shared]` TEMP; the match payload binding takes its OWN +1 (balanced by the arm-exit RcDec), so the temp's transferred ref had no release — one stranded count per pop. Fix: fresh-temp scrutinee tracker `track_freshtemp_shared_option_scrutinee` (the shared-handle sibling of the boxed-enum / inline-Result trackers, control_flow_match.rs) queues a tag-guarded `RcDecOption` on the materialized scrutinee at scope exit — count-based, correct whether an arm binds, wildcards (`Some(_)` resolves the payload type via the enum-instance-type fallback), or moves the payload. Verified valgrind-clean with interp parity: 50-node pop-reassign loop, wildcard arm, None arm, moved-out payload (no double-free), read-only bind. Regression: asan_vec_shared_pop_match_releases_temp_ref_no_leak. The kata #105 iterative stack form is now unblocked.
investigation: 2026-07-15: surfaced by kata #105's iterative stack construction (walk preorder, maintain a Vec[TreeNode] ancestor stack, `let mut node = top; while ...: match stack.pop() { Some(popped) => node = popped }; node.right = Some(child)`). On karac build the whole rebuilt tree leaks once per construction (interp + all four surfaces produce the CORRECT sink 279291561; only memory leaks — LSan/valgrind `definitely lost`, x86-64). ISOLATION: the leak is the `node = popped` reassignment when `popped` is bound out of `stack.pop()`. MINIMAL REPRO (24 B leaked, one node): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut next: Option[Node] } fn main() { let old = Node { val: 7, next: None }; let keep = Node { val: 1, next: None }; let mut stack: Vec[Node] = Vec.new(); stack.push(keep); let mut node = old; match stack.pop() { None => {} Some(popped) => { node = popped; } } println(f"node {node.val}"); }` — the val-7 `old` node (the local's PREVIOUS value) is never rc-dec'd on the reassignment, so it leaks. DISCRIMINATOR: reassigning the same local from a NON-pop Option is CLEAN — replace `stack.pop()` with a literal `Some(Node { val: 9, next: None })` and the old value IS released (0 leaks). So the defect is specific to the assign path when the RHS is a match-binding moved out of `Vec[shared].pop()`: the pre-store old-value drop the plain-reassign path emits (verified by the clean literal-Some counterpart, and by `x = b` / match-from-fresh-Some both clean) is skipped. Plain shared-local reassign (`let mut x = a; x = b;`) is CLEAN; peek-init + reassign-from-fresh is CLEAN; only pop-sourced RHS leaks. RELATION: sibling of B-2026-07-12-30 (Vec[shared] WHOLE-VARIABLE reassign `current = next`, fixed 924cd05) — that fix released the old Vec's elements on a Vec-typed overwrite; THIS is the scalar shared-struct local analog, and only on the pop-sourced RHS, so the 924cd05 fix does not cover it. Same systemically-under-supported Vec-of-shared surface as B-2026-07-11-29 / -12-4 / -12-30. FIX AREA: the Assign identifier-target path (src/codegen/stmts.rs) must run the target local's OLD value drop before the store for a shared-struct/`Option[shared]`-typed local even when the RHS is a value produced by `Vec::pop` / a match-binding move (the pop lowering likely hands the assign path a value flagged 'already owned, move-only' that suppresses the old-value release). WORKAROUND for katas: use a recursion that threads state without a Vec[shared] stack + pop-reassign — kata #105 ships the bounds-recursive split (the star) and a `mut ref i64` preorder-cursor recursion, both byte-identical across all surfaces and valgrind-clean; the iterative stack form is withheld pending this fix (cf. #102's BFS, withheld until B-2026-07-12-30 landed).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #105 (construct binary tree from preorder+inorder) — the canonical iterative stack construction leaked the whole rebuilt tree on every call — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Assign identifier-target path; reassigning a shared-typed local from a Vec[shared].pop() value)

title: A `Vec[shared]` local with EXACTLY ONE pushed element that is never read after the push leaks that element at scope-exit drop (two+ elements, or any subsequent read, is clean) FIXED 2026-07-15 (a384971). ROOT CAUSE CORRECTION: not a drop-lowering bug — `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` was already clean, and the leaked realloc sat inside `__par_branch_0_0`. AUTO-PAR branched the push (no later statement reads `v`, so the captured-mutation gate saw no outside read); the branch env bit-copies the `{ptr,len,cap}` header, the push realloc'd a branch-local buffer, and the parent dropped the stale empty header — orphaning the buffer + element. Both ledger discriminators explained: a read after the push IS the outside-read the old gate caught, and a second push created a write-write conflict that re-serialized the statements. Fix: the analyzer computes `captured_container_mutations` (captured mutations targeting a heap-owning container local — Vec/String/Map/Set, classified from let annotations + `pattern_binding_types`; name-based, conservative) and codegen's par-group gate compiles such groups sequentially — the parent's scope-exit drop is an implicit read of the container header that the outside-reads set cannot see. Scalar captured mutations stay parallelizable (a lost dead scalar write is inert). Regression: asan_write_only_vec_shared_single_push_no_leak (incl. a linked payload).
investigation: 2026-07-15: found while minimizing B-2026-07-15-1. A `Vec` whose element type is a shared struct (or `Option[shared]`), with exactly ONE element pushed and then NEVER READ again before scope exit, leaks the element (LSan/valgrind `definitely lost`, x86-64). MINIMAL REPRO (56 B: the node + its box): `shared struct Item { val: i64, tag: i64 } fn main() { let mut v: Vec[Item] = Vec.new(); v.push(Item { val: 1, tag: 2 }); println("done"); }`. TWO INDEPENDENT DISCRIMINATORS, each flips it to CLEAN: (1) push a SECOND element (`v.push(Item { val: 3, tag: 4 })`) — 0 leaks; (2) READ the vec after the push (`println(f"len {v.len()}")`) — 0 leaks. Holds for temporaries and named/match-bound pushes alike; a `Vec[i64]` (non-shared element) is always clean. The exactly-one-and-write-only shape suggests the single-element vec is scalar-replaced / treated as dead (never-read) and its drop lowered to a buffer-free that skips the per-element rc-dec, while the heap node it points at was already allocated → leak; two elements force the ordinary heap/realloc drop path, and any read keeps the vec live. Distinct from B-2026-07-15-1 (that is a pop-sourced REASSIGN; this is a pure push+scope-exit-drop with no pop/reassign). Same Vec-of-shared under-support family. FIX AREA: drop lowering / liveness for a write-only Vec[shared] local — the element-releasing walk (`vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr` path) must run even when the vec is never read after its last write and holds a single element. Not a kata blocker (real code reads its vectors), but a latent correctness hole for any build-a-one-element-vec-and-drop pattern.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #105 probing — minimized while isolating B-2026-07-15-1 — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (drop lowering for a write-only single-element Vec[shared] local)

title: No clean way to read a `mut ref i64` into a plain `i64` for indexing/arg-passing: `let ci: i64 = cur;`, passing `cur` to an `i64` param, and `cur as i64` all reject; only an arithmetic expression (`cur - 1i64`) auto-derefs FIXED 2026-07-15 (42a9f2c): `ref`/`mut ref` SCALARS (numeric/bool/char) now read as their value type in every value position — annotated let (`let ci: i64 = cur`), argument passing (`f(cur)`), index exprs (`preorder[cur]`), and casts (`cur as i64`) — via one ref-peel in `check_assignable` + the index and cast rules, matching arithmetic's existing auto-deref. The ownership checker also fired use-after-move on later uses of the borrow after such a read; Ref/MutRef-of-basic-Copy-scalar is now itself Copy in `is_copy_type` (reading it copies the pointee — it can never move or alias owned state). Codegen and interp needed NO changes (both already read through ref-param slots once typechecking accepts; verified interp==JIT==AOT incl. the write-back through the borrow). Heap borrows unchanged (`mut ref String` -> `String` stays rejected — negative guard test). Regressions: test_mut_ref_scalar_reads_as_value / test_mut_ref_heap_does_not_read_as_value (typechecker), test_e2e_mut_ref_scalar_value_reads (codegen).
investigation: 2026-07-15: threading a preorder cursor as `mut ref i64` through a recursion, `preorder[cur]` is rejected: `index must be an integer or range, found 'mut ref i64'`. Attempts to copy the scalar out all fail: `let ci: i64 = cur;` -> `expected 'i64', found 'mut ref i64'`; calling `f(cur)` where `f` takes `i64` -> same; `cur as i64` -> `cannot cast 'mut ref i64' to 'i64'`. The ONLY context that auto-derefs a `mut ref` scalar is arithmetic — `cur + 1i64` compiles, and indexing with an arithmetic expression `preorder[cur - 1i64]` works. So the workaround is to index via arithmetic (kata #105's cursor solver does `cur = cur + 1i64; let root_val = preorder[cur - 1i64];`), but there is no straightforward `*cur` / copy idiom. Low severity (clean workaround exists) but a real papercut: a `mut ref` scalar should be readable into its value type by a plain `let`/annotation/cast or arg coercion, as arithmetic already does implicitly.
