FIX AREA: typechecker coercion — allow a `mut ref T` (T scalar/Copy) to coerce to `T` in value position (index expr, argument, annotated let, cast), matching the auto-deref arithmetic already performs.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #105 — writing the `mut ref i64` preorder-cursor recursion — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (reading/copying a `mut ref <scalar>` into a plain scalar)

title: Auto-par parallelizes an independent recursive divide-and-conquer (build left subtree, build right subtree) with NO minimum-granularity cutoff, spawning threads for ~15-node subtrees — a 160x wall-time regression vs KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 (22.4s vs 0.138s @ K=200k), same correct output; a genuine data dependency (a shared cursor) correctly serializes FIXED 2026-07-15 (67cc2db): the analyzer records each statement's bare callee names (`StmtInfo.called_fn_names`) and `find_parallel_groups` WITHHOLDS any group in which a member statement calls the ENCLOSING function — the recursive divide-and-conquer shape (`let left = build(..); let right = build(..)` inside `build`) now runs sequentially under the default build. Rationale: without a work-stealing scheduler with a lazy sequential cutoff, spawn-per-call recursion is always dispatch-dominated (~70µs per `karac_par_run`, O(nodes) dispatches per top-level call; the bounded 2x-per-split win never amortizes it). Local re-measure: 175x regression (1.72s vs 0.0098s, 15-node tree x 20k reps) closed to sequential speed (0.0076s default vs 0.012s ap0), identical output. NON-recursive independent fan-outs keep parallelizing (the two-work(30M) probe still 2.1x faster than KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0), and the shared-cursor control stays sequential as before. RESIDUALS (documented): mutual recursion through a helper (`build` -> helper -> `build`) is not detected (direct self-calls only), and a future work-stealing pool with a sequential cutoff could re-enable recursive parallelism profitably — tracked as the fix-area suggestion, not required for correctness-of-performance today. Regressions: test_self_recursive_calls_do_not_form_parallel_group + test_non_recursive_independent_calls_still_group.
investigation: 2026-07-15: kata #105's recursive reconstruction `fn build(...) -> Option[TreeNode] { ...; let left = build(...left window...); let right = build(...right window...); Some(TreeNode{val, left, right}) }` — the two recursive calls are INDEPENDENT (disjoint index windows, no shared mutable state), so auto-par correctly identifies them as parallelizable and spawns them. But with NO cost/size threshold: each ~15-node subtree build (and its sub-subtrees) spawns OS threads, and the benchmark's K=200k repetitions turn that into millions of thread creations. MEASURED (K=200000, x86 container, same 15-node tree pool): default `karac build` 22.4s real (user 10.6s, SYS 21.3s — the sys time IS the thread-spawn syscall overhead) vs `KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0` 0.138s — a 160x regression. Output is IDENTICAL and correct on both (sink 888540434), so this is purely a parallelization-granularity pathology, not a miscompile. CONTROL (proves it is the granularity cutoff, not recursion per se): the cursor variant of the same kata — `fn build(..., cur: mut ref i64)` threading ONE shared preorder cursor, so left MUST finish before right (a real data dependency) — auto-par correctly leaves SEQUENTIAL: default build 0.125s == ap0 0.131s, same sink. So auto-par's dependency analysis is right; what is missing is a minimum-work threshold before it spawns independent recursive calls. FIX AREA: auto-par cost model — gate recursive-call parallelization on an estimated-work threshold (subtree/problem size), or use a work-stealing pool with a sequential cutoff (the standard divide-and-conquer parallelism guard) instead of spawning per recursive call regardless of size. IMPACT: any recursive divide-and-conquer with independent subcalls (tree build/traversal, quicksort, mergesort, map-reduce over a tree) is pessimized 100x+ under the default build at scale; the correct output masks it until you measure wall-time. WORKAROUND: KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 for such workloads (kata #105's benchmark times the sequential lane — apples-to-apples with the single-threaded C/Rust/Go mirrors — and documents this; the default build stays byte-identical for correctness). Kata #103 also ships the cursor solver, which is naturally sequential under the default build.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #105 benchmark — the recursive index-bounds tree reconstruction ran 160x SLOWER under the default (auto-par) build than KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 — SURFACE NOTE: auto-par (parallelization cost model / granularity cutoff for recursive divide-and-conquer)

title: Nested enum-variant patterns over BOXED payloads miscompile: `Option.Some(Option.Some(x))` silently takes the wrong arm, `Wrap.W(Option.Some(x))` binds 0, multi-arm forms segfault — the match side never deboxed the oversized nested payload
investigation: 2026-07-15: an inner Option[i64] VALUE is 4 words ({tag, 3 payload words}); Option's seeded payload area is 3 words, and payload_word_count_for_type_expr's enum-in-enum carve-out sizes a user enum's enum-typed payload area to 1 word. Both pack sides therefore heap-box the inner enum (coerce_to_payload_words: box ptr in payload word 0) — by design (docs/spikes/oversized-enum-payload.md). The MATCH side never deboxed: pattern_payload_word_count and pattern_payload_llvm_type defaulted a TupleVariant (and unit-variant Binding) sub-pattern to 1 word / i64, so reconstruct_payload_value's debox predicate (want > field_words.len()) never fired. The nested tag check in and_in_nested_variant_conditions then compared the BOX POINTER against the expected tag → wrong arm silently taken (interp `inner 42`, codegen `other`); the bind path read undef payload words → `got 0`; the 3-explicit-arm form segfaulted; the 32-byte box leaked. Result[Option[T],E] escaped by luck (Result's 5-word area fits the 4-word inner Option inline), as did enum-in-Option for ≤3-word user enums. The typechecker's E_ENUM_NESTED_ENUM_PAYLOAD carve-out (validate_enum_payload_no_nested_enum) collects only PROGRAM-DECLARED enums, so prelude Option/Result as inner payloads legitimately flow to codegen — the boxing surface is the supported path, and its match side was simply unfinished. Fix (3 legs, all in control_flow_match.rs): (1) pattern_payload_word_count gets a TupleVariant arm + a unit-variant Binding leg — width = the variant's enum tagged-union word count (shared enums stay 1 RC-pointer word), guarded on no typechecker-recorded binding type so ordinary bindings are untouched; (2) pattern_payload_llvm_type gets the symmetric arms (debox load must read the inner enum's full {tag, words...} shape); (3) and_in_nested_variant_conditions gates the nested checks behind the outer tag comparison (branch + phi — lazy AND) because the debox load on a NON-matching variant's zero payload word would NULL-deref (match None against Option.Some(Option.Some(x))). Verified: all 12 probe shapes agree across interp/JIT/native and are valgrind-clean, incl. triple nesting Option[Option[Option[i64]]] (recursive gate), Option[Option[String]] moved-out and dropped-unmatched (box + heap interior freed), user enum Wrap.W(Option[T]) all variants, Result.Ok(Option.Some(x)). Regression tests: tests/codegen.rs mod nested_enum_payload_patterns_b_2026_07_15_5 (3 E2E), tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_nested_option_pattern_boxed_payload_lifecycle_clean. Fixed in e05c93d.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 4 (match-pattern battery) — interp printed `inner 42` where JIT/native printed `other` (wrong arm), a user-enum variant bound `got 0`, and the multi-arm form segfaulted (exit 139) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (match pattern lowering — nested enum-variant sub-patterns over heap-boxed payloads: control_flow_match.rs pattern_payload_word_count / pattern_payload_llvm_type / and_in_nested_variant_conditions)

title: A bare-`T`-annotated local in a monomorphized generic fn (`let mut best: T = items[0]` with T→String) never registers as a tracked heap binding, so reassignment (`best = items[i]`) leaks the previous buffer — the running-max scan leaks every replaced value
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from `largest[T: Ord]` (probe g1): leak fires exactly when the `best = items[i]` reassignment executes (l5: ['ab','zzzz'] leaks 3 bytes = len+1 of the replaced 'ab'; l4 compare-only is clean; l6 concrete Vec[String] twin is clean). Root cause: the Let arm's annotation-driven detection chain (stmts.rs ~2192) checks the RAW annotation TypeExpr by name — `is_string_type_expr('T')` misses — so the mono'd String local never lands in `string_vars`/`vec_elem_types`; the Assign arm's eager old-value free is gated on `lhs_is_tracked_vec` (vec_elem_types membership) and never arms. The RHS clone side was already mono-aware (clone_owned_vec_index_element), so the reassignment cloned correctly and only the OLD buffer stranded — leak, not double-free. Fix: resolve the annotation through the existing `subst_monomorph_type_params` (the B-2026-07-13-2/-3 helper: type_subst_names + element-aware type_subst_type_exprs) before the detection chain; no-op outside monomorphs and for concrete annotations. This also corrects every other name-keyed registration for bare-`T` annotations (Map/Set/Slice/CString legs). Verified: l1–l6 + g1 all interp/JIT/native-agree and valgrind-clean; regression test asan_generic_mono_bare_t_local_reassign_no_leak PROVEN to catch the bug (pre-fix: LeakSanitizer 13 bytes in 2 allocations = the two replaced values; post-fix clean). Fixed in a9f3f7f.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 4 (generics battery) — valgrind flagged 5 bytes definitely lost on `largest[T: Ord](items: ref Vec[T]) -> T` over Vec[String]; the concrete Vec[String] twin was clean — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (let-annotation type detection inside monomorphs — stmts.rs Let arm; Assign arm eager old-value free)

title: Interpreter emits a spurious cascading second diagnostic after a runtime error inside a compound expression: `min / -1 + 0` reports the integer-overflow error, then continues evaluating the enclosing `+` with the error placeholder and adds "operator 'Add' is not defined for operands of type 'Unit' and 'Unit'" FIXED 2026-07-15 (b3be90d) — root cause was the LOWERED-OPERATOR dispatch path (dispatch_lowered_op, eval_stmt.rs), NOT eval_expr's Binary arm: after karac::lower rewrites `a + b` into `<type>.add(a, b)`, scalar binops reach the interpreter through dispatch_lowered_op, which evaluated lhs then rhs with NO pending_cf guard between them. So a faulted lhs (`min / -1` overflow -> pending_cf set, Unit poison) did not short-circuit: rhs was still evaluated (check_cf short-circuits it to Unit), then eval_binary(Add, Unit, Unit) recorded the spurious second 'operator Add is not defined for Unit and Unit'. Fix: add the same per-operand pending_cf short-circuit the ExprKind::Binary/Unary arms already perform — return the poison immediately after any faulted operand (binary + neg/not). Now exactly one diagnostic (the real fault) is reported; verified for overflow, div-by-zero, and f-string-interpolation shapes. Statement-level propagation and cross-backend exit codes were already correct (AOT traps exit 1 at the same op). Test test_no_cascading_diagnostic_after_fault_in_compound_expr; interp 1224 green.
investigation: 2026-07-15: `print(f"{min / -1 + 0}")` — the interpreter reports `runtime error: integer overflow` at the division, then the enclosing Add evaluates against the error's Unit placeholder and emits a second, confusing `operator 'Add' is not defined for operands of type 'Unit' and 'Unit'` diagnostic (with a misleading parenthetical claiming a typecheck-error leak). Statement-level propagation is CORRECT: a runtime error aborts execution (exit 1, subsequent statements do not run — verified with a 3-statement probe), and JIT/native panic cleanly at the same operation with exit 1, so there is no semantic divergence — both backends trap i64.MIN / -1 and abort. The defect is purely that sub-expression errors inside a compound expression do not short-circuit the enclosing operator evaluation before the abort propagates, producing a noisy cascade. Fix direction: make eval_expr's binop/interp paths propagate the first runtime error immediately (Result-style early return) instead of yielding the Unit placeholder to the parent operator. Low priority: diagnostics quality only; no wrong output, no leak, matching exit codes.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 4 (numeric battery) — i64.MIN / -1 inside a compound f-string expression — SURFACE NOTE: interpreter (runtime-error propagation inside compound expressions)

title: A closure that captures and calls another closure (`let base = |x| x+1; let composed = |x| base(x)*10`) returns 0 in codegen (interp: 50) — the captured closure isn't recognized as callable inside the body; a String-returning captured closure additionally fails module verification
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from a higher-order closure probe. Two coupled defects in closures.rs. (1) compile_closure does `std::mem::take(&mut self.closure_fn_types)` at the body-scope boundary (isolating the outer map), so a captured closure-valued free var (`base`, re-registered in self.variables by the capture-load) is NOT in the emptied closure_fn_types when its body-scope call `base(x)` dispatches — the `closure_fn_types.contains_key` check in compile_call misses, and the call falls through to the unknown-callee const-0 stub. Result: codegen returns 0 where the interpreter computes the real value (r1: interp 50 / codegen 0; r2: interp 5 / codegen 0). Direct top-level closure calls (r3) and scalar captures (r4) were always fine. Fix: after the take, re-register each captured free var's outer closure-value FunctionType (from the saved map) into the fresh body-scope closure_fn_types, so the indirect-call path (compile_closure_call → load_variable + build_indirect_call) fires. (2) Exposed by (1): a String-returning captured closure called inside another closure (`|s| wrap(wrap(s))`) failed module verification (`ret {ptr,i64,i64} ... i64` — return type does not match) because infer_closure_return_type's Call arm only consulted module fns (`self.module.get_function`), not captured closure vars, so the enclosing closure's return type fell to the i64 default while the body returned the String aggregate. Fix: the Call arm now looks up `closure_fn_types[fname].get_return_type()` after the module-fn miss (closure_fn_types still holds the outer entries at inference time — the body-scope take happens later). Verified: r1/r2/r5 (3-level chains)/r6 (loop)/p5_nc2/p5_nc3 all interp/JIT/native-agree; String captured-closure calls (r7/r8/r9/r10) produce correct output. Regression: tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_closure_captures_and_calls_another_closure. Known residual: the nested `wrap(wrap(s))`-inside-a-closure shape leaks the intermediate inner-call String (3 bytes) — filed B-2026-07-15-9 (low); single/direct/top-level calls are leak-clean. Fixed in cb46eed.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 5 (nested/higher-order closure battery) — `composed = |x| base(x) * 10` printed 50 in the interpreter but 0 in JIT and native — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (closure body compilation — closures.rs compile_closure closure_fn_types isolation; infer_closure_return_type Call arm)

title: A nested indirect closure call whose inner call yields a fresh heap value consumed by the outer call (`|s| wrap(wrap(s))`) leaks the intermediate String — compile_closure_call compiles args via compile_expr without the fresh-owned-temp free the direct-call path (calls.rs is_fresh_heap_call_arg) performs FIXED 2026-07-15 (7db3655) — the leaked buffer is the closure`s owned heap PARAM (the fresh temp the caller passes), not the `intermediate`: `wrap` is caller-retains (frees only its own `"["+s` concat, never its param), so the fresh `String.from(..)` passed into the closure must be freed by the CALLER. compile_closure_call / _value_call compiled args with a bare compile_expr and never did the direct-call path`s is_fresh_heap_call_arg -> materialize_owned_temp; the single-`wrap(s)` shape was masked by LLVM dead-malloc elision, the nested `|s| wrap(wrap(s))` shape survived O2 and leaked once per call. Root: closures never adopted the function owned-heap-param architecture. Fix (co-dependent halves): (1) register a closure`s owned (non-ref) Vec/String params in owned_vecstr_params for the body compile (saved/restored around it, so the outer fn`s set + capture defensive-copy are untouched); (2) deep-copy such a param when it flows to the closure tail return (deepcopy_owned_param_branch_tail, like compile_function`s tail) so `|s| s` / `|s| { s }` don`t alias the caller`s buffer; (3) at BOTH closure call sites, materialize + free a fresh owned heap arg (new free_fresh_owned_heap_closure_arg mirroring the direct-call guards). Both halves are required together — caller-frees WITHOUT callee-defensive-copy double-frees the identity closure. Verified clean (valgrind + ASan, AOT=JIT=interp) across nested-consume (the leak), single-consume, identity `|s| s` / `|s| { s }` (double-free guard), bound-local arg `f(g)` (no double-free), and the Vec sibling. 3 new asan tests; memory_sanitizer 721 / codegen 2383 / par_codegen 159 / interpreter 1224 green; fmt + clippy clean.
investigation: 2026-07-15: surfaced once B-2026-07-15-8 made `|s| wrap(wrap(s))` compile and run correctly. The inner `wrap(s)` produces a fresh owned String temp that is passed by value to the outer `wrap(...)`; compile_closure_call's arg loop (closures.rs) compiles each arg with compile_expr and pushes it, but — unlike the direct module-fn call path (calls.rs ~1571, `is_fresh_heap_call_arg` → caller-side free) — never frees a fresh owned heap arg temp the callee consumed. Narrow: r8 (single indirect String call), r9 (direct), r10 (two top-level calls) are all leak-clean; only the nested-indirect-call-inside-a-closure shape leaks. Fix direction: teach compile_closure_call / compile_closure_value_call to apply the same fresh-owned-temp arg cleanup the direct call path uses, gated on the closure param consuming the arg by value (a by-ref/borrow param must NOT free). Low priority: 3-byte leak, correct output, rare shape.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 5 — while fixing B-2026-07-15-8, valgrind flagged 3 bytes lost on `|s| wrap(wrap(s))` — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (indirect closure call — closures.rs compile_closure_call / compile_closure_value_call argument compilation)

title: zip adaptor surface gaps in codegen: `zip().map(f).collect()` (a map over the zipped tuples) and single-binding `for pair in zip { pair.0 }` both loud-bail (`no handler for method 'zip' on non-identifier receiver`) while the interpreter runs them; `for (a,b) in zip` and identity `zip().collect()` work FIXED 2026-07-15 (465d54b) — both shapes lowered. (1) Single-binding zip for-loop: new compile_for_zip_single_var binds the whole (EA,EB) tuple to the one variable (the two-source sibling of compile_for_enumerate_single_var), body reads .0/.1; gated to SCALAR sources (both peel to trivially-copyable Vecs) — a heap-element single-var binding still bails (the 2-sub destructure path handles heap via per-side borrow-marking). (2) zip().map(f).collect(): new try_compile_zip_map_collect rewrites `A.iter().zip(B.iter()).map(f).collect()` to `{ let mut acc: Vec[R] = Vec.new(); for <f.param> in <zip> { acc.push(<f.body>); } acc }`, reusing the for-loop zip arm so the map closure param (single-var OR 2-tuple destructure) binds over each pair; R comes from the collect owned_temp_drops result type. Verified AOT=JIT=interp, valgrind-clean, across single-var scalar zip, min-length stop, single-var + destructuring map params, unequal lengths, and a String-producing (f-string) map. Tests test_e2e_for_zip_single_var / test_e2e_zip_map_collect; e2e_for_unlowered_iter_adaptors_bail_loud updated to a heap-element single-var zip (still bails). codegen 2386 / par_codegen 159 / interpreter 1225 / memory_sanitizer 722; fmt + clippy clean. NOTE the separately-flagged Iterator terminals min/max/find/position remain a symmetric BOTH-backend feature gap (typecheck rejects), not addressed here.
investigation: 2026-07-15: codegen's zip support is two narrow shapes — (1) the for-loop tuple-DESTRUCTURE `for (a, b) in xs.iter().zip(ys.iter())` (control_flow_for.rs ~435, requires PatternKind::Tuple with 2 subs) and (2) the identity collect `xs.iter().zip(ys.iter()).collect()` → Vec[(EA,EB)] (method_call.rs try_compile_zip_pipeline_collect, ~7434, which bails unless the result type is a 2-tuple). Everything else loud-bails at the `no handler for method 'zip' on non-identifier receiver` fallthrough with a `--interp` hint. Confirmed-broken shapes: (a) `zip().map(|p| p.0 + p.1).collect()` — the map transforms the 2-tuples to Vec[i64], so try_compile_zip_pipeline_collect's 2-tuple gate returns None and the chain falls through (interp: 11,22,33; codegen: build error); (b) `for pair in a.iter().zip(b.iter()) { pair.0 }` — a single Binding, not a Tuple pattern, so the for-loop zip arm misses (interp: 10:1 20:2 30:3; codegen: build error). Not a correctness hazard (loud bail, no wrong output can ship). Fix path: extend the fused iterator engine so a zip source can feed a map/filter stage before a collect/for terminal, and admit a single-binding tuple-typed loop var over zip (bind the (EA,EB) tuple, let the body read `.0`/`.1` — the enumerate single-var precedent, B-2026-07-14-8). Flagged for coordination with the B-2026-07-14-8 adaptor-surface owner rather than fixed inline, to avoid conflicting edits in the adaptor-lowering area. ALSO in this probe: iterator terminals min/max/find/position are unimplemented on Iterator in BOTH backends (typecheck: `no method 'min' on type 'Iterator'`) — a symmetric feature gap, not a divergence; noted here, not separately filed.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 7 (iterator terminals + zip battery) — `a.iter().zip(b.iter()).map(|p| p.0+p.1).collect()` and `for pair in a.iter().zip(b.iter()) { pair.0 }` both run in the interpreter but loud-bail in codegen — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (iterator adaptor lowering — zip feeding a map/collect pipeline; zip in a for-loop with a single (non-destructure) binding). control_flow_for.rs zip for-loop arm; method_call.rs try_compile_zip_pipeline_collect

title: A monomorphized generic struct whose field IS a bare type param (`struct Box[T] { value: T }`, used as `Box[String]` / `Box[Vec[..]]`) never frees the heap field at scope exit — the mono struct-drop classifier reads the erased field name `T` (not Vec/String) and classifies it no-cleanup; the CONCRETE-field twin (`struct Box { value: String }`) is clean
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from a generic-Box probe. emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl (synth_drop.rs ~1053) classifies each field by struct_field_type_names[struct] — for `Box[T]` the field `value: T` records the DECLARED name `T`, which matches none of the Vec/String/Map/enum/struct arms, so it is FieldDrop::None and never freed. The mono subst {T:String|Vec[..]} is applied only to resolve a `Vec[T]` field's ELEMENT (the B-2026-07-11-35 push leg), never a field that IS the bare param. Leak sizes: Box[String] via literal or a `wrap[T]` fn — 5 bytes ('hello'); Box[Vec[i64]] direct literal — 32 bytes; concrete `Box{value:String}` and `Box{value:Vec}` — CLEAN (non-generic path). (A generic Box[Vec] built through a `wrap()` fn happened to be masked clean by the fn's arg handling, but the direct literal leaks — so the field-drop gap is fundamental, not fn-specific.) FIX-BLOCKER (why not landed): the natural fix — resolve the field's declared name through the subst in the classifier so `T`→`String`/`Vec` classifies VecOrString — was implemented and made all pure-scope-exit cases clean, BUT it regresses every MOVE-OUT case into a DOUBLE-FREE (crash): `let c = b;` (rebind), `let s = take(b)` (by-value arg consume), and field-move-out all then free the field twice. Root cause of the regression: the PAIRED move-suppression zero_struct_move_caps (call_dispatch.rs ~3917) ALSO reads the declared field name `T`, matches no arm, and so never zeros the mono field's cap on a move — while the newly-synthesized drop fn now frees it. A complete fix must make BOTH the drop-synthesis classifier AND zero_struct_move_caps mono-aware and agree; zero_struct_move_caps currently takes only struct_name (no subst/inst), so the instantiation must be threaded through all its move-site callers. That is delicate move-suppression machinery (leak↔double-free tension, and the CLAUDE.md arm64-leak caveat means a local x86 leak check does not clear it), so the one-sided change was REVERTED rather than shipped as a regression. Fix path: add a subst param to zero_struct_move_caps mirroring emit_struct_drop_synthesis_mono, thread the binding instantiation (enum_inst_var_types) from every struct move site, then land the classifier resolution + move-cap resolution together and verify no double-free across rebind/by-value-arg/return/Vec-element move-out under LSan (both arches). DEEPER INVESTIGATION 2026-07-15 (bounded single-field fix ATTEMPTED, verified UNSAFE, REVERTED; the fix-blocker is CONFIRMED but RE-SCOPED — the ledger's 'every move-out double-frees' was too broad, and the true blocking site is different). Implemented the natural classifier fix but GATED to a SINGLE-FIELD wrapper `W[T] { f: T }` (the reported repro): in emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl, when kinds.len()==1 && the sole field is None, resolve field 0's declared TypeExpr through the mono subst (already threaded via track_struct_var_inst -> generic_struct_subst_from_inst) and classify a Vec/String monomorph as FieldDrop::VecOrString; also substituted the field TypeExpr BEFORE vec_inner_type_expr so a `Vec[String]` monomorph drains its elements. RESULTS: (1) pure scope-exit Box[String] (5B) and Box[Vec[i64]] (32B) LEAKS both go valgrind-CLEAN. (2) CONTRARY to the ledger's blanket claim, the simple move-out shapes do NOT regress to double-free — `let c = b` (rebind), a by-value-arg consume `sink(b)`, field-move-out `let s = b.value`, `v.push(Box{..})` into Vec[Box[String]], and an annotated `let b: Box[String] = mk()` return are ALL valgrind-clean, because those bindings suppress the WHOLE StructDrop (or handle caps correctly), not via the mismatched cap-zeroing zero_struct_move_caps was feared to miss. (3) The REAL double-free is a DIFFERENT site: a generic ACCESSOR `fn get(ref self) -> T { self.v }` over Box[String] — `free(): double free detected` on the gbox multi-instantiation program. Root: maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg's borrowed-receiver field-return deep-clone (runtime.rs ~3196) was DELIBERATELY left SHALLOW for a generic heap field (its own comment, ~3181-3185: 'the generic heap-field return-clone is a separate concern out of this narrow-int bug's scope') on the THEN-true assumption that Box[String] has NO scope-exit drop — so returning `self.v` handed back an ALIAS that was leak-clean only because nothing else freed it. Once the classifier adds the scope-exit drop, that alias double-frees against the receiver's drop. The CONCRETE twin `struct Box { v: String }` .get() is CLEAN (its field-return already deep-clones), so the gap is purely the bare-T (subst-resolved) leg of the return-clone gate + emit (which must clone with the CONCRETE field type, not the last-writer-wins `karac_clone_T`). (4) LLVM-LAYOUT-ERASURE constraint the ledger's subst-threading plan missed: struct_types["Box"] lowers the bare-T field to a single i64 WORD (llvm_type_for_type_expr with no active subst at declaration), while instances are the monomorphized `{ {ptr,i64,i64} }` — so field-GEP with the erased struct type is only correct for a SINGLE field (offset 0); a multi-field wrapper with a mid bare-T heap field mis-offsets every field after it. (5) Two RESIDUAL leak sub-cases even with the classifier fix: an un-annotated `let b = mk()` (the binding records no instantiation -> name-shared drop, no subst) and Box-as-a-nested-struct-field (the parent's drop recurses through the NAME-SHARED child drop, dropping the subst). RE-SCOPED FIX PLAN (must land TOGETHER, each an independent double-free/leak site): (a) classifier single-field bare-T resolution [done+verified safe in isolation], (b) make the borrowed-receiver field-return deep-clone fire for a subst-resolved direct Vec/String buffer and emit with the CONCRETE field type — WITHOUT regressing concrete-struct returns (already clean via te_owns_heap_below_buffer==false for a bare String), (c) verify the field-reassignment `self.v = v` path frees-old/stores-new without doubling the scope-exit drop, (d) inst inference for un-annotated bindings, (e) nested-struct subst propagation. The interaction surface (a partial landing crashes on the ubiquitous `get`/accessor pattern) plus the CLAUDE.md arm64-LSan caveat is why this second attempt was also reverted rather than shipped partial. NOT a double-free in the current tree (stays a LEAK); reverted to keep it so.
resolution: 2026-07-15 (5843cb9): landed the full coordinated fix as five paired sites, verified TOGETHER (interp==JIT==AOT + valgrind/LSan-clean on x86) where the two prior attempts were reverted for double-freeing. (a) Drop classifier (synth_drop.rs emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl): a SINGLE-field wrapper's bare-T field is resolved through the mono subst and classified VecOrString for a String/Vec/VecDeque monomorph (and a Vec[String] monomorph drains its elements — the VecOrString arm now substitutes the whole field TypeExpr first). Single-field gate on purpose: struct_types erases a bare-T field to one i64 word, so the {ptr,len,cap} reinterpret is offset-correct only at offset 0. (b) Move-suppression dual (call_dispatch.rs): new zero_struct_move_caps_mono zeroes the bare-T field cap+len on a whole-struct move (let c=b / return b / by-value consume), threaded from the source binding's enum_inst_var_types instantiation. (c) Borrowed-receiver field-return deep-clone (runtime.rs maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg): a get(ref self)->T{self.v} accessor over a single-field wrapper now clones with the CONCRETE resolved field type (karac_clone_str) instead of the shallow last-writer-wins karac_clone_T, so the returned value owns an independent buffer; multi-field wrappers keep the shallow path (no mono drop → a deep clone there would leak). (d/e) Nested-struct leg: the NestedStruct classification + emit and the zero_struct_move_caps recursion all thread the nested wrapper's own mono subst (nested_struct_field_subst — derived from the declared Box[String] field, resolving the parent's subst first), so Outer{inner:Box[String]} and generic Gen[U]{inner:Box[U]} are leak- and double-free-clean at scope exit AND on a whole-parent move. Battery (all clean): scope-exit Box[String]/Box[Vec[i64]]/Box[Vec[String]], accessor get, rebind, by-value sink, field move-out, reassignment self.v=nv, multi-instantiation with accessor, Vec[Box[String]] push, un-annotated wrap() return, nested-struct + whole-parent move + generic outer. Full suite green (memory_sanitizer 727, codegen 2389, typechecker 2044, ownership 353, interpreter 1226). Three LSan regression tests added (asan_generic_wrapper_*). RESIDUALS (documented, NOT crashes): (1) a MULTI-field wrapper with a mid bare-T heap field still leaks — LLVM-layout erasure limits the drop/GEP to offset 0; (2) a method call on a bare-T field of a by-value/ref generic-struct param (`b: Box[String]` → `b.v.len()`) loud-bails in codegen — that is the field-receiver dispatch gap already tracked as B-2026-07-15-20 (its fix path covers the owned/ref param leg), NOT a new bug; the regression tests route around it via field-move-out then a String local method call. arm64-LSan caveat (CLAUDE.md): x86-verified; the permanent memory-sanitizer-arm64 CI leg is the cross-arch gate.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 10 (generic-struct heap-field battery) — a monomorphized generic struct `Box[T]` with `T` bound to String/Vec leaks the whole field buffer at scope exit; the concrete-field twin is clean — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (per-monomorph struct-drop synthesis — synth_drop.rs emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl field classifier; PAIRED move-suppression call_dispatch.rs zero_struct_move_caps)

title: An f-string that embeds a String-returning call/method/slice DIRECTLY (`f"...{obj.describe()}"`, `f"...{greet(x)}"`, `f"...{s[a..b]}"`) leaks the fresh String temp's buffer — the append copies the bytes but fstr_render_part's plain-String path never scope-tracks it; `print(fn())` and binding the result first are both clean
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from a generic-trait-bound probe but NOT generic-specific. fstr_render_part (runtime.rs ~7021) has dedicated scope-tracked arms (track_vec_var on a fresh alloca) for user-Display / user-enum / collection / Option-Result parts so their rendered buffers survive the outer f-string's memcpy and are freed once at scope exit — but a plain String-typed part (`d.describe()` returning String) falls to the tail `compile_expr` + `compile_fstr_part_to_cstr` path, which just extracts (ptr,len) from the String struct and NEVER tracks the buffer. Since emit_string_append_raw COPIES the bytes into the accumulator, the fresh call-temp's buffer is dead after the append and leaks. Confirmed: u1 concrete method-call (7 bytes 'dog Rex'), u4 free-fn (6 'hi bob'), u6 two interps (14 = 2×7), t2 generic (15 = 7+8); u3 (bind describe() to a let first, then interp the binding) and u5 (print(fn()) direct) are CLEAN — the leak is specifically the inline fresh-temp interp. Scales with count and is unbounded in a loop. Fix: in fstr_render_part, when the compiled String part value is the vec-struct layout AND the part expr yields a fresh owned temp (expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp for a call/method, or expr_is_fresh_owned_string_slice for `s[a..b]`), store it into a track_vec_var'd alloca and extract (ptr,len) from there — mirroring the existing user-Display/enum/collection arms. A PLACE-expr String (identifier/field, owned elsewhere) is deliberately NOT tracked (gated on the fresh-owned-temp predicates) so its owner's single free stands — no double-free. Verified: u1/u2/u4/u6/t2 leak→clean; double-free boundary clean (v1 identifier owner still prints, v3 f-string moved into a Vec, v4 String slice source intact, v2 100-iter loop bounded). Regression test asan_fstring_fresh_string_temp_interp_no_leak is PROVEN to catch it (pre-fix: LeakSanitizer 56 bytes in 9 allocations; post-fix clean). Fixed in 0e4875d.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 11 (generic trait bounds) — valgrind flagged 15 bytes lost on `announce[T: Describable]` returning `f"here is a {x.describe()}"`; reduced to a NON-generic f-string leak — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (f-string interpolation — runtime.rs fstr_render_part plain-String path)

title: A closure that mutates a captured collection through a mutating METHOD (`acc.push(x)`, `buf.push_str(s)`, `m.insert(k,v)`) does not write through to the outer binding — both backends inferred the capture mode from direct-assignment roots only, missing method mutation (design.md § Closures line 4940: a `mut ref self` method call captures its receiver by mut-ref)
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from a closure-collector probe. Both backends compute a closure's mut-ref capture set from collect_assigned_roots (which records `=` / `[i] =` targets ONLY), so a method mutation `acc.push(x)` never marked `acc` — codegen captured the Vec BY VALUE (the closure grew a private {ptr,len,cap} copy; the outer len stayed 0 → `acc.len()`==0, plus a leak of the grown buffer) and the interpreter snapshot-copied it. The interpreter APPEARED correct for Vec only because Value::Vec is Rc-shared (the by-value snapshot aliases the same buffer), but String/Map are not Rc-shared so their mutation was lost in the interpreter too (buf read back empty, m.len()==0). design.md § Closures (line 4940) is explicit: a method call captures its receiver whole with the METHOD's receiver mode; a `mut ref self` method (push/push_str/insert/…) captures mut-ref, so the mutation must propagate. Fix (both backends, shared AST analysis): new ast::collect_mut_method_receiver_roots_{block,expr} + is_mutating_collection_method (a curated set of DEFINITELY-mutating built-in collection methods — push/pop/push_str/insert/remove/clear/sort/…; read-only len/get/iter absent so a read capture is never over-marked, which would wrongly force by-ref capture and trip the escaping-closure rejection). closures.rs unions it into mutref_caps (the env then stores a POINTER to the outer slot; the body's push/realloc updates the outer {ptr,len,cap} in place; the pointer env-field is excluded from the env-drop buffer free → no double-free); eval_expr.rs unions it into the SharedCell wrap set (writes propagate through the cell via env.set write-back). Verified interp==codegen and leak-clean across Vec (multi-call accumulation), String push_str, Map insert, loop mutation, and a read-only capture (unchanged, by-value); an ESCAPING mut-capture now loud-bails cleanly (the documented heap-closure-mutation-escape limitation, correctly detected for method mutation too) where it previously miscompiled silently. Regression: tests/interpreter.rs test_closure_mutates_captured_collection_via_method; tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_closure_mutates_captured_collection_via_method; tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_closure_mut_captured_collection_no_leak. NOTE: multi-call of a push_str/insert closure is blocked by a SEPARATE ownership over-strictness (B-2026-07-15-14), so the String/Map regression tests use a single mutating call (Vec multi-call is check-clean). Fixed in 25f43a1.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 13 (closure mutating a captured collection) — `let mut push = |x| acc.push(x); push(1)` left `acc.len()` reading 0 under codegen (interp 1); String/Map worse (both backends lost the mutation) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (closures.rs mutref_caps capture-mode inference) + interpreter (eval_expr.rs closure SharedCell wrap set); shared detection in ast.rs collect_mut_method_receiver_roots

title: `karac check` rejects calling a String/Map-mutating closure more than once (`append("a"); append("b")` → `value 'append' moved here, used again`) while the identical Vec-push closure is accepted — the ownership checker classifies push_str/insert as CONSUMING the receiver (once-callable closure) instead of `mut ref self` (multi-callable) FIXED 2026-07-15 (e69d078) — root cause was NOT push_str/insert receiver-mode classification (both buf and acc are correctly mut-ref-captured). It is the use-classifier counting the closure`s OWN PARAM consume as a capture consume: the Closure body is walked with consume_origin_ctx=ClosureCapture, and a non-Copy param `s: String` passed by value into the mutator arg (`push_str(s)` / `insert(k,..)`) is a Consume → tagged ClosureCapture → the closure is marked once-callable → the 2nd call is use-after-move. The Vec twin `|x: i64| acc.push(x)` passed ONLY because i64 is Copy (never consumed). Fix: track a closure_local_stack (the closure`s own param names seeded on entry + inner `let`s) and skip the ClosureCapture origin for a consume of any closure-local name — both the consume_origins count driving once-callable and the closure_capture_consumes map the ownership pass reads. A consume of an OUTER binding is unaffected, so `|| sink(outer)` over a non-Copy outer stays once-callable (regression-tested). Tests test_closure_consuming_own_non_copy_param_is_multi_callable (+Map.insert) and test_closure_consuming_outer_non_copy_capture_still_once_callable; ownership 353 / typechecker 2043 / interpreter 1225 / closures 14 / codegen 2386; fmt+clippy clean. E2E: String closure + a unit-returning Map closure build+run AOT==interp, valgrind-clean. RESIDUAL (separate, filed B-2026-07-15-17): a closure that RETURNS m.insert(...)`s Option result hits a pre-existing closure-return-type codegen verifier error — surfaced, not caused, by this checker fix.
investigation: 2026-07-15: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-15-13. A closure `let mut append = |s| buf.push_str(s)` called twice reports `append` moved-then-used (UseAfterMove) at `karac check`, but `let mut push = |x| acc.push(x)` called twice (Vec) is check-clean, as is a single push_str/insert call. Per design.md line 4876 a `mut ref self` receiver borrow is multi-callable, so a String/Map-mutating closure should be callable repeatedly. Root cause (to confirm): the use-classifier infers the closure's callability from its captures' modes, and push_str/insert (compiler builtins, not declared `mut ref self` in stdlib .kara the way Vec.push is) are classified as consuming the receiver, making the closure once-callable. The Vec.push path resolves to a `mut ref self` signature (e.g. column.kara / builtin) and stays reusable. Fix direction: ensure the builtin String/Map/Set mutators (push_str, insert, insert_str, remove, clear, …) carry a `mut ref self` receiver mode in whatever table the closure-callability inference consults (mirroring ast::is_mutating_collection_method), so a multi-call mut-capturing closure over them is accepted. Impact: a common idiom (a string-builder / accumulator closure called in a loop) is rejected today; the runtime already handles it correctly (B-2026-07-15-13). Filed separately because it is an ownership-checker fix, not a codegen/interp runtime fix.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 13 (while fixing B-2026-07-15-13) — `karac check` rejects a closure that calls `buf.push_str` / `m.insert` more than once, but accepts the identical shape over `acc.push` (Vec) — SURFACE NOTE: ownership / use-classifier (method receiver-mode classification for compiler-builtin collection mutators: push_str / insert / …)

title: `Option.ok_or(e)` panics the compiler (ExtractOutOfRange at pattern_binding.rs) — it builds the result value in the OPTION layout {tag,w0..w2} (4 fields) but the value is a Result {tag,w0..w4} (6 fields); a downstream `match Ok(v)/Err(e)` extracts 5 payload words from the 4-field value and overruns
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from an Option/Result combinator probe. calls.rs's `ok_or` arm constructed both the Ok and Err aggregates with `struct_ty = recv_struct.get_type()` — the OPTION receiver's LLVM type `{i64 tag, i64 w0, i64 w1, i64 w2}` (4 fields, 3 payload words). But the method's result type is `Result[T,E]`, whose seeded layout is `{i64 tag, i64 w0..w4}` (6 fields, 5 payload words) with Ok/Err field_word_offsets both `(0, 5)`. So a downstream `match r { Ok(v)/Err(e) }` reads (0,5) and its bind loop does `build_extract_value(sv, start_word+j+1)` for j in 0..5 → field index 5 on a 4-field value = ExtractOutOfRange → inkwell `.unwrap()` panics the backend. This broke `ok_or` for EVERY payload type: both `ok_or(-99)` (i64 Err) and `ok_or("missing")` (String Err) ICE (the heap-safety gate only checks the OPTION's payload T = the Ok type, so a trivially-copyable Ok slips through to the broken construction). Fix: build the result in the Result layout — get `enum_layouts["Result"].llvm_type`, pack `e` into the Err slots (coerce_to_payload_words up to the Result payload width, 5), and copy the receiver's Some payload words into the Ok slots (the Ok binding reads only its natural width downstream, so trailing Result slots stay undef). Verified interp==JIT==native and leak-clean across Some→Ok, None→Err (String and i64), unwrap_or on the result, and a None/Some loop; the String Err payload round-trips leak-clean (asan). Regression: tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_option_ok_or_builds_result_layout (proven to reproduce the exact ExtractOutOfRange panic pre-fix); tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_option_ok_or_string_err_no_leak. NOTE: a heap Ok payload (`Option[String].ok_or`) and `map_err` over a String payload remain documented loud-bail deferrals (separate from this ICE). Fixed in b287a24.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 16 (Result/Option combinators) — `o.ok_or("missing")` matched as Result panicked the compiler (ExtractOutOfRange) under karac build; interp correct — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (calls.rs Option/Result combinator lowering — the `ok_or` arm builds the result value in the Option layout)

title: Closure-param inference is missing for direct collection/Result methods that take a closure (`Vec.retain(|x| …)`, `Result.and_then(|x| …)`) — the param `x` stays `?T` and any use (`x != 3`) fails, while the `.iter().map/filter/any` adaptor path infers the element type fine
investigation: 2026-07-15: `v.retain(|x| x != 3)` on a `Vec[i64]` reports `cannot compare '?T0' and 'i64'` — the closure param `x` is never bound to the element type `i64`, so the body's comparison fails. Same for `c.and_then(|x| if x > 0 { … })` on a `Result[i64,_]` (`?T8` vs i64). By contrast the iterator-adaptor path threads element types correctly: `v.iter().map(|x| x+1).sum()`, `.filter(|x| x>2).count()`, `.any(|x| x>2)` all infer without annotation, as does `Option.filter(|x| x>3)`. So the gap is specific to DIRECT collection/Result methods whose closure param should be seeded from the receiver's element / Ok type (retain, and_then; likely also sort_by's comparator params, dedup_by, etc.) — the expected-type is not pushed into the closure param during check. Workaround: annotate (`|x: i64|`). Impact: ergonomic only — a common idiom needs an explicit annotation; no wrong output, both backends agree. Fix direction: when checking a method-call argument that is a closure against a `Fn(A) -> R` parameter type, push `A` into the closure's param inference (the same expected-type propagation the iterator-adaptor path already does) for the direct-method dispatch surface.
resolution: 2026-07-15 (c4820b1): fixed by seeding the closure's un-annotated param from the receiver's payload BEFORE inferring the body, then inferring the body freely. Two coordinated changes: (1) `infer_closure_ret` (expr_method_call.rs, the and_then / or_else / map / map_or / map_err path) now publishes a per-closure param seed keyed by the closure span (`closure_param_seeds`) and drops the old infer-body-then-unify-param order (a bare `Ok(..)`/`Some(..)` body was un-adoptable under a check-mode fresh return var); the synth-mode closure arm in exprs.rs consumes the seed by index instead of minting a fresh var. (2) `Vec[T]/VecDeque[T].retain(pred)` gets a dedicated typechecker arm (concrete `Fn(T) -> bool` check-mode pushdown) plus an interpreter impl (snapshot-filter-writeback, mirroring sort_by). Verified interp==AOT for and_then/map; retain runs in interp. An explicit param annotation still wins. AOT codegen for `retain` itself still loud-bails (interp-first) — tracked separately as B-2026-07-15-19.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe rounds 17-18 — `v.retain(|x| x != 3)` and `r.and_then(|x| ...)` fail typecheck (`cannot compare '?T0' and 'i64'`) while `v.iter().filter(|x| x > 2)` infers fine — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (closure-parameter type inference for direct collection / Result methods that take a predicate/mapper closure)

title: A closure whose tail expression is an Option-returning method call miscompiles: `let put = |k, v| m.insert(k, v)` (Map.insert -> Option[V]) fails codegen with `Module verification failed: Function return type does not match operand type of return inst! ret {i64,i64,i64,i64} ... i64` — the closure fn is declared to return i64 (or a narrower shape) while its body returns the 4-word Option layout. The interpreter runs it correctly. FIXED 2026-07-15 (286636f) — infer_closure_return_type (src/codegen/closures.rs) had a MethodCall arm only for `cmp`; every other method-call tail fell to the i64 default, so an Option/Result-returning method tail (Map.insert -> Option[V], Vec.pop -> Option[T]) declared the closure fn `-> i64` against the 4-word Option body -> LLVM verifier failure. Added a general MethodCall arm that consults the lowering pass`s span-recorded enum_inst_type_exprs (via enum_inst_type_from_span) and returns the matching enum`s type-erased layout — the same treatment the Some/Ok/Err constructor arms already get; non-enum method results keep the i64 default unchanged. Verified AOT==JIT==interp, valgrind-clean, across building the closure AND consuming the returned Option via match, for both Map.insert and Vec.pop. Test test_e2e_closure_option_returning_method_tail; codegen 2388 / par_codegen 159; fmt+clippy clean. RESIDUAL (not this bug): a closure tail that is a String/Vec-returning method (`|s| s.to_uppercase()`) is a separate un-recorded shape that still falls to the i64 default — not covered here (enum-only).
investigation: 2026-07-15: surfaced immediately after B-2026-07-15-14 removed the spurious ownership rejection of `let mut put = |k: String, v: i64| m.insert(k, v)`. With check now passing, codegen emits a closure fn whose declared return type does not match the Option[i64] (`{i64,i64,i64,i64}`) the tail `m.insert(k,v)` returns -> LLVM module-verifier error, build fails loud. Narrowing the closure to a UNIT-returning body (`|k,v| { m.insert(k,v); }`) builds and runs clean (AOT==interp==2, valgrind-clean), confirming the ownership fix is sound and the gap is purely the closure return-type inference for an Option-returning tail. The sibling `infer_closure_return_type` (src/codegen/closures.rs) already special-cases a bare `None` tail and `cmp`/Ordering; it must also infer the Option[V] layout for a method-call tail whose result type is Option (Map.insert, Vec.pop, etc.), matching the typechecker`s inferred closure return type at the closure span. Fix direction: consult the typecheck result`s expr_types[closure_body_span] (or the recorded Fn(..) return type) to size the closure fn`s return type, rather than the shallow structural inference that defaults such a tail to i64. LOUD bail (no miscompile); interpreter is the correct oracle. Low severity: a rare shape (closure returning a collection-mutator`s Option result); the common unit-returning mutator closure is unaffected.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-15-14 — once the ownership checker stopped false-rejecting a Map-mutating closure, codegen was reached and failed — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (closure return-type inference — a closure whose body/tail is a method call returning Option, e.g. Map.insert -> Option[V])

title: A `<generic-struct>.field.method()` receiver reads the WRONG field for a multi-heap-field generic struct: `Pair[Vec,Vec].second.len()` returns the first field's length — the field GEP used the base (erased-i64) struct type, so field-1 landed at byte 8 (word 1 of field 0) instead of after field 0's full width
investigation: 2026-07-15: reduced from a nested-generic probe. lower_field_access_ptr (calls.rs) resolves a `<struct>.field.method()` receiver by GEPing the field off the receiver pointer, but the plain-struct branch used `struct_types[type_name]` — the BASE generic struct type, where every generic-param field is erased to i64 (1 word). For `Pair[A,B]` instantiated `Pair[Vec[i64], Vec[i64]]`, the real mono layout is `{vec(3 words), vec(3 words)}`, but the base type is `{i64, i64}`, so `build_struct_gep(base, ptr, 1)` computes the field-1 offset as 8 bytes (word 1 of field 0's `{ptr,len,cap}`) instead of 24. Reading a Vec back through that misaligned pointer, `p.second.len()` returned field 0's length (pg1: interp 5 / codegen 2), `Pair[String,String].second.len()` returned 0 (misread first's cap=0 for a static literal), `Trip[Vec,i64,Vec].c.len()` read the middle scalar (5). A SINGLE wide field or a scalar field was fine — the scalar-field print path goes through compile_field_access (extractvalue on the loaded MONO value) and binding the field first (`let s = p.second; s.len()`) also extracts correctly; only the field-receiver method-dispatch GEP was wrong. It also crashed outright on `p.second[i]` index-through-field. Fix: GEP with the per-monomorph struct type — resolve the receiver's recorded instantiation (`enum_inst_type_of_expr`) and `mono_struct_type(name, args)`, falling back to the base type for a non-generic struct. Verified interp==JIT==native for read (.len()), mutate (.push() write-through), index-through-field, and 3-field/2-type-param interleaves (Pair[Vec,Vec], Pair[String,String], Trip[Vec,i64,Vec], Rec[Vec,String]). Regression: tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_generic_struct_multi_heap_field_method_receiver (proven to reproduce empty/wrong output pre-fix). NOTE: the struct still LEAKS its heap fields at scope exit — the separate, already-filed B-2026-07-15-11 generic-struct-drop gap — so this is an output-only correctness fix (was wrong-value + leak; now correct-value + leak, the leak tracked by B-11). (NOTE: the shipped code comment / commit reference this as B-2026-07-15-17 due to a concurrent ID collision with another agent's unrelated -17; the ledger ID is B-2026-07-15-18.) Fixed in bf4b002.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 21 (nested generics) — `Pair[Vec[i64], Vec[i64]]`.second.len() returned the FIRST field's length under JIT/native; interp correct — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (field-receiver method dispatch — calls.rs lower_field_access_ptr plain-struct GEP)

title: `Vec[T]/VecDeque[T].retain(|x| pred)` has no AOT codegen lowering — typechecks and runs correctly in the interpreter (B-2026-07-15-16 added both), but `karac build` loud-bails 'method retain not yet supported in codegen'; needs the same snapshot-filter-writeback shape the interpreter uses, lowered to LLVM (allocate kept-buffer, invoke the predicate closure per element, compact in place)
investigation: 2026-07-15: B-2026-07-15-16 wired `Vec.retain` through the typechecker (Fn(T)->bool param seed) and the interpreter (snapshot the backing Vec, invoke the predicate per element, keep the Bool(true) elements, write back). AOT codegen has no arm for `retain`, so it hits the generic unsupported-method bail — clean and loud, no wrong output. Interp / `karac run` / `--interp` all execute it correctly. Fix direction: add a codegen arm mirroring the interpreter shape — read the Vec header (ptr,len,cap), allocate/compact a kept region (or compact in place with a write cursor), invoke the predicate closure value per element (same closure-invocation path map/filter adaptors already use in codegen), drop non-kept heap elements to avoid leaks, and update the len. Element drop-glue must run for filtered-out non-Copy elements (leak-class care — cross-check under LSan). Until then the interpreter is the oracle for retain programs.
resolution: 2026-07-15 (93c8c0d): added an AOT codegen arm in compile_vec_method (vec_method.rs) mirroring the interpreter's snapshot-filter-writeback as a single in-place linear pass with a write cursor: for r in 0..len, load data[r], bind the inline predicate's param and evaluate it; on keep, byte-move data[r]->data[w] and w++; on reject, free the element via emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr (the same per-element drop truncate uses, skipped for trivially-copyable elements); finally len=w (buffer+cap unchanged so a later push reuses cap). The param is bound by SHADOWING (take/restore_var_metadata + a slot save/restore) so a capturing predicate still sees the outer scope and a param shadowing an outer binding is reinstated intact; register_var_from_type_expr wires .len()/.field dispatch on the element. Compaction is drop-safe: len=w excludes the stale byte-duplicate tail [w,len) from the Vec's own scope-exit drop, so every buffer frees exactly once (a self-store at w==r is a no-op) — a bug there would be a double-free (SIGABRT), not a leak, and the LSan test would catch it. Only the INLINE-closure form is lowered (codegen owns the element drop glue a runtime callback couldn't emit); a captured-closure VALUE / fn-ref receiver falls through to a clean, actionable deferral bail pointing at --interp (interp stays the oracle for that shape). Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean across scalar, heap Vec[String] (filtered buffers freed), captured-predicate, keep-all, remove-all, nested Vec[Vec], struct-with-heap-field elements, retain-then-push (cap reuse), and VecDeque (routes through the same path). Full suite green (codegen 2392+1, memory_sanitizer 729+1, interpreter 1226, typechecker 2044; clippy+fmt clean). Adds a codegen E2E test (scalar+heap+capture) and an LSan test (heap + nested filtered-element freeing). Deferred: the captured-closure-value form (still interp-only).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-15-16 — `Vec.retain(|x| pred)` now typechecks and runs in the interpreter, but `karac build` (AOT) loud-bails — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Vec/VecDeque.retain in-place predicate filter — no LLVM lowering)

title: A generic-struct field-receiver method loud-bails for a ref-param receiver (`p: ref Pair[Vec,Vec]` → `p.second.len()`) or an indexed receiver (`v[i].second.len()` over `Vec[Pair[Vec,Vec]]`): the synth field-element binding is registered with the bare generic param type, not the concrete `Vec[i64]`, so `.len()` finds no handler
investigation: 2026-07-15: sibling of B-2026-07-15-18 (which fixed the DIRECT local-identifier receiver `p.second.len()` — both the GEP struct type and, via resolve_generic_field_te, the synth element type). lower_field_access_ptr resolves the field's TypeExpr through resolve_generic_field_te(container, ...), which pulls the concrete instantiation from enum_inst_type_of_expr(container). For a DIRECT local `p` that reads enum_inst_var_types['p'] and resolves `second: B` → `Vec[i64]`. But for (a) a `ref Pair[..]` PARAMETER receiver and (b) an `v[i]` INDEXED receiver over `Vec[Pair[..]]`, enum_inst_type_of_expr returns None (params aren't recorded there; an Index expr is span-keyed and empty), so field_te stays the bare param `B`; compile_method_via_synth_elem_ptr then register_var_from_type_expr's the synth `__field_elem_N` as a non-Vec, and `.len()` falls through with `no handler for method 'len' on variable '__field_elem_0'` (a clean codegen error + `--interp` hint). Direct-local (post-B-18), field-store (`p.second = v`), and index-store (`p.second[i] = x`) all work; gs1/gs2 verified. LATENT RISK: my B-18 GEP fix keys the mono struct type off the SAME enum_inst_type_of_expr(inner); for the indexed/ref-param receiver that also returns None → the GEP would fall back to the erased base struct type, so if a future change wired the dispatch without also resolving the instantiation, the field GEP would SILENTLY read the wrong field (the B-18 hazard). Fix path (do both together): teach the field-receiver instantiation resolution to source the concrete args from (1) a ref/owned PARAM's declared type (the signature is known at compile time) and (2) a Vec/Slice/Array element's recorded instantiation (var_elem_type_exprs[container]) for an indexed receiver — then both resolve_generic_field_te (dispatch) and mono_struct_type (GEP) get the concrete `Pair[Vec,Vec]`. Loud-bail, so no correctness hazard today; medium because generic-struct-by-ref and Vec-of-generic-struct are common shapes. (NOTE: filed under B-2026-07-15-20; a concurrent agent used -19 for an unrelated retain-codegen gap.)
resolution: 2026-07-15 (355d5da): resolved the field-receiver instantiation for all three loud-bailing receiver shapes via two paired sources feeding BOTH the dispatch resolution (resolve_generic_field_te) and the mono-struct field GEP (calls.rs lower_field_access_ptr). (1) functions.rs param-bind: record a concretely-instantiated generic-STRUCT param into enum_inst_var_types (sibling to the existing generic-enum param record) — registration_te already peels ref/mut ref, so the OWNED param (`b: Box[String]` → `b.v.len()`) and the ref param (`p: ref Pair[Vec,Vec]` → `p.second.len()`) are both covered by the same record. This is the piece the mono-path record (mono.rs concrete_generic_struct_inst) skipped: it returns None for a fully-concrete instantiation (any_bound=false), so a non-generic function's param was never recorded. (2) expr_ops.rs new receiver_struct_inst helper wraps enum_inst_type_of_expr with an INDEXED-container fallback — the instantiation of `v[i]` is the container's recorded element TypeExpr (var_elem_type_exprs), gated to a genuine generic struct/enum inst and excluding a range index; routed into resolve_generic_field_te and the calls.rs GEP lookup so `v[i].second.len()` over Vec[Pair[Vec,Vec]] resolves. LATENT B-18 GEP hazard the investigation flagged is closed in the same stroke: both dispatch and GEP now source the same instantiation, so the field GEP uses the correct mono struct type for param/indexed receivers (no silent wrong-field read). Interp was already correct; this closes the AOT loud-bail (build failure, not a miscompile). Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind/LSan-clean across all three shapes plus a mono self-receiver, a non-generic-struct param, and a Box[Vec] owned param (all unaffected). Full suite green (codegen 2390, memory_sanitizer 727+2, typechecker 2044, ownership 353, interpreter 1226, par_codegen 159; clippy+fmt clean). Adds a codegen E2E test (Pair[Vec,Vec] ref-param + indexed, output correctness) and an LSan test (single-field Box[String] across the three receiver shapes). NOTE: a MULTI-field generic struct (`Pair[Vec,Vec]`) still leaks BOTH bare-T fields at scope exit — that is the pre-existing B-2026-07-15-11 offset-erasure residual (present with no method call at all), orthogonal to this dispatch fix; the LSan test uses single-field wrappers to isolate the dispatch signal.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round 22 (sibling shapes of the B-2026-07-15-18 field-GEP fix) — `show(p: ref Pair[Vec,Vec]) { p.second.len() }` and `v[i].second.len()` over `Vec[Pair[Vec,Vec]]` loud-bail in codegen while interp runs — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (field-receiver method dispatch over a GENERIC struct — calls.rs lower_field_access_ptr / resolve_generic_field_te instantiation resolution for ref-param and indexed receivers)

fix: a8d47f2 (Part A: RC-elision default-ON) + 36748bb (Part B: Some-binding elision + TCO) + Part C (borrow-forward relaxation of condition 1, folds in the wrapper/helper shape #110)
title: Read-only `Option[shared]` tree traversal ran ~1.8x behind equal-safety Rust — the residual was per-node REFCOUNT TRAFFIC (the balanced retain/release pair, NOT elided; the earlier 'RC is free / already elided' note was wrong) plus its TCO-blocking release epilogue. FIXED: the rc-elision convention (`rc_elide.rs`) is now default-ON (Part A: param + child-arg retains) and the Some(n)-binding acquire/RcDec is elided on an elidable-param scrutinee (Part B), which unblocks LLVM tailcallelim. #112 has_path_sum: 2.16x -> 1.13x behind C (parity with equal-safety Rust); zero rc ops per node + tail recursion loop-ified
investigation: 2026-07-15: minimal probe — `fn sum(node: Option[TreeNode]) -> i64 { match node { None => 0, Some(n) => n.val + sum(n.left) + sum(n.right) } }` over 8 pooled balanced 31-node trees, K=3,000,000 reps (the #104/#110/#111 read-only-traversal shape). Best-of-5 wall clock, same container: kara by-value 0.49s, kara ref-borrow (`ref Option[TreeNode]`) 0.49s, C clang-O3 0.22s, rustc -O 0.17s, rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on 0.34s. THREE findings. (1) RC IS FREE: by-value and ref-borrow are identical to the millisecond, so the retain/release on a non-escaping shared traversal is already elided (borrow_elision.rs) — the long-standing '~11% RC pass-by-value' figure (originally measured for kata #100 and copied into #104/#110/#111 READMEs) is STALE; there is no measurable refcount cost on this shape today. (2) ~HALF the headline 2.1x (kara 0.49 vs stock rustc 0.17) is OVERFLOW-CHECKING, which kara does by default and stock `rustc -O` skips: the equal-safety baseline is rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on at 0.34s (on this add-heavy micro-traversal overflow checks alone ~2x Rust; on a real kata ~25% — #111 rust 275ms -> rust-ovf 344ms). kara emits the same `sadd.with.overflow` + branch as Rust's checked build (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs emit_checked_int_arith), so this component is at parity and is a deliberate safety choice, not a defect. (3) RESIDUAL ~1.45x: kara 0.49 / rust-ovf 0.34 = 1.44x on the probe, corroborated by kata #111 (kara 504ms / rust-ovf 344ms = 1.47x). This is the genuine codegen headroom — per-node Option[shared] niche decode + match + field GEP + the two recursive calls lower heavier than C/Rust's raw null-check-load-recurse. IMPACT: only bites PURE read-only traversals (no allocation, no compute per node) — the exposed worst case shared by #100/#104/#110/#111. Allocation- or compute-bound siblings amortize it away and land kara at-or-ahead of Rust (#107 kara 30% ahead of Rust; #108/#109 tie or ahead). So it is a real but narrow perf item. FIX DIRECTION: leaner Option[shared] match/field lowering (fold the niche null-check with the field load; avoid redundant reloads of the shared pointer across the two child recursions) and tighter recursion codegen; measure against this probe. NOT urgent, NOT a correctness issue. FOLLOW-UP for the corpus: correct the stale '~11% RC' wording in the traversal-kata READMEs (kara-katas #104/#110/#111/#100) to the measured decomposition above (RC free; gap = overflow-safety vs stock rustc + ~1.45x Option[shared]-lowering residual vs equal-safety rustc). 2026-07-16 CORRECTION + measured decomposition (perf pass, container figures): kara sequential 0.448s vs rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on 0.346s = 1.29x on the probe. (1) The 'RC IS FREE / retain-release already elided (borrow_elision.rs)' claim was WRONG: borrow_elision.rs is the Vec-index CLONE elision, not shared-RC elision; the by-value==ref-borrow timing equality showed EQUAL rc cost in both modes, not zero. The compiled `sum` carries FIVE plain rc RMWs per Some-node visit: +1 Some-binding acquire (pattern_binding.rs), +1 per n.left/n.right chain arg (share_option_shared_field_ref_for_arg), -1 binding cleanup, -1 param exit dec (track_rc_option_var); the caller retains the pool element per rep. (2) IR-surgery experiment (strip those rc ops from the captured module, identical opt -O2 + llc pipeline both sides): 0.453 -> 0.390 best-of-7 = -14%, ~2/3 of the residual closed; output exact, valgrind clean — an rc-silent borrowed traversal is semantically sound on this shape. Remaining ~9% vs rust-ovf is genuine niche/match/recursion lowering density. (3) CONCRETE FIX DIRECTION: interprocedural borrowed-param convention for read-only non-escaping Option[shared] params — ownership-side analysis emitting a plain-data fn->per-param-borrow-flags map (whitelist: param used only as match scrutinee; Some-binding used only for scalar field reads and Option[shared] field args to borrowed positions; no stores of any kind; no calls other than borrowed-position calls (fixed-point over candidates, self-recursion included); every call site's arg a borrowable place (identifier/field/index/None), no fresh temps; fn never reified; pass disabled for REPL/test-module/declare-only compiles to keep cross-module conventions consistent), consumed at exactly four codegen sites: skip the share_option_shared_{ref,field,index}_ref_for_arg trio for borrowed positions in the call arg loop; skip track_rc_option_var's cleanup for borrowed params in the fn prologue (keep the var_option_shared_heap registration); skip the Some-binding acquire+cleanup when the match scrutinee is a borrowed param (pattern_binding.rs — sibling of the existing b2_skips_counts C2a count-free role gate). (4) The probe now lowers sequentially BY NECESSITY: the auto-par reduction on this loop shape was an unsound data race (B-2026-07-16-3, fixed b057501); re-parallelizing read-only shared traversals requires the rc-silent convention FIRST plus a reduction-gate relaxation that proves rc-silence of the emitted body. 2026-07-16 RESOLUTION (Part A landed): the fix direction in point (3) above — the interprocedural borrowed-param convention for read-only non-escaping Option[shared] params — ALREADY EXISTS as `src/rc_elide.rs` (`safe_elidable_ref_params`, four soundness conditions + condition-4 payload-escape guard, `docs/spikes/rc-elide-ref-params.md`); it had shipped GATED OFF behind `KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS`. Disassembly of the #112 hot `has_path_sum` confirmed the earlier 'RC is free / already elided' framing was FALSE at HEAD: the by-value walk emits 2 retains + 2 releases per visited node (Some-binding acquire + one child-arg retain per recursion + two exit decs), and by-value (0.53s) is actually FASTER than ref-borrow (0.64s), not equal — the ref form adds indirection. FLIPPED the convention DEFAULT-ON (ownership.rs, opt out `=0`) after re-validating at HEAD: memory_sanitizer 736/0/1 == elide-off, codegen E2E 2400/0, par_codegen 173/0, lib 1025/0, clippy clean; corpus re-bench #100/#104/#111/#112 = 1.32x/1.23x/1.29x/1.18x faster, byte-identical output, #110 correctly excluded, +0 compile time. This elides the child-arg retains + the param-exit dec (the C2b borrow-skip channel) — ~2/3 of the per-node RC, matching the earlier IR-surgery -14%. REMAINING (Part B, this session): the surviving Some(n)-binding retain/release (pattern_binding.rs) keeps the right-recursion out of tail position, blocking the tail-call→loop that C/Rust get (C runs 1 real call/node + a loop on the right spine; kara runs 2 real calls). Eliding it — sound by the SAME condition-4 proof (the payload is projection-only) — should let LLVM tailcallelim fire; realistic ceiling ~0.33-0.38s (≈1.2-1.3x equal-safety Rust; the per-node overflow-check `jo` remains, which C omits). Status stays open until Part B lands or is shown infeasible. 2026-07-16 PART B + RESOLUTION: after Part A one RC pair survived — the Some(n)-binding acquire + scope-exit RcDec (pattern_binding.rs:198). For an elidable param condition 4 already proves the payload is projection-only (a non-escaping alias kept alive by the caller-held param), so the pair is the same balanced no-op; elided it via a new pattern_binding_scrutinee_is_elidable_param flag (compile_match sets it from scrutinee_is_elidable_param, a bare-ident-names-an-elidable-param classifier). Second-order payoff: that RcDec was a POST-CALL RELEASE EPILOGUE keeping the tail recursion out of tail position — removing it lets LLVM tailcallelim loop-ify the self-recursion (the C/Rust structure). Object-code confirmed: #112 has_path_sum now has ZERO rc ops per node and the right recursion is a `jne` back-edge, not a `call`. Measured (bench K=6M best-of-7 median): baseline(=0) 0.559s -> Part A 0.453s -> Part B 0.291s = 1.92x, vs C 0.258s (2.16x -> 1.13x behind C; at parity with rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on, the lone residual being the per-node overflow-check `jo` C omits). Corpus: short-circuit shapes TCO and ~double (#100 is_same/and 2.20x, #112 or 1.90x); combine-both shapes shed the RC but can't tail-call (#111 min 1.37x, #104 max 1.31x); #110 correctly excluded. Validated default-path: memory_sanitizer 736/0/1 == elide-off, codegen E2E 2400/0, par_codegen 173/0, lib 1025/0, clippy clean; #112 byte-identical interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par/=0 + valgrind-clean; new pin asan_rc_elide_some_binding_or_recursion_walk_no_leak. Fixed in a8d47f2 (Part A) + 36748bb (Part B). Escape hatch KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0. RESIDUAL: the combine-both (max/min) shapes still make two real calls — a further TCO would need accumulator-passing or a different lowering, not pursued. 2026-07-16 PART C: Part B left one shape excluded — a thin wrapper delegating to a recursive helper, `fn is_balanced(root) -> bool { check(root) != -1 }` (#110). `check` passed conditions 2-4 but condition 1 rejected it because the wrapper calls `check(root)` with a BARE IDENTIFIER (scored as a move). It isn't a move: `root` is a `Ref`-mode param (confirmed via `karac query ownership`), so its referent is kept alive by the enclosing frame for the whole call. Relaxed condition 1 to accept a BORROW-FORWARD — a bare identifier naming a `Ref`-mode parameter of the function being walked — alongside the projection form (rc_elide.rs Scan.cur_ref_params, set per-body from param_modes; disabled inside closures, fail-closed; a bare OWNED local is still a move). Soundness is the same outlives-the-call guarantee a projection gives and does NOT require the enclosing fn to elide (a non-elided is_balanced still holds root's +1 across the check(root) call). Result: `check` now elides ({"check": [("node",0)]}); #110 goes from the sweep's holdout to 1.67x faster (elide-off 0.597s -> 0.357s, zero rc ops per node; no TCO since 1+max(l,r) combines both children). Validated default path: memory_sanitizer 738/0/1, codegen E2E 2400/0, par_codegen 173/0, rc_elide unit 7/0 (+2 new: borrow_forward_of_ref_param_keeps_helper_elidable, bare_local_forward_still_excludes_helper); #110 byte-identical interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par/=0 + valgrind-clean; new asan pin asan_rc_elide_borrow_forward_ref_param_no_leak. So the whole read-only-traversal sweep (#100/#104/#110/#111/#112) now elides. RESIDUAL unchanged: combine-both (max/min) shapes still make two real calls (no TCO) — an accumulator-passing lowering could add it, not pursued.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata-corpus perf investigation (owner asked whether the ~1.8x read-only-tree-traversal gap vs C/Rust is fixable or expected) — measured decomposition on a minimal tree-sum probe — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (per-node Option[shared] traversal lowering: niche-match + field GEP + recursion)

title: `let bound = o.inner` moving a struct-typed heap-bearing field out of an owned struct double-frees the inner Vec buffer at scope exit — both `bound`'s and `o`'s StructDrop free it (`free(): double free detected`, exit 134). The struct-var tracking path in the Let arm suppressed only Identifier (`let g = f`) and TupleIndex (`let inr = h.ps.0`) move-out RHS, never a FieldAccess struct-typed field, so no cap-zero disarmed the source's drop.
investigation: df3 (generic Outer[T]{inner:Inner[T]{data:Vec[T]}}) and df4 (non-generic equivalent) both double-free on `let bound = o.inner`; both pass `karac check`. Root cause: in stmts.rs the struct-var tracking path (`track_struct_var_inst` for a `var_type_names`-annotated struct binding) applies move-suppression only for `ExprKind::Identifier` (suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex) and `ExprKind::TupleIndex` (suppress_tuple_index_move_source) RHS — there was no `ExprKind::FieldAccess` arm, so moving a struct-typed field out of an owned struct left the source field's Vec caps live and `o`'s StructDrop freed the same buffer `bound` had taken over. Fix: add the FieldAccess arm calling `suppress_struct_field_move_into_literal(value)` (guarded on a non-owned_struct_params source, mirroring the Vec-field #17-gap-2 and enum-field #19 peer sites); its nested-aggregate arm (`zero_aggregate_field_caps`) recurses into the moved-out struct's Vec/String leaves and cap-zeroes them in the source slot. Only the `Vec.new()+push`-into-a-tracked-local construction reproduces — an inline array-literal field materializes its temp directly into the field on an already-balanced path (v1/v3 probes clean pre-fix), so the regression tests build each Vec via a local. The double-free fires at scope exit AFTER all prints, so the output-only codegen harness cannot witness it (verified: E2E program printed correct output with exit 134 pre-fix); the ASAN sibling is the memory guard. Verified clean post-fix under valgrind (df3/df4/deep-nest/multi-field/self.field-in-method/sibling-heap-field) and via `asan_nested_struct_field_move_out_no_double_free` (ASAN-dirty pre-fix, clean post). Distinct from the deferred B-2026-07-15-11 generic-struct-drop leak (which is a mono-awareness gap in move-out cap-zeroing for a whole-struct move-out); this is a plain missing-arm gap that reproduces for non-generic structs too, and is fixed without touching the mono-suppression machinery.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round (nested-struct move-out) — `let bound = o.inner` moving a struct-typed heap-bearing field out of an owned struct; native (karac build) double-freed the inner Vec buffer (exit 134), interpreter oracle correct. Both generic and non-generic reproduce; both `karac check`-clean. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (stmts.rs Let-arm struct-var move-suppression — the FieldAccess RHS case was missing)

title: Moving a struct with a Map/Set handle field or an enum-with-heap-payload field double-frees the handle/enum buffer at scope exit (SIGSEGV for Map/Set, `free(): double free` for enum). A Map/Set field is a bare `ptr` the struct's `FieldDrop::MapOrSet` frees UNCONDITIONALLY via `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec`; `zero_struct_move_caps` had no Map/Set arm and the nested-field suppressor's LLVM-type walk saw neither the ptr handle nor the enum's i64 words, so the SOURCE stayed live and double-freed against the destination's drop.
investigation: Found while probing siblings of the B-2026-07-15-22 nested-struct-field move-out fix. dfA (`let g = f`, Holder{m: Map[i64,i64]}) SIGSEGVs (exit 139); dfE (Set field) same; df9/dfB/dfC (nested-field move of a struct carrying Map/Set) SIGSEGV; dfD (nested-field move of a struct with an enum-with-String-payload field) `free(): double free` (exit 134). All karac check-clean, interpreter correct. Root cause: `zero_struct_move_caps` (call_dispatch.rs) — the type-name-driven whole-struct move-suppressor — handles Vec/VecDeque/String (cap+len zero), Option (tag zero), enum (payload caps), nested struct (recurse), tuple, and the HTTP handle, but has NO Map/Set arm; the field's `FieldDrop::MapOrSet` (synth_drop.rs) frees the handle UNCONDITIONALLY (no `handle != 0` guard, unlike HttpHandleFree). And `suppress_struct_field_move_into_literal` (param_own.rs, the nested-field move-out suppressor used by B-22's FieldAccess arm) matched only vec-struct fields and `aggregate_has_heap_field` structs via the LLVM-type-driven `zero_aggregate_field_caps`, which cannot classify a Map/Set ptr handle nor an enum's all-i64 tagged-union words. The line-4350 comment in `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex` had flagged the Map/Set gap as 'needs a separate runtime change (null-marker convention through karac_map_free)' — STALE: `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` (map.rs:432) and every map-free variant already early-return on `map.is_null()`, so nulling the source handle is a pure codegen null-store. Fix: (1) add a Map/Set arm to `zero_struct_move_caps` storing a null ptr into the handle slot; (2) route named non-shared struct fields through `zero_struct_move_caps` in `suppress_struct_field_move_into_literal` (comprehensive: Vec/String/Map/Set/enum/nested), keeping `zero_aggregate_field_caps` for mono-correct Vec/String cap-zero (idempotent overlap on Vec). Verified: Map+Set whole-struct move, Map nested-field move (non-generic + generic Map[K,V]), Vec+Map mixed, enum-with-String nested move — all output-correct + valgrind/ASAN clean (asan test was 2500-error dirty pre-fix). Regression tests: test_e2e_struct_move_out_map_set_enum_field_no_crash + asan_struct_move_out_map_set_enum_field_no_double_free. KNOWN REMAINING (filed as B-2026-07-15-24): a bare generic-param heap field positioned BEFORE a Map field in the same struct still mis-GEPs the Map handle at the base-layout offset (mono-blind) — pre-existing, unchanged by this fix.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round (Map/Set/enum move-out) — moving a struct carrying a Map/Set handle field or an enum-with-heap-payload field (whole-struct move `let g = f` OR nested-field move `let bound = o.inner`) crashed native (karac build): SIGSEGV for Map/Set, `free(): double free` for enum. Interpreter oracle correct; all karac check-clean. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (call_dispatch.rs `zero_struct_move_caps` had no Map/Set arm; param_own.rs `suppress_struct_field_move_into_literal` relied on LLVM-type-driven `zero_aggregate_field_caps` blind to Map/Set ptr handles and enum all-i64 leaves)
