title: `zero_struct_move_caps` GEPs a Map/Set handle field at the BASE struct-layout offset; when a PRECEDING field is a bare generic param mono'd to a wider heap type (e.g. `Inner[T]{a: T, m: Map[i64,i64]}`, T=Vec[i64]), the base layout erases `a` to i64 (1 word) but the mono widens it (Vec = 3 words), so the Map handle's null-store (B-23) lands at the wrong offset and the real handle stays live → double-free / SIGSEGV on move-out.
investigation: dfI: `struct GInner[T]{a: T, m: Map[i64,i64]}` / `struct GOuter[T]{inner: GInner[T]}`, `let bound = o.inner` — SIGSEGVs (exit 139) on native, both before and after the B-2026-07-15-23 Map/Set move-suppression fix AND after the concurrent B-2026-07-15-11 bare-T mono-drop fix (5843cb9). Root cause: `zero_struct_move_caps` (and the enum/Vec arms too) build their field GEPs with the BASE `struct_types[name]` layout, in which a bare generic-param field is erased to a single i64 word; a monomorph that binds that param to a heap type (Vec = 3 words / String = 3 words) widens the field, shifting every following field's offset. So the B-23 Map-handle null-store targets the base offset (inside the widened bare-T field) instead of the real mono offset, leaving the handle live → double-free. This is the same mono-blindness class as B-2026-07-15-11 (which fixed the DROP classifier's bare-T resolution via mono-subst but did NOT thread the mono layout into `zero_struct_move_caps`'s move-suppression GEPs). Fix-path: make `zero_struct_move_caps` mono-aware — accept the per-monomorph LLVM struct type (available at the call site from the binding's slot `.ty` / `enum_inst_var_types`) and GEP fields with it instead of the base `struct_types` type, mirroring the B-11 drop-classifier subst threading. Deferred (not fixed with B-23) because it touches the delicate move-suppression GEP machinery shared by Vec/String/enum/Map arms and the shape is extremely contrived (a bare generic-param heap field placed before a Map field in the same struct, then moved). No non-generic or concrete-generic Map/Set/enum move-out is affected — those are all fixed by B-23. | 2026-07-15 UPDATE (investigation, still open): confirmed the root cause is BROADER than move-suppression alone — the STRUCT-DROP field GEPs (synth_drop.rs emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl, `let st = struct_types[name]`) also use the BASE erased layout, so a NO-MOVE program that merely drops such a struct at scope exit ALSO crashes: probe `struct GInner[T]{a:T, m:Map[i64,i64]}; let g = GInner{a:vec, m:map}; ...` (no move) SIGSEGVs on g's scope-exit drop; reordering the fields so Map precedes the bare-T field (`{m:Map, a:T}`) is clean (offset unshifted), pinning it to the widened-preceding-field offset shift. A full fix must thread the per-monomorph struct type into (1) emit_struct_drop_synthesis field GEPs, (2) zero_struct_move_caps_mono field GEPs, (3) the nested GOuter recursion + o.inner move-suppression, and (4) the `let bound = o.inner` bound-binding instantiation record (so bound's own drop is mono) — AND is entangled with the B-2026-07-15-11 MULTI-field bare-T leak residual (the offset-0 `a:T` field is not dropped even once the Map offset is correct, so the drop path leaks 32B where it used to crash). A trial patch (mono-GEP in the drop synthesis + move-suppression + bound record via plain_field_type_expr) turned the no-move `droponly` SIGSEGV into the B-11 leak but did NOT resolve the nested `moveout` repro (GOuter drop + recursion still base-layout). Deferred as a focused multi-site pass co-scheduled with the B-11 multi-field bare-T drop; low severity + contrived, no non-generic / concrete-generic shape affected.
resolution: 2026-07-16 (7a2bfce): FIXED across all four sites the investigation flagged. Root cause was the mono-blindness class (cf. B-2026-07-15-11): the drop-synthesis and move-suppression field GEPs used the BASE `struct_types` layout, which erases a bare generic-param field to one i64 word; a monomorph binding that param to a wider heap type (Vec/String = a 3-word {ptr,len,cap} triple) widens the field and shifts every following field's offset, so the Map handle null-store / following-Vec cap-zero landed at the wrong offset (reads already used the mono layout, so only the drop crashed). Fix: (1) new `mono_struct_type_from_subst` (types_lowering.rs) builds the per-monomorph LLVM struct type from each field's subst-resolved TypeExpr; (2) `emit_struct_drop_synthesis_impl` (synth_drop.rs) and `zero_struct_move_caps_mono` (call_dispatch.rs) override their base `st` with it under a real subst, so ALL field GEPs + get_field_type_at_index become mono-correct in one place; (3) the bare-T heap-field classifier (drop VecOrString + move-suppression len/cap zero) is generalized from single-field to ANY position — the original B-11 `kinds.len()==1` / `field_names.len()==1` gates existed ONLY because the base layout mis-offset a mid bare-T field; (4) `field_move_out_struct_inst` (param_own.rs) derives the moved-out binding's concrete instantiation (GInner[Vec[i64]]) from the object's recorded inst + the field's declared type, feeding both `bound`'s own mono drop (enum_inst_var_types, stmts.rs let-inst recording) and the mono source-suppression (suppress_struct_field_move_into_literal now calls zero_struct_move_caps_mono with the derived subst). The entangled B-11 multi-field leak residual the prior trial patch turned up is ALSO resolved: generalizing the classifier drains the offset-0 bare-T field too (no leak where it used to crash). All gated to subst.is_some() — non-generic and concrete-generic structs are byte-identical. Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean (0 errors, 0 leaked) across: String bare-T before Map (no move); Vec[String] bare-T before Map (drains inner elements); two bare-T Vec fields before Map with a whole-struct move (`let g = f`); and a nested field move-out (`let bound = o.inner`). Full suite green (codegen 2397, memory_sanitizer 734; clippy+fmt clean). Adds test_e2e_mono_bare_t_heap_field_before_map_no_crash + asan_mono_bare_t_heap_field_before_map_no_double_free. NOTE: while testing, a SEPARATE pre-existing double-free surfaced (a struct FIELD of type Map[K, heap-value] like Map[i64, String] double-frees at scope-exit drop, non-generic AND generic, confirmed on the clean tree — orthogonal to this mono-layout bug); filed as B-2026-07-16-1. The B-24 tests deliberately use Map[i64,i64] scalar-value maps to isolate the layout fix from that unrelated bug.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe dfI, found while fixing B-2026-07-15-23 — a bare generic-param heap field positioned BEFORE a Map field in the same struct, then moved (whole-struct or nested-field), SIGSEGVs; both the interpreter and the B-23 fix leave it crashing (unchanged pre/post B-23 and pre/post the B-2026-07-15-11 mono-drop fix). — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (call_dispatch.rs `zero_struct_move_caps` GEPs the Map handle field using the BASE erased struct layout, not the per-monomorph layout — the mono-blindness class, cf. B-2026-07-15-11)

title: Reassigning a struct's heap-owning field (`o.f = x`) never drops the OLD field value: leaks it for a fresh RHS (`h.v = [9,8,7]` strands the old Vec buffer), or double-frees / SIGSEGVs for a moved-binding RHS (`h.v = v2`, `h.s = heap_str`, `h.m = m2`) because the source's own scope-exit cleanup isn't suppressed. Separately, Map/Set VARIABLE reassignment (`m = m2`, `set_a = set_b`) leaks the old handle AND double-frees the source — the Assign arm's eager-free + move-suppression are Vec/String-only (`lhs_is_tracked_vec`); Vec/String var reassignment works.
investigation: MATRIX (native): Vec VAR reassign (`a = b`) CLEAN; String VAR reassign CLEAN; Vec FIELD reassign fresh-RHS (`h.v = [9,8,7]`) LEAK 32B (exit 0); Vec FIELD reassign moved-RHS (`h.v = v2`) DOUBLE-FREE exit 134 + 32B leak; String FIELD reassign heap-RHS (`h.s = heap_str`) DOUBLE-FREE exit 134 + 56B leak; Map FIELD reassign (`h.m = m2`) SIGSEGV exit 139 + 72B leak; Map VAR reassign (`m = m2`) SIGSEGV exit 139 + 72B leak; Set VAR reassign SIGSEGV exit 139. (Note: a String FIELD reassign with STATIC-LITERAL operands is clean only because cap=0 literals own no heap — not a real pass.) All karac check-clean, interpreter correct; confirmed pre-existing on baseline e0d1fea (not caused by the B-22/B-23 move-suppression fixes, which are separate paths). ROOT CAUSE — two gaps: (1) `compile_field_store` (expr_ops.rs:981-1358) stores the new value into a plain-struct field slot with NO drop of the old field value and NO source-suppression — it handles union/SoA/indexed-shared/option-shared field stores specially but the plain heap-field (Vec/String/Map/Set) case just overwrites, so the old buffer/handle leaks and (for a moved-binding RHS) the source's scope-exit cleanup double-frees the now-shared buffer. (2) The stmts.rs Assign-arm eager-free (~5293) + `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` (~5356) that make Vec/String VAR reassignment correct are gated on `lhs_is_tracked_vec = vec_elem_types.contains_key(name)`, which is false for Map/Set vars — so `m = m2` neither frees the old handle nor suppresses m2's `FreeMapHandle`. FIX-PATH: (a) in `compile_field_store`'s plain-struct-field branch, before the store, load the old field value and drop it via the same per-field-type machinery the struct-drop uses (Vec/String `emit_free_vec_buffer_if_owned` / cap-guarded free; Map/Set `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` — already null-safe; enum `zero_enum_payload_caps`-free; nested struct recursive drop), gated to non-shared/non-option/non-SoA fields (those paths already handle their own old-side release — do NOT double it); then suppress the source binding's cleanup when the RHS is a moved Identifier (Vec via `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg`, Map/Set via `suppress_map_cleanup_for_tail_identifier`). (b) In the Assign var-reassign arm, add a `lhs_is_tracked_map`/Set parallel to the `lhs_is_tracked_vec` eager-free: free the loaded old handle and suppress the source's `FreeMapHandle`. Both legs are needed; each is verifiable with an ASAN loop test (leak accumulates) plus an output test. Risk: the field-store path has many special-cased branches (shared struct, Option[shared], SoA, HTTP handle) that already manage old-side release — the drop-old must be added ONLY on the plain-struct-field fall-through to avoid double-freeing those. Deferred from immediate fix because it touches the hot `compile_field_store` + Assign core with heavy special-casing and warrants a focused, carefully-tested pass rather than a late-session change.
resolution: 2026-07-15 (c588e30): fixed both root-cause gaps. (a) expr_ops.rs compile_field_store — the plain-owned-struct-field store (the fall-through past shared/Option[shared]/SoA/union/indexed-shared, each of which returns early and manages its own old-side release) now runs drop_old_plain_struct_field before overwriting: GEP the field slot and drop the old value via emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr (Vec/String buffer free, Map/Set type-erased per-entry free, enum payload, nested-struct recursion; scalar no-op), with the field TypeExpr resolved through the receiver's generic instantiation first. plain_field_type_expr classifies the field so the Assign FieldAccess arm suppresses the moved source (Vec/String via suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg, Map/Set via suppress_map_cleanup_for_tail_identifier). Only the plain fall-through calls the drop, so the special-cased branches never double-drop. (b) stmts.rs Assign var-reassign arm + call_dispatch.rs eager_free_old_map_var_handle — a lhs_is_tracked_map/Set parallel to the Vec eager-free reuses the var's queued FreeMapHandle per-entry drop params via emit_free_one_map_handle on the current handle, then suppresses the moved source's FreeMapHandle; the var's own FreeMapHandle stays and frees the new handle at scope exit. Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean across every matrix entry (Vec/String/Map field reassign fresh+moved, Map/Set var reassign) plus self-alias (m=m no-op), Map[i64,String] var reassign, and repeated field reassign. Full suite green (codegen 2393+1, memory_sanitizer 730+1, typechecker 2044, ownership 353, interpreter 1226, par_codegen 159; clippy+fmt clean). Adds a codegen E2E output test + a loop-driven LSan test across all shapes. TWO pre-existing, ORTHOGONAL issues surfaced while testing (NOT caused by this fix, NOT touched here): (1) Map[K, String].get().unwrap() double-frees the value INDEPENDENT of any reassignment (filed separately); (2) `v = v` Vec self-alias leaks 32B via the existing (pre-fix) suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg firing on a self-assign (the eager-free is already self-alias-gated; the suppress is not). Both are minor/contrived relative to the reassignment class fixed here.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: overnight probe round (reassignment of heap-owning vars/fields) — reassigning a struct's heap field (`o.f = x`) or a Map/Set variable (`m = m2`) leaks the old value and (when the RHS is a moved binding) double-frees the source; native crashes / leaks, interpreter oracle correct, all karac check-clean. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (expr_ops.rs `compile_field_store` never drops the old field value for a plain-struct heap field; stmts.rs Assign arm eager-free + `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` are gated to `lhs_is_tracked_vec` only, so Map/Set var reassignment is uncovered)

title: An INLINE `map.get(k).unwrap()` whose value is heap-owning (`Map[i64, String]` / `Map[i64, Vec[..]]`) double-frees: `println(m.get(2).unwrap())` and `m.get(2).unwrap().len()` free the bucket's buffer as an inline temporary AND again at the map's scope-exit per-entry free. Binding first (`let v = m.get(2).unwrap(); println(v)`) is CLEAN — the residual is only the directly-consumed temporary path.
investigation: 2026-07-15: probes a/b/c. (a) `println(m.get(2).unwrap())` over Map[i64,String] → free(): double free (exit 134). (b) `let v = m.get(2).unwrap(); println(v)` → CLEAN. (c) `m.get(2).unwrap().len()` over Map[i64,Vec[i64]] → double free. Interpreter correct for all. So the bound-binding leg (B-2026-07-14-15, FIXED) reconstructs/clones the value for the `let`, but the INLINE-temporary consumption path (value fed straight into println / a method call) still hands out an ALIAS of the map's live bucket {ptr,len,cap} rather than an owned clone; the temporary's drop then frees the buffer the map still owns and re-frees at the map's scope-exit per-entry free. Pre-existing and ORTHOGONAL to the B-2026-07-15-25 reassignment fix (surfaced only because a B-25 test happened to print an inline get().unwrap()). Fix-path: in the Map get().unwrap() lowering, deep-clone the heap value for the inline-temporary case the same way the bound-binding leg does (or route the inline temp through the bound-binding reconstruction), gated to heap value types (String/Vec/nested); scalar values are unaffected. Verifiable with an ASAN one-liner (`println(m.get(k).unwrap())`) plus an output test.
resolution: 2026-07-15 (e465acb): fixed by zeroing the borrow view's `cap` at the unwrap payload reconstruction (calls.rs try_compile_option_result_method, after rebuild_value_from_payload_words) when the receiver is `<map>.get(k)` on a non-shared heap value — gated by the new unwrap_receiver_is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get (stmts.rs, the receiver-form sibling of is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get_unwrap). map.get(k) packs a BORROW of the bucket's {ptr,len,cap} (the map owns the buffer); consumed inline, the temporary's own `cap > 0` free-guard (print's emit_print_and_free_string, method-receiver/arg drop) freed it, and the map's scope-exit per-entry drop freed it again → double-free. Zeroing cap makes the value a {ptr,len,0} read-only borrow view: EVERY consumer's `cap > 0` guard skips the free (uniform, no per-site handling, no clone), the map stays sole owner, and reads (len/print/index) use ptr+len unaffected. Harmless for the let-bound case (borrow-elided, reads only) and it also closes that binding's latent move-out double-free. Gated to the map.get shape, so a genuinely-owned unwrap payload keeps its real cap and still frees (verified: an owned Option[String] unwrap in a loop stays leak-clean). Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean across println / method-receiver / fn-arg / bound consumption over String and Vec values, in a 60-iter loop (map still frees its values — no leak). Full suite green (codegen 2394+1, memory_sanitizer 731+1, typechecker 2044, ownership 353, interpreter 1226, par_codegen 159; clippy+fmt clean). Adds a codegen E2E + LSan test. NOTE: a separate pre-existing gap surfaced — inline-indexing an unwrapped map-get Vec (`m.get(k).unwrap()[i]`) loud-bails 'Index operator applied to non-array type' (an inline-index-on-temporary limitation, orthogonal to this double-free; binding first works); filed as B-2026-07-15-27.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while testing B-2026-07-15-25 — an INLINE `m.get(k).unwrap()` of a heap-typed Map value (String/Vec) double-frees; binding to a `let` first is clean. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (Map `get(k).unwrap()` inline-temporary path for a heap value — the unwrapped value aliases the map's live bucket buffer instead of cloning, so the inline temp's drop AND the map's scope-exit per-entry free hit the same buffer)

title: Inline-indexing a temporary Vec (`m.get(k).unwrap()[i]`, and likely any `<method-chain>[i]`) loud-bails 'Index operator applied to non-array type'; binding the temporary first (`let v = m.get(k).unwrap(); v[i]`) works. Interp correct.
investigation: 2026-07-15: `let mut m: Map[i64, Vec[i64]] = Map.new(); m.insert(1,[10,20,30]); println(m.get(1).unwrap()[1])` — interp prints 20, AOT `karac build` bails 'codegen failed: Index operator applied to non-array type'. Binding first (`let v = m.get(1).unwrap(); println(v[1])`) is clean (20). So codegen's Index lowering only recognises an identifier/known-array receiver, not an inline temporary Vec produced by a method-call chain; the compile_index arm doesn't materialise a synth binding for a non-identifier Vec-typed receiver the way the field/for paths do. Orthogonal to the B-2026-07-15-26 double-free (that was fixed independently; this is a missing lowering, not a memory bug). Fix-path: in compile_index, when the object is a non-identifier expression whose value is a Vec {ptr,len,cap}, compile it to a temp, mint a synth Vec identifier (register vec_elem_types/var_elem_type_exprs from the inferred element type), and recurse — the same synth-identifier hoist compile_nested_index_read / compile_for_indexed_iter already use. Low severity — the bound form is a one-line workaround and the interpreter is the oracle.
resolution: 2026-07-16 (354b4df): fixed by materializing the borrow view and indexing it. `map.get(k)` returns `Option[ref V]`, so `m.get(k).unwrap()` is a BORROW of the map's bucket buffer — a `{ptr,len,cap=0}` view (B-2026-07-15-26 zeroed the cap). compile_index now routes the shape through compile_inline_temp_vec_index_ex(.., owns_temp=false): materialize the view into a synth Vec local, read the element (deep-cloning a heap element so the read stands alone), and — unlike the fresh-owned temp path (`make()[i]`/`shape()[k]`, owns_temp=true) — do NOT drop the temp (the map owns the buffer AND its elements; because emit_vec_drop_fn drains 0..len before the cap-guarded buffer free, a drop would double-free every heap element). New gate map_get_unwrap_vec_value_te (non-shared Vec value, bare-identifier map receiver). The consuming position frees the deep-cloned heap element: expr_is_inline_temp_vec_heap_index now recognises the borrow shape too. Also threads the same borrow-container hoist through compile_indexed_receiver_method so `m.get(k).unwrap()[i].method()` (e.g. `.len()`) lowers instead of bailing 'requires the indexed container to be a named variable'. Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean (definitely/indirectly lost = 0, no double-free) across a scalar-elem value (`Vec[i64]`, the reported repro), a heap-elem value (`Vec[String]`) as a println/fn-arg/let/`[i].method()` consumer, and a nested `Vec[Vec[i64]]`, in a 60-iter loop; fresh-owned `make()[i]` still drops (no regression). Full suite green (codegen 2396, memory_sanitizer 733; clippy+fmt clean). Adds test_e2e_inline_index_map_get_unwrap_vec_value + asan_inline_index_map_get_unwrap_vec_value_no_leak. NOTE: a `<map>.get(k).unwrap()[i]` String VALUE (char-index) and a VecDeque value are out of scope (Vec value only); a genuinely non-borrow method-chain returning an OWNED Vec by value (a user method `obj.make()[i]`) remains routed only through inline_temp_vec_te's free-fn-Call arm — unchanged here.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while fixing B-2026-07-15-26 — inline-indexing an unwrapped map-get Vec loud-bails in codegen; binding first works. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (index operator on a non-identifier temporary — `<expr>[i]` where <expr> is a method-call chain like `m.get(k).unwrap()`)

title: `<struct-field-map>.get(k).unwrap()` of a heap value (a `Map[_, String]`/`Map[_, Vec]` FIELD) double-frees the value buffer, both bound (`let v = h.ms.get(1).unwrap()`) AND inline (`println(h.ms.get(1).unwrap())`), against the struct's scope-exit map drop. `map.get(k)` returns `Option[ref V]` (a borrow), but the borrow-elision detectors (B-2026-07-14-15 bound registration, B-2026-07-15-26 inline cap-zero) only matched a bare-IDENTIFIER map receiver — a field-access receiver (`h.ms`/`self.ms`) fell through. A bare `Map[_, String]` LOCAL is clean; only a struct-field map crashes. REVISED from the initial 'struct-field Map drop' framing — the double-free is the field-access get().unwrap() consumer, not the field drop (a no-get struct with a `Map[_, String]` field drops clean). Interp correct; codegen-only.
investigation: 2026-07-16: initially filed as a struct-field `Map[K, heap-value]` drop double-free, but valgrind pinned the true root cause: the `world` String value (5 bytes) was freed FIRST inline in main (the `println(mv.sm.get(3).unwrap())` consumer's cap>0 free-guard) and AGAIN by `karac_map_free_with_drop_vec` (the map drop). Isolation: (a) a struct with a `Map[_, String]` field but NO get().unwrap() drops CLEAN — so it is the get/unwrap consumer, not the field drop; (b) `let v = h.ms.get(1).unwrap(); println(v)` ALSO double-frees (unlike B-26, where the bound form was clean) — so BOTH the bound and inline legs are broken for a field receiver; (c) a bare `Map[_, String]` LOCAL get().unwrap() is clean. So the get/unwrap borrow-elision detectors (`is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get_unwrap` bound, `unwrap_receiver_is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get` inline cap-zero) both require a bare `Identifier(map_var)` receiver and look up `var_elem_type_exprs[map_var]`; a field-access receiver (`h.ms`, `self.ms`) is not an Identifier, so neither fires, the unwrapped value keeps its real cap, and the consumer's free-guard double-frees the map's stored buffer.
resolution: 2026-07-16 (3a44455): fixed by a shared `map_receiver_value_type_expr` (stmts.rs) that resolves the map VALUE type for either a bare-identifier receiver (`var_elem_type_exprs`) OR a struct-field receiver (`mv.sm`/`self.sm` — from the field's declared `Map[K,V]` type via `var_type_names`+`struct_field_type_exprs`, with the owning struct's mono subst from `enum_inst_var_types` applied so a generic `Map[K,V]` field resolves to the concrete value). The B-26 inline cap-zero detector routes through it, so `<field-map>.get(k).unwrap()` produces a `{ptr,len,0}` borrow view — which fixes BOTH the inline consumer AND the bound binding (its drop skips on cap=0), leaving the map the sole owner. `map_get_unwrap_vec_value_te` (the B-2026-07-15-27 index helper) routes through it too, so `h.mv.get(k).unwrap()[i]` (previously a loud 'Index operator applied to non-array type' bail — the field-access sibling of B-27) now lowers. Verified interp==JIT==AOT and valgrind-clean (0 errors, 0 leaked) across a String value (inline println / `.len()` receiver / by-value fn arg / bound read / `self.field` receiver via a method), a Vec value (inline index + bound), and a generic struct field, in a 100-iter loop. Full suite green (codegen 2398, memory_sanitizer 735; clippy+fmt clean). Adds test_e2e_field_map_get_unwrap_heap_value + asan_field_map_get_unwrap_heap_value_no_double_free.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while fixing B-2026-07-15-24 — a struct FIELD of type Map[K, heap-value] (Map[i64, String]) double-frees at scope-exit drop; isolated and confirmed PRE-EXISTING on the clean tree (independent of the B-24 mono-layout fix, whose changes are all gated to subst.is_some() and never touch the non-generic path). — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`<struct-field-map>.get(k).unwrap()` of a heap value — the get/unwrap borrow-elision detectors recognised only a bare-IDENTIFIER map receiver, so a field-access receiver `h.ms`/`self.ms` kept its real `cap` and double-freed the borrowed bucket buffer against the struct's scope-exit map drop)

title: Three recent runtime-symbol surfaces missing from the JIT keep-list — critical_section acquire/release (b6ea37a1), the tracing span/exporter octet, and the http response-header READ quartet — so `karac run` (JIT) failed at lookup on ANY program whose module declares them (critical_section is declared by every module → even hello-world class programs); 54 cli tests red on main.
investigation: 2026-07-16: `cargo test --features llvm --test cli` on main (3405f351) = 469 passed / 54 failed, all with `JIT session error: Symbols not found`. Three distinct missing surfaces, one shared root cause (the B-2026-07-12-22 class — new runtime externs added without keep-list entries, so LTO strips them from the karac_jit_runner binary and the LLJIT dlsym generator can't materialize them): (1) `karac_critical_section_acquire/release` (b6ea37a1, embedded RAII guard — release is called by synthesized drop, declared in EVERY module → every `karac run` failed); (2) the `karac_tracing_*` octet (get/set_active_span, get/set_min_level, set_exporter, get_exporter_data, get_exporter_fn, reset — codegen emits the whole surface); (3) `karac_runtime_http_response_headers_count/header_key_at/header_val_at/headers_free` (the response-header READ surface; the set_* trio WAS in the list). Reproduced identically on the primary checkout without any local changes — pre-existing on main, not introduced by the collect slice.
resolution: 2026-07-16: added all 14 symbols to `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` in three keep! groups with per-group comments naming the class. Full `--test cli` flipped 469/54-red → 523/523 green. Guard against recurrence is procedural (CLAUDE.md + project_jit_default_flip_blocked memory both say new JIT-visible runtime externs go in the keep-list); a structural lint (compare codegen-emitted `karac_*` decls vs keep-list) would close the class for good — left as a note, not filed as a tracker item.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while re-benching the LBM #[par_unordered] kata (collect-generalization slice) — full `cargo test --features llvm` showed 54 cli/jit tests red on main

title: #[par_unordered] collect combine helper used get_store_size for its byte-offset GEPs and its size-keyed symbol, but Vec push/index GEPs stride by elem_ty.size_of() = ALLOC size — for a tail-padded struct element ({i64,i32}: store 12, alloc 16) every element past the first worker's chunk was memcpy'd at the wrong offset (shifted 4·chunk_index bytes), silently corrupting the collected Vec.
investigation: 2026-07-16: found by reading, confirmed by construction — the collect generalization (81377276, one day earlier) introduced get_store_size keying; all its test elements ({f64,i64}=16/16, f64=8/8, i64=8/8, {f32 x9}=36/36) have store==alloc so the suite stayed green. LLVM guarantees alloc size is a multiple of ABI alignment; store size is alloc minus tail padding, so any element type with tail padding diverges. Port-side classification: LATENT — selfhost/src/main.kara has zero #[par_unordered] uses and no kata collects padded structs; first user to collect a {i64,i32}-shaped struct in a par loop would have hit silent corruption (append path) or a corrupt final Vec (grow path).
resolution: 2026-07-16 (fix commit 70fd850a, post-rebase): switched the elem_size source to ensure_target_data().get_abi_size() (the established API elsewhere in codegen — cabi.rs, types_lowering.rs) so combine offsets, the size-keyed helper symbol, the tabulate view stride, and Vec's own GEP strides all agree on alloc size. Pinned twice: IR test asserts a {i64,i32} element emits __karac_reduce_combine_collect_b16 and NOT b12; E2E pre-seeds the accumulator so the caller-side install takes combine's append path (the byte-offset memcpy the IR pin can't validate) and verifies all 1000 elements + the seed against their index formulas.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: audit while building the collect TABULATE lowering — the worker chunk-view design reuses the combine helper's elem_size for view offsets, which forced checking what Vec's own push/index GEPs stride by

title: lljit_prototype::lljit_gdb_registration_listener_registers_dwarf_module fails on macOS (M5 Pro, Darwin 25.5): after installing + materializing a DWARF-carrying module, the process-global __jit_debug_descriptor carries no registered entry ('the listener did not fire'). CI never catches it: the llvm-feature CI jobs run a targeted suite list that excludes lljit_prototype, and the plain `cargo test --all` job compiles without --features llvm.
investigation: 2026-07-16: fails identically at three endpoints — the collect-tabulate worktree, pristine origin/main (e62061f4), AND 73bc02a3 (the commit that last touched tests/lljit_prototype.rs, whose author shipped it green on 2026-07-12; also fails at a914475f). Same code green-then-red with no intervening commit touching the surface ⇒ environmental: most plausibly an Xcode CLT/macOS update changed how the test binary's __jit_debug_descriptor global interposes against LLVM's own copy of the GDB JIT interface symbols on Darwin (the listener may be registering into LLVM's descriptor while the test reads the test binary's). The other 18 lljit_prototype tests (materialization, lookups, execution) all pass — JIT itself is healthy; only the debug-registration OBSERVATION fails. Fails in isolation (not test-ordering). 2026-07-16 (Linux session): FIX LANDED 913ec89, entry kept OPEN pending Darwin re-verification. Two legs: (1) the test's observation now resolves __jit_debug_descriptor from the IMAGE THAT HOSTS LLVM at runtime (dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT, LLVMContextCreate) -> dladdr -> dlsym that image), matching the diagnosed mechanism — the listener writes dylib-hosted LLVM's own copy of the interface globals while the link-time extern bound elsewhere; the static-LLVM configuration (Linux CI, default dev) falls back to the link-time extern (Rust test binaries export no LLVM symbols to RTLD_DEFAULT), which there IS the single unified copy — verified 19/19 on Linux, behavior identical. (2) the CI gap is closed: lljit_prototype added to the codegen-e2e job's --features llvm suite list, so the Linux leg of the GDB-registration smoke test is now enforced on every change. REMAINING (why still open): the dylib leg cannot be exercised from this Linux container (static LLVM); OWNER ACTION on the affected Mac (M5 Pro, Darwin 25.5): `cargo test --features llvm --test lljit_prototype`. If it still reds there, the next hypothesis is the dylib not exporting the descriptor at all (hidden visibility in the new toolchain's libLLVM) — in which case the dlsym probe returns null and falls back to the mis-bound extern; the follow-up would be reading the descriptor via the dylib's local symbol table or gating the assertion on descriptor-resolvability.
resolution: FIXED 8579cdac (Darwin leg) atop 913ec89 (Linux CI enrollment + descriptor-resolution helper). Darwin re-verify on the affected machine (M5 Pro, Darwin 25.5) showed 913ec89's dylib leg STILL fails: the GDB JIT descriptor is a SINGLE copy here — the link-time `extern` binding, the LLVM-hosting-image dlsym resolution, and `llvm_hosted_descriptor()` all agree on one address — so 913ec89's 'multiple descriptor copies' premise does not hold on the affected machine. Temporary instrumentation confirmed the wiring is correct (our RTDyld object-layer creator runs, `LLVMCreateGDBRegistrationListener` returns non-null, the register call succeeds), yet LLVM 18.1.8's GDBRegistrationListener does NOT populate `__jit_debug_descriptor` for JIT'd Mach-O objects on this toolchain — a best-effort debugger-integration limitation of the LLVM build, NOT a kara regression (JIT execution + DWARF emission unaffected; sibling `lljit_dwarf_debug_info_preserved_through_jit` passes). Fix: gate the strong end-to-end 'descriptor carries an entry' assertion to Linux/ELF (where LLVM populates the interface; the codegen-e2e CI leg 913ec89 added enforces it) and assert the reachable-interface check (version==1, single resolved descriptor) on macOS. Darwin now GREEN: 19/19 lljit_prototype on M5 Pro.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: full-suite gate of the collect-tabulate slice — one red binary in an otherwise green run (35 suites)

title: Auto-par reduction lowers a loop whose body carries plain `shared` RC traffic into a multi-threaded worker — racing non-atomic rc-inc/rc-dec across workers frees live objects (UAF reads surfacing as spurious 'integer overflow' panics + glibc heap-corruption aborts)
investigation: 2026-07-16: `while rep < K { total = total + sum(pool[rep % 8]); rep = rep + 1; }` over `pool: Vec[Option[TreeNode]]` (TreeNode plain `shared`) was recognized as a scalar Add reduction and lowered to `karac_par_reduce` (`__karac_reduce_worker_0`). The worker body retains the pool element per iteration (plain load/add/store on the rc header — `opt.chain.inc.real`), and `sum`'s owned param releases at exit; every tree-internal retain/release in `sum` is plain too. With N workers over the SAME 8 trees, lost rc updates drive counts to 0 while live: reps>=~500 abort with `free(): unaligned chunk` / `double free or corruption`, reps>=~2000 read recycled memory (garbage `n.val` -> panic 'integer overflow' at the sum line). Race triad: KARAC_PAR_WORKERS=1 always correct; multi-worker corrupt; valgrind (serialized threads) clean. Neither recognize_reductions nor the reduce.rs lowering consulted shared-ness anywhere; the explicit-boundary machinery (`src/cross_task_safe.rs`, enforced at spawn/par{}/send) was never applied to the COMPILER-CREATED task boundary. FIX: recognize_reductions declines any loop whose body contains a typed expression failing `is_cross_task_safe` — implemented as a span sweep over `TypeCheckResult.expr_types` entries inside the body block (shape-blind: a future ExprKind cannot reopen the hole), so the loop falls back to sequential codegen. Untyped analysis callers unchanged (every lowering path runs typed). `par` (atomic Arc) types remain eligible; int/Vec[scalar] reductions unaffected (par_codegen 165/165 green, incl. the 8137727 struct/float collect fan-out tests). Deliberately conservative: a body-local FRESH shared object (thread-local, technically race-free) also declines — the cost is a sequential loop, never a miscompile; re-enabling read-only shared traversal under auto-par requires the rc-silent borrowed-param convention (B-2026-07-15-21 fix direction) plus a gate relaxation that PROVES rc-silence of the emitted body. Also fixed en passant: `is_cross_task_safe`'s struct/enum expansion had no cycle guard — a recursive owned aggregate (`struct A { xs: Vec[A] }`) reachable from any boundary check recursed forever; expansion now carries a seen-set. Regression tests: typed-analysis decline/keep pair (tests/concurrency.rs), sequential-correctness E2E (tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_reduction_over_shared_pool_declined_and_correct), ASAN race regression (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_reduction_over_shared_pool_no_race_uaf).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: B-2026-07-15-21 perf pass — the ledger probe (8 pooled shared trees, while-loop sum) crashed at HEAD with glibc heap corruption before any timing could be taken — SURFACE NOTE: concurrency analysis (auto-par loop-reduction recognition) + codegen reduce lowering

title: Storing a `ref String`/`ref Vec` borrow into a value position — `Some(s)` with `s: ref String`, or passing a declared `ref String` struct field to a `ref` param — materializes the LENDER's full {ptr,len,cap} triple, so the consumer's cap-guarded cleanup frees the borrowed buffer and the lender's own drop double-frees (AOT aborts; interp correct — silent run/build divergence)
investigation: 2026-07-16: two legs, one discipline violation. LEG 1 (`asan_option_ref_from_param`): `fn wrap(s: ref String) -> Option[ref String] { Option.Some(s) }` — build_nonshared_enum_value packs the deref'd triple (real cap) into the payload words; the match arm reconstructs `n` and its binding cleanup (`cleanup.cap > 0 -> free`) frees s's buffer; `println(s)` then reads freed memory and s's scope-exit free doubles it. Valgrind: both frees inline in main, block alloc'd by push_str. LEG 2 (`asan_borrowed_field_into_ref_param`): `Parser { source: ref String }` lowers the field as a BORROW-POINTER slot by design (design.md Feature 4 Part 3), but the ref-arg path's field intercept (B-2026-07-12-1) only recognized owned Vec/String fields (`field_ty == vec_struct_type`); a declared-`ref` field fell to the rvalue path, which deref'd the borrow into a `ref_rvalue_arg` temp and queued the cap-guarded free — the 'cap>0 guard keeps registration safe' assumption is FALSE for borrows (the temp's cap is the lender's). FIX (two scoped arms + shared helper): (1) `zero_cap_if_ref_heap_borrow` — at both enum-construction pack sites, a payload arg that is a ref-heap borrow (Identifier naming a ref/mut-ref param with triple pointee, or a field access whose DECLARED type is ref/mut-ref String/str/Vec/VecDeque) gets its cap word zeroed: the payload becomes a read-only view (same borrow-view discipline as the map get/unwrap family, B-2026-07-14-15/-26 lineage), every downstream cap-guarded free skips, the lender stays sole owner. (2) the ref-arg field intercept gains a declared-`ref`-field arm: the slot already holds the exact `ptr` ABI the callee's `ref T` param expects, so forward the stored borrow pointer directly — no temp, no cleanup. Both probes: interp==JIT==AOT, valgrind 0 errors. safety_design runtime_confirmation 54/54 (was 52+2). E2E output regression: tests/codegen.rs test_e2e_ref_borrow_stored_positions_no_double_free. ID NOTE: the fix commit's message cites B-2026-07-16-4 and the concurrency-gate commit's code comments originally cited B-2026-07-16-2 — both numbers were taken by a sibling session's entries mid-flight; the authoritative IDs are this entry and B-2026-07-16-6 (comments renumbered in the ledger commit). Residual (documented, not fixed here): `Option[ref T]` payloads still pack 3 words rather than a single borrow-pointer word — a layout follow-on, orthogonal to safety now that the view discipline holds.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: full `cargo test --features llvm` sweep during the B-2026-07-16-6 verification — tests/safety_design.rs runtime_confirmation had 2 ASAN-red tests (present at least since 63d618e, 2026-07-12; the adversarial-accept runtime mirrors skip on compile failure, so these went red silently when the shapes started compiling) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (borrow materialization: enum payload pack of `Option[ref String]`, ref-arg forwarding of a declared `ref` struct field)

title: rc-elide conditions 1-4 do not constrain where a payload PROJECTION flows: an elided fn passing `n.parent` (any projection) to a mutating callee can release the only count keeping the borrowed node alive (the elided frame holds no +1 of its own) — UAF mid-arm on up/back-pointer shared graphs; condition 5 is a priority fast-follow now that the default-ON flip (a8d47f2) has landed
investigation: 2026-07-16: found by review, not by a failing test — no current elided shape passes projections to a mutating callee (is_mirror/sum recurse only into themselves), the corpus has no elided back-pointer traversal, and no kata/test writes that shape — but since a8d47f2 flipped the elision DEFAULT-ON, the gap is no longer shielded by the env gate. Mechanism: under elision the current frame borrows its param's referent with NO own +1 (the caller's root is the shield). Conditions 1-4 permit the arm to pass any projection (`n.parent`) to an arbitrary callee; that callee, following the ordinary OWNED protocol (its own counts balance), may field-assign through the alias (`parent.left = None`), releasing the parent-field +1 — which for an interior node is the ONLY count — freeing the borrowed node while the arm still reads it. Also holds via let-aliases of projections (`let x = n.parent; mutate(x)`) since PayloadScan does not add let-bound projections to the lineage. The un-elided protocol shields this exact shape (the frame's transferred +1 keeps the node >= 1 for the call duration). FIX DIRECTION (condition 5, all cheap syntactic fail-closed checks — design in docs/spikes/rc-elide-ref-params.md § 'Flip-blocker'): 5a elidable fns contain no field/index store and no lineage-rooted method call; 5b every shared/Option[shared] param of an elidable fn is itself elided; 5c lineage projections (and their let-aliases, which must join the lineage) may be passed only at elided positions of 5-satisfying fns — the elided call web becomes hermetically read-only over shared state, so no release can occur while any borrow in the web is live. is_mirror/is_symmetric/sum all satisfy 5a-5c (the known wins survive). TIMELINE: found during the Part B (binding-pair) review in one session while a sibling session flipped default-ON (a8d47f2) with criteria 1-4 met — the two crossed mid-flight; condition 5 was not in the criteria list. Until it lands, the exposure is bounded by the shape requirement (back-pointer graph + mutation through a projection-fed callee), which no current corpus program has; KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0 is the opt-out if a user hits it. 2026-07-16 REPRODUCED + FIXED (same session, follow-up pass): the hazard is REAL and WORSE than filed — on the default path the make/walk/detach probe (walk elidable under 1-4; arm passes n.back to detach which does m.left = None, releasing the borrowed node's only count) produced a SILENT WRONG ANSWER (printed 674712875783995725 instead of 8 — no crash) plus a valgrind invalid-read; interp and KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0 were correct (a silent run/build divergence). FIX (2639536): condition 5 as designed, implemented as a fixed-point refinement in safe_elidable_ref_params over the condition-1-4 set — 5a no field/index-target stores and no lineage-rooted method calls or for-iterations; 5b every shared-carrying param must itself be elided; 5c lineage projections flow only to elided positions of surviving fns or nested destructures, with let-aliases of projections joining the lineage (closing the PayloadScan tracking gap). Lineage is TYPE-TRACKED (binding -> payload struct) via a shared-carrying-types fixpoint + per-(struct,field) classification, so scalar reads (n.val) stay unrestricted while handle-bearing hops stay confined; tuple/enum/dyn hops fail closed; removals cascade to callers. All known winners survive (sum, has_path_sum, is_mirror/is_symmetric cross-fn; treesum 0.383s vs 0.443s opt-out best-of-9). Verified: probe prints 8 + valgrind 0 errors under default and =0; rc_elide unit tests 11/11 (6 new); new ASAN pin asan_rc_elide_mutation_through_alias_projection_guarded; codegen 2400 / memory_sanitizer 739 / par_codegen 173 / safety_design 54 green on the default path; clippy + fmt clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: design review of src/rc_elide.rs conditions 1-4 while completing the B-2026-07-15-21 binding-pair elision — SURFACE NOTE: rc-elide analysis (src/rc_elide.rs, env KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS — default OFF)

title: LLJIT (`karac run` / KARAC_TEST_JIT) produces EMPTY OUTPUT for programs using regex, alloc_zeroed, string methods (trim/case/strip/replace/sorted/split/lines/to_cstring), or a detached spawn (task_detach) — 16 runtime `karac_*` symbols missing from `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols`, so the dlsym generator can't materialize them and `main` fails to link. Same class as B-2026-07-12-22 (realloc) + the critical-section pair.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: user asked to take the regex-via-LLJIT CI failure (6 red in Codegen E2E via LLJIT); root-cause widened to a whole keep-list class — Repro: `KARAC_TEST_JIT=1 cargo test --features llvm --test codegen regex` (and 15 other tests) → stdout empty, stderr `JIT session error: Symbols not found: [ _karac_regex_validate, _karac_regex_is_match, ... ]; karac_jit_runner: lookup_address("main"): Failed to materialize symbols`. The JIT resolves runtime symbols from the karac_jit_runner process via DynamicLibrarySearchGeneratorForProcess; a `#[no_mangle]` runtime fn not referenced by the binary AND not in `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` gets dead-stripped, so the JIT can't find it. TWO axes: (1) REGEX (karac_regex_validate/is_match/find/find_all/replace_all) was un-preserved AND the `regex` runtime feature was not enabled on the JIT-linked `karac-runtime` dep (regex is `#[cfg(feature=regex)]` -> separate libkarac_runtime_regex.a for AOT, but the JIT links the dep at build time with a fixed feature set) — so the symbols didn't exist in the process at all. This was the ONLY CI-visible leg (x86 red too — the feature gap is arch-independent; 6 failures in the x86 `Codegen E2E via LLJIT` step). (2) alloc::karac_alloc_zeroed_or_panic, clone::karac_string_{trim,trim_start,trim_end,to_lowercase,to_uppercase,strip_prefix,strip_suffix,replace,sorted}, string_split_ffi::karac_runtime_string_{lines,split,split_whitespace}, crate-root karac_runtime_string_to_cstring, and scheduler::karac_runtime_task_detach (11 symbols) were ARM64-ONLY: macOS ld64 `-dead_strip` removes unreferenced no-mangle symbols that the x86 Linux linker keeps, and the via-LLJIT CI leg is x86-only, so these were silently red on arm64 and invisible to CI. FIX (902a163f): (a) `llvm` feature pulls `karac-runtime/regex`; (b) cfg-gated regex keep! + the 11 other keep! entries (string/alloc/task, native-`net`-gated for task_detach) in runtime/src/lib.rs; (c) a `KARAC_TEST_JIT=1` rerun added to the macOS-arm64 E2E CI job so the arm64-only keep-list class is enforced (the x86 leg cannot catch it — the -dead_strip asymmetry). VERIFIED M5 Pro/Darwin 25.5: via-LLJIT codegen 2400/0 (was 27 red), AOT 2400/0 (unaffected — archives built separately, keep-list only touches the JIT in-process symbol table), clippy+fmt clean. Note: `karac run` defaults to the interpreter today (JIT is opt-in via KARAC_TEST_JIT/KARAC_REPL_JIT), so not yet user-facing, but this is a prerequisite for the JIT-default flip (LLJIT productionization Slice 5).

source: kata-gap: LeetCode #114 Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List — `let root = if .. { build_balanced(..) } else { build_bst(..) }; flatten(root); spine_hash(root)` heap-corrupted under codegen
title: An `Option[shared]` bound from an `if`/`if let`/`match`/block EXPRESSION, then passed by value MORE THAN ONCE, is a use-after-free: the `let`-path `shared_option_info` detection matched only single RHS shapes (Call/Some/Index/FieldAccess/Identifier) and never looked inside a control-flow expression, so the binding was never registered in var_option_shared_heap — no call-site retain, no scope-exit dec; the first callee's param-drop freed the chain, the second use read freed memory (interp correct; JIT+AOT corrupt). Same class as B-2026-07-11-21 (Some RHS) / B-2026-07-11-29 (v[i] RHS), extended to control-flow-expr RHSs (case g)
investigation: 2026-07-16: #114 flatten (in-place tree rewiring) crashed with glibc 'malloc(): unaligned tcache chunk detected' under `karac build`/`karac run` (JIT+AOT) while `karac run --interp` was correct. Bisected to a MINIMAL repro with NO mutation needed: `let root = if 1==1 { mk(7) } else { mk(9) }` (mk returns Option[shared]) then `readv(root); readv(root)` (two by-value passes) — build prints garbage for the second read; valgrind: Invalid read of size 8 (UAF). BOUNDARY: only reuse (single by-value use survives by luck); only niche Option[shared] (bare `shared` struct value from an if-expr is fine); triggered by if-expr / match-expr / bare-block `{ mk() }` / Some-literal-in-branch RHS, i.e. any BLOCK-YIELDING initializer — NOT a direct call (`let root = mk(7)`, works) nor an identifier rebind (`let root = x`, works). ROOT CAUSE (Explore-mapped): Kara uses a caller-retains model — each by-value pass of an Option[shared] binding emits an unconditional rc_inc at the call site (share_option_shared_ref_for_arg, call_dispatch.rs), balanced by the callee's exit dec, plus one scope-exit dec for the owning binding; BOTH the arg-site inc and the scope-exit dec are gated on the binding being registered in var_option_shared_heap via track_rc_option_var. The registration is a purely syntactic decision on the `let` RHS `value.kind` in the shared_option_info detection block (stmts.rs ~2994-3303), with arms for (a) annotation, (b) Call returning Option[shared], (c) FieldAccess, (d) Identifier alias, (e) Some(shared), (f) v[i] — but NO arm for ExprKind::If/IfLet/Match/Block. So a control-flow-expr RHS left shared_option_info=None, track_rc_option_var was never called, the arg-site retain early-returned to a no-op (var_option_shared_heap.get -> None), and no scope-exit dec was queued. First readv freed the tree (its param drop, the only count), second readv UAF. The branch tails DO own their +1 (each mk() is a Call move-out) — only the outer-binding RC registration was skipped. FIX (3137755): added case (g) — helper control_flow_owned_option_shared recurses the branch/arm/block tails and registers via track_rc_option_var ONLY when every tail is an owns-+1 producer (Some(<shared>) literal / call returning Option[shared] / None), so like cases (b)/(e) no extra inner inc is flagged; any aliasing tail bails (binding stays unregistered) so a scope-exit dec is never queued against an un-owned handle (fail-closed, no new double-free surface). VALIDATED default path: memory_sanitizer 741/0/1, codegen E2E 2400/0, par_codegen 173/0, lib 1033/0, clippy clean; minimal repros (if/match/block/Some-branch x single/no/multi-use) + #114 byte-identical interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par + valgrind-clean; new pin asan_if_expr_option_shared_reused_no_use_after_free. The kata #114 is unblocked by this.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (let-binding RC registration: shared_option_info detection in the `let` statement path)

title: FIXED — User `defer` blocks execute FIFO-inline (at declaration point, in declaration order) instead of LIFO-at-scope-exit when the enclosing function's body is AUTO-parallelized (wrapped in karac_par_run). design.md mandates reverse-declaration LIFO at scope exit. Interpreter correct; native+JIT wrong. Perfect correlation: par_run present ⟺ defer divergence. Fixed by bailing auto-par to sequential when the fn contains any user defer/errdefer.
investigation: Repro d3h: `fn main(){ let mut log:Vec[i64]=Vec.new(); defer{println(1)} defer{println(2)} defer{println(3)} log.push(99); println(0) }`. interp=>0,3,2,1; native+JIT=>1,2,3,0 (defers before body's 0, declaration order). Isolation: 3 defers no-heap=>correct/no par_run; +Vec.push=>wrong/par_run; heap Vec literal no push=>correct/no par_run; Vec-loop+defers=>wrong/par_run; EXPLICIT par{}+defers=>CORRECT even with par_run. Disassembly confirmed `call karac_par_run` in every diverging main, none in correct ones. dintl (println between defers) native=1,50,2,0 vs interp=50,0,2,1 confirms each defer body materialized in-place. Long-standing (baseline e0d1fea). FIX (correctness-first, cannot introduce new bugs): `block_has_user_defer` recursive AST walk + gate at top of analyze_function returns empty FunctionConcurrency when the body has any user defer => sequential lowering (LIFO-correct). Explicit par{} (compile_par_block) separate path, unaffected. Validated: d3h/dloop/dintl match interp+JIT; codegen 2401 (incl new test test_e2e_defer_lifo_when_body_would_auto_parallelize) + par_codegen 183 + concurrency 125 green; clippy+fmt clean. (ID landed at -10 after concurrent collisions took -8 (LLJIT keep-list) and -9 (if/match Option[shared] reuse).)
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (defer ordering) — a fn whose body auto-parallelizes (karac_par_run wrap) runs its user defer blocks FIFO-inline instead of LIFO at scope exit; native+JIT diverge from the interpreter oracle. Silent wrong output. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (auto-par whole-function lowering / concurrency.rs analyze_function — par_run-wrapped body emits function-scope defer/errdefer in-place instead of on the fn-scope-exit cleanup drain)

source: kata-gap: LeetCode #115 Distinct Subsequences — nested Vec[Vec[i64]] 2-D DP ~1.6x behind equal-safety Rust; asked whether the gap is optimizable
title: A `Vec` built by `Vec.new()` + a counted `push`-loop reallocs ~log(n) times (growth-doubling) where the trip count is statically derivable — auto-pre-sizing (the imperative sibling of the collect-tabulate recognizer) would emit one alloc per Vec. On #115's nested Vec[Vec[i64]] DP this is ~1.7x of the gap to Rust; `Vec.with_capacity` recovers it (kara reaches nested-`Vec<Vec>` parity). The residual vs a flat Vec[i64] is the inherent per-row malloc of a row-of-rows
investigation: 2026-07-16: #115 num_distinct (2-D DP over strings, natural nested `Vec[Vec[i64]]` table) benched ~1.63x behind equal-safety Rust (kara 429ms vs rust-ovf 264ms), ahead of Go. Empirical decomposition (container, best-of-7 median): (1) FLAT vs NESTED — the SAME DP over a flat `Vec[i64]` indexed `dp[r*w+c]` runs 0.13s, 3.3x FASTER than the nested `Vec[Vec]` (0.43s), and faster than even C's nested `long**` (0.20s). So kara's indexing + arithmetic is not the bottleneck; the row-of-rows STRUCTURE is. (2) BUILD vs FILL — a build-only variant (construct the table, return before the fill loop) times ~= the whole nested run, while flat build-only is 0.02s: the inner DP fill is nearly free; TABLE CONSTRUCTION dominates. This DISPROVES the first-guess 'nested inner-loop indexing / row-pointer hoisting' hypothesis (which the #115 README initially, wrongly, stated — since corrected). (3) PUSH-GROWTH — rows are built `let mut row = Vec.new(); while j<=n { row.push(..) }`, which reallocs ~log2(n+1) times (0->1->2->4->8 for n=7). Pre-sizing each row with `Vec.with_capacity(n+1)` drops kara to 0.256s — AT PARITY with Rust's nested `Vec<Vec<i64>>` (264ms) — with no other change. So ~1.7x of the gap (0.44->0.26s) is repeated push-growth reallocation; the residual (0.26 vs flat 0.13) is the inherent per-row malloc of a nested container. FIX DIRECTION: a codegen/analysis pass that recognizes a `Vec.new()` (or `Vec[]`) local whose only mutations before first read are `push`es inside a loop with a statically-derivable trip count, and pre-sizes it (emit the `with_capacity` reserve) — the imperative counterpart of the `collect`-tabulate recognizer landed in dae4e30 (70fd850a). Applies to every push-in-a-counted-loop kata, not just #115. Broad, source-transparent (~1.7x here), and independent of the data-structure choice (a flat backing store is the user's lever for the remaining per-row-malloc residual). NOT urgent, NOT a correctness issue. Corrected the #115 README to the measured decomposition (was asserting nested-index density / row-pointer hoisting).
resolution: 2026-07-16: resolved by the sequential collect-tabulate lowering (dae4e309, filed independently before this entry was seen): recognized counted single-push loops now reserve exact capacity once (realloc(NULL,·)==malloc) and store in place — no growth-doubling reallocs at all, plus loop-vectorization. Residual: non-tabulate-shaped push loops (conditional pushes, acc-reading bodies, control-writing bodies per B-2026-07-16-15) keep growth-doubling by design; file a new entry if a hot workload surfaces one.

title: FIXED — Builtin collection LOOKUP methods (Map.get/remove/contains_key, Set.contains/remove, Vec.contains, String.contains) consumed their key/value argument instead of borrowing it. design.md's tables spec `key: ref K` / `val: ref T`. Broke get-then-insert with a heap key (String) — the word counter. Fixed by hardcoding `[Ref]` param modes for these single-arg lookups in collect_method_param_modes.
investigation: Repro: `fn main(){ let mut m:Map[String,i64]=Map.new(); let k:String="key"; let v=m.get(k).unwrap_or(0); m.insert(k,v+1); }` => error[ownership]: value 'k' moved here (at get), used again (at insert). Root cause: Map/Set/etc. registered via register_builtin_types with an EMPTY `struct Map[=K,=V]{}` (runtime/stdlib/map.kara is 10 lines, no methods), so their instance methods have no syntactic signature. collect_method_param_modes reads non-self param modes from stdlib/user signatures; with none for the builtins, Map.get's key arg had no entry and fell to the consume default. Only heap keys (String) observed the spurious move; a Copy key (i64) is unaffected. Fix: hardcode vec![OwnershipMode::Ref] for the single-non-self-param lookups (Map/SortedMap get/remove/contains_key, Set/SortedSet contains/remove, Vec/VecDeque contains, String contains) at the top of collect_method_param_modes, before the stdlib/user walk. EXCLUDES insert/entry (key & value are K/V, consumed). Follow-up: get_or(key: ref K, default: V) also covered — key borrows [Ref], default consumed [Own] — verified insert still consumes: get(k) after insert(k,_) is correctly still a move error. Validated: word-counter + contains_key/Set.contains/Vec.contains idioms check clean and run correct interp+JIT+native; regression tests test_map_get_then_insert_string_key_borrows / test_set_and_vec_contains_borrow_heap_element / test_map_insert_still_consumes_key_after_lookup; suites green ownership 1226(+3)/typechecker 353/interpreter 2044; clippy+fmt clean. (ID -12 after -11 collision.)
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (realistic word-frequency counter) — `let c = m.get(k).unwrap_or(0); m.insert(k, c+1)` with a String key rejected by karac check as use-after-move of k. Fundamental get-then-insert idiom fails. — SURFACE NOTE: ownership (collect_method_param_modes — builtin collection lookup methods have no syntactic signature so their key/value arg fell to the consume default)

fix: c585377 — the index-expression typecheck had no Map/SortedMap arm, so a non-integer key fell to the generic integer-or-range gate; an integer key slipped through but returned Type::Error (no Map arm in the element match), check-passing while the interpreter unreachable!'d and only codegen worked. BOTH backends already had native Map-index support the typecheck gate was blocking — codegen's compile_map_index (read, hashes any K) + compile_index_store (write, insert/overwrite); the interpreter had neither. Fix = a typecheck arm (types m[k] -> V for read AND assignment target, checks the key against K with the B-2026-07-16-12 ref-key relaxation) + the interpreter's two missing arms (eval_expr Index read: (Value::Map|SortedMap, key) scan/OrdValue lookup, missing key = clear runtime error since m[k] panics where m.get(k) returns None; set_index store: insert-or-overwrite before the Int-index gate). No desugar, no codegen change. Verified read+write, String/int/SortedMap keys, interp/JIT/native parity, valgrind clean, missing-key panics both backends. Tests: test_map_index_read_and_write_string_and_int_keys, test_map_index_read_missing_key_panics; the existing codegen test_e2e_map_index_* tests are now backed by a real check gate.
title: `m[key]` (Map/SortedMap index operator) only accepts integer keys — a non-integer key (`m["x"]` on `Map[String,i64]`) is rejected 'index must be an integer or range, found String', despite design.md speccing `[]` → `index(ref self, key: ref K) -> ref V` (panics if key missing). `m[1]` on `Map[i64,V]` works only because i64 IS an integer. Workaround `m.get(k).unwrap()` works (and `get`'s key-borrow was fixed in B-2026-07-16-12).
investigation: Repro: `let mut m:Map[String,i64]=Map.new(); m.insert("x",42); println(m["x"]);` => error at the index expr. `m[1]` on Map[i64,i64] passes (integer key satisfies the generic gate at typechecker/exprs.rs:2772 `is_integer(idx) || is_range`). Root cause: the index-expression typecheck has arms for Vec/Slice/Array/Column/String-slice but NO Map/SortedMap key-index arm, so a Map receiver with a non-integer key falls through to the generic integer-or-range requirement and errors. Fix-path (feature, spans 3 layers): (1) typechecker — add a Map/SortedMap arm before the generic gate that, when obj_ty is Map[K,V]/SortedMap[K,V], unifies idx_ty with K and returns V (a `ref V` per spec; panics-if-missing at runtime); (2) interpreter — lower `m[k]` to get-then-unwrap-or-panic (E_MAP_KEY_MISSING); (3) codegen — same, reusing the Map.get + Option.unwrap lowering. Deferred (not a quick fix): the `.get(k).unwrap()` idiom is the working equivalent, so this is an ergonomic sugar gap rather than a miscompile. Distinct from B-2026-07-16-12 (that fixed the ownership move on `get`/`contains_key` keys; this is the index-operator surface, unimplemented for non-integer keys).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe — `m["key"]` (Map index operator with a non-integer key) rejected by karac check: 'index must be an integer or range, found String'. design.md's Map/SortedMap tables spec `[]` → `fn index(ref self, key: ref K) -> ref V`. — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (exprs.rs index-expression handling — no Map/SortedMap arm; falls through to the generic integer-or-range gate). Also unimplemented in interp/codegen.

fix: 5090b76 — all six shapes now RUN with real types across interp/JIT/AOT. max/min join the iterator terminal surface (Option[T], numeric-or-String, reduce-shaped typing + span-keyed elem recording); direct terminals on iterable receivers route through infer_iterator_method and lowering canonicalizes them to the .iter() chain (receiver identified via method_callee_types — expr_types cannot answer receiver questions, MethodCall.span == receiver.span); join/concat are Vec[String]/VecDeque methods lowered through a new karac_string_join runtime entry (read-only element walk, vector keeps ownership) with interp seq arms (positional separator: ["", "x"].join("|") == "|x"). Codegen max/min reuse the reduce lowering via a synthesized comparison closure; FLOAT elements bail loud to --interp pending B-2026-07-17-11 (pre-existing reduce float-accumulator bug this desugar would inherit as a silent wrong answer). Pin: asan_direct_vec_iterator_terminals_and_string_join_no_leak. NOTE the root cause was WIDER than filed — see B-2026-07-17-12 (unknown methods on non-exhaustive prelude types silently type as Type::Error and unify with anything; v.some_typo() and let x: bool = v.sum() both passed check pre-fix).
title: `karac check` accepts iterator-reduction / string-collection methods DIRECTLY on a Vec (`v.sum()`, `v.max()`, `v.min()`, `v.product()`, `v.join(sep)`, `v.concat()`) but no backend implements them — interp reports 'method not found (no dispatch arm)' and codegen reports 'not yet supported' / build-fails. The idiomatic `.iter()` form works (`v.iter().sum()` = 120). This is a check/execution consistency hole: `karac check` (the AI-first wedge — a program that checks clean should run) passes code that traps at runtime on all three surfaces.
investigation: Confirmed set (check=PASS, both backends fail): Vec[String].join(sep) [codegen: 'method join not yet supported'; interp: 'no dispatch arm'], Vec[String].concat(), Vec[i64].sum(), Vec[i64].product() [codegen 'not yet supported'], Vec[i64].max()/min() [codegen build-fail, interp no-dispatch]. Working equivalents: `v.iter().sum()` / `v.iter().map(..).sum()` run correctly on all backends (probe itc => 120/13). Root cause: the typechecker resolves these on a bare Vec via iterator-trait method dispatch (no explicit arm in typechecker/vec_method.rs; `infer_vec_method` has none of sum/max/min/join/concat) — i.e. it treats a Vec as directly iterable and admits the Iterator/slice reduction methods, returning a plausible type (i64 / String) without the interpreter or codegen having a matching direct-on-Vec dispatch arm. Two fix-paths: (A) IMPLEMENT these as Vec convenience methods on both backends (interp dispatch arm building the reduction/joined result; codegen lowering — sum/max/min/product are simple fold loops, join/concat need a runtime string-concat helper `karac_vec_string_join`); OR (B) make the typechecker REJECT the direct-on-Vec form for reduction/collection methods, emitting a diagnostic that points to the `.iter().<reduce>()` idiom (turning a runtime trap into a compile-time 'method not found on Vec; use v.iter().sum()'). (B) is the smaller, consistency-restoring fix and matches the likely design intent (Rust-style: sum/max/min are Iterator methods, join is a slice method), but needs care to not reject legitimately-dispatched iterator-trait calls; (A) is more user-friendly but feature-sized across interp+codegen+runtime. Deferred pending a design decision on which collection convenience methods v1 promotes to bare-Vec receivers. Not a miscompile of valid working code — an unimplemented-method surface that should either work or fail at check time. Distinct from B-2026-07-16-13 (Map index operator).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (word-join / RPN aggregation) — `v.join("-")` on Vec[String] and `v.sum()`/`v.max()`/`v.min()`/`v.product()`/`v.concat()` on a Vec pass karac check but error at runtime in BOTH the interpreter ('no dispatch arm') and codegen ('method not yet supported' / build-fail). The idiomatic `.iter()` form (`v.iter().sum()`) works. — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (iterator-trait method resolution accepts reduction/collection methods DIRECTLY on a Vec receiver) vs interpreter + codegen (only the `.iter()` adaptor path implements them)

title: Seq-tabulate (dae4e309) miscompiled counted push loops whose body ALSO writes the while-loop's control state: `while c < n { out.push(c); if c == 3 { c = c + 2 }; c = c + 1 }` produced 10 elements where source semantics push 8 (and reads diverge past the skip) — the precomputed iter_total over-iterates. Same hazard for bound writes (`if x { n = 7 }`). Live on main ~1 day; compiled path only (interpreter correct = the discriminating oracle).
investigation: 2026-07-16: standalone repro: compiled binary printed len 10 / elements 0..9; `karac run --interp` printed len 8 / 0,1,2,3,6,7,8,9. Root cause: classify_seq_collect_tabulate checked only that the accumulator is mentioned exactly once (collect_is_tabulate_shape); the PAR classifier's protections (any unrecognized outer write declines) were not mirrored on the seq path. extract_loop_shape strips only the TERMINAL step-one increment; additional counter/bound writes compiled inline while the loop scaffolding iterated a precomputed total.
resolution: 2026-07-16 (b4f86484): classify_seq_collect_tabulate now takes the loop expr and declines when any outer body write (Assign/CompoundAssign targets + collect_expr_inner_writes over all constituent exprs, body-local lets excluded) intersects the while-condition's reads (collect_expr_reads; the terminal step-one increment is the one exemption — it is what extract_loop_shape strips) or the for-loop variable (source rebinds it fresh per iteration; the lowering persists one alloca — for-range BOUNDS stay writable since source pre-evaluates the range). E2E pins the 8/6/7 source oracles for counter-skip and bound-write loops; IR pins decline-dirty/keep-clean (a scalar accumulation beside the push still tabulates).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: hypothesis while investigating the selfhost_codegen oracle red (a lexer's conditional skip-advance is exactly this shape); reproduced standalone BEFORE trusting the diagnosis — the oracle red itself turned out to predate the slice (B-2026-07-16-16)

title: tests/selfhost_codegen.rs (selfhost_codegen_matches_seed_run) is RED on main: the self-hosted emitter compiles and runs, but executing its emitted IR prints "" where the seed run prints the expected stdout (all corpus entries, starting at fn main(){println(\"hi\")}). The emitted IR text LOOKS structurally plausible (datalayout, @main present) — the divergence is somewhere in the emitted body or the driver's dump path.
investigation: 2026-07-16: fails identically on pristine main (d3cfcc35 era) AND at 0cb5a42e — BEFORE the seq-tabulate (dae4e309) and par-worker (b4f86484) slices merged — and is KARAC_NO_AUTOPAR=1-independent, so it is NOT the tabulate lowerings and not analysis-driven at all. Window: green in the seq-tabulate worktree's full-suite run (base ≈ f987496e+2) → red at 0cb5a42e; the commits in that window are the concurrent session's (8579cdac B-4 GDB fix era and neighbors). NOT re-verified against each individually — one build per endpoint only. Also seen same-day: shared_ownership_matrix frontier mismatch (alias/ResultOk+ResultErr now Clean, FLOWS.expected says Leak) — that one is the expected-table lagging the concurrent session's own B-2026-07-16-9-era leak fixes (the assert message itself says to update FLOWS.expected when a residual closes) and is left to that session; only the selfhost oracle is filed here.
resolution: RE-VERIFIED GREEN 2026-07-17: tests/selfhost_codegen.rs passes on main (multi-run, full corpus, stdout+exit parity incl. fib/FizzBuzz slices landed 07-12..07-17). The red window was transient — resolved by intervening main commits in the 07-16..07-17 range; exact fix SHA not isolated (fix field left empty).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: full-suite gate of the par-worker-vectorization slice

source: kata-gap: LeetCode #115 Distinct Subsequences — the nested Vec[Vec[i64]] DP builds each row with a balanced `if j==0 { row.push(1) } else { row.push(0) }` counted loop; the follow-on to B-2026-07-16-11 whose seq-tabulate resolution explicitly declined conditional-push bodies and invited a new entry
title: The loop-bound pre-sizing pass fired only on a STRAIGHT-LINE single push per iteration; a body whose sole fill is a balanced `if COND { v.push(a) } else { v.push(b) }` (both arms push exactly once, condition doesn't touch v — net one push/iter, trip count still exact) was declined, so the Vec kept ~log(n) growth-doubling reallocs. This is the DP-base-case shape (`if j==0 { row.push(1) } else { row.push(0) }`, #115) and the conditional-push residual seq-tabulate (dae4e309, B-2026-07-16-11) declines by design. Extended `body_fills_once` with a balanced-if path and taught the bound-invariance guard (`expr_mutates_ident`) to recurse precisely into if/Block exprs instead of failing open.
investigation: 2026-07-16: #115's nested Vec[Vec[i64]] table builds each row with a balanced-if counted push loop. B-2026-07-16-11's straight-line presize + seq-tabulate both decline conditional pushes, so rows kept growth-doubling. Extended presize to recognize a body whose only mention of the target is a single top-level balanced `if { v.push } else { v.push }` (both arms exactly one push, no early exit, condition v-free) — net push count == trip count, so the reservation stays exact; with_capacity is a pure capacity hint so output is invariant regardless. Also fixed the real blocker: the loop-invariance check `ident_mutated_in_block`/`expr_mutates_ident` failed OPEN on any `if` expr (`_ => true`), so a balanced-if body made the pass conclude the bound 'might be mutated' and decline; now it recurses precisely into if (condition + both blocks) and bare-block exprs, failing open only on genuinely unanalyzable nesting (match/loops/closures). EMPIRICAL WIN IS MARGINAL, and the motivating premise (B-2026-07-16-11 investigation's '~1.7x of #115's gap is push-growth realloc; with_capacity drops kara to 0.256s') does NOT reproduce: hyperfine 30-run, this x86 container — nested baseline 426ms == balanced-if-presize 421ms == explicit with_capacity 420ms (1.00x, ZERO win). #115's rows are <=7 elements, so the realloc chain (0->4->8) is negligible. A 60x-amplified microbench (5000-element rows, 40k reps) shows the pass is real but small: 1.07x (88.7ms baseline -> 82.8ms presize). #115's ACTUAL gap is the nested structure's per-row malloc COUNT, not realloc: a FLAT Vec[i64] (dp[r*w+c]) runs the same DP in 235ms vs nested 426ms — 1.81x (not the 3.3x the #115 README claimed; corrected) — the only real lever, and a data-structure choice no auto-transform performs. Composes cleanly with seq-tabulate (keys off the body push shape, not the init; still vectorizes straight-line loops after presize rewrites their init; declines exactly the conditional bodies this pass now takes). Gates green: 18 presize unit tests, codegen E2E 2401, par_codegen 183, memory_sanitizer 741, clippy --all-targets --features llvm clean, fmt clean; #115 byte-identical interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par/AUTO_PAR=0 and valgrind-clean (0 errors, 0 bytes at exit).
resolution: 2026-07-16: fixed in 53f5c09 (perf(presize): pre-size counted push-loops with a balanced if/else fill). Sound and gated; win is marginal (~1.07x large conditional-push loops, no-op on #115's tiny rows). Closes the conditional-push residual B-2026-07-16-11's resolution flagged. #115's remaining gap to Rust is the nested per-row malloc (flat backing store is the 1.81x lever, a user data-structure choice); the #115 README is corrected to the reproduced decomposition.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: lowering (presize.rs collection pre-sizing): a counted push-loop whose fill is a balanced if/else (both arms push once) was not recognized, so the Vec kept growth-doubling reallocs

title: FIXED — Reassigning a heap-owning STRUCT variable (`a = b`) double-frees: the Assign arm never suppressed the moved source `b`'s StructDrop, so both `a` (now holding b's value) and `b` freed the same field buffers at scope exit. JIT (-O0) aborts with 'free(): double free detected'; native masks it at -O in the minimal case but trips under karac_par_run. Fixed by suppressing the source's StructDrop (zero_struct_move_caps) for a tracked non-shared struct LHS, mirroring the Vec/Map var handling.
investigation: Repro hr4 (simple): `struct Box{items:Vec[i64]} fn main(){ let mut a=Box{items:[1,2,3]}; let b=Box{items:[4,5]}; a=b; println(a.items.len()); }` => interp 2, JIT double-free, native vg-clean-at-O. hr1 (move-out swap): `let tmp=a; a=b; b=tmp;` => JIT double-free. h2/h3 native valgrind Invalid free under karac_par_run. Isolation: Vec var reassign (hr2) clean, fresh-literal RHS (hr3) clean, struct-var reassign with a moved-binding RHS is the trigger. Root cause: stmts.rs Assign arm — the Identifier-target path has `lhs_is_tracked_vec` (eager-free + suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg) and `lhs_is_tracked_map` (B-2026-07-15-25 eager-free + suppress_map_cleanup) branches, but NO struct-var branch, so `a = b` for a struct did the generic store and left b's StructDrop live → a and b both free b's buffers. Fix: add `lhs_is_tracked_struct` detection (var_type_names[name] in struct_types, non-shared) and, gated on !rhs_is_self_alias, call suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg(value) after the store — it routes a struct Identifier RHS through zero_struct_move_caps, zeroing b's field caps so its drop no-ops; a's own StructDrop remains the unique owner. The move-out-then-reassign case is covered because `let tmp = a` already zeroed a's caps (so a's eager side is a no-op) and the source-suppress handles b. No eager-drop-of-old needed: measured no leak across simple/swap/loop/String-field/self-alias shapes (native valgrind 0 errors, no definitely-lost). Regression test asan_struct_var_reassign_no_double_free (loop + 3-way swap + String-field, 100 iters). Suites green msan 742 / codegen 2401; clippy+fmt clean. SEPARATE remaining bug found alongside (filed distinctly): h3/hh8 — TWO heap-returning calls (`first_word`) in one auto-parallelized main double-free; vanishes with KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 (auto-par heap-return interaction, not struct reassign). (ID -18 after -15/-16/-17 collisions.)
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (heap-struct move-swap; Option-chaining) — `let tmp = a; a = b; b = tmp` and simple `a = b` on heap-owning structs double-free. JIT aborts immediately; native masks at -O but trips under karac_par_run (h2/h3 probes). — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (stmts.rs Assign arm — the Identifier-target reassignment had cases for Vec/String and Map/Set vars but no struct-var case, so `a = b` never suppressed the moved source's StructDrop)

fix: d9cd7a2 — three coordinated changes: (1) analyzer move-hazard gate (concurrency.rs) keeps statements that CONSUME an owned-heap capture (match/if-let payload move, owned call arg, Option/Result combinator receiver, bare-RHS alias/aggregate move) out of par groups — the hazard is stmt-vs-parent-scope-exit, invisible to the stmt-vs-stmt conflict graph; (2) the par-branch publish loop now tag-sentinel-suppresses FreeInlineOptionPayload / FreeInlineOptionMapPayload / FreeInlineResultPayload for published slots (pre-fix the worker freed the payload right after copying it into the returns struct); (3) the parent slot-rebind loop re-registers the payload free against its fresh alloca (RHS-span -> enum_inst_type_exprs, mirroring the sequential let path) so suppression (2) does not trade the double-free for a per-slot leak.
title: A function returning `Option[String]` built from a MOVED Vec element (`let words = s.split(" "); if words.len()>0 { Some(words[0]) } else { None }`) double-frees the element buffer when called TWICE inside an auto-parallelized `main` (JIT: 'free(): double free'; native: valgrind Invalid free under karac_par_run). Single/sequential invocation is CLEAN (the element move-out of `words[0]` into `Some` is correctly suppressed there); the bug appears only when main's statement mix makes it auto-parallelize (par_run=1). KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 fixes it — an auto-par correctness bug, not the element-move-out itself.
investigation: Repro (h3/hh8): `fn first_word(s:String)->Option[String]{ let words=s.split(" "); if words.len()>0 {Some(words[0])} else {None} } fn main(){ let r=first_word("hello world"); match r {Some(w)=>println(w),None=>println("e")}; let e=first_word(""); println(e.unwrap_or("none").len()); }` => interp `hello`,`0` correct; JIT double-free; native `hello`,`0` then valgrind Invalid free under karac_par_run in main. Isolation: single call + match (hh4/hh5) CLEAN, par_run=0; single call + unwrap_or (hh6) CLEAN; a fn returning a FRESH Some (`Some(f"...")`, not a Vec element) called twice (hp4) CLEAN; two Vec-returning calls consumed by .len() (hp5) CLEAN even at par_run=1; two `first_word` calls both via `match` (hp3) CLEAN, par_run=0. The double-free requires ALL of: (a) the returned Option's String is a MOVED Vec element (`Some(words[0])`, words a split-produced local), (b) TWO such calls in main, (c) a statement mix that makes main auto-parallelize (par_run=1 — the `unwrap_or(...).len()` chain in the 2nd statement tips main over the auto-par heuristic). KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 => clean on all backends, confirming the fault is in the auto-par whole-function lowering, not the sequential element-move-out suppression (which is correct — single instances are clean). Fix-path: the auto-par lowering (reduce.rs / par_blocks.rs) must preserve the Vec-element move-out cap-suppression (zero of the moved element's slot so the local Vec's scope-exit element-drain skips it) when it relocates the call/consume statements into par workers — currently the suppression is emitted in the sequential position but the par-lowered statement stream drops or duplicates it, so both the returned Option and the source Vec's element-drain free the element buffer.
