Same complex subsystem as B-2026-07-16-10 (defer-under-auto-par); a conservative interim fix is to bail auto-par for a function whose statements move heap elements out of a local container into a returned/consumed Option (analogous to the defer bail). Deferred: narrow trigger + delicate auto-par lowering; the KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 escape hatch is the workaround. Distinct from B-2026-07-16-18 (struct-var reassignment, already fixed). — FIX SESSION (2026-07-17): IR diff (KARAC_JIT_RUNNER capture) showed first_word byte-identical par vs seq — the original fix-path hypothesis (worker drops the moved-element suppression inside the callee) was WRONG; both legs live in main's par lowering. Leg A (fires in the repro): `match r` lifted into __par_branch_0_0 consumes its bit-copied env alloca and cap-suppresses only the COPY; parent's FreeInlineOptionPayload on the original %r fires again -> double-free of the 'hello' buffer. Leg B (latent in the repro because ''-split yields cap 0; real with non-empty payloads): a published Option[String] slot's branch-exit drain freed the payload just copied to the returns struct — the publish suppression scan knew Vec/RC/Map/File/Enum/Struct/UserDrop/SoA/Column/DataFrame/Tensor shapes but not the three inline-payload actions. Probes: consuming-match repro (gate serializes, clean), publish-only two-literal-call shape (still parallelizes, 18 par refs, clean), is_some-only-read shape (parent re-registration frees exactly once — LSan-guarded). Un-annotated hazard classification goes through TypeCheckResult.expr_types keyed by the LET RHS span (pattern_binding_types records only the head name 'Option', losing the payload). Also surfaced B-2026-07-17-4, the SEQUENTIAL unwrap_or double-free the repro's second statement was masking. Pins: asan_auto_par_consuming_match_on_option_string_no_double_free, asan_auto_par_published_option_string_slots_no_double_free_no_leak, asan_published_option_slot_payload_never_consumed_no_leak, + 5 analyzer pins in tests/concurrency.rs (consuming shapes blocked; ref-param readers and publish-only calls still group; parallax-lite positive control unchanged at 44 par refs).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (first_word word-splitter called twice) — a fn returning `Option[String]` built from a moved Vec element (`Some(words[0])` after `words = s.split(...)`), called TWICE in an auto-parallelized main, double-frees the element buffer. JIT aborts; native Invalid free under karac_par_run. KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 makes it clean. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (auto-par whole-function lowering — the Vec-element move-out suppression for `Some(words[0])` that is correct in sequential codegen is not preserved when the caller (main) is auto-parallelized)

title: A `.to_string()` chained as the receiver of another method (`s.to_string().to_uppercase()`, `s.trim().to_string()…`) build-failed with 'Vec/String method to_string is not yet supported in codegen' while the interpreter ran it. Common idiom; loud build-fail with an `--interp` fallback hint, not a silent miscompile.
investigation: The parser sets `MethodCall.span == receiver.span`, so in a chain `recv.inner().outer()` the inner `to_string` and the outer method collide on ONE `method_callee_types` span key. The `String.to_string()` / `StringSlice.to_string()` owning-copy special-case (src/codegen/method_call.rs) was gated ONLY on the shared-span `dispatch_key`'s receiver segment being 'String'/'StringSlice'; when the outer call's key shadows the inner's, the inner `to_string` gets dispatch_key=None (or the outer's method segment), the special-case never fires, and the inner call falls through to the Vec/String dispatcher (vec_method.rs) which has no `to_string` arm and loud-fails. Terminal `s.to_string()` worked because its dispatch_key resolves to 'String.to_string'. Fix: additionally fire the copy special-case on a statically String/StringSlice receiver via a new side-effect-free `expr_is_string_like` helper (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) — string literals, String/StringSlice identifiers/self (via `string_vars`/`var_type_names`), MethodCall receivers via `type_name_of_expr`, and the unambiguous builtin String→String chain methods trim/trim_start/trim_end/to_lowercase/to_uppercase/to_string (sorted/replace/repeat EXCLUDED — they also name Vec methods). Regression: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_chained_to_string_then_string_method (identifier / literal / mid-chain shapes). Distinct from B-2026-07-16-14 (reduction/collection methods on a bare Vec) and -13 (Map index operator).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (chained string methods) — `s.to_string().to_uppercase()`, `s.trim().to_string().to_uppercase()`, `"hi".to_string().to_uppercase()` all build-fail under `karac build`/JIT while the interpreter runs them fine. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`compile_method_call` `String.to_string()` owning-copy special-case) vs interpreter

title: A heap-String-returning method used as the RECEIVER of another method (`s.to_uppercase().to_lowercase()`, `e.to_uppercase().split(",")`, `c.trim().to_string()…`) leaks its fresh owned intermediate String buffer once per call — unbounded in a loop. Pre-existing; independent of B-20 (reproduces without any `to_string`).
investigation: `try_compile_nonident_collection_method` materializes a non-identifier String receiver into a synth `__recv_tmp` slot for dispatch but deliberately does NOT drop-track it ('the statement-level owned-temp machinery owns the free') — but that machinery tracks only the OUTERMOST temp of a statement, so an INTERMEDIATE chain receiver (`s.to_uppercase()` feeding `.to_lowercase()`) has no owner and leaks. Same gap in the `to_string` copy path. Fix: free the materialized receiver temp's buffer after the method consumes it, gated on `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp(object)` (Call/MethodCall, not borrow-returning — so a place-expr receiver `p.name.to_uppercase()` / `v[0].to_uppercase()` is NEVER freed, no double-free/UAF) OR `expr_is_fresh_owned_string_slice`, AND on the method's result being receiver-INDEPENDENT: a scalar/bool/Option-of-scalar result never aliases, and the freshly-allocating String→String family (trim*/to_lower/uppercase/sorted/replace/to_string/repeat) plus the copy-into-owned split family (split/split_whitespace/lines — runtime `karac_runtime_string_split*` `copy_nonoverlapping` each piece into its own cap>0 buffer) return independent memory; a heap-struct result from a BORROWING method is left conservatively un-freed (a safe leak, never a dangle). `free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap`'s cap>0 guard is the backstop for borrowed (cap==0) views. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_chained_string_method_intermediate_temp_no_leak (200× loop, chains + split + field-receiver double-free guard → 11400, LSan-clean). Verified across interp/JIT/native + valgrind.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: valgrind on native binaries during the B-20 probe — `s.to_uppercase().to_lowercase()` leaks the intermediate `to_uppercase` String buffer (5 bytes, `definitely lost`, `karac_string_to_uppercase` frame). Reproduces on any chained heap-String-returning string method, independent of B-20. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`try_compile_nonident_collection_method` expression-receiver string-method materialization + the `to_string` copy path)

title: `Option[String].unwrap_or(default)` / `Result[String,E].unwrap_or(default)` leaks a fresh heap-String default once per call when the receiver is data-dependent — the default is evaluated eagerly (before the tag branch) and the present (Some/Ok) path discards it without freeing. Unbounded in a loop.
investigation: `unwrap_or` codegen (src/codegen/calls.rs) compiles the default arg EAGERLY into `default_val` before branching on the Option/Result tag, then phi-merges present_val (payload) vs default_val. In the present (Some/Ok) path default_val is discarded but never freed → leak. A compile-time-constant `Some` receiver lets the optimizer elide the default entirely, so the leak only manifests on a data-dependent receiver (function return, runtime branch) — which is why pure-Some (o4) and pure-None (o5) probes were clean but the mixed probe (o3/o6) leaked exactly `Some-count` default buffers. Fixed by freeing default_val in the present block, gated on the two proven-safe fresh-owned shapes `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp` (Call/MethodCall) OR `expr_is_fresh_owned_string_slice` (`String[a..b]`), via `free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap` (cap>0-guarded, no-ops on a scalar/borrowed default). The absent path binds the same default to the result (freed once at scope); the present path frees it here (once) — no double-free. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_unwrap_or_fresh_string_default_no_leak (Option+Result, 200× loop → 4099, LSan-clean). Deliberately narrow — the identifier / array-literal / f-string default shapes are a separate pre-existing double-free/leak filed as B-2026-07-16-23.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (Option/Result heap-default methods) + valgrind — `get(i).unwrap_or("d".to_string())` over a data-dependent Option leaks the fresh String default 50× across a 100-iteration loop (150 bytes / 50 blocks, `karac_string_*` frame). Pure-constant-Some / pure-None probes are clean. — CLASS NOTE: memory leak (eager unwrap_or default discarded unfreed in the present path) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`Option/Result.unwrap_or` in src/codegen/calls.rs — the eager default's present-path discard)

title: `unwrap_or(<non-Call heap default>)` mismanaged the eager default's ownership. LEG 1 (FIXED in c9593ed): an owned-binding default (moved Vec/String identifier) DOUBLE-FREED. REMAINING (open): a DIRECT array/collection-literal default (`unwrap_or([9,9,9])`) LEAKS, and a DIRECT f-string default (`unwrap_or(f"…")`) DOUBLE-FREES. Only the Call/MethodCall/String-slice (B-2026-07-16-22) and owned-binding (leg 1) default shapes are now correct.
investigation: Three distinct root causes under one surface (unwrap_or's eager `default_val`, src/codegen/calls.rs), all pre-existing and all EXCLUDED from the B-2026-07-16-22 fix (which frees only fresh Call/MethodCall/slice defaults): (1) OWNED-BINDING default (`let d = ...; unwrap_or(d)`): default_val is a shallow copy of the binding's {ptr,len,cap}; the absent path returns it as the result (freed at scope) AND the binding `d` is freed at ITS scope → double-free (uo1: 'free(): double free detected'). Fix-path: route the default arg through the same move-suppression the normal consuming-call arg machinery uses (suppress `d`'s scope-exit free — the default is MOVED into unwrap_or) AND free default_val in the present path (so the moved buffer is reclaimed on the Some side). (2) F-STRING default (`unwrap_or(f"…")`): the InterpolatedStringLit temp is already tracked by the statement/f-string owned-temp machinery (B-2026-07-15-12 lineage), so it is freed there; naively freeing default_val in the present path double-frees (verified: adding InterpolatedStringLit to the B-22 free-gate crashed uo5). Fix-path: coordinate with the f-string temp table — either suppress the statement-machinery free for a default consumed by unwrap_or, or don't double-free. (3) ARRAY/COLLECTION-LITERAL default (`unwrap_or([9,9,9])`): a fresh Vec temp discarded in the present path leaks (uo4); adding ArrayLiteral to the free-gate did NOT reclaim it (the literal's lowering/tracking differs — needs investigation of whether it is a distinct alloca vs the phi-merged value). All three are interp-clean (the tree-walk interpreter's uniform value ownership handles them), so they are codegen-only. Reproducers: probe uo1 (owned-binding, double-free), uo4 (array-literal, leak), uo5 (f-string, double-free). Deferred as delicate move-machinery / temp-tracking coordination work distinct from the clean B-22 Call/MethodCall-default fix. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (c9593ed): LEG 1 (owned-binding, uo1) FIXED — the moved Vec/String binding's scope-exit free is now suppressed via `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` (zero its cap) BEFORE the tag branch, and the loaded copy is freed on the present path, so the buffer is freed exactly once per path. Gated to an inline-owned Vec/String binding (a `ref` binding / scalar default is untouched); this transparently also fixes the array-literal-VIA-BINDING case (`let d: Vec[i64] = [9,9,9]; opt.unwrap_or(d)`, probe ub2). Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_unwrap_or_moved_binding_default_no_double_free (String+Vec, 200x -> 3233). STILL OPEN: leg 2 — a DIRECT array/collection-literal default (`unwrap_or([9,9,9])`, uo4) leaks (the literal is not an identifier, so neither the fresh-temp free-gate nor the moved-binding suppression fires; the literal's lowering/tracking needs investigation — freeing it in the present path did NOT reclaim it in an earlier attempt); leg 3 — a DIRECT f-string default (`unwrap_or(f"…")`, uo5) double-frees (the InterpolatedStringLit temp is already tracked by the statement/f-string owned-temp machinery, so a naive present-path free crashes — needs coordination with that temp table). Both remaining legs are direct-literal defaults; the common `unwrap_or(binding)` and `unwrap_or(expr())` forms are now correct. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (ed0b9db, LEGS 2+3 FIXED — bug now CLOSED): leg 2 (direct collection-literal default — array `[9,9,9]`, prefix `Vec[9,9,9]`, repeat `[9;3]`) LEAKED on the PRESENT path; the literal materializes to a Vec struct so its `default_val` IS the {ptr,len,cap} — added the ArrayLiteral/PrefixCollectionLiteral/RepeatLiteral ExprKinds to the present-path free-gate (free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap). These register no scope cleanup, so the absent path frees exactly once via the result binding — no double-free (the earlier 'did NOT reclaim' note is obsolete post-leg-1; default_val is now the vec struct, not a stack array aggregate). Leg 3 (direct f-string default `f"…"`) DOUBLE-FREED on the ABSENT path: the InterpolatedStringLit's `acc` alloca is track_vec_var'd for scope free AND that same buffer becomes the unwrap_or result (freed again by the result binding). Fix: snapshot the innermost cleanup frame before compiling the default, remove the acc's FreeVecBuffer added during the compile (frame tail past the snapshot), and free default_val on the present path — the temp analogue of leg 1's cap-zeroing. Freed exactly once per branch. Both codegen-only (src/codegen/calls.rs unwrap_or arm). Verified interp/JIT/AOT parity + valgrind-clean across heap-element array literal, Vec[..] prefix, [v;n] repeat, and f-string defaults over 200 mixed present/absent iters. Pin: asan_unwrap_or_literal_and_fstring_default_clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe follow-on to B-2026-07-16-22 — `unwrap_or` with a non-Call heap default: an owned-binding default (`let d = "x".to_string(); opt(i).unwrap_or(d)`) DOUBLE-FREES ('free(): double free detected in tcache 2', native aborts / JIT crashes, interp fine); an array-literal default (`opt(i).unwrap_or([9,9,9])` for Option[Vec[i64]]) LEAKS; an f-string default (`opt(i).unwrap_or(f"default-{i}")`) DOUBLE-FREES. — CLASS NOTE: memory unsafety (double-free) + leak — unwrap_or eager default with owned-binding / f-string / collection-literal shapes — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`Option/Result.unwrap_or` eager-default ownership for non-Call default shapes)

fix: 3830213 — Result-carried payload exclusion at rc-elide classification (src/rc_elide.rs condition-1 filter): a param whose declared type wraps a shared handle in Result (at any nesting) is never admitted to the elidable set. Root cause confirmed as the e39db64 borrow-forward relaxation admitting the `eat2(r)` bare forward while `eat` itself stayed un-elided (forwarding escapes condition 2): `eat` compiled the forward as a MOVE (own release suppressed) and `eat2` skipped its elided release — nobody freed the Node. The pair-elision is edge-local (skip call-site retain + skip callee release = net zero) and is only balanced when the edge carries that pair: Option[shared]/bare-shared args follow the caller-retains convention (both halves exist), Result[shared] args follow the MOVE convention (no retain twin — the B-2026-07-12-24 scope-exit-dec residual), so Result edges have no pair to skip. Re-admitting Result is gated on B-2026-07-12-24 giving Result[shared] locals a real scope-exit release. Verified: matrix repro leak gone (valgrind 0 definitely lost, interp parity, elidable set empty for the chain), the Option twin of the SAME chain still elides and is valgrind-clean, treesum win intact at 26.8% elide-on vs off (0.364s vs 0.462s, within the 17-32% band), shared_ownership_matrix frontier restored (FLOWS.expected untouched per the filing's instruction). Unit pin: rc_elide::tests::result_carried_param_never_elides.
title: shared-ownership-matrix frontier REGRESSION: `forwarding_chain/ResultOk` + `forwarding_chain/ResultErr` went Clean → Leak with RC-elision ON (`KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS` default) — `fn eat(r: Result[Node, i64]) -> i64 { eat2(r) }` (owned param forwarded whole to a consuming callee) leaks the shared `Node` on both the Ok-side (`Result[Node, i64]`) and Err-side (`Result[i64, Node]`) containers; the `Option[Node]` cell of the SAME flow stays Clean, and the whole matrix passes with `KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0`. Prime suspect: `e39db64` (borrow-forward relaxation of RC-elision condition 1, B-2026-07-15-21 Part C — the forwarding-chain shape is exactly its target); suspect window includes `2639536` (condition-5 fix, same day).
investigation: 2026-07-17 (filing session, Linux container with LSan ACTIVE): full `cargo test --features llvm` on main at 7aa6372 fails ONLY shared_ownership_matrix (plus the known-red selfhost oracle B-2026-07-16-16 later in the target list): `frontier changed: forwarding_chain/ResultOk: got Leak expected Clean; forwarding_chain/ResultErr: got Leak expected Clean`. Bisect-by-toggle: `KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0 cargo test --features llvm --test shared_ownership_matrix` → GREEN (1 passed); default (elide ON) → RED — so the leak is inside the elision feature, not the Interner slice landed the same day (whose dispatch arms are all gated on `Interner`-named bindings and never fire in the matrix programs). Repro cell (matrix `forwarding_chain` × `ResultOk`): `shared struct Node { val: i64, mut left: Option[Node], mut right: Option[Node] }` + `fn take() -> Result[Node, i64] { let mut src: Vec[Option[Node]] = Vec.new(); src.push(Some(Node { val: 7, left: None, right: None })); match src[0] { None => Err(1), Some(n) => Ok(n) } }` + `fn eat2(r: Result[Node, i64]) -> i64 { match r { Ok(n) => n.val, Err(_e) => 0 } }` + `fn eat(r: Result[Node, i64]) -> i64 { eat2(r) }` + main `let d = take(); eat(d)` — LSan reports the Node leak; swap the container to `Option[Node]` and it is Clean (Option has the full move-out coordination; the pre-existing alias/closure_capture Result-only Leak cells are the B-2026-07-12-24 residual, but forwarding_chain was Clean before this window). Leak-only: the matrix hard safety invariant holds (no MemError cell, so no double-free/UAF). FIX DIRECTION: the borrow-forward relaxation must not classify a param whose whole value is FORWARDED TO A CONSUMING (owned-param) callee as an elidable borrow when the payload is `Result[shared]` — either exclude Result-carrying owned-forward edges from the elidable set (conservative, mirrors the Option-vs-Result coordination asymmetry) or transfer the release obligation to the consuming callee. DO NOT update the matrix FLOWS.expected — the frontier print exists precisely to catch this. Next for the rc-elide owner: confirm which of `e39db64` / `2639536` flips the cells, then re-run the matrix + the rc-elide spike bench so the fix keeps the 17–32% read-only-tree-walk win.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: caught by the full `--features llvm` suite gate run during the Phase-8 Interner codegen slice, on Linux where `-fsanitize=address` runs LeakSanitizer; likely landed unseen because the rc-elide session's local asan gate ran on macOS, which has NO LSan (the exact CLAUDE.md leak-class blind spot — the Linux `memory-sanitizer` CI leg is the authoritative gate). — CLASS NOTE: leak-only regression on a default-ON optimization path (RC-elision borrow-forward relaxation) — SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`src/rc_elide.rs` classification + the borrowed_arg_skip/borrowed_param_dec_skip release-skip channel).

title: `String.replace(from, to)` never freed its fresh-owned String ARGUMENTS — a fresh-temp arg (`s.replace(a.to_string(), b.to_string())`) leaks once per arg per call, unbounded in a loop. `replace` was the sole arg-consuming String method missing the `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` cleanup its siblings already do.
investigation: `String.replace`'s codegen (src/codegen/vec_method.rs ~1551) compiled both args to `(ptr,len)` pairs for `karac_string_replace` (which copies the matched/replacement bytes into its own fresh result buffer) but discarded the arg struct values without freeing — so a fresh-temp arg (cap>0, no other owner) leaked. Confirmed by probe a4 (`s.replace("-".to_string(), "_".to_string())` ×100 → 200 bytes / 200 blocks = 2 args/iter) and a2 (chained-temp arg → `karac_string_to_lowercase` frame). Audited the sibling arg-consuming String methods: `contains` (5753), `starts_with` (703), `split` separator (819), `find` (1197), `strip_prefix`/`char-index` family all already call `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` — `replace` was the only omission (verified sib1/sib2/sib3 probes leak-free). Fixed by capturing both arg struct values and calling `free_fresh_owned_str_arg(&args[i].value, val)` after `build_string_xform_result`. `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` self-gates on `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp` (or fresh string-slice) + `free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap`'s cap>0 guard, so a borrowed identifier / rodata literal arg is never freed; a moved owned-binding arg is reclaimed by the existing arg-move machinery (verified r4 leak-free), and reusing an identifier/field arg after the consuming call is correctly rejected at check time (r1/r2 ownership move errors). Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_string_replace_fresh_temp_args_no_leak (fresh-temp + chained-temp + moved-binding shapes, 200× → 5400, LSan-clean). Sibling of the chained-string-method leak B-2026-07-16-21 and the unwrap_or default leak B-2026-07-16-22 — the same fresh-owned-temp-consumed-by-a-copying-builtin class.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (`String.replace` in src/codegen/vec_method.rs — missing arg cleanup) — CLASS NOTE: memory leak (fresh-owned String argument discarded unfreed) — SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (String methods with fresh-temp arguments) + valgrind — `s.replace("-".to_string(), "_".to_string())` in a loop leaks both fresh-temp args (200 blocks / 100 iters, `karac_string_*` frame). Sibling `contains`/`starts_with`/`split`/`find`/`strip_prefix` with fresh-temp args are clean.

fix: 13eda85 (direct-return leg, sibling session) + 2df786d (rebind leg) — both legs were the same missing-SelfValue gate at different call sites of the owned-struct move-out suppression. Direct return (`fn ident(self) -> T {{ self }}`): suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex's var_name match gained a SelfValue -> 'self' arm (gated to an inline-struct self slot so ref self never GEPs through a borrow pointer). Rebind (`let mut b = self; ...; b` — the builder/fluent shape): the Let-arm's move-source resolution in stmts.rs likewise resolves SelfValue to 'self', routing it into the same helper (and into suppress_user_drop_for_var for user-Drop structs) exactly like a plain owned-struct Identifier move. NOT a deeper deep-copy interaction after all — the deep-copy at entry was correct; only the suppression routing missed SelfValue. Verified: builder chain, String-field rebind, user-Drop rebind — interp/JIT/native parity, valgrind clean (drop count exactly matches the two deep-copied instances); memory_sanitizer + codegen suites green. Pins: asan_owned_self_direct_return_no_double_free (13eda85), asan_owned_self_rebind_builder_chain_no_double_free (this fix).
title: An owned `self` receiver method returned/moved with a non-empty heap (Vec/String) field DOUBLE-FREES the field buffer. DIRECT-RETURN leg (`fn ident(self) -> T { self }`) FIXED in 13eda85. The REBIND leg (`fn add(self){ let mut b = self; b.field.push(x); b }`, the common builder/fluent shape) STILL crashes (native UAF / JIT abort; interp correct) — a deeper deep-copy-at-entry interaction. Free-function equivalent is clean throughout; the bug is `self`-receiver-specific.
investigation: Minimal repros (interp = oracle; JIT/native diverge): (min6) `impl Builder { fn ident(self) -> Builder { self } }` — with `parts` empty it's CLEAN (min7), with `parts` non-empty (cap>0) JIT gives empty (native masks; VG shows UAF). (min2/ff2) the builder `fn add(self, p) { let mut b = self; b.parts.push(p); b }` chained — the SECOND `add` (self carries a 1-element Vec) double-frees BOTH native and JIT (VG=1..10; the push's realloc reads a freed buffer). NOT heap-element-specific: `Vec[i64]` field crashes too (min3), so it is the Vec BUFFER, not its String elements. The FREE-FUNCTION twin `fn add(bld: Builder, p) -> Builder { let mut b = bld; b.parts.push(p); b }` is CLEAN (min4) — the ONLY difference is the moved source being `self` (`ExprKind::SelfValue`) vs a plain owned struct param `bld` (`ExprKind::Identifier`). ROOT CAUSE DIRECTION: the owned-struct move-out suppression sites gate on `ExprKind::Identifier` and skip `ExprKind::SelfValue`, so `self`'s scope-exit StructDrop is NOT suppressed when self is moved out — both the returned value's owner (caller's binding) and self's StructDrop free the same field buffer. Two suppression sites are implicated: (1) the `let mut b = self` binding move-suppression in the Let arm (src/codegen/stmts.rs ~4302, `if matches!(&value.kind, ExprKind::Identifier(_)) { suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg(value) }` — misses SelfValue); (2) the tail-return suppression `suppress_cleanup_for_tail_return` → `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex` (src/codegen/call_dispatch.rs ~4364, `let var_name = match &arg_expr.kind { Identifier(n) => n, _ => return }` — returns early for SelfValue). Owned `self` IS made callee-owned (deep-copied at entry, StructDrop registered) by `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` (functions.rs ~1636) exactly like a free-fn owned struct param, so the drop exists; it is just not SUPPRESSED on move-out because SelfValue isn't recognized. FIX-PATH: teach the owned-struct move-out suppression to treat `ExprKind::SelfValue` as the binding named `self` (var_type_names['self'] / variables['self'] are populated) — i.e. add a `SelfValue => "self"` arm to `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex`'s var_name resolution AND broaden the Let-arm `matches!(Identifier)` gate to also accept SelfValue, mirroring the `ExprKind::SelfValue => Some("self")` pattern already used in the FieldAccess move-out arms (stmts.rs:4095/4319/4725). VERIFY the struct-cap-zeroing (`zero_struct_move_caps` / `track_struct_var` source suppression) actually fires for a struct var named 'self' (the Identifier path handles Vec/String/tensor/map vars directly; the named-struct StructDrop suppression is the exact mechanism to trace and mirror). DELICATE: owned-struct move semantics are load-bearing (B-2026-06-10-2, #14/#17 caller-owned vs callee-owned) — any fix needs the full codegen + memory_sanitizer suites (incl arm64 LSan) green. Deferred from an otherwise-productive fresh-temp-leak session to avoid a risky late change. Distinct from the fresh-temp/consumed-arg leak family (B-2026-07-16-21/22/24); this is a receiver-ownership double-free. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (13eda85): DIRECT-RETURN leg FIXED — `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex` now resolves `ExprKind::SelfValue` to the `self` binding (inline-owned-guarded via a `StructType` slot check, so a `ref self` pointer is never touched), so an owned `self` whose TAIL expression is `self` gets the same struct-cap-zeroing an owned struct Identifier tail does — self's StructDrop no-ops and the caller's binding is the sole owner. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_owned_self_direct_return_no_double_free (200x, non-empty Vec[i64] field -> 400, ASAN/LSan-clean); full codegen (2415) + memory_sanitizer (748) green (no regression). STILL OPEN — the REBIND leg (`let mut b = self; …; b`): broadening the `let`-binding move-suppression gate (stmts.rs ~4303) to accept SelfValue did NOT fix it (min5/min2 still crash), so the `let b = self` source-cap suppression flows through a DIFFERENT let-arm than the one modified, OR the deep-copy-at-entry (`make_aggregate_param_callee_owned`) creates a THIRD aliasing owner the two-site suppression doesn't cover. Needs tracing which let-arm handles a SelfValue RHS whose destination is a named struct, and whether the entry deep-copy + `let b = self` produce two independent buffers (leak) vs one aliased buffer freed thrice (crash — observed). The reverted stmts.rs gate change is the starting point for whoever resumes.
detail: SURFACE NOTE: codegen (owned `self` receiver move-out suppression — `SelfValue` not handled where an owned struct `Identifier` is) — CLASS NOTE: memory unsafety (use-after-free / double-free) — owned `self` receiver returned/moved with a non-empty heap field — SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (builder / fluent API — owned `self` receiver returning self). Minimal: `struct Builder { parts: Vec[i64] } impl Builder { fn ident(self) -> Builder { self } }` with a NON-EMPTY `parts` field (cap>0). `b0.ident()` double-frees the Vec buffer.

fix: d9cd7a2 — calls.rs unwrap_or arm now calls suppress_inline_option_result_binding_move(object) at the merge point, the exact suppression unwrap/expect gained in B-2026-07-10-2 (same no-op cases: fresh-temp receiver, non-heap payload; the absent path zeroes a payload-less slot harmlessly). Pin: asan_sequential_unwrap_or_on_named_option_binding_no_double_free.
title: `let a = r.unwrap_or("x").len();` on a let-bound `Option[String]` double-frees SEQUENTIALLY (no auto-par): unwrap_or's present branch reconstitutes the payload as a shallow alias the chained consumer frees as an owned temp, and the receiver binding's scope-exit FreeInlineOptionPayload frees the same buffer again ('free(): double free'; interp correct). unwrap/expect already suppressed the source (B-2026-07-10-2); unwrap_or missed it.
investigation: Isolated from the B-2026-07-16-19 repro: its `e.unwrap_or("none").len()` statement was only clean because a ''-split payload has cap 0 — re-probing with `first_word("hello world")` crashed with KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 and 0 par refs in the captured IR, proving the auto-par framing wrong for this leg. Audit of the other extracting combinators: map / map_or / and_then / filter / map_err / ok / err / or / and / ok_or / flatten and unwrap_or_else / map_or_else / or_else are all heap-gated (loud interp-hint bail on non-trivially-copyable payloads) and `take` zeroes its source by construction — unwrap_or was the single heap-reaching hole.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe_method.kara written while decomposing the B-2026-07-16-19 auto-par double-free into capture-consume vs publish legs.

title: wasm link fails with cryptic `undefined symbol: __wasm_first_page_end` (from rustup's self-contained wasi libc.a dlmalloc.c.obj) when the PATH `wasm-ld` is older than the active rustup toolchain's wasi-libc — the linker resolution order (KARAC_WASM_LD > PATH wasm-ld > brew > rust-lld) picks the stale system linker over the version-matched rust-lld sitting in the same rustup toolchain that supplied the libc.
investigation: Hit 2026-07-17 in the Linux dev container after `rustup update stable` (1.94.1 -> 1.97.1, done to match CI's new clippy): all 40 wasm-family cli E2E tests failed at link — the NEW wasi-libc's dlmalloc references `__wasm_first_page_end`, a linker-synthesized symbol newer LLD provides, while PATH wasm-ld is Ubuntu LLD 18.1.3. hello-world links (no dlmalloc pull); anything using malloc through wasi-libc fails. Workaround verified: KARAC_WASM_LD=$(rustc --print sysroot)/lib/rustlib/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/bin/gcc-ld/wasm-ld -> all green. CI unaffected (its runner image carries a new enough lld). FIX DIRECTION: (a) minimal — catch the `__wasm_first_page_end` undefined-symbol error in the wasm link driver and append an actionable hint naming the rustup gcc-ld/wasm-ld path; (b) stronger — prefer the ACTIVE rustup toolchain's own wasm-ld/rust-lld over PATH when the self-contained sysroot comes from that toolchain, since libc and linker version-drift is exactly what the current order invites.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: environmental finding during the B-2026-07-16-19 verification sweep; filed so the next session doesn't re-diagnose it. CLASS NOTE: diagnostics — the link failure is legitimate given the mismatched linker; the bug is the resolution order + the cryptic error. FIX NOTE (6e88245): took fix (b) — resolve_wasm_linker (src/codegen/driver.rs) now prefers the ACTIVE rustup toolchain's rust-lld -flavor wasm right after KARAC_WASM_LD, before the PATH/brew probes; rust-lld is lld's wasm driver and is version-matched to the wasi-libc (both from the same toolchain), so it can't drift. Best-effort — falls through to PATH/brew if rustc/rust-lld unresolvable (non-rustup). Also added fix (a): format_wasm_link_error appends a version-drift hint on a __wasm_first_page_end/__wasm_init_memory link failure. Verified full --features llvm cli suite (523, incl. wasm_wasi + wasm_browser+threads fathom/plume E2Es) GREEN with NO KARAC_WASM_LD override on a container whose PATH wasm-ld is stale Ubuntu LLD 18.1.3. Unit tests wasm_link_error_appends_version_drift_hint / _no_hint_for_unrelated_failure.

title: In-place single-row DP `while k >= 1 { row[k] = row[k] + row[k-1]; k = k-1 }` (Pascal #119, and the general rolling-DP shape) keeps a per-iteration bounds check codegen does not eliminate — kata #119 ran 416ms vs equal-safety Rust (rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on) 311ms = 1.34x, and vs C (no checks) 239ms = 1.74x. asm isolation: the update loop emits `cmp %rbx,%rdx; ja <panic>` before the `row[k]` / `row[k-1]` loads (one check covers both, since k-1 < k), while the SIBLING `row_hash` fold loop (`while j < row.len() { row[j] }`) in the SAME binary has NO bounds check (BCE fired there). So codegen's BCE handles the canonical forward `j < v.len()` and the binary-search `mid` (B-2026-06-16-1) but NOT this pattern: the index `k` is bounded by the OUTER loop var `i` (k <= i-1) and `i <= row_index` while `len = row_index + 1`, so `k < len` holds only transitively (k <= i-1 <= row_index-1 < len). The overflow check (`jo`) is present in both kara and equal-safety Rust and is NOT the gap (rust -O 274ms vs rust-ovf 311ms shows the overflow tax is ~13%; the residual 311->416 is the bounds check). Same BCE class as the fixed binary-search B-2026-06-16-1 (there get_unchecked pinned the check as ~85% of the gap), a distinct index-derivation pattern.
investigation: 2026-07-17: #119 get_row (in-place rolling Pascal row, O(k^2) `row[k]+=row[k-1]` updates) benched 1.74x behind C and 1.34x behind equal-safety Rust (kara 416.2ms, rust-ovf 311.3ms, rust -O 274.4ms, c 239.0ms, go 439.4ms; hyperfine 30-run, x86 container). asm (objdump of the AOT binary, get_row inlined into main): the hot in-place update loop is `8d80: cmp %rbx,%rdx; 8d83: ja <panic>; 8d89: mov (%rdi,%rdx,8),%rsi; 8d8d: add -0x8(%rdi,%rdx,8),%rsi; 8d92: jo <panic>; 8d98: mov %rsi,(%rdi,%rdx,8); dec %rdx; ...` — ONE bounds check (`cmp;ja`) + ONE overflow check (`jo`) per iteration; the check on `row[k]` also covers `row[k-1]` (k-1 < k). The row_hash fold loop at 8dd0 (`while j < row.len()`) has NO `cmp;ja` before its `row[j]` load — BCE fired there — proving the pass works for the forward-len pattern but not this one. Equal-safety Rust (bounds elided by LLVM, overflow on) is 1.34x faster; the delta is the bounds check kara keeps. FIX DIRECTION: extend BCE to prove `k < len` when the index is monotone-bounded by an enclosing loop variable whose max is < the pinned Vec length — i.e. propagate the transitive chain (k <= i-1, i <= row_index, len == row_index + 1) the length-pin analysis already has the pieces for (bce_length_pin.rs / control_flow_bce.rs). NOT a correctness issue; the check is sound, just not elided. NOT worked around in the kata (#119 is written the natural in-place way; the gap is documented honestly in its README). Broad: this is the rolling-1D-DP shape (#119, and the rolling variants of #72/#115-class DPs), so the pattern recurs across the DP katas.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #119 Pascal's Triangle II — in-place single-row DP `while k >= 1 { row[k] = row[k] + row[k-1]; k = k-1 }`. Re-filed after a sibling ledger canonicalization (b3dafdf/e849c04) dropped the original d5949c9 entry; the #119 kata README cites this id. 2026-07-17 UPDATE (took a crack, refined the fix surface): the length-pin path is a DEAD END for this shape, confirmed empirically. Extended bce_length_pin.rs to recognize the inclusive `while j <= BOUND` / `for 0..=BOUND` fill (pinning `BOUND+1 <= v.len()`, sound; the `no_fire_inclusive_*` unit tests flip to fire): the pin forms but the descending in-place loop's bounds check is UNCHANGED (asm still shows `cmp;ja <panic>` before `row[k]`), and a controlled A/B (same karac +/- the change) shows 0% runtime delta on #119. Reason: `vec_len_pins` is consumed ONLY by resolve_len_pin (control_flow_bce.rs ~239), the ascending-guard `while idx < bound` -> UpperBound path; the descending loop yields no UpperBound (its guard gives only a LowerBound) and is served instead by the MONOTONE-ASSUME tier (control_flow_bce.rs ~248-802: emit `llvm.assume(k <= init)`). For LLVM to fold the check it needs `init < len`, but nothing connects the init (`i-1`, an enclosing-loop-var expr) to the pinned length relationally. REAL FIX: a relational fact in the monotone tier -- when a descending index's init derives from an enclosing loop var bounded by `while i <= N` / `i < N` and the Vec length is pinned to `N+1` / `N`, emit `assume(init < v.len())`. Soundness-critical (a wrong one is an OOB) -- needs the full codegen-E2E + memory_sanitizer x86+arm64 gates. The inclusive-fill change was NOT landed (a no-op is not worth the surface). After the 2026-07-17 sibling BCE/opt merges the container gap shrank to kara 354ms vs rust-ovf 298ms = 1.19x (was 1.34x at filing); the descending bounds check (asm `8d83: ja`) is still present, the likely residual. | 2026-07-17 DEEPER (IR/asm concept tests, attempt 2): the missing fact is NOT `init < len`, and NOT the length pin. Dumped the codegen IR (compile_to_ir_with_options) and the FINAL asm for the descending loop: (1) the monotone tier already emits `assume(k <= k.mono.init)` for the descending k; (2) codegen ALWAYS emits the per-iteration `icmp uge k, len; br oob` bounds check in the pre-opt IR — whether it survives is up to LLVM's opt passes, so pre-opt IR inspection is misleading; the FINAL asm is the signal. Concept test A (add an explicit dominating `if k >= row.len() { return }` guard on the descending init before the loop, so LLVM has `init < len` directly): the per-iteration check STILL survives in the final asm (`8d80: cmp;ja`) — so handing LLVM `init < len` is NOT sufficient; LLVM's assume+CVP does not fold this shape. Concept test B (make the descending loop READ-ONLY, `s = s + row[k] + row[k-1]`, no index store): the check STILL survives — so it is not a store-aliasing / len-invariance defeat either. CONCLUSION: LLVM will not fold a descending loop's `k < len` check from `assume(k <= init)` (+ even an explicit init<len) for this shape, period. The ONLY viable fix is a KARA-SIDE SKIP that mirrors the WORKING ascending path (`collect_asserted_bounds_from_guard` -> UpperBound -> index_bounds_already_proven skips emitting the check): add a descending-init UpperBound fact when (a) a length pin is active for the vec [re-add inclusive-fill pin, B=BOUND+1] AND (b) the descending index's init is provably < the pin bound. (b) is a RELATIONAL proof: the init is `outer_var - const` (const>=1), the enclosing loop bounds `outer_var <= U` / `< U`, and the pin bound B satisfies `U - const < B` by BoundTerm arithmetic -> init < B <= len. BLOCKER: the enclosing loop COUNTER bound (`i <= row_index`) is not currently tracked as a queryable fact at the inner index site (collect_asserted_bounds_from_guard records UpperBounds only for vars used AS INDICES, and `i` is just the outer counter) — so this needs new enclosing-loop-counter-bound tracking infrastructure, making it a multi-piece soundness-critical feature (OOB risk; full codegen-E2E + memory_sanitizer x86+arm64 gates), NOT a slice. Recommend it stays filed with this precise path until prioritized. | 2026-07-17 FIXED (6474a73): built the kara-side descending-loop skip. bce_length_pin.rs `compute_descending_skips` recognizes the nested shape {inclusive/exclusive counted fill pinning len>=B_pin} + {enclosing `while i<=U`/`<U` or `for i in _..U`/`..=U`} + {inner `while k>=LO` indexing v[k], k monotone non-increasing, k init=E(i) linear non-decreasing in i}, and proves `k <= k_init = E[i:=u_max] < B_pin <= len` via a linear-normal-form identity (B_pin - E[i:=u_max] cancels to a positive constant, so init's idents are B_pin's invariant idents). It pushes an UpperBound{k,v} fact through the existing asserted_index_bounds channel in compile_while (keyed on the inner loop cond span, precomputed per-function in functions.rs), so emit_split_bounds_check drops the UPPER half; the lower `<0` half stays and LLVM folds it from the `k>=LO` guard. Upper-only is sufficient AND robust to body mutation order (proven empirically identical to a both-halves forced-skip oracle: 297ms == 298ms). The monotone check uses the exhaustive region_bindings collector (not stmt_writes_ident, which is blind to a nested `if{k=i}` reset). Result: #119 hot loop loses its `cmp;ja` bounds check (final asm has only the `jo` overflow + the `k>=1` guard), kara ~356ms->~297ms (1.20x on a quiet host) = equal-safety Rust parity. Tests: 7 analysis unit tests (6 negatives), an IR wiring test (store keeps lower-only check), an ASAN E2E gate (#119 n=30 -> 2^30). Full codegen-E2E (2415) + memory_sanitizer x86 (753) + par_codegen (187) green; #119 valgrind-clean and byte-identical across run/interp/build/auto-par. Arm64 leak leg deferred to CI (no arch-specific alloc/free change; the skip only removes a read/write-address check, no RC/ownership surface touched).

title: `match <non-Option scalar/String> { Some(v) => …, None => … }` (Option-variant patterns on a scrutinee that is NOT an Option) PASSES `karac check` but PANICS the interpreter: 'internal error: entered unreachable code: non-exhaustive match … should be caught by exhaustiveness checker' (src/interpreter/pattern_match.rs:46). Also accepts a USER enum variant pattern on a non-matching scrutinee (`match i64 { Color.Red => … }`). Result patterns (`Ok/Err`) are CORRECTLY rejected — an asymmetry. A plausible real mistake (confusing Channel `recv()->T` with `try_recv()->Option[T]`) yields an internal-error crash, not a clear diagnostic.
investigation: ROOT CAUSE (precise): `check_pattern_against` (src/typechecker/patterns.rs ~571) validates a `TupleVariant` pattern (`Some(v)`, `Ok(v)`) only when the scrutinee `expected` is a `Type::Named { name, args }` whose `name` is an enum in `self.env.enums` with the matching variant; otherwise it falls through to a SILENT FALLBACK ('bind sub-patterns to Error': `for pat in patterns { self.check_pattern_against(pat, &Type::Error, mode) }`) that binds the sub-pattern to `Type::Error` and returns WITHOUT reporting a mismatch. So `Some(v)` against `i64` binds `v: Error` (no error). Compounding: the `PatternKind::Binding` arm (~540) treats an unqualified name that is NOT a unit variant of the (non-enum) scrutinee as a plain VARIABLE binding — so `None` against `i64` becomes a catch-all variable named `None`, making the match look EXHAUSTIVE to the checker. The interpreter, by contrast, treats `None` as the real Option.None variant, so at runtime neither `Some(v)` nor `None` matches the i64 value → the exhaustiveness `unreachable!` fires (pattern_match.rs:46). Result `Ok/Err` is rejected because it routes differently (a check that DOES validate the scrutinee is a Result). SCOPE: Option `Some/None` and user-enum `Enum.Variant` patterns on any non-matching concrete scrutinee (i64, String tested) pass check + ICE; Ok/Err correctly rejected. FIX-PATH: in the `TupleVariant` fallback (and the parallel unit-variant/`None` Binding path), when `expected` is a CONCRETE non-Error type that is NOT the enum owning the variant (a primitive, a struct, or an enum lacking the variant), emit a type error ('variant pattern `Some` does not match scrutinee type `i64`; did you mean `try_recv()` which returns `Option[T]`?' for the channel case) instead of the silent Error-bind. GUARD against false positives: keep the silent-recover ONLY when `expected` is already `Type::Error` (poisoned upstream) or an unresolved type variable, so genuine error-cascade suppression is preserved. DELICATE: pattern-checking is load-bearing across every match/if-let/let-destructure; the full typechecker (1692+), interpreter, and codegen suites must stay green, and the exhaustiveness checker's own variant-coverage logic must not regress. SECONDARY (defense-in-depth): the interpreter's pattern_match.rs:46 `unreachable!` should degrade to a graceful runtime diagnostic rather than a Rust panic (coding-standard: 'never just panic'), so even a future soundness gap surfaces as an error not an ICE. Not a memory-safety bug; a check/runtime consistency hole in the AI-first wedge (a program that `karac check`s clean must run).
detail: SURFACE NOTE: typechecker (pattern-vs-scrutinee type checking — `check_pattern_against`) → interpreter ICE — CLASS NOTE: typecheck soundness hole (ill-typed variant pattern accepted) → interpreter panic (unreachable non-exhaustive match) — SOURCE NOTE: dogfizz probe (channel recv/try_recv confusion) — `match rx.recv() { Some(s) => …, None => … }` (recv returns T, not Option[T]; try_recv returns Option[T]) passes `karac check` but PANICS the interpreter with an internal-error ICE. Minimized to `let x: i64 = 5; match x { Some(v) => …, None => … }` — no channels needed. — FIX NOTE (b71fdd3): front-end gate in `check_pattern_against`. New conservative predicate `variant_pattern_scrutinee_mismatch` flags a variant pattern only when the borrow-peeled scrutinee provably cannot own the variant (primitive / tuple / array / vector / slice / callable / known struct / enum lacking the variant); stays silent for `Type::Error`, unresolved type vars, generic params, and opaque types (`Shared`/`Rc`/`Arc`/pointers/existentials) so error-cascade suppression + generic code are untouched. `TupleVariant` fallback emits `PatternScrutineeMismatch` (E0273) then Error-binds sub-patterns for recovery; `Binding` arm classifies a dotted `Color.Red` or a bare PascalCase known-unit-variant (`None`) as a variant pattern (mirrors interpreter) and emits the same error instead of silently binding a variable (case-class invariant means no genuine snake_case binding is stolen; a PascalCase non-variant still binds). Both match drivers skip the exhaustiveness check on a scrutinee-mismatched match so it reports one clear type error, not a redundant non-exhaustive tail. Defense-in-depth: interpreter `eval_match`'s `unreachable!` degrades to `record_runtime_error` (no Rust panic). Regression tests in tests/typechecker.rs + tests/interpreter.rs. typechecker/interp/codegen(--features llvm)/memory_sanitizer green; fmt+clippy clean.

title: `Vec[Tensor]` element ownership was never wired through codegen — a `Vec[Tensor]` (tensor-valued-autograd `Tape` grads/values columns) leaked every element `[rank][dims][data]` block on shared-struct field drop and on index-store overwrite, and double-freed on a moved named-tensor store. Each element is a single `ptr` matching none of the existing vec-struct / shared-refcount element shapes, so all three ownership paths fell to a bare store / primitive no-op.
detail: Three facets, one root cause (codegen had no Tensor-element case for a Vec). (a) LEAK — shared-struct `Vec[Tensor]` FIELD drop: `emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr` had no Tensor arm → the per-element drop was `emit_primitive_drop_fn` (ret void) → the buffer freed but each element block leaked. (b) LEAK — index-store overwrite (`grads[i] = old + g`): `compile_vec_index_store` fell to a bare `build_store`, orphaning the displaced old block. (c) DOUBLE-FREE — moved named-tensor store (`grads[i] = seed`): the Vec took over the block but `seed`'s own `FreeTensor` also fired. Severity high because facet (c) is a genuine double-free (crash), though the dominant, feature-blocking facets were the two leaks.
investigation: Surfaced building tensor-valued autograd (a shared struct `Tape { grads: Vec[Tensor[f32,[?]]] }`): the AOT binary leaked/segfaulted while the interpreter was correct. Bisected under Linux LSan/ASAN into three isolating repros. Fix in four parts: (1) `emit_tensor_drop_fn` (src/codegen/clone_drop.rs) — load the block ptr from the element slot and `free` it; `free(null)` is a no-op so the move-suppression sentinel needs no guard (same convention as `FreeVecBuffer`'s `elem_is_tensor` drain). (2) A `head == Some("Tensor")` arm in `emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr` routing to it. (3) The shared-struct VecOrString drain (src/codegen/synth_drop.rs) calls it for a Tensor element via a local `elem_is_tensor` check — deliberately NOT by widening `elem_te_needs_direct_recursive_drain`, which also gates the owned-param deep-COPY in param_own.rs (no tensor-element clone exists, so widening it would unbalance copy-depth vs drop-depth → double-free for a VALUE struct field; a shared struct is RC-shared, never deep-copied, so the shared drain has no copy peer). (4) A Tensor branch in `compile_vec_index_store` (src/codegen/collections.rs) freeing the displaced old block before the store, plus `target_owns_heap_vec_elem` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) extended so a Tensor-element Vec index target suppresses the moved source binding's `FreeTensor` (both Identifier and FieldAccess container shapes). Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs asan_shared_struct_vec_tensor_field_drop / asan_vec_tensor_element_overwrite / asan_vec_tensor_accumulate_loop — LSan-clean + value-correct; full memory_sanitizer (750) and tensor codegen (59) suites green, no regressions. Enables the tensor-valued autograd surface (scalar core shipped in 78da0ca).

source: dogfood: sample profile of the 4g fresh-vs-reuse LBM gap during the allocator-recycling slice
title: Par-tabulate install of a pre-seeded accumulator takes the combine APPEND arm — a serial total×elem memcpy on the parent thread per dispatch while every worker parks; the Vec.new()-in-counted-loop → with_capacity(n) rewrite pre-seeds EVERY rewritten par collect, so the idiomatic shape paid 2.1× wall (1M LBM 123.6 vs 57 ms). Misdiagnosed on 2026-07-16 (4737706a) as page-fault serialization; the accidental control: Vec.clear() FREES its buffer (cap→0), so the 'reuse' kata never reused memory — it just hit the zero-copy ADOPT arm.
investigation: Source detail: sample profile of the 4g fresh-vs-reuse LBM gap during the allocator-recycling slice — the new cache's on/off A-B showed IDENTICAL page reclaims + instructions (killing the recorded fault hypothesis); main+_platform_memmove = 61% of loop wall, workers 70% cvwait, effective cores 5.9 vs 11.5 — emit_reduce_collect_call always premalloc'd a separate iter_total×elem buffer and installed via the size-keyed combine: adopt (dst.cap==0) is zero-copy, but the with_capacity rewrite makes dst.cap==n → append.in.place memcpys the whole result while 12 workers sit in cvwait. Fix: in-place install — load acc {data,len,cap} under the iter_total>0 guard; when cap−len ≥ total, base = data+len·elem (workers write the accumulator buffer directly; the tabulate recognizer guarantees the body never reads the acc, same ownership argument as the noalias scopes) and the install is a single len=need store; smaller/empty accs keep premalloc+combine (adopt/append). Checkpoints byte-exact; pre-seeded-append (len>0 offset) + small-cap-combine E2Es + tab.inplace/tab.premalloc/tab.install.* IR pins. 1M LBM idiomatic: 123.6 → 57.0 ms — ties C-OpenMP 59.0 same-session, 1.7× rayon (100–102), beats the manual reuse kata (60.5).

source: probe: string-churn instruction-count regression probe vs installed main
title: Routing Vec/String frees through an unattributed karac_free_buf declaration turned every cleanup drain into a clobber-everything opaque call — LLVM knows libc free BY NAME (TargetLibraryInfo), so DSE/dead-heap-elision/store-forwarding died: +55% instructions on a String-churn loop. Caught in-slice by the regression probe; never shipped.
investigation: Source detail: string-churn regression probe during the same slice (hyperfine + /usr/bin/time -l instruction counts vs installed main): 269M → 419M instrs (+55%) with byte-identical pre-opt IR except the callee — opt -O2 A/B on the two IRs (identical except free → karac_free_buf) showed the old binary eliding entire dead iterations (stores + concat malloc + memcpy all DSE'd against the known free) while the new one kept them. Fix: restate the contract on the declaration — allocptr (load-bearing; allockind('free') is inert without it) + nocapture + noundef on the pointer param; allockind('free'), alloc-family='malloc', memory(argmem: readwrite, inaccessiblemem: readwrite), nounwind, willreturn, mustprogress on the function. Post-fix: 274M vs 269M baseline (+1.9%, the genuinely-executed frees). Checklist recorded in memory for future libc-call replacements; alloc-side wrappers (karac_alloc_or_panic) remain unattributed — possible follow-on (willreturn is WRONG there: they abort on OOM).

source: test-infra: e2e_vec_binary_stays_lean_no_heavy_runtime_floor on the final stable-archive suite run
title: The buffer-cache's first cut used OnceLock/Mutex/env::var_os/eprint_fmt inside the force-kept karac_alloc_or_panic/karac_free_buf closure — ONE reachable slice_index_fail links rust_begin_unwind → the std panic/backtrace cluster, ballooning EVERY Vec/String binary 33 KB → 285 KB. Caught in-slice; never shipped.
investigation: Source detail: e2e_vec_binary_stays_lean_no_heavy_runtime_floor in the final stable-archive suite run (two earlier runs were VACUOUS for this test — archives were rebuilding concurrently, and link-time tests soft-skip on link failure) — -why_live chain: karac_alloc_or_panic → buf_cache::take → stats dump → StackMsg::write_str (core::fmt machinery) → slice_index_fail → panic_fmt → rust_begin_unwind → std panicking/backtrace (fs/path/thread). Even fatal::eprint_fmt is NOT safe from kept symbols — the fmt::Write plumbing itself carries the panic path; the kept-closure discipline is raw write_stderr bytes only. Fix: rewrote the cache module libc-style — CAS spinlock (no std Mutex), getenv tri-state (no env::var_os/OnceLock), get_mut/leading_zeros/saturating ops (no indexing/ilog2 panic paths), hand-rolled panic-free counter dump. minlink probe 379 KB → 35.3 KB (baseline 34.6 + ~0.7 KB cache). Process rule recorded: the final gating suite must run against STABLE archives (reference_fresh_worktree_no_archives_vacuous_green extended).

fix: 75e248d — the reduce lowering (try_compile_iter_chain_reduce) synthesizes an Option[<elem>] accumulator folded via a match and compiles that AST WITHOUT a re-typecheck pass, so the synthesized Some(<acc>) payload binding had no pattern_binding_types entry and codegen's payload reconstruction fell to the raw-i64 default: a float acc read the payload word via `sitofp i64 -> double` (the f64 bit pattern reinterpreted as an integer VALUE = garbage, so the fold never landed Some and reduce returned the None arm), and a narrow u8/i32 acc skipped truncation. Root cause was WIDER than filed — it hit narrow ints too, not just floats. Fix: give the synthesized Some(acc) binding a unique synthetic span (usize::MAX - uid, distinct per reduce) and register the element's surface name in pattern_binding_types there, so the existing float-bitcast / int-truncate reconstruction arms fire exactly as for a typechecked match. Also un-gated the direct v.max()/v.min() float fast path (B-2026-07-16-14 had bailed floats to --interp pending this) — elem_is_int became elem_is_scalar (adds f32/f64). Verified float/narrow-int reduce + direct float max/min interp/JIT/native parity, valgrind clean. Tests: test_e2e_iter_chain_reduce_float_and_narrow_int_payload (codegen) + a float leg on the B-16-14 asan pin. RESIDUAL (out of scope, filed B-2026-07-17-13): narrow-UNSIGNED reduce now yields the correct bit pattern but still PRINTS signed ([200u8].max() -> -56) — the pre-existing Option-through-unsigned-print gap (B-2026-07-03-21 class), reproduces with a hand-written match over Option[u8], untouched here.
title: Iterator.reduce over FLOAT elements returns the None arm under karac build (interp correct): `[1.5, 2.5, 0.5].iter().reduce(|a, x| if x > a { x } else { a }).unwrap_or(0.0)` prints 0 under JIT/AOT, 2.5 under --interp. The Option[A]-accumulator reduce lowering mis-handles a float accumulator (seeded-None fold never lands Some, or the payload words reconstruct wrong). Found probing B-2026-07-16-14's max/min desugar, which now GATES float elements to a loud interp-hint bail so it does not inherit this as a silent wrong answer — un-gate max/min when fixing.
investigation: Repro: fn main() { let f: Vec[f64] = [1.5, 2.5, 0.5]; println(f.iter().reduce(|a, x| if x > a { x } else { a }).unwrap_or(0.0)); } — interp 2.5, JIT/native 0 (the unwrap_or default, printed via the int formatter — possibly a second symptom: the default float renders as '0' not '0.0' through this path). Int elements are correct through the same lowering. Suspect the synthetic Some(x)/None match in try_compile_iter_chain_reduce packs/reconstructs the f64 payload word as i64 (bitcast vs value-cast mismatch), so the tag or payload comparison goes wrong. Fix direction: check the payload word packing in the reduce lowering's synthetic Option construction for FloatType elements, mirroring how Option[f64] literals pack elsewhere; then remove the float gate in method_call.rs's max/min arm and extend the asan pin with a float leg.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while implementing B-2026-07-16-14 (max/min desugar reuses the reduce lowering; the float leg diverged between backends on first probe).

title: Unknown methods on non-exhaustive prelude types (Vec/String/Map/Set/...) silently type as Type::Error, which unifies with ANYTHING: `v.some_typo()` passes karac check, and pre-B-2026-07-16-14 even `let x: bool = v.sum()` checked clean. EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE (expr_method_call.rs ~5400) covers only Option/Result, so every other built-in receiver gets the silent fall-through — the check/execution contract (the AI-first wedge: check-clean must run) is open on exactly the receivers LLM authors touch most.
investigation: The fall-through exists to tolerate user impl blocks on prelude types and comptime-derived method sets; Option/Result graduated to exhaustive once their method surfaces were complete (with the args-specialization and unsatisfied-bound refinements at the same site). Fix direction: grow EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE type-by-type as each surface is confirmed complete — Vec first (its method set is the largest and most-used; B-2026-07-16-14 closed the known gaps), then String, then Map/Set. Each addition needs a sweep of the examples/kata corpus for silently-Error-typed method calls that would newly fire (grep karac check over examples/ + kata solutions), and the same user-impl/derive escape hatches Option/Result already have (is_user_defined || method_on_other_specialization || comptime-derive gates). UPDATE 2026-07-17 (0f0c35a, PARTIAL — bug stays OPEN): two concrete sub-gaps found + fixed while investigating the Vec silent-Error hole. (a) `Vec.extend_from_slice`/`.extend` rejected a structural `Type::Slice{element}` source (accepted only `Type::Named{Slice|Vec}` + `Type::Array`) — what `String.bytes()`/`Vec.as_slice()`/`v.slice(a,b)` produce; `buf.extend_from_slice(s.bytes())` typed as `Type::Error` not `()` though both backends accept a 2-field slice source. Added the `Type::Slice` arm (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs ~3728). (b) examples/leetcode/group_anagrams.kara passed `karac check` but CRASHED under `karac run --interp` ('method collect not found on type Vec'): `Map.values()` returns an eager Vec, codegen treats `values().collect()` as identity (B-2026-07-08-17) but the interpreter has no such intercept — a run-vs-build divergence masked by this same silent-Vec hole; dropped the redundant `.collect()` (values() already returns the Vec[Vec[String]], output matches the .py oracle on interp+AOT). REMAINING (the actual gate flip): adding `Vec` to `EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE` requires first MODELLING the iterator-terminal-on-Vec surface — collect/map/filter/fold/any/all/count/position — which codegen handles via SYNTAX-DIRECTED intercepts keyed on the receiver EXPRESSION shape (values()/keys()/chars()/adaptor-chains/identifier; src/codegen/method_call.rs ~5140-5194), a form that does NOT map onto the type-directed typechecker (which sees only that values() returns Vec[V] then `.collect()` on a Vec). Flipping the gate without modelling these breaks supported patterns like `values().collect()` at `karac check`. AUDIT HAZARD: the codegen test harness (tests/codegen.rs run_program_capturing_inner) does NOT gate on typecheck errors, so the codegen suite stays green even when a gate flip wrongly REJECTS a program at check — it cannot validate the flip; a manual audit of codegen's direct-Vec-method intercepts is required. With the gate ON, the examples sweep showed ONLY these two breakages (a+b), so the non-iterator Vec surface is otherwise complete; extend_from_slice/push/pop/insert/remove/swap_remove/sort*/reverse/clear/truncate/fill/swap/get/first/last/contains/binary_search/split_at/chunks/windows/sorted*/retain/concat/join/clone/get_unchecked/try_* all resolve. Kata corpus UNAVAILABLE in the cloud container (../kara-katas absent), so full corpus verification of a gate flip is deferred to a machine with the katas. UPDATE 2026-07-17 (dc094bc, FIXED for Vec/VecDeque): the gate flip the prior partial deferred is now landed, after the audit it asked for. Empirical audit (interp + AOT, all archives built): the ONLY methods reaching the silent None arm on a Vec are (a) genuine typos and (b) iterator adaptors/terminals called DIRECTLY on the Vec (no `.iter()`) — map/collect/count/filter_map/flat_map/take/skip/ enumerate/zip/rev, plus `Map.values().collect()`. Every (b) case was verified to run on NO backend (interp 'method not found'; AOT link fail or empty-miscompile — `values().collect()` prints empty, not the [1,2] identity the old B-2026-07-08-17 note implied). The native Vec surface resolves in dedicated arms before None, and the SUPPORTED iterator form `v.iter().<adaptor>()...` routes through the `Iterator[T]` dispatch (never None) — both unaffected. So `Vec` and `VecDeque` were added to `EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE`: an absent method is now a real NoMethodFound, and when the name is an iterator-surface method (`is_iterator_surface_method`, synced to `infer_iterator_method`'s require_known_method list) the diagnostic carries an actionable `.iter()` hint. Safety: rejects only programs that already failed at runtime — an examples-corpus sweep showed 0 new rejections vs baseline (23 pre-existing fails unchanged); the AUDIT HAZARD (codegen harness not gating typecheck) is moot because no accepted-program lowering changed — codegen 2438 / msan 770 / typechecker 2056, all 0 failed. The replaced test `test_method_resolution_prelude_silent_fallthrough_preserved_when_method_absent` (which asserted the pre-fix leniency) became `test_unknown_method_on_vec_is_rejected` + `test_direct_iterator_adaptor_on_vec_rejected_with_iter_hint` + `test_iterator_chain_via_iter_still_resolves_on_vec`. REMAINING (NOT this bug): String/Map/Set keep the silent fall-through — each needs its own surface audit + corpus sweep before graduating (tracked as a follow-up, not open here). The two sub-gaps from the 0f0c35a partial (extend_from_slice Slice source; group_anagrams redundant .collect) were already fixed there.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: root-cause finding from the B-2026-07-16-14 investigation (the six filed methods were instances of this hole, not its extent).

title: A narrow-UNSIGNED value carried through an Option payload prints SIGNED: `Option[u8]` holding 200u8, unwrapped and printed, shows -56 (200 as i8) under karac build; interp correct. The unwrap/reconstruct produces the right bit pattern (i8 truncation is correct) but the print path reads it as signed because the reconstructed value's unsigned surface type is not threaded to expr_is_unsigned_int through the Option unwrap. Reproduces with a hand-written `match racc { Some(a) => .. }` over Option[u8] (NOT reduce-specific) — same class as B-2026-07-03-21's narrow-unsigned slot printing, extended to the Option-payload path.
investigation: Found while fixing B-2026-07-17-11 (float reduce): the narrow-int payload reconstruction was fixed (truncates correctly) but a u8 max reduce printed -56 not 200. Isolated to the Option-unwrap-then-print path via `let mut r: Option[u8] = None; for x in v.iter() { r = match r { None => Some(x), Some(a) => Some(if x>a {x} else {a}) } }; println(r.unwrap_or(0u8))` — JIT -56, interp 200. The unwrap_or reconstructs the u8 payload word (correct bits) but the resulting value / the `m` binding does not carry the unsigned surface type so println's formatter picks the signed i64 path. Fix direction: record the unsigned surface type of an Option[uN] unwrap result (var_type_names / the expr_is_unsigned_int lookup at the println arg), mirroring B-2026-07-03-21's par-slot signedness fix but for the unwrap-reconstruct path. Low: only narrow UNSIGNED (u8/u16/u32) values >= 2^(N-1) misprint; i64/u64/signed and small unsigned values are correct.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: isolated from the B-2026-07-17-11 float-reduce fix (the narrow-int payload roundtrip was corrected; only the unsigned PRINT remained). CLASS NOTE: miscompile (wrong printed output for a narrow unsigned through Option) — a display/signedness gap, not a memory-safety issue. FIX NOTE (8d21349): expr_is_unsigned_int's MethodCall arm now handles the Option/Result value-unwrap family (unwrap/expect/unwrap_or/unwrap_or_else/unwrap_or_default) — the result type is the payload T, so its signedness is the inner payload's; consult method_unwrap_inner_types (typechecker-populated, keyed by call span) and return type_expr_is_unsigned_int(inner_te). is_some/is_none excluded (bool result). interp/JIT/AOT parity for u8/u16/u32 + Result[u8]; signed payloads (i8/i64) stay signed. Pin: test_e2e_option_unsigned_unwrap_prints_unsigned.

title: Descending counted loop `while k >= 1 { ..; k = k - 1 }` keeps an explicit `cmp $1` after the decrement instead of folding the guard into the `dec`'s flags. Surfaced as the RESIDUAL gap on Pascal #119 after the descending bounds-check skip (B-2026-07-17-1) removed the per-iteration bounds check: the in-place update loop then runs 8 instructions (`mov;mov;add;jo;mov;dec;cmp $1;ja`) where equal-safety `rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on` runs 7 (`mov;mov;add;jo;mov;dec;jne`) — rust lowers its `(1..=i).rev()` countdown to `dec; jne` (compare-against-0 via the decrement's ZF), kara emits a separate `cmp $0x1,%rdx` dependent on the `dec`. That one extra dependent instruction per iteration is a ~1.14-1.19x residual on the tight O(k^2) update loop (kara 341ms vs rust-ovf 289ms = 1.18x; the bounds-check fix had already moved it from 1.29x). Distinct mechanism from B-2026-07-17-1 (bounds-check elision): this is loop-guard strength reduction / countdown canonicalization.
investigation: 2026-07-17: after B-2026-07-17-1 (descending bounds-check skip) landed, a drift-immune triple hyperfine (baseline-kara via KARAC_BCE_DESC_SKIP=0, fixed-kara, rust-ovf, same invocation) showed rust-ovf 1.29x faster than baseline-kara and 1.19x faster than fixed-kara — so the bounds check was ~8% and a ~1.19x residual remains. asm diff of the O(k^2) in-place update loop (get_row inlined into main, x86 container): KARA fixed `8c50: mov %rsi,%r8; 8c53: mov -0x10(%rdi,%rdx,8),%rsi; 8c58: add %rsi,%r8; 8c5b: jo; 8c61: mov %r8,-0x8(%rdi,%rdx,8); 8c66: dec %rdx; 8c69: cmp $0x1,%rdx; 8c6d: ja 8c50` (8 insns). RUST-ovf `147c0: mov %r9,%r10; 147c3: mov -0x8(%r14,%rdi,8),%r9; 147c8: add %r9,%r10; 147cb: jo; 147d1: mov %r10,(%r14,%rdi,8); 147d5: dec %rdi; 147d8: jne 147c0` (7 insns). The ONLY structural difference in the hot loop is kara's extra `cmp $0x1,%rdx` (the `k >= 1` guard) which rust folds into `dec; jne`. Both keep the `jo` overflow check (equal safety) and neither has a bounds check. ROOT: kara lowers `while k >= 1` as an `icmp` against the literal 1 on the loop var, emitted separately from the `k = k - 1` decrement; the backend does not merge them into a flag-reusing `dec; jne`/`jg`. Since in a descending loop that has just executed `k = k - 1` with prior `k >= 1`, the guard `k >= 1` is equivalent to `k != 0`, so `dec` + `jne` is sound. FIX DIRECTION: recognise the `while VAR >= C { ..; VAR = VAR - S }` countdown shape and lower it to a decrement-to-bound canonical loop (or emit the guard as `VAR != C-1`-after-step so the backend fuses it with the `dec` flags), mirroring how rust's `.rev()` range canonicalises. Likely small and NOT soundness-critical (it changes loop control emission, not memory safety), but touches loop lowering. NOT worked around in the kata (#119 stays the natural in-place descending form). Broad: every descending counted loop (`for k in (a..b).rev()`-equivalent while form) pays this, so it recurs across in-place-DP / reverse-scan katas.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #119 Pascal's Triangle II — residual perf gap found while verifying the B-2026-07-17-1 fix on the container-x86 bench. The two are distinct codegen mechanisms and are filed separately per ledger discipline (B-2026-07-17-1 = bounds-check elision, now fixed; this = descending-loop guard strength reduction, open). Measured x86 cloud container, hyperfine 30-run, equal-safety (`rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on`). | 2026-07-17 INVALID (misdiagnosis — the cmp $1 costs nothing). Took the fix; measurement refuted the premise. (1) The `cmp $0x1; ja` is FREE: a drift-immune A/B of two #119 binaries identical except for the descending loop's guard lowering (`dec; cmp $1; ja` vs a `dec; jne` countdown) measured 0% wall-clock delta (both ~1.20x behind equal-safety Rust). The loop is counter-dependency-bound and the branch predictor hides the extra compare, so the asm diff does not translate to time. (2) The blocker hypothesis was also wrong: the residual `assume(k <= k_init)` monotone fact (which B-2026-07-17-1 made redundant) does NOT gate the UPDATE loop's canonicalization — suppressing it (verified removed from the k-loop IR) leaves the update loop's `cmp $1` intact (the earlier 'confirmation' misread the FILL loop's `dec; jne`, an unrelated ascending loop). The assume-suppression change was reverted. (3) The DP update loop is actually AT PARITY with equal-safety Rust: an isolated in-place `row[k]=row[k]+row[k-1]` microbench measured 1.01x, and the real-kata update-loop asm is byte-identical to Rust's modulo the free `cmp $1`. (4) The real ~1.20x residual in the #119 BENCHMARK is a PARITY ARTIFACT, not a codegen gap: the Rust mirror fills via `vec![1i64; n]` (a tuned bulk-fill) while the kara mirror used `Vec.new()` + a push loop. When Rust is switched to a matching push loop, KARA IS 1.45x FASTER. Fixed in the kata by switching kara to `Vec.filled(n, 1)` (bulk-fill parity with `vec![1;n]`); the kata then measures the DP loop fairly. (5) A residual bulk-fill difference (`Vec.filled` vs `vec![1;n]`) of ~1.10x MAY exist but was noise-limited (hyperfine sigma +/-0.15..0.35 on this container) and is not separately filable until measured on a quiet host. NET: no compiler change; B-2026-07-17-1 (the bounds check) remains the real, shipped win, after which the #119 DP loop is Rust-parity. CLOSED INVALID 2026-07-17: the investigation's own 2026-07-17 addendum refutes the premise — a drift-immune A/B of two #119 binaries differing ONLY in the descending guard lowering (`dec; cmp $1; ja` vs `dec; jne`) measured 0% wall-clock delta (counter-dependency-bound loop; the branch predictor hides the extra compare). The DP update loop is already at equal-safety-Rust parity (isolated microbench 1.01x); the ~1.20x benchmark residual was a fill parity artifact (Vec.new()+push vs vec![1;n]), fixed in the kata via Vec.filled. No compiler change is warranted — the shipped win was B-2026-07-17-1 (bounds-check elision).

title: Two annotated opaque-handle-new bindings (`let t: Interner = Interner.new()` / `let a: Arena[T] = Arena.new()`) in the same fn are auto-parallelized into a par group, and the branch fn's scope-exit handle free (`FreeInternerHandle` / `FreeArenaHandle`) runs BEFORE the parent's joined binding uses the handle — the parent then locks a freed `Mutex` and the program HANGS pre-output (futex spin inside `karac_runtime_interner_intern`, no stdout). The UNannotated form (`let t = Interner.new()`) dodged it by accident: its RHS type is uninferable, so the return-slot inference already bailed the group to sequential. Introduced by the Interner codegen slice (2026-07-17, Phase 8) and latent until a two-handle program with annotations was compiled; caught while building the sibling Arena slice.
investigation: 2026-07-17: `let t: Interner = Interner.new(); let u: Interner = Interner.new(); t.intern("x"); u.intern("y"); println(t.len())` built clean but hung with NO output. gdb on the hung binary: 5 threads (native pool), main parked in `syscall`→`karac_runtime_interner_intern`→futex — i.e. blocked on the interner `Mutex`. `--concurrency-report` showed the two `Interner.new()` lets in one `parallel_group` ("no data or effect dependencies"). Root cause: the annotated `Interner`/`Arena` binding lowers to a `ptr` slot, so `compute_return_slots_checked`'s `infer_let_binding_llvm_type` SUCCEEDS (unlike the unannotated form) and the group really parallelizes; the branch env bit-copies the handle, the branch's `let` queued a `FreeInternerHandle` against the branch-local alloca, and `emit_par_run`'s slot-source suppression loop has NO arm for the handle-free cleanup class — so the branch frees the shared handle at branch exit and the parent's post-join `intern`/`len` operates on a freed interner (dead `Mutex` → futex hang; would be a UAF on a different allocator layout). Interp + single-handle AOT were always fine. FIX (e01b609): `compute_return_slots_checked` now returns None (bail the whole group to sequential) for any group where an escaping binding's RHS is `expr_is_interner_new` / `expr_is_arena_new`, mirroring the existing heap-env-closure group-level bail — handle construction is cheap so the lost parallelism is negligible, and sequential is always correct. Regression: `tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_two_interners_par_group_bail` (+ the Arena E2E `test_e2e_arena_foreign_checkpoint_ignored`, whose two-arena shape exercises the same bail). A richer slot-suppression + parent-re-registration arm (letting handle-new lets parallelize) is the future alternative if a real workload ever wants it.
resolution: Fixed by bailing par groups that let an opaque-handle-new binding escape; landed with the Arena codegen slice.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while adding Arena[T] codegen (the sibling opaque-handle primitive) — the two-handle par shape is common to both Interner and Arena. — CLASS NOTE: manifests as a hang (futex on a freed Mutex) on the glibc allocator; the underlying defect is a use-after-free of the runtime handle, so a different allocator could surface it as a crash/miscompile instead. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen auto-par return-slot planning (`compute_return_slots_checked`, src/codegen/stmts.rs) × the opaque-handle scope-exit cleanup (`FreeInternerHandle`/`FreeArenaHandle`).

title: Spurious RC fallback on the natural 'build a nested Vec row-by-row' shape: `while … { let mut row = Vec.new(); while … { row.push(x) } outer.push(row) }` RC-boxes `row`. The loop-body `let mut row = Vec.new()` freshly re-declares `row` each outer iteration, so consuming it via `outer.push(row)` is safe — but the reachability RC-need predicate (`reassign_kills_by_reachability`) counted only `x = …` Reassign use-sites as back-edge kills, NOT `let x = …` Define re-declarations. With no kill recognised on the C→U path, it saw the inner-loop `row.push(x)` (use U) as a reuse-after-consume of the previous iteration's `outer.push(row)` (consume C) across the outer back-edge, and defensively inserted an Rc for `row` (unnecessary retain/release overhead per row built, plus a spurious `perf[rc-fallback]` note). The single-push variant (`let mut row=Vec.new(); row.push(i); outer.push(row)`) did NOT trigger it — the INNER build loop is what makes (C, U) dominance-incomparable and routes the only C→U path through the back-edge Define, the exact shape the reachability companion exists for.
investigation: 2026-07-17: surfaced by the #120 Triangle kata's triangle-building loop (`while i<h { let mut row=Vec.new(); while j<=i { row.push(...) } tri.push(row) }`), which emitted `perf[rc-fallback]: RC fallback inserted for 'row' (direct re-use after consume); consume at <tri.push> other use at <row.push>`. The 'other use' (row.push) is BEFORE the consume (tri.push) in program order — the only 'after' path is the outer back-edge, where `row` is a FRESH `let mut row = Vec.new()`. ROOT CAUSE: `rc_predicate.rs::reassign_kills_by_reachability` — the loop-carried reachability companion to the dominance-based RC-need check — gates its three kill sub-checks (cb-after-C, ub-before-U, other-block forbidden set) on `r.kind == UseKind::Reassign` only. A loop-body `let mut row = Vec.new()` emits `UseKind::Define` (cfg.rs ~783, recorded specifically so `loop_of_consume_candidates` can tell a loop-LOCAL fresh let from an outside-loop value moved in), NOT Reassign — so the Define block never entered the `forbidden` set, `reach(&empty) && !reach(&forbidden)` stayed false (forbidden empty), and the predicate declined to kill → RC fired. The three SIBLING predicates (rc_predicate.rs ~239/499/648: loop-of-consume, trigger-2/3) already match `Reassign | Define` together; this reachability companion was the lone omission. FIX: an `is_rebind = |k| matches!(k, Reassign | Define)` helper applied to all three sub-checks. Removing the Define-block from the forward C→U reachability disconnects U from C (the only path threads the back-edge Define) → kill → RC suppressed. Purely removes an unnecessary Rc; a genuine RC/UAM shape (mutually-exclusive branches, no rebind) has `reach(&empty)==false` and is unaffected. NOT worked around in the kata (#120 keeps the natural nested-build loop). Broad: this shape is EVERY row-by-row 2D/nested-collection construction — a very common pattern — so the spurious Rc + note recurred widely.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #120 Triangle — the demo solver's deterministic-sink triangle builder. Class false-positive (the reuse-after-consume predicate wrongly fires); the user-visible effects are an unnecessary Rc (perf) + a spurious perf[rc-fallback] diagnostic. Verified memory-safe: rc_predicate + ownership unit tests, full non-codegen suite (2053), codegen E2E (2432), and memory_sanitizer ASAN (765) all green — the RC suppression introduces no use-after-move / double-free (the risk direction for an RC-removal). Regression test loop_body_let_rebind_moved_out_no_rc (tests/rc_fallback.rs).
