title: A tensor VARIABLE reassignment (`w = w - g` / `w = w + d`, where `w: mut Tensor`) never freed the displaced old block — one `[rank][dims][data]` leak per assignment, unbounded in a loop. Directly hits the gradient-descent update `w = w - lr·grad`, so any autograd training loop leaks one weight tensor per step. The Identifier-assign handler had eager-free arms for Vec/String/Map/struct vars but none for a Tensor var, so the store silently orphaned the old block.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found dogfooding examples/autograd_training.kara (the gradient-descent loop leaked one 28-byte [3]-tensor per step under Linux LSan). The tensor tape ops were clean; the leak was the plain `w = <fresh tensor>` update, orthogonal to the tape. Also surfaced a test-harness fidelity gap fixed in the same commit: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs's ownership gate never ran `expand_gated_stdlib_imports`, so a gated-stdlib `ref`-param fn (`TensorVar.leaf(tape, w)`) was seen as MOVING its arg (the checker couldn't see the `ref` without the module spliced) -> false `UseAfterMove` when the arg is reused, even though `karac check` accepts the program.
investigation: Isolated to a non-autograd repro (`let mut w = Tensor.from([..]); while k<5 { w = w + d; ... }` -> 140 bytes / 5 allocations, LSan). The Identifier-target arm of `StmtKind::Assign` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) stored the fresh value into the slot without freeing the old contents; its Vec/String (`trigger_eager_free`), Map/Set (`eager_free_old_map_var_handle`), and struct arms had no Tensor sibling. Fix: before the store, if the target is a tensor var (`tensor_var_infos`), load the old block pointer and `free` it -- gated `!rhs_is_self_ident` to skip `w = w` (whose value IS the old block), `free(null)` being a no-op so the move-suppression sentinel needs no guard; after the store, call `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg(value)` so a moved named-tensor RHS (`w = other`) disarms its `FreeTensor` (the LHS now owns the block). Mirrors the Vec/Map/struct eager-free + move-suppression already in the arm. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_tensor_var_reassign_loop_no_leak (isolated) + asan_autograd_gradient_descent_training (the real training loop) + the codegen/interp E2E training tests. Full memory_sanitizer (770) and tensor codegen (63) suites green -- the eager-free fires for every tensor-var reassignment with no double-free regression (tensor move `w = other` and self-assign `w = w` both handled).

title: Unknown methods on the Type::Named numerical prelude types `Tensor` and `DataFrame` silently typed as Type::Error (same check/execution hole B-2026-07-17-12 closed for Vec/VecDeque): `t.some_typo()` / `d.some_typo()` passed `karac check` then ran on no backend. EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE covered only Option/Result/Vec/VecDeque, so these two numerical receivers kept the silent fall-through.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: follow-up graduation from B-2026-07-17-12 (whose title over-broadly named 'Vec/String/Map/Set/...'). A per-type sweep found the actual remaining silent-fall-through receivers were NOT String/Map/Set — those already reject unknown methods via their own dedicated `require_known_method` dispatch arms (String via stdlib_seq, Map/Set/SortedMap/SortedSet via stdlib_map, Column via its own arm; all verified rejecting `some_typo()`). The genuinely-open Type::Named holes were `Tensor` and `DataFrame`; the third hole found — fixed-size `Array[T, N]` — is STRUCTURAL (`Type::Array`, not `Type::Named{"Array"}`) and never reaches the Named-keyed None arm, so it is tracked separately (B-2026-07-17-19, open).
resolution: Added `Tensor` and `DataFrame` to `EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE` (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs), so an absent method on either is a real NoMethodFound instead of the silent all-unifying `Type::Error`. Audit: the numerical surfaces (`Tensor.reshape/permute/zip_with/matmul/map/sum/fold/argmax/...` via `infer_tensor_*`; `DataFrame.insert/width/height/column/column_names/has_column/describe/select` via the dataframe arms) all resolve BEFORE the None arm, so only genuinely-absent names are rejected. Verified the full Tensor/DataFrame method surface used across tests+examples resolves at check (incl. the codegen-only blind spot — codegen.rs's `df.sum()` is a misleadingly-named Column receiver, not DataFrame). Safety: examples-corpus sweep 0 new rejections vs baseline (23 pre-existing fails unchanged); typechecker 2058 / codegen 2439 / memory_sanitizer 770 all 0-failed. Pins: tests/typechecker.rs::test_unknown_method_on_tensor_dataframe_rejected + test_graduated_numeric_types_legit_methods_still_resolve. NOTE: `Tensor`/`DataFrame` are not `.iter()`-adapted, so their rejection carries the edit-distance typo suggestion, not the Vec/VecDeque `.iter()` hint.

title: Unknown methods on a fixed-size `Array[T, N]` silently type as Type::Error and pass `karac check`, then run on no backend — the same check/execution hole B-2026-07-17-12/-18 closed for the Named prelude types, but `Array[T, N]` is a STRUCTURAL `Type::Array{element,size}`, not `Type::Named{"Array"}`, so it bypasses the Named-keyed EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE None arm entirely; adding "Array" to that list is dead code.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: discovered during the B-2026-07-17-18 Tensor/DataFrame graduation sweep. `let a: Array[i64, 3] = [1, 2, 3]; a.some_typo();` passes `karac check` (verified) but `karac run --interp` reports 'method not found', and a direct iterator adaptor `a.map(...)` (no `.iter()`) is likewise silent-then-unrunnable — exactly the Vec-class hole, but on the structural array type. The fixed-array receiver's methods (`a.len()`, `a.iter()`) resolve via a separate structural-`Type::Array` dispatch path, and unknown ones fall through to a silent branch that never binds `type_name = "Array"`, so the Named-keyed exhaustive gate cannot see them.
resolution: FIXED for the common SCALAR-element case (4e6cbc8), across typechecker + codegen (interp already matched). A scalar-element fixed `Array[T, N]` now models `len`/`is_empty`/`get`/`first`/`last`/`contains` at check (get/first/last -> Option[T], contains -> bool), with matching codegen (`compile_fixed_array_read`, src/codegen/method_call.rs: bounds-check -> GEP over the array's stack storage -> Option via phis / unrolled-OR contains; wired into the owned-array and `ref Array` blocks). Modelling `get` as Option[T] closes the soundness hole (`let x: bool = a.get(0)` is now a real error, not silent Error); typing it precisely also fixes the float-payload reconstruction (an f64 `get` printed its i64 bit pattern under AOT before, because the untyped payload defaulted to i64). Any OTHER method on a fixed array (`to_vec`/`slice`/`rev`/typos — interp 'method not found'; direct iterator adaptors — miscompile) is now a real NoMethodFound with the `.iter()` hint for iterator-surface names, instead of the silent Type::Error catch-all. Verified interp == JIT == AOT on i64/f64/bool/`ref Array`, valgrind-clean, examples sweep 0 new rejections, typechecker 2060 / codegen 2439 / memory_sanitizer 770 all 0-failed. Pins: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_fixed_array_read_methods, tests/typechecker.rs::test_fixed_array_read_methods_are_modelled + test_absent_fixed_array_methods_rejected_with_iter_hint. DEFERRED (not this bug): NON-scalar element arrays (Array[String/Vec/struct, N]) — get/first there need heap/borrow (Option[ref T]) handling the scalar codegen arm does not provide, so they stay rejected at check (a loud, correct error rather than the old silent-then-crash); and the wider Vec surface on arrays (to_vec/slice/rev) remains unimplemented by design (use `a.iter()...`).
investigation: INVESTIGATED 2026-07-17 (deep root-cause; stays OPEN, re-scoped from 'quick gate flip' to a modeling task). The fixed-size `Array[T, N]` method surface is INCONSISTENTLY MODELLED in the typechecker, and the runtime treats a fixed array as a Vec: `interpreter.rs:1764` maps `Value::Array(_) => "Vec"`, so array method calls dispatch through the Vec handler. Empirical surface (interp, `let a: Array[i64,3]`): `len`→3, `get(0)`→Some(10), `first`→Some(10), `last`→Some(30), `contains`→true, `iter().sum()`→60 all RUN but type as SILENT `Type::Error` at check (bool-trick: `let x: bool = a.len()` passes); `to_vec`/`slice`/`rev` → interp 'method not found'; `a.some_typo()` → 'method not found'; `a.map(|x| x*2)` RUNS but returns the array UNCHANGED (`[10,20,30]`, closure not applied — a miscompile, same class as the Vec direct-adaptor hole). So the None-arm reaches a STRUCTURAL receiver: the `_ =>` catch-all (expr_method_call.rs ~5317) for non-Named/TypeParam/Existential receivers routes `Type::Array` to the silent `else` (~5377, shared with String/bool/char/Slice/Tuple) that returns `Type::Error` without a diagnostic. WHY THE SIMPLE FIXES DON'T WORK: (1) adding "Array" to EXHAUSTIVE_PRELUDE is DEAD CODE — a fixed array is `Type::Array{elem,size}`, never `Type::Named{"Array"}`, so `type_name` is never "Array" (confirmed: `a.some_typo()` still passes after the -18 change). (2) A blanket coerce `Type::Array -> Type::Named{"Vec",[elem]}` at the top of `infer_method_call` (obj_ty, line 1488) is UNSAFE: several arms are array-AWARE and lower differently for array vs Vec — e.g. `as_ptr`/`as_mut_ptr` (~2123) GEPs element 0 of the ARRAY STORAGE for `Type::Array` but LOADS THE VEC HEADER's data field for a Vec, so a coerced array would make codegen read a Vec header out of raw array storage (garbage pointer). `as_slice`/`as_ptr` already have explicit array branches; coercion would silently mis-route them. (3) A precise reject-allowlist at the catch-all can't be made both safe and useful: since interp dispatches array-as-Vec, the safe allowlist (superset of everything that runs) is ~Vec's WHOLE surface — including `sum`/`max`/`min`/`product`, which route through Vec's direct-terminal arm (B-2026-07-16-14) and may run on an array — so a narrow reject risks false-rejecting a working method. FIX DIRECTION (proper, needs codegen coordination): MODEL the read-only fixed-array method surface as dedicated `Type::Array` arms (mirroring the Vec typing but keeping array-storage semantics): `len -> the int width Vec.len uses`, `get(i) -> Option[T]`, `first`/`last -> Option[T]`, `contains(x) -> bool`, `is_empty -> bool`, `get_unchecked` — verifying each against BOTH backends (interp already works via array-as-Vec; confirm codegen). THEN reject the genuinely-absent remainder (`some_typo`, `to_vec`, `slice`, `rev`) and route the iterator adaptors (`map`/`filter`/`collect`/…) to the `.iter()` hint (a direct `a.map` miscompiles today). Deferred: LOW severity (fixed arrays are far less common than Vec; the failures are loud-at-runtime or a contained miscompile, not silent corruption), and the fix is a per-method modeling pass with codegen coordination, not the gate flip the -12/-18 Named types allowed. NOT started as code. CODEGEN DIVERGENCE (2026-07-17, AOT via `--features llvm`): the array read surface is ALSO inconsistent across backends, which rules out the 'just model get/first/contains at check' path. On AOT, `a.len()`→3 and `a.iter().sum()`→60 BUILD+RUN, but `a.get(0)` / `a.first()` / `a.contains(20)` all BUILD-FAIL (codegen has no fixed-array arm for them) even though interp runs them via array-as-Vec. So typing get/first/contains as resolved at check would just move the hole from 'silent-Error, interp-only' to 'check-passes, AOT-build-fails' — a NEW check/execution divergence. A correct fix therefore spans all three layers: (a) typechecker modelling of the fixed-array read surface, (b) codegen arms for array get/first/last/contains (mirroring the Vec lowering but over array storage, not a Vec header — cf. the `as_ptr` array-vs-Vec split), (c) interp already works. Only `len`/`iter` are safe on both backends today. This is a bounded but genuine multi-backend feature, not a low-risk gate flip; keeping OPEN at low severity with this full characterization so it can be scheduled as real work.

title: Copying a Vec field out of a MATCH-BOUND enum payload borrowed from a ref-Vec element double-frees under AOT: `for it in items { match it { Fu(f) => { let ps = f.params; for p in ps {..} } } }` with `items: ref Vec[It]` aborts free(): double free (interp correct, prints the right sum). The struct-only twin (no enum layer — `for it in items { let ps = it.params; }` on ref Vec[F]) is CLEAN; the enum-payload match binding is essential.
detail: Minimal repro (both backends, karac check-clean): struct P { n: i64 } / struct F { name: String, params: Vec[P] } / enum It { Fu(F), Other }; fn collect(items: ref Vec[It]) iterates, matches Fu(f), copies `let ps = f.params`, sums ps. interp: 3, exit 0. AOT: free(): double free in tcache 2, SIGABRT 134. The copy of the payload's Vec field appears SHALLOW (or the copy gets a second owned drop) when the payload came from a borrowed element's match binding. SOURCE NOTE: surfaced porting the selfhost codegen signature-collect pass (Phase 12 Codegen Slice 10) — `let ps = f.params` inside `collect_sigs(items: ref Vec[Item])`'s Func(f) arm crashed the GENERATOR binary itself; worked around with indexed reads through the ref (`f.params[i].ty`, the typechecker's proven `s.fields[fi].ty` idiom), which is clean. WORKAROUND: index the borrowed payload's Vec field per-element instead of copying the whole Vec out.
investigation: FIXED (3140c6d). Root cause: the struct payload binding `f` from a match arm on a BORROWED ref-Vec enum element is classed read-only (`scrutinee_is_readonly_owned_agg_loop_var` -> `pattern_binding_is_borrow`), which suppresses `f`'s OWN drop but materializes `f` as a POINTER alias into the container-owned enum payload. `let ps = f.params` then copied the Vec field's `{ptr,len,cap}` by value (shallow) so `ps` aliased the container's buffer; `ps`'s scope-exit `FreeVecBuffer` plus the caller's element drop both freed it -> double-free. The struct-only twin (`for f in items { let ps = f.params }` over `ref Vec[F]`) was already clean: its `f` is in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars`, which drives `deep_copy_owned_struct_param_field_move` (clone-on-extract). The enum-payload match binding reached NEITHER `for_loop_owned_agg_vars` NOR `owned_struct_params` NOR `shared_enum_payload_view_vars`, so the deep-copy early-returned. (String fields were unaffected — they deep-copy via a separate always-on path; only the Vec field-copy gates on the owned-source sets. Confirmed discriminators: `.clone()`, indexed reads, and iterating `f.params` directly were all clean.) FIX: a new `borrowed_agg_payload_struct_vars` set (codegen.rs), populated per borrow-path match arm by `register_borrowed_agg_payload_struct_bindings` + `collect_variant_payload_binding_names` (tuple- AND struct-variant patterns; control_flow_match.rs), is consulted in `deep_copy_owned_struct_param_field_move`'s source condition. So a Vec field copied out of such a binding deep-copies its buffer, exactly like the for-loop struct-element sibling. The binding is a pointer alias (not a struct VALUE), so no StructType gate is applied; the field-access RHS gate keeps a non-struct payload inert. Verified interp == JIT == AOT (i64-struct + String Vec elements), valgrind-clean, examples sweep 0 new failures, codegen 2441 / memory_sanitizer 771 / non-codegen all 0-failed. Pins: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_match_bound_enum_payload_vec_field_copy_no_double_free, tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_match_bound_payload_vec_field_copy_over_ref_vec. RELATED FINDING (separate, filed B-2026-07-18-4): the STRUCT-VARIANT twin `enum It { Fu { params: Vec[P] } }` + `match it { Fu { params } => { let ps = params } }` (binding the Vec field DIRECTLY, not via a struct payload `f.params`) is a DIFFERENT shape — it miscompiles to an EMPTY Vec under AOT (sum 0 vs interp's correct value), not a double-free — and pre-dates this fix (verified RED on clean main); untouched here.

title: CONTEXT-DEPENDENT AOT memory corruption in the selfhost codegen generator once the Slice-12 struct machinery (a ~21-Vec-field Emitter struct + StructLit/Field emit_value arms with nested loops over self-field tables) executes on a struct-bearing input: the karac-build generator SIGABRTs (free(): double free / glibc abort) while `karac run --interp` produces byte-correct IR (verified: the interp-emitted IR runs green under the JIT). NOT attributable to a single line: bisection kept moving the crash across shapes (returning `self.st_ty[i]` from a method, `lit_ops[li].clone()`, bare `let want = self.st_field_names[off+fi]` in a loop, even an EMPTY while body once a sibling table field was removed) — the trigger is the surrounding frame/layout context, not the specific read. A dummy replacement Vec field does NOT restore green, so it is not purely field-count.
detail: Repro: check out the Slice-12 selfhost/src/codegen.kara (commit with this ledger entry), build the selfhost driver per tests/selfhost_codegen.rs with a struct input program (e.g. 'struct P { x: i64, y: i64 } fn main() { let p = P { x: 1, y: 9 }; println(p.x.to_string()) }') via `karac build`, run the generator: SIGABRT. Same driver under `karac run --interp`: correct IR, and the IR executes green. The struct machinery is INERT for non-struct inputs — the full 68-program oracle (stdout+exit+valgrind) stays green with the machinery landed, so the corruption requires the StructLit/Field arms actually running. Related-but-distinct: B-2026-07-17-20 (indexed enum-payload Vec-field copy, minimal 25-line repro) — that one is a clean per-shape bug; this one is context-dependent (the same reads are green in reduced arms and red in the full arm). Likely the B-2026-07-12-31 frame-corruption FAMILY (large recursive match + many heap locals/fields) — that fix (237e27b) may need extending to this shape. SOURCE NOTE: surfaced porting Phase 12 Codegen Slice 12 (POD structs); Slice 12 corpus entries are DEFERRED until this is fixed (machinery landed inert + interp-verified). [renumbered from B-2026-07-18-1 on 2026-07-18: id taken by a concurrent session]
resolution: ROOT-CAUSED + FIXED 2026-07-18: NOT frame corruption — a for-loop element classification gap. `field_copy_supported` hard-bailed on a DIRECT bare-`shared` field, so a `Vec[S]` element (S = {String, shared handle}) was never registered in `for_loop_owned_agg_vars`; a destructured String leaf (pushed onward) or a whole-move (`let x = lf`) aliased the element's buffers and double-freed against the container's per-element drain. Debug route: valgrind'd the crashing generator, runtime-side 2-byte-clone logging matched the invalid-free address to a struct FIELD-NAME clone ('x'), standalone-minimized to 14 lines (V5), plus a whole-move sibling (V6). Fix: the for-loop registration gate ORs a STRICT-SHARED copy-support mode (`copy_support_for_loop_shared_mode`: direct bare-shared fields supported — move-out copies rc-INC via the existing `deep_copy_rc_inc_bare_shared` arm, symmetric with the drain's rc-DEC; `Option`/`Result` fields banned — a shared-bearing struct's drain skips Option fields, so a registered element's aliased Option leaf would lose its `!view_src` leaf-cleanup and leak, caught by asan_vec_of_struct_shared_and_option_field_consumed_no_leak on the first attempt); the whole-move deep-copy site engages the rc-inc mode. The 'context-dependence' during the original bisection was an artifact of which reads happened to run, not the trigger. Selfhost struct generator now AOT-green + valgrind-clean end-to-end; codegen 2441 / msan 771 / selfhost oracle / par 190 all green. Regression tests: codegen::for_loop_shared_bearing_struct_element_destructure_and_whole_move + msan::asan_for_loop_shared_bearing_struct_elem_move_out_no_double_free.

title: KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 (auto_par_disabled) did NOT disable the parallel REDUCE lowering — only the parallel-group dispatch. A `sum += f(x)` / `for k in 0..N { acc = acc + k }` reduction still emitted a `call void @karac_par_reduce` fan-out under KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0, so the 'sequential' build a user explicitly asked for was still multi-threaded (measured 386% CPU, par_reduce call site present; wall-clock 3.7x faster than a truly sequential build). The gate is checked in compile_function_body (which short-circuits the parallel-GROUP dispatch to sequential compile_block), but reduction lowering (try_emit_reduction_lowering, reduce.rs) is reached from compile_block / compile_stmt (stmts.rs:44/813) — which run in the disabled mode too — and never consulted the gate. Consequence beyond benchmarking: the whole kata corpus's KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 A/B verification surface (the documented 'third surface' for catching effect-analysis bugs) never actually exercised sequential codegen for reduce-shaped programs — both the default and AUTO_PAR=0 builds ran the same parallel reduce, so a parallel-reduce-only divergence would slip past the A/B check.
investigation: 2026-07-18: surfaced by the #123 (Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III) kata benchmark. The natural `for _ in 0..K { sum = sum + max_profit(data) }` outer loop compiled to a karac_par_reduce fan-out under the DEFAULT build (reduce_worker symbol; CPU ~200-386%). Checking whether KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 gives a sequential baseline: objdump showed BOTH the default AND the AUTO_PAR=0 binary contain exactly one call to karac_par_reduce, and both ran at ~375-386% CPU (user>>wall) — so AUTO_PAR=0 was NOT sequentializing the reduce. ROOT CAUSE: try_emit_reduction_lowering (reduce.rs, entered from stmts.rs compile_block/compile_stmt) had no auto_par_disabled check; the gate only existed in compile_function_body for the parallel-group path. The reduce is a SEPARATE lowering reached even when auto_par_disabled routes to sequential compile_block. FIX: early-return Ok(None) (fall back to the plain sequential loop) from try_emit_reduction_lowering when self.auto_par_disabled, EXCEPT for the sequential tabulate (a reduction.seq Collect — not a fan-out, kept so AUTO_PAR=0 drops parallelism not sequential optimizations). After: AUTO_PAR=0 reduce = 0 par_reduce calls, 99% CPU, single-threaded, SAME sink; default unchanged (fans out, ~3.7x speedup on a large-K reduce). NOT a miscompile (the parallel reduce computed the correct answer); this is a run-vs-build/surface-fidelity bug — the sequential escape hatch was incomplete. AUTO-PAR ITSELF IS NOT A PESSIMIZATION (an earlier hunch, retracted): at large K it is a ~3.7x win; the apparent K=10 slowness was thread-startup overhead on a tiny memory-bound workload, unmeasurable at this container's noise floor. UPDATE 2026-07-18 (4d6efad, FIXED): implemented all five in codegen (src/codegen/maps.rs) — `compile_sorted_map_option_lookup` (min/max/floor/ceiling -> Option[(K,V)] via emit_sorted_keys_buf + emit_sorted_key_cmp_fn + coerce_to_payload_words + build_option_some_via_phis; deep-clones the chosen key + looked-up value) and `compile_sorted_map_range` (inclusive [lo,hi] -> Vec[(K,V)]). Gated to a sorted receiver via `sorted_collection_vars`. Integer/String keys; other Ord key types stay interpreter-only. Byte-identical to karac run on i64 and String K/V. Tests: codegen x2, memory_sanitizer x1 (i64 ordered methods + String range). RESIDUAL: String-keyed min/max/floor/ceiling return a boxed Option[(String,String)] whose inner-heap drop leaks via a PRE-EXISTING consumer gap shared with Vec.pop of a heap tuple — filed as B-2026-07-18-3; i64 ordered methods + String range LSan-clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #123 kata bench (kara-katas). Class run-vs-build (the KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 'sequential' surface diverged from a genuine sequential build). Verified: par_codegen (190) + codegen E2E (2435) + memory_sanitizer ASAN (768) + non-codegen suite all green; new #[ignore] serialized-env regression test test_auto_par_env_gate_disables_par_reduce (tests/par_codegen.rs) pins par_reduce present with the gate on, absent with it off. The #123 kata's bench keeps a serial K-loop (data-dependency), so it is single-threaded on both surfaces regardless — the fix restores the ability to get a sequential reduce build for the rest of the corpus and for auto-par-vs-sequential benchmarking.

title: Consuming a BOXED `Option` payload whose type is a heap-containing tuple (e.g. `Option[(String,String)]`, a >3-word payload that `coerce_to_payload_words` heap-boxes) does not free the reconstructed tuple's inner heap (the String buffers) at the binding's scope exit — a per-consumption leak. Reads correctly (output right), so unbox-for-read works; only the drop of the reconstructed wide payload's inner heap is missing. Affects `Vec[(String,String)].pop()` and any `-> Option[wide-heap-tuple]` (the SortedMap min/max/floor/ceiling among them).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found implementing the SortedMap ordered-methods codegen (B-2026-07-18-1); its String-keyed min/max/floor/ceiling return Option[(String,String)] and leaked. Isolated to a PRE-EXISTING shared consumer gap by reproducing on Vec[(String,String)].pop() (an existing boxed-Option producer that transfers ownership without cloning) — same leak, so not SortedMap-specific.
investigation: Repro (Linux LSan): `while n<3 { let mut v: Vec[(String,String)] = Vec.new(); v.push((f"key-{n}-padpad", f"val-{n}-padpad")); match v.pop() { Some(kv) => println(f"{kv.0}={kv.1}"), None => ... } }` leaks 72 bytes / 6 objects = 2 String buffers/iter (karac_string_clone). Root: an Option payload wider than the 3-word inline area is heap-boxed (coerce_to_payload_words mallocs a box, stores the tuple, word0 = box ptr; docs/spikes/oversized-enum-payload.md). Match-consume unboxes for READING kv.0/kv.1 correctly (output right) and frees the box, but the reconstructed tuple's String FIELD buffers are not dropped at the binding's scope exit. FIX-PATH: at the boxed-payload unpack/drop site (reconstruct_payload_value / rebuild_value_from_payload_words + the enum-payload drop recomputing the word_count(T)>area predicate), after loading + freeing the box, drop the reconstructed value's inner heap (its String/Vec fields) — or register the match binding for the aggregate's per-field drop. Scalar/i64 boxed payloads unaffected. Distinct from B-2026-07-18-1 (the SortedMap feature is correct/byte-identical; it merely newly exercises this shared gap). UPDATE 2026-07-18 (eab5026, FIXED for the bound case): the leak was in track_freshtemp_boxed_enum_scrutinee (control_flow_match.rs) — the box drop's inner-heap cleanup was derived only via inner_struct_name, which covers a whole-STRUCT payload binding (Some(h), h: H) but not a whole-TUPLE binding (a tuple has no type name). For a whole-tuple binding whose recorded pattern_binding_types is "Tuple", now synthesize the tuple's per-element drop fn (synthesize_tuple_drop_fn_te) and route the box drop through track_boxed_enum_var_with_inner_drop — mirroring clone_and_track_borrow_binding's tuple branch. Sound/no double-free: box-only-free never touches the inner buffers and a whole-tuple match binding is not otherwise drop-registered; all-inline wide tuples (drop fn None) stay box-only (no change); the per-element destructure Some((a,b)) was already clean. Verified LSan-clean: extended asan_sorted_map_ordered_methods_no_leak (String min/max/floor/ceiling consumed via Some(kv)) + new asan_boxed_opt_heap_tuple_consume_no_leak (Vec[(String,String)].pop, both whole-tuple and destructure). Full ASAN 773 + codegen 2445 green. RESIDUAL (now the ONLY remaining facet, a DISTINCT older deferral): an UNBOUND Some(_) boxed heap-tuple — a wildcard pattern carries no static payload type, so the box tracker is skipped entirely (word count 0 <= area); needs the scrutinee's static type (spike §1, oversized-enum-payload.md). Not what this entry's repro (Some(kv)) describes, so this entry is closed; the Some(_) facet stays deferred in-code.

title: A STRUCT-VARIANT enum payload's Vec field bound DIRECTLY in a match arm over a borrowed ref-Vec element, then moved into a local (`enum It { Fu { params: Vec[P] } }`; `for it in items { match it { Fu { params } => { let ps = params; for p in ps {..} } } }`, `items: ref Vec[It]`), miscompiles under AOT to an EMPTY Vec — the sum is 0 vs the interpreter's correct value. check-clean, interp-correct, AOT wrong-output (not a crash).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while fixing B-2026-07-17-20 (the TUPLE-variant sibling `Fu(f)` + `let ps = f.params`, a field-COPY that double-freed — fixed in 3140c6d). This entry is the STRUCT-variant shape where the Vec field is bound directly as `params` (not through a struct payload `f`), then whole-moved via `let ps = params`. Distinct mechanism (Vec-identifier-move from a borrowed struct-variant field binding, not a field-access copy) and distinct symptom (empty-Vec miscompile, AOT sum 0, NOT a double-free). Verified RED on clean main (independent of the -20 fix): `interp=9`, `aot=0` for a 2-element `Vec[P]`. The B-2026-07-17-20 fix (`borrowed_agg_payload_struct_vars` + `deep_copy_owned_struct_param_field_move`) does not apply — that path gates on a FIELD-ACCESS RHS (`f.params`), whereas `let ps = params` is an Identifier RHS.
investigation: FIX DIRECTION: the borrowed struct-variant field binding `params` reads an empty/wrong Vec when bit-copied out of the pointer-aliased payload. Likely the borrow-path payload binding for a struct-variant field materializes the Vec by-value from an alias whose contents aren't loaded (or a move that zeroes the source). Parallel to B-2026-07-17-20 but for the whole-binding move rather than the field-access copy — the `let ps = params` Identifier-RHS move needs the same deep-copy-from-container-owned treatment, routed through the identifier-move path (`deep_copy_for_loop_agg_element_move` sibling / `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg`) rather than `deep_copy_owned_struct_param_field_move` (which is field-access-only). Medium severity: silent wrong-answer under AOT (not a loud crash), on a borrowed struct-variant enum shape. FIX (8c9fb2b): threaded a new `borrow_vec_typed_exprs` span set from the typechecker's `expr_types` (spans typed `Ref`/`MutRef` of a `Vec`/`VecDeque`/`Slice`) through lowering (src/lowering.rs) into codegen (src/ast.rs Program field + src/codegen.rs field/init/assign/par-swap) — the authoritative borrow-vs-owned discriminator. In the Let path (src/codegen/stmts.rs) a bare-Identifier RHS in that set is a re-borrow: codegen copies the source's `vec_elem_types`/`var_elem_type_exprs` registration to the new binding (so `.len()`/`for` dispatch resolve), borrow-elides it (skips `track_vec_var`), and skips the source `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg` — the container stays the sole owner, so the alias never double-frees. `local_scope[ps]` keeps `ref Vec[T]`, so a chain `let ps2 = ps` re-infers the borrow and elides identically (no codegen propagation needed). Gated to a bare-Identifier RHS so the FIELD-ACCESS shape `let ps = f.params` stays on the distinct B-2026-07-17-20 deep-copy path; the borrow classification lives in `expr_types` (NOT `bind_pattern_types`), so `ref Vec` PARAMS keep their `ptr` state-struct field (test_slice_8ad_non_generic_ref_vec_param_stores_data_ptr green — the reason an earlier `bind_pattern_types` peel was rejected). Verified interp==JIT==AOT (9) on the repro plus chained / heap-element `Vec[Q{String}]` / owned-move-out (regression) / `mut ref` variants; valgrind-clean (the 300-iter par_reduce `possibly lost` is pre-existing auto-par thread-pool noise, identical with and without the re-borrow, 0 definite/indirect). Pins: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_match_bound_struct_variant_vec_field_reborrow_over_ref_vec and tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_match_bound_struct_variant_vec_field_reborrow_no_double_free. Suites: codegen 2448 / typechecker 2065 / memory_sanitizer 775 / ownership 357 / interpreter 1239 / effect 402 / parser 755 / resolver 324 all 0-failed; clippy + fmt clean.

source: dogfood: running the resident-GPU LBM kata under the interpreter (KARAC_RUN_JIT=0) during the GPU-SLIP-4h slice
title: gpu.upload / gpu.download ICE the interpreter with `unreachable!("variable 'gpu' not found")` — no interpreter arm exists for the resident-buffer API, so the module identifier falls through to value evaluation. Any `karac run` (JIT off) of a resident-GPU program crashed instead of erroring.
investigation: eval_method_call's module-intercept table had a (gpu, dispatch) arm only; upload/download fell through to eval of `gpu` as a variable → resolver-backed unreachable!. Fixed alongside GPU-SLIP-4h: (gpu, upload|download) now return a clean record_runtime_error diagnostic ('requires the compiled path (karac build) — resident GPU buffers have no interpreter model'), matching the ptr-intrinsics compiled-only pattern. Test: tests/interpreter.rs::gpu_upload_download_get_clean_compiled_only_diagnostic. A real interpreter model for resident buffers (upload=identity, download=identity over the host Vec) would give run/build parity and is a possible follow-on; not needed for the compiled-path GPU story.

tracker: loop-local `let x = Some(shared); vec.push(x)` over-releases the LAST-pushed Vec[Option[shared]] element (use-after-free); fix the loop-body scope-exit RC handling of a named shared binding moved into a Vec
title: AOT MISCOMPILE (use-after-free): a loop containing `let x = Some(shared T); vec.push(x)` frees the LAST pushed element, so reading vec[N-1] after the loop returns garbage / crashes. interp (oracle) is correct; AOT diverges. Regressed since ~e2fb091b (kata #101 was 80745775-correct earlier the same day); live on main 84c01472. RC-elision-independent (fails with KARAC_RC_ELIDE_REF_PARAMS=0 too).
detail: MINIMAL REPRO (interp prints 0, AOT prints 1 deterministically; 8M-rep #101 additionally crashes intermittently — ASLR-dependent UAF): `shared struct TreeNode { val: i64, mut left: Option[TreeNode], mut right: Option[TreeNode] } fn main() { let mut pool: Vec[Option[TreeNode]] = Vec.new(); let mut i = 0i64; while i < 2i64 { let sub = Some(TreeNode { val: 2i64, left: None, right: None }); pool.push(sub); i = i + 1i64; } match pool[1i64] { Some(n) => println(f"val={n.val}"), None => println("x") } }` -> interp val=2, AOT val=3 (garbage; val reads N+1 where N=iteration count; left reads None-sentinel). NARROWING (all interp-vs-AOT): (a) NOT the recursive traversal — a hand-built is_mirror is correct; (b) NOT copy_tree / the struct-literal double-use `left: sub, right: copy_tree(sub)` — correct without a loop (unroll); (c) NOT the Vec realloc moving a real shared element — correct unrolled with pool[0] a real tree; (d) NOT the match/clone read path — single push + direct `match pool[i]` read is correct (sA/sB), and a `let x`+shadow without a loop is correct (sC); (e) a loop that pushes a `Some(...)` LITERAL directly (no `let`) is correct (sD). ONLY the combination LOOP + `let <name> = Some(shared); vec.push(<name>)` fails, and only the LAST-pushed element (vec[0..N-2] are correct — matches pool[0] correct / pool[1] wrong). IR (ir_for_with_ownership on the minimal repro): the loop body is malloc(rc=1) -> push arg rc_inc (rc=2) -> store the {tag,ptr,0,0} fat-Option into the Vec -> loop-body scope-exit rc_dec (rc=1), which LOOKS balanced per iteration (pool owns rc=1); strides are consistent (32-byte 4-word Option element in push-store, index-read, realloc alloc_bytes, and free-buf). @main has 2 malloc, 1 rc_inc site, and 3 rc_dec/__karac_rc_drop_TreeNode sites. The last iteration's node is nonetheless freed before the post-loop read -> UAF. Likely cause: the loop-body RcDecOption for the loop-local `sub` binding is over-applied for the final iteration relative to the push's balancing inc (suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg / track_rc_option_var interaction across the loop back-edge). NOT YET FIXED — root-caused to this minimal repro + IR; the exact spurious dec needs a focused pass. IMPACT: blocks rebenching the RC-elision-flip tree-kata cluster (#100/#101/#104/#110/#111/#112) since #101's bench builds a pool of shared trees this way; and is a silent wrong-answer + memory-safety bug for the common `for/while { let node = Some(shared…); v.push(node) }` shape. VERIFIED live on main 84c01472; setup ruled out (trivial + non-loop shared programs correct, deterministic codegen, clean archives).
investigation: Root cause (pre-opt IR via the KARAC_JIT_RUNNER intercept): the counted single-push loop is rewritten by the SEQ-TABULATE lowering (stab.*, dae4e309) — the push becomes a raw 4-word store of x's Option[shared] into the reserved buffer (a MOVE, no rc-inc), but the binding's per-iteration cleanup (opt_rc_cleanup_*) still rc-decs the payload: alloc rc=1 → move → dec → rc=0 → free at PUSH time, every iteration. The vec holds dangling pointers; the read loop's match-bind inc/dec then double-frees (SIGTRAP). Why the gate missed it: llvm_ty_is_pod checks the LLVM type — Option[shared T] lowers to {i64,i64,i64,i64} (pointer rides as ptrtoint), all scalar words. SIBLING HOLE confirmed: heap-payload enums (Tok.Word(String) → tag+3×i64 words) crash identically, AND the PAR collect gate (81377276) has the same LLVM-level check — #[par_unordered] push of a heap-payload enum crashed on main too (Option[shared] happened to be declined par-side). FIX: both gates (seq tabulate reduce.rs:~3057, par collect reduce.rs:~1566) now additionally consult vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr(elem_te) — the SAME per-element drop oracle the scope-exit cleanup drain uses — and decline any element needing drop work, so the gate and the cleanup can never disagree. Scalars/POD structs/Option[scalar] return None and keep the lowering (LBM seq 5.19B instr + par 50 ms + checkpoints unchanged). Tests: IR decline pins (no stab.run / no worker_collect_tab_) + value-survival E2Es for Vec[Option[shared]] and Vec[heap-enum], seq + par. guardmalloc + leaks clean on all four repros. Follow-on (perf, not correctness): true move-suppression threading would let RC elements tabulate; declined shapes fall back to the plain push path, which is sound.

title: PRE-EXISTING on main (not from the B-2026-07-18-2 fix — reproduced with it stashed): tests/http_client_codegen.rs test_ir_http_error_drop_frees_message + test_ir_response_drop_frees_headers_handle are RED — the synthesized HttpError/Response drop fns no longer free the message String buffer / headers handle (the IR-shape assertion finds only a cap-zero GEP, no free). Likely fallout of the 3324eea exact-free-buf-hints drop-site rework window.
detail: Found during the mandatory full `cargo test --features llvm` sweep for the B-2026-07-18-2 fix; bisected by stashing that fix — still red, so pre-existing. The drop body shows `drop.field0.cap.p` GEPs with no `free` call on the message/handle. If the free moved to a different mechanism (exact free-buf hints), the IR-shape tests may need updating instead — needs the drop-site rework author's call: real leak vs stale test assertion.
investigation: RESOLUTION (fcdf520, stale-test false-alarm — NOT a real leak): the drop DOES free the message/handle; the free just moved MECHANISM. The exact-free-buf-hints drop-site rework (3324eea / 7395055, phase-10 line 282) re-emitted aggregate field buffer releases through `karac_free_buf` (runtime/src/alloc.rs:521 — a genuine free that either parks the buffer in the size-class recycle pool or calls libc `free`) instead of a bare `@free`. The two IR-shape tests asserted only `drop_body.contains("@free(")`, so they went RED when the call became `@karac_free_buf` even though the free is real and still guarded/invoked. fcdf520 updated both assertions to `@free( || @karac_free_buf(` (git log -p tests/http_client_codegen.rs confirms the 07678e8 `@free(`-only → fcdf520 two-form transition). Both tests are GREEN on current main (78816a1): `__karac_drop_struct_Response` frees the headers handle (`karac_runtime_http_response_headers_free`) + the body String buffer, and `__karac_drop_struct_HttpError` frees the message buffer, and `main` invokes both at their match-arm scope exits. No code change needed — verified full http_client_codegen suite 12/12 green.

title: Plain struct-variable REASSIGNMENT (`let mut p = P { x: 1, y: 2 }; p = P { x: 10, y: 20 }`) now emits a reference to `karac_runtime_gpu_free_soa`, so BOTH `karac run` (JIT 'Symbols not found: karac_runtime_gpu_free_soa') and `karac build` (link error demanding the opt-in GPU archive: 'this program calls gpu.dispatch') fail for a 2-line non-GPU program; `--interp` correct (prints 30). A basic language construct is unusable on both compiled backends without the heavyweight GPU archive.
detail: Found by the selfhost codegen oracle (tests/selfhost_codegen.rs) when the Slice-12 struct corpus landed: the Kara-emitted IR printed the CORRECT '30' while the seed's `karac run` printed '' — the oracle's seed leg was the broken one. Suspect window: the 2026-07-18 GPU interleaved-device-layout commit (fcdf5202) — the struct-reassign lowering appears to unconditionally route old-value cleanup through the gpu SoA free helper, which flips the gpu-archive auto-selection heuristic (karac_runtime_gpu_* reference => GPU program). Repro: the 2-line program above; karac run => JIT symbol error, karac build => gpu-archive link demand, karac run --interp => 30. SOURCE NOTE: surfaced landing the Phase-12 Slice-12 struct corpus (unblocked by the B-2026-07-18-2 fix); the corpus entry is swapped for a non-reassign shape until this is fixed.
investigation: FIXED (13f9c2a). Root cause: `gpu_buffer_type()` is an ANONYMOUS `{i64, i64}` (src/codegen/method_call.rs), structurally identical to any 2-field all-`i64` user struct (`struct P { x: i64, y: i64 }` -> `{i64, i64}`). The struct-var reassign arm (src/codegen/stmts.rs ~5357) keyed the gpu-buffer double-buffer path on `vs.ty == gpu_buffer_type()`, so a plain `p = P { .. }` matched and called `compile_gpu_buffer_assign`, whose old-value cleanup emits `karac_runtime_gpu_free_soa`. That symbol reference flips the gpu-archive auto-selection heuristic, so `karac run` fails at JIT materialization and `karac build` demands the opt-in `libkarac_runtime_gpu.a`. Confirmed the collision is width-specific: a 3-field i64 struct (`{i64,i64,i64}`) reassign was already clean. Regression from fcdf5202 (the GPU-SLIP-4h interleaved-device-layout commit added the `gpu.download`-into-plain-Vec path; the reassign type-equality check pre-existed but the gpu buffer type became reachable/relevant on this shape). FIX: a dedicated `gpu_buffer_vars: HashSet<String>` (src/codegen.rs), inserted at the SOLE gpu-buffer binding site `compile_let_from_gpu_upload` (handles `let b = gpu.upload(..)` and resident `gpu.dispatch`) and cleared per-function; the reassign arm now gates on `gpu_buffer_vars.contains(name) && vs.ty == gpu_buffer_type()`. A plain struct is not in the set -> normal struct-reassign path, no GPU symbol; a real GpuBuffer `grid = gpu.dispatch(..)` double-buffer move still routes correctly (gpu IR pin test_ir_gpu_upload_unlayouted_defaults_to_single_interleaved_group green). Verified interp == JIT == AOT (30) with no gpu-archive demand, valgrind-clean (plain reassign + 200x String-field-struct reassign loop), codegen 2447 / memory_sanitizer 773 / selfhost_codegen / non-codegen all 0-failed. Pin: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_two_field_i64_struct_reassign_not_gpu_buffer. NOTE: the selfhost Slice-12 corpus entry that was swapped for a non-reassign shape can now be restored to a struct-reassign form (left to the corpus owner).

source: kata: SWEEP_TRACKER_v2 equal-safety triage (2026-06-15) — the kara/ovf >= 1.15 cluster is dominated by String concat/char-append katas (#30 1.56x, #5 1.33x, #10 1.22x, #17/#405/#415 1.19x, #7 1.16x vs rust -C overflow-checks=on)
tracker: docs/implementation_checklist/phase-10-targets.md § 'String char-append residual'; data: Kara/SWEEP_TRACKER_v2.md § Equal-safety investigation + Kara/ovf_equal_safety_triage.tsv
title: String built one byte at a time (`out.push_str(s[d..d+1])` in a loop) emits ~1.15-1.56x more instructions than equal-safety Rust: push_str's per-append copy is a branchy variable-length inlined memcpy because the slice length reaches codegen as a RUNTIME value; Rust's &s[d..d+1] carries the const-foldable length 1 and collapses to a single store.
investigation: Root-caused 2026-06-15 (instruction-count method, load-immune): push_str already does amortized power-of-two growth (min-cap 8) — NOT O(n^2) realloc; the gap is purely the per-append copy, build_memcpy(dest, 1, src_ptr, 1, src_len) in src/codegen/vec_method.rs (~line 2245) with src_len runtime. LLVM inlines it as a branchy variable-length copy, not a sized store, because it cannot prove len==1. FIX HYPOTHESIS (ready, not yet built): const-fold fixed-width slice lengths (x..x+k -> const k) so the copy collapses to a k-byte store, and/or a small-constant-length push_str fast path. Correctness-sensitive — push_str is the whole string surface: needs a focused slice with memory_sanitizer ASAN + Linux LSan + codegen E2Es, and the fixed-width const-fold must not weaken bounds-check semantics. Re-measure vs rust -C overflow-checks=on (equal-safety), instruction-count not wall-time. Filed retroactively 2026-07-18 — the finding lived only in session memory + the workspace sweep tracker, violating the every-surfaced-gap-gets-a-tracker-row rule (its sibling, the #1665 low-cardinality sort gap, was correctly filed as B-2026-06-13-2). RESOLUTION (07678e8, already-fixed-before-filing): the fix hypothesis had ALREADY landed on 2026-07-13 (5 days before this retroactive 2026-07-18 filing from the stale sweep-tracker note). `const_slice_width` (src/codegen/collections.rs:4523) folds a fixed-width borrowed String slice — both integer-literal bounds (`s[1..3]`) and the `s[a..a+k]` structural-equality shape (the char-append `s[d..d+1]`, k=1, handling both the pre-lowered `Binary{Add}` and post-lowering `Call(Path([ty,"add"]))` forms) — to a compile-time i64 constant, which is emitted as the borrowed view's `len` field (collections.rs:2794) instead of the runtime `bs.view.len = sub end, start`. That constant flows into `push_str`'s memcpy length (vec_method.rs:3551). VERIFIED end to end: (1) IR test tests/codegen.rs::ir_fixed_width_string_slice_folds_view_len asserts the fold fires for `s[d..d+1]`/`s[1..3]` (no `bs.view.len`) and that a genuine runtime width `s[a..b]` keeps the subtraction; (2) ASSEMBLY-level, on a non-constant-foldable runtime-string workload (`fs.read_to_string` → `while d<n { out.push_str(s[d..d+1]) }`), the 1-byte append lowers to `movzbl (%src),%eax` + `mov %al,(%dst,%len,1)` — a single inline byte load+store, NOT a `memcpy@plt` call (the only in-loop memcpy@plt is the cold realloc/grow buffer copy) — i.e. the sized store the fix targeted, matching Rust's const-length `&s[d..d+1]`; (3) interp==JIT==AOT parity (26) on that workload. No code change needed. The residual is closed for the fixed-width char-append shape; a genuinely runtime-width slice keeps the exact copy by design (correct, just not constant-collapsible — same as Rust).

title: An ASSOCIATED-fn call (`Type.method(...)`) passing a FRESH-TEMP value to a `ref`/`mut ref` param passed the temp BY VALUE instead of spilling it to a slot and passing a pointer. Only an Identifier arg took the `get_data_ptr` pointer path; any rvalue (`Tensor.from([...])`, an f-string, an arithmetic result) fell through to `compile_expr` and was pushed as the aggregate value. For String/Vec this is an LLVM verifier error (`{ptr,i64,i64}` vs the callee's `ptr`) that aborts `karac build`; for Tensor (whose value is already a `ptr`) it type-checks but the callee dereferences the block's rank word as a pointer -> deterministic SIGSEGV (load from ~0x1). The free-fn call path (`compile_call`) already materialized rvalue ref args; only the `compile_assoc_call` general user-fn path was missing it. Surfaced by the autograd `TensorVar.leaf(t, Tensor.from([...]))` shape (a named-binding leaf worked; the idiomatic inline temp crashed). A latent, heap-layout-dependent crash — the tensor-autograd ASAN corpus used named bindings, so it never exercised this.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found building the std.autograd activations/losses (task 21). Class miscompile (fresh-temp ref arg ABI). The materialized fresh-owned TENSOR temp additionally needed a `FreeTensor` (track_tensor_var on the slot) — the tensor sibling of the `track_vec_var` materialize already makes for a Vec/String temp — else the block leaked once per call. RESIDUAL: the FREE-FN arg path (`f(Tensor.from(...))` where f is a free fn) shares the same value-corruption via B-2026-07-18-10's element-width bug but its arg loop is more complex; extend the same `fn_param_tensor_info` threading + FreeTensor tracking there when a workload needs free-fn tensor ref args.
investigation: Repro: `shared struct S{mut vals:Vec[Tensor[f32,[?]]]} impl S{fn ins(t:S,value:ref Tensor[f32,[?]]){let v=value+0.0; t.vals.push(v)}} fn main(){let s=S.new(); S.ins(s, Tensor.from([-1.0,2.0])); ...}` SIGSEGVs under karac build (named `let x=Tensor.from(..); S.ins(s,x)` is clean). String sibling: an assoc fn `P.ins(ref String)` called with `f"..."` fails LLVM verification. FIX: `compile_assoc_call`'s general user-fn arg loop now mirrors the free-fn ref-arg path — Identifier place -> get_data_ptr; `vec[idx]` -> ref_arg_index_borrow_ptr; else materialize_rvalue_for_ref_arg (spill to a slot, pass the ptr). A materialized fresh-owned tensor temp (expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp) registers FreeTensor on the slot. Verified: min tensor repro + String repro run correct; asan_freshtemp_tensor_ref_arg_assoc_call_no_crash + full autograd both-backend suites green; codegen 2446 + memory_sanitizer clean. UPDATE (a117d7e): the free-fn arg path is now fixed too — the rvalue-ref site in `compile_call` threads the callee's declared tensor element type (via `fn_param_tensor_info`) around the arg compile and registers the materialized fresh-owned tensor temp's `FreeTensor`, mirroring the assoc-call path. `f(Tensor.from([...]))` into a `ref Tensor[f32,…]` free-fn param now reads correct + is leak-clean. No residual.

title: `Tensor.{from,zeros,ones,full}` in an ARGUMENT position laid its data out at the literal's DEFAULT element width (f64 for `-1.0`, i64 for `1`) rather than the callee's DECLARED tensor element type. `compile_tensor_from`/`_new` take the element type from `pending_let_tensor_info` (set only by a `let: Tensor[T,S]` annotation) or fall back to the leaf literal width; in an arg position nothing set it, so `f(Tensor.from([-1.0, 2.0]))` into a `ref Tensor[f32,…]` param stored 8-byte doubles that every 4-byte f32 reader (`t[i]`, `t.sum()`, the tape) misread — silently wrong output under karac build (interp is untyped f64, so it never diverged). The typechecker accepts the call (coerces to f32), so it is purely a codegen element-width bug.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: the value-corruption half of the autograd inline-temp bug (crash half is B-2026-07-18-9). Explicit-suffix literals (`Tensor.from([-1.0f32, 2.0f32])`) already worked (leaf width = f32). Class miscompile (element-width). RESIDUAL: the free-fn arg path (`f(Tensor.from(...))`) is not yet threaded (its arg loop is more complex); assoc-fn/method calls — which autograd and idiomatic `Type.method(Type.from(...))` use — are fixed.
investigation: IR of `f(Tensor.from([-1.0,2.0]))` (f32 param) showed `store double -1.0` into a malloc(32) block (rank+dim+2xf64) — an f64 layout for an f32 tensor. FIX: a new `fn_param_tensor_info` table (function name -> per-param Option<TensorVarInfo>, built in declare_function/mono alongside fn_param_ref, peeling one ref) lets the assoc-call arg loop set `pending_let_tensor_info` from the callee's DECLARED tensor param type around compile_expr(arg) — the argument-position sibling of the let-annotation threading. Verified byte-identical interp==AOT (to f32 precision) on all five new autograd ops + a P.second(Tensor.from([-1,2,3]))->2.0 E2E; codegen 2446 + memory_sanitizer green. UPDATE (a117d7e): the free-fn arg path is now fixed too — the rvalue-ref site in `compile_call` threads the callee's declared tensor element type (via `fn_param_tensor_info`) around the arg compile and registers the materialized fresh-owned tensor temp's `FreeTensor`, mirroring the assoc-call path. `f(Tensor.from([...]))` into a `ref Tensor[f32,…]` free-fn param now reads correct + is leak-clean. No residual.

title: `OnceLock[T].get().unwrap_or(<value>)` fails the LLVM verifier and `.unwrap()` faults at run time (interp fine) — the `Option[ref T]` payload from a borrow-returning `get()` was reconstructed as a POINTER, not the value. `OnceLock[T].get() -> Option[ref T]`, and the typechecker records the unwrap-inner type as `ref i64` (whereas the sibling `Vec.get`/`Map.get`, also `-> Option[ref T]`, record `i64`). Left as `ref T`, `rebuild_value_from_payload_words` lowers it to a `ptr` and `inttoptr`s the payload WORD (which physically holds the VALUE, not a pointer): `unwrap_or(0)` builds a merge phi mixing `ptr` (present) with the i64 default → `error: codegen failed: Module verification failed: "PHI node operands are not the same type as the result! %uo.val = phi ptr [ %or.pl.itop, %uo.present ], [ 0, %uo.absent ]"`, and `unwrap()` silently builds a bogus pointer (`inttoptr 7`) that segfaults when the consumer derefs it. The interpreter auto-derefs and returns the value, so both are silent-in-interp memory/verifier divergences.
investigation: 2026-07-18 (found while surveying phase-8 next steps, via the Arena/Interner handle-codegen work): `let c: OnceLock[i64] = OnceLock.new(); c.set(7); println(c.get().unwrap_or(0))` — interp prints 7, `karac build` fails the LLVM module verifier on the unwrap_or phi. Bisect of consumption shapes: `c.get()` alone, `.is_some()`, and `match c.get() {..}` all OK; `.unwrap()` COMPILES but was runtime-unsafe (bogus ptr); only `.unwrap_or` hit the verifier (it is the one that phis the payload against a user default of a different type). Root cause in `once.rs::compile_once_get`: the runtime `karac_runtime_once_get` returns a borrow into the sealed value, and codegen LOADS `T` through it and packs the VALUE into the Option payload words — so `Option[ref T]` is physically identical to `Option[T]`. But `try_compile_option_result_method` (src/codegen/calls.rs) read `inner_te = ref i64` from `method_unwrap_inner_types` and lowered THAT (a ptr), reinterpreting the value word as an address. Confirmed the established `Option[ref T]` path is fine: `Vec.get(i).unwrap()`/`.unwrap_or(99)` return correct values (their recorded inner type is the deref'd `i64`), so the defect is the OnceLock-vs-Vec inconsistency in what the typechecker records, not the payload packing. FIX (162a13f): strip the outer `Ref`/`MutRef` from `inner_te` at the top of `try_compile_option_result_method`, normalizing OnceLock.get to the Vec.get path — reconstruction is then over the value type for every Option/Result method (unwrap/unwrap_or/expect/map/…), matching both the physical payload and the interpreter's auto-deref. No-op for the already-value Vec/Map path; a wide `T` is still deboxed by the `word_count > area` branch. Verified interp == JIT == AOT on OnceLock[i64] unwrap/unwrap_or (set + unset cell → default), OnceLock[String], Map[String,i64], Map[i64,String]. Full --features llvm codegen (2449) + interpreter suites green. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_oncelock_get_unwrap_and_unwrap_or.
resolution: Fixed by stripping the outer borrow from the recorded Option/Result inner type in codegen, so a borrow-returning get()'s value-packed payload reconstructs as the value rather than a pointer.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: pre-existing gap in the shipped OnceLock codegen (2026-07, Phase 8), surfaced while scoping the next phase-8 item after the Arena slice. — CLASS NOTE: run-vs-build divergence with two faces — a hard compile-time LLVM verifier reject on unwrap_or, and a silent runtime segfault on unwrap (bogus inttoptr deref); interp correct in both. — SURFACE NOTE: typechecker records `ref T` vs `T` inconsistently across Option[ref T] producers (OnceLock.get vs Vec.get) into method_unwrap_inner_types; the fix is a codegen-side normalization in try_compile_option_result_method (src/codegen/calls.rs).

title: `Stats.*` on a `Slice[T]` value (`Stats.mean(v.as_slice())`, the declared `ref Slice[f64]` param's canonical form) read ZERO elements in the tree-walk interpreter — the arg extraction in eval_call.rs matched only `Value::Array`, so a `Value::Slice` fell to the empty fallback: `sum`/`prod` returned a spurious 0/-0 and `mean`/`variance`/`stddev`/`median`/`percentile` panicked 'called on empty slice'. Codegen read the slice correctly, so `karac run` diverged from `karac build` (the interp saw an empty dataset, the build the real one). A Vec/Array arg worked (it IS a Value::Array); only a genuine Slice value (from `.as_slice()` / range-index / a `ref Slice` forward) was misread.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found probing std.stats run-vs-build parity (the `[~]` stats checklist item — functionally the free-fn surface is complete on both backends for Vec/Array f64+i64; the `[~]` reflects the trait-dispatched Reduce/ElementwiseMap architecture + Column integration, not a functional gap). Class run-vs-build. Empty-input behavior was already consistent across backends (sum -0/0, min/max None, mean/median trap with the same message) — only the non-empty Slice extraction diverged.
investigation: Repro: `let v: Vec[f64]=vec![3.0,1.0,4.0]; let sl: Slice[f64]=v.as_slice(); Stats.sum(sl)` → interp -0 / `Stats.mean(sl)` → interp panic, both correct under karac build (8 / 2.6667). `sl.len()`=3 in interp, so the slice value itself was fine — only the Stats arg extraction ignored it. FIX: add a `Value::Slice { storage, start, len, .. } => storage.read()[start..start+len].to_vec()` arm to the Stats element extraction in eval_call.rs (the int/float static-mode detection already handled Type::Slice, only the value extraction missed it). Verified interp==build on sum/mean over a slice; empty-input parity unchanged. Tests: interpreter test_stats_on_slice + codegen E2E test_e2e_stats_on_slice.

source: kata: re-measuring the B-2026-07-18-8 cluster with today's compiler (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 instruction counts vs rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on, outputs identical)
fix: 90fe2ad (attributed, not bisected — B-2026-07-18-15, the String push/push_str accumulator mis-lowered through the Vec TABULATE path; same window + same shape as this kata's string-building loops)
tracker: docs/implementation_checklist/phase-10-targets.md § 'String char-append residual' (same entry — this row carries the #415 outlier)
title: [RESOLVED — re-measured at parity] Kata #415 add_strings recorded 13.4x equal-safety Rust at filing (89.1B vs 6.25B instrs). Re-measured on today's main (after ~100 intervening commits) at the EXACT config (total=500000, KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0, rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on): kara ~1.06x Rust — the 13.4x is NOT reproducible on any lane. Almost certainly a compiler regression that was fixed in the window, not a stale/wrong-lane measurement.
investigation: Found 2026-07-18 during the B-2026-07-18-8 kata sweep; investigation was stood down mid-attribution for agent-ownership reasons, so this row records the leads: (1) shapes the char-append fix does NOT cover dominate the kata: `out.push_str(add_strings(a, b))` — a fresh-String-TEMP argument (the chained-temp machinery), `a.bytes()` per call (two 38-byte Vec allocs), and a ~19.5 MB accumulator; (2) `sample` shows the process cvwait-heavy EVEN under KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 — parked-pool idle threads pollute flat samples (use the main-thread call graph or user-CPU, not top-of-stack counts), but pool machinery being active at all in the seq lane is itself worth confirming; (3) discriminate first: git-bisect the instr count on main (the deterministic method) between the June triage window and today, OR attribute directly via the main-thread call graph at 500K iterations. Related context from the same sweep: #405 is already ~parity (1.04x), #5/#7/#17 are unmoved by char-append changes (different hot patterns), #30's 1.52x is not char-append either — the old 'string cluster' attribution was too coarse; each kata needs its own attribution before any fix. RESOLUTION 2026-07-18 (re-measured on main @ the karac-tools clone with today's compiler, both operands byte-identical output 1000513006): the ACTUAL bench kata (karalang/kara-katas leetcode/401-500/415-add-strings/bench/add_strings.kara) now runs at PARITY with its Rust peer, NOT 13.4x. INSTRUCTION COUNT (callgrind, total=20000, KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0): kara 188,940,285 vs Rust (rustc -O -C overflow-checks=on) 178,318,651 = 1.06x. USER TIME (total=500000): kara 0.553s seq / 0.599s auto-par vs Rust 0.520s = 1.06-1.15x; BOTH the seq (KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0) and default (auto-par) lanes are parity-class — the ledger lead #2 worry (pool active in the seq lane inflating cost) does NOT manifest (user≈wall, no idle-thread drag). callgrind attribution of the current kara binary: main 37%, `karac_string_slice_borrow` 17.9% (the `digits[d..d+1]` digit-table render is a genuine ZERO-COPY borrowed slice — bounds + UTF-8-boundary check, no malloc; the per-call cost vs Rust's raw `D[d] as char` is the checked-str-slice-vs-raw-byte idiom difference, not a codegen defect), ~40% malloc/realloc/free (the growing per-call result String + the ~19.5 MB accumulator, matched by Rust's own ~40% malloc share — Rust callgrind: _int_malloc 13.5% + realloc 7.5% + finish_grow 6.9% + _int_free 6.9% + _int_realloc 6.6% ≈ same profile). PROBABLE CAUSE OF THE FIX (attributed, NOT git-bisected — a full bisect would need ~7 old-compiler builds; deferred as the bug is already gone): B-2026-07-18-15 / 90fe2ad — 'String accumulator built by push(char)/push_str in a counted loop was mis-lowered through the Vec TABULATE reduction path' (a silent heap-overflow correctness bug whose mis-lowering ALSO routed the string build through the bulk/tabulate machinery). The kata's digits_of / add_strings / render all build Strings via push/push_str counted loops — exactly that shape — so pre-fix they took the pathological tabulate path (≈89B instrs) and post-fix use the correct sequential String-push lowering (≈4.7B extrapolated), landing at Rust parity; kara improved ~19x while Rust was unchanged, consistent with a kara-side regression being fixed. RECOMMENDATION: close as resolved-by-fix; if exact-commit attribution is wanted, bisect the total=20000 callgrind Ir on main across the B-2026-07-18-8-filing..HEAD window (deterministic; the fix commit will show the ~19x step-down).

title: Interpolating a WHOLE TUPLE value in an f-string / `println` (`f"{t}"` where `t: (i64, i64)` or `(i64, String)`) passes `karac check` and renders `(3, 7)` in the interpreter, but FAILS `karac build`/JIT with 'Display of a struct in an f-string is supported when the interpolated expression is a variable or field access … bind a struct literal or call result to a `let` first'. The hint is MISLEADING — `t` is already a let-bound variable, so following it does not help. Affects ALL tuple element types (scalar and heap). Field-index interpolation (`f"{t.0} {t.1}"`) works on every backend.
investigation: `f"{t}"` on a tuple-typed variable reaches codegen's f-string Display path, which handles user structs (via a variable / field-access receiver) but has NO arm for an anonymous tuple aggregate, so it emits the generic 'bind to a let first (user-struct Display, subtask-5 follow-on)' error even though the operand is ALREADY a variable. The interpreter renders a tuple as `(a, b)` (comma-joined, parenthesized), so this is a check/execution consistency hole (the AI-first wedge: check-clean must run) — loud, not a silent miscompile, and a clean workaround exists (interpolate the fields: `f"{t.0}"`). Surfaced while probing the freshly-landed SortedMap ordered methods (4d6efad) — those methods return `Option[(K,V)]` and are otherwise CORRECT in codegen (verified: min/max/floor/ceiling extracting `kv.0` run identically on interp/JIT/native). Two fixes: (A) implement tuple Display in codegen's f-string path (render each element via its own Display, comma-joined + parens, matching the interpreter's format); OR (B) at minimum FIX THE DIAGNOSTIC to say 'a whole tuple cannot be interpolated in an f-string yet; interpolate its fields (e.g. `f"{t.0} {t.1}"`)' instead of the wrong 'bind to a let first' advice. (A) is the real fix; (B) is a cheap diagnostic-honesty improvement. Repro: tf1 (`(i64,String)`), tf2 (`(i64,i64)`), tf3 (field-index workaround, works). RESOLUTION (f882072): implemented fix (A) — codegen now renders a whole tuple value in f-string / println via the element-wise emit_tuple_display_fn ((a, b) format, matching the interpreter). Forwarded the tuple TypeExpr by span (display_tuple_types, mirroring display_option_result_types); added try_compile_tuple_display (variable slot / spilled call-result) and routed the f-string part path + compile_print through it before their struct-value error arms. Handles scalar / String-element / mixed / nested / call-result tuples. Test: codegen e2e_whole_tuple_display_matches_interpreter.
detail: LEDGER-LINT FIXUP (canonicalized enums, values preserved): original surface='codegen (f-string / Display of a whole tuple value) vs interpreter'; class='check-passes/codegen-defers consistency hole + misleading diagnostic'; source='dogfizz probe (SortedMap ordered methods min/max/floor/ceiling return Option[(K,V)]; interpolating the returned pair in an f-string). Minimized to `let t: (i64, i64) = (3, 7); println(f"{t}")`.'.

title: String accumulator built by `push(char)` in a counted loop was mis-lowered through the Vec TABULATE reduction path, overrunning the byte buffer by 3 bytes/char (silent heap-buffer-overflow). A `String` shares `Vec`'s `{ptr,len,cap}` LLVM layout, so `llvm_ty_is_vec_struct` cannot distinguish them. The sequential tabulate lowering (try_emit_seq_tabulate_lowering) and the Collect lowering (try_emit_collect_reduction_lowering) both matched a `let mut s = ""; while i<n { s.push(c); i=i+1 }` shape as a Vec build: they reserved `trip_count * elem_size` bytes (elem_size = 1 for the String byte buffer) and did fixed-stride ELEMENT stores. But `String.push(char)` writes a variable-width (up to 4-byte) UTF-8 char slot, so the store wrote a 4-byte char into a 1-byte stride and overran the reserved buffer by 3 bytes per char. Valgrind: `Invalid write of size 4 ... N bytes inside a block of size N` (a 7-char build overruns a 7-byte block at offsets 4,5,6). The overflow is SILENT under a plain run — it corrupts adjacent heap but often leaves the observable output correct (e.g. the #14 bench still printed the right sink), so only ASAN/valgrind surfaces it. NOT introduced by any recent change (revert-tested against the pre-B-16/B-18-1 tree — still overran).
investigation: 2026-07-18: surfaced while re-verifying the reduce-shaped kata corpus after B-2026-07-18-1 (the AUTO_PAR=0 reduce gate). Kata #14 (longest-common-prefix) bench/vertical.kara builds a `Vec[Vec[String]]` via a `make_string` helper that `push`es chars in a `while` loop, then reduces prefix lengths — it crashed/corrupted under `karac build` while `karac run` (tree-walk) was clean, so the kata README had marked vertical scanning `✓ via karac run` only. MINIMIZED to `fn build(n){ let mut out=""; let mut i=0; while i<n { out.push('x'); i=i+1 } return out }` — valgrind shows `Invalid write of size 4` inside the 7-byte block, i.e. the tabulate reserved 7 bytes (trip_count*1) and stored 4-byte char slots at fixed stride. RULED OUT (revert-tested, each with a full rebuild): the malloc-family attrs (56c65d8), my own B-16 RC-predicate fix, and B-18-1 — the overflow persisted with all three reverted, so it is a long-standing latent codegen bug, not a regression. ROOT CAUSE: both Vec reduction lowerings key off `llvm_ty_is_vec_struct(acc_slot.ty)`, which is TRUE for a String (same {ptr,len,cap} layout), so a String accumulator entered the fixed-stride Vec element-store path. FIX: bail a String accumulator out of BOTH lowerings — `if self.string_vars.contains(&acc) { return Ok(None) }` in try_emit_seq_tabulate_lowering and try_emit_collect_reduction_lowering — falling back to the correct per-push String codegen (which grows the buffer for the char encoding). self.string_vars is the existing String-typed-var tracking set. After: the minimized repro and #14 (solver + bench, default AND AUTO_PAR=0) are valgrind/ASAN-clean and byte-identical to the interpreter; a load-bearing regression test asan_string_push_counted_loop_no_overflow (tests/memory_sanitizer.rs) trips the overflow under ASAN with the guards removed and is clean with them.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #14 kata bench (kara-katas), longest-common-prefix vertical scan; the make_string char-push loop. Class miscompile (silent heap-buffer-overflow from a wrong element stride — same family as the SoA index-store overflows B-2026-06-20-7). Verified: codegen E2E (2441) + par_codegen (190) + memory_sanitizer ASAN (771, incl. the new guard) all green; new regression test negative-controlled (guards removed => ASAN overflow, valgrind Invalid write of size 4). The fix touches only reduce.rs; no runtime/ABI change, no archive rebuild, no interpreter change (interpreter was already correct).

title: `<int>.parse(s)` / `<int>.from_str_radix(s, r)` / `f64.parse(s)` never freed their fresh-owned String ARGUMENT — a fresh-temp arg (`i64.parse("42".to_string())`) leaks once per call, unbounded in a loop. The numeric parse assoc-fns were the only arg-consuming string surface missing the `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` cleanup the String methods (contains/replace/split/find) already do.
investigation: The int/f64 `parse` and `from_str_radix` codegen (src/codegen/assoc_call.rs) compiled the String arg to `(data, len)` for the runtime externs `karac_runtime_parse_i64` / `_parse_i64_radix` / `_parse_f64` (all read-only — they only read the bytes and write an out-slot / return a success flag) but discarded the arg struct value without freeing, so a fresh-temp arg (cap>0, no other owner) leaked. Confirmed by probe pv2 (`i64.parse("42".to_string())` ×100 → definitely-lost accumulates) and the realistic pv (`i64.parse(p.to_string())` over split elements). Fixed by calling `free_fresh_owned_str_arg(&_args[0].value, s_val)` after each of the three parse externs reads the string. `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` self-gates on `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp` (or fresh string-slice) + `free_str_vec_buffer_if_heap`'s cap>0 guard, so a borrowed identifier / rodata literal arg is never freed, and a MOVED owned-binding arg (`let s = ...; i64.parse(s)`) is reclaimed by the existing arg-move machinery — verified leak-free + no double-free across fresh-temp (pv2/fp/rx), moved-binding (pid), and literal (plit) args on interp/JIT/native + valgrind. Regression: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_int_float_parse_fresh_temp_arg_no_leak (int parse + radix parse + moved binding, 200× → 60800, LSan-clean). Same fresh-owned-temp-consumed-by-a-copying-runtime-call class as B-2026-07-16-24 (String.replace args).
detail: LEDGER-LINT FIXUP (canonicalized enums, values preserved): original surface='codegen (`<int>.parse` / `<int>.from_str_radix` / `f64.parse` assoc-fns in src/codegen/assoc_call.rs — missing arg cleanup)'; source='dogfizz probe (numeric parse in a loop) + valgrind — `i64.parse("42".to_string())` / `i64.parse(x.split(...)-element)` leaks the fresh-temp String arg (5 bytes/4 blocks per call). Sibling String methods (contains/replace/split) with fresh-temp args are clean.'.

title: [FIXED] The typechecker did NOT propagate a method parameter's / struct field's (substituted) expected type into the ARGUMENT's own inference, so a type-inferred constructor argument (`Vec.new()`, `Map.new()`, `"".to_string()`) whose element/type param must come from the callee's signature / a generic struct's type param was left as `?T` and rejected. Map/SortedMap builtin leg fixed in 872f9a5 (`check_map_slot_arg`); the general leg — a generic STRUCT LITERAL field seeded from a `let` annotation (`let b: Box[Vec[i64]] = Box { value: Vec.new() }`, which also unblocks the downstream `Box[Vec[i64]].replace(Vec.new())` user-generic-method call) — fixed in 60b7150 (`check_expr` threads the expected type's args into `infer_struct_literal_expected`, seeding each struct type param and pushing the substituted concrete field slot into the value's check). Free-function call args always inferred fine (expected-type-directed).
investigation: Isolated (first-session probes inf1-inf7): a free-fn arg `fn sink(v: Vec[i64]) …; sink(Vec.new())` infers Vec[i64] (PASS); `let v: Vec[i64] = Vec.new()` infers (PASS); inferring from a later `.push` (PASS). But `m.get_or(k, Vec.new())` where the Map value type is `Vec[i64]` FAILS (`Vec<?T0>`), and a USER generic method `impl[T] Box[T] { fn replace(mut ref self, new: T) }` with `b.replace(Vec.new())` where `T = Vec[i64]` FAILS identically (`Box<Vec<?T1>>`) — so it is NOT builtin-specific, it is the general method-call-argument expected-type channel. A concrete (non-generic) default works (`Map[String,i64].get_or(k, 0)` PASS), because `0` needs no propagated type. ROOT DIRECTION: the method-call argument checker (src/typechecker/expr_method_call.rs) infers each argument WITHOUT first substituting the callee's generic params from the (resolved) receiver instantiation and passing the substituted param type down as the argument's expected type — the free-function path already does this (which is why `sink(Vec.new())` works). FIX-PATH: at the method-call arg-checking site, substitute the receiver's concrete generic args into each declared parameter type and thread it as the expected type into `infer_expr(arg)` (the same expected-type-directed inference free-fn calls use), so `Vec.new()` unifies its element with the map value type. DELICATE: expr_method_call.rs is large (~370KB) with heavy special-casing; the fix must not regress overload/into/generic-method resolution — full typechecker suite + example sweep must stay green. Safe rejection (no runtime impact) so low severity, but a real AI-first-wedge ergonomics gap (a very common idiom needs a manual annotation). UPDATE 2026-07-18 (872f9a5): FIXED the Map/SortedMap builtin leg — `check_map_slot_arg` (src/typechecker/stdlib_map.rs, the shared key/value arg checker used by insert/get/get_or/…) now pushes the slot as the EXPECTED type via `check_expr` when the slot is CONCRETE (resolved), so a constructor default resolves its element against it; guarded to a resolved slot (a bare `Type::TypeVar`/`Type::TypeParam` slot — `Map.new()` with no annotation, or a generic-body `V` — stays on plain `infer_expr` + `unify_types` back-propagation, preserving the K/V-from-insert/get pinning). Regression: tests/typechecker.rs::test_map_constructor_default_arg_infers_from_concrete_value_slot (both the concrete-slot forward fix AND the unresolved-slot back-inference guard). Suites green: typechecker 2067, codegen 2460, full non-codegen (only the pre-existing unrelated `book_snippets` import failure). STILL OPEN — the general USER-GENERIC-METHOD arg leg: `impl[T] Box[T] { fn replace(mut ref self, new: T) }` called `b.replace(Vec.new())` where `T = Vec[i64]` still errors `expected 'Box<Vec<i64>>', found 'Box<Vec<?T1>>'`. It reaches a DIFFERENT dispatch arm than the trait-assoc (dispatch_trait_assoc_fn ~620), the ambient-resource (~1068), and the impl-candidate (~1466) arms — a `mut ref self` instance-method path where the receiver's own type gets entangled with the arg's fresh element var. That arm needs the same `check_expr`-with-substituted-param treatment, but the `mut ref self` receiver-type interaction makes it delicate (the naive check_expr at the ~1466 candidate arm did NOT fix it and had no test coverage, so it was reverted). Deferred as the narrower residual; the common Map idiom — the ledger headline — is resolved.
detail: LEDGER-LINT FIXUP (canonicalized enums, values preserved): original surface='typechecker (expected-type propagation into METHOD-CALL arguments — `expr_method_call.rs`)'; class='missing-feature (bidirectional inference gap; safe rejection of valid code, not a miscompile)'; source="dogfizz probe (nested-collection idioms) — `m.get_or(key, Vec.new())` on a `Map[K, Vec[E]]` and `Box[Vec[E]].replace(Vec.new())` are rejected at check with `expected 'Vec<E>', found 'Vec<?T>'` (uninferred element). The equivalent FREE-FUNCTION call (`fn sink(v: Vec[i64]); sink(Vec.new())`) infers fine.". GENERAL LEG FIXED 60b7150 (2026-07-18): the residual was NOT the method-arg inference (that already worked once the receiver is concrete — `mk()->Box[Vec[i64]]` then `.replace(Vec.new())` passes) but the RECEIVER CONSTRUCTION: `infer_struct_literal` instantiated each generic struct type param as a fresh metavar and had no access to the `let` annotation's type args, so `Box { value: Vec.new() }` typed as `Box[Vec[?T]]`. Non-generic struct fields already push down (`Holder { items: Vec.new() }` works) because their field type is concrete; the generic analog needed the expected type-args seeded. Fix: new `check_expr` StructLiteral arm (plain generic struct, matching name+arity, no spread, not shared/par) -> `infer_struct_literal_expected(Some(args))` seeds param_subs from the expected args and pushes the substituted CONCRETE field slot into `check_expr` (unresolved metavar slots keep pushing the raw declared type + post-check unify, so the unseeded solve-from-values path is unchanged). Verified: typechecker 2067 + interpreter + codegen 2463 suites green, clippy/fmt clean, gm1-gm6 repros correct on interp/JIT/native + valgrind-clean, new regression test_generic_struct_literal_field_infers_from_annotation.

title: The whole `std.encoding` surface (`Base64`/`Hex` encode/decode, `Url` encode/decode) SILENTLY MISCOMPILED under `karac build` / `karac run` (JIT). The `#[compiler_builtin]` bodies are stubs implemented only in the interpreter; codegen had NO intercept, so `Base64.encode(bytes)` etc. fell through `compile_assoc_call`'s `Ok(const 0)` tail and returned the INTEGER 0 for a `String` function — silent wrong output (`Base64.encode([72,105])` printed 0 under build, `SGk=` under interp). SECONDARY (interp): `Base64.encode`/`Hex.encode` on a `Slice[u8]` value (`v.as_slice()`, the declared `Slice[u8]` param form) read ZERO bytes — the byte extraction matched only `Value::Array`, so a `Value::Slice` produced '' (the Stats-on-slice bug class, B-2026-07-18-12).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found by a run-vs-build parity sweep of the stdlib after the Stats-on-slice fix. Class run-vs-build (silent 0 miscompile is the severe half). FIX SCOPE: codegen now REJECTS Base64/Hex/Url encode/decode with an actionable 'interpreter-only in v1 (tracked B-2026-07-18-20), run under karac run' message instead of silently emitting 0 — turning a silent wrong-output into a loud, correct signal. The interpreter Slice-arg extraction is also fixed (views storage[start..start+len]). FOLLOW-UP (open): implement the encode/decode functions in codegen (runtime `karac_base64_encode`/`karac_hex_encode` + dispatch, or inline) so encoding works under `karac build`, not just `karac run` — the encode direction (bytes->String) is the common, high-value case; decode returns Result[Vec[u8],DecodeError].
investigation: Repro: `Base64.encode(vec![72u8,105u8])` -> interp 'SGk=', build '0' (silent). `Hex.encode(...)` -> interp '4869', build '0'. `Base64.encode(v.as_slice())` -> interp '' (Slice extraction bug), build '0'. Root: (a) codegen had no `Base64`/`Hex`/`Url` intercept in compile_assoc_call, so the call hit the const-0 default (a String fn returning i64 0); (b) eval_call.rs encoding byte extraction matched only Value::Array.
