FIX: (a) reject in compile_assoc_call (after the fallible-alloc reject) for type in {Base64,Hex,Url} + method in {encode,encode_url_safe,encode_upper,decode}; (b) add a Value::Slice arm to the encoding byte extraction. Verified: interp now 'SGk='/'SGk='/'4869' (slice==vec); JIT+build error loudly with the actionable message (no silent 0). Tests: interpreter test_encoding_on_slice + codegen encoding_builtins_rejected_by_codegen. No existing codegen test used encoding (nothing relied on the silent 0).

title: Chained `arena.get(r).field` on a struct-element `Arena[T]` silently reads the `i64 0` placeholder instead of the stored field (AOT/JIT; interp correct). The bound form `let n = arena.get(r); n.field` works — only the direct field-access-on-get-result miscompiles. `compile_field_access`'s generic StructValue tail resolves the field index via `field_index_for` → `type_name_of_expr`, which had NO arm for a compiler-builtin `arena.get(...)` MethodCall, so it returned None and the access fell through to the const-0 placeholder. Shipped with the Arena codegen slice (e01b609, 2026-07-18).
investigation: 2026-07-18 (found while adding Arena-as-function-parameter support, via the interp-vs-AOT probe harness): `struct Node { val: i64, kids: i64 }; let a: Arena[Node] = Arena.new(); let r = a.push(Node{val:42,kids:7}); println(a.get(r).val)` — interp prints 42, AOT prints 0. Bisect: `let n = a.get(r); println(n.val)` → 42 (correct, `n` is a registered struct binding); `println(a.get(r).val)` chained → 0. General chained struct-returning calls are fine (`mk(10).val` where `fn mk() -> Node` → 42), so the gap is specific to the compiler-builtin `arena.get`. Root cause: `compile_arena_get` for a struct element returns a correct SSA struct VALUE (loads it from the copy-out slot); `compile_field_access`'s generic tail (src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) compiles the object to that struct value and calls `field_index_for(object, field)` to get the extractvalue index, which delegates to `type_name_of_expr`. That fn resolves Identifier/SelfValue/StructLiteral/FieldAccess/Index and user-impl MethodCall (via fn_return_type_names) but NOT a compiler-builtin `arena.get()` — so it returned None, `field_index_for` returned None, and the tail emitted the `i64 0` placeholder. FIX (5c3f61f): add an arm to `type_name_of_expr`'s MethodCall case that resolves `<arena>.get(r)` to the arena binding's Struct element-kind name (from arena_vars) — the extractvalue then finds the field. Verified interp == JIT == AOT. Regression: tests/codegen.rs::test_e2e_arena_chained_get_field. Landed together with the Arena/Interner-as-parameter feature (same commit).
resolution: Fixed by teaching type_name_of_expr to resolve the element struct type of a compiler-builtin arena.get() call, so chained field access finds the field layout.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced by the interp-vs-AOT probe harness while extending Arena/Interner to function parameters; the struct-element chained-get was never covered by the original Arena E2E tests (which used the bound `let n = a.get(r)` form). — CLASS NOTE: silent miscompile (wrong value 0, no crash/verifier error) on AOT+JIT, interp correct — a run-vs-build divergence in effect, filed as miscompile since the compiled output is simply wrong. — SURFACE NOTE: codegen field-access field-index resolution (type_name_of_expr / field_index_for, src/codegen/expr_ops.rs) missing a compiler-builtin arena.get MethodCall arm.

source: internal:gpu-lavapipe-283 (surfaced while validating the KARAC_GPU_BACKEND=cpu escape hatch, phase-10 [LINUX LANE] entry 283, against lavapipe/llvmpipe on the headless Linux box)
title: Both GPU dispatch entrypoints (karac_runtime_gpu_dispatch + karac_runtime_gpu_dispatch_resident) called `std::slice::from_raw_parts(uniform_ptrs, n_uniforms)` unconditionally, so a ZERO-uniform kernel — codegen passes `uniform_ptrs = null, n_uniforms = 0` — hit `from_raw_parts(null, 0)`, which VIOLATES the aligned-non-null precondition. Release-benign (the empty slice is iterated zero times and nothing is dereferenced), but a debug/`-C debug-assertions` build ABORTS with 'unsafe precondition(s) violated: slice::from_raw_parts requires the pointer to be aligned and non-null'. Never observed before because it needs a GPU adapter PRESENT (gpu_context() == Some) to reach the dispatch body, and no dev/CI host had one until a software Vulkan adapter (lavapipe) was installed.
investigation: 2026-07-18: installing mesa-vulkan-drivers (lavapipe/llvmpipe, a DeviceType::Cpu Vulkan adapter) to validate the new KARAC_GPU_BACKEND=cpu selector made gpu_context() return Some for the first time on the Linux box, so the previously-skipped gpu runtime tests actually ran their bodies. `five_group_kernel_exceeds_default_storage_buffer_limit` (a zero-uniform dispatch: it calls karac_runtime_gpu_dispatch_resident with n_uniforms=0, uniform_ptrs=null) then panicked at the `let uniform_slice = from_raw_parts(uniform_ptrs, n_uniforms)` line with the aligned-non-null precondition message. ROOT CAUSE: from_raw_parts requires a non-null aligned pointer EVEN for a zero-length slice; the codegen ABI legitimately passes null for the empty-uniform array, so the two disagree. FIX: guard the empty case in both entrypoints — `let uniforms: Vec<&[u8]> = if n_uniforms == 0 { Vec::new() } else { from_raw_parts(uniform_ptrs, n_uniforms).iter().map(...).collect() };` — so the null pointer is never handed to from_raw_parts. After: the full `cargo test -p karac-runtime --features gpu` suite is 321 passed / 0 failed / 3 ignored against lavapipe, both with KARAC_GPU_BACKEND=cpu and on the default auto-select path.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found by the compiler team while landing entry 283 (KARAC_GPU_BACKEND=cpu), NOT by a kata/dogfood author. Class crash (a debug-mode UB-precondition abort; the underlying defect is passing a null pointer to from_raw_parts, which is UB regardless of build mode — the debug assertion merely surfaces it). severity low: release binaries are unaffected (empty slice, no deref), the trip requires both a live GPU adapter and a zero-uniform kernel, and it is loud (immediate abort, never a silent miscompile). Fix touches only the two extern-C dispatch entrypoints in runtime/src/gpu.rs; no ABI, layout, or codegen change. Verified: full gpu suite 321/0/3 against lavapipe (with and without the env var); clippy -p karac-runtime --features gpu clean; the two named entry-283 gates (doubles_an_f32_buffer_on_the_gpu, doubles_past_the_1d_dispatch_cap_via_2d_grid) pass.

title: Field access of ANY field on a type with no named fields — a primitive (`i64`/`f64`/`bool`/`char`), `String`, or other non-struct/non-union — was SILENTLY ACCEPTED by the typechecker. `infer_field_access`'s `_ => return Type::Error` arms returned the poison `Type::Error` WITHOUT emitting a diagnostic, so `"42".parse` / `n.foo` / `b.bar` PASSED `karac check`, then ICE'd the interpreter ('entered unreachable code: field access ... receiver was Value::String not Struct') and failed codegen ('Undefined variable'). Struct field access ('no field X on struct S') and method CALLS on primitives ('no method X on type T') were already checked; only bare field access on a fieldless receiver leaked through.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found by a run-vs-build parity sweep — `"42".parse[i64]()` (a turbofish-style non-construct; Kāra has no turbofish) parses as field-access `.parse` + index `[i64]` + call, passed check, and ICE'd interp / failed build. Class soundness (typecheck accepted an ill-typed program) + diagnostics (ICE instead of a clean error). Numeric tuple indexing (`t.0`) is a distinct AST node (TupleIndex), never routed through infer_field_access, so it is unaffected.
investigation: Minimized: `"42".parse`, `"hi".foo`, `let n:i64=5; n.foo`, `"42".parse[i64]` ALL passed `karac check`; `"42".parse()` (method call) and `s.b` (struct field) correctly errored. Root: infer_field_access (src/typechecker/fields.rs) resolves the receiver type name via a match on Named/Shared/Ref; the `_` arms (a non-nominal receiver — primitive/String/etc.) `return Type::Error` silently. FIX: a new `field_access_on_fieldless_type` helper emits `no field '<field>' on type '<T>'` (T via `impl_table_key`) before returning Type::Error, from both the outer `_` arm and the Ref-inner `_` arm. Verified: the primitive/String/bool cases now error cleanly (no ICE); valid field access (struct, `t.0` tuple index, `ref` struct field, `i64.MAX` const, method calls) unaffected. Typechecker 2066 + interpreter 1242 + codegen green. Test: typechecker test_field_access_on_fieldless_type_rejected.

title: Displaying an `Option[ref T]` with a SCALAR payload — the type of `Vec.first()` / `.get(i)` / `.last()` (the borrow-typed accessor, B-2026-07-14-11) — failed codegen with the deferred struct-Display error ('Display of a struct argument is supported when the argument is a variable or field access ... bind ... to a `let` first') while the interpreter rendered `Some(10)`. Hit by both a bare `println(v.first())` and — misleadingly, since the diagnostic tells the user to do exactly this — an UNannotated `let x = v.first(); println(x)`. Adding the explicit annotation `let x: Option[i64] = v.first()` (which drops the `ref`), or `.to_string()` / `match` / `unwrap_or`, all worked; a free-FUNCTION call returning `Option[i64]` (no `ref`) also worked. So the divergence was specific to the ref-typed Vec accessors bound/printed without an annotation.
detail: Class run-vs-build (interp rendered it, codegen refused). The Display registration (`var_option_payload_te` / `var_result_payload_te`, types_lowering.rs) and the bare-call-result path (`try_compile_option_result_display`, synth_display.rs) both gate on `is_reconstructable_display_payload`, which only matches a `TypeKind::Path` primitive/String — a `TypeKind::Ref(i64)` payload fails, so the binding fell through to the generic struct-Display error. For a SCALAR element the `ref` is a pure type-system artifact: the accessor returns the value BY COPY in the Some payload word, so the runtime layout is byte-identical to a plain `Option[T]` (proven — the annotated `let x: Option[i64] = v.first()` form already rendered the correct value, and `match v.first() { Some(n) => ... }` already extracted the right scalar). SEVERITY low: many workarounds; no runnable-corpus example prints a ref-payload Option. Sibling of the still-open compound/heap-payload Display deferral (B-2026-07-08-9 follow-on) — a `ref String` / `ref Vec` payload is a REAL pointer whose layout differs from the inline value, so it is deliberately NOT peeled and stays on that deferred path.
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep. Instrumented codegen's let-binding surface detection: both `let x = f()` (works) and `let x = v.get(0)` (fails) recorded surface='Option', but the inner TypeExpr was `Option[i64]` for the fn call vs `Option[ref i64]` for the Vec accessor — the `Ref` wrapper is what `is_reconstructable_display_payload` rejects. FIX: `peel_scalar_ref_display_payload` (synth_display.rs) strips a single `ref`/`mut ref` layer IFF the inner is a scalar-word primitive (`is_scalar_word_display_field`, made pub(super)); applied at both the Option and Result registration in `register_var_from_type_expr` (types_lowering.rs) and at the two payload guards in `try_compile_option_result_display`. Verified: i64/f64/u8 scalar `.first()`/`.last()`/`.get()` — both `let`-place and bare-call sites, plus the None arm and an f-string — now MATCH the interpreter; `Vec[String].first()` (ref String, a pointer) stays on the deferred error path unchanged (no miscompile); `Some(5)` / `Result` printing unaffected. Test: codegen e2e_option_ref_payload_display_matches_interpreter. Full 36-probe parity sweep clean, codegen suite green, clippy --all-targets --features llvm clean.

title: A std-rooted `import` naming a nonexistent baked-stdlib module (`import std.math`, `import std.completelybogus`, `import std.foo.{Bar}`) was SILENTLY ACCEPTED by `karac check` in single-file mode. It bound a dead module alias (`import std.math` -> `math`), and using it (`math.pow(2.0, 10.0)`) then ICE'd BOTH backends with 'internal error: entered unreachable code: variable \'math\' not found ... should be caught by resolver'. Multi-file TREE mode already rejected it (validates against `ProgramTree.graph.by_path`); only single-file mode skipped the check.
detail: Class diagnostics (a resolve-time UnknownModule error was owed; instead the program passed check and ICE'd a backend — the invariant the panic message itself asserts). `collect_import`'s single-file branch bound imports WITHOUT validation on purpose — a single file may be one translation unit of a larger build whose module tree the resolver can't see, so USER cross-file imports must stay trusted. But `std` is the compiler's OWN namespace, fully known at compile time regardless of build shape, so a std-rooted import is always verifiable. Valid gated imports (`std.web`/`std.autograd`/…) are spliced + removed by `expand_gated_stdlib_imports` BEFORE resolution, so any std-rooted import still reaching `collect_import` is a typo/nonexistent module. NOTE: `use` (UseDecl -> collect_use) is the SEPARATE trusted cross-module-reference mechanism (`use std.collections.HashMap`) and is deliberately NOT validated. Prelude-global types (`Stats`, `Base64`) need no import; `import std.stats.{Stats}` was never valid in tree mode and is now consistently rejected in single-file mode too (no test relied on it — only a stale comment).
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (`import std.math; math.pow(...)` — a natural but wrong guess, since Kara exposes math as float METHODS `.pow()`/`.exp()`, not a module). Both `karac run --interp` and `karac run` panicked identically at interpreter/eval_expr.rs. Isolation: `math.pow` WITHOUT the import correctly errors 'undefined name math'; WITH the import it passes — so the bogus import registered `math` and suppressed the undefined-name error. Root: single-file `collect_import` binds every import unvalidated. FIX: `report_bogus_single_file_std_import` + `prelude::is_importable_stdlib_module` / `importable_stdlib_module_names` — validate the whitelist (GATED_STDLIB_SOURCES paths + std.prelude, mirroring the tree builder's `by_path` seeds) and emit the same `UnknownModule` (with a did-you-mean) the tree path does, still binding names to avoid UndefinedName cascades. Verified: bogus std imports now error cleanly (no ICE) under both check and run; valid gated/prelude/user imports and all `use` decls still pass; resolver 2068 + typechecker + interpreter + codegen green. Tests: resolver test_bogus_std_import_rejected_single_file / test_valid_std_and_user_imports_still_accepted.

title: The 2-arg `assert(cond, "msg")` form — accepted by the typechecker, run by the interpreter, and emitted by the COMPILER itself for tensor shape-checks (design.md dim asserts) — was REJECTED by codegen with 'assert() expects 1 argument, found 2', a run-vs-build divergence: it passed `karac check` + `karac run --interp` but failed `karac run` / `karac build`. Separately, the interpreter accepted the 2nd arg but SILENTLY IGNORED it, always reporting the bare 'assertion failed' instead of the supplied message.
detail: Class run-vs-build (codegen refused a construct check + interp accept). `compile_assert` hard-required exactly 1 arg (`args.len() != 1 -> Err`); `eval_builtin_assert` read only `args.first()` and never looked at `args.get(1)`. design.md documents the user API as 1-arg `assert(cond)` (source-text capture for the message) but the compiler emits `assert(a.shape[1] == b.shape[0], "shape mismatch...")` for dim checks, so the 2-arg form is a real internal shape. FIX makes both backends accept 1-OR-2 args and, on failure, use a string-LITERAL 2nd arg as the message (the common case incl. the compiler's shape-asserts), falling back to 'assertion failed' for a dynamic message — kept SYMMETRIC so the two backends report identical text (a dynamic-String message rendered at runtime is a deferred follow-up; codegen's failure record takes a compile-time &str). NOTE: the assert-FAILURE output FORMAT still differs between backends (interp 'runtime error: <msg>' + trace vs codegen's KARAC_TEST_FAILURE record) — a pre-existing cosmetic difference shared by 1-arg asserts, out of scope here.
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (`assert(1 == 2, "not equal")`): interp reported the assertion, codegen failed with 'assert() expects 1 argument, found 2'. Root: arity guards in compile_assert (codegen) and the ignored 2nd arg in eval_builtin_assert (interp). FIX: compile_assert accepts args.len() in {1,2}, extracts a StringLit 2nd arg into the failure record's message (else 'assertion failed'); eval_builtin_assert mirrors it (plus a pending-control-flow guard after evaluating cond, matching sibling builtins). Verified: `assert(cond, "msg")` now compiles + runs under codegen and reports 'msg'; interp reports 'msg' too; passing 2-arg asserts fall through; 1-arg asserts unchanged. Tests: interpreter test_assert_two_arg_uses_string_literal_message / test_assert_two_arg_passing_does_not_fire; codegen e2e_assert_two_arg_message_form_compiles_and_runs. Suites: interpreter+typecheck 2068, codegen green, clippy --all-targets --features llvm clean.

title: Assigning a captured LOCAL `let mut` binding from inside a `par { }` branch is NOT caught by the concurrency-write checker, and produces DIVERGENT run-vs-build garbage. `fn main() { let mut a = 0; let mut b = 0; par { a = 10; b = 20; } print(a + b); }` passes `karac check` but yields interp=20 (only the last branch's env merges back: a=0,b=20) vs build=0 (no write-back: a=0,b=0) — neither is the naive 30, and the two backends disagree. The design's data-race-freedom guarantee (design.md line 104) and the par-write rule (design.md line 1329) intend such a write to be a COMPILE ERROR unless the binding is Atomic/Mutex/RwLock/Arc.
detail: Class soundness (an ill-formed data-racing program passes the static check, then the two backends produce different results). The existing par-write check (`check_modbind_par_conflicts` / `check_one_par_block`, effectchecker/modbind_synth.rs) is built ENTIRELY on `modbind_let_mut` — MODULE-level bindings, which get synthetic per-binding `writes(BINDING_resource)` effects. It correctly rejects `let mut counter` at module scope written from par (verified: emits ModuleBindingWriteInPar). LOCAL `let mut` bindings get NO synthetic resource, so the effect-set-based check never sees a captured-local write. SCOPE: the single-write case (`par { a = 10; }`) is consistent across backends (both 10) though arguably still owed a rejection; the clearly-divergent case is 2+ branches writing distinct captured locals. FIX DIRECTION (deferred — non-trivial, over-rejection risk, warrants review): extend the par-region check to flag a direct `Assign`/`CompoundAssign` inside a par branch whose target root is a `let mut` declared in an ENCLOSING (non-par) scope and not re-declared within the branch, emitting the same 'wrap in Atomic/Mutex/#[thread_local]' diagnostic. Needs per-par-site outer-local tracking (which the module-binding pass doesn't currently thread) and careful scope handling (branch-local decls, shadowing, params) to avoid false positives.
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (batch 7). `par { a = 10; b = 20; }` on captured locals: `karac check` = All checks passed; `karac run --interp` printed 20, `karac run` (JIT) printed 0. Confirmed the module-level analogue IS caught (both the direct-write and writes-effect-via-fn forms fire error[effect] ModuleBindingWriteInPar), isolating the gap to captured LOCAL bindings. Left OPEN: the correct fix is a new capture-write analysis in the concurrency checker (see FIX DIRECTION) — deferred rather than routed around, per the fix-or-ledger rule. FIX (3136b0f): added `check_captured_local_par_writes` (src/effectchecker/modbind_synth.rs), run from effectchecker.rs after check_modbind_par_conflicts. A conservative one-pass AST analysis (`ParWriteScope`) collects enclosing `let mut` locals (partitioned into non-primitive vs the Atomic/Mutex/RwLock/Arc primitive escape) and every par-branch-local shadow name, then flags an Assign/CompoundAssign inside any par branch whose root is an enclosing non-primitive `let mut` that is neither a primitive nor a branch-local shadow. The resolver already guarantees an assigned name is in scope, so a surviving root is necessarily a capture — no per-branch scope stack needed; a sibling-branch shadow is conservatively skipped (rare false negative, never a false positive). Emits the existing ModuleBindingWriteInPar kind (E0408) with a local-specific message. Verified: the 2-write repro now rejects both `a` and `b`; false-positive guards pass (branch-local `let mut` write, read-only capture, outer-shadowed-by-branch-local). The fix even caught a latent captured-local-write race in the test suite's own test_par_block_reader_reader_allowed (its `snap = COUNTER` scaffolding), now rewritten to read into branch-locals. Pins: effectchecker test_par_block_captured_local_let_mut_write_rejected + _atomic_local_method_not_rejected + _branch_local_let_mut_not_rejected. Same suite run as B-2026-07-18-28.

title: SILENT MISCOMPILE of the design-recommended Atomic-in-`par` escape hatch: a `par { }` block with 2+ branches that mutate a captured `Atomic[T]` writes back NONE of the mutations under codegen. `fn main() { let c = Atomic.new(0); par { c.fetch_add(1, MemoryOrdering.SeqCst); c.fetch_add(1, MemoryOrdering.SeqCst); } print(c.load(MemoryOrdering.SeqCst)); }` prints 2 under `karac run --interp` but 0 under `karac run` / `karac build`. design.md line 1329 explicitly directs programmers to wrap par-mutated state in `Atomic[T]`/`Mutex[T]` — so this is the SANCTIONED pattern producing a wrong answer, silently, with no diagnostic.
detail: Class miscompile (correct, idiomatic program → wrong result under codegen; interp is right). Trigger characterized: 2+ par branches mutating a captured Atomic — same atomic or distinct, direct `.fetch_add`/`.store` or via a `ref Atomic[T]` fn arg — all yield build=0 (every mutation dropped). SINGLE-branch par writes the captured atomic back correctly (build matches interp), as does non-par code. ROOT (from par_blocks.rs `compile_par_block` / `emit_par_branch_fn`): par-block captures are copied BY VALUE into each branch's env struct, and write-back exists ONLY for `let`-introduced branch bindings (ReturnSlots), NOT for mutations of pre-existing captured bindings. An Atomic is stored as a bare i64 alloca (`let c = Atomic.new(0)` -> value slot), so each branch's `fetch_add` RMWs its own private copy and the parent `c` alloca is never touched; the multi-branch path also lacks the single-branch capture write-back. FIX DIRECTION (deferred — deep par-capture-ABI change, high risk, warrants review): capture concurrency primitives (`Atomic`/`Mutex`/`RwLock`/`Arc[...]`) BY POINTER — thread the parent alloca address through the env struct and bind the branch-local variable to that shared address so RMWs hit one cell (also the only way real cross-thread atomicity holds). Sibling of B-2026-07-18-27 (captured LOCAL `let mut` in par — that one should be REJECTED by the checker; this one must WORK).
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (batch 9). Isolation matrix: no-par fetch_add x2 -> both 2; single-branch par fetch_add -> both 5; single-branch par store -> both 7; TWO-branch par (2x fetch_add same atomic) -> interp 2 / build 0; 3-branch -> interp 3 / build 0; 2 distinct atomics one per branch -> interp 2 / build 0; 2 branches via `ref Atomic` fn calls -> interp 2 / build 0. So the discriminator is branch-count >= 2 with a mutated captured atomic; single-branch has capture write-back, multi-branch does not. Left OPEN (see FIX DIRECTION) — a par-capture-ABI change deferred for careful review rather than routed around, per the fix-or-ledger rule. FIX (3136b0f): captured Atomic/Mutex are inline-value cells, so `emit_par_run` copied them BY VALUE into each branch's env struct and every fetch_add/lock hit a private per-branch copy (parent read 0). Now `is_par_shared_cell_type` (Atomic/Mutex) drives a BY-POINTER capture: the env field holds the parent alloca ADDRESS (src/codegen/par_blocks.rs emit_par_run) and the branch prologue binds the branch-local directly to that shared address (no private alloca), so all branches RMW/lock the ONE parent cell — the only way real cross-thread atomicity/mutual-exclusion holds (parent outlives all branches; karac_par_run is a barrier). COUPLED ownership exemption: `classify_binding_type` (src/ownership/concurrent_shared.rs) now returns None (cross-task-safe, same as `par struct`) for Atomic/Mutex/RwLock/Arc, which the baked stdlib otherwise mis-classifies as plain structs and rejects with E_CONCURRENT — so the sanctioned pattern now passes `karac check` AND runs correctly (the exemption is sound precisely because this codegen fix makes the sharing real). Verified interp==JIT==AOT: 2-branch Atomic->2, 3-branch->3, distinct atomics->2, 2/4-branch Mutex->2/4; check clean. Pins: tests/par_codegen.rs::test_e2e_par_multi_branch_bare_atomic_local_shared_by_pointer + ..._bare_mutex_local...; effectchecker test_par_block_atomic_local_method_not_rejected. Suites: codegen 2460 / par_codegen 196 / effectchecker 405 / ownership 357 / concurrency 130 / typechecker 2066 / interpreter 1244 / memory_sanitizer 779 all 0-failed; clippy+fmt clean. NOTE: the `ref Atomic` fn-arg leg from the original isolation matrix (`bump(c: ref Atomic[i64])`) is a DISTINCT pre-existing NON-par miscompile filed separately as B-2026-07-18-30 — it fails outside par too, so it is not part of this par-capture fix.

title: REBUILDING or RE-WRAPPING a match-bound shared-enum payload node double-frees under AOT (interp correct): both `MethodCall(MethodCallExpr { object, method, args, span })` from freshly-destructured parts AND the minimal `MethodCall(mc)` re-wrap of the UNTOUCHED bound payload crash the selfhost emitter generator with free(): double free. The `mc.method.clone()` peek + re-wrap combo also crashes; only full single-destructure + passing the PARTS to a helper (never reconstructing an Expr node) is clean.
detail: Surfaced wiring println(s.substring(..)) in the selfhost emitter (Phase 12 Codegen Slice 17): the println arg arm needed to route a non-to_string MethodCall into the general value path, which naively re-wraps the bound payload — `match value { MethodCall(mc) => ... emit_value(MethodCall(mc)) }`. Under `karac build` the generator aborts free(): double free; `--interp` is correct. The parts-reconstruction variant (destructure then rebuild the node) crashes identically. WORKAROUND (landed): factor the dispatch into a helper taking the destructured parts (`emit_method_value(object, method, margs)`) so no Expr node is ever rebuilt. Not yet standalone-minimized — the repro is the Slice-17 selfhost generator pre-refactor (this commit's history); likely related to the B-2026-07-18-2 family (shared-enum payload ownership at move boundaries).
investigation: MINIMIZED STANDALONE REPRO (2026-07-18, deterministic 3/3): `struct Mc { object: E }` / `shared enum E { Call(Mc), Leaf }` / `fn helper(e: E) -> i64 { match e { E.Call(mc) => 5, E.Leaf => 1 } }` / `fn emit(e: E) -> i64 { match e { E.Call(mc) => helper(E.Call(mc)), E.Leaf => 1 } }` / `fn main() { let e = E.Call(Mc { object: E.Leaf }); print(emit(e)); }` -> `karac check` passes, `--interp` prints 5, `karac build`/JIT prints 5 then aborts `malloc(): unaligned tcache chunk detected`. MINIMIZATION MATRIX: the trigger requires (a) a shared-enum variant whose payload is a plain struct with a RECURSIVE SHARED-ENUM FIELD (`object: E`) — a String-only or Vec-only payload does NOT crash; (b) re-wrapping the match-bound payload into the enum CONSTRUCTOR (`E.Call(mc)`) — passing the BARE bound `mc` to a helper is clean; (c) the constructor value being CONSUMED as a non-tail value (a call argument, or a `let` then passed) — re-wrap + tail-RETURN (`match e { E.Call(mc) => E.Call(mc) }`) is CLEAN, because the tail-move path suppresses the scrutinee drop; a `ref`-scrutinee match is clean (no move). ROOT-CAUSE HYPOTHESIS: constructing `E.Call(mc)` from a match-bound payload `mc` shallow-copies the payload struct (incl. the nested `object: E` Arc handle) WITHOUT a retain, treating it as a move; but when that constructor is a CALL ARGUMENT (not the arm tail), the arm's scrutinee-drop suppression (compile_tail_final_expr + the ~10 suppress_* helpers in control_flow_match.rs, e.g. suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out) does NOT fire — those are gated on the constructed value being the ARM TAIL. So the original scrutinee `e`'s rc-drop frees the nested `object` Arc AND the consumer (helper) frees it again -> double free. FIX DIRECTION (deferred — deepest drop subsystem, high regression surface across match/enum/shared, warrants review): make the move-out analysis recognize that an enum constructor built from a match-bound payload consumes that payload (and thus the scrutinee's nested heap) EVEN WHEN the constructor is nested in a call argument / let, not only at the arm tail; or make the constructor retain the nested Arc when the source binding is NOT being moved out. A blanket retain is unsafe (it would LEAK the clean tail-return case, which relies on the move). Sibling of the B-2026-07-18-2 shared-enum-payload-move family. Workaround remains landed (selfhost emit_method_value takes destructured parts, never rebuilds an Expr node). ROOT CAUSE (2026-07-18, deepened via dogfood): the struct payload (`Mc`/`MethodCallExpr`) carries a DIRECT shared/RC child field (`object: E`/`Expr`). At the match-bind site (`pattern_binding.rs` ~615-643) `struct_clone_fully_duplicates` bails (its `emit_struct_clone_fn` would SHALLOW-copy the shared handle with no rc-inc), so the payload is bound as a NON-OWNING VIEW aliasing the RC box's inline payload and recorded in `shared_enum_payload_view_vars`. That view-map only duplicates a child on a LATER `let S {..} = view` DESTRUCTURE; it does NOT cover moving the WHOLE view into a re-wrapped shared-enum node (`E.Call(mc)`) or passing it by value — those make the new box a SECOND owner of the aliased buffers. Multi-heap-field payload (`{object: E, method: String}`) -> double-free; single non-String field (`{object: E}`) -> UAF 'Invalid read of size 8'. Read-only use of the bound view (no re-wrap) is clean. TWO FIX ATTEMPTS TRIED + REVERTED (main clean): (A) extend `suppress_shared_enum_payload_move_out` to zero the whole NestedStruct field-region in the box (mirroring its VecOrString arm) — eliminates the double-free & UAF for read-only/single-shot, but the view has no own-drop, so the re-wrap-then-consume chain (process box AND emit box both zeroed) LEAKS every heap child, one alloc/iteration (unbounded); memory_sanitizer+codegen suites still pass. (B) upgrade the view to an OWNED deep-copy-with-shared-retain at bind (`deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` under `deep_copy_rc_inc_bare_shared`+`copy_support_for_loop_shared_mode`, mirroring `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` param_own.rs:105-109) + `track_struct_var` — HEAP-CORRUPTS ('malloc(): unaligned tcache chunk' / Invalid read): the in-place deep-copy on the match-bound VIEW alloca is unsound as-is (bound alloca is a by-value view whose shape/state the copy+drop rc-symmetry doesn't match). CORRECT FIX likely needs the deep-copy-with-retain applied to a freshly-materialized OWNED slot (not the aliasing view) with a matching struct-drop (rc-DEC symmetric with the retain rc-INC), validated on ASAN+codegen+selfhost. Std probe dir repros: rw4c ({object:Expr,method:String} re-wrap = double-free), rw4f ({object:Expr} = UAF). ATTEMPT-3 DEEP DIAGNOSIS (2026-07-18): the correct fix is a THREE-piece coordinated change, and each simpler subset fails a specific way. Piece 1 (materialize an OWNED copy of the view with shared-child RETAIN): `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place(alloca, tn)` under `deep_copy_rc_inc_bare_shared=true` already does this correctly — its bare-shared arm rc-INCs a direct shared-ENUM field too (`shared_heap_type_for_type_expr` resolves shared enums, not just shared structs) and its Vec/String arm dups the buffer. Piece 2 (a balancing DROP): `track_struct_var(tn, alloca)` is ALSO already correct — it delegates to `track_struct_var_inst`, which for a `struct_owns_shared_field` struct emits the COMBINED drop (`emit_vec_elem_struct_with_shared_drop_fn`: value-drop + shared-field rc-DEC walker `emit_nested_struct_shared_rc_decs`), NOT the plain value drop (a struct's own `__karac_drop_struct_<S>` SKIPS shared fields by design — they are rc-dec'd by the let/value-path machinery, synth_drop.rs:259-273). So pieces 1+2 balance a bound-and-read view (rw4f-style) — BUT they HEAP-CORRUPT the re-wrap case (rw4c) because of the MISSING Piece 3: when the owned `mc` is MOVED into the re-wrapped node `MethodCall(mc)` (a shared-enum VARIANT CONSTRUCTOR arg), the new box shallow-copies `mc`'s struct value (same method buffer ptr, same object rc handle) and becomes a second owner, but `mc`'s combined-drop is NOT retracted — the existing move-out suppression set (`suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg`/`zero_struct_move_caps`) does not fire for a struct moved into a shared-enum variant constructor, and even if it cap-zeroed the method buffer it would NOT retract the combined-drop's rc-DEC of `object`. Result: `mc`'s drop AND the new box's drop both free the method copy + rc-dec `object` -> double-free / 'unaligned tcache chunk'. CORRECT FIX = 1 (deep-copy view -> owned, done via existing helper) + 2 (combined drop via `track_struct_var`, done) + 3 (NEW: at shared-enum variant construction, when a struct-payload arg is a tracked owned binding that is moved in, RETRACT its `StructDrop` cleanup action wholesale — the shared sibling of `suppress_map_cleanup_for_tail_identifier` — so exactly the new box owns it). Piece 3 (full StructDrop retraction on move-into-a-shared-enum-constructor, correct across the NESTED re-wrap cascade process->emit) is the remaining work; it touches the move-out machinery and needs the full memory_sanitizer+codegen+selfhost suites to validate the RC balance. All three attempts (box-suppression leak, deep-copy-only corruption, this 3-piece analysis) are reverted; main is clean and still reproduces. RESOLVED ea5a844 (2026-07-18): shipped the 3-piece fix (view->owned deep-copy-with-retain at bind + combined drop via track_struct_var + `suppress_struct_cleanup_for_tail_identifier` retraction at the shared-enum variant constructor). CRITICAL GATE REFINEMENT vs the recipe above: piece-1's copyability gate must be `struct_clone_fully_duplicates` UNDER `copy_support_for_loop_shared_mode` (NOT `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct`, which treats a bare `Vec` as copyable without inspecting the element). The loose gate admitted the self-hosted parser's `CallNode { args: Vec[Arg] }` (Arg carries `Option[String]` + shared `value`) and its element deep-copy LEAKED (regressed asan_vec_of_struct_shared_and_option_field_consumed). The tight gate recurses into Vec elements and leaves a `Vec[struct-with-shared/Option]` payload as a VIEW (old, sound behavior), while still owning the flat shared+String+Vec[primitive] shape the bug reported. Validated: memory_sanitizer 782 + codegen 2463 + selfhost-codegen oracle green, clippy/fmt clean, all rw4* / rw_reg / L1 / L2 / fail29 repros clean on interp/JIT/native + valgrind; regression test added.

title: A `ref Atomic[T]` FUNCTION PARAMETER does not mutate the caller's atomic under codegen — `fn bump(c: ref Atomic[i64]) { c.fetch_add(1, SeqCst); }` called twice on a local `let c = Atomic.new(0)` prints interp 2 but JIT/AOT 0. check-clean, interp-correct, codegen wrong-output. DISTINCT from B-2026-07-18-28 (that was the par by-value capture): this reproduces with NO par block at all, so it is a general ref-Atomic argument-ABI miscompile, not a par-capture issue.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced by the B-2026-07-18-28 isolation matrix (the `2 branches via ref Atomic fn calls` leg), then bisected to a NON-par repro (`bump(c); bump(c);` with no par) that ALSO gives JIT/AOT 0 — so it is independent of the par-capture write-back. Class miscompile: a correct program silently returns the wrong result under codegen. The `ref Atomic[i64]` param apparently receives a copy of the atomic value (or a temp's address) rather than the caller alloca's address, so the callee's `fetch_add` RMWs storage the caller never reads back. Likely in the ref-Atomic argument lowering / `resolve_atomic_storage` param path. Medium: silent wrong answer, but on the less-common `ref Atomic` fn-wrapper idiom (the direct `c.fetch_add` form and the par-struct-Atomic-field form both work).
investigation: RESOLUTION (6057527): fixed in `resolve_atomic_storage` (src/codegen/method_call.rs). A `ref`/`mut ref Atomic[T]` param's alloca holds a POINTER to the caller's atomic storage (ref_params), so the identifier arm now loads that pointer and uses it as the atomic address (elem type = ref_params[name], the atomic value type) instead of RMW'ing the pointer-holding slot. Covers ref fetch_add / mut ref store / read-only load; composes with the par-capture-by-pointer fix (-28) for a captured atomic passed through a ref helper inside par. codegen 2461 + memory_sanitizer 779 green. Test: codegen e2e_ref_atomic_param_aliases_caller_cell.

title: A GENERIC function returning `Option[T]` from a Vec accessor (`v.first()` / `v.last()`), monomorphized with a HEAP `T` (`String`), DOUBLE-FREES under AOT (interp correct). `fn last[T](v: Vec[T]) -> Option[T] { v.last() }` called with `Vec[String]` -> `--interp` prints the element, `karac build`/JIT aborts `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`. Non-generic `Vec[String].last()` is CLEAN, and the same generic at `T = i64` is CLEAN — the defect is monomorphization of the Option[heap] payload's ownership/drop, not the accessor itself.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp correct, AOT corrupts the heap). Minimization: (a) non-generic `match v.last() { Some(s) => .. }` over `Vec[String]` — CLEAN; (b) `fn last[T](v: Vec[T]) -> Option[T] { v.last() }` / `fn first[T](...) { v.first() }` at `T = String` — DOUBLE FREE; (c) same generic at `T = i64` — CLEAN. So the trigger is the MONOMORPH of a generic `-> Option[T]` body whose `T` is a heap type, returning a Vec-accessor Option. Likely the mono'd return-slot / Option-payload drop double-counts the String buffer (the mono either clones-out AND lets the container drop, or the returned Option[String] and the source both free). Sibling of the Option[ref T]/heap-payload family (B-2026-07-18-24 was the primitive-payload Display leg; this is the generic-mono heap double-free leg). FIX DIRECTION (deferred — monomorphization + heap-payload drop, warrants review + memory_sanitizer): audit the mono return path for a generic `-> Option[T]` returning a borrow-typed Vec accessor at a heap T; the container-vs-returned-payload ownership must be moved (one owner), matching the non-generic path that is already correct. FIX NOTE (056cbb3): root cause is a TYPECHECKER false-accept, not a codegen defect — `types_compatible`'s permissive `(TypeParam(_), _) | (_, TypeParam(_)) => true` wildcard equated `ref T` with `T` when recursing into the `Option` arg (the declared owned `Option[T]` return vs the accessor's `Option[ref T]`), so the ill-typed generic definition slipped through while its concrete `Option[ref String]` vs `Option[String]` and `Option[ref i64]` vs `Option[i64]` twins were already correctly rejected. Fix: an arm BEFORE the wildcard rejects a param-WRAPPING borrow (`ref <param>` / `mut ref <param>`) against a bare type param (restricted to a param inner, so a concrete `ref String` unifying with an unconstrained `fn f[U](x: U)` arg is unaffected). The definition is now rejected for all `T`, consistent with both concrete twins — reclassified surface codegen->typecheck, class double-free->soundness (observable was a double-free; the defect + fix are in the typechecker).
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (generic collection helpers over String). Repro: `fn last[T](v: Vec[T]) -> Option[T] { v.last() } fn main() { let v = ["x".to_string(), "y".to_string()]; match last(v) { Some(s) => print(s), None => print("n") } }` -> interp `y`, build `free(): double free`. `karac check` passes.

title: A GENERIC function body that RECONSTRUCTS a struct with heap fields, monomorphized with a HEAP `T` (`String`), emits INVALID LLVM IR (interp correct). `fn swap[T](p: Pair[T]) -> Pair[T] { Pair { a: p.b, b: p.a } }` at `T = String` -> `--interp` swaps correctly, `karac build` fails 'Module verification failed: Invalid InsertValueInst operands!  %field = insertvalue { i64, i64 } undef, { ptr, i64, i64 } %b, 0'. The generic IDENTITY (`fn id[T](p: Pair[T]) -> Pair[T] { p }`) at `T = String` is CLEAN, and `swap` at `T = i64` is CLEAN — the defect is the struct-LITERAL construction inside a monomorph body using the generic BASE struct type (`{ i64, i64 }`) instead of the mono type (`{ {ptr,i64,i64} x2 }`).
detail: Class run-vs-build (interp correct, AOT emits invalid IR / build fails loudly). The verifier dump is exact: the mono'd `swap[String]` body builds `insertvalue { i64, i64 } undef, { ptr, i64, i64 } %b, 0` and returns `{ i64, i64 }` while the fn's return type is `{ {ptr,i64,i64}, {ptr,i64,i64} }` — the struct literal `Pair { a: p.b, b: p.a }` is lowered with the GENERIC base `Pair` LLVM type (i64 placeholder fields) rather than the monomorphized `Pair[String]` type. ATTEMPTED FIX (reverted, insufficient): substituting the recorded instantiation through `subst_monomorph_type_params` in `struct_inst_mono_type_for_expr` before `mono_struct_type` did NOT fix it — the generic FREE-fn mono body's struct literal either has no span-recorded instantiation (`enum_inst_type_from_span` returns None -> `compile_struct_init` falls back to the base type) or the active `type_subst_names` isn't populated for a generic-free-fn mono at that point. FIX DIRECTION (deferred — mono type-substitution subsystem, warrants review): ensure a generic-free-fn monomorph body records/threads the concrete instantiation for its struct literals (so `struct_inst_mono_type_for_expr` returns `Pair[String]`), or resolve the struct-literal type through the active mono substitution at `compile_struct_init`. Sibling of B-2026-07-03-23 (per-instantiation generic struct-literal layout — that fixed the direct-call-site instantiation; this is the inside-a-generic-body leg). FIX NOTE (62c5330): two coupled legs, both -O2-masked / live under `karac run`/-O0. (1) Invalid IR — `mono_struct_type_from_active_subst` (types_lowering.rs) resolves the struct's declared fields through the ACTIVE monomorph subst and builds the concrete `{{ptr,len,cap} x2}` layout; `compile_struct_init` (exprs.rs) uses it as the span-record fallback, so the literal no longer uses the erased `{i64,i64}` base type. (2) Double-free (exposed once the IR was valid) — the mono param was caller-retains (bare-`T` fields fail `field_copy_supported`), so the callee MOVED the fields into the returned literal, aliasing the caller's buffers (caller drop + returned value both free). `try_make_generic_struct_param_callee_owned` (param_own.rs) entry-copies the mono param's heap fields at the CONCRETE layout (offset-correct for any field count — unlike the single-field-gated base bare-`T` drop of B-2026-07-15-11) and registers the per-monomorph drop, making the param callee-owned like its non-generic twin. Regression: a `KARAC_OPT_LEVEL=0` build+run cli test (a -O2 test passes vacuously); confirmed it aborts on the pre-fix binary.
investigation: Found by a run-vs-build parity sweep (generic struct helpers over String). Matrix: non-generic Pair-String swap CLEAN; generic swap[T] String INVALID IR; generic swap[T] i64 CLEAN; generic id[T] String (no reconstruction) CLEAN. So the trigger is struct-LITERAL reconstruction (`Pair { a: p.b, b: p.a }`) inside a generic body monomorphized with a heap T. `karac check` passes.

title: `Option/Result.map` over a HEAP payload (String/Vec) now works under codegen, unblocked by fixing a chained-method span collision. Two coordinated fixes: (Slice 1, 58a45ea) the parser sets `MethodCall.span = receiver.span`, so a CHAINED `opt.map(f).unwrap_or(d)` shares one span and the `method_unwrap_inner_types` / `method_unwrap_err_types` side-tables collided (outer call's insert clobbered the inner's) — the inner `map` then read `unwrap_or`'s scalar inner type and mis-classified a heap payload; re-keyed those two tables on the call's `args_close_span` (unique per call, with a receiver-span fallback for synthetic nodes) in both the typechecker inserts and codegen reads. (Slice 2, 4b941dc) heap-payload `.map()` lowers via match synthesis (`match opt { Some(x) => Some(f(x)), None => None }`), reusing the match ownership machinery, with the typechecker recording the mapper's solved `Fn(T)->R` so codegen types the closure.
investigation: 2026-07-18: `Option/Result.map` over a heap payload was previously gated (interpreter-only). Attempting codegen surfaced TWO blockers. (A) Ownership: the hand-rolled trivially-copyable map path shallow-copies the payload, unsound for a mapper that MOVES (`|x| x` → dangling result) or RETURNS heap. Fixed by synthesizing the equivalent match and delegating to the match codegen's move-out/drop/receiver-suppression machinery. (B) Chained-call span collision: `SpanKey` is `(offset,length)` and the parser aliases every chained method call to the root receiver span, so `opt.map(f).unwrap_or(d)` — the ubiquitous idiom — had map and unwrap_or overwrite each other in method_unwrap_inner_types; the inner map read the outer's i64 inner type and crashed the LLVM verifier. Root-caused via the parser rule (`src/parser/exprs.rs:195`) and confirmed the `args_close_span` disambiguation precedent (already used for width-recovery: `pow`/bit-intrinsics record at the 'non-aliased close-paren leaf'). Slice 1 re-keys method_unwrap_inner_types + method_unwrap_err_types on `args_close_span` via new `token::method_call_key` / `SpanKey::for_method_call` (5 TC inserts + 6 codegen reads; `compile_method_call`/`try_compile_option_result_method` take a dedicated `args_close_span` param used ONLY for these two tables — all other span-keyed tables still key on the receiver span, their inserts unchanged; the Option gate runs before the synthetic re-dispatch helpers so those pass the fallback). Slice 2 adds the match synthesis + the typechecker Fn(T)->R recording + a narrow codegen preference for the recorded String/Vec return when the structural heuristic gives up to i64 (Ordering-returning cmp closures / sort_by untouched) + synthesized-pattern-binding-type seeding keyed on the mapper span. Verified interp == JIT == AOT and valgrind/LSan-clean for: scalar-returning mappers, the chained `map(f).unwrap_or(d)` idiom (the Slice-1 payoff), identity MOVE, Result Ok/Err, annotated-param heap-returning mappers, named-fn mappers. Tests: test_e2e_option_result_map_heap_payload, test_e2e_option_map_heap_unannotated_gated, asan_option_map_heap_payload_no_leak. Full --features llvm suite green (codegen 2463, interpreter, ownership, par_codegen, memory_sanitizer); clippy + fmt clean.
resolution: Fixed. Slice 1 (58a45ea) re-keys the method_unwrap_* side-tables on args_close_span to end the chained-call collision; Slice 2 (4b941dc) lowers heap-payload map via match synthesis with the recorded mapper Fn type.
detail: RESIDUAL: an UN-ANNOTATED closure whose body produces a heap String (`|s| s.to_uppercase()` without a param annotation) is a clean loud BUILD gate, not silent — the typechecker can't infer the mapper's return from the payload T without an annotation (B-2026-07-12-10 lineage) and codegen can't recover String-vs-Vec from the shared LLVM type; annotate the param (`|s: String| ...`) or run `--interp`. — SCOPE NOTE: Slice 1 fixed only the two method_unwrap tables; the other 7 chained-collision-prone method-call side-tables (method_callee_types, temp_recv_*, iter_terminal_*, channel_elem_types, task_join_return_types) plus the adjacent expr-result tables remain on the receiver-span key (each has its own workaround or no active bug) — a follow-up slice can re-key them on args_close_span the same way. — SOURCE NOTE: overnight autonomous continuation of the Phase-8 handle/codegen work; heap-map was the requested item, the span collision the discovered prerequisite.

title: A fresh owned/heap ARGUMENT temp passed to a predicate call inside a `while` CONDITION leaked one allocation PER ITERATION (unbounded) under AOT/JIT — `while has_more(items[i].clone()) { .. }`, `while wants(k.clone(), n) { .. }`. The identical call in an `if` condition (or anywhere in the loop BODY) was clean. Root: `compile_while` gave the loop body a per-iteration `scope_cleanup_actions` frame but compiled the CONDITION with no frame, so the `.clone()` arg temp was registered in the ENCLOSING scope's frame — drained once after the loop, never per-iteration. Interp/JIT/native all produced CORRECT output; only the heap accounting leaked.
detail: Surfaced writing two realistic collection programs (a word-frequency counter and a per-region sales aggregator) through the interp/JIT/native harness: output matched on all three backends but native valgrind flagged N-block leaks scaling with the loop trip count. Bisected to a predicate free-fn call in the `while` guard whose String args were `.clone()` temps. FIX (2 legs, 7bcbd47): (1) `compile_while` (control_flow.rs) now pushes a per-iteration cleanup frame around the condition and drains it (`drain_top_frame_with_emit`) immediately after the guard i1 is materialized and before the conditional branch — the guard is `bool`, so no heap value escapes into the body, making the drain safe on both the taken and not-taken edges. (2) That alone converted the leak into a DOUBLE-FREE whenever the predicate sat in the RHS of a short-circuit `and`/`or` in the guard (`b >= 0 and f(v[i].clone())`): the conditionally-created RHS temp's tracking slot (a reused entry alloca, `cap` still non-zero from the last taken iteration) got re-drained on a skip iteration. So `compile_short_circuit` (expr_ops.rs) now scopes its RHS in its own frame drained INSIDE the rhs block — freeing the temp on the path where it was created, exactly once — mirroring how `if`/`match` already scope their conditional sub-blocks (short-circuit was the sibling that lacked it). Verified: unbounded-scaling repro (100 iters -> 0 leaks), short-circuit-skip repro, both realistic programs leak-free on interp/JIT/native + valgrind; memory_sanitizer (781) + codegen (2463) suites green, clippy/fmt clean; regression test asan_while_condition_predicate_call_clone_arg_no_leak.

title: `SortedMap` lacked the `.entry()` API that `Map` has, so idiomatic ORDERED aggregation (`m.entry(k).and_modify(|c| c += 1).or_insert(1)`, `m.entry(k).or_insert(Vec.new()).push(x)`) was rejected at check with `no method 'entry' on type 'SortedMap'` — a user reaching for a deterministic key-sorted word-count / histogram had to hand-roll get_or + insert. Surfaced writing a realistic collection program.
detail: SortedMap shares Map's `KaracMap` storage (ordering applied only at iteration), so the fix reused the existing entry machinery rather than duplicating it. (1) typechecker: added the `entry` arm to `infer_sorted_map_method` returning `Entry[K, V]` (mirrors Map; the or_insert/or_insert_with/and_modify chain then flows through the shared `infer_entry_method`). (2) codegen: ALREADY WORKED unchanged — `try_compile_entry_chain` keys off `map_key_types`, where a SortedMap var is registered under the shared Map/Set side-tables (`extract_map_kv_types` handles `Some("Map") | Some("SortedMap")`), and the chain mutates order-independent storage. (3) interpreter: taught the entry machinery about `Value::SortedMap` at five sites — the `entry` arm (creates `Value::Entry{slot_idx: None}`, key-based since a BTreeMap has no positional slot), the `or_insert_with` occupancy check, `and_modify` (by-key SharedCell mutate + write-back), `entry_or_insert_ref` (BTreeMap insert-if-absent), and the `MapSlotRef` `read_map_slot`/`write_map_slot` choke points (resolve a SortedMap slot by `OrdValue` key so `.push`/`*r += 1` write through). Verified: entry+and_modify+or_insert, or_insert_with, bare or_insert, i64+String keys, push-through-mut-ref all agree on interp/JIT/native + valgrind-clean; typechecker+interpreter+codegen suites green, clippy/fmt clean; regression tests at all three layers.

title: A CHAINED width-sensitive integer intrinsic (`x.leading_zeros().leading_zeros()`, also `rotate_left`/`count_ones` chains) miscompiled under codegen while `--interp` was correct: `let x: u8 = 1; x.leading_zeros().leading_zeros()` -> interp 29, JIT/AOT 27. The parser aliases a chained call's `MethodCall.span` to its receiver's span, so every call in a chain shares ONE `method_callee_types` key and the last (outer) insert wins; `receiver_int_kind` consulted that table first and read the OUTER call's recorded receiver width (u32) for the INNER call, lowering `1u8.leading_zeros()` as a 32-bit `llvm.ctlz` (-> 31, outer -> 27) instead of the width-correct 8-bit (-> 7, outer -> 29).
detail: Class miscompile (interp != JIT == AOT; silent wrong output, no crash/verifier error). This is one concrete manifestation of the method_callee_types leg called out in B-2026-07-18-33's SCOPE NOTE (the chained-method span-collision affects several receiver-span-keyed side-tables, method_callee_types among them). Rather than re-key the whole method_callee_types table (which sprawls to ~15 reader sites across codegen, lowering, monomorphization, the must_use/unsafe lints, and — critically — the ownership borrow checker, where a keying desync silently changed move/borrow classification), the fix is contained to the ONE consumer that the miscompile flows through. `receiver_int_kind` (src/codegen/method_call.rs) already had a `type_name_of_expr(object)` fallback AFTER the table read; the fix simply REORDERS it to prefer the receiver's own resolved type over the span-keyed table. `type_name_of_expr` resolves an identifier receiver by name (`var_type_names`) and a field/method receiver by layout / `fn_return_type_names` — all unaffected by the parser's span aliasing — so it is authoritative when it answers. For the INNER call the receiver is a named binding (`x` -> u8), resolved correctly; for the OUTER call the receiver is the inner call's result (a builtin-returning MethodCall that `type_name_of_expr` can't name), so it falls through to the table, whose last-insert value IS that outer call's own entry (u32) — correct. Net: both calls now get their width-correct receiver kind. `parse()` rejects any non-int type name, so a non-int receiver still falls through to the table exactly as before. No keying change, no new params, no blast radius beyond the one function.
investigation: Found while scoping the B-2026-07-18-33 follow-up (re-key the remaining chained-collision-prone method side-tables on args_close_span). A full re-key was prototyped and REVERTED: it required threading args_close_span through ~15 reader sites in 8+ files and destabilized the ownership borrow checker (a.add(s) with a `ref` param spuriously flagged use-after-move) in ways that a green x86 codegen run did not surface — too broad and risky for the payoff. Reduced to a witness: `x.leading_zeros().leading_zeros()` on a u8 reproduced interp 29 / JIT 27 on stock main. `leading_zeros` is width-sensitive and its result type (u32) differs from a narrow receiver, so a same-named chain collides in method_callee_types with observable divergence. Confirmed the reorder fixes it across interp == JIT == AOT for leading_zeros / rotate_left+count_ones / u16 chains; full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2464 incl. the new test, interpreter 1244, ownership 357, par_codegen 196, memory_sanitizer 782); clippy + fmt clean.
resolution: Fixed (a71708d). `receiver_int_kind` prefers `type_name_of_expr(object)` over the span-keyed `method_callee_types` read; regression test e2e_chained_width_sensitive_int_intrinsic pins interp == codegen. The broader method_callee_types / iter_terminal_* / channel_elem_types re-key remains a deferred follow-up (B-2026-07-18-33 scope note) — no other active miscompile is known to flow through those tables' chained-collision today.

title: A by-value-`self` method returning a HEAP field directly as its tail — `fn get(self) -> String { self.v }`, the canonical owned accessor — DOUBLE-FREED under AOT (interp correct). `self.v` parses as `FieldAccess { object: SelfValue, .. }`, but the tail-return field-move-out suppression matched only `FieldAccess { object: Identifier(_) }`, so `self`'s callee-owned StructDrop freed the moved buffer the caller's returned value now owns.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp correct, AOT corrupts the heap / aborts free(): double free). Found by a parity sweep. Minimization: the free-fn form `fn get(b: B) -> String { b.v }` is CLEAN (Identifier arm fires); `let r = self.v; r` is CLEAN (local rebind); `fn id(self) -> B { self }` (whole self) CLEAN; `ref self` field read CLEAN; primitive field `self.n` CLEAN. ONLY the by-value-`self` HEAP-field direct tail return double-freed, for both 1-field and multi-field structs and both String and Vec fields. ROOT: `suppress_source_vec_cleanup_for_arg_ex` (call_dispatch.rs) FieldAccess arm gated on `object.kind == Identifier`; `self.v` is `object == SelfValue`. FIX: accept `SelfValue` (normalise to the "self" binding) so `zero_struct_field_move_cap` cap-zeroes the moved field in `self`'s slot and its StructDrop skips it. Verified: codegen 2466 + memory_sanitizer 783 green; tests codegen e2e_self_field_move_out_tail_return_no_double_free + memory_sanitizer asan_self_field_move_out_tail_return_no_double_free. Same move-out-suppression family as the struct-param-field-move fixes (B-2026-06-10-2).
investigation: Repro: `struct B { v: String } impl B { fn get(self) -> String { self.v } } fn main() { let b = B { v: "hi".to_string() }; print(b.get()); }` -> interp `hi`, build `free(): double free`. Instrumented the tail-return suppressor: for the method the tail final_expr was `FieldAccess { object: SelfValue, field: "v" }`, and the FieldAccess arm's `if let Identifier(s) = &object.kind` guard skipped it; the free-fn `b.v` form hit the arm (all conditions passed) and was suppressed.

title: An owned HEAP param (or local) moved into a VEC/ARRAY LITERAL that is returned/bound DOUBLE-FREES under AOT (interp correct): `fn dup(x: String) -> Vec[String] { [x] }` -> `--interp` builds `["z"]`, `karac build`/JIT aborts `free(): double free`. Every OTHER move-out sink is clean — `fn f(x: String) -> String { x }` (direct return), `W { s: x }` (struct literal), `(x, 1)` (tuple), `Some(x)` (Option), and `Vec.new() + v.push(x)` all correctly transfer ownership; the array/vec LITERAL element position is the one sink that does not suppress the moved element source.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp correct, AOT/JIT corrupts the heap). Minimization: `[x]` returned / `let v = [x]; v.len()` (non-escaping local Vec of the param) / `[a, b]` (distinct params) all double-free; a LOCAL element (`let s = ..; [s]`) is CLEAN; `[1,2,3]` / string literals / fresh-temp elements / kept-and-reused array / nested `[[1,2],[3]]` all CLEAN. So the trigger is an owned (caller-retains) heap PARAM moved into a Vec-literal element slot. ROOT: `[x]` with a Vec sink routes through `compile_vec_prefix_literal` (NOT `compile_array_literal` — the earlier investigation's wrong-path note), which already ran the move-out cap-zero suppression per element. But an owned param registers NO callee-side `FreeVecBuffer` (caller-retains), so cap-zeroing its slot suppresses nothing — the Vec element still aliased the caller's arg buffer and both freed it. FIX: `compile_vec_prefix_literal` now routes each element through `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg` (ordered BEFORE the cap-zero so the copy reads the source's real `cap`), exactly like return/tuple/push/struct-field/map-value sinks already do — so the Vec owns an INDEPENDENT copy of an owned-param element. No-op for locals, fresh temps, `cap == 0` sources, and POD elements. Same move-out-suppression family as B-2026-07-18-37 (self.field tail-return) and B-2026-06-10-2 (struct-param field move).
investigation: Found by a parity sweep. Repro: `fn dup(x: String) -> Vec[String] { [x] } fn main() { print(dup("z".to_string())[0]); }` -> interp `z`, build `free(): double free`. Local element (`let s = ..; [s]`) clean; struct/tuple/Some/direct-return/new+push sinks all clean. `karac check` passes.

title: An iterator chain whose SOURCE is a TEMPORARY Vec (a `vec![…]` literal or a call result, NOT a `let`-bound variable) SILENTLY miscompiled to 0/empty under codegen while `--interp` was correct. `vec![1,2,3].iter().sum()` / `.fold(0,f)` / `.count()` / `for x in vec![1,2,3].iter()` -> interp 6/6/4/6, JIT/AOT 0. `s.split(",").iter().map(f).sum()` (call-result source) loud-failed with `Vec/String method 'iter' is not yet supported`. The BOUND form (`let v = vec![…]; v.iter().sum()`) was always correct; `collect` over a temp source was already correct (its own materialize path).
detail: Class miscompile (interp != JIT==AOT; silent 0/empty output for the reduce terminals + bare for-loop, loud fail for a call-result source). TWO coupled root causes. (1) TYPECHECKER GAP: the fresh-temp element-type recording into `temp_recv_elem_types` (expr_method_call.rs, the block that rescues `make_vec().get(0)` / `for x in make_vec().iter()`) was gated on the `.iter()`/read-method RECEIVER being an `ExprKind::Call | MethodCall`. A `vec![…]` literal is an `ExprKind::PrefixCollectionLiteral` (and `[…]` an `ArrayLiteral`) — neither — so NO element type was recorded for a collection-literal `.iter()` source. Codegen's synthetic `for <elem> in <base>` (which every reduce terminal desugars to via `try_compile_iter_chain_fold`) peels the `.iter()` and recurses on the literal, falling to `try_compile_for_vec_value` (control_flow_for.rs), whose span lookup then MISSES both `temp_recv_elem_types` and `owned_temp_drops` -> the loop body is silently skipped and the accumulator keeps its seed (0). FIX 1: accept a `PrefixCollectionLiteral | ArrayLiteral` receiver in that block too, but ONLY for the `iter`/`into_iter` arm (the read-method arms `get`/`first`/`last`/`contains` stay Call-receiver-only, unchanged) — so the element type is recorded at the literal's span (the parser aliases the `.iter()` MethodCall span to its receiver span, which is where codegen looks). (2) TENSOR-PATH COLLISION exposed by fix 1: `try_compile_tensor_reduce_expr_receiver` (tensor.rs) intercepts `sum`/`mean`/`prod`/`min`/`max` on a NON-IDENTIFIER receiver (`a.zip_with(b,f).sum()`) purely on the presence of a `temp_recv_elem_types` entry at the (collided) call span, and runs BEFORE the iterator-chain sum/min/max intercept. Once fix 1 populated `temp_recv_elem_types` for a Vec iter source, the tensor path mis-stole `vec![…].iter().sum()`/`.min()`/`.max()`/`.product()`, treated the Vec as a Tensor, compiled `.iter()` as a value, and loud-failed. FIX 2: an ITERATOR terminal records `iter_terminal_elem_types` at that span; a genuine Tensor reduction never does (a Tensor `.sum()` is typed by the tensor path, not the Iterator protocol). Guard the tensor expr-receiver path to decline (`Ok(None)`) when `iter_terminal_elem_types.contains_key(&key)`, so the iterator-chain intercept (which correctly materializes the Vec source via fix 1) handles it. `fold`/`count`/`reduce`/`any`/`all` were never tensor-reduction names, so they were fixed by fix 1 alone; `sum`/`product`/`min`/`max` needed both.
investigation: Found by a floor-stdlib codegen dogfood sweep (interp-vs-JIT parity over realistic collection idioms). Localized with a read-only Explore pass: working (named) path resolves the Vec by name at control_flow_for.rs; broken (temporary) path misses the span lookup and hits the `Unknown iterable -> skip, return 0` line. The silent-vs-loud fork traced to whether `iter_terminal_elem_types` was recorded (sum engaged the terminal -> for-loop -> silent 0; split-result declined -> generic dispatch -> loud `iter` error). The tensor collision was caught via KARAC_DBG instrumentation showing sum's own intercept was never entered after fix 1 — an earlier tensor-reduce intercept (also keyed on `temp_recv_elem_types` at the collided span) consumed sum/min/max/product but not count/fold/reduce (non-tensor names). Verified interp==JIT==AOT for sum/product/fold/count/max/min/map().sum/filter().sum/into_iter/for-loop over both `vec![…]` and `split()` sources; the guarded tensor chain reduction `test_e2e_tensor_reduce_on_chain_receiver` (`a.zip_with(b,f).sum()`) still passes; full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2467, interpreter 1246, ownership 357, par_codegen 196, memory_sanitizer 784); clippy/fmt clean. Regression tests: test_e2e_iter_chain_over_temporary_vec_source (codegen) + asan_iter_chain_over_temporary_vec_source (LSan-clean, incl. a String-element temp source exercising per-element buffer free).
resolution: Fixed. FIX 1 (typechecker) records the fresh-temp element type for a collection-literal `.iter()`/`.into_iter()` source so codegen materializes it instead of silently skipping. FIX 2 (codegen tensor path) declines when the span carries an `iter_terminal_elem_types` entry so an iterator terminal is never mis-stolen as a Tensor reduction.

title: Displaying `Option[ref String]` — the borrow-typed result of `Vec[String].get(i)` / `.first()` / `.last()` — failed under codegen with the deferred struct-Display error while `--interp` rendered `Some(alpha)`. `let v: Vec[String] = ["a","b"]; println(v.get(0))` -> interp `Some(a)`, `karac build` `error: codegen failed: Display of a struct argument is supported when the argument is a variable or field access ...`. Both the bare-call site (`println(v.get(0))`) and the let-place site (`let x = v.get(0); println(x)` / `f"{x}"`) hit it. `Option[String]` (owned, e.g. from a fn returning `Option[String]`) and `Option[ref T]` for a SCALAR T already worked.
detail: Class run-vs-build (interp renders, codegen refused with a loud deferral — never a miscompile). Discovered alongside B-2026-07-18-39 in the same floor-stdlib dogfood sweep. Root: `peel_scalar_ref_display_payload` (synth_display.rs) peeled a `ref T` Option/Result Display payload to `T` only for SCALAR T; a `ref String` was left wrapped and then rejected by `is_reconstructable_display_payload`, falling to the deferred struct-Display error. A stale code comment claimed `ref String` was 'a real pointer whose layout differs' and had to stay deferred — but that is FALSE for these accessors: `vec_method.rs` `get`/`first`/`last` build the `Some` payload via `coerce_to_payload_words(elem_val, 3)`, loading the element's whole `{ptr,len,cap}` INLINE into the 3 payload words — byte-identical to `Option[String]`. FIX: extend the peel to also accept an inline-displayable inner (`is_inline_displayable_payload`, i.e. `ref String` / `ref str`), so the payload routes through the owned `Option[String]` renderer. Both the call-result path (`try_compile_option_result_display`, synth_display.rs) and the let-binding registration path (`types_lowering.rs`, `var_option_payload_te`/`var_result_payload_te`) call the same peel, so one change fixes both. OWNERSHIP: safe — Display is read-only, appending a COPY of the bytes into a fresh accumulator (`render_via_display_fn`), so the borrowed shared Vec buffer is never freed (no double-free with the Vec's own drop); the scalar-ref same-layout argument extends to the shared 3-word String value. Corrected the stale comment.
investigation: Isolated with an interp-vs-JIT probe matrix: `Option[i64]` from `.get()` OK; `Some("hi")` literal / `None` / `Ok("hi")` / fn-returning-`Option[String]` all OK; only the `Option[ref String]` accessor results failed. `let x: Option[String] = v.get(0)` type-errors ('expected Option<String>, found Option<ref String>'), confirming the accessor's borrow type. Read `vec_method.rs` `get` to confirm the payload is built inline (`coerce_to_payload_words(_, 3)`), not as a pointer — invalidating the stale peel comment. Verified interp==JIT==AOT for get/first/last + bare-call + let-place + f-string + None + Vec-usable-after; full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2468, interpreter 1246, memory_sanitizer 785); clippy/fmt clean. Regression tests: e2e_option_ref_string_payload_display (codegen) + asan_display_option_ref_string_from_get (LSan-clean borrow-not-freed gate).
resolution: Fixed (6c76b81). `peel_scalar_ref_display_payload` peels `ref String`/`ref str` (inline-displayable inner) to the owned renderer; borrow buffer untouched by read-only display.

title: `Iterator.rev()` was unimplemented — `v.iter().rev()` rejected with `no method 'rev' on type 'Iterator'` in both backends. Now SHIPPED: typechecker + interpreter (9dcf1b8, composes with adaptors on both sides + any terminal/for-loop), and codegen for the common BOUND-Vec base via reverse-iteration (c2a0f23). Residual: codegen still LOUD-defers a temp/literal or range base and any positional-adaptor+rev combination (see RESIDUAL).
detail: Class missing-feature (a genuinely absent combinator, not a bug — the prior uniform typecheck rejection was honest). SHIPPED: (1) typechecker `rev() -> Iterator[T]` (stdlib_iter.rs, element type unchanged, added to the recognized-adaptor name list); (2) interpreter (method_call_iter.rs) — reversal is NOT a lazy per-element `IteratorStep`; it drains the upstream via repeated `iterator_step` (firing every existing adaptor closure in forward order), reverses the collected Vec, and returns a fresh `IteratorSource::Eager`, so it composes with adaptors on BOTH sides (`map(f).rev()`, `rev().map(f)`, `rev().filter(p)`) and any terminal/for-loop. DEFERRED (this OPEN leg): codegen. The forward-only fused-chain lowering (`peel_fused_map_filter_chain` + the synthetic `for elem in <base>` desugar + the terminal intercepts) has no way to express a reversal without materialize-and-reverse plumbing threaded through every terminal. A syntactic `chain_receiver_contains_rev` guard in BOTH `compile_method_call` (catches terminal/adaptor OVER a rev chain, and a bare `.rev()`) and `compile_for` (catches `for x in <chain>.rev()`, which would otherwise hit the `_ =>` fall-through and SILENTLY skip the body — empty output vs the interpreter's reversed sequence) bails loud, naming rev + pointing at `--interp`. RUN-VS-BUILD NOTE: this introduces an intentional, LOUD interp-vs-build gap for rev (consistent with the repo's documented-deferral pattern, e.g. heap-map unannotated / reduce heap payload) — never a silent divergence. FIX DIRECTION for the codegen leg: desugar `<chain>.rev().<rest>` by materializing the pre-rev chain into a fresh owned Vec, reversing it in place (`Vec.reverse` exists), and iterating that forward — reuses collect + reverse + forward-iterate, all already lowered; the wiring across the terminal intercepts is the remaining work. — CODEGEN LEG UPDATE (c2a0f23): implemented via REVERSE-ITERATE, not the earlier materialize-and-reverse (which hit the parser's chain-span aliasing: a synthetic prelude `collect` collided on the shared span with the downstream terminal's side-table metadata). Key enabler: every fused terminal — collect INCLUDED — lowers to an `ExprKind::For` over the base and thus converges on `compile_for` -> `compile_for_vec_var`, so a single reverse-aware base loop (element index `len-1-i`, loop control unchanged) covers for/collect/fold/sum/count/max uniformly. The `pending_reverse_iter` one-shot signal is set by the rev guards after stripping `.rev()` and consumed+cleared by `compile_for_vec_var` (nesting-safe). Reverse-safety is gated to a bound-Vec identifier base with only order-independent map/filter/inspect steps, so the consuming arm is guaranteed reverse-aware — no silent forward iteration; a `consumed` post-check is a safety net. Verified interp==JIT==AOT across all terminals + both-side map/filter + String elements; LSan-clean (asan_iter_rev_bound_vec_reverse_iterate); full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2478, interpreter 1248, memory_sanitizer 793, ownership, par_codegen); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Surfaced in the same floor-stdlib dogfood sweep as B-2026-07-18-39/-40 (a probe matrix over iterator combinators found `rev` was the one adaptor typecheck rejected outright). Confirmed the interpreter's lazy `Value::Iterator { source, steps }` streaming model can't carry a per-element reverse step, so a drain-reverse-replay is the correct interpreter shape; verified it composes on both sides and with for/collect/fold/filter. Codegen deferred after confirming the fused-chain desugar is forward-only and reversal would require multi-site materialize-and-reverse plumbing (out of scope for a contained slice). Verified: interp correct for for-loop/collect/map-both-sides/fold/filter; codegen bails loud (naming rev + --interp) for terminal-over-rev, adaptor-over-rev, rev-over-adaptor, and the for-loop — never a silent skip. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2469, interpreter 1247); clippy/fmt clean; no user-method-named-`rev` regression. Tests: test_iter_rev_reverses_order (interpreter), e2e_iter_rev_deferred_loud_message (codegen, all four shapes).
resolution: Fixed. Interp+typecheck (9dcf1b8) drain-reverse-replay. Codegen (c2a0f23) reverse-iterate lowering for a bound-Vec base: strip `.rev()`, set a one-shot `pending_reverse_iter` signal, re-dispatch the stripped chain; `compile_for_vec_var` mirrors the element index to `len-1-i` (loop control unchanged, no allocation, leak-free). RESIDUAL (loud-deferred, not a bug — clean `--interp` bail, never silent): a TEMP/literal source (`vec![..].iter().rev()`), a RANGE base (`(a..b).rev()`), and any POSITIONAL adaptor combined with rev (`enumerate`/`take`/`skip`/`step_by`/`*_while` — semantically not a plain reverse). A follow-up slice can extend the reverse-aware base to the range and temp-Vec for-loop arms (the safety check `rev_chain_reverse_iterable` gates on a bound-Vec identifier precisely so the signal is always consumed by a reverse-aware loop — extending it means making those arms consume it too).

title: A closure that CAPTURES a whole heap String/Vec and RETURNS it double-frees under AOT/JIT (interp correct): `fn f(x: String) -> String { let g = || x; g() }` and the escaping `fn mk(x: String) -> Fn() -> String { || x }` both abort `free(): double free`. Read-only captures (`|| x.len()`), POD captures (`|| n` for i64), and uncalled closures are all clean. Affects BOTH stack-env (non-escaping) and heap-env (escaping) closures, and both param and local captures.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp clones so it stayed correct; AOT/JIT corrupts the heap). ROOT: the captured value's buffer is owned by something the frame still frees, in BOTH env models. Stack-env: the env holds a bit-copy of the source `{ptr,len,cap}` header — an ALIAS — and the ownership pass treats the capture as a read/borrow (the source is usable after the closure), so the enclosing frame stays owner and frees the source at scope exit. Heap-env: the RC env box OWNS the captured buffer (deep-copied at capture for an owned param, moved-in with a cap-zeroed source for a local) and frees it via the per-closure env-drop fn at RC-zero; the env can be invoked any number of times, so the return cannot move the buffer out of the env. Either way, returning the captured value handed back an alias of a buffer freed elsewhere, so the receiver's free collided with the owner's free. FIX: register each captured whole-heap Vec/String var in `for_loop_borrow_vars` for the closure body compile, routing every retaining-consume site (tail return, tuple, push, struct field, map value — all keyed on that set via `maybe_defensive_copy_param_arg`) through a defensive deep-copy; the returned value then owns an INDEPENDENT buffer while the env/source keeps its own. Set taken+restored around the body so the outer fn / sibling closures don't inherit the marks. Same move-out/alias-consume family as B-2026-07-18-38 (owned param into Vec literal), B-2026-07-18-37 (self.field tail-return), and the for-loop-element borrow deep-copy (B-2026-07-09-13 class).
investigation: Found by a parity sweep (closure surface). Repros: `fn f(x: String) -> String { let g = || x; g() } fn main() { println(f("z".to_string())); }` and `fn mk(x: String) -> Fn() -> String { || x } fn main() { let g = mk("E".to_string()); println(g()); println(g()); }` -> interp prints the string, build aborts `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`. Verified fix: 786+ memory_sanitizer asan tests (Linux LSan-active) stay green, 68 codegen closure tests green, escaping closure invoked 3x is leak- and double-free-clean. Regression tests: codegen `e2e_closure_returns_captured_heap_no_double_free`, memory_sanitizer `asan_closure_returns_captured_heap_no_leak_no_double_free` (covers stack-env param, stack-env local, escaping heap-env multi-call).

title: A closure that captures a Vec and RETURNS it, then the result is INDEXED, fails codegen with `Index operator applied to non-array type` (interp correct): `fn f(v: Vec[i64]) -> i64 { let g = || v; g()[1] }` and the local-capture sibling `let v = [1,2,3]; let g = || v; g()[0]`. The closure-call result's element/Vec type is not tracked, so `g()[i]` doesn't recognize the return as a Vec. `g().len()` (non-index) on the same shape WORKS, so it is specifically the index-into-closure-call-result type inference that is missing.
detail: Class codegen-gap (run-vs-build: interp iterates/indexes correctly; build rejects at codegen). The closure-call return type is not propagated as a Vec[T] to the index site, so `compile_index` sees a non-array LLVM type and errors. Distinct from B-2026-07-18-42 (the double-free on returning a captured heap value, now fixed) — this is the TYPE-TRACKING of a closure-call result used in index position. FIX DIRECTION (deferred): record the closure's declared/inferred return element type at the call site (mirror how a named-fn call result carries `vec_elem_types` for downstream `[i]` / iteration) so `g()[i]` resolves. Surfaced alongside -42 in the same closure parity sweep. FIXED: record the closure's returned Vec[T] TypeExpr at its let site (the `let_binding_fn_value_type` path in stmts.rs — the closure-literal binding path, which returns before the generic pending-closure path) into a new `closure_ret_vec_te` table (lifecycle mirrors var_elem_type_exprs: cleared per fn, saved/restored across monos). `inline_temp_vec_te`'s Call arm consults it first for a closure callee. `closure_tail_vec_return_te` handles bare `|| v` and block-tail `|| { …; v }`. Covers param capture, local capture, Vec[String], and block-body closures.
investigation: Found by a parity sweep. Repros: `fn f(v: Vec[i64]) -> i64 { let g = || v; g()[1] } fn main() { println(f([4,5,6])); }` -> interp `5`, build `error: codegen failed: Index operator applied to non-array type`. `g().len()` on the same closure works, isolating the gap to index-position type tracking of a closure-call result.

title: A GENERIC struct's owned by-value param/self whose HEAP FIELD is returned (moved out) double-frees under AOT/JIT (interp correct): `fn take[T](b: Box[T]) -> T { b.v }` and method `impl[T] Box[T] { fn get(self) -> T { self.v } }` at T=String both abort `free(): double free`.
