The non-generic twin (`struct B { v: String } fn B.get(self) -> String { self.v }`, B-2026-07-18-37) and `ref self` (borrow, deep-copies) are clean; T=i64 (POD) is clean.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp correct, AOT/JIT double-frees). The monomorph analogue of B-2026-07-18-37. TWO root gaps. (1) `zero_struct_field_move_cap` GEP'd the generic BASE struct type — whose bare-`T` field is an erased `i64` placeholder — and the FieldAccess-arm guard required `slot.ty == base_st`, but a monomorph's slot is the CONCRETE mono layout (`{ {ptr,len,cap} }`), so the guard rejected the mono slot and the GEP offset was wrong. FIX: both the guard and the cap-zero now resolve the mono struct type via `mono_struct_type_from_active_subst` (a no-op outside a monomorph, so non-generic callers are byte-identical) and resolve the field type through `subst_monomorph_type_params`; Vec/String is matched by concrete LLVM shape so the mono `str` spelling is caught. (2) A generic METHOD monomorph records `type_subst_names["T"] = "str"` (typechecker-internal spelling) vs the free-fn twin's `"String"`; `field_copy_supported` recognized `"String"` but not `"str"`, so `aggregate_param_copy_supported_struct_mono` bailed and owned `self` was NEVER entry-copied — it stayed a caller-retains alias, so returning `self.v` handed back a buffer both the caller and the return binding freed. FIX: treat `"str"` as `"String"` in `field_copy_supported` (every other codegen consumer already does; `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field` keys on `is_string_type_expr`, which matches both). Fixes String/scalar for both free fns and methods, single- and two-field generic structs. RESIDUAL: a whole-`Vec[E]`-typed generic field drops the element type from the monomorph subst (type_subst_type_exprs empty) so its entry-copy can't duplicate the buffer — separate open bug B-2026-07-18-45.
investigation: Found by a parity sweep (generic struct surface). Repro: `struct Box[T] { v: T } impl[T] Box[T] { fn get(self) -> T { self.v } } fn main() { let b = Box { v: "hi".to_string() }; println(b.get()); }` -> interp `hi`, build `free(): double free`. Verified: 787 memory_sanitizer asan (Linux LSan) + 2471 codegen green after the fix (core `zero_struct_field_move_cap` / `field_copy_supported` changes are byte-identical for non-generic callers). Regression tests: codegen `e2e_generic_struct_heap_field_move_out_no_double_free`, memory_sanitizer `asan_generic_struct_heap_field_move_out_no_double_free` (free-fn + method, single/two-field, T=String).

title: A generic struct whose type param is bound to a WHOLE Vec (`Box[Vec[i64]]`) and whose field is returned (moved out) double-frees under AOT/JIT (interp correct): `fn get[T](b: Box[T]) -> T { b.v }` / `impl[T] Box[T] { fn get(self) -> T { self.v } }` at T=Vec[i64] both abort `free(): double free`. The T=String sibling is fixed (B-2026-07-18-44); the residual is specifically a whole-Vec-typed T.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build). ROOT: for a whole-`Vec[E]`-typed T the monomorph records only `type_subst_names["T"] = "Vec"` (head), and `type_subst_type_exprs` is EMPTY (te_keys=[] observed) — so `subst_monomorph_type_params(Path("T"))` yields bare `Path("Vec")` with NO element, `extract_vec_elem_type` returns None, and `deep_copy_one_aggregate_field`'s entry-copy is SKIPPED (its `elem_ty` gate fails). The struct param's Vec field therefore stays a shallow alias of the caller's buffer while the mono struct-drop AND the returned value both free it. B-2026-07-13-2/-3 populate `type_subst_type_exprs` for a Vec whole-collection PARAM, but the whole-Vec-typed generic-STRUCT-field case (T bound via a struct literal arg `Box { v: [1,2,3] }`) isn't threaded. FIX DIRECTION: populate `type_subst_type_exprs` with the full `Vec[E]` TypeExpr when inferring a type param bound to a whole Vec (in `infer_type_args` / the generic-call subst build), so the entry-copy sees the element type. Affects BOTH free fns and methods. Same family as B-2026-07-18-44 (its Vec residual). FIXED: `resolve_generic_struct_param_substs` (mono.rs) unifies the declared struct param (`Box[T]`) against the arg identifier's recorded concrete instantiation (`enum_inst_var_types["b"] = Box[Vec[i64]]`), binding T's element-aware TypeExpr in subst_type_exprs (+ head-name subst_names) for any position whose concrete arg is a heap collection. The mono entry-copy then deep-copies the Vec field. or_insert (never overwrites a typechecker binding); runs before take_var_side_tables. Also makes Box[Vec[i64]] vs Box[Vec[String]] mangle distinctly. Covers free-fn + method, T=Vec[i64]/Vec[String], two-field struct, distinct instantiations.
investigation: Found alongside -44 in the same generic-struct parity sweep. Repro: `struct Box[T] { v: T } fn get[T](b: Box[T]) -> T { b.v } fn main() { let b = Box { v: [1,2,3] }; let r = get(b); println(r.len()); }` -> interp `3`, build `free(): double free`. Debug: inside the mono body `type_subst_names={"T":"Vec"}`, `type_subst_type_exprs` empty.

title: A closure that captures a whole heap-bearing STRUCT (a struct with a String/Vec field) and RETURNS it miscompiles under AOT/JIT (interp correct): `struct W { s: String } fn f(w: W) -> W { let g = || w; g() }` prints GARBAGE instead of the field. Distinct from B-2026-07-18-42 (which fixed whole Vec/String captures returned from a closure); a struct capture is not tracked in `vec_elem_types` so the borrow-alias deep-copy that -42 wired in does not fire for it.
detail: Class miscompile (run-vs-build: interp correct, build reads garbage — a UAF/shallow-alias on the captured struct's heap field). ROOT: B-2026-07-18-42 marks a captured whole Vec/String var as a `for_loop_borrow_vars` alias so a body return deep-copies it, but the gate keys on `vec_elem_types` membership — a captured STRUCT (heap-bearing via a String/Vec field) is absent from that map, so the capture aliases the source struct's field buffers and the returned struct hands back dangling/aliased pointers. FIX DIRECTION: extend the -42 capture-alias treatment to heap-bearing struct/enum captures (deep-copy the captured aggregate's heap fields on a body return, or register a struct-aware borrow-alias), mirroring `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned`'s struct entry-copy. Same closure-capture family as B-2026-07-18-42. FIXED: track whole heap-bearing struct/enum captures during env unpack (`captured_heap_agg_type` in closures.rs — excludes Vec/String [handled by -42], shared/RC aggregates, and POD aggregates) and deep-clone at the body's tail return via `emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr` (`deepcopy_captured_heap_agg_tail`, recursing through block tails). Return-time copy so an uncalled closure doesn't leak. The ESCAPING heap-env struct-capture case is NOT this bug — it hits the pre-existing documented deferral E_ESCAPING_CLOSURE_HEAP_CAPTURE_NOT_YET (B-2026-06-22-2 Slice 2), a loud codegen error with a workaround, not a silent miscompile.
investigation: Found in the closure parity sweep. Repro: `struct W { s: String } fn f(w: W) -> W { let g = || w; g() } fn main() { println(f(W { s: "z".to_string() }).s); }` -> interp `z`, build prints a garbage byte. `karac check` passes.

title: An enum METHOD with owned `self` that MATCHES its heap payload double-frees under AOT/JIT (interp correct): `impl E { fn take(self) -> String { match self { E.V(s) => s } } }` aborts `free(): double free`. The free-fn `fn get(e: E) { match e { … } }`, a LOCAL match, `ref self`, and POD payloads are all clean. Double-frees EVEN when the arm returns a scalar derived from the payload (`match self { E.V(s) => s.len() }`), not just when the payload is moved out.
detail: Class double-free (run-vs-build: interp correct). The enum-payload analogue of B-2026-07-18-37 (self.field move-out). ROOT: `suppress_destructured_enum_payload_cleanup` matched the match scrutinee only as `ExprKind::Identifier`, returning early for `ExprKind::SelfValue` — so a method matching its OWNED enum `self` never cap-zeroed the consumed payload in the source. `self` is entry-copied + given a callee-owned enum-drop by `make_aggregate_param_callee_owned` (functions.rs), so that drop freed the payload buffer AND the moved-out binding `s` (or its downstream consumer) freed it again. It fires even for a scalar-returning arm because the payload BINDING `s` is itself the second owner (its own `track_vec_var` free), independent of whether `s` escapes. FIX: add the `SelfValue` arm (normalized to the "self" binding), mirroring B-2026-07-18-37's struct-field fix. Same owned-self move-out family as B-2026-07-18-37 / -44.
investigation: Found by a parity sweep. Repro: `enum E { V(String) } impl E { fn take(self) -> String { match self { E.V(s) => s } } } fn main() { let e = E.V("hi".to_string()); println(e.take()); }` -> interp `hi`, build `free(): double free`. Verified: 789 memory_sanitizer asan (Linux LSan) + 2473 codegen green. Regression tests: codegen `e2e_enum_method_owned_self_payload_no_double_free`, memory_sanitizer `asan_enum_method_owned_self_payload_no_double_free` (payload returned, payload consumed-to-scalar, Vec payload).

title: A USER method whose name collides with a builtin Vec/String method (`get`/`take`/`unwrap`/…), called on a NON-IDENTIFIER receiver (a struct/enum LITERAL or call result) with a heap-shaped receiver, was misrouted to the Vec/String handler under AOT/JIT (interp resolved the user method): `struct R { v: String } impl R { fn get(self) -> String { self.v } } fn main() { println(R { v: "q".to_string() }.get()); }` -> `Vec.get requires an index argument`. Binding the receiver to a variable (`let r = R{..}; r.get()`) worked; an i64-returning `get` on a literal worked.
detail: Class run-vs-build (codegen misdispatch). ROOT: a single-heap-field struct (`R { v: String }`) shares String's `{ptr,len,cap}` LLVM shape, and a LITERAL receiver's user type is absent from `var_type_names` (keyed by binding name), so the builtin Vec/String shape-based dispatch claimed the call by method name — `Vec.get` ("requires an index argument"), `take`/`unwrap` ("not yet supported"). An i64-field struct (LLVM `{i64}`) doesn't match the shape, so it dispatched correctly; an identifier receiver resolves its user type by name, so it worked too. FIX: hoist `try_compile_freshtemp_user_method` (materialize the receiver, re-dispatch via the identifier path) to run BEFORE the builtin routing when the typechecker's `dispatch_key` names a user `Type.method` — the typechecker's resolution is authoritative. GATED to true TEMPORARY receivers (struct/enum literal, call result); PLACE receivers (`v[i]`/`s.f`/`t.0`) are EXCLUDED so a `mut ref self` method keeps writing back through the element/field pointer via the specialized indexed/field-receiver paths (materializing a place into a temp would drop the write-back — regressed `test_e2e_indexed_receiver_user_struct_method` in the first, ungated attempt). Distinct from B-2026-07-18-47 (the enum-self drop bug found in the same sweep). Surfaced a SIBLING interp bug: a user method literally named `unwrap` mis-resolves to the builtin Option/Result unwrap in the INTERPRETER (prints the enum Display) — filed as B-2026-07-18-49.
investigation: Found while writing B-2026-07-18-47 tests. Minimization: `R { v: String }.get()` (literal recv, String ret) FAILED; `let r = R{..}; r.get()` MATCHED; `R { v: i64 }.get()` MATCHED; `mk().get()` (call-result recv) FAILED pre-fix. Verified: 2474 codegen + 790 asan green after the fix (the place-receiver gate keeps `v[i].bump()` mut-ref write-back intact). Regression tests: codegen `e2e_user_method_builtin_name_on_literal_receiver`, memory_sanitizer `asan_user_method_builtin_name_on_literal_receiver_clean` (struct literal, enum literal, call-result receiver).

title: A user method literally named `unwrap` on a user enum/struct is mis-resolved to the builtin Option/Result `unwrap` by the INTERPRETER (prints the value's Display), while codegen (post B-2026-07-18-48) correctly calls the user method: `enum E { A(String) } impl E { fn unwrap(self) -> String { match self { E.A(s) => s } } } fn main() { let e = E.A("hi".to_string()); println(e.unwrap()); }` -> interp `A(hi)` (WRONG — the enum Display), build `hi` (correct). The reverse divergence direction from most run-vs-build bugs (interp wrong, build right).
detail: Class run-vs-build (interpreter mis-resolves a user method to a builtin). ROOT (hypothesis): the interpreter's method dispatch treats `unwrap` as the builtin Option/Result unwrap (or a Display/identity fallback) BEFORE checking the receiver's user impl block, so a user-defined `fn unwrap(self)` on a non-Option/Result type is never called — the interpreter yields the receiver's default Display (`A(hi)`) instead of the user method's result. Independent of the receiver form (fires for an identifier receiver too), so distinct from the codegen-side literal-receiver misdispatch B-2026-07-18-48 (which surfaced this once the build side started resolving the user method correctly). FIX DIRECTION: in the interpreter's method dispatch, prefer a user impl method matching `<ReceiverType>.unwrap` over the builtin Option/Result unwrap when the receiver is not an Option/Result. Likely affects other builtin-named methods with interp-side special handling; audit the builtin-method dispatch order. FIXED: `try_eval_option_result_method` (interpreter/method_call_optres.rs) runs before the user-impl dispatch and its `unwrap`/`expect`/`unwrap_err`/`expect_err` arms fall through to `other => other.clone()`, returning the receiver for a non-Option/Result value. Now bails (returns None → user-impl dispatch) when the receiver is a user struct/enum (not Option/Result) with an env-registered method of this name. Also covers the struct twin. Option/Result builtins unaffected (no env-registered Option.unwrap).
investigation: Found while extending B-2026-07-18-47 tests (method named `unwrap`). Repro above: interp `A(hi)`, build `hi`. `get`/`take` do NOT collide in the interpreter (they resolve to the user method), so this is specific to `unwrap` (and probably other Option/Result builtins the interpreter special-cases). `karac check` passes. INVESTIGATION NOTE (dead-end ruled out): adding `Value::EnumVariant` to the impl-method-first gate in `interpreter/method_call.rs` (~line 1530, the block that already prefers a user impl over the builtin arms for Struct/Column/Tensor/DataFrame receivers) did NOT fix it — `E.A(..).unwrap()` still returns the enum Display. So the `unwrap` interception happens EARLIER than that gate (an arm above it, or a special eval_method_call pre-pass), not in the builtin container arms below it. General enum user methods (`extract`/`take`) DO dispatch correctly via the later general `try_eval_impl_method` at ~line 2230, so only `unwrap` (and likely other reserved Option/Result builtin names) is special-cased ahead of user dispatch. Next: grep the dispatch path ABOVE line 1530 (and eval_call/eval_method_call) for where a 1-tuple enum variant's `unwrap` returns the receiver.

title: A GENERIC struct literal whose field type WRAPS the type param in a container (`items: Vec[T]`, `v: Option[T]`) did NOT infer `T` from a concrete initializer — `let b = Bag { items: ["hi".to_string()] }` (struct Bag[T]{items:Vec[T]}) was rejected `expected 'Vec<?T0>', found 'Vec<String>'`, forcing a `let b: Bag[String] = ...` annotation. A DIRECT-`T` field (`a: T`) already inferred fine.
detail: Class missing-feature (typecheck inference gap). Found by a fresh dogfooding parity sweep (generic struct helpers over String/i64). Matrix: `One[T]{x:T}` with `x:5` infers T=i64 (OK); `Bag[T]{items:Vec[T]}` with `items:[1,2]` -> `expected Vec<?T0>, found Vec<i64>` (REJECTED); `Boxed[T]{v:Option[T]}` with `v:Some("x")` -> `expected Option<?T0>, found Option<String>` (REJECTED); annotated `let b: Bag[String] = ...` (OK); B-2026-07-18-17 seeded `Box[Vec[i64]]{value:Vec.new()}` (OK). Root cause (`infer_struct_literal_expected`, fields.rs): the UNSEEDED generic solve computes a per-field `push_ty` and falls back to the RAW declared type (bare `TypeParam`, checked permissively via the wildcard) only when the substituted slot is a bare var/param at the TOP; a param nested one level down (`Vec[T]` -> `Vec[?T0]`) has `Vec` at the top, so the metavar-laden slot was pushed into `check_expr`, which reported a spurious `expected Vec<?T0>, found Vec<String>` mismatch instead of binding — the post-check `unify_types` that would have solved `T` never got the chance. FIX: extend the raw-fallback to any slot that (after substitution) still MENTIONS a type param anywhere (`!seeded && contains_type_param(&raw)`), so a container-wrapped param is checked permissively and `T` binds via the post-check unify — mirroring the direct-`T` field. The seeded path (concrete expected args, B-2026-07-18-17) is unchanged; a genuinely-uninferable `Vec.new()`-only field still errors 'cannot infer T'.
investigation: Repro: `struct Bag[T] { items: Vec[T] }` + `fn main() { let b = Bag { items: ["hi".to_string()] }; }` -> rejected pre-fix, accepted (interp==JIT==AOT-O0==AOT-O2 parity) post-fix.

title: A GENERIC struct literal that binds the SAME type param from CONFLICTING field values was silently ACCEPTED — `Two[T] { a: 1, b: "s".to_string() }` (struct Two[T]{a:T,b:T}) typechecked, greedily pinning `T=i64` from `a` and DISCARDING the failed unify against `b`'s String (a String stored in an i64-typed `T` slot -> miscompile). Pre-existing (affects direct-`T` multi-field too), surfaced while fixing B-2026-07-18-50.
detail: Class soundness (false-accept of an ill-typed literal). Found by a fresh dogfooding parity sweep. The struct-literal generic solve (`infer_struct_literal_expected`, fields.rs) called `unify_types(&slot, &actual)` for each field to bind the struct's type params, but IGNORED the returned bool — so a second field whose value conflicts with the param bound by the first field silently kept the greedy first binding. Matrix: `Two{a:1,b:"s"}` (i64 vs String), `Bag{items:[1],tag:"x"}` (Vec[i64] vs String tag) both ACCEPTED pre-fix; the conflict only surfaced downstream (if the mismatched field was later used inconsistently) or never (silent miscompile). PRE-EXISTING and orthogonal to B-2026-07-18-50, but that fix (routing container-wrapped fields through the same permissive-check + post-unify) would have WIDENED this hole from the container side, so both were closed together. FIX: check the `unify_types` result; on failure, deep-resolve both sides and emit a TypeMismatch UNLESS either is Error/Never or still mentions an unbound param (avoid cascades / premature errors). Numeric widening is preserved — `unify_types` routes `(Int, Int)` through `types_compatible` (`(Int, Int) => true`), so `Two{a:1i32, b:2}` still unifies and does NOT error; only a genuine cross-kind conflict returns false.
investigation: Repro: `struct Two[T] { a: T, b: T }` + `fn main() { let _x = Two { a: 1, b: "s".to_string() }; }` -> `All checks passed` pre-fix (wrong); post-fix `field 'b' has type 'String', but the type parameter was already bound to 'i64' by an earlier field of 'Two'`. Numeric `Two{a:1i32,b:2}` stays accepted.

title: Whole-`Vec[String]` variable reassignment (`cur = nxt`) freed only the OLD Vec's outer {ptr,len,cap} buffer and stranded every element String — the BFS double-buffer / worklist idiom leaked one full generation of element Strings per overwrite. The move-overwrite eager-free routes through emit_owned_vec_element_release_on_overwrite (stmts.rs Assign arm, trigger_eager_free), which released a Vec[shared]/Vec[Option[shared]] element's per-element RC drop (B-2026-07-12-30) but bailed (returned false) for a value String / nested Vec element because vec_elem_agg_drop_for_type_expr is None for those — so the caller fell to emit_free_vec_buffer_if_owned (OUTER buffer only) and the element Strings leaked. A leak per overwrite, unbounded in a loop (any Vec[String] double-buffer / frontier swap).
investigation: 2026-07-18: surfaced by kata #126 (Word Ladder II) — the BFS level frontier swap `cur = nxt` over a `Vec[String]`. Correct on all four surfaces (interp/JIT/build/AUTO_PAR=0 all print the same sink) but valgrind flagged `definitely lost` in karac_string_clone. MINIMIZED to `let mut cur: Vec[String] = Vec.new(); cur.push(heapstr); let mut nxt = ...; cur = nxt;` — the old cur's element String leaks (control without the reassignment is clean, so scope-exit Vec[String] drop is correct; only the reassignment path was incomplete). ROOT CAUSE: emit_owned_vec_element_release_on_overwrite short-circuits (`let Some(agg_drop) = ... else return false`) for any element with no aggregate drop fn — which includes String and nested Vec, both heap-owning VALUE elements. The comment even acknowledged the gap (phase-7 'Move-overwrite inner-element drop'), on the theory that per-element aliases (`let x = out[i]`) free the elements at their own scope exit; but a frontier swap with no per-element alias strands them. FIX: a String / nested-Vec value element now drains through the SAME recursive inline elem_ty (vec-struct) FreeVecBuffer walk the scope-exit cleanup uses for a Vec[String]/Vec[Vec] binding (elem_ty=Some(vec_struct), elem_agg_drop=None). Safe against a live per-element alias for the same reason scope-exit is: an index-read of a non-Copy element CLONES (clone_owned_vec_index_element), so the overwritten generation's elements have no other owner — no double-free. Scalar/inline-tuple elements keep the cheaper outer-buffer-only free. VERIFIED: minimal repro + #126 kata valgrind-clean (the only residual 'possibly lost' is benign auto-par pthread TLS, gone under KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0); full LSan memory_sanitizer suite 793/0 (incl. the kata-17-style per-element-alias tests — no double-free regression) + new regression asan_vec_string_var_reassign_loop_no_leak; codegen E2E 2477/0; par_codegen 196/0; clippy + fmt clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #126 Word Ladder II kata (kara-katas), BFS `cur = nxt` frontier swap over Vec[String]. Class leak (codegen move-overwrite inner-element drop gap). Same family as B-2026-07-12-30 (which fixed the Vec[shared] element case of the same overwrite path); this closes the value String / nested Vec element case. Tensor / Map element overwrite leaks remain a separate (rarer) gap — not addressed here.

title: `Vec[T].dedup()` was unimplemented — `v.dedup()` rejected `no method 'dedup' on type 'Vec'` in both backends (`dedup` was already in ast.rs's mutating-method list for ownership, but had no typecheck/interp/codegen dispatch). Now shipped across all three layers: removes CONSECUTIVE duplicate elements keeping the first of each run (Rust `Vec::dedup` semantics); non-adjacent duplicates preserved.
detail: Class missing-feature (a floor collection method found absent by a dogfood audit of common Vec/String/iterator operations; uniform typecheck rejection, no miscompile). Implemented: (1) typechecker (expr_method_call.rs) — no-arg method on `Vec[T]`/`VecDeque[T]` (+ ref/mut-ref receiver) returning Unit. (2) interpreter (method_call_seq.rs) — snapshot-dedup-writeback keeping an element iff it differs from the last KEPT one (`value_compare == Equal`), all element types. (3) codegen (vec_method.rs) — in-place compaction mirroring `Vec.retain`'s write-cursor skeleton: the keep decision is `w == 0 || data[w-1] != data[r]` via `compile_binop(BinOp::Eq)` (scalar icmp / String memcmp / struct field-eq — the same equality `Vec.contains` uses; it may emit its own blocks so the branch is taken from the current insert point), and removed duplicates are freed through `emit_drop_fn_for_type_expr` (heap elements included, POD skipped). `len = w` at the end excludes the stale byte-duplicate tail from the Vec's scope-exit drop, so the buffer frees exactly once (drop-safe, mirroring retain). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for scalar / String / all-equal / no-dup / empty; LSan-clean for the heap-duplicate drop path (asan_vec_dedup_drops_removed_heap_duplicates_no_leak). Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2479, interpreter 1249, ownership 357, memory_sanitizer 795); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Found by a broad interp-vs-build probe sweep of common floor operations (String/Vec/Map/Option/Result/iterator methods) — nearly everything worked interp == build; `dedup`, `String.parse`, `iter.find`, `iter.position` were the uniform-reject gaps. `dedup` was the cleanest (in-place mutation, no Option/type-directed return), implemented first. Tests: test_e2e_vec_dedup_scalar_and_heap (codegen), test_vec_dedup_removes_consecutive_duplicates (interp), asan_vec_dedup_drops_removed_heap_duplicates_no_leak.
resolution: Fixed (f5156fb). Typecheck + interp (all element types) + codegen (in-place compaction with element Eq + drop-glue, heap-safe).

title: `Iterator.position(pred) -> Option[i64]` was unimplemented (rejected `no method 'position' on type 'Iterator'`). Now shipped across all three layers: the 0-based index of the first YIELDED (post-adaptor) element the predicate holds for, or None.
detail: Class missing-feature (a standard iterator terminal found absent by the floor-method audit; sibling of B-2026-07-19-1 dedup). (1) typechecker (stdlib_iter.rs) — `position(pred: Fn(T)->bool) -> Option[i64]`, added to the recognized adaptor name list. (2) interpreter (method_call_iter.rs) — short-circuit terminal mirroring `any`/`all`, tracking a running index incremented per `iterator_step` yield, returning `Some(idx)` on first match / `None` on exhaustion. (3) codegen (method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_position`) — desugars to a fused for-loop `{ let mut __pos: Option[i64] = None; let mut __idx: i64 = 0; for elem in base { <steps>; if pred { __pos = Some(__idx); break; } __idx = __idx + 1; } __pos }` reusing the any/all engine's `build_fused_chain_body` + `fused_chain_prelude` + the reduce desugar's `Some(x)` ctor pattern (bare-Identifier callee). The index counts the POST-adaptor sequence (each sink invocation). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for hit / miss / first-element / filter+position / map+position / String source / range. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2480, interpreter 1250, par_codegen 196, memory_sanitizer 795); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Same floor-method audit as B-2026-07-19-1. Tests: test_e2e_iter_chain_position_terminal (codegen), test_iter_position_returns_index_of_first_match (interp).
resolution: Fixed (6448f0d). Typecheck + interp (index over yielded elements) + codegen (fused for-loop with running index + short-circuit break, Option[i64] result).

title: `karac check` reports a hard `error[ownership]: value 'v' moved here, used again here` for a reused OWNED heap value (`f(v); f(v)` where `v: Vec`/`String`/`Map`/`Set`), while `karac build`/`run` treat it as advisory and compile it correctly + memory-safely (codegen defensive-copies the reuse). The fatal/advisory split is BY DESIGN — `has_fatal_ownership_errors` (cli.rs) promotes only the `ExclusiveBorrowAliasedArgs` soundness case to fatal; every other ownership error, including UseAfterMove, is diagnostic-only for build/run. So build's leniency is intended, NOT a soundness hole (my first framing was wrong). The real friction: the UseAfterMove diagnostic was NOT machine-fixable, so `karac fix` could not repair it and it blocked the Mend loop on natural code that reuses a collection (an LLM writing two read-only helpers over the same Vec).
investigation: 2026-07-19: surfaced writing kata #137 (two read-only bitmask helpers over the same `nums: Vec[i64]`). Initially mis-filed as a check-vs-build inconsistency/soundness bug. Deeper read corrected that: (1) the UseAfterMove hard error is LOAD-BEARING and intentional — 58 assertions across tests/ownership.rs enforce Kāra's documented `owned = move` semantics, so softening the checker to rc-fallback (the originally-proposed fix) would break the language; and (2) `karac build`/`run` proceeding past it is DELIBERATE (has_fatal_ownership_errors makes only ExclusiveBorrow fatal; the rest are advisory because codegen safely defensive-copies the reuse). So neither check nor build is 'wrong' — the gap was purely that the diagnostic had no `karac fix`. FIX (98be8da): populate the UseAfterMove error's `replacement` with a `.clone()` insertion at the consume site (`f(v)` -> `f(v.clone())`), GATED on clone-availability via new `moved_type_supports_clone` — true only for `String`, built-in heap collections (Vec/Map/Set/VecDeque), and RC types (shared struct/enum, Rc/Arc). Gate is required: a plain move-only struct has NO `.clone()`, and a blind insertion produced `no method 'clone' on type 'State'` (caught by the concurrent-plain-struct migration cli test, which is back to 4 fixes). The move-semantics is unchanged — the diagnostic still fires; only its machine-fixability was added. VERIFIED end-to-end: `f(a); f(a)` over a Vec now check-errors -> `karac fix` inserts `.clone()` -> re-check clean -> build+run correct + valgrind-clean. Tests: ownership 358 (incl. a new plain-struct no-autofix negative), cli 488, repl 48, rc_fallback 136, codegen 2480, memory_sanitizer 795; clippy + fmt clean.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: LeetCode #137 Single Number II kata (kara-katas). Class false-positive is a loose fit — the precise story is a diagnostics/Mend-loop gap: the diagnostic was correct but not machine-fixable, blocking `karac fix`. NOT a soundness bug (build's advisory handling is by design and memory-safe) and NOT a wrong-answer bug. Fix makes the UAM diagnostic auto-repairable for clone-able types; plain move-only structs keep the suggestion but no auto-fix. Corrects this entry's original mis-framing as a check-vs-build inconsistency.

title: `Iterator.find(pred) -> Option[T]` was unimplemented (rejected `no method 'find' on type 'Iterator'`). Now shipped: typechecker + interpreter (all element types) + codegen (SCALAR payloads; heap elements bail LOUD to `--interp`). Returns the first YIELDED element the predicate holds for, or None.
detail: Class missing-feature (a standard iterator terminal found absent by the floor-method audit; sibling of B-2026-07-19-1 dedup / B-2026-07-19-2 position). (1) typechecker (stdlib_iter.rs) — `find(pred: Fn(T)->bool) -> Option[T]`, records the element TypeExpr span-keyed (same `iter_terminal_elem_types` table as sum/reduce) for codegen, added to the recognized adaptor name list. (2) interpreter (method_call_iter.rs) — short-circuit terminal returning the matched element BY VALUE in the Some payload; all element types (scalar + heap). (3) codegen (method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_find`) — desugars like `position` but stores `Some(elem)` instead of the index: `{ let mut __find: Option[T] = None; for elem in base { <steps>; let param = <adapted-elem>; if pred { __find = Some(param); break; } } __find }`. GATED to scalar (trivially-copyable) payloads via `iter_terminal_elem_types` + `is_trivially_copyable_te`: a heap `Some(elem)` would alias the borrowed source buffer and double-free (the exact reduce/max heap deferral). A heap element (or unpeelable chain) bails LOUD with a clean `--interp` pointer (not the generic 'no handler' fall-through). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for hit / miss / first / filter+find / map+find / range (scalar); heap element defers loud; interp handles String. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2482, interpreter 1251, par_codegen 196, memory_sanitizer 795); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Same floor-method audit as B-2026-07-19-1/-2. The scalar-only codegen gate mirrors the reduce/max heap deferral precedent (a borrowed-element Option payload needs a clone for ownership safety, which is the deferred piece). Tests: test_e2e_iter_chain_find_terminal_scalar, test_e2e_iter_chain_find_heap_element_deferred_loud (codegen), test_iter_find_returns_first_matching_element (interp, incl. String).
resolution: Fixed (2a48965). Typecheck + interp (all types) + codegen (scalar Some(elem) desugar; heap loud-deferred to --interp).

title: String-receiver `"42".parse()` (the Rust-familiar sugar) was rejected (`no method 'parse' on type 'String'`) — only the type-receiver `i64.parse(s) -> Option[T]` existed. Now the string-receiver form works in annotated `Option[T]` positions (type-directed), desugaring to the existing `T.parse(s)`.
detail: Class missing-feature (ergonomic sugar over the existing type-receiver parse; sibling of B-2026-07-19-1/-2/-4). The CORE parse functionality already shipped as `i64.parse("42") -> Option[i64]` (interp + codegen); this adds the string-receiver spelling for the annotated position. (1) typechecker (exprs.rs `try_apply_parse_coercion`, mirroring `try_apply_into_coercion`): a string-receiver `s.parse()` checked against an expected `Option[T]` — T a numeric primitive the type-receiver parse supports (i8..i64 / u8..u64 / usize / f64; isize+f32 excluded to stay in lockstep) — records the target in a NEW `parse_conversions` side-table (threaded typechecker -> TypeCheckResult -> lowering, like `into_conversions`) and returns `Option[T]`; called in check_expr alongside the into/tryinto coercions. (2) lowering (`rewrite_parse_call`): rewrites `s.parse()` to the PATH-call form `T.parse(s)` = `Call(Path([T, parse]))`. CRITICAL: NOT the method-call form `T.parse(s)` with a synthetic `Identifier("i64")` receiver — pipeline order is typecheck -> lower -> ownership, and the ownership MethodCall consume path (`method_call_consumes_receiver` -> `consume_named_binding(root_identifier)`) would fire the String receiver's recorded Owned self-mode on the rewritten `i64` receiver, spuriously reporting `value 'i64' moved ... used again` when parse is used at 2+ sites. A path callee has no receiver for that path to fire on. (3) interpreter (eval_call.rs): added the `<numeric>.parse` PATH-call rule (the method form stays in method_call.rs); codegen already serviced the path form. Works for let annotations, variable receivers, fn-return + argument positions, i64/f64/u32 targets, bad input (None). BOUNDARY (documented): the UNANNOTATED (`let n = s.parse()`) and `.parse().unwrap()`-chained forms are NOT covered (the expected type isn't visible to parse in infer mode / through unwrap) and still use `i64.parse(s)`; the `s.parse[i64]()` explicit-type-param spelling needs parser generic-method-arg support (design.md line 4757). Verified interp == JIT == AOT + ownership-clean; full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2483, interpreter 1252, ownership 795, par_codegen 357, memory_sanitizer 196); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Same floor-method audit as B-2026-07-19-1/-2/-4. Route to the sugar chosen after finding: (a) `.parse[i64]()` doesn't parse as a generic-method call; (b) no `ParseError` type exists; (c) `i64.parse(s)` already covers the functionality. The into-coercion template made the annotated-position sugar a contained, additive win reusing the existing parse machinery. Debugged two rewrite-form pitfalls: the method-call form tripped the ownership consume (fixed by the path form), and the path form initially lacked an interpreter rule (fixed by the eval_call path handler). Tests: test_e2e_string_receiver_parse_sugar (codegen), test_string_receiver_parse_sugar (interp).
resolution: Fixed (89366dd). typecheck coercion (parse_conversions) + lowering path-call rewrite to T.parse(s) + interpreter path-call rule; codegen reuses the existing path-call parse. Annotated-position sugar only; unannotated/.unwrap() use i64.parse(s).

title: A field store of an `Option[shared]` into a Vec-INDEXED shared-struct element (identifier root — `v[i].next = Some(v[j])`, `nodes[i].field = X`) had TWO codegen bugs, both crashing/miscompiling while the interpreter was correct (run-vs-build). (1) OFFSET: the store hardcoded `info.heap_type` + `(idx+1)` for the field GEP; a HEADERLESS shared struct (Phase-D) allocates WITHOUT the leading refcount word, so `(idx+1)` on the header-including type addressed one field too far and the write landed 8 bytes PAST the element block (heap buffer-overflow -> SIGSEGV). The READ path already used the correct `shared_gep_layout` offset, so pre-crash a read returned the stale field. (2) RETAIN: the store was a raw `build_store` with NO RC retain, so the linked node was under-counted and both the field's drop and the Vec element's drop freed it (double free). Both the sibling non-identifier-rooted branch and the bare-identifier branch already routed through `shared_gep_layout` + `emit_[niche_]option_shared_field_store`; this third (identifier-rooted, indexed) path was the odd one out.
investigation: 2026-07-19: surfaced by a LeetCode #138 (Copy List with Random Pointer) kata attempt — a `shared struct Node { val, id, mut next, mut random }` graph built + deep-copied via `copies[i].next = Some(copies[j])` index-store. `karac run --interp` gave the correct sink; `karac build`/`run` SEGFAULTED. MINIMIZED to `let mut v: Vec[Node] = Vec.new(); v.push(..); v.push(..); v[0].next = Some(v[1]); println(id_of(v[0].next))`. valgrind: `Invalid write of size 8, 8 bytes after a block of size 24` — the 24-byte block is the headerless {val,id,next}, and the store GEP'd field (idx+1)=3 => offset 24 (one past). LOCAL `a.next = Some(b)` was fine (bare-identifier path uses shared_gep_layout); `v[i].field` (indexed) was the broken path. FIX (66c27ef): (1) route the field GEP through `shared_gep_layout(seg, info.heap_type)` -> (gep_ty, base) so a headerless layout GEPs at base 0 (idx, not idx+1); (2) for an `Option[shared]` field, dispatch to `emit_niche_option_shared_field_store` / `emit_option_shared_field_store` (retain new inner -> store -> release old), passing the in-scope `rhs_is_fresh`, instead of a raw store. AFTER: the minimized repro and a 5-node linear list (build-by-index-store + walk + drop) are correct AND ASAN/LSan-clean; a new regression asan_vec_indexed_shared_option_field_store_no_crash_no_leak pins it. Gates: codegen 2482, memory_sanitizer 796, par_codegen 196; clippy + fmt clean. SCOPED-OUT RESIDUAL (not a codegen bug): a graph with back-pointers (the #138 `random` field) forms REFERENCE-COUNT CYCLES, which RC leaks by construction (same as Rust's `Rc` — you would use a `Weak` ref to break the cycle). The #138 kata is therefore held pending a decision on cycle handling (Kāra has a `Weak[T]` tier); the acyclic case is fully fixed here.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced by a #138 Copy List with Random Pointer kata attempt (kara-katas), held because its natural cyclic structure leaks under RC. Class miscompile (silent heap-overflow + double-free on a run-vs-build path). Same family as the SoA field-store offset bugs (B-2026-06-20-7) and the shared-element index-assign leak (B-2026-07-12-29): the identifier-rooted indexed-shared field STORE was the last of the three field-store branches still hardcoding the header offset + skipping the retain. RESIDUAL RC-cycle leak is a language property (weak refs), not tracked as a codegen defect.

title: `Iterator.last() -> Option[T]` and `Iterator.nth(n) -> Option[T]` were unimplemented (rejected `no method 'last'/'nth' on type 'Iterator'`). Now shipped across all three layers: `last` returns the last yielded element (or None); `nth(n)` returns the 0-based n-th yielded element (or None).
detail: Class missing-feature (element-returning iterator terminals found absent by the floor-method audit; siblings of B-2026-07-19-4 find). (1) typechecker (stdlib_iter.rs) — both `-> Option[T]`; record the element TypeExpr span-keyed (like find) for the codegen scalar gate; added to the recognized adaptor name list. (2) interpreter (method_call_iter.rs) — `last` drains keeping the last yield; `nth` returns the n-th yield (negative n -> None immediately); all element types. (3) codegen (method_call.rs `try_compile_iter_chain_last_nth`) — desugars to the fused for-loop like find: `last` assigns `__r = Some(elem)` every iteration (no break); `nth` binds `let __nb = n` + a running `__i` and, at `__i == __nb`, assigns `Some(elem)` and breaks, else `__i += 1`. SCALAR payloads only (iter_terminal_elem_types + is_trivially_copyable_te gate); a heap element bails LOUD to `--interp` (the find/reduce/max heap deferral). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for last (basic/empty/filter/map) and nth (basic/oob/index-0/filter/range); heap last defers loud; interp handles String. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2484, interpreter 1253, memory_sanitizer 795); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Second floor-method probe sweep (after the B-2026-07-19-1..5 batch) surfaced last/nth/flatten/split_off/replacen as the remaining gaps. last/nth implemented first (closest to the find engine). Tests: test_e2e_iter_chain_last_nth_terminals (codegen), test_iter_last_and_nth_terminals (interp).
resolution: Fixed (77cd44c). Typecheck + interp (all types) + codegen (scalar Some(elem) desugar; heap loud-deferred).

fix: e119392 (typecheck + store/read/drop codegen, slices 2-4) + 8f606de (indexed receivers + read-drift fix, slice 5); runtime + layout groundwork edd99f9 (slice 1a) / 85080f1 (slice 1b)
title: `weak T` struct fields are DECLARATION-ONLY: the modifier parses, type-lowers to `Type::Weak`, and satisfies the ownership cycle checker (`struct Child { parent: weak Parent }` — test_weak_breaks_cycle passes), but there is NO way to construct, assign, or access a weak-field VALUE. design.md § Cycles specifies full Swift-like semantics ('the `weak` annotation breaks back-edges, returns `Option[T]` on access'), none of which is implemented: no `weak <expr>` downgrade expression (the `weak` keyword is only a TYPE-position token), no `.downgrade()`/`.weak()` method, no coercion from a strong `T`/`Option[T]`, and no codegen. A `weak T` field therefore can only ever be declared — a struct/shared-struct that has one cannot be constructed, making the feature unusable at runtime.
investigation:
2026-07-19: probed after the #138 (Copy List with Random Pointer) kata was held on an RC-cycle leak (B-2026-07-19-6 residual) — the idiomatic RC fix is a non-owning `random: weak Node` back-edge to break the cycle. Empirically, EVERY value form for a `pw: weak P` field is rejected: `pw: p` (strong P) -> 'expected weak P, found P' (no downgrade coercion); `pw: Some(p)` -> 'expected weak P, found Option

'; `pw: weak p` -> parse error ('weak' reserved, not an expr prefix); `pw: p.weak()` -> parse error (weak can't be a method name); `pw: p.downgrade()` -> 'no method downgrade on type P'. `Token::Weak` appears in the parser only in `starts_type` (type-position detection), and there is no `downgrade`/`make_weak`/weak-value construction anywhere in typechecker or codegen; no test in the suite constructs, assigns, or reads a weak field (only 4 tests, all pure DECLARATIONS: parser.rs/lexer.rs/selfhost_parser_types.rs parse-only + ownership.rs test_weak_breaks_cycle). So `weak` is a compile-time cycle-checker annotation with no runtime realization. IMPACT: cyclic shared-struct graphs (linked list with back-pointers, tree with parent pointers, graph adjacency) cannot be made leak-free — the documented escape hatch (weak back-edges) is unusable, so such structures leak under RC (B-2026-07-19-6's residual). NEEDED (design.md § Cycles scope): a weak-value DOWNGRADE expression/method producing `Type::Weak(T)` from a strong `T`, an ACCESS that returns `Option[T]` (Swift-like — None once the strong count hits zero), a null/empty-weak for construction, and codegen (a weak slot that does NOT contribute to the strong refcount, with the upgrade nil-check). SCOPE NOTE: a full weak-ref implementation (weak counts, upgrade, dead detection) is a real feature slice, not a quick fix; filed open.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probed while evaluating whether kata #138 could be made cycle-free (surfaced by B-2026-07-19-6's held kata). Class missing-feature — `weak T` is parseable + cycle-checker-honored but has zero value semantics (construct/assign/access/codegen all absent), contradicting design.md § Cycles which specifies Swift-like `weak` returning `Option[T]` on access. Blocks leak-free cyclic shared-struct graphs. Not a regression; the modifier was declaration-only from the start. DESIGN: docs/spikes/weak-refs.md lays out the two-count control-block implementation (force-headed weak-targeted types, downgrade/upgrade/weak-drop runtime primitives, the shared_gep_layout base bump, and a 5-slice LSan-gated plan). FIXED 2026-07-19: implemented end-to-end (interpreter, JIT, AOT) via the docs/spikes/weak-refs.md 5-slice plan — a two-count `{strong, weak}` control block (runtime/src/weak.rs), force-headed weak-targeted types with base-2 `shared_gep_layout`, typechecker `Option[T]` read typing + downgrade store coercion, and downgrade/upgrade(borrow-model, liveness-checked)/weak-drop codegen. Unblocked kata #138 (Copy List with Random Pointer): a `random`-cyclic graph now reclaims leak-free. Deferred: the atomic `par`/Arc weak sibling (a `weak <par T>` target is rejected until then).

title: `Vec[T].split_off(i) -> Vec[T]` was unimplemented. Now shipped across all three layers: split at index i, self keeps [0, i), the returned Vec owns [i, len); i clamps to [0, len].
detail: Class missing-feature (floor Vec method found absent by the audit; sibling of B-2026-07-19-1 dedup). (1) ast.rs — `split_off` added to the mutating-method list (it truncates self). (2) typechecker (expr_method_call.rs) — `split_off(i: i64) -> Vec[T]` on Vec[T]/VecDeque[T]. (3) interpreter (method_call_seq.rs) — snapshot, Rust `Vec::split_off` at the clamped index, write back the head, return the tail as a fresh `Value::Array`. (4) codegen (vec_method.rs) — clamp i to [0, len]; `malloc` a fresh tail buffer of (len-i) elements; `build_memcpy` the [i, len) tail into it (byte-copy — heap elements MOVE, no clone); set `self.len = i` so the moved tail is excluded from self's scope-exit drop; build+return the tail Vec `{buf, len-i, len-i}` via get_undef + insert_value (the vec-literal construction pattern). No drop-glue: each element frees exactly once (the returned Vec owns the moved tail, self's shortened len covers the rest). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for scalar splits, index clamping (0/len/oob), heap Vec[String] (both halves usable), consuming both halves; LSan-clean for the heap-tail move (asan_vec_split_off_moves_heap_tail_no_leak_no_double_free). Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2485, interpreter 1254, ownership 797, memory_sanitizer); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Second floor-method probe sweep. split_off codegen modeled on Vec.truncate (len-adjust) + the vec-literal alloc/build pattern (malloc + memcpy + insert_value). Tests: test_e2e_vec_split_off_scalar_and_heap (codegen), test_vec_split_off_splits_at_index (interp), asan_vec_split_off_moves_heap_tail_no_leak_no_double_free.
resolution: Fixed (293b6b5). Typecheck + interp + codegen (malloc+memcpy tail, self.len=i, move-not-clone, heap-safe).

title: `String.replacen(from, to, n) -> String` was unimplemented (only `replace` existed). Now shipped across all three layers: replace at most the first `n` non-overlapping occurrences; a negative count clamps to 0 (replace nothing).
detail: Class missing-feature (floor String method found absent by the audit; sibling of the runtime-backed `replace`). Design choice: `n` is an i64 count, and a negative value clamps to 0 (replace nothing) — the conservative interpretation that avoids feeding a wrapped/underflowed value into a usize count; documented identically in all three layers. (1) runtime (clone.rs) — `karac_string_replacen` mirrors `karac_string_replace` with a trailing `n: i64` param, `count = if n < 0 { 0 } else { n as usize }`, `haystack.replacen(from, to, count)`; added to the lib.rs `__preserve_no_mangle_symbols` keep-list. (2) typechecker (stdlib_seq.rs, infer_str_method) — `replacen(from: String, to: String, n: int) -> String`; first two args str-like, third an integer. (3) interpreter (method_call_seq.rs) — `str::replacen` at the clamped count. (4) codegen (vec_method.rs) — declare `karac_string_replacen_fn` (field + module.add_function + init assignment in codegen.rs); route through `build_string_xform_result` with the extra count operand after the two (ptr,len) arg pairs; fresh-owned f-string args freed via `free_fresh_owned_str_arg`; `replacen` registered as receiver-independent in the fresh-temp heap-string cleanup gate (method_call.rs) and in `closure_body_produces_heap_string` (calls.rs). Requires the runtime archive rebuilt (new symbol) for codegen E2E. Verified interp == JIT == AOT for partial/over/zero/negative counts and length-changing replacements; LSan-clean over a looped heap workload (asan_string_replacen_heap_no_leak). Full --features llvm typechecker (2073) + string-scoped codegen/interpreter suites green; clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Floor-method audit follow-on to `replace`. replacen codegen modeled directly on the `replace` arm (same (ptr,len) receiver+arg extraction) plus one i64 count operand. Tests: e2e_string_replacen_codegen (codegen), test_string_replacen_interpreter (interp), asan_string_replacen_heap_no_leak (asan).
resolution: Fixed (9a21d56). Typecheck + interp + codegen (runtime-backed karac_string_replacen, negative count clamps to 0).

title: `Iterator.rev()` codegen residual (B-2026-07-18-41) — a BARE range base `(a..b).rev()` / `(a..=b).rev()` was loud-deferred to `--interp`. Now SHIPPED via a descending `compile_for_range` loop; covers the for-loop plus collect/fold/sum/count terminals and rev-under-map.
detail: Class missing-feature (the follow-up slice named in B-2026-07-18-41's RESIDUAL note: extend the reverse-aware base from a bound-Vec identifier to a range). The rev codegen leg strips `.rev()`, sets the one-shot `pending_reverse_iter` signal, and re-dispatches the stripped chain; previously only `compile_for_vec_var` consumed the signal, so a range base fell through to the loud `--interp` bail. Now `compile_for_range_with_step` also consumes it (nesting-safe `std::mem::take`) and emits a DESCENDING loop: init = `end-1` (exclusive) / `end` (inclusive), condition `i >= start` (SGE), decrement by the unit step. SOUNDNESS: reversal only reorders the value set `[start, end)` — every value is still visited — so the bounds-check-elision facts (`collect_asserted_bounds_from_for_range`, `start <= i < end`) that let `v[i]` inside the body skip its check stay valid unchanged. GATING: `rev_chain_reverse_iterable` accepts a range ONLY when it sits DIRECTLY under the `.rev()` (`matches!(object.kind, ExprKind::Range)`); an adaptor between rev and the range (`(a..b).step_by(k).rev()`, `(a..b).map(f).rev()`) routes through the fused-chain for-loop, NOT `compile_for_range`, so it would leave the signal unconsumed — those stay a loud defer (the `consumed` post-check is the safety net; never a silent forward iteration). BONUS REACH: because collect/fold/sum/count over a range and `rev().map(f)` also bottom out in `compile_for_range` (via the fused terminal desugar `for elem in <range>`), `(a..b).rev().collect()`, `.fold()`, `.sum()`, `.count()`, and `(a..b).rev().map(f).collect()` now work too (fold genuinely reverses: `(0..4).rev().fold(0,|a,x| a*10+x)` = 3210). Forward ranges are byte-identical IR (reverse=false → init=start, original pred/bound, original nsw/add increment). Verified interp == JIT == AOT (exclusive / inclusive / empty / nested / vec-index in reverse + all four terminals); LSan-clean over a looped reverse-order heap-index MOVE (`for i in (0..3).rev() { d.push(v[i]) }` over Vec[String], asan_iter_rev_range_reverse_iterate). Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2489, interpreter 1257, memory_sanitizer); clippy/fmt clean.
investigation: Follow-up to B-2026-07-18-41 (the range/temp residual explicitly deferred there). Confirmed the descending-loop rewrite is contained to compile_for_range_with_step and that forward IR is unchanged when reverse=false. Verified the still-deferred residuals (step_by+rev, map-OVER-rev, temp-Vec source) all bail loud with the rev+`--interp` message, never silent. Tests: e2e_iter_rev_range_reverse_iterate + updated e2e_iter_rev_deferred_loud_message (codegen), test_iter_rev_range_interpreter (interp), asan_iter_rev_range_reverse_iterate (asan). Remaining residual: a TEMP/literal Vec source (`vec![..].iter().rev()`) and any positional-adaptor+rev combination (semantically not a plain reverse).
resolution: Fixed (20bcdc5). Codegen descending compile_for_range loop for a bare range base; consumes pending_reverse_iter; BCE facts preserved; forward IR unchanged. Extends B-2026-07-18-41.

title: `Iterator.flatten()` was unimplemented — `xs.iter().flatten()` rejected with `no method 'flatten' on type 'Iterator'`. SHIPPED across typecheck + interp + codegen: an iterator of iterables yields the inner elements in order (≡ flat_map(|x| x)). Codegen covers the for-loop AND the fused terminals (collect/sum/count/fold, incl. adaptor-then-terminal); only a bare materialized flatten VALUE still loud-defers to `--interp`.
detail: Class missing-feature (a genuinely absent combinator; `flatten` existed only for Option/Result, not Iterator). Staged like `rev` (B-2026-07-18-41): interp+typecheck first, codegen a follow-up. (1) typecheck (stdlib_iter.rs infer_iterator_method) — `flatten() -> Iterator[U]` where the receiver element is a Vec / array / slice / Iterator (possibly behind an `.iter()` borrow); the new element type is `element_type_of(item)` (peels the borrow, preserves the borrow-aware element form so it types exactly like the nested `for inner in outer { for x in inner }` it desugars to); a non-iterable element is a TypeMismatch; added to the recognized-adaptor name list. (2) interp (method_call_iter.rs) — EAGER drain-flatten-replay (mirrors rev's drain-reverse-replay, NOT a lazy per-element step): drain the outer via iterator_step (firing every upstream adaptor closure), expand each inner iterable through the new `flatten_one_inner` helper (Value::Array clone / Slice range / nested Value::Iterator drained), return a fresh `IteratorSource::Eager`; composes with adaptors and terminals on BOTH sides and empty inners contribute nothing. (3) codegen — LOUD-defer only (slice 2 TODO): guards naming flatten + `--interp` in `compile_for` (before the silent `_ =>` fall-through that would drop a `for x in xs.iter().flatten()` body → empty output vs interp) and in `compile_method_call`. CRITICAL PLACEMENT: the method-call guard sits AFTER the Option/Result combinator dispatch (`try_compile_option_result_method`), because `flatten` is ALSO an Option/Result combinator (`Option[Option[T]].flatten()` / `Result.flatten()`, which codegen fully supports) — placing the guard before it regressed `test_e2e_option_result_combinators_nonclosure` (caught in the full-suite run). After the dispatch, a non-Option/Result receiver has already fallen through, so any `.flatten()` reaching the guard is an ITERATOR flatten. Verified interp correct across for-loop / collect / map / filter+sum / count / String elements / empty inners; codegen bails loud for every shape (for-loop, collect, sum, map-over-flatten) and Option/Result flatten still compiles (direct + chained with unwrap/is_none). Full --features llvm suites green (typecheck 2073, interpreter 1258, codegen 2490); clippy/fmt clean. — SLICE 2 (1f1e879): codegen for the `for <pat> in <recv>.flatten()` shape via `try_compile_for_flatten` — a nested-loop desugar `for __flt in <recv> { for <pat> in __flt { body } }` (flatten is flat_map with an identity inner, so the outer loop binds each inner iterable and the inner loop yields its elements), reusing the flat_map desugar's label/break/continue rewriting (user label on the outer loop; unlabeled break exits the whole flat sequence; labeled continue renamed to the inner loop). Gated on the receiver passing the flat_map inner-iterable whitelist; fails closed to the loud defer. Wired into compile_for BEFORE the loud-defer guard, only when flatten is the OUTERMOST call on the iterable. Verified interp == JIT == AOT for scalar / String elements / empty inners / break / continue; LSan-clean over a looped heap flatten-and-clone (asan_iter_flatten_for_loop_heap_no_leak). Full codegen suite green (2492). RESIDUAL (slice 3): the fused TERMINALS over flatten (`.flatten().collect()`/`.sum()`/`.fold()`/`.count()`) and flatten-not-outermost chains still loud-defer. — SLICE 3 (ffd6384): codegen for the fused TERMINALS over flatten. (a) `peel_base_is_structural_adaptor` now recognizes a bare `flatten()` as a valid fused BASE (`for_loop_iterates_flatten`), so the peel-based terminals (fold/sum/count/product/reduce/for_each/any/all AND the general adaptor collect engine's map/filter-then-collect) treat a flatten receiver as a zero-step structural base — the synthesized `for elem in <flatten-chain>` routes through the slice-2 flatten for-loop desugar. (b) `try_compile_flatten_collect`: a dedicated intercept for the bare `<recv>.flatten().collect()` (the general collect engine's own base-peel doesn't recognize a flatten base), building a single accumulating loop over the flatten chain, result type from owned_temp_drops. The compile_method_call guard is narrowed to a BARE flatten VALUE (materialized `let it = xs.flatten()` with no driver) which still loud-defers; the for-loop UNLOWERED_FOR_ADAPTORS backstop lists `flatten` so any unlowered flatten for-loop shape bails loud. Verified interp == JIT == AOT for collect/sum/count/fold/map-collect/filter-sum; LSan-clean over a looped heap flatten-collect. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2493, interpreter 1258, memory_sanitizer 800). RESIDUAL: only a bare materialized flatten iterator VALUE (`let it = x.flatten(); for i in it`) still loud-defers (same materialized-binding limitation as bare rev).
investigation: Floor-stdlib dogfood sweep — flatten was the remaining common iterator combinator typecheck rejected outright. Confirmed the interpreter's lazy streaming model can carry flatten as an eager drain (like rev), and that codegen's forward-only fused-chain desugar can't yet express the nested iteration — deferred loud (slice 2). The Option/Result-flatten codegen collision was found by the full codegen suite (test_e2e_option_result_combinators_nonclosure) and fixed by ordering the guard after the Option/Result dispatch. Tests: test_iter_flatten_interpreter (interp), e2e_iter_flatten_deferred_loud_message (codegen). RESIDUAL (slice 2): codegen lowering — the for-loop shape desugars to the flat_map nested loop (`for inner in xs { for x in inner { body } }`, reusing try_compile_for_flat_map), and `.flatten().collect()` to the fused terminal.
resolution: Fixed slices 1+2+3 (0425a45, 1f1e879, ffd6384). Typecheck + interp (eager drain-flatten) + codegen for the for-loop AND the fused terminals (collect/sum/count/fold, incl. adaptor-then-terminal). Only a bare materialized flatten value still loud-defers.

title: Indexed-shared-struct field READ (`nodes[i].field`) hardcoded heap offset `idx + 1` instead of routing through `shared_gep_layout`, so it mis-read any shared struct whose box base is not 1: a WEAK-HEADERED type (`{ strong, weak, fields… }`, base 2) read the weak COUNT where the value should be (`orig[i].val` -> `1`), and a HEADERLESS type (base 0) read one word too far. Silent run-vs-build divergence — the interpreter and the BARE-receiver read (which already used `shared_gep_layout`) were correct, so `nodes[i].field` disagreed with `node.field` on the same value.
investigation: 2026-07-19: surfaced by kata #138's deep-copy (`copies.push(Node { val: orig[i].val, … })`): `orig[i].val` on a weak-headered `Node` came back as `1` (the weak count at heap index 1) instead of the real value at index 2 under `karac build`/`run`, while `karac run --interp` was correct — a run-vs-build divergence. Root cause: the indexed-shared field-READ branch in `compile_field_access` GEP'd with a hardcoded `(idx + 1)` against `info.heap_type`, the exact drift the STORE side had at B-2026-07-19-6 (which was fixed to route through `shared_gep_layout`). The read branch was never updated, so the two sides diverged the moment a non-base-1 layout existed. Weak refs (B-2026-07-19-8) introduced the base-2 weak header, making the latent read bug observable; headerless types (base 0) were also mis-read but the shape rarely hits an indexed read. FIX: compute `(gep_ty, base) = shared_gep_layout(seg, info.heap_type)` and GEP at `idx + base` with `gep_ty` (also sourcing the loaded field type from `gep_ty`), so the indexed read matches the bare read and the store side through the one funnel.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #138 (Copy List with Random Pointer), the first `weak`-ref kata. Class miscompile (wrong field offset -> wrong value, run-vs-build divergence). Same drift class as B-2026-07-19-6 (the store-side twin); the fix is the same `shared_gep_layout` funnel. Fixing it also corrects the headerless-type indexed read (base 0) as a bonus. Regression guard: tests/memory_sanitizer.rs::asan_weak_field_indexed_deep_copy_no_leak.

title: Iterator predicate/Option-adaptor cluster UNIMPLEMENTED: `filter_map`, `find_map`, `partition` are rejected `no method '<name>' on type 'Iterator'` in all backends, while the sibling adaptors `flat_map` / `scan` / `position` / `find` / `last` are present. `v.iter().filter_map(|t| match t { ... => Some(..), _ => None })` (a common filter+map fusion over an enum) has no path — the user must fall back to a manual loop or `.filter(...).map(...).unwrap()`.
detail: Class missing-feature. Found by a fresh dogfooding parity sweep (enum tokenizer filter_map + Result/Vec pipelines). Survey with correct signatures: PRESENT — `map`/`filter`/`fold`/`enumerate`/`zip`/`rev`/`flat_map`/`scan`/`position`/`find`/`last`/`collect`/`sum`/`count`/`any`/`all`; ABSENT — `filter_map` (map+filter fusion, `Fn(T)->Option[U]`), `find_map` (first `Some` from a `Fn(T)->Option[U]`, terminal), `partition` (split into two collections by a `Fn(T)->bool`, terminal returning `(C, C)`). Each is caught cleanly at typecheck by the B-2026-07-17-12 loud-unknown-method guard (`no method '<name>' on type 'Iterator'`), so this is a genuine end-to-end gap, not a silent-Error hole. `filter_map` is the highest-value (the idiomatic filter+transform); `find_map`/`partition` complete the predicate-adaptor family. Each is its own fused-chain slice (typecheck signature in stdlib_seq.rs + interp in the iterator eval + codegen fused-chain peel), mirroring how `flat_map` / `scan` / `take_while` landed as individual B-8-tail slices. FIX DIRECTION: add `filter_map` first as a fused map+filter step (closure returns `Option[U]`, keep the `Some` payloads); then `find_map` as an early-exit terminal and `partition` as a two-accumulator terminal. PROGRESS (f99553e): `filter_map` landed for TYPECHECK + INTERP — the iterator-method gate + a `stdlib_iter.rs` arm (closure `Option[U]` return -> chain element `U`, non-Option = TypeMismatch, mirroring `flat_map`) + an `IteratorStep::FilterMap` interp step (yield `Some` payload, drop `None`; correct for scalar AND heap `U`). PROGRESS (867916e): `filter_map` CODEGEN now landed too — full interp/JIT/AOT parity. Fused-chain step (`FusedStepKind::FilterMap`) lowered to a synthesized `match f(x) { Some(v) => <rest-of-chain>, None => {} }` reusing the Option-match codegen (covers all fused terminals + the for-over-chain desugar); the deferred heap/narrow/float payload sizing is solved via the same span-registration trick `reduce` uses — a deterministic `filter_map_bind_span` (derived from the closure body's span) stamps the synthetic `Some(v)` binding, and the typechecker's `filter_map` arm registers the payload's surface type at that same span so `reconstruct_payload_value` materializes a heap `U`/narrow/float correctly (raw-i64 default was the earlier heap-garbage bug). `<filter_map-chain>.collect()` routes through the fused for-loop lowering via a `Vec[U]` push accumulator (`try_compile_filter_map_collect`) since the general collect engine's separate peel has no `filter_map`. `filter_map` removed from `UNLOWERED_FOR_ADAPTORS` + added to `fusable_outer`. New codegen E2E `test_e2e_filter_map_scalar_and_heap` + ASAN/LSan `asan_filter_map_heap_collect_no_leak`. So `filter_map` is fully DONE (typecheck f99553e + interp f99553e + codegen 867916e). PROGRESS (d60138f): `find_map` now landed too across ALL THREE backends. `find_map(f: Fn(T) -> Option[U]) -> Option[U]` — first `Some(u)` the closure produces, or `None` (map+find fusion). Typecheck: closure `Fn(T)->Option[U]` (non-Option = TypeMismatch), result `Option[U]`; U registered in `iter_terminal_elem_types` (Option[U] annotation) + at the deterministic payload-binding span (Some(v) sizing), same trick filter_map uses; added to the iterator-surface gate + require_known_method survey (backfilled the missing `filter_map` survey entry too). Interp: first `Some` payload (scalar AND heap U). Codegen: desugars like `find` but the sink is a synthesized `match f(x) { Some(v) => { __fm = Some(v); break }, None => {} }`; trivially-copyable payload U only (i64/f64/narrow) — a heap U (String/Vec) bails LOUD to `--interp` exactly like `find`'s scalar gate (no silent miscompile). So `find_map` is DONE. PROGRESS (242c07c): `partition` now landed too — the WHOLE cluster is complete and this entry is CLOSED. `partition(pred: Fn(T) -> bool) -> (Vec[T], Vec[T])` — eager two-collection terminal (matches, non-matches). Typecheck: result `(Vec[T], Vec[T])`, T recorded in `iter_terminal_elem_types`; added to the iterator-surface gate + require_known_method survey. Interp: split the drained elements into two vecs (scalar AND heap T). Codegen: desugars to two `Vec[T]` push accumulators over the fused for-loop returning a `(__pt, __pf)` tuple (the verified block-returns-tuple-of-owned-Vecs path); trivially-copyable element T only — a heap T (String/Vec) bails LOUD to `--interp` (each element would need a clone into a target Vec), no silent miscompile. New typecheck/interp/codegen-E2E/ASAN tests. SUMMARY: filter_map (typecheck+interp f99553e, codegen 867916e) + find_map (d60138f) + partition (242c07c) — all three implemented across typecheck+interp+codegen with interp/JIT/AOT parity (heap payloads defer loud to interp for the find_map/partition/collect-of-heap edges, matching the pre-existing `find` scalar gate). Cluster done.
investigation: Repro: `fn main() { let v = [1,2,3]; let _r: Vec[i64] = v.iter().filter_map(|x| Some(x + 0)).collect(); }` -> `error[typecheck]: no method 'filter_map' on type 'Iterator'`. Same for `find_map`, `partition`. `flat_map`/`scan`/`position`/`last` all typecheck clean, confirming the gap is these three specifically.

tracker: src/codegen/vec_method.rs (Vec method dispatch — `sorted` falls to the 'not yet supported' tail; in-place `sort` is fully implemented)
title: `Vec[T].sorted()` (immutable sort returning a NEW Vec) is UNIMPLEMENTED in codegen for every element type — it falls to the generic 'Vec/String method \'sorted\' is not yet supported in codegen' error, while in-place `Vec[T].sort()` IS fully supported (integer/String/float/tuple/nested-Vec via the default-order thunks). The interpreter implements `sorted()` for all these element types, so a program using `v.sorted()` runs under `karac run --interp` but fails BOTH `karac build` and `karac run` (JIT). A `String` var's `.sorted()` (sort the CHARS) is separately handled (routes to `karac_string_sorted`); this gap is specifically the `Vec[T].sorted()` value-returning form.
investigation: 2026-07-19: surfaced by kata #140 (Word Break II), which sorts its result sentences for a deterministic mirror: `return sentences.sorted();` over a `Vec[String]` type-checks and runs correctly under `--interp` but `karac build`/`run` both fail at codegen with 'Vec/String method \'sorted\' is not yet supported in codegen'. Confirmed the gap is element-type-independent: even `Vec[i64].sorted()` hits the same error. Root: `vec_method.rs`'s dispatch has a full in-place `"sort"` arm (clones nothing; sorts the backing buffer in place via `emit_default_sort_thunk` / `_string` / the `karac_cmp_<T>` family) but no `"sorted"` arm, so it reaches the catch-all unsupported tail. FIX (deferred): implement `sorted()` = deep-clone the Vec (`emit_vec_clone_fn(elem_te)`, which already handles per-element String/heap clone) into a fresh temp, run the SAME in-place sort emission on the clone, and return the cloned+sorted value. The building blocks (`emit_vec_clone_fn` + the `sort` thunks) both exist; the work is factoring the `sort` arm — currently coupled to the receiver `var_name`/`data_ptr` alloca — to operate on an arbitrary cloned Vec value. WORKAROUND in the kata: use in-place `.sort()` then return (the natural sort-before-return idiom; fully supported).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #140 (Word Break II). Class codegen-gap (interp-only method, run-vs-build divergence at the codegen error boundary — build/JIT reject, interp runs). Low severity: in-place `.sort()` is a fully-supported idiomatic equivalent for the common 'sort then use' pattern, so the gap rarely blocks real code; it only bites the immutable `let s = v.sorted()` form where `v` must stay unsorted. Not a regression — `sorted()` was never lowered for Vec. Building blocks for the fix (emit_vec_clone_fn + sort thunks) already exist.
resolution: Fixed (c6848c4). Lowered `Vec[T].sorted()` by desugaring to `{ let mut tmp: Vec[E] = v.clone(); tmp.sort(); tmp }` in vec_method.rs — reusing the deep-clone (emit_clone_fn_for_type_expr) and the in-place `sort` arm, so every element-type comparator sort supports (signed/unsigned int, String, float, tuple, nested-Vec) applies to the clone identically; an unorderable element fails LOUD via sort's own error. The synthetic block is built with a unique usize::MAX-based span so no typechecker side-table is hit (the let carries an explicit annotation; sort reads var_elem_type_exprs). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for i64/u64(unsigned order)/String/float, receiver stays unsorted; LSan-clean over a looped heap Vec[String].sorted() (asan_vec_sorted_heap_clone_no_leak_no_double_free). Full --features llvm codegen suite green (2494).

title: Iterating a `Vec` that lives in a TUPLE element (`t.0.iter()`, `t.0.iter().fold(..)`) silently iterated ZERO times under codegen (JIT + native) while the tree-walk interpreter iterated the real elements — a silent wrong-answer miscompile. `for x in t.0.iter() { .. }` never ran its body, and every fused terminal that desugars to that for-loop shape (`fold`/`sum`/…) returned the accumulator's init unchanged (e.g. `t.0.iter().fold(0, |a,x| a+x)` → 0, not the real sum).
detail: Class miscompile (silent wrong answer, no diagnostic). Found by a fresh dogfooding composition probe (iterator adaptors + tuples). ROOT CAUSE: the for-loop `.iter()`/`.into_iter()` dispatch (`compile_for` in control_flow_for.rs) peels the transparent iterator wrapper and routes the receiver by shape — an `Index` receiver (`coll[i].iter()`) to `compile_for_indexed_iter`, a `FieldAccess` receiver (`obj.field.iter()`) to `try_compile_for_field_iter` — but had NO `TupleIndex` sibling. So `t.0.iter()` fell to the generic `compile_for(object)` recurse on the bare `TupleIndex`, which the dispatcher has no arm for, hitting the silent `_ =>` default that skips the body (the same silent-skip class as the historical field-iter and indexed-iter gaps). Because `try_compile_iter_chain_fold` / `sum` synthesize a `for <elem> in <base>` over the peeled base and compile it through the same `compile_for`, the identical hole zeroed those terminals too. Struct-field iteration (`t.a.iter()`) was unaffected (the `FieldAccess` arm handled it), so the bug was specific to tuple-element sources. FIX (eeee817), two contained pieces: (1) codegen — a `TupleIndex` receiver arm calling `try_compile_for_tuple_index_iter`, which GEPs into the tuple's inline `{ptr,len,cap}` element storage via the structural place resolvers `field_chain_place_ptr` + `place_chain_aggregate_llvm_type` (pure LLVM-type walks, so they work for an inferred `let t = f()` binding as well as an annotated one — no dependence on the lossy per-var tuple name registry), mints a read-only synth identifier aliasing that storage (no drop registered — `.iter()` borrows, the tuple owns and frees its Vec once at scope exit), and recurses through the proven vec-var for-loop path. (2) typechecker — the element's FULL `TypeExpr` is needed to size the loop binding, but the per-var tuple registry (`tuple_var_elem_type_names`) is lossy (stores only type-name segments, hardcodes `generic_args: None`, and isn't populated at all for a Call-RHS binding), so codegen can't recover `Vec[i64]` from it (only bare `Vec`). Instead the typechecker records the element type in the span-keyed `temp_recv_elem_types` table for a `TupleIndex` `.iter()`/`.into_iter()` receiver, keyed by the receiver span — which the parser makes EQUAL to the `.iter()` MethodCall span — exactly where the codegen helper reads it (`temp_recv_elem_types[tuple_index.span]`). `Vec`/`VecDeque` receivers only. VERIFIED: interpreter / JIT / native agree for scalar (i64) and heap (`String`) elements, over both inferred and annotated tuple bindings; valgrind- and ASAN/LSan-clean for the heap-element case (read-only alias → no double-free, no leak). TESTS: codegen E2E `e2e_iter_over_tuple_element_vec` (for-loop + fold, scalar + String, inferred + annotated) and memsan `asan_iter_over_tuple_element_vec_no_leak`. RESIDUAL: a `Slice` tuple element (distinct `{ptr,len}` header) still falls through (the reconstruct-as-`Vec` helper doesn't model it) — rare; and see B-2026-07-20-2 for the sibling `t.0[i]` index gap (loud, separate path).
investigation: Repro: `fn make() -> (Vec[i64], Vec[i64]) { let mut a: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); a.push(10); a.push(20); (a, Vec.new()) } fn main() { let t = make(); let s = t.0.iter().fold(0, |acc, x| acc + x); println(s); }` → interp prints 30, `karac build`/JIT printed 0. `for x in t.0.iter() { .. }` likewise ran the body 0 times under codegen. Control: `struct P { a: Vec[i64] } … for x in p.a.iter()` worked on all backends (FieldAccess arm), pinning the gap to tuple-element sources.

title: Indexing a `Vec` that lives in a TUPLE element (`t.0[i]`) fails codegen LOUD — `error: codegen failed: Index operator applied to non-array type` (JIT + native) — while the tree-walk interpreter reads the element correctly. Fails closed with the `--interp` hint (not a silent miscompile), but a genuine run-vs-build gap: `t.0.len()` and now `t.0.iter()` (B-2026-07-20-1) work, so a program that reads `t.0.len()` then indexes `t.0[i]` in the same scope compiles the former and rejects the latter.
detail: Class codegen-gap (loud, fails closed — distinct from the SILENT B-2026-07-20-1 iterator sibling). Found in the same dogfooding probe. The index-expression codegen (`compile_index` / `lower_field_access_ptr`'s index arm in collections.rs) resolves a Vec/array root from an Identifier, `coll[i]`, or a `FieldAccess` (struct field), but has no `TupleIndex` root — so `t.0[i]` hits the generic 'non-array type' bail. WORKAROUND: destructure first (`let (a, b) = t; a[i]` — `a` is then a plain Vec binding that indexes fine), or `--interp`. FIX DIRECTION: mirror B-2026-07-20-1's structural resolution — GEP into the tuple element via `field_chain_place_ptr` + `place_chain_aggregate_llvm_type` to reach the element's `{ptr,len,cap}`, then reuse the existing Vec index-load/bounds-check on that pointer. Lower priority than the miscompile leg because it is LOUD (no wrong answer) and the destructure workaround is idiomatic. FIXED (8efca21): a `TupleIndex`-rooted index arm in `compile_index` (collections.rs) GEPs into the tuple element's `{ptr,len,cap}` storage (structural, works for inferred + annotated bindings), mints a read-only synth identifier, and recurses through the identifier-keyed Vec index dispatch; the typechecker records the element `TypeExpr` in `temp_recv_elem_types` for a `TupleIndex` index receiver (keyed by the receiver span, `Vec`/`VecDeque` only). Verified interp/JIT/native agree for scalar + `String` reads over an inferred binding, valgrind-clean. New codegen E2E `e2e_index_into_tuple_element_vec`. Sibling store/method-on-indexed gaps split out to B-2026-07-20-3 / B-2026-07-20-4.
investigation: Repro: `fn make() -> (Vec[i64], Vec[i64]) { let mut a: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); a.push(10); (a, Vec.new()) } fn main() { let t = make(); println(t.0[0]); }` → interp prints 10; `karac build`/`karac run` (JIT) both fail with `codegen failed: Index operator applied to non-array type`. `t.0.len()` on the same value compiles and runs.

title: Index-STORE into a `Vec` that lives in a TUPLE element (`t.0[i] = v`) is DROPPED by the tree-walk interpreter (SILENT — the store is a no-op: `let mut t = make(); t.0[1] = 99; println(t.0[1])` prints the ORIGINAL 2, not 99) and REJECTED loud by codegen ("Index assignment target must be a variable"). Both backends are wrong: interp silently, codegen loud. A plain `a[i] = v` on a bare Vec binding stores correctly, so the gap is specific to a tuple-element lvalue root.
detail: Class miscompile (the interp side is a SILENT wrong-answer; the codegen side is a loud fail-closed). Found alongside B-2026-07-20-1/2 in the tuple-element probe. The interpreter's index-assignment lvalue resolution evidently does not thread the mutation back into the tuple element's storage — it mutates a copy (or a detached view) and the write is lost, with no diagnostic. Codegen's index-store path (`compile_index_store` / the Assign arm) requires the index target root to be a named variable and rejects a `TupleIndex` root outright. FIX DIRECTION: interp — resolve `t.0[i]` as a place (mutable projection into the tuple element's Vec) so the store lands in-place; codegen — mirror B-2026-07-20-2's structural GEP for the store lvalue (reach the element `{ptr,len,cap}`, then reuse the Vec index-store). HIGH because the interp leg is silent; uncommon pattern (mutating a Vec through a tuple field of an owned `mut` tuple), so lower blast radius. WORKAROUND: destructure the tuple (`let (mut a, b) = t; a[i] = v`) — a plain Vec binding stores correctly. FIXED (b7d2bc8): interp — `set_index`'s target resolver gets a `TupleIndex` arm (eval the tuple index; `Value::Array` clones the `Arc<RwLock<Vec>>`, so the write aliases the element's storage — same mechanism as the `FieldAccess` arm). codegen — `compile_index_store` gets a `TupleIndex` root arm mirroring the read-side B-2026-07-20-2 fix (GEP into the element, synth identifier with the element `TypeExpr` from `temp_recv_elem_types` recorded for the `TupleIndex` index receiver — the Assign target is inferred as an Index, so the recording fires for the store LHS too, then recurse through the Vec store). Verified interp/JIT/native agree for scalar + `String` stores; valgrind- and ASAN/LSan-clean (the heap store frees the overwritten element once). Tests: interp `test_index_store_into_tuple_element_vec`, codegen E2E `e2e_index_store_into_tuple_element_vec`, memsan `asan_index_store_into_tuple_element_vec_no_leak`.
investigation: Repro: `fn make() -> (Vec[i64], i64) { let mut a: Vec[i64] = Vec.new(); a.push(1); a.push(2); a.push(3); (a, 0) } fn main() { let mut t = make(); t.0[1] = 99; println(t.0[1]); }` -> interp prints `2` (store lost, SILENT); `karac build`/JIT fail `codegen failed: Index assignment target must be a variable`. Control `let mut a: Vec[i64] = ...; a[1] = 99; println(a[1])` -> interp prints `99` (correct).

title: Calling a method on an indexed element of a tuple-element `Vec` (`t.0[i].len()`) fails codegen LOUD — "codegen: indexed-receiver method 'len' requires the indexed container to be a named variable in v1 (got non-identifier inner expression)" — while the interpreter runs it.
