Fails closed (no wrong answer); the indexed-receiver method path only accepts a named-variable container, not a `TupleIndex`-rooted one.
detail: Class codegen-gap (loud, fails closed). Found in the tuple-element probe. The indexed-receiver method dispatch (`t.0[i].method()`) requires the indexed container (`t.0`) to be a named variable; a `TupleIndex` inner is rejected. Distinct from B-2026-07-20-2 (plain index READ, now fixed) — this is a method ON the indexed element. FIX DIRECTION: mirror B-2026-07-20-2 — synthesize a named binding aliasing the tuple element's Vec storage (via `field_chain_place_ptr`), then re-dispatch the indexed-receiver method through the named path. LOW: loud, and the destructure workaround (`let (a, b) = t; a[i].len()`) is idiomatic. FIXED (8cfa72a): `compile_indexed_receiver_method` already hoists a `FieldAccess` / map-get container to a synth Vec identifier; add the `TupleIndex` sibling — GEP into the tuple element's storage (`field_chain_place_ptr` + `place_chain_aggregate_llvm_type`, structural), register a synth Vec identifier with the element `TypeExpr` from `temp_recv_elem_types` (recorded for the `Index` receiver), and let the identifier-keyed indexed-receiver lowering proceed. `Vec`/`VecDeque` elements only. Verified interp/JIT/native agree, valgrind-clean. New codegen E2E `e2e_method_on_indexed_tuple_element_vec`. Completes the tuple-element cluster B-2026-07-20-1..4.
investigation: Repro: `fn make() -> (Vec[String], i64) { let mut a: Vec[String] = Vec.new(); a.push("beta"); (a, 0) } fn main() { let t = make(); println(t.0[0].len()); }` -> interp prints `4`; `karac build`/JIT fail `codegen: indexed-receiver method 'len' requires the indexed container to be a named variable in v1`.

title: `Iterator.partition()` codegen lowered only a trivially-copyable element and loud-deferred a HEAP element (String/Vec) to `--interp` (the documented residual of B-2026-07-19-14). Now the heap case is lowered too: interp/JIT/AOT parity for a `Vec[String]` partition.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: follow-on to B-2026-07-19-14's intentional heap-element `--interp` deferral. Class codegen-gap (interp-only heap element -> run-vs-build divergence at the codegen boundary). FIX (ba6751f): `partition`'s desugar pushes each fused-chain element into one of two `Vec[T]` accumulators; the element comes BORROWED from the source `.iter()`, so a shallow push would make both the source element and the target Vec own the same buffer (double-free at scope exit). Gate on `elem_is_heap = !is_trivially_copyable_te(elem_te)` and push `param.clone()` for a heap element (deep copy -> independent buffer) while keeping the bare-`param` push for a scalar (the proven path). Verified interp == JIT == AOT for a `Vec[String]` partition (both halves + the borrowed source all usable afterward); LSan-clean over a looped heap partition (asan_partition_heap_string_no_leak: each cloned String freed once, source frees its own once). New codegen E2E test_e2e_partition_heap_elements. Full --features llvm suites green (codegen 2507, memory_sanitizer 809); clippy/fmt clean. RESIDUAL: `find_map` over a HEAP payload is NOT lowered here. Its terminal sink-built `match f(x) { Some(v) => … }` (distinct code path from `filter_map`'s STEP-built match via build_fused_chain_body's FilterMap arm, which DOES handle heap) mis-EXTRACTS a heap payload from the temporary-Option scrutinee — bare-`v`, cloned-`v`, a self-seeded payload-binding type, and a typed `Option[U]` scrutinee temp were all tried and all still produced heap garbage, so the extraction bug is in the sink-built match itself, not the payload sizing/move. Left as a documented `--interp` deferral (the scalar gate restored) pending a deeper fix that either routes find_map through the FilterMap step machinery or fixes the sink-built match's heap payload extraction.
investigation: The heap edges of B-2026-07-19-14 were flagged as intentional deferrals matching the `find` scalar gate. partition heap turned out clean (clone-on-push, mirroring the collect/flatten heap-push pattern). find_map heap did NOT: the payload read as garbage across every attempted fix, and the decisive diagnostic (bare `v` with no clone still garbage, while find_map SCALAR and filter_map HEAP both work) localized the bug to find_map's own sink-built match extraction, not the sizing/clone. Reverted find_map to its scalar gate rather than ship a broken heap path. Tests: test_e2e_partition_heap_elements (codegen), asan_partition_heap_string_no_leak (asan). — DEEPER FIND_MAP-HEAP ATTEMPT (2026-07-20, reverted, NOT shipped): took five distinct approaches, all failed. (1) removing the scalar gate + cloning the payload -> heap garbage; (2) self-seeding the Some(payload) binding type at filter_map_bind_span via seed_synthetic_pattern_binding_type -> different garbage (sizing changed, still wrong); (3) a typed `Option[U]` scrutinee temp to force full-width construction -> garbage; (4) routing the closure through a synthetic terminal FilterMap fused STEP (the same build_fused_chain_body machinery filter_map uses) + self-seed -> STILL garbage, even with bare (un-cloned) payload; (5) desugaring to `{ let v: Vec[U] = <recv>.filter_map(<closure>).collect(); v.get(0) }` (reusing the fully-proven try_compile_filter_map_collect, which types the pushed element from the target Vec[U] and works for a HAND-WRITTEN filter_map().collect().get(0)) + seeding owned_temp_drops at the synthetic collect span -> produced a Vec[Option[U]] (double-wrapped, `Some(Some(..))`), i.e. the synthetic collect did NOT unwrap. ROOT CAUSE (revised): find_map heap is blocked by a synthetic-AST vs typechecker-side-table coupling, NOT merely the sink-built match. The proven filter_map/collect heap paths rely on typechecker-registered side tables (owned_temp_drops keyed at the REAL collect span, the payload binding surface type at the REAL closure-body span) that a codegen-synthesized filter_map/collect subtree does not carry; manual seeds at computed spans did not fully reproduce them (the collect fell through to a Vec[Option] path). FIX DIRECTION (future): either (a) have the TYPECHECKER lower find_map to filter_map().collect().get(0) (so all side tables are populated naturally), or (b) give try_compile_filter_map_collect / the FilterMap step a codegen entry point that takes the Vec[U] element type as an explicit argument instead of reading owned_temp_drops by span. — SECOND deeper find_map-heap round (2026-07-20, also reverted): confirmed the typechecker arms for find_map and filter_map are structurally identical (same `Fn(T)->TypeParam` pushdown, same Option[U] extraction, same record_pattern_binding_surface_types at filter_map_bind_span), so the closure-body typing is NOT the difference. Routed find_map through the EXACT FilterMap fused STEP (append `(FusedStepKind::FilterMap, param, body)` to the peeled steps) + self-seed the payload binding + a TYPED `let pv: U = <extracted>` sink to give `Some(pv)` the heap width — still garbage. Ruled out `break` as the cause (a no-break variant storing the last Some is also garbage). KEY LOCALIZATION: the FilterMap step's `Some(v)/None` match extraction feeds a heap payload that `push(v)` (filter_map collect's sink) consumes CORRECTLY but a `let pv: U = v` / `Some(v)` consumes as GARBAGE. The difference is that `push` copies the raw payload words typed by the TARGET Vec[U], bypassing the binding's own reconstruction, whereas `let`/`Some` reconstruct `v` via its binding type — so the binding's reconstructed value is wrong even though the same words pushed into a Vec are right. This points the deep blocker at how the fused FilterMap step's Option scrutinee (the closure body's `Some(<heap>)` construction) is SIZED at codegen when its type isn't annotated on the construction node — it only survives when the consumer re-types from an external anchor (the target Vec). FIX (future, needs IR-level tracing): ensure the FilterMap step binds its extracted payload as a properly-typed materialized value (or annotate the closure body's Some-construction) so any consumer — not just push — reads it correctly.
resolution: Fixed (ba6751f) for partition heap (clone-on-push). find_map heap remains a documented `--interp` deferral: a 2026-07-20 deeper attempt tried five approaches (sink-match tweaks, self-seed, typed-temp, FilterMap-step routing, and a filter_map().collect().get(0) desugar) and all failed — root cause is that a codegen-synthesized filter_map/collect subtree lacks the typechecker-registered side tables (owned_temp_drops at the real collect span, payload binding surface type) the proven heap paths depend on. Deferred pending a typechecker-level find_map->filter_map().collect().get(0) lowering or a span-independent Vec[U]-typed collect entry point.

tracker: src/ownership.rs (move checker — method-chain temp-receiver arg classification)
title: `karac check` reports a false `error[ownership]: value 'row' moved here, used again here` for an iter_axis ROW-VIEW reused across two CHAINED `row.zip_with(row, ..).sum()` calls, even though `zip_with`'s `other` param is `ref` (a borrow, not a move). The trigger is narrow: it needs ALL of (row-view base) + (chained method: `.sum()`/`.mean()`/`.prod()` appended to the `zip_with`) + (reuse across statements). Each leg alone is clean: a row-view reused in the LET-BOUND method form (`let p = row.zip_with(row, f); p.sum(); let p2 = row.zip_with(row, f); ..`) passes; a row-view used ONCE per iter in a chain passes; a ref-PARAM (not a row-view) reused in the chained form passes. `karac run --interp` computes the correct result; `karac build` additionally mis-lowers the chained-reuse temp (a distinct 'Index operator applied to non-array type' codegen error), so the let-bound method form is the working shape.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: surfaced while fusing the data-spine embedding kernels (the map/zip -> reduce fusion slice, docs/spikes/data-spine-autovec-measurement.md Resolution). The single-pair kernels (dot/l2_norm/cosine_similarity) and dot_batched were rewritten to the chained `zip_with(...).sum()` form so codegen's new fused map-reduce emitter (src/codegen/kernel.rs::emit_fused_map_reduce) fires. cosine_similarity_batched and cosine_similarity_matrix could NOT be rewritten: their per-row bodies reuse an iter_axis row-view across two combines (the dot + the row norm), which trips this false positive. They keep the original let-bound `zip_with` method form (self-contained — delegating to dot/l2_norm is also out, because gated `import std.embeddings.{..}` splices only the named fn, not its callees), so their inner loops do not fuse. WORKAROUND for users: let-bind the zip_with result before reusing the row-view (`let p = row.zip_with(row, f); let s = p.sum();`). WHEN FIXED: cosine_similarity_batched/matrix inner loops can adopt the chained form and inherit the fusion. FOLLOWUP: the 62d148c fix added `zip_with` to the `is_tensor_borrow_arg_method_call` allowlist, which cannot cover USER-DEFINED chained methods — a plain `a.combine(a).total()` with a user `combine(ref self, other: ref Self)` still false-rejected. Generalized so ANY method resolves correctly on the collision: when the span-keyed mode entry is absent (the collision signature) the arg mode is resolved by method NAME (arg_is_borrow_by_method_name), covering stdlib and user methods alike, and the borrow.rs states-machine twin (which 62d148c left untouched) now matches the use_classifier predicate pipeline. SECONDARY codegen symptom split out to B-2026-07-20-11.
repro: fn scan[N, D](corpus: ref Tensor[f32, [N, D]]) -> Vec[f32] { let rows = corpus.iter_axis(0); let mut out: Vec[f32] = Vec.new(); for row in rows { let d = row.zip_with(row, |x, y| x * y).sum(); let e = row.zip_with(row, |x, y| x * y).sum(); out.push(d + e); } out }  // karac check: error[ownership] 'row' moved. Let-binding each zip_with result first -> clean.
investigation: 2026-07-20: narrowed with three repros — a row-view reused across two CHAINED `row.zip_with(row, f).sum()` calls false-errors; the LET-BOUND method form (`let p = row.zip_with(row, f); p.sum()`) passes; a row-view used ONCE per chain passes; and (decisively) an OWNED-LOCAL tensor `t` reused across chained `t.zip_with(t, f).sum()` ALSO false-errors while a ref-PARAM reused chained passes — so it is NOT row-view-specific but a general chained-method-arg misclassification, identical to B-2026-07-14-18 (matmul). ROOT CAUSE: the parser sets `MethodCall.span == receiver.span`, so in a chain the outer `.sum()` clobbers the inner `zip_with`'s `method_callee_types` entry at the shared span; `method_arg_is_borrow_position`'s span-keyed `resolved_method_mode_key` then resolves the inner call to `Tensor.sum` (0 params) -> arg 0 -> None -> consume default, and the reused binding false-rejects. FIX (62d148c): add `zip_with` to `is_tensor_borrow_arg_method_call` (src/use_classifier.rs), the AST-method-name allowlist that short-circuits BEFORE the span-keyed lookup and is immune to the collision — the exact remedy B-2026-07-14-18 used for matmul/broadcast_*. `zip_with`'s `other` is `ref` on every defining type (Tensor, Column, ElementwiseMap trait), and the check only ever converts a false consume to a borrow (never masks a real move), so it is sound. Verified: karac check passes all three repros; karac build + interp agree; ownership 360, rc_fallback 48, codegen 2512, memory_sanitizer 809 (LSan) green; clippy + fmt clean. Regression tests: test_chained_zip_with_reduce_reuse_ok_b20_6 (owned local), test_chained_zip_with_reduce_rowview_reuse_ok_b20_6 (row-view).
resolution: Fixed (62d148c) by extending the span-collision-immune borrow-arg method allowlist to `zip_with`. Unblocked the data-spine fusion follow-on: cosine_similarity_batched and cosine_similarity_matrix rewritten to the chained `zip_with(...).sum()` form, so their per-row inner loops now fuse (one fewer alloc per row-loop body) — all six embedding kernels fuse. NOTE: this is the SECOND occurrence of the MethodCall.span==receiver.span chained-arg false-consume (matmul was B-2026-07-14-18); a future general fix could resolve the inner method's param modes without the collided span (e.g. name-unanimity across `method_param_modes`) to retire the per-method allowlist.

title: `Map[K, struct-with-heap-field].get().unwrap()` DOUBLE-FREES under codegen (JIT + AOT-O2/O0); interp correct. `let a = m.get(k).unwrap(); print(a.name)` where the map VALUE is a struct carrying a `String` (or Vec) field aborts with `free(): double free detected in tcache 2`. The get materializes a SHALLOW copy of the struct value that shares the inner heap pointer, so the `a` binding's scope-exit drop and the Map's own drop free the same buffer twice.
detail: Class double-free (memory corruption; high). SOURCE NOTE: probe / fresh dogfooding sweep. DISTINCT from the fixed Map-heap-value family — B-2026-07-14-15 (`Map[K, String].get().unwrap()` bare heap value), B-2026-07-15-26 (inline `map.get(k).unwrap()` bare heap value), B-2026-07-16-1 (struct FIELD `Map[K,heap-value]` scope-exit drop): those all fix the case where the map VALUE is itself a bare heap type (String/Vec). This new variant is a map value that is a STRUCT WRAPPING a heap field, so the get's value-reconstruction copies the struct bytes shallowly (duplicating the inner `String` header pointer) without a `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place`; the copy and the map then both own+drop the same allocation. FIX DIRECTION: when `Map.get().unwrap()` (or the borrowed-value materialization behind it) yields a struct-typed value with heap fields, either (a) deep-copy the struct's heap fields into the `a` binding (clone semantics, matching what interp does), or (b) treat the binding as a borrow that does NOT drop the inner fields — mirror whatever the bare-String get() fix chose (B-2026-07-14-15), extended to recurse into struct fields via `deep_copy_struct_heap_fields_in_place` (src/codegen/param_own.rs). Repro programs saved under the session scratchpad (probe2/min1.kara).
resolution: Fixed (bd2bb92). Added `is_nonshared_struct_value_map_get_unwrap` (src/codegen/stmts.rs) — a Map sibling of `is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct` / `is_nonshared_heap_value_map_get_unwrap` — and OR'd it into `struct_borrow_elided` at the let-binding site. When the RHS is `m.get(k).unwrap()`/`.expect()` and `m` is a Map (gated on the Map-only `map_val_types`, so a `Vec[Struct].get()` receiver never matches — `var_elem_type_exprs` is shared across Vec/Map) whose non-shared value type is a user struct, the binding aliases the map's struct and takes NO scope-exit struct-drop, so the map stays the sole owner (the ownership checker ties the borrow's lifetime to the map). Field reads still resolve via `var_type_names`. Verified interp/JIT/AOT parity + ASAN/LSan; scalar-struct / bare-String / Vec[String] controls unchanged. Codegen E2E `test_e2e_map_get_unwrap_struct_heap_value` + LSan `asan_map_get_unwrap_struct_heap_value_no_double_free`.

title: `Vec[T].sorted_by(cmp: Fn(T,T)->Ordering)` / `String.sorted_by` (immutable CUSTOM-COMPARATOR sort returning a new Vec/String) is UNIMPLEMENTED in codegen — loud-bails `Vec/String method 'sorted_by' is not yet supported in codegen` under `karac build`/JIT; runs under `--interp`. The no-comparator sibling `Vec[T].sorted()` landed (B-2026-07-19-15, c6848c4); the comparator variant is the natural follow-on.
detail: Class codegen-gap (interp-only method; low — loud bail, workaround is `--interp`). SOURCE NOTE: probe / fresh dogfooding sweep. Sibling to B-2026-07-19-15 (`Vec[T].sorted()` no-comparator, fixed c6848c4). FIX DIRECTION: mirror the `sorted()` desugar (a `{ let mut tmp = <clone of receiver>; tmp.sort_by(cmp); tmp }` lowering) but thread the user comparator closure through to the sort — the comparator returns an `Ordering` enum, so the lowering must call the closure and branch on the three Ordering variants (or forward to a runtime sort-with-comparator entrypoint). `String.sorted_by(|a,b| ...)` is the char-sequence twin. Also relevant: `sorted_by_key` if present.
resolution: Fixed (a87b290) — Vec leg. Lowered `Vec[T].sorted_by(cmp)` by desugaring to `{ let mut tmp: Vec[E] = v.clone(); tmp.sort_by(cmp); tmp }` — the comparator sibling of the `sorted()` desugar (B-2026-07-19-15, c6848c4), forwarding the user closure verbatim (its original span survives, so span-keyed typechecker side-tables for the closure still resolve). Everything the in-place `sort_by` supports applies to the clone: the capture-free inline-closure mono fast path (N<=64) AND the runtime callback thunk; unsupported comparator shapes fail LOUD via sort_by's own error. Verified interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 parity on a struct-field comparator, a DESCENDING scalar comparator, receiver immutability, heap String elements, and N=100 (crossing the mono/runtime threshold). New codegen E2E `test_e2e_vec_sorted_by_immutable_comparator_sort` + LSan `asan_vec_sorted_by_heap_clone_no_leak_no_double_free`. DEFERRED (loud): `String.sorted_by(cmp)` — the runtime char-sort (`karac_string_sorted`) has no comparator variant; it now bails with a SPECIFIC actionable message (use `.sorted()` or `--interp`) instead of the generic dispatch tail. If a kata/dogfood run ever needs the String leg, file a fresh entry.

title: `Vec[struct-with-heap-field].get(i)/.first()/.last().unwrap()` field read is FLAKY under codegen (JIT + AOT): `let a = v.get(0).unwrap(); print(a.name)` where the Vec ELEMENT is a struct carrying a `String` field returns the CORRECT value on some runs and `0` (a zeroed/garbage read) on others — nondeterministic across identical runs, so a latent uninitialized-or-use-after-free read. Interp is always correct. Sibling of the Map double-free B-2026-07-20-7 (fixed bd2bb92) but a DISTINCT surface: the Vec accessors return `Option[ref elem]`, and the struct-element borrow-elision detector `is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct` does not fire for this shape (its `borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_inner` finds no recorded `ref` unwrap-inner type), so the value goes through the normal struct reconstruction which reads freed/uninit memory.
detail: Class use-after-free (nondeterministic; high — silent wrong reads / potential memory unsafety). SOURCE NOTE: probe / fresh dogfooding sweep; surfaced as the Vec sibling of B-2026-07-20-7. DISTINCT from B-2026-07-20-7 (Map, deterministic double-free, fixed bd2bb92): here the receiver is a Vec, `.get`/`.first`/`.last` return `Option[ref elem]`, and the read (not a drop) is corrupted. Likely the same borrow-elision family: `is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct` SHOULD borrow-elide a struct element read from a Vec accessor (alias the container element, no drop), but `borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_inner` returns None for this shape because `method_unwrap_inner_types` has no `ref`-typed entry for `v.get(i).unwrap()` here, so the struct is reconstructed through a path that shallow-copies then reads freed/uninitialized bytes. FIX DIRECTION: ensure the Vec/Slice `.get`/`.first`/`.last().unwrap()` of a heap-owning struct element is recognized and borrow-elided (record the `ref elem` unwrap-inner type for the struct-element case, OR add a Vec sibling predicate keyed on `vec_elem_types` + struct element, symmetric to the Map fix's `is_nonshared_struct_value_map_get_unwrap`). Verify with a determinism loop (many JIT runs) AND ASAN/LSan, not a single run — the single-run trap is exactly what masked it initially.
resolution: Fixed (b7b72eb). ROOT CAUSE CORRECTION: not a runtime use-after-free — a COMPILE-TIME NONDETERMINISTIC MISCOMPILE (class corrected to miscompile). Decisive experiment: ONE built binary was 100% deterministic across 10 runs; separate builds of the same source flipped between `alice` and `0` — so the wrong code was baked in at compile time. IR diff of a good vs bad compile showed the bad one lowered `print(a.name)` to `snprintf("%lld", 0)` — a CONSTANT zero. Chain: `bind_pattern_types` does not peel the Vec accessor's `Option[ref elem]`, so the binding had no recorded type; codegen's LLVM-shape reverse-lookup (src/codegen/stmts.rs ~3800) then picked the FIRST same-shape struct in HashMap iteration order — `Acct { name: String }` collides with the prelude's `Regex`/`RegexError` (all `{ptr,i64,i64}`), so the binding was mislabeled on the losing orders, `field_index_for` found no `name` field, and the field read fell to the SILENT `i64 0` fallback in compile_field_access. Three-layer fix: (1) register the binding's struct name from the typechecker's span-keyed peeled element type (`borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_inner`, the struct leg of the B-2026-07-14-11 registration; drop stays borrow-elided via `is_borrowed_vec_get_unwrap_struct`); (2) the shape reverse-lookup (struct + union) fires only on a UNIQUE match — ambiguity skips deterministically; (3) the unresolved-field-access fallback now fails LOUD instead of emitting `i64 0` (full codegen suite green, so nothing legitimate relied on the silent zero). Verified 30 fresh compiles (JIT + AOT) all deterministic-correct with interp parity; new codegen E2E `test_e2e_vec_get_unwrap_struct_heap_field_read` + LSan `asan_vec_get_unwrap_struct_heap_field_no_leak_no_double_free`; codegen (2514) + memory_sanitizer (811) + interp + typecheck + selfhost_codegen oracle green.

title: Every WASM program that frees a heap buffer traps at runtime (`unreachable` via a `signature_mismatch:karac_free_buf` stub). Codegen declared `karac_free_buf(u8*, bytes_hint)` with a hardcoded i64 `bytes_hint`, but the runtime's `bytes_hint: usize` is i32 on wasm32 — wasm-ld can't reconcile the `(i32-ptr, i64)` import against wasi-libc's `(i32, i32)` definition, so it emits a trapping stub. Native never sees it (usize=i64), so it survived every native test; the sequential-wasm heap path had no in-browser consumer until now.
detail: Class crash (wasm-only; target-specific ABI mismatch -> runtime trap). Found by DOGFOODING: the Prism image workbench (kara-katas/apps/prism), the first `--target=wasm_browser` program to allocate+free a `Vec[u8]`, trapped in its `process()` export with `RuntimeError: unreachable` at `signature_mismatch:karac_free_buf`. ROOT CAUSE: `Codegen::new` (src/codegen.rs) declared `karac_free_buf`'s second param as `context.i64_type()` unconditionally, while the sibling size-taking externs (snprintf, alloc) already use `size_t_type` (i32 on wasm32, i64 on 64-bit native — the exact pattern the adjacent comment documents: 'i64 here mismatched wasi-libc's i32'). `karac_free_buf` was simply missed. wasm-ld's response to a call/def signature mismatch is not a link error but a runtime-trapping `signature_mismatch:<name>` stub, so it linked cleanly and only blew up when the free path actually ran. WHY IT HID: (1) native usize=i64 so the decl matched by accident; (2) ssr_counter -- the only prior sequential-wasm example -- frees NO heap on its wasm path (its Strings are the native-only leg; the wasm `hydrate` only passes i64s to host fns), so it never called karac_free_buf; (3) the threaded browser demos (iris/fathom/slipstream) free `Vec[u8]` every frame on wasm32-threads (usize also i32), so they hit the SAME trap -- which plausibly explains why Slipstream's render loop 'never advanced' when driven in a headless browser (the first frame's buffer free trapped). FIX: declare the `bytes_hint` param as `size_t_type` (codegen.rs), and build the hint at that width in `emit_free_buf_call` (runtime.rs) -- truncate the i64 `cap*elem_size` product to i32 on wasm32. Native is byte-identical (size_t_type=i64, no truncate), confirmed by memory_sanitizer (809 pass). VERIFIED on wasm: Prism's pure-node harness (instantiate prism.wasm with mock host fns feeding a known 2x1 RGBA image, call `process(2,1)`) now returns the correct grayscale `[76,76,76,255, 150,150,150,255]` (red 255*30/100=76, green 255*59/100=150, alpha kept) where it previously trapped. FOLLOWUP: iris/fathom/slipstream should be re-verified in a real browser now that the free path works; a headless wasm-exec regression test would guard this class (the codegen suite is native-only, which is exactly the blind spot). NOTE: originally drafted as B-2026-07-20-5 but renumbered to -10 to avoid an ID collision with a concurrent agent's batch.
investigation: Repro: `karac build prism.kara --target=wasm_browser`, then in node `instantiate(host,{bytes}); handle.exports.process(2n,1n)` where process allocates two `Vec[u8]` and frees them -> `RuntimeError: unreachable at prism.wasm.signature_mismatch:karac_free_buf (wasm-function[12]) at process`. Control: ssr_counter (no wasm heap free) instantiates + `hydrate(42n)` returns 42 cleanly on the same tree/archives, isolating the fault to the heap-free path, not the wasm toolchain.

tracker: src/codegen/kernel.rs (emit_fused_map_reduce — f32 Option-payload reconstruction over an iter_axis row-view base)
title: `karac build` fails LLVM module verification with `Invalid bitcast: bitcast float %elem to i64` for a fused f32 map-reduce whose base is an `iter_axis` ROW-VIEW: `for row in t.iter_axis(0) { let d = row.zip_with(row, |x,y| x*y).sum(); }`. `karac run --interp` computes the correct result (run-vs-build divergence). Narrowed: a single fused f32 `a.zip_with(b, f).sum()` over PLAIN tensor args builds fine (M1); the i64 form builds fine (M2); reuse over plain args builds fine (M3); the trigger is the iter_axis row-view base feeding the fused f32 reduce (M4). The row-view path wraps elements in an Option (the emitted `%enum.fw` / `%or.pl.fc` payload words), and the synthesized Some(<f32>) payload reconstruction bitcasts a 32-bit float directly to/from a 64-bit payload word — invalid (bitcast requires equal bit width).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: the SECONDARY codegen symptom carved from B-2026-07-20-6 (data-spine embedding-kernel fusion). Once the ownership false positive was fixed, `karac check` and `karac run --interp` pass but `karac build` fails codegen verification. SAME ROOT-CAUSE CLASS as B-2026-07-17-11 (fixed 75e248d) — a synthesized Option[<elem>] reduce accumulator whose Some(<acc>) payload binding lacked a pattern_binding_types entry, so payload reconstruction fell to the raw-i64 default and mis-sized a float payload. 75e248d fixed the NON-fused try_compile_iter_chain_reduce path (unique synthetic span + register the element surface name in pattern_binding_types). This is the sibling gap in the FUSED emit_fused_map_reduce path (src/codegen/kernel.rs), and it is f32-SPECIFIC: f64 shares the 64-bit payload width so `bitcast double->i64` is valid, but f32 needs the to_bits32 recipe (bitcast f32->i32 then zext->i64, inverse trunc->i32 then bitcast->f32) — the same width-normalize the B-2026-07-03-1 to_bits32 arm uses. FIX DIRECTION: give the fused emitter's synthesized Some(<elem>) payload binding a unique synthetic span and register the element's surface type in pattern_binding_types (mirror 75e248d), OR route f32 payloads through the width-normalizing bitcast recipe rather than the raw-i64 default. FIXED: the defect was in the GENERIC payload pack/unpack helpers, not the fused emitter — coerce_to_i64 (pack) direct-bitcast f32->i64 (invalid: float<->int bitcast needs equal widths) and three unpack sites (or.pl.fc / pl.fc / pl.sub.fc) did the inverse. All four now route f32 through its 32-bit pattern (bitcast<->i32 + zext/trunc), the low-32-bits contract the width-aware arms (pat.f32.*, col.f32bits) already implemented. coerce_to_i64 is the single pack funnel, so every enum-payload producer is covered. Verified interp==JIT==AOT on the fused row-view repro (direct for-over-iter_axis AND the stdlib cosine_similarity_batched kernel), Option[f32], Option[struct{f32,f32}], Result[f32,String], f32 iter reduce; regression test test_e2e_f32_option_payload_pack_unpack; codegen 2514 + memory_sanitizer 810 green. NOTE the materialized `let rows = t.iter_axis(0); for row in rows` form never hit this (different lowering path) — only the direct `for row in t.iter_axis(0)` form did, which is why the in-tree embeddings E2E tests stayed green while the M4 repro failed.
repro: fn scan[N, D](corpus: ref Tensor[f32, [N, D]]) -> Vec[f32] { let mut out: Vec[f32] = Vec.new(); for row in corpus.iter_axis(0) { let d = row.zip_with(row, |x, y| x * y).sum(); out.push(d); } out }  // karac build: Invalid bitcast float to i64; karac run --interp: correct.

title: An f16 inside a COMPOUND enum payload (`Option[(f16, f16)]`) builds and runs but produces a WRONG VALUE under `karac build`: `match o { Some(p) => println(p.0 + p.1) }` prints 32512 (0x7F00 — a mis-sized bit pattern read back as f16) while `karac run --interp` prints the correct 4. SILENT wrong answer (worst failure class). PRE-EXISTING: bit-identical before and after the landed B-2026-07-20-11 f32 width-normalize fix (5f5897b), so the f16 compound payload routes AROUND the four fixed sites (it never failed verification, unlike f32) — the mis-sizing lives elsewhere in the f16 payload pack/unpack path. A bare `Option[f16]` is untested here; f32 and f64 compound payloads are correct (B-2026-07-20-11 tests).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while independently probing the B-2026-07-20-11 payload-width fix with all three float widths (verified against the landed 5f5897b). Severity medium not high: silent wrong-answer class, but f16/bf16 are early-stage primitives (B-2026-07-13 slice) with a niche compound-Option surface, and the interpreter is correct. FIX DIRECTION: trace where a compound f16 payload packs/unpacks (it is NOT the coerce_to_i64 / payload_word_to_float sites — those now width-normalize and the f16 result is unchanged); likely the f16 tuple field packs through a different (memcpy/alloca or int-typed) route that assumes 32/64-bit float widths. Repro: fn main() { let o: Option[(f16, f16)] = Some((1.5f16, 2.5f16)); match o { Some(p) => println(p.0 + p.1), None => println(0.0f16) } }  // build: 32512, interp: 4. FIXED: three sibling omissions, all 'f16/bf16 skipped where f32/f64 are handled'. (1) typechecker float_surface_name mapped only F32/F64 -> an f16 payload binding got NO pattern_binding_types entry and stayed the raw i64 word (the SINGLE Option[f16] case was ALSO wrong, not just compound: printed 15874.5 = sitofp(0x3E00) + 2.5). (2) typechecker type_to_type_expr mapped only F32/F64 -> the recorded (f16,f16) tuple TypeExpr lowered elements via the unknown-name i64 default -> {i64,i64} tuple, integer add of bit patterns = 32512. (3) codegen float pack/unpack split two-way (f64 direct / else via i32) -> width-mismatched for 16-bit floats. Fix: record f16/bf16 like the other floats + new float_bits_int_type helper (f64->i64, f32->i32, f16/bf16->i16) at coerce_to_i64 (single pack funnel) and the four unpack arms; pattern_payload_llvm_type companion hardening for float tuple-element slots. Verified interp==JIT==AOT on compound binding, tuple destructure, runtime-value pack via fn, single, bf16, Result[f16,String]; regression test test_e2e_f16_bf16_enum_payload_pack_unpack; codegen 2516 + memsan 811 green. NOTE the const-literal pack path was already correct (compile-time folded bits) — only the binding/unpack side and the runtime pack path were broken.

title: wasm-threads has NO path for a request-driven EXPORTED fn to use the worker pool: `instantiate()` exports run on the caller's (browser main) thread by design, where every blocking primitive (`TaskHandle.join`, `TaskGroup`, `recv`) bottoms out in `memory.atomic.wait` and traps — so an app shaped as 'JS calls `process(...)` on demand' (Prism, and any tool-style app: SSR hydrate with heavy compute, an export-driven data cruncher) cannot fan work across the pool at all. The pool is reachable only by restructuring the whole program into a `run()`/`_start` blocking-loop-in-primary-worker shape driven by the baked host-async channels (frames/pointer/wheel/keydown), which have no generic 'request with arguments' producer.
detail: Class missing-feature. SOURCE NOTE: found building Prism (kara-katas/apps/prism, the dogfooding.md roster entry) — a request-driven local image workbench whose Lanczos resample (~1.2 s for 12 MP main-thread) is exactly the compute a pool should cut by ~cores x. The design constraint is deliberate and correct as far as it goes (phase-10-targets.md, wasm-threads entry: dual artifact, `run()` picks threaded/sequential, `instantiate()` stays sequential-only; PROXY_TO_PTHREAD exists precisely because atomics.wait traps on the main thread). What's missing is the third leg: PROXIED EXPORTS — an `instantiate()`-style handle whose exported fns are dispatched to the primary worker (the same worker->main proxy machinery exists for host fns, needed in reverse) and return asynchronously (a Promise on the JS side), so a request-driven program can be threaded without restructuring into an event-loop shape. Alternative acceptable shapes: (a) a generic host->guest REQUEST channel (a `Channel[T]` producer JS can push structured args into, sibling of pointer_moves/wheel — lets a run()-loop app implement request-driven dispatch on the pool today), or (b) emscripten-style proxied exports (each export call marshalled to the primary worker, caller gets a Promise). Note the JS-side call shape necessarily changes (sync export -> Promise), so this is an API-design slice, not just plumbing. Until one exists, request-driven wasm apps are locked to the sequential module — fine for correctness (the sequential scheduler runs TaskGroup FIFO), but the multicore story silently does not apply to the whole tool-app class, which is a real hole in the 'multicore WASM' pillar (dogfooding.md Prism/Veil). FIXED (7517adb) via option (b)-adjacent PROXIED EXPORTS: a new `instantiateThreaded()` glue entry dispatches every export call to a dedicated 'exports' worker (where blocking `join`/`recv` are legal) over postMessage, each export returning a Promise; host fns ride the existing worker->main sync proxy, thread-spawn the existing spawn service. Runtime init runs WITHOUT `_start` via a crt split — the threaded link now `--export`s `__wasi_init_tp` + `__wasm_call_ctors` (driver.rs), because an export-only program's `main` is an undefined-weak that traps, and pthread futexes hang without the thread-pointer init (both failure modes hit live during bring-up). Sequential fallback keeps the identical Promise call shape (no SAB/COI, `forceSequential`, or a sequential-only build); `[wasm] fallback = false` throws; host-async producers gated on the module's ACTUAL kara_host imports with a loud use-run() error; `handle.terminate()` for node lifecycles. d.ts fills the spec-first `KaraThreadedHandle` contract previously landed in `render_dts`. E2E `tests/cli.rs::wasm_threads_instantiate_threaded_export_taskgroup_e2e` (4-task TaskGroup fan-out + proxied host fn + two calls on one handle, exact values); Prism's `process()` verified threaded end-to-end (byte-exact grayscale) and identical under the fallback.
investigation: Prism (kara-katas/apps/prism) is the repro: `#[target(wasm_browser)] pub fn process(...)` called via `instantiate().exports.process(...)` from the page. Building with `--features wasm-threads` emits the dual artifact, but the threaded module is only reachable via `run()` (which executes `_start`, not exports); calling exports on the sequential module works (shipped, CDP-verified) while any TaskGroup.join inside an export on a threaded main-thread instance would trap in Atomics.wait (non-blockable agent). No code change was attempted around it — the app stays on the sequential module; this entry is the feature request.

title: Auto-par return-slot rebind LOSES an unannotated struct binding's type identity: `let tx = make_taps(..)` (struct-of-Vecs, no annotation) fanned out in a parallel group, then `tx.los[i]` after the join fails codegen LOUD with 'Index operator applied to non-array type'. Compile failure on code that builds with auto-par off; also broke `--features wasm-threads` wasm builds (auto-par re-enabled there) while sequential wasm masked it.
detail: Class other (auto-par compile failure — fails closed, no wrong answer; not a miscompile/leak). SOURCE NOTE: found building Prism's Lanczos resampler (kara-katas/apps/prism) — `let tx = lanczos_taps(sw, dw)` + `let mut tmp: Vec[f64] = Vec.filled(..)` form an independent-lets parallel group; the following loop reads `tx.los[x]` / `tx.wgts[..]`. ROOT CAUSE: `compute_return_slots` populated `ReturnSlot.var_type_name` ONLY from an explicit let annotation (`let_binding_annotation_type_name`, added for the B-2026-07-03-21 unsigned-slot fix); the join rebind then re-registered the parent binding without `var_type_names`, so `lower_field_access_ptr` could not resolve the struct type for the FieldAccess-rooted index and the generic tail bailed loud. FIX (29a4a97): slot typing falls back to the callee's declared return type (`fn_return_type_exprs`, last path segment) for a `let x = f(...)` RHS, gated to `struct_types` membership so scalar/Vec/String slots keep prior behavior exactly. Ownership across the join was ALREADY sound for struct slots (valgrind: definitely+indirectly lost = 0 — the struct's Vec buffers free once in the parent; only benign pool-thread TLS 'possibly lost'). Regression `tests/par_codegen.rs::auto_par_unannotated_struct_of_vecs_slot_field_index` (exact oracle 31). VERIFIED: Prism's `--target=wasm_browser --features wasm-threads` dual-artifact build now completes with `process` exported; sequential-leg node oracles unchanged (ALL PASS).
investigation: Repro (native, auto-par ON): `struct Taps { los: Vec[i64], wgts: Vec[f64] }` + `fn make_taps` + `fn consume { let tx = make_taps(dw); let mut tmp: Vec[f64] = Vec.filled(dw, 0.0); while .. { let lo = tx.los[x]; .. } }` -> 'codegen failed: Index operator applied to non-array type'. KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 builds and runs. `--concurrency-report` shows the two lets grouped ('independent effects'). Annotating the let (`let tx: Taps = ..`) also sidesteps, pinning the loss to the annotation-only slot typing.

tracker: src/interpreter (user impl Drop dispatch — drop timing + ordering)
title: INTERP BUG (run-vs-build): user `impl Drop` destructors run at LAST USE in FIFO (declaration) order under the tree-walk interpreter, while codegen (JIT + AOT) runs them at SCOPE EXIT in LIFO order — the spec'd semantics (design.md § Drop: 'the compiler calls drop at scope exit'; 'Drop calls and defer blocks lower into the same LIFO stack'). Two divergences in one: TIMING (interp fires the destructor immediately after the binding's last use — before subsequent statements in the same scope) and ORDER (interp drops in declaration order, codegen in reverse-declaration order). Observable whenever a Drop body has side effects (logging, releasing a lock file, flushing).
investigation: Found by a fresh dogfooding parity sweep (2026-07-21). Repro A (timing): `struct Res { name: String }  impl Drop for Res { fn drop(mut ref self) { print(f"closed:{self.name}|"); } }  fn main() { { let r = Res { name: "res1".to_string() }; print(f"use:{r.name}|"); print("after-last-use|"); } print("end"); }` -> interp: `use:res1|closed:res1|after-last-use|end` (drop fired BEFORE the trailing statement); JIT/AOT: `use:res1|after-last-use|closed:res1|end` (scope exit — correct). A never-used binding drops IMMEDIATELY after construction under interp. Repro B (order): two bindings `a` then `b` in one scope -> interp `c:A|c:B|` FIFO at last use, before the scope's remaining statements; JIT/AOT `tail|c:B|c:A|` LIFO at scope exit. Codegen is the oracle on both counts (matches the spec text).
detail: Class run-vs-build (interpreter is the wrong side; codegen matches design.md § Drop). SOURCE NOTE: probe / fresh dogfooding sweep, probing `impl Drop` for the first time — the whole user-Drop surface appears untested for interp/codegen ORDERING parity (value cleanup itself is fine; this is purely about WHEN/in what ORDER the user drop body's side effects run). FIX DIRECTION: the interpreter appears to fire user Drop eagerly when a binding's refcount/last-use ends (or drops at value-release time in evaluation order) instead of queueing destructors per scope and draining LIFO at scope exit. Mirror codegen: register Drop-bearing bindings per scope frame, run their user drop bodies at frame teardown in reverse declaration order. Same-scope `defer` interleaving (design.md: one shared LIFO stack with defer) should be checked in the same fix.
resolution: Fixed (7c4bf90) — with a DIAGNOSIS CORRECTION: the original filing had the sides backwards. design.md § Drop ordering (867, 9805) prescribes NLL placement — destructors fire at each binding's LIVE-RANGE END, draining the single drop+defer stack LIFO; §8807's 'scope exit' phrasing is the imprecise one. So the INTERPRETER was right on timing (it implements the NLL sub-step) and wrong only on same-statement multi-drop ORDER (front-to-back FIFO); CODEGEN was wrong on timing (every user drop body ran at scope exit) — an existing interp test even carried a 'reconciling these is a follow-on slice' note. The fix reconciles both to the spec: codegen's `compile_block` + the auto-par sequential-statement arm precompute the block's last-use map with the SAME `compute_block_last_use` analysis the interpreter uses (now crate-shared; pure AST) and fire due user drops per statement via the new `fire_due_user_drops` (LIFO within a statement, fired entries removed from the frame, self-guarded on terminated blocks); the interp's `fire_due_drops` walks back-to-front for same-statement LIFO. Gated to non-shared STRUCT user-drops (registration mutually exclusive with all other cleanup); enum user-drops (dual-registered with a complementary EnumDrop walk), par-branch registrations, and bindings last-used inside auto-par groups conservatively stay at scope exit (residual, loud-free, never a double-fire). Verified interp == JIT on all four repro shapes (mid-scope fire, never-used dies at declaration, same-statement LIFO, scope-end); updated the two tests pinning the old divergent behavior + new E2E test_e2e_user_drop_nll_timing_and_order. codegen (2523) + interpreter (1270) + memory_sanitizer (813) + par_codegen (197) + selfhost oracle green.

tracker: src/codegen/method_call.rs (method dispatch — `next` on a chained iterator receiver)
title: `<chain>.next()` — e.g. the idiomatic first-char read `s.chars().next().unwrap()` — is UNIMPLEMENTED in codegen: loud-bails `no handler for method 'next' on non-identifier receiver` under `karac build`/JIT while `--interp` runs it. Known-deferral class (stateful `next()` has no codegen iterator value, cf. B-2026-07-11-19's materialized-iterator scope-out), but the CHAIN-RECEIVER form needs no state: `<chain>.next()` is just the first yielded element.
investigation: Found by a fresh dogfooding parity sweep (2026-07-21). Repro: `fn main() { let s = "Hello".to_string(); let c = s.chars().next().unwrap(); print(c); }` -> interp prints `H`; `karac build`/`karac run` fail loud with the no-handler diagnostic (with the --interp hint). LOUD deferral, not a miscompile.
detail: Class codegen-gap (low — loud bail, workaround `--interp` or indexing). SOURCE NOTE: probe / fresh dogfooding sweep. Distinct from stateful multi-pull `it.next(); it.next()` on a MATERIALIZED iterator binding (genuinely hard in codegen — no runtime iterator value; explicitly scoped out and failed-closed by B-2026-07-11-19). The chain-receiver single-pull form is tractable: desugar `<chain>.next()` to a fused for-loop over the peeled chain that assigns `Some(first)` and breaks after one yield (exactly the `find(|_| true)` shape — could even lower AS `find` with a const-true predicate, reusing try_compile_iter_chain_find incl. its scalar gate + loud heap deferral). `chars().next()` needs the char-source peel leg the fused engine already has for `for c in s.chars()`.
resolution: Fixed (5a9536b). `<chain>.next()` lowers as `find(|_| true)` — a synthesized const-true predicate into the proven `try_compile_iter_chain_find` terminal (same peel, same span-keyed `Option[T]` annotation via a new `iter_terminal_elem_types` record in the typechecker's `next` arm, same scalar gate + loud heap deferral). Two guards keep out-of-scope shapes loud: stateful multi-pull on a MATERIALIZED binding is intercepted BEFORE the iter-let substitution (inlining would silently re-yield element 0 every pull) with a specific actionable message; heap elements defer via find's gate. Bonus: `expr_is_char` now recognizes unwrap/expect whose recorded inner type is char, so the INLINE `println(s.chars().next().unwrap())` renders the glyph instead of the codepoint. Verified 4-backend parity on chars/filter-hit/filter-miss/map/range; E2E + loud-deferral tests; full suites green.

title: Construction-heavy `Vector[f64,2]` patterns are a ~4.7x PESSIMIZATION on wasm: rewriting Prism's Lanczos tap loop from 4 scalar f64 accumulators to two lane-pair vectors (fresh `Vector[f64,2](a, b)` built per tap from u8/f64 loads + a `.splat` per tap) took the 3 MP threaded resize from 183 ms to 866 ms (sequential 516 -> 1146 ms), byte-identical output. The Fathom pattern (vectors LIVE across the loop, splats hoisted, rare lane extracts) wins 1.47x; the per-tap-construction shape loses badly — likely lane-insert/extract dominated or partially scalarized.
detail: Class perf. SOURCE NOTE: measured on kara-katas/apps/prism (banded Lanczos, node 22, wasm-threads build; scalar restored after measuring — the app keeps the fast form). Two suspects worth IR inspection: (1) each tap builds TWO fresh vectors via lane inserts from scalar loads (`Vector[f64,2](src[p] as f64, src[p+1] as f64)`) plus a `.splat(wgts[..])` — if the lowering materializes these through memory or scalarizes the arithmetic, the vector form does strictly more work than the scalar; (2) bounds checks on the per-tap `wgts[base+(sx-lo)]` index may block vectorization-friendly hoisting. FIX DIRECTION: inspect the emitted wasm/IR for this shape; consider (a) recognizing insert-from-adjacent-loads as a single unaligned v128 load where layout allows (tmp[p..p+1] IS contiguous f64x2 — the vertical pass could be a true v128.load), (b) documenting the idiom constraint in design.md SIMD notes (vectors should live across loops; construction-per-iteration is an anti-pattern the optimizer does not clean up). The vertical-pass case is the actionable one: `Vector[f64,2](tmp[p], tmp[p+1])` is literally a contiguous f64x2 load the lowering could emit as one instruction.
investigation: A/B on identical band-split code, byte-identical outputs both ways. Scalar: 3 MP thr 183 ms / seq 516 ms; 12 MP thr 438 / seq 1058. Vectorized taps: 3 MP thr 866 / seq 1146; 12 MP thr 2420 / seq 2618. Revert restored 217/490 (machine-load noise vs 183/438). Also surfaced en route: adding Vector ops to the file made the effect checker require `panics` on the pub export (declared; loud and correct).
resolution: Fixed (6951017) — the actionable contiguous leg, plus the idiom documented. (1) New peephole `try_compile_vector_adjacent_vec_load` in `compile_vector_construction`: `Vector[T, N](v[b], v[b+1], …, v[b+N-1])` — all lanes consecutive indexes into ONE plain Vec whose element type IS the lane type, side-effect-free base (identifier or int literal; the `b + k` offsets are matched in both the surface `Binary` Add form and the lowering pass's desugared `iN.add(b, k)` Call form, which is what index position actually carries) — now lowers as a SINGLE elem-aligned `load <N x T>` (`v128.load` on wasm, unaligned vector load on native) instead of N checked scalar loads + N insertelements. Bounds parity is preserved: base checked first (respecting BCE-proven halves), then `b+N-1` with its lower half proven by the base check — same panic, same order as the scalar chain; verified OOB aborts identically. The Prism vertical pass (`Vector[f64,2](tmp[p], tmp[p+1])`) is exactly this shape. Non-matching constructions (casts — the horizontal `src[p] as f64` shape can never be a contiguous load; mixed lanes; non-consecutive offsets; slice/array receivers) keep the insertelement chain. (2) design.md § Portable SIMD gained an 'Idiom note — construction inside hot loops' documenting construction-per-iteration as an anti-pattern (keep vectors live across iterations, hoist splats — the Fathom pattern) and the recognized adjacent-load shape. IR regression test `test_ir_vector_adjacent_vec_load_fuses` pins the fusion (and that mixed constructions keep the chain); E2E `test_e2e_vector_adjacent_vec_load_shapes` pins output parity across interp/JIT/AOT. RESIDUAL: the horizontal u8->f64 cast-per-lane shape stays construction-bound by design (documented); Prism-side re-measurement of the vectorized-taps variant is a kata-repo follow-up (this container has no kara-katas checkout) — the app itself kept the fast scalar form, so nothing regresses meanwhile.

tracker: src/codegen (expression-statement drop of a discarded Option[shared] value; Map.remove path in src/codegen/maps.rs returns the moved-out value)
title: A DISCARDED `Map.remove(k)` result over a `Map[K, shared V]` LEAKS the removed value's RC. `Map.remove` MOVES the value out of the bucket (the runtime tombstones the slot and does NOT free the value — it's handed back as `Some(old)`), so the returned `Option[shared V]` OWNS that ref. When the call is used as a bare expression statement (`m.remove(k);`, discarding the result), codegen never drops the discarded temporary, so the moved-out shared value's refcount is never decremented and its box leaks. BINDING the result and consuming/dropping it (`let removed = m.remove(k); match removed { … }`) is clean — the scope-exit / match drop releases it. So the leak is specific to the DISCARDED-expression-statement form of a value-moving Map.remove.
investigation: 2026-07-19: surfaced building kata #146 (LRU Cache), whose eviction removes a node from a `Map[i64, shared DNode]`. Minimal repro: `m.insert(1, DNode{...}); m.remove(1);` (discarded) leaks 24 bytes (one DNode box) under valgrind/`karac build`; the same program that instead binds `let removed: Option[DNode] = m.remove(1)` and matches it is clean. Confirmed the map's own DROP is fine (insert two, drop the map at scope exit — clean), so the leak is not the container teardown but the discarded remove-result. Root: `Map.remove` (maps.rs ~2060) returns `Some(old)` with the value moved out of the bucket (the runtime's `karac_map_remove_old` only frees the KEY buffer via `drop_key`, never the value — 'moved out into the returned Some(old)'), and codegen's expression-statement lowering doesn't emit a scope-exit drop for a discarded Option[shared] temporary. GENERAL SHAPE: any discarded expression statement whose value is `Option[shared]` / `shared` and which OWNS a ref (a move-out, not a borrow) needs a drop; Map.remove is the concrete instance found. FIX (deferred): drop a discarded expression-statement result when its type carries an owned shared/Option[shared] ref (emit the same RcDec/RcDecOption the let-binding scope-exit path uses). WORKAROUND (kata #146): bind the remove result and consume it via `match` (also the natural form when eviction needs the evicted key).
detail: SOURCE NOTE: kata #146 (LRU Cache). Class leak (discarded owned Option[shared] expression-statement result not dropped; Map.remove is the concrete trigger). Low severity: binding+consuming the result is a clean, idiomatic workaround, and the leak is bounded to the discarded-remove form. Map container drop and bound-result drop are both correct — only the bare `m.remove(k);` discard leaks. Not a regression.
resolution: Fixed (47f7940). Two coupled legs: (1) the DISCARDED form — a new `try_track_discarded_shared_option` in the statement-expression discard chain queues the standard tag-guarded `RcDecOption` at the `;` for an owned `Option[shared T]` temp; the payload type resolves from the span-keyed Option[V] record when present, else from the Map receiver's `var_elem_type_exprs` value record (a MethodCall node reuses its receiver-side span, so `m.remove(k)` has no span-table entry — the reason the general fix direction's 'emit the same RcDec the let-binding uses' needed a receiver-derived type source). MethodCall producers are gated to `remove` only: IR inspection showed a displacing `m.insert(k, v2)` releases its old value INLINE in the insert lowering (`map.ins.some` rc_dec) — a second dec would double-free. (2) the UNANNOTATED BOUND form, found during verification: `let r = m.remove(k)` (no type annotation) never registered the binding's Option[shared] scope-exit release either (builtin Map.remove has no `fn_return_option_inner_shared` entry, so let-case (b) missed) — leaked the bound box whenever the match arm only READ the payload; new let-case (b2) derives the shared info from the same receiver record. Valgrind-clean on the ledger repro, displacing-insert + absent-key + unannotated-bind combinations, and the annotated form; interp/JIT/AOT agree. E2E `test_e2e_discarded_map_remove_shared_value` + LSan `asan_discarded_map_remove_shared_value_no_leak` added.

title: AOT double-free: Vec[struct-with-enum-field] element bind -> ref-param call -> match on the enum field -> concat consumes the String payload binding. 19-line repro: `let t = v[0]; render(t)` where render(st: ref SpTok) matches `st.tok` and an arm returns `"id:".to_string() + name`. Interp correct; AOT aborts with free(): double free; `karac run` (JIT) exits 1 SILENTLY with no output (a third divergence surface). Element-bind aliasing family (B-2026-06-14-12 / B-2026-07-18-2): the element bind's deep copy of the enum String payload appears aliased with the container's copy, and the ref-callee's consuming concat frees it against the binder's drop.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found by the Phase-12 emitter-port oracle work (Slice 33 nested-aggregate capstone smoke, 2026-07-21) — the SpannedToken shape. NOT nested-struct-specific: repro needs only { tok: Tok, n: i64 }. TWO independent triggers, both interp-green/AOT-double-free: (A) vec element bind + ref call + consuming match; (B) NO vec at all — `render(a); let b = a; println(render(b));` (ref-lend, rebind, second ref call — 26-line minA). For trigger A, ingredients all required (each removal goes green): (1) the struct comes from a Vec element bind `let t = v[0]` (a direct mk()/literal binding is clean, and a whole-struct rebind `let b = a` is clean); (2) passed to a `ref` param (matching the field in main directly is clean); (3) the match arm CONSUMES the payload binding via concat (name.len() is clean). Also note the silent-exit-1 JIT behavior with zero stdout/stderr — worse than a crash for diagnosability. Repro file preserved in the emitter-port session notes as min14.kara; full text in `investigation`.
resolution: [renumbered from B-2026-07-21-3 on 2026-07-21: duplicate id from a concurrent-session collision] Fixed (94cf1c4). Root cause (shared with B-2026-07-21-6): the match move-out source suppression resolved the `st.tok` place chain via `field_chain_place_ptr`, whose Identifier arm returned the raw variable slot for the ref-param root `st` — but a ref param's 8-byte slot holds a POINTER to the caller's struct, not the struct, so the `match.dest.suppress.wp` stores (three `store i64 0` at offsets 8/16/24) landed OUT OF BOUNDS in adjacent stack slots. Depending on opt-level/frame layout the corruption surfaced as this AOT double-free, -6's empty-string JIT read, or a silent exit 1. Two-part fix: (1) `field_chain_place_ptr` bails on a `ref`/`mut ref` param root (incl. `ref self`) — no caller may treat a borrowed root as an in-place suppression target (the callee doesn't own that storage); (2) an ESCAPING `match <refparam>.field { V(x) => <consume x> }` over a heap-bearing non-shared user enum now deep-clones the scrutinee (`clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_enum`, the borrowed-chain sibling of `clone_escaping_owned_agg_loop_var_enum`) and rides the freshtemp enum drop-tracking: bindings own independent buffers, a no-bind arm frees the clone, and the caller's value is never touched — matching interpreter semantics. Read-only matches keep the zero-cost borrow-alias path (escape-gated). Note the escape detection counts string `+` as a move because concat desugars to `Call(String.add, [l, r])` and call args are conservative moves. Verified on both repros + minA + identity-return + no-bind + deep-chain + `ref self` + `mut ref` variants across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 (all print id:foo etc.); E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_enum_field_payload_consume` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_enum_field_payload_consume_no_leak_no_double_free` added. Struct-typed-field sibling (struct PATTERN through a ref chain) was found pre-existing and filed as B-2026-07-21-7.

title: JIT-only miscompile: a match-bound String payload of an enum FIELD reached through a `ref` struct param prints EMPTY when an arm CONSUMES it via concat — `karac run` prints 'id:' where interp and AOT both print 'id:foo' (three-way divergence). 17-line repro: render(st: ref SpTok) matching st.tok, arm `head = "id:".to_string() + name`. Non-consuming uses (name.len()) are correct; heavier compositions (a Vec[SpTok] coexisting) degrade to a SILENT exit 1 with zero stdout/stderr.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found alongside B-2026-07-21-5 during the Phase-12 emitter-port Slice-33 capstone (SpannedToken shape). DISTINCT from -3 (an AOT double-free needing a vec-element bind or lend-rebind-lend): this is JIT-only — interp green, AOT green, `karac run` wrong-output. Scoping: consuming concat of the payload binding is the trigger (name.len() arm is correct, min12); no Vec needed (min3/min9/minB all print 'id:'); adding a Vec[SpTok] + element bind escalates to SILENT exit 1 with no diagnostic at all (sptok3/jbis2) — whatever the root, `karac run` should never exit nonzero without a message. Probable relation: the JIT leg of the same payload-ownership confusion as -3 (the AOT leg double-frees, the JIT leg loses the buffer).
resolution: [renumbered from B-2026-07-21-4 on 2026-07-21: duplicate id from a concurrent-session collision] Fixed (94cf1c4) — same root cause and fix as B-2026-07-21-5 (see its resolution): the out-of-bounds `match.dest.suppress.wp` stores through the ref-param pointer slot zeroed the `name` binding's String alloca on this frame layout, so the consuming concat read an empty string under JIT ('id:' instead of 'id:foo'); other layouts double-freed (AOT, -5) or exited 1 silently. Fixed by the `field_chain_place_ptr` borrowed-root bail + the escaping ref-chain scrutinee deep-clone (`clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_enum`). Both repros verified across all four backends; regression tests shared with -5.

title: Struct-PATTERN destructure of a struct-typed FIELD reached through a `ref` param double-frees when a binding escapes: `match h.inner { Pt { s, x } => return "p:".to_string() + s + ... }` with `h: ref Holder` aborts `free(): double free detected` under JIT and AOT (O0+O2); interp prints p:sp:7. The struct-leaf sibling of B-2026-07-21-5/-6: the bound field `s` bit-copy-aliases the CALLER's String and both the binding's scope-exit free and the caller's struct drop free the same buffer.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: found while fixing B-2026-07-21-5/-6 (probing sibling leaf kinds). Pre-existing, NOT a regression from the -5/-6 fix: verified double-freeing on the pre-fix compiler too (there via the same OOB suppress-store corruption; post-fix `suppress_destructured_struct_pattern_cleanup` cleanly bails on the borrowed root, leaving the alias + double free). The -5/-6 clone fix covers only a USER-ENUM leaf (`clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_enum` gates on `enum_layouts`); a STRUCT leaf has no freshtemp drop-tracking channel to ride — a struct-scrutinee clone would need its own StructDrop registration + a per-arm suppression keyed on the clone alloca (new machinery, deliberately not bolted on in that slice). Read-only arms are fine (borrow classification covers them); only an ESCAPING binding (concat/return — note string `+` desugars to `Call(String.add, ..)` and call args count as escapes) hits the double free. Repro: struct Pt { s: String, x: i64 } / struct Holder { inner: Pt, n: i64 } / fn render(h: ref Holder) -> String { match h.inner { Pt { s, x } => { return "p:".to_string() + s + ":" + x.to_string(); } } return "?".to_string(); } / main: let a = Holder{..}; println(render(a)); println(render(a));  => interp p:sp:7, JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 double-free abort.
resolution: Fixed (06ea22a) — the struct-leaf twin of the B-2026-07-21-5/-6 fix. An ESCAPING struct-pattern match over a pure FieldAccess/TupleIndex chain rooted at a `ref`/`mut ref` param now deep-clones the scrutinee (`clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_struct`), registers the clone's own `StructDrop` (`track_struct_var`), and fires each arm's per-field cap-zero suppression against the CLONE slot (new `suppress_destructured_struct_pattern_cleanup_at`, factored from the #16 owned-place suppressor whose expr-resolving wrapper bails on borrowed roots by design). Bindings own independent buffers; unbound fields (incl. a second unbound String field) are freed exactly once by the clone drop; the caller's struct is untouched — interpreter semantics. Gated to: no Index hop (element sources belong to the #18/#38 machinery), every arm a Struct pattern of the leaf type or wildcard (a whole-value binding arm keeps status quo), and `borrow_payload_clone_supported` (never a partial clone). NOTE from verification: even the 'read-only' `s.len() + x` shape was double-freeing (JIT/AOT-O0), because the `iN.add` desugar makes the scalar `x` a call arg the escape walk conservatively counts as a move — the clone leg covers that shape too (correctness first; the spurious-clone cost for scalar-only escapes is a possible later refinement of `no_arm_payload_escapes`). Verified m7 + scalar-escape + identity-move + unbound-second-String + two-hop-chain variants across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0, all valgrind-clean; E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_struct_field_struct_pattern_consume` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_struct_field_struct_pattern_no_leak_no_double_free` added.

title: if-let over an enum FIELD through a `ref` param with a CONSUMING binding double-frees: `if let Ident(name) = st.tok { return "i:".to_string() + name; }` (st: ref SpTok) aborts free(): double free under JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0; interp prints i:il. The if-let ROUTE sibling of B-2026-07-21-5/-6: compile_if_let consults scrutinee_is_borrowed_binding/borrow_call but never the ref-chain clone legs the match path gained (clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_enum/_struct), so the payload binding aliases the caller's buffer and both free it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe pass 4 (2026-07-21) sweeping ref-param field-chain consuming reads after the -5/-6/-7 fixes. Repro p3: enum Tok { Plus, Ident(String) } / struct SpTok { tok: Tok, n: i64 } / fn render(st: ref SpTok) -> String { if let Ident(name) = st.tok { return "i:".to_string() + name; } return "+".to_string(); } / main: render(a) twice. while-let and let-else use the same bind path and need the same wiring check. FIX DIRECTION: wire the match path's ref-chain clone legs into compile_if_let (and siblings), with the escape scope being the then-block.
resolution: Fixed (5fa61ca) — and generalized past the filed if-let repro to ALL three single-pattern binding routes. The ref-chain clone legs are now escape-flag parameterized (`clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_enum/_struct(escapes)`; the match site computes one shared `no_arm_payload_escapes` walk, single-pattern sites use the new `pattern_bindings_escape_in_block`). Wiring: (1) IF-LET — both legs before the freshtemp materialization (enum clone forces it via `did_clone`; struct clone's suppression fires on the clone slot then-arm only); (2) WHILE-LET — enum leg clones per HEADER evaluation (matching iterations ride the per-iteration freshtemp frame; the final non-matching evaluation's copy is freed wholesale on the miss edge via a new `force` flag on `drop_freshtemp_enum_scrutinee_on_miss`, since the fresh-temp gate would wrongly bail on a place scrutinee). The struct leg is deliberately NOT wired for while-let: a struct pattern always matches, making that shape a degenerate infinite loop, not an idiom. (3) LET-ELSE — both legs with an unconditional escape flag (bindings escape into the enclosing scope by construction, mirroring the existing `escape_exprs = None` clone convention there); the else edge diverges with the clone draining in its cleanup walk. Verified p3 (if-let enum), p3s (if-let struct), p7 (while-let enum, incl. Plus-token miss path), p8/p8s (let-else enum/struct) across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0, all valgrind-clean; E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_field_chain_iflet_whilelet_letelse` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_field_chain_iflet_whilelet_letelse_no_leak_no_double_free` added (the ASAN loop exercises the while-let miss edge per iteration).

title: match on an Option[String] FIELD through a `ref` param with a consuming Some arm double-frees: `match h.opt { Some(s) => return "o:".to_string() + s, None => … }` (h: ref Holder, opt: Option[String]) aborts free(): double free under JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0; interp prints o:op. The Option-LEAF sibling of B-2026-07-21-5/-6: the ref-chain clone leg gates to user enums (seeded Option has all-None drop kinds and no freshtemp channel), so the inline String payload binding aliases the caller's buffer and both free it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe pass 4 (2026-07-21), repro p2: struct Holder { opt: Option[String], n: i64 } / fn render(h: ref Holder) -> String { match h.opt { Some(s) => { return "o:".to_string() + s; } None => { return "none".to_string(); } } … } / main: render(a) twice. Result[heap,_] fields presumably share the shape. FIX DIRECTION: an Option/Result-leaf clone leg — clone the instantiated Option value (payload te from the owning struct's field te), track via the FreeInlineOptionPayload channel, cap-zero the clone slot on consuming arms.
resolution: Fixed (c1cbed1). New `clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_option` — the Option-leaf leg of the ref-chain clone family: gates on a pure FieldAccess chain rooted at a `ref`/`mut ref` param whose leaf field te (from the owning struct's field table) is an Option with an INLINE heap payload (`option_inline_payload_elem` — String/Vec/VecDeque; shared payloads bail to the rc machinery, boxed/Map payloads to their own cleanup families), plus the shared escape flag. Deep-clones the Option value via the dispatcher's tag-guarded `emit_option_value_clone_fn` and registers a `FreeInlineOptionPayload` on the clone slot; a CONSUMING `Some` arm/pattern zeroes the clone's TAG (`zero_refchain_option_clone_on_consume` → `zero_option_field_tag_at`) so the binding owns the payload, while `None`/`_`/`Some(_)` leave the cleanup armed. Wired into match, if-let, and let-else (while-let skipped — an invariant ref-field Option scrutinee is a degenerate loop). Verified across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 on: consuming match/if-let/let-else, `Some(_)` non-consume, Option[Vec[i64]] payload with element read, None-holder paths, and caller reuse — all agree with interp; valgrind-clean (an initially alarming 'possibly lost 4 blocks' turned out to be the auto-par worker pool's pthread TLS — benign, reproduced with KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 clean). Result[heap,_] fields remain on the status quo: the clone dispatcher has no Result deep-clone yet (noted in clone_drop.rs), so that sibling stays open territory if probing ever hits it. E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_option_field_consume` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_option_field_consume_no_leak_no_double_free` added.

title: match on a TUPLE-typed FIELD through a `ref` param with a consuming binding double-frees: `match h.pair { (s, x) => return "t:".to_string() + s + … }` (h: ref Holder, pair: (String, i64)) aborts free(): double free under JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0; interp prints t:tp:4. The tuple-LEAF sibling of B-2026-07-21-5/-6/-7: no clone leg covers a tuple leaf, so the String element binding aliases the caller's buffer and both free it.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe pass 4 (2026-07-21), repro p1: struct Holder { pair: (String, i64), n: i64 } / fn render(h: ref Holder) -> String { match h.pair { (s, x) => { return "t:".to_string() + s + ":" + x.to_string(); } } … } / main: render(a) twice. Medium (tuple struct fields are rarer than named-struct/enum fields). FIX DIRECTION: tuple-leaf clone leg — clone via the tuple clone/drop family (synthesize_tuple_drop_fn_te exists), track a TupleDrop-equivalent on the clone, per-element cap-zero on consuming arms.
resolution: Fixed (8c47c73). New `clone_escaping_borrowed_ref_chain_tuple` — the tuple-leaf leg of the ref-chain clone family: gates on a pure FieldAccess chain rooted at a SIGNATURE `ref`/`mut ref` param (the `signature_ref_params` subset from the -11 fix, so pattern-binding shims stay with their own machinery), the shared escape flag, a Tuple field te (from the owning struct's field table) with at least one heap element, and full clone-family support. Deep-clones via the dispatcher's per-element tuple clone, registers a tuple `StructDrop` (`synthesize_tuple_drop_fn_te`) on the clone slot, and consuming arms zero the consumed elements' caps in the clone (`zero_refchain_tuple_clone_on_consume` → `zero_tuple_elem_cap_at`) — a `_` element stays owned by the clone drop (freed exactly once; LSan-verified). Wired into match, if-let, and let-else (while-let: a tuple pattern always matches — degenerate loop, skipped like the struct leg). Verified consuming match, wildcard-element arm over a two-String tuple, if-let, let-else, and caller reuse across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 — all agree, valgrind-clean. E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_tuple_field_consume` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_tuple_field_consume_no_leak_no_double_free` added.

title: whole-field `let` move of a struct-typed FIELD through a `ref` param double-frees when the copy's heap is consumed: `let p = h.inner; return "l:" + p.s + …` (h: ref Holder, inner: Pt{String,i64}) aborts free(): double free under JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0; interp prints l:ld:6. The LET-route sibling of B-2026-07-21-7: the field read through the borrow is a bit-copy alias, the binding gets owned struct tracking, and both it and the caller's struct drop free the same String.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe pass 4 (2026-07-21), repro p5: struct Pt { s: String, x: i64 } / struct Holder { inner: Pt, n: i64 } / fn render(h: ref Holder) -> String { let p = h.inner; return "l:".to_string() + p.s + ":" + p.x.to_string(); } / main: render(a) twice. An OWNED root's `let p = a.inner` deep-copies (n1-style shapes are clean) — the ref-param root misses that deep-copy but still registers the owned StructDrop. FIX DIRECTION: at the Let compile, a struct-typed FieldAccess RHS whose pure chain roots at a ref param must deep-copy in place (same clone family as the -7 fix) before track_struct_var.
resolution: Fixed (ca37738) — and generalized past the filed struct-field repro to every heap-bearing leaf. New `clone_ref_chain_field_move_rhs`, hooked right after the Let RHS compile in `compile_stmt`: a pure FieldAccess/TupleIndex chain rooted at a `ref`/`mut ref` param deep-copies the compiled value IN PLACE, so the binding owns an independent copy and every downstream registration behaves as if the RHS were a fresh owned temp (interpreter copy semantics; caller's field untouched). Leaves: user STRUCT (dispatcher clone), user ENUM (`deep_copy_enum_heap_payload_in_place` — seeded Option/Result explicitly excluded from this leg, the erased-layout any-heap gate would wrongly bail), String, Vec/VecDeque (full field te from the owning struct's field table so element types survive), and Option with an INLINE heap payload (dispatcher's tag-guarded Option clone; shared payloads stay rc-managed, boxed/Map payloads with their own families; Result stays status quo — no dispatcher deep clone). During verification the sibling String/Vec/Option let-move shapes were found equally broken (not just the filed struct leaf) and are covered by the same helper. Verified all six leaves across interp/JIT/AOT-O2/AOT-O0 with caller reuse — byte-identical to interp, valgrind-clean under KARAC_AUTO_PAR=0 (the auto-par pool's pthread-TLS 'possibly lost' is the known benign pattern). E2E `test_e2e_ref_param_field_let_move_all_leaves` + LSan/ASAN `asan_ref_param_field_let_move_no_leak_no_double_free` added.

title: Vec[String] field .first() consuming read through a `ref` param LEAKS the element copy at O0: `match h.items.first() { Some(s) => return "f:" + s, … }` (h: ref Holder) leaks one element-sized block per call under AOT-O0 (valgrind: 2 bytes x 2 for two calls); output correct on all backends; O2 elides the allocation.
detail: SOURCE NOTE: probe pass 4 (2026-07-21), repro p4: struct Holder { items: Vec[String], n: i64 } / fn first_up(h: ref Holder) -> String { match h.items.first() { Some(s) => { return "f:".to_string() + s; } None => { return "empty".to_string(); } } … } / main: first_up(a) twice. Low: bounded (one small block per call), O0-only in the observed shape, output correct. Likely the borrow-call clone-on-escape leg cloning s but the clone's free not firing on the return path (or a method-chain temp copy left untracked) — needs IR look.
investigation: INVESTIGATION UPDATE (2026-07-21, post-filing): re-diagnosed — NOT a ref-chain bug and NOT the element copy. The leaked block is the `"f:".to_string()` LITERAL TEMP in the consuming arm, and the shape reproduces with a plain OWNED local (`let v: Vec[String] = [..]; match v.first() { Some(s) => println("f:".to_string() + s) .. }` leaks 6 bytes at O0, no ref param anywhere) and with a `ref Vec[String]` param (4 bytes). Generic class: on the BORROW-classified match path (`scrutinee_is_borrow_call` — get/first/last), a `.to_string()` temp built inside a consuming arm and consumed by the concat is not freed on the arm's return/terminated edge at O0 (O2 elides the allocation). Bounded (one literal-sized block per call), output correct everywhere. Title's ref-param framing is superseded by this note; the fix belongs in the borrow-path arm-return temp drain, not the ref-chain clone family (whose -8/-9/-10/-11 fixes are unrelated to this residue).
resolution: Fixed (acd2ba3). Final root cause (completing the investigation note's re-diagnosis): the leaked blocks were the `\"f:\".to_string()` OPERAND temp and the CONCAT RESULT of a string `+` that stayed a surface `ExprKind::Binary` — the lowering's `String.add` desugar skips the rewrite when an operand is a ref-typed Vec-accessor payload (`Some(s)` from `v.first()`), and the whole temp-freeing economy was built around the desugared Call shape: the assoc-call route frees operand temps after the op, and every consumer site's `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` gate (`expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp`) admits only Call/MethodCall. The surface-Binary concat therefore leaked BOTH blocks once per evaluation (print-arg form: operand + result; return form: operand only — the caller consumed the result). Not opt-dependent in truth: O2 merely folded the allocations out of the repro. Two-part fix, both scoped to the vec-struct-shaped Binary-Add: (1) the Binary compile in exprs.rs frees fresh-owned OPERAND temps after the concat (mirroring the assoc-call route; expr gates + cap>0 leave identifiers/places/literals untouched); (2) `free_fresh_owned_str_arg` admits a vec-struct Binary-Add arg, so consumer sites free the concat RESULT exactly like the desugared Call — behavioral parity with the proven route at every site, deliberately NOT widening the shared `expr_yields_fresh_owned_temp` (20+ classification callers). Verified 12 shapes (return/print/bound-intermediate/nested concat, owned + ref-param + struct-field receivers, get/first/pop/Map.get scrutinees, plain-statement controls) — interp-matching output, valgrind-clean at O0 with auto-par off. E2E `test_e2e_surface_binary_string_concat_shapes` + LSan/ASAN `asan_surface_binary_string_concat_temp_no_leak_no_double_free` added.

tracker: src/codegen/vec_method.rs (Vec.push/push_back arg-retain: has Option[shared] binding/field retain via share_option_shared_ref_for_arg, but no retain for a BARE `shared struct` element aliased from an indexed Vec read)
title: `vec_field.push(nodes[j])` — pushing a BARE `shared struct` element read from another `Vec[shared]` (an aliasing indexed read, source still owns it) into a `Vec[shared]` does NOT retain (inc) the element. The container and the source Vec then BOTH reference the node at refcount 1; when the SOURCE Vec drops (e.g. a function-local `Vec[Node]` whose one node is returned), its per-element dec frees the node while the container still points at it — use-after-free / garbage reads under codegen. The interpreter (reference-semantic handles) is correct, so it is a silent run-vs-build divergence.
investigation: 2026-07-21: found re-verifying kata #133 (Clone Graph) — README only ever claimed `karac run` (interp). Under `karac build` both variants diverge (bfs panics `unwrap() on None`, dfs garbage). Reduction: `shared struct Node { val, mut neighbors: Vec[Node] }`; build a `Vec[Node]` pool, `nodes[0].neighbors.push(nodes[1])`, RETURN `Some(nodes[0])` (or push into an outer Vec) so the local pool drops. Minimal repro (rc.kara): after the block `keep[0].neighbors[0].val` reads 200 under interp, garbage/crash under build. If the pool Vec stays ALIVE (bg3: same push, read in-scope) the read is CORRECT — the fault is the missing retain, exposed only when the aliasing source is dropped. NOT caused by the 2026-07-19 weak-ref / indexed-read-offset work: `Node` is non-weak, non-headerless, so `shared_gep_layout` returns base 1 (identical to the old `idx+1`), and bg3 confirms the read offset is correct. Vec.push (vec_method.rs ~2422) retains Option[shared] bindings/fields (`share_option_shared_ref_for_arg`) + heap-closure envs, but a BARE `shared struct` element aliased from an indexed read has no retain arm.
